No. 1721.No. 1.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 1st of January, 1721
England

PETERSBURG the 1st of December. His Majesty having been indisposed at Cronstadt and confined to his chambers for several days by an Express, has this day appeared in public again for the first time, attended divine service in the Church of the Resurrection, and subsequently celebrated the Feast of St. Andrew with great splendor. Due to the severe frost which we have here, all the rivers have frozen over.

Lorraine

NANCY the 19th of December. The Duke [Editor's note: Leopold, Duke of Lorraine] with the Prince and Princesses, together with the entire Court, have returned here from Lunéville. In recent days many more mines have been discovered, and work is underway to conduct trials of all the mines, which exist in abundance. The shares of our Company of Commandeurs are now at only 20 per cent premium; this decline is believed to have arisen because many private individuals who purchased them with no other purpose than to trade them once they had risen to a high price, now finding themselves unable to pay the premiums, such holders are obliged to sell them as quickly as possible. An Arrest has been published here from Monsieur R... of the Council of State of His Royal Highness, dated the 13th of this month and registered in the Court of the Mint on the 16th, whereby His Royal Highness has ordained the following:

First Article.

That on the 10th of January next, the gold and silver Leopoldus coins, minted pursuant to the Edict of the 7th of June 1718, in all States, Lands and Dominions under his authority, shall no longer be current after the 10th of February following, and whereas the gold Leopoldus coins of 25 to the Mark shall be valued at no more than 46 Livres; the silver ones of 10 to the Mark at 7 Livres 3 Sols 4 Deniers; the quarter crowns of 360 to the Mark, minted pursuant to the same Edict, at 49 Sols, the double and half in proportion: that the gold Louis coins of 25 to the Mark shall be received on the same basis as the gold Leopoldus coins of the same weight and fineness, the double and half in proportion; and that the French Ecus of 10 to the Mark shall likewise be received on the same basis as the silver Leopoldus coins of 10 to the Mark, each and twelfth parts in proportion: that the pieces of 36 and of 18 Deniers manufactured at the Mint shall remain reduced to 30 and 15 Deniers and the Liards to 3 Deniers. His Royal Highness wills that on said 10th of January, the Mark of Gold, at the fineness of the gold Leopoldus coins of 22 Carat, shall be received at the Mint only at the rate of 1123 Livres 3, and that the Mark of Silver of Ecus fineness, or of 11 Deniers, shall likewise be received there at the rate of 75 Livres, and the value under or over said fineness in proportion.

II.

That on said 10th of February, the same species shall have no other course until the 10th of March following, to wit the gold Leopoldus coins at 42 Livres, the silver ones at 7 Livres, the half crowns at 2 Livres 4 Sols 10 Deniers, the double and half in proportion: that the gold Louis coins of the same weight and fineness, and the French Ecus, shall be received on the same basis as the gold and silver Leopoldus coins; that the pieces of 30 and of 15 Deniers, manufactured at his Mints, shall continue in course at said rate; and the Liards at 2 Deniers; and that the Mark of Gold of the above-mentioned fineness shall remain fixed on said 10th of February at 1025 Livres, and the Mark of Silver of the same fineness at 65 Livres 6 Sols 8 Deniers.

III.

That on the said 10th of March next the same species shall be reduced, to wit the gold Leopoldus and Louis coins of 22 to the Mark to 38 Livres, the silver Leopoldus coins and French Ecus of 10 to the Mark to 6 Livres 6 Sols 8 Deniers, the half crowns to 40 Sols 7 Deniers, the smaller portions of these species in proportion: that the pieces of 30 and of 15 Deniers struck at the Mint shall hold course at the said rate of 30 and 15 Deniers, and the Liards at 2 Deniers; that all other gold and silver species, as well as those of mixed mint, shall remain rejected and out of course, to begin from the aforesaid 10th of January next, and shall not be received otherwise than as bullion at said Mint and Exchange; and that the Mark of Gold shall remain fixed on the aforesaid 10th of March at 925 Livres and the Mark of Silver at 61 Livres 13 Sols 4 Deniers.

IV.

His Royal Highness expressly forbids all persons, of whatever quality and condition they may be, to carry or cause to be carried outside his States, whether by land or water, directly or indirectly, and for whatever cause and pretense it may be, any species or materials of gold, of silver, and of mixed substances, without his written permission, on penalty against the contraveners, for the first time a fine of 300 Livres and confiscation of said species and materials, as well as of the horses and carriages that shall be used for the same transport, together with the merchandise with which they shall be packed, of which half shall be awarded to the informer or to those who shall prosecute the accused without denunciation, and the other half for His Royal Highness; and being found guilty the second time, on the same penalty, and moreover punished with death and confiscation of all goods, such that the penalties may be administered or moderated by the judges in their application.

V.

It shall nevertheless be permitted to foreigners who bring gold and silver species to His Royal Highness's Mint, or to the Exchange, or to the Commissioners of the Exchanges appointed in various cities, to carry out of the States the mixed metals and silver species that shall be given to them in exchange, under written certification from the Directors of the Mint and the exchangers, or the same Commissioners in their capacity.

VI.

Those who shall travel to other countries shall always be permitted to take with them the money they need for their travel expenses.

VII.

Moreover His Royal Highness forbids all persons in his States to exchange, and to conduct any species-exchange against goods outside the merchants, on penalty of death above the fines and confiscations hereinbefore stated; and to goldsmiths, to melt or deform any gold or silver species, on penalty of 3000 Livres fine for the first time, and for the second time on corporal punishment.

VIII.

All those who shall have knowledge of the surrender and exchange of gold and silver materials contrary to the Ordinances may make their disclosure and denunciation under the name of such persons as they shall deem necessary for this purpose; being paid by the Director of the Mint, on whose behalf the fines and confiscations shall accrue to them, under those same names.

IX.

His Royal Highness expressly commands, on penalty of interdiction and even more if necessary, all the officers and justices of his States, those of his Majesty, the exchangers and Commissioners of the Exchange, to investigate, prosecute and arrest without distinction all those who are held suspect of acting against the present decree.

Germany & the Adjacent Realms, Etc

VIENNA the 18th of December. Last Sunday, being the third Sunday of Advent, public chapel was held morning and afternoon in the Palace. The following morning His Imperial Majesty held Council concerning the present state of affairs; and on Thursday the Emperor entertained himself outside this city with the boar hunt. A few days ago the Count of Colonitz, Prince of the Holy Roman Empire and Bishop of this city, took the oath as actual Councilor of State, and he has already taken possession of it with the customary solemnities. The Count of Virmond, who has been at various Courts in the Empire, has returned here, and has had the honor of greeting the Emperor and thanking His Majesty for the governorship of Zevenbergen; he was received very amicably by the Emperor; and it is said that he is preparing to go and take possession of his governorship. The East India Company which has been established here has today received news from Messina, confirming that its ship Charles VI has arrived there safely, that the merchandise loaded in it has been sold; and that various Sicilian merchants, who are members of this Company, have already paid their share in the capital. Concerning the Diet in Hungary it is now quite silent, and the necessary arrangements for it will not yet be completed, as is believed; many think that this postponement must be attributed to the absence of Count Erdoedi, who is on the embassy to Poland. Today it is being spoken of as if the Bank might soon come to advance between 20 and 30 millions to His Imperial Majesty on various Imperial revenues, which would be pledged for it. It is said that the Emperor has sent orders to Cardinal von Althan to go to Naples in quality of Viceroy, in place of Cardinal von Schönborn, who returns to his bishopric of Olmütz; that Cardinal del Giudice will look after Imperial affairs at the Court of Rome in place of Cardinal von Althan; and that Cardinal Borromeo, after having taken his place in the College of Cardinals at Rome, will go to Sicily as Viceroy, in place of the Duke of Monteleon, who is to be invested with some other office. The Court has sent orders to its Minister at Constantinople to support the request of the King and the Republic of Poland in their insistence on the fortifications of Choczim, as being contrary to the Peace of Carlowitz. The Emperor has granted 600,000 florins to the dowager Princess of Baden, in consideration of the sums which the late Prince her husband had to claim from this Court. Count Francis von Kevenhüller and Count von Lamberg have arrived here from Lintz, Count Hofpman from Silesia, Baron von Mandorf from Edenburg, and Count von Tallewitz from Dresden.

REGENSBURG the 23rd of December. It has been foreseen for a long time that the Electoral Directorate in this state of affairs would arrange a meeting with the Protestant Ministers, but it was decided by the latter, should this come to happen, not to appear therein; and said Directorate having announced the meeting to the Princely Estate Corps to deliberate on the Imperial Commission Decrees, pursuant to the aforesaid decision no one appeared on behalf of the same at the Town Hall, but they held a private conference among themselves and conferred on the measures that ought to be taken because of this incident; and as far as can be learned, they will suspend their meeting until the answer to their remonstrances made to the Emperor on this matter is communicated to them; on the other hand the Roman Catholic Ministers have also held a meeting at a third location, and have made it their intention to send a deputation to the Emperor regarding what has befallen them. One can well see that this matter has still considerably agitated minds on both sides; but nevertheless it is generally welcomed, even by Roman Catholics, that now it is clearly [text ends]

Here is the English translation:

ENGLAND

[Text for England section not included in provided transcription]

HAMBURG the 17th of December. According to the letters from Berlin, they have not yet finished with the building of the Palace; they were only engaged there with breaking down what still remained standing and removing the Garden; meanwhile they are busy ordering the materials for the rooms and having them prepared, from which one trusts that the building will soon be resumed. In Meißen they have arrested 3 Robbers who were guilty of murdering a Student, and when these were examined, they were found guilty of many other crimes.

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM the 19th of December. By letters of the 11th of this month from Lisbon, there is news from the Bahia de Todos los Santos that, notwithstanding they expected a harvest of Sugar and Tobacco there, the Fleet with its 3 Warships was not expected at Lisbon before May or June. In the said Bahia, the Ship of Captain Maanoel Lopez Antony has safely arrived from Lisbon bound for Rio de Janeiro, in which latter Place the Ship N. S. de los Anyos and St. Antonio, Captain Antonio Fernandes de Moura, from Port to Port bound for Bahia, had arrived; also another Portuguese ship from Port to Port bound for Rio, has entered a Haven 6 miles from Bahia. Because of the long delay of the Fleet from Bahia, one hopes that a Ship with Gold will come with it. According to the aforementioned letters from Lisbon (where various Ships have safely arrived), on the Coast of Algarve an unknown Warship with 13 Guns has been stranded, and most of the People from it have drowned. At St. Domingo has arrived the Ship le Savoy Captain Schabofftery Budam from Guinea; at Venice the Upron Galy Captain William Knox from Livorno; at Rochelle Captain Pierre de la Haye from Lisbon; in Norway Sippe Annes and at Riga Gabbe Cornel. from here, as also Juriaan Eppe from Königsberg; at Copenhagen Leendert Snoum, Thomas Carstens and Klaas Janssen from Riga, Danzig and Königsberg; at Lübeck Christiaan Pieterse from Danzig; on the River of London John Houlandson, Richard Anderson, Isaak Webb and other Sloops from the Maas. The Ships of Corne Zegeis and Jochem Schaap have departed from Riga toward here and France; from Rochelle Corn. Sybrants and others toward Honfleur; from Nantes the St. Andries Willem Broeder toward the Baltic, and the Jager Pieter Leenderse toward here. The Ship of Klaas Janssen from Königsberg, bound for Copenhagen, has been stranded on Bornholm. At Danzig they again had such a strong Frost that the Ships and Quays might remain frozen in the Ice; also the Frost at Königsberg was so severe that the Ships of Klaas Jonker, Pieter Makkepers and Bude Binkels lay frozen there, but the Goods in them were brought over the Ice. Among other Ships, Cornel. Turk has been taken here before the City, and that of Captain Wind is not yet known to have suffered damage, although one feared that some accidents will have happened in Texel thereby. From the Vlie or Friesland no Vessels have yet arrived here.

NB. The French and English Letters have not yet arrived.

THE HAGUE the 31st of December. One believes that the Lords States of Holland and West-Friesland will convene tomorrow or 3 days hence, being the 1st of January. Yesterday morning the Marquis Beretti Lardi, Ambassador of the King of Spain, was with the Lord Slott, presently presiding at the Assembly of the Lords States General for the Province of Overijssel, about 11 o'clock, and this Minister came, was in Conference with the Lords Deputies of the States General: his Excellency is about to depart to Kamerik [Cambrai]: some letters suggest that the opening of the Peace Congress there will be postponed to the month of February, because all the nominated Ministers who must be present at the opening of said Congress cannot for the most part arrive there until the end of January. The Imperial Minister the Count of Windischgrätz, who as first Minister is to attend the aforementioned Congress on behalf of his Imperial Majesty, is meanwhile preparing his departure there; but one believes that said Minister will first go to Brussels, to be closer at hand when that Congress is opened. One expects that by Colonel Stanhope a Courier from Madrid will be sent to the British Court. According to letters of the 20th of this month from Geneva, they had news there that the pestilential Disease in various Places in Provence not only continued but was also beginning to spread further toward the Land of Italy, wherefore the King of Sardinia had sent some Troops to the River Var to occupy all the approaches to his States. Yesterday the Britannian, as well as the Prussian Ministers, were each separately in conversation with some Gentlemen of the Government. Daily Expresses go and come from London and Hanover. According to letters of the 15th of this month from Stockholm, the Imperial Minister the Count of Trystag had on the 16th had his private Audience with the King and Queen of Sweden. Prince George of Hessen Kassel, Brother of their Majesties, was expected there, and upon his arrival a great Hunt was to be held. One hears until now of no particular damage which would have been caused by the continuing heavy stormwinds; only one suggests that near a corner of Holland a Ship would be aground; also they say that near the Briel a Ship with Passengers would have capsized and some People drowned. P.S. One assures that the Spanish Ambassador the Marquis Beretti Lardi yesterday would have given notice to this State of his forthcoming departure to Kamerik, and that to this end at the end of this or beginning of the coming week he would undertake the journey there.

At Moses Haasvelberg, residing at Rotterdam in the Holland Coffeehouse on the Exchange, are sold and available the following Elixirs, namely the celebrated English Purging-Elixir of Doctor Daffy, Stoughton's very excellent, the very excellent Tinctura Antihecttica of Doctor Daggatt for Stone and Gravel, Mighton's very splendid Stomach-plaster, George Cuslijn's Stomach-Ranceel [?], Fabré's Elixir de Montpellier, the Royal Purging Epsom-Salt, Doctor Montoignon's Or Potable and Elixir for very many Ailments, Laudeleon's Essence of Opium, Edwarte van Ambert's and Bilalis Proprietatis, prepared through Sal Tartari; the necessary vessels for the use of the above-mentioned Bilalis are also available there.

At Leyden printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedstraat opposite the City Hall. The first of January 1721. With Privilege.

N° 1721.

ITALY

NAPLES the 14th of December. [Partial text regarding news from Mantua and various appointments and diplomatic matters]

GENOA the 18th of December. [Fragment about this City being embarked and reference to this day]

VENICE the 11th of December. [Fragment about new Provedireur and various appointments in Dalmatia and Istria]

GERMANY

VIENNA the 11th of November. [Fragment regarding Berlin and Imperial military appointments and administrative matters]

Course of Exchange

The Course of EXCHANGE is today on Madrid 90. Cadiz 113. Sevilla 114. Bilbao no fixed Price. Lisbon 41 and 3 quarters. Genoa 80 and 3 quarters. Paris 38 and 1 half to 78. Livorno 66 and 1 half. London 32 Sh. to 32s. Louzain 38 and 1 eighth St. Frankfurt current 29 and 1 half Rixdollars. Danzig 291 Polish gr. Breslau 34 and 1 quarter St. Hamburg 32 and 3 quarters St. Antwerp 2 and 1 half per C. Ghent 1 and 3 quarters. Brussels 3. Zeeland 3 quarters. Rotterdam 5 eighths. Amst. Ago is 102 to 102 3/4. W. C. 100 to 104. both current. The English Securities have no fixed Prices.

Notices & Advertisements

Martin Copius Director of the National Lotteries, makes known on express order to those Interested in the National Lottery taking place, that the exchange of Tickets from the 4th to the 5th Class can be done from now on at the respective Offices, as also on Mondays at Hendrik Blank, at Dordrecht at Johannes van Braam, at Rotterdam at Abraham Hoorndaal, and at Leyden at Hendrik van Damme; that this exchange will be continued until Saturday the first of February 1721 and no longer, and that the Drawing of the 4th Class will again unfailingly be begun on the 10th of that same month February. Note: the drawn Prizes from the 4th Class can be received everywhere.

Directors and Principal Participants of the Commerce, Navigation and Insurance Company at Edam, having taken serious consideration on the point of what would bring the most advantage to those Interested, notify that the third installment is postponed until the 25th of June of the coming Year 1721, when from that day until the 4th of July following, one per Cent must be paid, on pain as set down in the Project, with a pause for that mentioned Year; further that with special consent of the Honorable Lords Burgomasters it is established never to demand more than 10 per C. of what was subscribed, that is besides the 3 per Cent already furnished, still 7 per Cent: moreover that for the payment of interest at 4 per Cent per year will be paid all Wednesdays and Thursdays afternoon after the first of March next.

Available for sale a Dwelling, standing and located in the Barony of Wassenaar, just before the Deyl, with a Farm House, Barn and Stables, with about 22 morgen of Land, for Hay, Pasture, as well as Arable Land, presently being used by Jan Dirkse Mosser; to inquire at the Widow Babut, at the house of Mons. van Leeuwen, Messenger of the Generality in the Bockhorstraat in The Hague.

On Monday the 6th of January 1721, in the morning at 10 o'clock, will be sold at Leyden at the Heerst Logement at the Burg, an extremely well-located Dwelling and Lands, lying in the Hondsdijk, with 2 Orchards there at and next to it, as also the Uyterdijk before said Dwelling and the low Ryndijk, being a well-planted and planted Pleasure Garden, with a delightful Summerhouse therein, of old named Tuyk-zigt, overlooking the Rhine and Ryndijk; being these Lands and Orchards for a great part planted with beautiful and solid Stone, Oak and Pear trees; in total 17 morgen, 314 rods; item also, morgen, 301 rods, located by and around the aforementioned Dwelling, thus in total 22 morgen 15 rods; all under the free Lordship of Koudekerk.

At Amsterdam at the Janssoons van Waesberge, Philosophical Booksellers on the Water, is printed and published the second Part of the wonderful Life-adventures of Robinson Crusoe, containing an account of what happened on his Island, his second Journey there, together with his return Journey through Persia, China, Tartary and Muscovy, full of unheard and strange encounters, both at Sea and on Land, faithfully related, with figures, in octavo.

At Leyden at Coenraad Wishoff Bookseller, is printed and published, as also in other Cities at most Booksellers, 8 Branchia Observationii ad Jus Romanum, V. III. Leibnitius contra V. G. Jurisprudentia Justiniana Maccum Vindicatus, Or Disputationes duæ ad Julium Pontanum, In quibus militia, ad Jus Civile, Naturæ, Gentium, Antiquitates, & Studia Humaniora pertinentia, illustrantur, Accedit V. M. Berengur Vertit Disputatio ad I. 4º. V. de loco. T

At Utrecht at Jacob van Poolsum, is printed and published, also in most Cities: Salomonis van Til Opus Analyticum, in quo S. Scripturæ elegantia, cum Notis in Sacram Scripturam, ad Joh. Henrici Heidegger Enchiridion Biblicum, 2 vol. in 4. idem: Jacobi Elsneri Observationes Sacræ, in Novi Foederis Libros, quibus Plura illorum loca ex Auctoribus Judaeis et Auctoribus Profanis Græcis & Antiquitate exponuntur & Illustrantur, in 8. Idem D. Martin's Treatise on Natural Religion.

At LEYDEN at the JANSSOONS VAN DER AA, Booksellers, is printed and issued and published an EVERLASTING LOVERS ALMANAC, Compiled following the general Almanac style; showing the Vicissitudes of Love affairs that occur in all Countries, by HILOMUSUS PHILOKALUS, in Octavo, with figures; also available in other Cities at the Booksellers.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 1st of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 2.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 3rd of January, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, 10 December. With the service that begins nine days before the feast of St. Januarius, there has been found the beginning of the custom and the Lord Don Joseph Odoardij, one of the Near ones of the Vicariate, through whom the Emperor chose [him] as President of the Royal Chamber named in the City. On the previous Wednesday the Marquis di Aitzia arrived here with his Niece, and through the gate of Capua entered, much [of] his Chaise, in which case before the Wheels went over his leg, and the Marquise his Wife sent away some days after his departure. The Marquis Saiazzo has been named by the Nuncio in the Compartment of Syracuse.

GENOA, 13 December. That Belaboured [arrangement] of the Population through this City is sharply forbidden; and they have pointed out to the places where they shall be able to find their sustenance on common costs.

VENICE, 21 December. The Lord Marcus Antonitus Diedo new Provveditor-general of Dalmatia, has, after he had taken his leave of the Government, embarked on the Galley of the Lord Jacobus Bragadin, with whom he departed the previous Wednesday toward Dalmatia. Thursday evening the Lord Coronata Londis was named Provveditore-general of the Sea. The Weather, which has been very dry until now, has changed to Rains. The trade in Grains in Terra Ferma [?] causes their Prices here to fluctuate greatly.

Germany & Neighbouring Kingdoms, Etc

VIENNA, 21 December. The Emperor has appointed the Marquis of Weterioo as his secret Councillor and Ambassador, and thereupon the Lord of Amsterdam to the States General of the United [?] of the Emperor's party [has been] called, and on their own costs a Regiment [has been] sworn. These days the Cardinal Confragos has complained of his [?]; on the 20th the Emperor had [a meeting], wherein he retained 2 Horses, to use during his stay there, to serve him. The previous Wednesday the Polish Count Dallavile arriving here from Saxony has had [an audience] by innate [?] the Imperial Majesty, and the following day by the Empress Widow Amalia, [and received] congratulations, wherein by the birth of the young Heir-Prince of Saxony [he] made [some remark]; being introduced by the Lord of Zech and the Legation-Secretary Anacharis, it was half remarked that the recently recognized [?] of the Forschriften concerning the Commission-Decree, by his K.M. was accepted; but it appears that the continuation of the mentioned [matter] was not as great as one had hoped in the beginning. The Emperor-General Kyga Committeemen of the Great Saxon have [brought] that to l'burt toward Piesburg in [?], and the Diet [continues]...

REGENSBURG, 16 December. One is informed from God's hand that the present Evangelical and Reformed Community through the Protestant to these high Principals have given notice of the misfortune, which they [encountered] the previous Friday with the Elector [?] Collegium over the imperial Commission-Decree; secondly [concerning] the Benefit of the law [which] could be of bad consequence, [so] that the Protestant Corps concludes therefrom, that at Vienna no Rescript has been made on their Remonstrances against the mentioned Decree; concerning the Privileges and Rights [which] are [?] the Protestant Princes; in the aforesaid separate Relation is very ample and clear and [of] merit [to be] made public. One still thought that the Elector-Fürstdom [?] Directorium would have dared renew the matter and bring it to the Diet; since the Protestants had previously declared, that they, should this happen, would not compromise, [noting] that the Roman Catholic Princes and Princes are not concerned in Detail to treat over the Grievances which the Protestants have against [them], but that this alone [belongs] to his K.M.; all [matters of] the K.K. competence; to whom they shall await resolution thereon; and so that this matter gives much to do among the Leaders of the Diet; on the other hand the Roman [Princes] also show themselves very covetous, because the Electoral Collegium has let them gather without appearing thereby, wherefore they in a separate confidential [meeting] have resolved themselves on this account to lodge a complaint with the Emperor.

France

PARIS, 27 December. Wednesday, Christmas Eve, the King attended the Chapel of the Tuileries at the first Vespers, which was served by the Bishop of Meaux, the following, being Christmas Day, [he made] Confession with his Majesty to the Abbot of Fleury, his Confessor, where the King heard the great Mass, which was celebrated by the aforesaid Bishop pontifically and in Music; that afternoon his Majesty heard the Sermon by Father Quinquet Theatine and attended subsequently the Vespers. The Cardinal of Noailles served the midnight Mass in the Church of Notre-Dame pontifically, and the Lady Poulumat Madame [?] was represented. Between the People of St. Nicolas des Champs and the Monks from the Priory of St. Martin, there was a sharp dispute over Rights, on the occasion of the midnight Mass. After on the night between the 21st and 22nd the Lords Bourgeois Treasurer of the Bank, du Renei, Feuclau, Fromaget and others had been brought to the Bastille, all was hastily sealed at Huyzard, and afterwards several Chests with Papers were examined in their presence; the first mentioned is accused of having sent out of the Country several chests and vats with Gold Specie, belonging to the Bank, but he maintains to be able to prove that this was not done without [proper] order; the second is said to have signed more Bank notes than he was authorized to do, and the others are laid various charges [?]: the Beneficiaries and Promoters [of] 200 of the Bank as well as the Company of the Indies, who found themselves in the elevation of the System [and] the Company [?] have financed with designs of private interest, find themselves in this revolution of affairs particularly frustrated, and a certain Abbot who was one of the foremost, has now removed himself to a Private Collegium, whilst the others still busy themselves to find places of refuge. One flatters oneself [that] at the beginning of January coming one shall see emerge a sufficient Arrangement of affairs, but People are uneasy [and do not doubt] that the revolution requires a fair amount of time; now it is said that one had wished to assign the Office of Comptroller-general in this change again to the Lord des Mareis, but that Lord being unwilling to accept it, that has fallen to the Lord le Felletier de la Houflaye. Since some days nothing else is spoken of here but the departure of the Lord Law; and the latest news that one has here is that he finds himself at [?] to stay. It is claimed that at his departure from here, the Post-holders were forbidden on severe penalties, [to] anyone, within the time of seven hours any Horses [to be] rented: over the further Particulars of the affairs of mentioned here little is spoken; only [it] is reported that he beside the Money made over in other Lands, 40 Millions in Jewels has taken with [him]: the remarkable Buildings which he has left behind, and where he employed the best Stoneworks and most capable Craftsmen and also well paid them, are not yet completed, but the Lord Peteguctin who has painted the Galleries, finds himself (because he is not yet [permanently] assigned) troubled over his departure; meanwhile one does not know to what the great Palace with its dependencies shall be employed: some wish that the King shall take it to himself, or give it to the Duke of Chartres by its Establishment. Wednesday the new general rehearsal of the [?] of the Queen was held; but one did not find the performance of the Farce of Cardens as amusing as one had imagined. One does not doubt that the Bank reckonings shall be completely renewed shortly; they now [stand at] 28 p.ct. in Money, the Shares at [?] and 1 1/2 in Money and the Bank notes in Shares drive their Trade around the Bank and the State [Chamber] in the sight of the Archers. The reports, as if the bad Sickness increased in Provence, are without foundation and are declared false, for the late houses [show] clearly that the evil notably decreased; through the late falls the City of Aix was much affected, and the communication with Marseilles began to [be interrupted] well, but gradually [improved]. One sees an Edict of the King's Council of State in which [it] of 17 December determined moderation of the incoming Revenues on the [Staple], [goods] from England.

LONDON, 17 December. The Parliament under the Bishop of Lincoln opened [Editor's note: This refers to the opening of Parliament in December 1720]; on this occasion Members of Parliament, including Hickman and others, made their addresses. Lord William Porter presented [?], and the Comptroller George [?] brought important matters before the House regarding Royal finances; when these were heard with approval, orders were made that the General Fund for the year 1719, for the year 1720, and a levy concerning [?] and Houses in the year 1710 should be established; a Fund of 13 [?] was set up to be collected; the ordinary Navy was appointed, as well as the maintenance of the Guards, [?] and Marines for that year; a statement was made regarding the ordinary accounts for the service of [?] for the following year; an estimate of the debts of the Shipbuilders for the year 1720, and estimates on various matters; and three [?] years, an accounting of monies, and concerning the money in the last session until [?], it was found: likewise an accounting of the money for that year, it was found: likewise an accounting of the money for the House, it was resolved to present an address to the King to beseech His Majesty to give order that the said accounting be examined for the House in just proportion, and moreover that the said Address be presented by such Members of this House and the payments, alongside [?], and an accounting of the Community [?] and an accounting of all money received or paid until Michaelmas onwards. It was also shown how much remained of the Aggregate Fund, and how much of the South Sea Company, along with the general Fund; the House resolved to present an address to the King, as His Majesty had given order to fulfill what was requested therein; and when this was reported by Mr. [Tebby?] on behalf of the House, the Lord [?] of the half-pay standing officers, and an estimate of the Guards and [?] of the Land-Militia, as well as for the Colonies, such as Minorca and Gibraltar, the House decided to debate on the coming Saturday the Subsidy which they shall grant to the King; but after the House had adjourned to the following day. There were discussions of an estimate for the present state of the Nation and public Credit, and they deferred further considerations until the coming Tuesday. That same day the Directors of the South Sea Company laid before the House various accounts and records, and requested of the House that they, with all possible speed, work upon a complete accounting of all their affairs, pending the House's address, and when 100 Guilders shall be satisfied, they will deliver the same to the House: the House in Grand Committee turned to examining a memorandum of the said Directors, wherein by the gentlemen Steele, [Slopny?], [Mulner?], [Wilfred?], [Lawlone?] and Walpole, some brief arguments for and against were raised, but because of what was proposed no decision was taken, and [the matter] was made [?].

IN BRUSSELS, 31 December. It is now believed that complete reports have been received of the route taken by Lord [?], and in that case the fortifications on the coast of [Vianen?] would proceed. It is given out that he here [?] a species [?], by way of justification, and against which no [?] has fallen and particularly to the advantage of the affairs of the King. There is a [?] on [?] to be cleared, but there [?] will be another route. The States wish to have [?] to proceed with the Works at [?], which are very [?], [?] to do in Ghent, particularly reported here.

At AMSTERDAM, 1 January. At Cape Saint-Domingue the ship Sierra, Captain Maiya, has not arrived, and at Martinique the Fleet, Captain Jacques Felipe from Bordeaux. At Bilbao the Dirk Rysberg was still awaiting fair winds; and all the ships that had lain at Porto Galéro had not yet departed thence. Among other ships which expected to pass through the Bay of Bayonne, that of Commodore [?] alone managed it; but Skipper Jan [Kockemoer?], having [?] in the vicinity of the [?], was again [?], in order to have 200 men sail. The ship the Jager, Gerrit de Boer, departed from Saint-Martin towards Honfleur; and from Nantes the [?] Anna Constantia, Marten Pieterse, the Windwacht Gertrit Adriaen and [?] others sailed towards Bordeaux; the [?] Andries [?] from Königsberg and Jan Jochemd [?] from Lübeck; at Bordeaux the Young Willem Jan [?], the Coat of Arms of Schiedam, [?] Crux, and various other ships, which, though they touched the stock exchange [?], yet sustained no damage; but there was a rumor that three or four of the outgoing ships would have been lost. In the East the ships of [Uldewald?] to Copenhagen remained, and Klaas Cornelis Kriesman.

THE HAGUE, 3 January. The Lords States of Holland and West Friesland will convene next Wednesday, the 4th of this month, to which letters of convocation have been sent to the respective cities of this Province.

FRANKFURT, 29 December. Some days ago an Express messenger passed through here from England to Germany, without anything of his dispatches being able to be discovered. It is said that the Elector of Trier intends to remain in Lower Sicily during the winter.

MAINZ, 29 December. Whereas it has been perceived that His Electoral Grace is being slandered by ignorant persons, inasmuch as it is said that [?] large [?] with the Emperor [?], in order to certain great [?] with the Emperor [?], [?] to remedy the religious grievances [?] with the Emperor [?], [the Elector has made it known] that His Electoral Grace moreover would have insisted urgently that in the matter of the [?] such harsh [?] should not be undertaken: the Elector has caused it to be published that the aforementioned [?] malicious and invented matter [?], since His Electoral Grace is in the absolute intention to comply in all respects with the recently received Imperial [?], and likewise with the abolition of the religious changes which were undertaken after the Peace of Baden.

[LOTTERY NOTICES AND FINANCIAL ANNOUNCEMENTS]

Course of Exchange

**ROME, 14 December.** The Pope is completely recovered from his indisposition in health, so that His Holiness on the previous Sunday, which as Friday was sanctified to the Pontificate, showed himself in the Chapel; the Cardinals admired [him] at the Hand-kiss, and received the Compliments of like satisfaction over his recovery in the same Year of his Jubilee. Subsequently the Holy Father heard Mass, which was celebrated by Cardinal Cortini: now the Pope betook himself from the Chapel Sixtine to his Sedan-chairs, and Cardinal Aquaviva saw him the agreeable news which His Eminence has received by Express from Spain, containing a Report of the Defeat of the Moors in Africa, described the Believing [forces] glorified. The Standard of the Marquis de Leede [was captured], together with all his Cannon, Baggage &c., and that on the Moors' side 5000 were left on the Field: in this news the arrival of his Lordship is praised, because the mentioned Cardinal assured His Holiness with that same Express report received that the Holy Aldobrandini with all signs of approval removed himself from the Expedition in the observed manner and the Tribunals of the Nunciature opened anew in Spain, in the course of which matters were already expedited, whereby one presented a complete Execution of the Accords between this Court and Spain; this last news has been confirmed thrown, by an Express which the Harbour of Partina sent to the Pope, with addition that the King of Spain and Don Docecit has caused to be published, whereby all that by the Spanish Court, in prejudice of Rome, until the day of publication, has been done, [is] null and void declared. The Lord Tits has [been ordered] from the Compartment of Civita Vecchia to that of Viterbo; the Lord Antoniusvit [from] that of Acoli to that of Ancona; the Lord Imperiale from Civita di Castello to the Lord Bonadianni of Terni to Civita di Castello; and that the Lord Aquaviva shall assume the Ecclesiastical Garb in Viterbo; Lucide to Boaigne [appointed]; and one is assured that the Lord Crisp is called to the Archbishopric of Ravenna. Meanwhile there was a gathering of Bishops at [?] whereby one trusts that there shall be held a Constitution of the aforesaid Bishoprics, and especially the Church of Ravenna for the Lord Crisp pleased [to resign], the Cap, the great Three the Ambassador of Portugal, a Moor and a Servant of that Ambassador, for the contempt which they have shown to the Court servants, in the previous case, on condition that [they] be named. It is spread here that one with the Courier, who brought the Letters of Parma to the Pope, would also have obtained news that a Marriage between the Prince All Honory would [be] Parma with his Princess Sobiesski, belonged to that Court; and that one spoke of a Marriage between the Prince of Alturien and the Archduchess Daughter of the deceased Emperor, of which latter reasons, however, we lack Confirmation. On the Feast of St. Lucie, the Absolution was celebrated in the Church of St. John Lateran, which by the Pope was given to Henry IV, King of France, where the Cap of St. Ottoboni Pienleglus [?] had died on Wednesday; wherein his 150, 200 Cardinals, prelates as Nobles received at table, where the Cardinals of Althan and Aquaviva have also been present. The EXCHANGE RATES are today: Master [?] at England 114. Bilbao 87½. Lisbon 41¾. Venice 77½ to 78. Livorno 86½. Genoa 49¼. London 32 Shillings 6 [pence]. Leipzig 38⅛. Saint Frankfurt 40½ to [Reichsthaler?]. Danzig 290 Roules. Berlin 3¾ [Shillings?]. Hamburg 12 11/16 [Shillings?]. Antwerp 2½ per Cent. Ghent 2¼. Brussels 2½. Zeeland ¾. Rotterdam ½. Bank Shares 3⅛. East India Company Shares 940 [?]. West India Company 703 to 104, both in cash.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 3rd of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 3.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 6th of January, 1721

TURKEY.

CONSTANTINOPLE, 2nd November. It is reported that the Treaty of Titeres, which was concluded between the Czar of Great Russia and the Ottoman Porte twenty-five years ago, has now been incorporated into a new treaty and worked out, and that for this purpose an Express messenger has been sent to the Czar's Majesties to ratify the aforesaid new state treaty. The King and Queen of Sweden have notified the Grand Vizier [Editor's note: referring to the former Grand Vizier] by letter of the elevation of His Second Majesty to the Crown, upon which letters of congratulation were opened by the Grand Vizier and former Vizier and sent to Their Second Majesties.

SPAIN.

CADIZ, 11th December. In this Bay there have arrived 2 English warships of Tartanes: the Ship "King George," Captain Lazarus Oxman from Amsterdam; the "Robin and Jan," John Sween from Herland; the "Apollon," Captain Marrocquin from Gibraltar; and the "St. Nicholas Galy," Captain Mazion from Lisbon; having departed from here the Captains Michiel Magnidge and Raimond King toward the Levant, Henry Frislere and [?] toward St. Lucia, Thomas Godâti, Ejiewan Kilvan and Cartos Pett toward London, Filipe Hore toward Barcelona, and the "Marechal d'Hilices," Captain Toure, toward Genoa; so that in this Bay there have also arrived two English ships against the Spanish War. 22 French [ships], Dutch ones, and among them Piet. Molenaer sailed toward Amsterdam, together with five Danish ships. The ship "St. Antonio," Captain Bog from Amsterdam, being cleared, has obtained permission to enter here, provided that its cargo is subject to the toll. Yesterday it was made public that all those who have licenses to send to the Galleys must appear during these days; so that it is expected that the said Galleys will yet depart before the end of February. From the West Indies there is no peace; the watch on Wallaga is believed to be busy with the new shipping of troops; for which reason it is assured that our army, outside the garrison of Ceuta, is about 9,000 men strong, and it is said that there are also troops from the King of Morocco in the Moorish army.

MADRID, 17th December. Thursday and Friday, the 12th and 13th, the King, the Queen, and Prince of Albuquerque amused themselves at Pardo with the Infante, and on Saturday with the Wolf-hunt; that evening Their Majesties returned to this city. On Sunday they attended, together with the Prince, the great Mass and the sermon by the Capuchin Father Rafael de Loyola; that same evening Don Joseph de Cordua y Alagon, Colonel of the Regiment of Majorca, arrived with the following news: that a second widow [?] of our army under the command of the Marquis de Leede has defeated the Moors on the 9th of this month; for which reason the following relation has been made public by order of the Court Press.

The Moorish camp being considerably strengthened by many regiments of foot and by the cavalry of the life-guard of the Queen of Morocco, they prepared themselves strongly enough to attack our army in the same retrenchment, for which reason our army, having reconnoitered the nearby heights several times, they broke up their camp in the early morning. From the side of the Fort of Parais, they broke camp and over the hills, which stretched from their camp to ours, marched toward our army; their behaves stretched out into the nearby heights on our left wing, and their cavalry positioned themselves on the heights that cover the road to Tangier, making a front for the center of our encampment; but outside the range of the cannon; the whole day they skirmished in some skirmishes with our advanced parties, but toward 4 o'clock in the afternoon they made as if they wanted to withdraw to their camp, but having approached the passes on the hills to climb them, and made a counter-march to occupy a broad and very strong line that extended from the sea to the heights of the road to Tangier, by which their camp was completely covered, so that one could not see them; upon this the Marquis de Leede sent a detachment of his cavalry toward the said road to Tangier, and after this detachment of the Moorish camp had reconnoitered in this depth, it began at twelve o'clock to retire, but the Moors attacked the said detachment in the depth not directly with foot and horse soldiers, so that the engagement was very sharp, because this detachment was supported by a continual fire of our advanced grenadiers, and one had the impression that a general action would arise from this, but the next morning it ended thus; the Moorish foot withdrew to the heights, and their foot-people maneuvered themselves during the entire night in the depth; but our foot-people remained in their shelters, and the cavalry returned to their camp-place. The next morning that day the Moors drew out in columns from the said depth, to which 3 foot and one horse [?]; the horse extended themselves on their left wing of the road to Tangier through a defile to the sea, and marched toward the side of our soldiers and their foot-people, meanwhile having been drawn out of the depth, approached to the road that was nearest to our camp; but when the Marquis de Leede had all the cavalry drawn out, and positioned them as best as possible from the right wing to the front of our center; to protect our right side [?], the Moorish cavalry advanced so that the terrain not being suitable for the cavalry to attack, and that of the Moors being stronger, the said retreat of the Moorish cavalry before the fire of our entire battalion was held, as happened, for a portion of our cavalry, alone under the command of Don Vincentius Fum-Saena, Colonel of the Regiment of Altunen, having remained, they were pressed with such violence by the Moors, that the infantry could not help them; they remained by this through the incessant fire that the Spanish guard made, were obliged; in which action Don Vincentio Fum-Daena was wounded, and several of our grenadiers and horses were slightly wounded; at the same time the Moors attacked one of our guards, in a house placed about the half-moon to defend our camp, which guard, after more agitation of some dead, the Moors having overcome this house, planted a flag there, and advanced to the ditch of the half-moon, in which 13 companies of our grenadiers were posted, who made such a strong fire that the Moors were so well paid for their boldness that they withdrew shortly after; meanwhile the Moors sent out their infantry along the entire low ground that is before our ditch, and continued to make a vigorous fire on our half-moon and the center of the line: during that time our cavalry had positioned itself behind the line, and the Moorish cavalry advanced to attack our right wing, which took place with such violence that they held themselves completely exposed to the fire of the Spanish and [?] guards, which guards, making up the right wing, and covering the second [?], the left side extended to a height, where they were fought by 2 battalions of the Regiment of Leon; during which the fire was continued both by the cavalry and the foot soldiers of the Moors, and since they were in a low place, our cannon was rather fruitless; because it could only sweep a part of their center, by which they nevertheless suffered great damage: the fire continued on both sides without the least interruption from 8 o'clock in the morning until about half 12 o'clock at midday, when the Moors withdrew; our fire was so great that they continued thirty shots, which each man had received, it has been renewed five times in such equal distribution; and although the grenadiers and other troops, who stood mostly exposed, were changed from time to time, and principally because their weapons became unusable through the incessant firing, they were nevertheless sometimes forced to have different positions violently vacated, as each one wanted to maintain himself in his post to the last drop of blood; and it was one of the most eminent spectacles to behold with what bravery and unparalleled eagerness the officers and soldiers fought; toward the aforesaid time about midday the Moors pulled their planted standards and flags out of the ground, and withdrew in good order to their previous camp with many losses, it is said, of about 5 to 600 men, among which were many generals and prominent officers, [several of whom had withdrawn from the field according to their custom; during which one saw the roads and passages full of Moors carrying away their dead; and as proof of the great loss, one saw the place where the fiercest fire raged, full of blood streams; on our side there have been few dead or wounded, among which, with respect to the generals and prominent officers, no one was lost, except Don Francisco van Lueis, Marshal de Camp, who commanded at the center, and Don Vincent Enno Buenas [?]; while almost all the other prominent officers have continued to receive [?], and among them the Marquis de Leede, a wound in the right arm. The Moors marched to their camp until nightfall, and then in a counter-march the heights wanted to occupy, so our troops were forced to remain under arms; which is why they are eagerly awaiting the opportunity to come back into luck to be able to engage the enemy hand to hand. One must note this day as one of the most glorious, on which the weapons of His Majesty have obtained a glorious victory, as much with respect to the great loss of the enemy, as to having overcome a army that was as much more numerous as ours [?], and to have caused great streams of the enemy's blood to flow over the earth, and this in spite of the fierce and furious attacks of the enemy, against which we, although they had stood for about 3 hours to bleed before the fire of our foot soldiers, by the valor, dignity, and unshakeable steadfastness of the weapons of His Majesty, have obtained a glorious victory. Don Joseph de Cordua y Alagon, Colonel of the Regiment of Majorca, was sent by the Marquis de Leede with this important dispatch, and having arrived at court on the 1st [?] of this month, he made a report to the King of all the above-recounted, while one awaits the particular circumstances of it; yesterday it was sung about at dinner in the Royal Chapel.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 30th December. Yesterday a great council meeting was held at the Bank, in which the Duke Regent, the Princes of the Blood, and other great personages of the kingdom attended, where they discussed matters of which one will be informed after several days. It is said that a prominent member of State has requested the Sieur de Matets in Peiroen to take a seat in the new Council of Finance, so that through his sound knowledge and ripe experience in these affairs, the workings thereof might be improved [?]; he has accepted it; it is confirmed that a similar invitation has also been made to Mr. Amelot, who has also accepted it with pleasure. Two Articles of the King's Council of State [were issued]; the first in date the 24th December, by which until March next is postponed the transfer of one third of the King's the duties [?] of the [?] in this city and suburbs incoming, as well as the other cities where the said rights are also subject. The other in date the 18th December, supplement the [?] in [...]

LONDON, 11 December. On Saturday the Controller made known to the House that their Address had been presented to the King on Thursday, and that His Majesty had agreed to comply with the Request therein contained, following a Petition from the Mayor and Bailiffs &c. of the City and County of Poole, in which complaint was made concerning the decline of Manufactures, and the great import of Cottons and Chintzes from the East Indies, and the export of Wool from the Kingdom &c., which Request was laid on Tuesday; whereupon the House ordered to thank Doctor Willows for the Sermon which he had delivered the preceding day in St. Margaret's Church at Westminster before the House; subsequently Mr. Lowads, pursuant to the Address presented to the House, laid before the House an Account, showing how the Money granted for the service of the Year 1720 had been applied; then the House ordered a Bill to be brought in for the better regulation of the Quartermasters through their Ranks and Ships, and some other Places, after which Mr. Treby presented to the House an estimate of the charges for the Hospital of Chelsea for the Year 1721. Subsequently the House ordered that the Directors of the South Sea Company should forthwith deliver the Lists of the Second, Third and Fourth Subscription in Money, and of the Sums paid thereupon, together with a particular Account of the Government Securities, purchased for account of the Company for £231,041, from the day of purchase, and by whom that was effected: an Account of £3,600 added by the Company sold for a sum of £1,259,325; as likewise an Account of the application of the Sums received by the Directors for the Dividend of Christmas last; after which the House, regarding the Subscription in gold, resolved that application should be made to the King for a grant of 10,800 Men for the service at Sea, at £4 per Man, one thousand thereof by the first of the Month, amounting to £19,049 for the ordinary Pay, including also the Sea Officers' allowances, and the sum of £52,200 for the Fleet's equipment; and for Guards and Garrisons of Great Britain 11,254 Men, including 1,849 Invalides and Officers, 200 in and out of Commission; for which there shall be granted the sum of £267,970 for roughly 1,494 Men; likewise the sum of £11,749 to maintain the Garrisons of the Colonies in America, Minorca and Gibraltar, as well as the Garrisons of Annapolis Royal and Placentia, and for the half Pay of certain half-pay Officers on Land and of the Regiments of Marines the sum of £9,340; the House deferred the report of these Resolutions to Monday, and on Wednesday to proceed to the consideration of the Subsidy. Yesterday Mr. Farrer reported the Resolutions taken in Grand Committee as before. The Subsidy being granted, upon review that portion of the King's Speech relating to Public Credit was examined, and it appeared that nothing was more fitting for Investigation; whereupon it was proposed to examine further the Subscriptions of the Annuities, where the Subscription under the Act had been effected; and whether Disputes had not arisen, and whether the Gentlemen Walpole, Craggs, the Solicitor General and others were in favor, and Bromley, [?] others against; the principal of the ensuing Debates (too great and numerous to find place here) being whether the Subscriptions had been closed with all necessary formalities, bringing forward thereunto Contract or Agreement, which was found in the Books wherein the Subscription was mentioned, over the signatures of 2 Persons therein named; authorized to make their payments; that further the Subscription under the said Conditions was proper, which was more than sufficient to justify the authority of the Company, and to show that they had acted fully, according to the Act of Parliament. The opposing Party contended that the Company had not executed the Act of Parliament, saying that the Act required that the Subscriptions should not only be stated favorably but that one should also have read the original Contracts; the Advocates for the Company being willing to give them freedom in this matter; being not satisfied therewith, since their Annuities had not been discharged in all respects, the Subscriptions should not have been stated, all according to the Act of Parliament: finally it was resolved by the House with a majority of 267 against [?] Votes that all the Subscriptions of the public Debt, Subscriptions in Money, and other Contracts made by the South Sea Company, according to the Act of the last Session of Parliament, shall remain in that state between the East India Company, or unless the general [?] make some change for the relief of the Proprietors; and the report thereof was laid before the House; as also the continuation of the consideration of the present state of public Credit of the Kingdom: after which the House adjourned until tomorrow morning. Today Mr. Farrer reported the regular ordering of the Grand Committee taken with regard to public Credit, which after some Debates was approved with a majority of 232 against 88 Votes; subsequently were read Petitions and Requests from Owners of Frigates, and also [?] Annuitants in the South Sea Company, with request to be heard in Person or by Advocates concerning the unjust Practices of certain Directors, upon which all Requests were laid on the Table. Today it was resolved to sit in Committee on Thursday to consider the present state of the Nation, with regard to public Credit, and with the power of the Committee to summon the Exchequer. The Book of Mr. Humphrey Mackworth, concerning the payment and discharge of the public Debt, was yesterday brought before the House. After the Mr. Middleton had paid the money at Whitehall for [?], he has closed his accounts: On Sunday the Mr. [?], Envoy of the King of Poland, addressed His Majesty concerning the matter of the young Crown Prince of Saxony; requesting His Majesty's patronage, which was accepted by the King. This week the General Nicholson departed for his Government of Carolina, with about three ships; also the Bishop [?], Lt. Ambassador of the King of Sweden; and then the Count Cadogan received a fine stud of Horses, given him by the Emperor. Four Highwaymen, who were captured yesterday, have been lodged in the Prison of Newgate.

---

AMSTERDAM, 4 January. Since it had rained very heavily at Bordeaux, measures were taken there for the detention of more Ships. Captain Toke Dirks has arrived here from Nantes; in a storm Pieter Snoeck from Lübeck to Hamburg Jan de Haan and Jan [?] Mooy from here to South [?] Maarten Bruyn, Reyert Tasman, and Cock, Laurens Gerdens, Jan Stam, Hendrik, Jacobus Negtegaal, and [?], Jochiem Vos, Michiel Walmoet, Wiggert Breveld, and by storm Lieye Pieters, Andreas Corn, Prater, and Pieter Lo[?] from Riga, along with some others. The ship of Pieter Roos, which having driven off course, ran aground on [?] strand near Harrenbosch. Near Flensburg came Dirk Lietke from Riga. [?] was wrecked Jarig Gerbrands, from Danzig to [?]; there is still a ship near Brest, with Brandy, [?] and the ship of Hendr. Schelhorn, from Lübeck to Lille, would have remained in the Kattegat. The ship of Mau[?] coming from Bilbao, was wrecked, but yesterday still lay in good condition in Teufel. Klaas Jantje Scheelman, who sailed from there hither, was driven by storm to St. Martin.

---

The Spanish Ambassador is at Berg te landt, who this week departs for Cambrai, being occupied with taking leave, and it is said concerning the affairs of the Crown of Spain in general, as also [?]. The Fiscal Nenny has returned to Brussels, whither it is said the Resident [?] is to follow this week. Yesterday morning Prince Kurakin, Ambassador of Russia, was in Society with several Gentlemen of the Government. [?] is arrived from the Province of Groningen. [?] of the Gentlemen States General appeared, and [?] of Gerdt van Sevenhuisen, Chancellor in the Lord's Lodgement [?], for several years a [?] the High Gate of the City of Leiden, [?] [?], Barn and Water Mill, further a separate House [?] in all about 283 Roods; someone should be further informed [?] Leiden to Pieter Ryneman on the Nut Geruldt Wolf. [?] and to sell a very pleasant Dwelling or Court House [?], and furnished with two delightful Views, [?]

---

LEIDEN AND OTHER PLACES. Anno 1721.

Upon consideration, along with a particular Account of the 3 years on St. Adrian, purchased for account of the Company for £23,1041, and by whom that was effected.

At Leiden: printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedstraat, over the Town Hall. 6 January 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Course of EXCHANGE is on Amsterdam 33s. 10d.; on sight 32s. 10d. Rotterdam 34s. 2d. Antwerp 34s. 6d. to 8d. Hamburg 34s. Paris and Bordeaux No quotations given; Madrid 46 and 3/4. Cadiz 47. Bilbao 49 and 1/2. Livorno 49 and 1 1/2 to 50. Genoa 41 and 1 1/2. [?] 3 and 2 and 1/2. Oporto 4 and 2. Dublin 12. The Stocks of the Bank of 1672, of the East India Company 166, of the South Sea [?] 13 1/4 to 1/2, the first Subscription [?], 3rd ditto, 3rd Subscription, ditto for Money, 3rd Subscription, [?], of the Annuities, Long Annuities, Bank Annuities, and ditto 4 per cent general Prices, the Millions Bank 150, African Company 42, [?] Building 17. The Course of EXCHANGE is today on Madrid 88 and 1/2, Cadiz 119 and 1/2 [?] 113. Bilbao 87 and 1/2 42. Lisbon 42 and 41 and 1. Venice 78 and 1/2 to 79. Livorno 85 and 1/2. Genoa 90. [?] 32 schillings 4 [?] 5. Geneva 92 and 1/2. Leipzig current 38 and 1 [?]. Like St. Antwerp 2 and 5 [?] St. Cologne 1 quarter. [?] 3. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 3 [?]. The Agio of the Bank 1 and 1 quarter.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 6th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 4.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 8th of January, 1721
Spain

SARAGOSSA, 11 December. The foot regiments of Liguria and Burgundy marched the day after Maillega.

GIRONA, 20 December. Several days ago, 300 horses arrived here for the further recruitment of the new cavalry.

Germany & Neighbouring Kingdoms, &C

REGENSBURG, 30 December. For some days now, the envoy Allisilor from the Chamber at Wetzlar has renewed his requests, in order that the Emperor's Decree regarding the directorate of the Reich's administration, laid down for the commissioning of the judges of the imperial diet, might be executed promptly. However, the ministers of the Protestant body have made it known to him that the matter has been rejected by the electoral college and they maintain, according to the terms of the Treaty of Westphalia, that it must be rectified; whereupon the Protestant body will first seek to accede to the execution only upon agreeing with the imperial decree, and not otherwise, because they are obliged to maintain the prerogatives and privileges of their principals, and it is feared that this matter will yet be subjected to many obstacles. The Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz, recently arrived from the Carthusian monastery in this city, has held lengthy discussions with the Roman ministers concerning religious matters, and a courier was dispatched from there to Vienna. It is believed that this conference concerned the Protestants' refusal to appear in the assembly, and [?] 300 June 1270 P.M. [Editor's note: this date reference appears corrupt in the original transcription] on their part no resolution had been taken, and to avoid deliberating with the Roman body on religious matters. It is said that a certain Roman minister, arriving in Vienna some days ago, intends to remain there for some time, in order to consult with that court on the necessary measures in this work, which continues to give the Protestants much concern, and must be their main goal—to counter and treat all the necessary councils of the other side; all the Protestant ministers remaining therein in good harmony, knowing well that the Roman [party] would not fail to turn their advantage and extend it at any opportunity; yet among the Roman [ministers] there are those discreet and wise enough to be persuaded by reason. Among the Protestants, great distress has arisen concerning two matters, and also because a rescript had come from the Palatinate, which is as sharp and vehement as any yet issued by that court, and against which harsh manner and procedure all concordable [?] have come together, because such appears more like an inquisition than a redress of the grievances of his Protestant subjects, as he has assured through his minister; and because all communication has been cut off even to the recent [?], it is feared that the Treaty of Westphalia, in which the oppressed Protestants are granted recourse to the guarantee of the guarantee [provision], [and] the further remedies of the religious grievances should benefit from the execution ordinances and the Gospel thus held forth [in order] to be able to make use of the prescriptions of the peace instrument with emphasis. IV. If this were not the sense of the Roman Catholics, they would have accepted the proposal which the evangelical discourse has made clear enough, and then would have brought the Roman disputation before the evangelical in this manner, without mentioning the commission decree of the 11th of April, or they would have requested a separate commission decree, concerning the deputation, from the Emperor, and if either of these had happened, none of the evangelical would have refused to appear at the council meetings and attend the deliberations. But V. since this did not happen, and instead something of great consequence was presented, one could not conclude otherwise than that the Roman Catholics wished to open a door for themselves, through the mediation of their votes, to extend the matter, which [would require] either an execution or an entirely different mediation than ordinary imperial deliberations require, and to present themselves as co-judges; and that in VI. well in matters in which they are the greater parties and interested; but VII. the [Elector] of Mainz, who has caused most of the grievances for some time, and furthermore holds the directorate and VIII. in religious matters such as the proper prior communication, wishes to establish something particularly for himself, in order then to deal with it generally; that IX. could not be done in the least, because the evangelical [party] had surrendered their subordinate complaint statement to his K.M. [Emperor's Majesty] some days before, of which the outcome would undoubtedly give his K.M. occasion not to rely on the deliberation of the commission decree of 12 April of this year, meanwhile one has many examples that when proposals against commission decrees have been brought in, they have remained undeliberated; and that the fatherly providence of his K.M. to preserve internal peace among the imperial states would not permit so important a matter to be handed over to a general committee, because thereby greater confusion would result in place of remedy. Namely, X. The Roman Catholics through such deliberations would obtain the desired occasion to bring all manner of controversial questions and disputes, as was evident in the years 1654 and 1656 at the imperial and deputation diets, and thus now also the clearest imperial institutions and conventions advantageous to the evangelical [party] would be rendered powerless; and why this must therefore be opposed by the evangelical [party], because thereby unavoidable schisms and embittered divisions would arise. It was therefore, XI. here without reason, that the evangelical [party] consulted on this, because this commission decree was brought forward at the last imperial assembly before the electoral feast without pressing necessity, and because they added to it the moderate [representation] of the Swabian Circle, which seemed to have been done solely because the evangelical [party], the welfare of that circle being of concern, would not trouble to appear at the assembly; for which and also for other reasons, the evangelical envoys could not engage in so delicate and serious a matter without first having received express orders and instructions thereto, without which they would have been answerable in the highest degree, because, in place of a good outcome, unrest and alienation would easily have arisen; but as soon as they shall have received from their principals the necessary instructions to appear upon the propositions of the aforementioned commission decree, they are prepared to appear without opposition, which they are also willing and prepared to do in other matters coming before the imperial diet, provided that the commission decree remains unmentioned in the notification letter. Secondly, for the rest, experience has sorrowfully taught that the forced and troublesome pressure of the evangelical envoys at the imperial diet has been conveyed in a malicious manner to his K.M., yet they have done this in service of their high principals, and consequently for the benefit of the Reich, and that in place of this just conduct it is unjustly represented as though they wished to undertake to offend his K.M.'s authority, for which the evangelical [party] nonetheless show at least as much highest respect (if not more) as the Roman Catholic envoys can show, wherefore they do not doubt that their high principals will have the goodness to instruct their ministers residing in Vienna in such a manner that they will be in a position to present the true state of affairs at the proper place and at suitable opportunities, and thus protect the evangelical ministers from the unjust calumnies of the Roman-minded; wherewithal &c. &c.

Dated Regensburg, 21 December 1720, by Electoral Saxony.

A written report by the evangelical envoys concerning the notification letter, dated 20 December 1720: concerning the conference of the evangelical [party] agreed upon 21 December 1720.

From the relations conducted in this manner, it can be seen how ready and willing the evangelical [party] have shown themselves toward the Catholic gentlemen, to bring the outstanding common points to an end; particularly in the repeatedly neglected concerns of the [Electoral] of the Swabian Circle, and because it was hoped that the Electoral Mainz directorate at the last imperial diet before the electoral feast, at which matters were chiefly bound in relation and correspondence, would follow the usual practice and fairness [and] would not have brought forward any new measures for the continuation of the foregoing, from which, contrary to all expectation, the opposite was experienced, according to the copy of the notification letter set forth hereafter, containing that the imperial commission decree of 4 April of this year would be taken up and thus the decree of 20 June 1710 would precede, and because it was found that this matter carries much difficulty with it, one did not dare to take it upon oneself to do anything therein without first obtaining permission and order as to how to conduct oneself, wherefore, and because the electoral feast time had been taken to send off the necessary instructions and it was deemed well to consider and set forth the following considerations beforehand.

First, that the contents of the commission decree were so constituted that one could not possibly submit the same to the judgment of the Roman Catholics. II. And although from the notification letter it appears that not the entire commission decree, but only a part, and which particularly the remedies of the religious grievances, would come into consideration, yet this was nevertheless no relief; because all that has hitherto been done by the evangelical [party] concerning the commission decree concerns the aforesaid religious grievances in particular, wherefore this and all that one would deliberate about actually lies within those same remedies; so that one began to instruct the repetition, the point of which [?] came into more marked [?], to which one might bring the same [?], it is hoped through the imperial institutions, and particularly the Westphalian treaty, expressly [?], while [?] from here the generally [?]

France

PARIS, 30 December. The rumour that has circulated here for the last two days that Mr. Law and his son had been killed near Brussels gave great pleasure to many people who believed it; meanwhile, one was undeceived last Saturday, and yesterday it was confirmed that he was escorted to our frontiers by Mr. Sarcbert, Captain of the Hunters at Chantilly, provided with a good passport; however, being at Valenciennes, he was detained there by Mr. d'Argenton, Intendant of that province, for 10 hours, on suspicion that he must have obtained his passport through trickery, and that possibly a convention with him must come to an end and contrary orders be given, but this not having happened, he let the aforesaid Mr. Law proceed, who, having arrived at Brussels, was received favorably by the Marquis de Prie, and was taken by him to the Comedy, from whence it is thought that he will have made his journey through Germany toward Italy.

Left Column

[Largely illegible - original transcription too corrupted for reliable translation]

...it is said here that he, having served many campaigns in this realm and [?], has caused [?]. Mercia Law, though not whether there is much more daughter nor prepared here, not what [?] also toward other paid weapons the Lord William Law, not was [?]...

Men breit in de Voorslaid of Mechelen [section too corrupted to translate reliably]...

PARIS, 9 JANUARY. On Monday, the 19th, there arrived a [?] Couette; on the 10th of this month, Goome departed with news that the [?] lay ill dangerously; [?] departed. His Holiness with scholars [?]. Wednesday the third there went Madame, but [section severely corrupted]...

According to further news, the ship of Captain Rennes, [?], was driven by fire and [?]. The Royal Palace [?]...

According to the 30th of [?], the Adrien with 2 seals [?] 2600, and those with 2 to 3500 [?]; but Bank notes become daily less...

[Several sections entirely illegible]

Nederlanden [The Netherlands]

EMDEN, 3 January. By very heavy storm winds from 31 December, the old dikes were broken through, whereby the entire city was set under water; on the land it has caused great damage likewise; especially in Keulnacht in the year 1717; the misery caused thereby is indescribable; many houses, people and beasts have been driven away; and since this misfortune [?]

The First Director of State Lotteries, by express order, made known to the [?] that the postponement of the drawings of the 4th to the 5th class from now on shall be [?] at Amsterdam by Hendrik Blank, at Dordrecht by Joannes van Braam, at Rotterdam by Joannes; [?] the continuation shall be made until Saturday the first of February; the [?] class will again become drawable on the 10th of the same month of February [?]

Thus the ship the St. Jan Baptist, commanded by Skipper Cornelis Schoon, [?] has wrecked and broken, being laden with Hamburg [?] goods, some of these goods [?] and salvage, the same at sea on the new Waals Island by the Bantemeestaart, shall [?]

There was presented for rent the fine Knightly House of the late Baron de la Tdop Thol, located in the Breythuist near Koudekerck, adjoining the late Leyden, [?] Easter stables, Coach house, fine orchards with fruit trees, all bounded by a quarter hour's walk, well and planted with tall trees, coming upon the Rhine, as meadows [?] having [?] thereto, [?] shall address themselves to the [?] who shall give further information.

At Leiden printed by FELIX DE KLOPI above the City Hall. 8 January 1721.

Right Column

### ROME

Notices & Advertisements

At Leiden by Christian Vermey is printed and to be obtained, likewise in the other [?] of Constantinople, Smirna-Holland and Terra-Holland, containing the aforementioned, also at [?] place, Enthulem, and other [?], with the surrounding villages and renowned abbeys of the Counts of Holland, [?], [?] and endowment of churches and monasteries. For ROTTERDAM, DELFT, and LEYDEN by the JANSSONIUS VAN DUALEN, Printer, [?] a worthy description of the wrecked fluyt-ship at Sloot of 9 [?], who saved their lives, in 8vo. Item, weekly account of the [?] belonging to the song, a brief note of all the principal events of the [?] ALMANACK, in octavo, with figures.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 8th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 5.
