The 13th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 7.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 15th of January, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 1st December. Due to violent weather, no boats from the ships that have arrived from Vera Cruz have yet come ashore; the fleet company that was reflected upon, which departed from Buenos Aires 400 [days?] ago, is now also soon expected. From the 9th to the present, the following have arrived in this Bay: the John Eves from London, John Balderton and the ship under Capt. le Duco and the ship Philip Jonas and Balderton, which brought troops to Ceuta, as well as the English ship the Sternes under Capt. Arthur, from cruising on the Salctis; furthermore there were the English warships under Capt. Stevens, the warship the Loo under Capt. George Frotherciam, 25 French ships, Holland [ships], 4 Oceania ships and 2 Hamburg ships: there were also 2 more ships entering this Bay. News has been received here that troops, ammunition, etc. are still being sent daily from Tariffs to Ceuta.

MADRID, 24th December. When the King entered his 350th year of age a few days ago, all the Grandes, the Officers, etc., as well as the foreign Ministries with the occasion of the Constitution and its Declaration, went to the Queen, the Prince of Asturias and the Infantes to discharge their duty in this regard; in the evening the King and Queen also withdrew.

Russia

PETERSBURG, 17th December. For some days there has been extremely severe cold here, as people have suffered and endured; through this fierce frost, many persons have had their noses, cheeks and chin frozen on their faces; and many travelers have even been found dead on the roads in this city and surrounding realm. Captain de Waard, who has been inspecting the gulf and harbors of Cambaya on behalf of the Czar for the past 2 years, has returned there, and has brought back a very accurate map of it, with which C. Muli appears to be extremely satisfied.

Germany & Adjacent Realms, Etc

VIENNA, 1st January. Last Saturday and Sunday, several feast days were observed in great splendor at the Imperial Court. During this time, the Count Barbasini was appointed Colonel over a Regiment of Dragoons, the Marquis of Belgiojofo to General Quartermaster, and the Prior Tisnol to Colonel of the Regiment of Grenadiers, and a Count of Daaun, the Lord King to the position of Quartermaster of the High Household Office. On Monday, the Emperor amused himself with hunting, and on the same day the Muscovite Ambassador Proclodinski arrived here and departed for Venice to attend the amusements of Carnival there, and when those have concluded, he will return here before Lent to receive the exchange of compliments and congratulations regarding the New Year from the Imperial Family and the general nobility. It had been reported in general that the suspected change of police and the beginning of the new year's revenues would be [?]; by means of these [matters] remained on the previous footing, and it is said that such will continue until the first of April: regarding the [?] the thanks would continue to go [?], and the second of the aforementioned changes that proceeded from foresight is this: that the Jesuit Fathers in the Cloister of St. Anna, who 96 years ago received 19,000 Florins from Emperor Ferdinand, had to relinquish the collection of common revenues on that day; and although this fell very hard upon these Fathers, they did not dare to oppose the collection, and the eternal Chamber was appointed to this position: and it is intended to restore the collected sum to the aforesaid Jesuit Fathers, thus compensating them for the loss of this daily income; through the Provincial Fathers the Clergy presented a petition to His Imperial Majesty, in order that the said earnings in his Society might continue, and because they alleged that their income would not be sufficient for the necessary [expenses?], the decision was made and the [?], wherein the annual revenue might be seen and the troop provisions completed, was conducted with much [?] in the Imperial authority, and with [?] metal steadfastness.

Great Britain

LONDON, 10th January. Yesterday, the sub-Governor and Deputies of the East India Company delivered to those of the House of Commons various books and accounts: and among others a particular account of the 9 million, 330,668 pounds Sterling loaned on the Company's capital, as well as an account of 258,089 pounds Sterling loaned on the Company's subscriptions; also other accounts taken by the Company; which were ordered by the House to be laid open; subsequently they made known to the House that the remaining accounts were being prepared with all speed; and that it would be possible for them also to be delivered to the House in the near future; whereupon the House ordered the said books to be laid on the table, to be examined by the members of the House with care, and to be prepared; at that time care was taken to be examined by the members of the House with care; the Purse report of the resolution concerning the Subsidy taken in committee of the whole House, which report was approved, and it was ordered [to bring in?], namely two [bills?] to levy 6 shillings [?] per pound on the revenues of the lands, taxes, persons, etc., and the other to continue the impost on malt, etc., both for one year, and this on the [?] of 1711.

Whereupon Mr. Stephen rose and said that, since the matters concerning the East India Company were nothing other than a chain of bad practices and embezzlements, he was of the opinion that in order to discover the opportunity thereof, one must investigate and examine it from the lowest to the highest link, to which end he proposed ordering the Directors to lay the following books and lists before the House, and that was agreed to by the House; which thereupon ordered that the Directors should lay open the calculations or the reasons which induced them to have the said subscription in money at 1000 to 100; and also how the gross [?], the calculations or the reasons which prompted them, or on what foundation they resolved to make a distribution of 30 percent to [?] and not less than 30 percent per annum for the next 12 years; that the sub-Governor and Deputies shall deliver a list of the names of the Directors, together with the names of the Treasurer, Secretary and bookkeeper of the Company; that the Directors shall deliver a book or books of the minutes of the committee of the Treasury of the said Company since the 25th December 1719; and that the aforesaid Directors should also lay open before the House the copies of the [?] of the Company: whereupon the House went into committee to take into consideration the present state of public credit, wherein it having made some progress, they resolved to continue on the coming Wednesday, to which day the House also adjourned. Yesterday a general meeting was held by the members of the East India Company; concerning the propositions which have been made to the Directors by the East India Company and the Government; concerning which propositions many arguments were made; all of which tended toward not accepting the proposals, because it is certain that the said propositions would be put forward by the East India Company merely to discover what deplorable difficulties, bad practices, conduct and embezzlements they have involved themselves in, and that in this case one must be particularly on guard, because this is not beforehand to the prior advantage of the Company, and if such [?] should be allowed, it would be nothing other than to their complete ruin; just as they have put the Company under their control; yes, by some it was even felt that they should account to friends in a unanimous gift of families, but that they did not want to plunge the interests of the Nation also into their misfortunes; and one of the members even made a calculation of all that the Company had gathered, and had found that [the text breaks off here].

REGENSBURG, 6th January. The Roman Ministers there have received news from Vienna that the Emperor, upon the remonstrances of the Protestant Corps, will provisionally come to no other decision: and that His Imperial Majesty has already taken an absolute resolution regarding the redress of the grievances of the Protestants, according to which the Electors and Bishops must regulate themselves; and that the Emperor would be disposed to await the outcome of the term of four months, expressed so clearly in the aforesaid redress, first: the aforementioned Roman Ministers maintained that the Protestants must here once more give satisfaction concerning the reciprocal grievances which they believe they have against them, which grievances they are collecting with all diligence in order to deliver them to the Emperor, as has already been done by the Bishop of Worms; who has given notice thereof through his Minister there to the Protestant Corps; and it is assured that they are already busily engaged in having their memorials printed: they also hold frequent conferences among themselves to devise the means of furthering their undertakings, in which the Cardinal of Saxony-Zeitz has not found himself less occupied; but His Eminence went on Friday on a journey to the Bishopric of Eichstätt. The news which the Protestant Corps received from the Hanoverian Legation Secretary stated that he arrived on the 19th of the past month at the court of the Elector Palatine; where, on account of the plague days, he had made a very [?] journey to Frankfurt, and the following day, having discharged some formalities with the Ministers, he delivered his credentials from the Protestant Corps in a private audience to the Elector, who received the same with much grace and requested that he be favorably disposed to redress all grievances to the complete satisfaction of the said Corps; which it is hoped will be evident in the execution: in any case, the aforesaid Legation Secretary will be regularly informed of it from post to post.

TABLE OF CONTENTS, 24 December. The King, having proceeded in ceremony to the Court, remained there closed in a carriage. Early thereafter he received the Regent, [who brought] the Letter Patent from Milan regarding the old predecessors' decree; in which the matters concerning the ordinance of Sicily and the Count of Sicily were concluded.

ROME, 21 December. The Pope, residing in the Quirinal, appeared [in public]; in the evening a private audience for Cardinal Friuli [was held, with] a gracious gift of 36 [items], which yielded 35 [uncertain]. It is believed this will be of service; [two] from Bouclé came here, which did not happen without [difficulty]. Monday morning audience for the Pope's [matters]; the Pope [?] his Holiness has ridden, [and] that evening [he] went again to the Apostolic Palace [and] named a certain Cardinal Nicholas Spinola [?], who thus [?]. Early in the morning [they] prepared, to the misfortune of the Peffianen; then the [Cardinal] on behalf of him [?] to the Pope; the Church of Maria Maggiore [?] at the time in the Qui[rinal] of St. John Lateran [?] has been glorified in the Quirinal [?]. Concerning St. [?], [as] the scribe continued, one [?] until the end of the Eerehaurdige [?].

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 13 January. Yesterday the Marquis Beretti Landi, Ambassador, passed through this City in ceremonial procession for the Congress at Cambrai, without [?]. No one wished to hear the Mitz Geteyl, [and] one noticed no discomfort regarding his [?], [which] afterwards he set forth his journey over Valenciennes, where it is said he will remain until he receives new orders from his Highness. The Abbot Strikland, Doctor, a Nobleman and Englishman, has arrived here, and it is said that he should have a Patent from the Emperor to be Bishop of Ypres, yet this [matter] continues [uncertain].

A general [position] was elected, which office was conferred on the Hect Joly of Rieueil. Since the general [position] of the Procureur général, of Fielty, with [?] Bidel of Asfeld; a new Lictoorsen Doeliezen stands in the house. The [?] State again [held] a general assembly in the Bank [?]; on the 3rd, not having been present; to come, which one [?]; in the assembly. Since some days ago here a strange report [circulated] that the Young [?] is to come to the aid of the Abbot du Burget. It is said that [?]; one adds moreover that His Holiness, in favor of the afflicted Inhabitants of Provence, should have relieved the Duke Regent, and that His Royal Highness is disposed to come to the aid of the People and the unfortunate Inhabitants of Rennes. It is said that the Cardinal of Rohan will certainly depart for Saverne in the coming week, and that from there he will continue his journey to Rome.

Some days ago the Marquis of Mont Salle Uzez was seized near the place called la Charité by 3 Boatmen, who inflicted wounds upon him, but he has not yet departed.

A few days [ago] the Mr. Mauberon, with the help of the Mr. Bi ron, concluded [a deal] with a packet of 3 [?] Regiment, which he had caused [?]. The Marquise de Vavatan, Wife of the générant Procureur général in the Royal Chamber at Rotterdam-[?], [has] deceased. With the Troops under the Troops [it] is continued. The performance of the King's ballet will take place tomorrow; and it is said that of the play of Cardenie there would be 3 weeks of Performances, but in the following [circumstances] Lyker for the [?] of all sorts of occasions to carry out; [and] that of Don Japheth of Armenia is performed on the Stage; the first Ball after the 3 [?] days was the same night and 7 [?].

England

PARIS, 10 January. One sees an Edict of the King's Council of State dated 5 January, ordering that the Treaties regarding the advantage upon the Spices and concerning the united Factions with the Company of the Indies [are to be] suspended, and the name of Anrierand Patchen mill and of no value shall be; and that, beginning with the first [day] of this Month, the said Company shall cease exercising the aforementioned administration and the management of the trading of the general Einaouuen. One sees here in Print a concise Account concerning the occurrences of the Plague in Marseille, both of the preceding and the registered [cases], which Report by the Messrs. Cluyneau, Verny and Soulier, Deputies of the Court, has been sent to the Knight of Langéon and to the Gentlemen Aldermen of the said City. It is reported that the Court has issued a Pension of 4,000 livres to several of the Doctors who served there during the Difficulty, and also to the Surgeons who, having been dispatched, in good order from the Province went there. The Edict of the King's Council of State, ordering the suppression of the new Mandates of the 4 appealing Bishoprics, which Mandates have been issued after the Declaration given regarding the Committee, but however long before the Registration thereof in Parliament was done, [by which] since they the Registration are for good approved, and by Parliament registered that certain Declaration can be made before the Registration is approved; and moreover one must already believe that the Parliament Court will maintain all everywhere great moderation, and that Registration is comprised in such Terms that for the Anti-constitutional [provisions] great opening for relief has been preserved. The Adjudicants determine their Taxes in Athins, [which] was the cause that they feared [a shortage] but not [?] or partly voluntarily [will be completed]. The City of Marseille has, notwithstanding the scarcity of Money, found means to obtain a Million at 4 per Cent, to make use thereof in these pressing necessities. The Mr. le Nain, the King's Advocate in the Chatelet, has [received] the approval [for] the service of Advocates.

The Lodger Cogan [announces] likewise that the completion of the drawing of the Lots of the [?] will have been done, after which the Interested Parties can claim themselves of the same [by] Mand[ate] February [?] their progress [will] have.

To obtain advantage, the same Yearly [loses] many Millions loss [General received] did, and because the Directors saw that the Minded were inclined to [?] Joy of exceeding thither gladly to the Properties to reject, was for [?] Bilal of Ast placed to do the remainder and ampler deliberation on this matter; adjourning next Thursday; Likewise was [a matter] presented to the Black Debates, Petitioned Bach held, where, after many and serious Debates, petitioned from the Fund of the Z. Z. Company, with an Annuity of 3 per Cent, to be paid weekly by the Exchequer, and [authority] to Directors to agree thereupon with the horn of the Communities; thus in the Debates was [it] [said] by the Directors Gould, Morris, Thompson and Art regarding [this] that they had nothing to do with the Company and not Parliament; but in the Z. Z. Comp[any], rather with Parliament, so generally was one of the opinion that the Bank, by reason of this agreement, [should] Yearly [?] the payment of the non-running Fund in the [?] over the payment of the non-running Fund in the [?], and that the Bank shall then only be used to 9,000,000 lbs. St., and because the principal yet outstanding matter, to place the public Credit [?], [if it] may succeed [through] the receipt of the [?] Millions Z. Z. Fund in the Capital of the Gothland Company, even as Z. Fund in the Capital of the Gothland Company, even as the all with those of the Bank as then done have, [it is] expected that all with the work will be equaled, to that receipt in the maatkeurderdiën the work were equaled, to that receipt in the maatkeurderdiën the work were equaled, to that receipt in the maatkeurderdiën saw rightly forwarded from that Assembly of the Congress at Zamneek might well run [on] into February; meanwhile prepares herself Mylady Carteret with her Spouse to the unstemmde Congress to secure [it]. Although one [received] last week a Co[py] of the Project of Knight Humphreys Makworth to every Peer of the Project of Knight Humphreys Makworth to every Peer has sent [it], yet is it greatly doubted whether the said Project well has sent [it], yet is it greatly doubted whether the said Project well will find passage now, because the same very matters contains, which one does not think with the inclinations of this [Country] to come agree. does not think with the inclinations of this [Country] to come agree.

According to the latest Letters from Gibraltar, the Moors [have been] made known, and requested his arrival there to the Moors [have been] made known, and requested to conduct over [and] Truce[s]; whereupon the Barbarians had sent him a Jew to enter into negotiations; and concerning this, the Letters say that one promised oneself good luck [?], because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because the Letters say that one [?] and good luck promised, because it seemed as before.

The Course at WESSEL for Amsterdam 33, 9. sight 32. 10 to 8. Rotterdam 34. Antwerp 34, 6. Hamburg 33, 33. Paris and Bordeaux fixed prices. Madrid 46 and 1 Viceiro 48. Bilbao 43 etc. generally fixed prices. Madrid 46 and 1 Viceiro 48. Bilbao 43 etc. half. Livorno 49 and 1 half to 6. Genoa 51. Venice 45 and 1 half. 1 half. Livorno 49 and 1 half to 6. Genoa 51. Venice 45 and 1 half; Lisbon 3 to 2 and 1 half to 3 quarter. Porto 5 to 3 and 1 half. Dublin 12 to 11 and 1 half. The Shares of the Bank 21 1/2 to 23. East India [Shares] of the Z. Z. 1st. the first Subscription, 2nd ditto, 3rd Subscription, ditto for Money, the Subscription, Subscr. of the Annuities, Lottery Annuities, Negotiable Annuities, the Prizes, the Million Bank 1 50. Affruitable Company 43 ditto [?] 35, the Reuis Assurance 1 and 1 quarter, ditto London 9 and 1 half; and Yolk Building 16.

PARIS, 10 January. It is said that the Project of the King's Council of State dated 7 January ordered that the Traders regarding the advantage upon the Spices and concerning the united Factions shall use the name of Jeremiah Pierpont; and that, beginning with the first [day] of this Month, the said Company shall cease exercising authority over the Administration thereof, and that the Collection thereof may [proceed] there [?] from Print a concise Account of...

Amsterdam

AMSTERDAM, 13 January. Letters have arrived here from Texel, which report that the Ship named the Lamoignon, and Captain Antoine Simean, [which] some time ago departed, in the last Storm with high and [rough] weather departed, having been set on the South Wall in the last Storm with high Water, on the order of her [?] Nop[?], the People were stripped naked from there, and wholly on a separate Place [was placed] in Quarantine to hold [?], all [such] that the Ship with its entire Cargo in Brandy has been [?], and burned down to the ground entirely.

AMSTERDAM, 13 January. According to a Letter from Madrid, the arrived Ships from Vera Cruz would have brought for His Majesty 2,144 Casks of Indigo (and not 380). Through [?] the Ship the Company Palliet of Barcelona arrived, and because it came from Cádiz, they hoped that the Goods therein would not be brought to the Lazaretto, just as the Cargo from the Ship [that] came from Sto. Holop[?] was brought [there]. According to the Letters from Marseille of the 10th instant, the Plague in that City was sufficiently halted, yet the distrust remained among the Inhabitants so great, [such] that one [would say] some other [not mentioned] Sickness [was] at home, as they almost [maintained] a communication with one another; indeed the Churches were visited only by a small number of People, and one reckoned that one will begin the Sickness until the Summer, [and] in all, about 7,500 Persons had died. The Spanish Ship bound from here to Bilbao, the Sta. Clara, Captain Manuel del Muerte, lay on the 3rd instant in Tormouth. 5 [and] likewise at the end of this [month] in Downs the Ship the St. Joseph Jan Noralis, also bound from here to Bilbao. The Letters from Kevel, Riga, Petersburg and other places in the East Sea have not yet all arrived. At Danzig (whence Suerit Imkes was sent to the Pillan [?]) Klaas Graaf from France has arrived; at Copenhagen Zacharias Hopman, from Kiel Harbor bound for Lübeck; and Jurgen Pieterse, from Copenhagen [willing to sail] to Stockholm, was, in [the] week [?] 265 arrived there, [having] there [?] come out. On the 7th of this [month], from the Vlie (where Klaas Pranger from Riga and 2 or 3 others [are] inside) well 20 Ships departed from the East Sea, and among them, Vollert Prietuele, In Tiel some Ships [have] arrived inside.

THE HAGUE, 14 January. The Gentlemen States of Holland and Free-Vlieland have been well pleased. Yesterday the Britannian Minister the Mr. Doyrotles [was] in conversation with some Gentlemen of the Government.

This Evening at [?] o'clock, being Wednesday the 15 January 1721, the Brokers will assemble in the Old Burgh, [to sell] a party of extra fine new Varffchen Konijan- and Red Mool-flags.

Since the Ship the St. Jan Baptist, [which] was commanded by Skipper Cornelis Schoon, and laden with Hamburg Fydipycea and Copethe Eastern end [has been] wrecked and broken, so one [seeks for] a [?] which 5 before and after Gieeu and [?] restored, who [?] of great [?], has in Caliberuer, [wishing to come] to Amsterdam on the new Waai-kyland, [offers] 12 [?] to Johannes Calkhuet, residing Bautemeeffiraat, [will] receive proper salvage fees.

Jeronimus van Goor and Balthazar Ryke, Brokers, shall on Friday the 14th January open a party of 3 [?] Cheircele Tydte, delivered in Hoorn;

Johannes Staats, Jan Lemmers and Hendrick Linquert, Brokers, shall in Amsterdam [at] [?] o'clock, on the Ringel in the Buig, [sell] a party of extra fine [goods] of about 130 Kams [that have been] sent; and they, the Brokers Jan Hoos, Jan Riedemaker and Com. Hoos, in Amsterdam [will] publicly [sell] new Values [?], lying in a small [place] on the Haersgraft.

Course of Exchange

The Course of the EXCHANGE today on Madrid 89. Cádiz 111. Seville 114. Bilbao 88. Lisbon 41 and 1 half. Venice 72 and 1 half. Liverpool 26 and 1 half. Genoa 89 and 1 half. [?] Payts as sight. London 12 Shillings. 7. Frankfort current 29 and 1 half Augs[burg]. [?] 290 Folio gr. Breslau 34 and 4 agtie St. Hamburg 12 and 5 agtie St. Augsburg 8 and 1 quarter per Cent. Ghent 8 and 1 half. Bruges and 1 quarter Zealand 1. Rotterdam 1 half. The Agio of the Bank 5 and 1 agtie. The [?]

Notices & Advertisements

On Saturday the 14th and Sunday the 15th January, one shall within the City of Leiden at the Here Logement Huymans Dwelling with the [property] of 18 mornings of Land, of which well 12 mornings Hayen Rijkdijk in Agthoorn, besides the Territory of Leyderdorp; [and] also at the Here Logement at the Bunf[?], publicly sell an [?] with well 12 [?] rye with Stone and Fine Antique, located at the [?].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 15th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 8.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 17th of January, 1721

ITALY.

NAPLES, 24 December. Several days ago, the Viceroy proceeded in ceremony to the Quirinal Palace to drive the first nail into a galley that has been begun to increase our fleet, after which His Eminence blessed it and named it the Elizabeth. On Saturday, here at Milan the Count of Fuentes, a grandee of Spain, was married to his niece, who was to marry the third son of the Duke of Monticcione, former king of Sicily. The Prince of Savoy and the Count of Sinfendorf have departed from here for Vienna.

ROME, 25 December. Sunday, being the fourth Sunday of Advent, a chapel service was held in the Quirinal Chapel, in which all the Cardinals appeared: in the afternoon, the Ambassador of Venice had a private audience with the Pope, to which His Excellency was introduced by Cardinal Panuli. In the evening, His Holiness sent a present of thirty-six candles with ornaments to the said Ambassador, to whom thirty-six pilasters were given by the bearer as a courtesy; but before the Minister would have the pontifical audience on Monday, that same Sunday evening Cardinal Barbarigo of Brescia arrived here to receive the Cardinal's hat. To the honor and glory of all, this will occur on the king's feast day. Monday morning the Portuguese Ambassador also had an audience with the Pope. Tuesday, being the day before Christmas, the Pope held a consistory for his family; but as His Holiness was preparing for the celebration of the following feast days, he did not assist at Vespers in the evening; that evening Miserere was sung in the great hall of the Apostolic Palace; after which the Pope entertained various Cardinals at a private supper: subsequently the said Cardinals went to the chapel, where Mass was sung by Cardinal Nicholas Synolia, because the Cardinal Albani, the chamberlain, who should have performed this, is not a priest but only a deacon. The following morning is Christmas: the Pope himself sang Mass, to the inexpressible joy of all persons present: when the chapel was finished, Cardinal Tanochara on behalf of the Compliment of Divine Protection and Shipwreck [?] spoke to the Pope: in the afternoon the solemn Vesper was sung in the church of Maria Maggiore. Thursday morning the second chapel service was held in the Quirinal, and in the afternoon Vespers in the Church of St. John Lateran. Yesterday, the feast of St. John the Evangelist, there was again a chapel service in the Quirinal, which the Pope attended. The night before last, the illness began through [?] of the Knights of St. John on the first [?], and as the pain continues, the recovery is being awaited at every moment, for which purpose the Reverend has placed [?] in the church of the Angels.

FRANCE.

MARSEILLE, 24 December. Since the 13th of this month, the sickness here has diminished considerably, so that only 3 or 4 people die daily: it is remarkable that the illness increased with the growth of the north wind, and decreased with the decline of the same wind, which is the same with the sickness in the countryside: several of the most distinguished citizens have returned from their country estates to this city, but nevertheless dare not have contact with one another: good guards also remain stationed at the city gates, and no persons are admitted without health certificates. Since the plague has been so far reduced, the magistrate has ordered that our ships coming from the Levant, which have entered here since the stoppage at Toulon and more than three sick, should sail back here: however, the goods from the last ships that arrived here from Captains Olivier and Joseph Paillert were only partly unloaded; and to prevent further damage, various barks and other vessels for this harbor must remain in quarantine.

PARIS, 10 January. According to letters from Saint Malo, six richly laden ships arrived there on account of the inhabitants, and daily more were expected on account of the Company: some merchants there had formed an association, and made an offer to the East India Company to undertake the payment of the dividend on shares at 2 and 3 quarter percent against 12,000 livres per share, on condition that the Company grant them all the privileges that have been granted to it, to trade alone and to the exclusion of all others, but they would not wish to burden themselves with more than 5,000 shares, which would amount to a distribution of 7 million. The Sieur d'Argenson came to congratulate the Duke Regent in the new year, and had a long conversation with His Royal Highness. It is now said that the Pope's illness was merely a feint: and in the letter that His Holiness wrote to the Duke Regent, for the benefit of the inhabitants of Provence, the Holy Father says, among other things, that he will devote a part of the fruits of the remaining extraordinary benefices to the relief of those unfortunate people. Meanwhile, it is understood that the plague has again resumed its fury in Provence, particularly toward the region of Aix; and people in Arles were not without fear: thefts and murders prevail here as much as ever, and thieves do not hesitate to steal in broad daylight. Due to bad weather, all provisions are excessively dear, which causes general misery. It is assured that our ministers are making preparations to depart for the Congress; which example is expected to be followed shortly by the other ministers; and it is believed that the difference over Gibraltar would soon be removed. One now sees the list of gentlemen who have been beneficed by the King with ecclesiastical offices, namely the Abbot of Tavannes to the bishopric of Chalons, the Knight of Orleans, grand prior, to the bishopric of Anville, the Abbot Langueron to the abbey of Bourbonne, the Abbot of Ajgogues to the bishopric of Perigeux, the Abbot of Corfians to the bishopric of Puit, the Abbot du Mourier to the abbey of the Victory of Senlis, the Bishop of Antu to the bishopric of Verdun, the Abbot Guillon [?] to the bishopric of Landes, the Abbot de la Farre to the abbey of Mortemat, the Abbot of Matignon to the bishopric of Coutance, the Abbot of St. Cuteur, the Abbot of Broglio to the abbey of St. Michael, the Bishop of Clermont to the abbey of Savigni, and the Bishop of Toul to the archbishopric of Vienna in Dauphiné, &c.

SWEDEN.

STOCKHOLM, 1 January. The young Emissary Milersky, having completed his commission on behalf of His Czarist Majesty here, departed again on 28 December last, accompanied by the Swedish adjutant general Kirchbarg, who wishes that we settle the exchange of prisoners on both sides; but many believe that this would concern much more important matters, with respect to peace. Her Majesty, who for several days was prevented by a cold from leaving her chamber, has recovered to her former health.

GERMANY AND THE NEIGHBORING REALMS, &c.

VIENNA, 4 January. Although His Imperial Majesty was inclined not to dispose of the bishopric of Neuwistad, which became vacant by the death of the Bishop of Lovina, before the archbishopric in this city was brought into perfect order when this matter came up, His Majesty nonetheless changed his decision and appointed the Abbot Baron Rovere to the said bishopric at the urgent insistence of the Empress Dowager Amalida. His Royal Majesty has elevated the Privy Councillor Russenbergie at Court to the dignity of Baron. Work is proceeding here on preparing an answer to the remonstrances delivered by the protesting envoys regarding the imperial commission decree to His Royal Majesty; which, it is said, the Emperor is having principally drawn up to demonstrate that he wishes to bring those possible differences to an end as soon as possible; [?] it is said that very great complaints have been lodged by the Prince-Electors concerning the publication of public documents regarding religious matters.

VIENNA, 4 January. It is assured that in the imperial council recently held it was decided to send Count Steven Kinzky first as envoy on behalf of His Imperial Majesty to the court of the Count of Great Russia, to which end instructions have already been given to him; and it is assured that the said minister may well be furnished with the character of ambassador plenipotentiary, to be able to make use of it should occasion arise. The Russian Minister, the Sieur Logofiansky, has on receipt of letters from his court, [?] with our ministers, and would rightly [?] over the state of affairs [?]

---

Left Column

The highly esteemed Caesar Maitz, together with the management of the Magazines of the Reich, has been received; the aforementioned Lordship, having awaited the broad Republic, has been thus treated... it is hoped that His Imperial Majesty will maintain [?] with the broad Republic as has been arranged, that in the future necessity... Meanwhile the Magistrate has found it proper that in future necessity... [text becomes increasingly illegible].

The Lord of Holmein reports further that His Excellency the Bishop of Bielefeld, and news from there should be noted; and there are to be found in the election of Weyberg by province... [continuing passages remain severely corrupted].

His Majesty by further circular letter to all Princes of the States, who have interest in the Northern Peace, has sent orders to Sweden... Two Imperial regiments have been sent to Hungary to that end... [text continues with significant gaps and corruptions].

From Rome, news has been received that... His Holiness has three daughters born, so His Holiness finds himself somewhat better, and... that a certain bell of St. Peter's Church there had begun... [passage becomes unclear].

Skipper Klaas Sprang Wildelaarts in this City has made a heavy Bankruptcy of about the Millions Florins.

There is an express to the Emperor. The noble Minister, the Baron of Bentenieder, has been sent to Paris...

By the Peace between the Czar of Muscovy and the Ottoman Porte [?], which has been continued, the confirmation is made...

---

Regensburg

Regarding the occurrences concerning the Protestant Corps on the second of January at Mannheim, the Legations Secretary of Keck, and the aforementioned Corps has again assembled. The Military instruction, whose content chiefly concerns the answer that the Elector Palatine gave to the aforementioned... the answer in courteous expressions received, and among others that the Elector—His Imperial Majesty's intentions regarding religious matters will be properly maintained, remaining without regard to the foregoing grievances which the Imperial Court has on the K[?]'s Deputation to investigate. Over which answer the Protestant Corps shows great satisfaction...

Church Masters of the German [Church] at Utrecht advertise, that they, according to the Magistrate's Authority, in February 1721, on Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday and Friday mornings, will examine all the belongings... to see the invited property letters and the quotients... by the heightening of the unusable and broken stones and stones. By the Stone-Cutter and Utrecht, Book-Sellers at the Exchange in Amsterdam, there shall be other large Book-sellers critique. A party of about one hundred and fifty Examples in folio, all on large paper, being all the exemplars of Jeroonimus van Goor and Balthazar Ryke, will on Friday the 24 January 1721 in the Niche in the Pale-Ground at Amsterdam, sell a party of 25 florins. Each of 15 Packages; lying in the care of the Lordship Abraham van Wylick, as purchase agent of each...

Consul of Rete von der Steng Brokers, will sell at Amsterdam before the Bridge by the Side Horse-Stall, on the grounds of Monsieur Simon Krämer, an excellent Cabinet of [?] and likewise white Marble Sculpture figures, Children, Wooden figures, Vases, etc., a large party of Antiques and diverse [?], Sculpture of children as [?] by the afore-mentioned Brokers may be seen.

All Lovers are advertised, and chiefly the Book-buyers regarding the proposed work of this week, called [?] Hoofingly[?]. Cameo Hommamese, [?] wherein Medicine [?] in folio, 6 well with care in copper engraved plates decorated; [?] common, but the said book then 40 fl. on small paper and 55 fl. on large paper [?] and [?] sufficient great with less than 48 fl. small paper and not less than 64 fl. large [?]... each opportunity his money for that time at [?] of Dourdt 22 [?]...

Advertisement of Real Estate: Extended Rules, Dwelling and situated in the Barony of Wallensart, or diluge, with about 22 morgens of land, 100 [?], Road, as Peel-Land, presently owned by the Widow Balthus, a house of Monsieur van Leeuwen, neighbor of the half-Community [...]

---

Leyden

### NEW NETHERLANDS.

AMSTERDAM, 15 January. At Bilbao (where no ships had been received) at the departure of the letters, a ship had arrived in sight; and around that several days [?]... wind and all the ships from Groningen, Kahiff; here the ship Naborgh Vogaard. The other ship the Gianetta, Capt. Rohler... the ship from Bilbao goes for the projects to Spain, and article there for the projects to Spain...

For Temel... for whom the Skull had sent to the [?]... why the Emperor of Morocco is very...

by 11 days at the height of [?]... was driven [?] Ferme Coert Tietça, from Gouda toward Port Louis going. In Terfel... Ferme Coert found himself further, and that under Block and Dirk Ryaberg of Bilbao; as likewise for the City Henefe Pier, of London... Antonio Stael...

Bank Agio 150; Z.Z. 214; some Subscriptions 54. Cert. of the Bank 150; Z.Z. 214; some Subscriptions of the West 670.

---

'S GRAVENHAGE, 16 January. The Gentlemen States of Holland and Friesland have assembled again today: it is thought that their Esteemed Great Majesties will depart on Saturday for some time to Scheveningen. The Fiscal-General of the Gentlemen, the Majesty's business was... The Treasurer-General of the Government. An express has passed to the British Court. It is assured that the Esteemed British... the English commission shall be spent out of English hand; while the Count of Windschalts as first Imperial Plenipotentiary shall now receive the King in the Cock toward there.

These weeks, the Gentlemen Commissioners of their Esteemed Great Majesties and the Council and Masters General have held a meeting on the Mint of these Belgic Lands.

By Magistrate's Authority, the Floor in the aforesaid Church [shall be] swept on [?] Fridays, mornings from 10 to 12, and afternoons from 4 to 5 hours, and the continuation of the last improvement, and then to fulfill the incomplete stones and stones, under penalty of [?] to the aforesaid Church. [?] on Monday the 20 January 1721 in the afternoon at 5 hours, holding fifty exemplars of the Bruin's Travels, over Muscovy, Examples from the press still remaining.

Day [?] the 24 January 1721 in the evening at 5 hours precisely, at the house of [?] and many of 15 cases extra fine Chinese silk, divided into 30 [?] of Wylick, where the same days before the sale and on the...

Rotterdam will sell on Monday, 17 February 1721, on the Regular [?] an excellent Cabinet of 18 red marble columns, and 6 plain gilded [?], Portiers, Medallions, [?] Lighter-spiders under these Groups of [?], as likewise Blue and Hartitlene [?]...

Advertisement for multiple cities, that after [?] February 1721 no...

[Illegible section follows]

[?] of Wallenaar, just beyond the Dyle, with a Red House, Shed [?], Land, presently used by Jan Ditkie Moltert; to be [?] of the Generalissimo in the Book-keeping Street in 's Gravenhage.

DE KLOPPER, on the Bredelaat 7 January 1721. With Privilege.

---

MADRID, 31 December. The Commissioner Quarter-master general through the Marquis de Lede [?]...

After the [?] of His Majesty [?] Vlissingen on the [?]... that the Unbelieving, notwithstanding the troops had been strengthened... the [?] at his camp powerfully did [?], so that the works... to another date half... from Ceuta [?]; and the works were forced to [?] to deliver [?] on the order of further... works, to cover, and especially... [text becomes increasingly illegible and corrupted]...

[Note: The right column and particularly the Madrid section suffer severe transcription corruption and numerous illegible passages that prevent reliable translation of large portions.]

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES today:** - Madrid 58¾ to 1½ - Cadix 115 and 1¼ to 1½ - Seville 114 and 1¼ - Bilbao 18 and 1½ - Lisbon 43 - Venice 72 and 1½ - Livorno 86 to 86 and 1½ - Genoa 80 and 1½ - Paris sight 43 - London 32 Shillings 4 to 6 - Frankfurt current 29 and 1½ Reichstaler - Danzig 200 Kop. per [?] - Bentheim 14 and 1¼ - Brussels [?] and ¼ - Zealand ¼ - Rotterdam ¼ to 1½

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 20th of January, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 24 December. The reason for the rich cargo of the fleet that ran aground here from Vera Cruz, under Admiral Don Balthazar de Guevara, is said to be that many English ships arrived there. Since the King had accepted the Quadruple Alliance, and a general Peace was expected to follow—which was much sought after by many Flemings and Hollanders—they remained behind and crossed over with the aforementioned fleet. This fleet departed from Vera Cruz in late September, arrived in Havana on 13 October, and the ship of Captain Ferrand was unloaded due to leaking, with its goods transferred to that of Captain Campanelle. The aforementioned ship of Captain Verano remained in Havana for three months to be repaired, and is now to return here with tobacco for the King. All the other ships departed from Havana on 3 November and arrived safely in this bay on the 13th and 23rd of this month. The rumor that the six ships that departed on 5 August under Admiral Chacon had arrived at Vera Cruz is not yet confirmed by navigators arriving in Cadiz; however, word was received in Havana that 19 ships, divided into two squadrons, passed Cabo Carinne, off the island of Cuba, on 14 and 16 October, and presumably arrived at Vera Cruz on the 21st or 22nd of that month. Although the aforementioned ships were already unloaded, the indult of the market was still needed; however, this had not yet arrived. The navigators report that the market at Vera Cruz did not prove as advantageous as expected, because among the English manufactures held there, the Dutch East India Company found many hides, a great quantity of wool and other manufactures, which, since they could not be traded, were all confiscated. From advice from Cartagena, word is received that in the South Sea, off the coasts of Chile, some French ships have been seen with the intent, as some believe, to conduct commerce there. The ships from Buenos Aires are now expected daily here, and since goods have been used in Cartagena, the colonists in March will depart from here towards there. Efforts continue to send troops by land to Tarifa to be transported thence to Africa; also, several boats are being readied daily to transport the necessary provisions, and two of these, laden with ammunition of war, etc., are sailing towards Ceuta or have already departed thither. No Algerian corsairs are now heard of on this side of the Strait. In this bay has arrived the Robert and Mary, Captain Dionis Obim, from the Canaries; meanwhile, the English warships the Chering, Captain Katniel, and the Gayano, and the Isoo, Captain George Hooten, have departed. The ship the Bidner, Captain Jan Cuys, sailed to Bristol; the St. Matth., Captain Marzenet Murfy, to the Levant; the Olive, Raymond King, and the St. Jaq. Baptist, Captain Clemcons, to Gibraltar, along with other ships to Malaga; and still lying here are one Hamburg ship, 33 English, 27 French; 7 Danish, and still further some 100 other ships, most laden with English manufactures.

---

Morocco

[From Monday's Courant]

The arrival of a field officer with confidential reinforcements, besides the reinforcements received in preceding days, led the Marquis de Liede to conclude that they might attack him the following day. He ordered the troops to proceed to their assigned posts at the first signal. That evening, around five o'clock, it began to rain, which continued so heavily the following day that the Moors were forced to abandon their undertaking. On the 10th, the officer commanding the vanguard reported to the Marquis de Liede that he had observed a corps of the Moorish army, which he believed intended to march along the dike of Inferno towards Tangier and extend themselves to the height of the Serail, in front of the center of our army. This indeed occurred; this detachment consisted of about 500 to 1,000 horses, which served as a guard for various officers of high rank at their head, who were distinguished by their equipages and clothing. After they had carefully reconnoitered our camp until two o'clock in the afternoon, they withdrew to their camp. This movement confirmed the opinion of the Marquis de Liede that the enemy would advance with their entire army, which the previous day could not have happened due to the rain. Thus, on the 21st, with the breaking of day, some detached parties of enemy horse appeared, which were followed at eight o'clock by a column of cavalry, directing their march towards the Serail, on the road to Tangier. Then followed two large columns of infantry, one directing towards the area of Tiamagnerom—which was towards the side of our left wing—and the other drawing towards the plain of las Caña, directly in front of the center of that wing. At this point, the enemy cavalry column made a counter-march towards their left side and positioned themselves in the plain against the center of our infantry; but no other movement of importance was observed until the moment of attack, when a party of about 60 to 70 horses showed themselves on the road to Tangier on a height to reconnoiter our camp. It was immediately discovered that these were the Bashaw and superior officers of the Moorish army, who remained there until about one o'clock in the afternoon, at which point they were seen to turn towards the left side to gather their army and give the signal for general attack, which they did, having positioned various small mounted parties on heights, who signaled from one to another, whereupon their infantry columns quickly set in motion. These remained very closely formed and made a broad front but had no depth, and with the same speed advanced to the height in front of the tenaille, which they attacked very heavily and planted their colors there, while they attacked the frieille riders, which they attacked with such resolution that it seemed scarcely believable, as they were completely exposed to the fire of our infantry and fortified batteries. Nevertheless, they continued their attack on the front, the tenaille, and other places, while the Moors were seconded at the same time by various corps positioned on the heights. The Marquis de Liede, having foreseen the importance of this post from the beginning and seeing that the enemy's main objective was to seize it, had provided it with all necessary means and sent troops from time to time to support our forces there. The combat began around one o'clock and lasted until six o'clock in the evening with such continuous fire that the oldest officers declared they had never seen the like in all the engagements they had witnessed in Europe. When the first attack was begun by a large corps of infantry of the Moorish army, it was seconded from time to time by a corps of 8 to 10,000 men; but observing that they could not take the tenaille, they made three special attacks simultaneously to seize it. However, our infantry and the cannon, loaded with grapeshot, flanked them well, and the enemy being thus dispersed and shattered, they lost considerable numbers. Realizing that all their attacks were futile, the enemy's fire began to diminish around half past four o'clock, after which they shortly pulled their colors from the ground and withdrew around six o'clock. The greater part of the Moorish cavalry accomplished nothing in this attack. It is difficult to express with what difficulty they dragged away their dead and wounded, which is why an exact number cannot be given; however, they finished off even the most seriously wounded before carrying them away. The enemy army is reported to have been about 60,000 men strong, including 15,000 horses, with the 10,000 guards of the King included in this number. Their loss is estimated at some 8,000 killed and wounded. The troops of His Catholic Majesty, from the time of four o'clock when the enemy attack commenced until they withdrew, have maintained their positions. On our side, about 300 killed and wounded have been obtained. Among the first is General Major Lion Felix de Arraran, who commanded at the attack; the Brigadiers Don Juan Pacheco Parlecary, Colonel of the Regiment of Murcia; Colonel Don Pedro de Granadiers in the Spanish Guard, wounded seriously, as well as Don Francisco Fantoni Voldersma, sub-lieutenant in the Spanish Guard, and various other officers, of whom a more detailed report is awaited. The Lieutenant General and [?] the Charlet, who lay sick in bed, were also present from the beginning to the end of the combat. The Brigadier Cusve de lire, Colonel of the Regiment of Belgia, also received a contusion. The Marquis de Liede had throughout the entire action...

---

Madrid

MADRID, 13 December. Saturday evening, the Knight Comicour, Quartermaster General and Colonel of the Cavalry, arrived, sent to the King by the Marquis de Liede with the following report:

That the weapons of His Catholic Majesty have achieved a second victory over the Moors on the said date, and one sees that notwithstanding their great losses and the strong resistance they encountered, our troops were being attacked again by them. In part because of their natural audacity and also because they had been strengthened from all sides with fresh troops—this consideration, joined with the care and prudence of the Marquis de Liede, led us to arrange that the camp should construct several new batteries, such that the entrenchment formed a half-circle from one side of the sea to the other, strengthening the front opening with points of the entrenchment arranged such that the musketry and guns flanked one another. Outside this line, two [?] batteries were placed commonly to cover the main approaches, and particularly a large work called the tenaille, at 50 paces distance in front of the center of the infantry and straight-angled communication... Before this work was a ditch; [?] feet deep and 10 feet wide with a parapet before it, and about it lay a frieille barrier to make it more difficult for the Moors.

The openings left in the line to [?] the outer posts were likewise occupied by 2 lines of frieille riders. The infantry lay in 2 lines... and at each place assigned positions to go to their post in case of attack, so that in this manner the 2 lines along the fortification... [text becomes increasingly fragmentary], calculated with trained manpower in corps... various companies that were led in the posted positions and others who might come to help if things went badly... still 2 regiments [?] dragoons, which were held behind the flank [?] of the troop under cover. These forces were such that the army consisted solely of 26 battalions of 12,000 men on foot and 32 squadrons of 4,000 horses and dragoons, only the canonical fanfare from the [?] entered to meet. Such was the disposition of our army when on the 18th of this month in the... Gebtifia reinforced cannon shots were heard; and according to report...

[Text ends fragmentarily]

Great Britain

LONDON, January 14. The two Houses of Parliament have remained closed since the Commons were able to reconvene; the House of Lords is likewise scheduled to do the same on Monday. The Directors of the East India Company have called a meeting for the coming Monday to confer with the others and to present the matter concerning nineteen months of public credit, which they will also bring before the House. It is reported that the Directors of the Bank have been authorized to come to an accord with those of the South Sea Company. The South Sea Company likewise held a general meeting, in which their Directors approved the accord reached with the Bank. This will be reported to the full Assembly for ratification, and orders will be given to proceed jointly with the East India Company in this assistance. At the same time, a Regulation was proposed to present to the creditors, requesting of the aforementioned South Sea Company the cession of various plantations in America, such as New Scotland, the island of Saint Christopher, etc. Some wished that one should also include the matter of the remaining funds from the third Subscription; but others maintained that this was not the point upon which the Assembly had been convened by the other party. The East India Company, according to their latest dispatch, also held a general meeting this morning; no one doubts that the example of the Bank will be followed there; it would then be highly probable that the capital, through the new Plan offered to them, will be accepted; thus the completion will not be lacking unless ratification by an Act of Parliament is withheld.

Yesterday a general meeting of the South Sea Company was held to deliberate upon the Proposition concerning the establishment of a portion of their capital to the amount offered by the East India Company. The Deputy Governor spoke in favor of both the Bank and the East India Company. The Deputy Governor spoke in favor of the Bank and requested some advantages from the King in America, after which the conditions were heard, upon which the Directors of the South Sea Company deliberated some debates, and it was said by Mr. Budgel that the Directors of the South Sea Company, through an oversight of affairs, would undoubtedly accept this, so as not to reflect upon the Directors; it is not within their power to rectify the situation; and that they must for their part stand rightly before Parliament; which argument, supported by Mr. Younge, wished to prevent this; every hour of delay, if the Assembly should wish to prevent it, the original would be approved; thus the accord of the Directors with both Companies is approved; thus it now falls only to the East India Company side to carry the project through Parliament. One cannot sufficiently express the movements of the Directors of the South Sea Company and those of theirs; concerning this Project by the Directors of the South Sea Company and theirs; concerning this Project with the East India Company to proceed; since by this most capable means those below are to remember their Malvoisies, to which end they have all their Friends in the wholesome [?] brought. Meanwhile, among the Directors of the East India Company in their conduct things are winding. Meanwhile, a Bill was brought before Parliament by which the Directors of the South Sea Company shall be ordered to leave the kingdom. Hereby the general Assembly of the East India Company has approved all that the Directors with those of the South Sea Company have concluded; with full power concerning to do all that they shall think fit, so that the joint Companies shall make their representations here thereon to the Lower House tomorrow.

Last Sunday, being New Year's Day (Old Style), the Prince and Princess of Wales also betook themselves to St. James to congratulate the King on that occasion; the Court was then of exceptional beauty and numbers, but the Ode composed in Music was composed following the custom on that day and night that Sunday, and since according to the custom here of the Land one is not permitted to hear any other Music on Sunday than the Psalms and Spiritual Songs, wherefore the usual Solemnities of New Year's Day were held on Monday at St. James, which consisted of a fair Concert of Instruments and Voices, in which the evening concluded with a Ball. The King has anew forbidden himself to hold any Masquerades in the year, because such diversions do not accord with the devotion and the public Supplication in the custody for the aforesaid Sickness, which in neighboring Lands, [?], to beg of Heaven. The day before yesterday in the evening about hours the Marquis of Bolingbroke, Ambassador of the King of Spain to this Court: an Express brought news of a third Victory, by the Spanish Army, which had been stationed before Ceuta, over the Moors. The engagement lasted about four hours past midday, but the Moors found that it was impossible for them to force the Spanish retrenchments, and they fled pell-mell with a loss of 9 to 1,000 dead and wounded, and on the Spanish side about 300 men are said to have been killed; it is said that the Army of the Moors was over 60,000 men strong.

ENGLAND

[This section appears to note that another presentation was made, less favorable than that of Mr. Walpole. [Editor's note: Sir Robert Walpole, then rising to prominence]]

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, January 11. The ship Erasmus Kol is bound from St. Lucar toward Hamburg, and Jan Snel departed the 5th of this month still at Rochelle. At Santa Cruz on Tenerife is the ship De Liefde, Pieter Door from here, with 3 Spanish ships likewise stationed there; at Passage, Captain Fabian du Foul, bound from Martinique to Bayonne; at Saint-Malo a richly laden French ship, which had only been out two months; and the ship of Hendrik Schelderhorn of Lübeck bound for Lübeck, was in the North Sea in a good state. Off Norrkoping sat a ship which, because it resembled the named ships in every way, is suspected to be of corsair origin. Skipper de Ruyter, bound from Lübeck to Windau, was left at Iceland; as also, as is reported from Hamburg, at Siel on the Jutland coast, the Hamburger and Water Todd bound toward Dover, Dirk Piet toward Bordeaux, and Jurgen Jurg bound toward Cádiz.

GRONINGEN, January 10. The Lords States of Holland and West Friesland have parted yesterday until further notice.

GENEVA, January 7. Coming from Provence the conditions there were still sad, which during their passage a Horse suffered on its journey now. Coming Thursday the Fourteenth in the Council the last letters from Turin of that city a Bank.

Advertisements

By Steenhouwe and Uytweer, Booksellers at the Bourse in Amsterdam, shall on these days for convenience, under the Booksellers be sold, and a party of about one [?] Mosovier, through Perffen and Indies, in Folio, all on large Paper, being all the Copies that still remain from the Print. [Booksellers are advertised], chiefly the Booksellers in the outer Cities, that after 1 February 1721 none of these [?], under threat of sale at 2 a 80 [?] [will be sold], up to 32 Guilders small Paper and 40 Guilders large Paper shall be required, for a fine of 3,000 Guilders. and it on for [?] large Paper unbound shall be sold; therefore can [?] Dordrecht or Ostend [?] to [?] [?] to [?], over the agreed Condition, none have part in the favorable Subscription.

[...] Authorities [?] [?] things shall be pointed out as such were succeeded a Catalogue of Authors which in the West shall be [?] on 24 January 1721, in the evening at 5 o'clock, in the Nees in the Brackish [?] Glass of diverse Marks, standing on a Floating house in the Fluweele

Amsterdam, shall on this day, being Monday 20 January 1721, in the afternoon at six o'clock, one hundred and fifty Copies of the Brown Choices, [?], being all the Copies that still remain from the Print, Booksellers in the outer Cities, that after 1 February 1721 none of these [?], under threat of sale at 2 a 80 [?] [will be sold], up to 32 Guilders small Paper and 40 Guilders large Paper shall be required, for a fine of 3,000 Guilders. and it on for [?] large Paper unbound shall be sold; therefore can [?] Dordrecht or Ostend [?] to [?] [?] to [?], over the agreed Condition, none have part in the favorable Subscription.

Tomorrow Saturday afternoon, shall be sold at Amsterdam on Friday 22 January 1721, Ground; a party extra fine [?] of about 10 a 90 Baskets French Glass of diverse [?] Burgwal by the Lommert, being to be viewed on the sale day.

[Amsterdam to sell on Monday 17 February 1721, on the Reguliers:] an exquisite Cabinet of 18 good Marble Columns, and [?] Busts, Vases, Pots, Medals, [?] Loves; in which [?] Groups of little Children, as likewise Blue and Heartsease Pedestals; as one [?].

Count of Meek at Epe orders the Firm Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam [?] by the City Horse Stable, on the Estate of Monsieur Simon Kramer, an excellent [?] half ditto, together with white Marble Group Busts, little Children, Busts, Vases, fine party work Antiques [?] further some Handsome Busts, Groups of Children, [?] by the Notice is to be seen, which by the afore[?]. Brokers are to be obtained.

Jan vander Steng Broker, presents to sell or to rent a magnificent Country House, with its Land thereto, located at the Village Sytham on the Royal Road, being upon the same fine Manor House, Farmer's Dwelling, Stables for 10 Horses, &c; also in Museum six with the half Lots of Apple trees, and [?] Vines, general Estate provided with fine Allées, Thicket, Orchard, Kitchen garden all with [?]; the same is about 4 morgen [Editor's note: a morgen was approximately 0.85 hectares] and the Land 5 morgen.

In winter Steng Broker, opportunity to sell an Estate with its Lands, Houses, Stables, Court house &c. named Baarnburg; having this Place a free view over own Land on [?], located at the Utrecht Towpath, between Alkmaar and Uitwaterfing, having [?]-way, together about 19 morgen: as likewise still an Estate with its Lands, Corner has now 2 open about 16 morgen Weeping [?]; yet 10 morgen Weyland afore[?]. Karstpel, over the Utrecht Cheuts, which Parcels shall be sold [?] 10 February at Alkmaar in the Reghuys.
