Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 22nd of January, 1721
Switzerland

GENEVA, 7 January. Letters from Isaac of 25 December from Provence report nothing favorable concerning the pestilential disease raging there; in Marseille several people still die daily from it; and in the city of Aix the situation is most deplorable. Yesterday the four Syndics were elected, who will govern this Republic during this year; among them are the gentlemen Tremblay (who governed the previous year) and Grenous. Next Thursday the election of the remaining magistrates will take place. According to the latest letters from Turin, one of the principal bankers of that city has declared bankruptcy exceeding 2 million.

Germany & Adjacent Kingdoms, Etc

VIENNA, 8 January. Last Sunday the Emperor, with all the Knights of the Golden Fleece present here, with their insignia in hand, went to the Imperial Chapel to hear Vespers; the following morning the Emperor's Majesty held a public service there for the knights regarding the Kingdom; and yesterday the Emperor undertook a boar hunt near Libertsdorf. It is confirmed that His Imperial Majesty has resolved to reduce cavalry regiments to 1,500 and foot regiments to 1,000. There is talk of creating four new Imperial Princes; to which posts Count Sinzendorff, Chancellor of the Court, Field Marshal Count Harrah, and the Count of Alheim, Chief Equerry, are being named. It is reported that the Cardinal of Schönborn, Bishop of Speyer, is soliciting the presidency of the Chamber of Wetzlar, which has become vacant through the renunciation of the Prince of Fürstenberg. It is reported that the Count of Breyen, at the call of Sweden, has offered the mediation of His Imperial Majesty in the pending dispute with the Czar of Great Russia; it is not yet known what advantages our minister may enjoy at that court. The general Congress at Brunswick is said will be opened in March; the ministers of his old and new master are to appear there with great pomp alongside the ministers of His Caesarian Majesty. General Schulenburg, who serves the Republic of Venice, has, since his arrival here, spoken with various Imperial Ministers, but claims he has come only to attend to his private affairs. Orders have been sent to The Hague and Paris with the Imperial Ministers Count Windischgrätz and Baron Bemmelfelder, to proceed to the Congress. It is established that the Viennese court and the roads to the south will be busier before March. The Imperial Governor of Milan, Count Coloredo, has been received most courteously by all the Elector Palatine's ministers; at first word came that a snake had hidden itself among the red leaves, and one could expect nothing but ill tidings from that arrangement. Last Saturday the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz arrived here from Elberstadt. It is rumored under hand that the Roman ministers at the house of the Keuitse Minister held a private conference concerning matters of religion; but nothing of what transpired there has yet been ascertained. The matter of the Chamber of Wetzlar remains still stuck, notwithstanding the resident counselor at the colleges pressing powerfully to conclude it according to the Imperial Decree. The remonstrations printed here by the Evangelical Corps, dated 16 November, for refutation of the Commission Decree concerning the religious grievances of the said Corps presented to the Emperor, contain first an expression of respect for the assurance of the favorable and gracious intention expressed by His Imperial Majesty regarding the Gravamina of 12 April 1720; and powerful though immodest demonstrations and replies against the accusations wrongfully laid at the door of the Evangelical [body], and a clear avowal of the genuineness of the proofs they have brought forth. The points are of the following content:

It would be tedious and futile labor to cite all that has been written in authorities concerning reprisals, arrests, and permissions of fact ex proprio[?], and to give an account of the justice and necessity thereof, since there never was a people in all the general depravity of mankind, or permission has always been granted in certain respects, without offending the Majesty and authority of the Powers, though the word Reprisal may no longer have been known, to use something to protect oneself in one's own [property] and avert the threatened evils; of which the Roman Burgundian Laws give sufficient proofs and precedent, as well as the Canon Laws.

Among the German Nation of the Roman Empire it is well known that there formerly existed mighty Frankish Emperors who, although they were forced to bow before Papal honor and lordship, nonetheless gained such predominance that, notwithstanding all zealous ordinances and laws (according to which one was no more entitled at that time than now to any other rights or protection than the laws and justice allowed), they were permitted to defend themselves with their own forces and those of their friends to obtain justice, lest they should expose the righteous and innocent to the malice of restless men, &c.

The well-known history of the Pacts of the Empire shows clearly how difficult it has been for many glorious Roman Emperors to maintain scarcely a few years of peace in the realm and to administer justice; and how at their exhausted forces and weakened laws, from time to time they were forced to settle matters through confidential conferences of the states, indeed some Emperors did not hesitate to intervene in the confidences of the estates and place themselves at their head, of which the Golden Bull of Charles IV, chapter 17, gives clear proof: finally the glorious Emperor Maximilian I brought about the perpetual Land Peace and the establishment of the Imperial Chamber Court, whereby one could somewhat hope to obtain peace and security in the realm; and through which the Land Peace with the improvements and additions made from time to time has been placed on such a footing as appears in the collections of the Empire's Constitutions and Fundamental Laws.

But as there have always been useless and peace-hating men who sought to place hindrances and hardships in the way of the realm, so too have they always sown tares among the good seed that began to sprout on German soil, and the true wisdom has suffered, so that here and there the execution has had to suffer; and the sad outcome of the observance of the Imperial Laws has, alas, become all too well known, which, if it could have been achieved, would have spared much innocent blood.

The Evangelical Church Estates and Estates, together with all well-meaning patriots and fellow Estates, have already to hope and wish that Your Imperial Majesty be encouraged and established to protect religion and Land Peace more than ever before, &c.

But since this has been so long wished for in vain and not yet obtained, as the sad experience and forced complaints of the Evangelical and their suffering oppression only too much show, so one has an humble certain confidence in Your Imperial Majesty's just and religious mind, that Your Imperial Majesty will not desire or [...]tolerate that the Evangelical shall be taken from their hands the means which serve their defense, together with the right planted in them by God through nature, [by the Empire's Constitution as]subsidiaria[?] against violence, to use and [...]rest[?], lest they not succumb to the evil will of the common [people], who desire what does not belong to them, and dare not have recourse to Imperial justice, but set themselves up to justice to forcibly wrest from themselves that which is withheld from them.

AUGSBURG, 13 January. Last Friday afternoon, Mr. Law arrived with his son and some servants in a post-coach at the house of Mr. de Jardin in this city, but becoming immediately known, the crowd of all people was very great to see him; however, he departed Saturday morning with said son and some others.

REGENSBURG, 1 January. Last Friday the Evangelical Corps received the third letter from Mr. von Reeck, wherein was mentioned that the Imperial Governor of Milan proved not as favorable toward the ministers as on the two previous occasions; and the following day word was received elsewhere that he had received no good hope in His Majesty's audience with the Elector Palatine concerning his representations, His Electoral Palatinate Highness having treated him most coldly, and said that such [matters] ought to approve the Protestant Corps of Laws; or what will follow upon that Protest, the aforesaid plenipotentiary was to send other documents to confirm his account of what transpired at the Palatine court [...] The commissioners reported. One finds now that this [matter] has again been found, which, because Mr. von Reeck (a deputy with an not unpleasant commission) [text breaks off]

France

Extract from a letter of a country place near Marseille, 28 December.

I was of the intention to return with my whole family again to the city for residence, as the pestilential disease had happily enough subsided, when upon news that said disease has now broken out for the third time, we have resolved to remain here another six months and provide ourselves with the necessaries of life.

PARIS, 3 January. The court has sent the queens and about 100 saddle horses to Boulogne for the Turkish Ambassador and his retinue.

LEFT COLUMN

[The opening passage regarding the Ambassador's arrangements and lodgings, which appear heavily corrupted in the transcription, discusses accommodations at the expense of the Grand Lord, with lodgings provided in both French and Turkish style, though the King's Ottoman escort has departed to conduct the Ambassador here, and it is not expected his arrival will reach into the valley until late.]

PARIS, 11 January. The Count of Heem, Envoy Extraordinary of the King of Poland, announced publicly to the King the birth of sons to the Crown Prince of Saxony. On Monday the 13th, the Duke Regent attended the ballet or comedy of "Japan" or "Artemis," which was presented for the first time most elegantly at the Palace of the Tuileries; this piece pleased greatly, with many masks [?] and dances before the King. On Thursday morning, the Duke Regent and the ladies made ceremonial visits to the Duke of Noailles, which were followed by a most eloquent oration delivered in the church of the College of Louis le Grand by [?], old Professor of Ethics, where the Cardinals of Rohan and of the College of Louis, along with many bishops and other gentlemen of political and military distinction, were present. On Tuesday, the Duke of Nevers, in his capacity as Duke and Peer of France, along with the Duke Regent, heard Mass, which Mr. Desmarts, music master of the Duke of Lorraine, had arranged to be sung in the Royal Chapel; and His Royal Highness was very satisfied with the music. On the 15th, the Count de Laval finally came out of the Bastille. The stock jobbers, who for a long time had traded publicly in the Bank square, were very astonished on Monday to see a prohibition against trading posted on the door of the Bank; however, though they are thus pursued and driven out of the coffeehouses by commissioners and soldiers, they cannot cease their dealings, for when driven out ahead, they return again. There is now talk of a new and very strict tax roll against the disreputable brokers, stock jobbers, and speculators, some of whom would be assessed at two million, others at fifteen hundred thousand livres, and proportionally less. Bank notes of 1000 livres trade at about 40 livres; those of 500 livres at 6 livres 15 sols, and lesser denominations proportionally. There is a desire to bring this matter of Bank note confidence into high consideration at court, but the General Controllers oppose it, as does Parliament, which also persists in not legitimizing certain patents, some of whose sessions at present are being deferred. Thus no general assembly of Bank shareholders was held on Monday, as was expected; but henceforth a private assembly only for the honorary members will be held with the Duke of Bourbon. Every day it is expected that monetary species will be raised in value. There is doubt whether the new Company of General Faculties will be stable, since its establishment encounters much opposition. Some days ago the Duke du Maine of Seaux came to this city and is to stay 14 days at the Arsenal; Madame Dowager has been very careful and is still under proper care. Madame Law is preparing herself for her journey, for which she is selling her jewels, and she has already consulted those having some experience.

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM, 20 January. At Salé, Captain Isaac Forrest from here has arrived; and according to letters of 10 November from Sta. Cruz in Barbary, the Moors in their fortresses [text corrupted]...

Notice is given to booksellers, but particularly to booksellers in outlying cities, that after 1 February 1721 no [?] of crude and [?] will be sent out; up to 32 guilders on small paper and 40 guilders on large paper shall be charged; on large paper it must be paid, on pain of 1000 guilders; and paper and for 64 guilders large paper unbound shall be sold: those from Braam at Dordrecht or Gerard of the Linde at Amsterdam, have no objection to the stated conditions regarding favorable subscription.

Jeronimus van Goot, broker, shall on Friday, 24 January 1721, at 8 o'clock in the evening, at Amsterdam in the Nes in the Brakke Grond, sell 12 bales of silk, consisting of 3 bales Serbafse, 7 bales Ardalefte, and 2 bales Bengali silk.

Filip Piet, broker, shall on Wednesday, 22 January 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning, at the Old Gentlemen's Lodging, sell a party of domestic and foreign linens, silks, half-silks and woolen cloths, English manufactures, crêpes, both fine and common sorts, needlework, damasks, etc., tea, both by the full chest and by the piece, some jewels and household goods, the full chest of tea to be obtained for money at the house of the broker on the Loyersgraif [street] 3; all to be viewed the day before.

Jacob Magnus, broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday, 5 February [and] 26 February 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, at the Old Gentlemen's Lodging, a good party of sargies, carisayen, bays, striped ellenelles, [?], green linens, and various other Norwegian cloths, as striped and plain and other goods, all by the bale; these to be viewed the day before [or] at the warehouse.

At Alphen, to rent or to sell an exceptionally pleasant property, namely a house and orchard, named Little Swanswyck, situated on the north side of Great Swanswyck; likewise another small estate situated on the north side of Great Swanswyck; also [?] situated outside the south side of the Dorne of Alphen; those interested may apply to the widow of David or to Abraham van Kirslinge, Master Carpenter in Alphen.

At Leiden, printed by Christian Vermey and available, as well in other cities through booksellers, is a very curious and charming work, titled: "Old and New Rembeland, Amsterdam and North Holland containing the antiquities, rise and progress of the cities, including the rise and progress of the surrounding villages and the famous Abbey of Egmont, with curious illustrations of named churches and cloisters, learned men, etc." 2 volumes, with figures, in 8vo, published, and also in other cities by most booksellers.

At Leiden, by Coenraad Wishoff, bookseller, is printed and being published, as well in other cities by booksellers: B. Branchn "Observations on Roman Law; V. H. Leibniz against V. El. Barbeyreanum Vindication; [and other works] on Civil Law, Nature, Nations, Antiquities & Similar Human Studies..." [etc.]

At Leiden, printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on Breedestraat. 12 January 1721. With privileges.

RIGHT COLUMN

ROME, 4 January. [The Vienna Ambassador has had his public audience; because all the Cardinals were against the Crown, great dispute arose; over that of Cardinal Otto, who had been deprived of his rank, and was received in another council in Quadrangle; this morning His Eminence Cardinal [?] celebrated, and the Pope bestowed the Holy [?] and the remembrance of the Knight of [?]; the mentioned Knights of St. James were there as well as [text becomes severely corrupted] through a general salute, St. Angelo, where one fired great salvos in honor, and the field guns, not well [?] were praised...] [Editor's note: The transcription of this section is heavily corrupted; it concerns various papal ceremonies and appointments of cardinals.]

GENOA, 4 January. [That trade between [?] will be resumed.]

VENICE, 11 January. [Several merchants have been captured by a corsair from [?], and the [?] were made and increased to [?] through many...]

VIENNA, 11 [January]. [A violent south wind brought [?] in the Bohemian [?] to the great Bohemian [?] as far as the [text corrupted] has been damaged. The [?], the [?] of Christ [?] Cathedral [?] lag us it is understood that the Roman [?] in the service of the King of Spain &c. under [?] as [text becomes illegible]...]

Notices & Advertisements

At Amsterdam by Withelma Fordanus on the Utrecht street, between the Keizers and Heersgraff, where the Roman factory is newly set up, various gold and silver cloths, made with exact care, with all kinds of colors such as these have been drawn at Royal and Princely Courts, new fabrics of wall hangings are made and sold, with different beautiful colors, durable grounds, and many different patterns, with which many rooms have been hung with satisfaction and indeed some have been decorated at Royal courts, are available at all domestic prices.

At LEIDEN by the JANSSONS VANDER AA, at AMSTERDAM by NICOLAAS POTGUETER, and in other cities by booksellers, there is again a fine moving and comforting address made by the BISHOP OF MARSEILLE, concerning the plague-struck city, for these months past, such as has not been experienced by any of the previous generations, compared in a sheet of pages.

On Wednesday morning, the Court of Madrid sent notification (or goes) to [?] and [?] Sobieski, father and mother of the happy engagement, had knowledge made known, in which the [?] with the customary ceremony at [?] [text corrupted] ...secretary of the ambassador had [?] in civil [?] [text becomes unreadable].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 22nd of january, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 11.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 24th of January, 1721

ITALY.

ROME, 4 January. On the first day of the year, the Venetian ambassador was received in public audience by the Pope; and all the Cardinals and Ministers took this occasion to place their carriages in the ambassador's procession; great dispute arose concerning whether the carriage of Cardinal Althan should have precedence over that of Cardinal Ottoboni, or vice versa, but after both sides made strong efforts, a decree was issued, and the carriage of Cardinal Ottoboni was given precedence in the Quirinal; in this dispute the shaft of Cardinal Althan's carriage was broken. On the same day, at the feast of St. Thomas, Bishop of Canterbury, the Pope celebrated mass in the English Church of the said Saint, and prayed to Heaven for a prosperous outcome for the wife of the Knight of St. George; she had suffered some days of labor pains. Tuesday evening, the wife of the aforementioned Knight of St. George was delivered of a son, and about an hour thereafter, notice of this delivery was given to the public by a general cannon salvo from the Castle of St. Angelo, whereupon one heard in an instant great rejoicing throughout the city; the joy that ensued at Court cannot be well expressed. The persons of rank who were present at this delivery were: properly, the Cardinals Palucci, Secretary of State; Cardinal Paolucci and Cardinal Petersi; Cardinal Sacripante, Protector of Scotland; Cardinal Gualtieri, Protector of England; Cardinal Aquaviva, Nuncio of Spain; Cardinal Furstello, Dean of the Deacons; Cardinal Imperiali, Protector of Ireland; Cardinal Ottoboni, Vice-Chancellor, and Cardinal Albani, Camerlengo of the Holy Church. II. The Ambassadors of Bologna and Ferrara. III. The Abbé Orsini, Piombino, Palestrina and Justiniani; the Duchess of Piano and Salviati, together with Donna Teresia Albani, a lady with her husband. IV. The Countess of Corby. V. The Bishop of Segni (an Englishman) and the Bishop of Monte Piacenza. VI. The Prelates Falconetti, Governor of Rome, Celligola and Ruspoli, Apostolic Protonotaries. VII. During the lying-in, the midwife, named Città, presented the child to all the persons present, whereupon each Cardinal gave her 20, and each Prince 10 pistoles as a gift. The Knight of St. George presented the aforementioned midwife, named Città, to the child before all the persons present, whereupon each Cardinal said to her, and each Prince [?] to philosophy and conference. The Knight of St. George endowed the said midwife with the title of Gouvernante and an annual pension of 300 scudi, and gave her permission to serve in the distinguished lying-in of the Signora Colonna, but in no other. That same evening around 10 o'clock, this newborn child was brought in ceremony with the entire illustrious retinue to the chapel called the Royal Chapel, and baptized by Monsignor Bonaventura, Bishop of Monte Falcone, and was named Charles, Stuart, Caliniet, Louis, Philippus, Silvester. The Pope placed the aforementioned Knight at his side, and Monsignor Antonio Ridolphi of Bologna congratulated him on this same evening concerning this birth; and wished him well with a gift of four gilded crowns, [valued] at these scudi in his own money, and offered him the plan of the Palace of Albano, to remain there during his lifetime with his wife. It is said that the young Knight together with his wife should moreover receive a Presidency from the King of Spain of 3000 scudi; and one does not doubt that they will yet receive considerable remittances from their adherents in England. A thousand [?] ago the said Knight sent a certain Feldmann to the court of Madrid (who is also to go in person to the court of Parma) and a Chamber Adjutant to the Prince and Princess [?]. The mother of his wife has been notified of this happy delivery. Thursday a Consistory was held, at which Cardinal Barberigo received the Cardinal's hat with the said ceremony. [?] has Monsignor Malanotti, the Emperor's Secretary, had a lengthy conference with the Venetian ambassador, but nothing of what was discussed there is reported at length. What was acceptable was the Oratorian [?] agrees with the Pope's pretensions; one wishes to see what shall come of it. Since this court has received further documents concerning the affair of Cardinal Alberoni, one trusts that that business will be terminated shortly.

GENOA, 4 January. Some letters from Livorno give hope that trade between this Republic and that city will soon improve.

VENICE, 11 January. News has been received that a ship, laden very richly by our merchants and dispatched to Constantinople, has been taken by a corsair of Tripoli. The letters from Parma say that the Empress is having the fortification works at Cremona strengthened and enlarged, and that meanwhile 6 regiments have assembled to march toward Germany. Although the roads, made impassable by the frequently fallen snow, are absolutely impassable.

Germany and the Neighboring Realms, etc.

VIENNA, 11 January. Reports from Silesia say that violent south-easterly storm winds have caused very great damage there in the country; many thousand large old trees were uprooted from the ground, in the city the church tower was blown away to the bells and many houses damaged. The Emperor has granted to the Turk permission there to demolish the fortifications of Chocim and other places, pursuant to the concluded Peace of Carlowitz; and one eagerly awaits here the answer that the Turk will give thereupon.

GOVERNMENTS.

From the Courier of Essek out of Slavonia, news is reported that Baron von Becker died on the first of this month. Count Dullowitz has had his farewell audience with the Emperor, the Count von Hohenfel taking his place from Dresden there to meet [him]. The Council of War has renewed the orders concerning the reduction that is to take place among the Imperial troops; the Colonels and other Commanders of the regiments are already busy setting each cavalry company at 40 men; however, the Carabineers shall remain at the old number, and each infantry company is to be brought to 60 men; such that the general total shall not fall short, as His K. Majesty is making a considerable reform among his troops, whereby much comes to be foregone. It is assured that they are not careless to maintain the militia in a complete state, and that a fund of 9 million [gulden] has been dedicated to paying the troops regularly: as second to relieve thereby the discharged soldiers of the second [?]; the militia of the Interregnums shall not demand more than hay, straw and quarters, the rest shall be paid from the Imperial treasury. The aforementioned His Imperial Majesty has let it be known that he is displeased about what was mentioned in the public news-papers, namely that the Count of Virmondt had been sent in a certain commission concerning this court to the Elector Palatine, since the said Count had only gone there to settle his private affairs, and had not been entrusted with any commission from this court. It is assured that the report is [false], namely that His Imperial Majesty has recalled the Cardinal of Alban from Rome to be sent thereafter to Naples, since it is said on the contrary that the Cardinal of Schönburg has been entrusted with that commission, with orders to put both ecclesiastical and temporal affairs there on a good footing, and to proceed against the rebels with all severity. It is maintained that the Neapolitans have sent a very humble petition to His Imperial Majesty, in which they express all obedient submission to their Sovereign, at the same time requesting that no new burdens be imposed upon them, since they have contributed much to the support of the Imperial troops there during the war in Italy that has just ended, etc.; which petition His K. Majesty has most graciously received. Since the mild winter season kept many people outside usual shipping, the number of unemployed persons grew so great that many had to be driven away, as some had been reduced to robbery by lack [of means]. Recently through that mob, an attempt was made to undermine the merchant warehouse in the city, which however failed them. Last week various criminals were executed, and others banished. Word has been received that the King of Poland has arrived. The Elector Prince of Saxony has thanked the court and the Empress Mother by letter for the presents sent; one was busy there preparing everything against the holding of Carnival in the valley, which one intended to hold quite splendidly, according to his custom; several great lords had formed companies for this purpose, to appear in orderly fashion; the wife of the Elector Prince won all the hearts of the subjects to her, by her affable intercourse and friendliness toward everyone, and this has notably increased since the birth of the Prince: it is said that the Elector Prince of Saxony might make a trip here in the coming summer. It appears that the Roman court is making new moves for the restitution of Comacchio. Just now the latest instructions have been sent to the ministers who are to appear at the Peace Congress in Aachen; and one is very hopeful whether France will restore to Spain the border fortifications of St. Sebastian and Fontarabia, etc. It is said that in Sicily a considerable treasure should have been discovered, which had been buried in the earth there; concerning which, according to the laws of the land, a decision must be made, since the owner of the land where it was discovered wishes to claim the buried treasure; among it, it is maintained, were many antique gold and silver pieces, which dated from the time of the ancient Roman Emperors.

REGENSBURG, 16 January. News could scarcely be believed here that the Electoral-Mediate Directorate, after what occurred between the Protestant and Roman Catholic colleges on 16 December last concerning the Commissional Decree, should have subjected itself to a second examination and comparison, which however happened last Monday, when the Protestant body was summoned regarding the same matter to appear at the town hall; however none of the Protestants appeared, who then formally protested against such rude proceedings; which caused great agitation among the Roman Catholics, who seemed to be fully supported; but the Protestants assert on the other hand that this affair must be examined by the Emperor and decided according to his findings; so that time will have to show what the outcome of the struggles in this matter will bring forth. In the meantime, the Protestants are arming themselves with all possible precautions against the constant disturbances that the Roman Catholics bring about to vex the Protestants; and one awaits with impatience the holding of the great conference at the Viennese court; in which the remonstrances of the Protestants will be weighed and thereupon a resolution will be taken, which one hopes, notwithstanding the intrigues of the Jesuits beforehand, will be in accordance with the customary clemency and justice of His K. Majesty, whose benevolence and authority is needed by the Protestants more than ever against the Elector Palatine, because from that quarter successive measures bring much disadvantageousness; the Herr von Reeck, delegate of the said body, has been ordered to remain at Heidelberg, since [text cuts off in original transcription]

I cannot complete this translation as requested. After careful examination, I find that the transcription provided is severely corrupted and deteriorated to the point where it cannot be reliably converted into coherent English text.

The issues include:

1. Extensive OCR/transcription errors: The text contains numerous nonsensical word combinations and fragmentary passages that don't form grammatically coherent Dutch sentences (e.g., "bedaan, dat niemand er in de zaken van Religie aan Vreemde gal mag").

2. Severely garbled sections: Large portions of the text appear to be transcription artifacts rather than actual content (e.g., the repetitive passages beginning "Dc di ggnde da de ortenkemft...").

3. Incomplete entries: Many sections trail off or break mid-sentence without clear resolution.

4. Lost structural integrity: While headers and some auction notices are partially legible, the substantive news content is too corrupted to translate accurately.

To produce a reliable translation, you would need: - A retranscription from the original image using more careful OCR correction - Or access to a better-quality facsimile of the original newspaper - Manual verification and correction of the transcription against the source

I recommend verifying the transcription quality before attempting translation.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 24th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 12.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 27th of January, 1721

LEYDSE

PORTUGAL.

LISBON, 24 December. Upon the departure of the Ad 95 that arrived here, we have news from two days out of Bahia, where one still had a report of the Ship that returned from Goa in the year 1719; but rather that the East India trading ship with the merchants ran aground in the Bahia waters in the beginning of May, and while proceeding the aforesaid ship heading to Goa encountered it in good condition. In Bahia there was a great harvest of sugar and tobacco, and a fleet was loaded from there in May; they had no shortage of goods there, but rather some scarcity in flour and provisions.

ENGLAND.

CADIZ, 31 December. It is said that the King has firmly resolved to have all gold and silver, with the last fleet from Spanish West India or otherwise, transported to Seville; whereby it is reckoned that his Majesty will profit some ten millions. Thus: great rejoicing has been kindled over the third victory in Africa against the Moors, who through their fierce resistance have caused many of our superior and inferior officers and common soldiers to be killed; the same remain still in their camp, and [?] the army among the Malachites; of the aforesaid army it is reported that one of the Moorish generals, Signoret, has been taken prisoner, or, as it is, from the Bailie of Coula to ours came [?], observing that as he departed, the Emperor Mulie Ismail had already shut himself in at Meknes through sickness & Co. Daily troops arrive in these roads, which are sent in squadrons to Ceuta. One awaits some order from Madrid regarding the loosening of the flotilla, to determine what will be the output of gold, silver and other goods. Of the 4 ships withdrawn from Cartagena to Vera Cruz, 2 have arrived there, and the 2 others [?], in three of which this Bay has had to garrison the Spanish flagship, though it is hoped that the ship Cleopatra, Captain Machier, coming from [?] de Grace, has not yet been admitted within. On the contrary, Captain Trock has departed for Havana, and some others of that nation as well.

MADRID, 7 January. [Editor's note: The text here refers to a royal ceremony.] The King's council [?] has communicated with their dignity, and thereafter all the mass was performed, during which they presented the customary offerings. That same day their Majesties, together with the Prince of Asturias, betook themselves to their house; where they will remain for some days. The Infante, who has been ill for several months, finds himself now somewhat better. The King has taken back the administration of Finance, as well as the dispatches concerning it, which were administered by the Marquis del Campo Floridis; and Don Fernando Verdes Montenegro has undertaken the general relations office. In place of Don Nicolás Imaude, who has been relieved of that service, his Majesty has appointed Don Juan Milan de Aragon with the title of Marquis, and Don Joseph of Cordova, as well as the Knights Genoiara, as Counselors; and the Regiment of Foot of Valdenaria has been given to Don Antonio Giefaldu. According to letters from the army at Ceuta of the 28th of the previous month, the Moors in the last battle would have lost Jicau-Jey and other great nobles; their position remains meanwhile still in their encampment at Los Castillos; and the people were by then secure among the Moors. Work was still great in [?], many in the field at Ceuta, though the same were still greatly in need of time to complete. These same letters confirm that in the last action, Don Felix y Aragon, Magistrate of the Camp, [?] officers and 65 soldiers were killed, and 311 lesser officers or soldiers were wounded.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 20 January. One sees an extract of the King's Council of State of the 13th of this month, wherein is also agreed the loan for the seeding of [?] liv. per action, in specie on the basis of 54 liv. of gold and 3 liv. of silver Louis on the same February to follow was fixed. The Duke Regent has thus done of the posts of Governor of the Company of the Indies and the Bank to the advantage of the Duke of Bourbon and the Prince of Conti; which Prince with a very handsome equipage was heard on the day the Duke of [?] left his Parliament: being his Highness accompanied by 72 officers; certain particular persons who were present at this ceremony, seeing that he was one of the house of Bouillon, and the privilege existed in the parquet to come forward to make himself recognized as such, was treated as a villain. The meetings that are held every Monday in the palace of the Duke of Bourbon are continued to work on the affairs of the Company of the Indies and the Bank, and also to determine a fair distribution for the stockholders in the actions. The bulls for the great office of the Order of St. Lazarus for the Duke of Chartres have come from Rome here, but it is found that they are 300 ampels more than those of the late [?] de Dangeau. The Benedictines who have separated from the Congregation, and claim upon the papal nominalist [?] not to comply where, have [?] to appeal. The court [?] now for the Constitution and late [?] in such manner that it is held on the 21st [?]: which were condemned by the [?] and the meeting of the Sorbonne, as vigorous Illuminants, will be again held on the first February in all their validities, with the right and exercise of a deliberative voice, and this well by express order of the court. The mother [?] of the Duke of Bourbon [?] with singular splendor. The great consumptions and expenses, excessively made by certain individuals, now begin noticeably to diminish; to the sorrow of the merchants, manufacturers, and shopkeepers; everyone now [cries out?] against the system of Mr. Law, and the sorrowful feeling of which arrangements, if one had only remained by the establishments of the Company of the Indies and had kept to it, would have been more advantageous to the kingdom; but now it is on the contrary very harmful through the violent interference of the banknotes; even to a sum of 460,000 millions or four milliards. It is believed that many of the general tax farmers will be forced to resign, so as to be excused, because they cannot raise themselves; the gentlemen Chevalier, le Gendre and two other gentlemen have presented themselves on the occasion of the banknotes of the [...]. Rents will also be obliged to furnish a great sum, which joint sum would amount to approximately 18 millions; this money being destined for the payment of troops. The Duke of Villeroy was not well pleased that those of Lyon addressed themselves in a certain petition directly to the Duke Regent, without first giving him notice, and showing him their dissatisfaction, has that gentleman very much [?] that, notwithstanding this case, he will nevertheless endeavor to bring all advantage to the merchants of Lyon. A certain Barquier, named Calabin, has made an offer to the court to pay within 8 years' time all the bank holdings, provided that he be allowed to enjoy a privilege, free commerce on Guinea be permitted, and all the domains which one has in the West Indies be ceded to him; from this offer, it is trusted that he would be supported by the chief exchanges in the kingdom. The news from Provence becomes no better; it is said that the evil in Alles grows strong and that the sickness has also come to St. Remy, Terrascoon and Beaucaire.

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 21 January. After it was last Friday resolved in the Lower House that the election of the 13 commissioners to investigate the conduct of the directors of the South Sea Company should take place by lot on Monday, the under and the respected governors of the South Sea Company presented to the House various books and accounts, together with a particular list of the said books and accounts, containing the authority given by the general meeting to the directors to be paid on the fund and capital, and also on the subscriptions: a list of the names of the directors, the original minutes of the committee of the 1st of [?], together with the further papers which the House required the directors to deliver to the same; whereupon the House ordered that the directors should present a statement of the present increase of the capital of the Company, and of the first and second subscription of the annuities, how much the House has still given in actions for the St. John distribution of per cent in capital, and how the holders of the fifth subscription in money respectively according to the order of the general meeting of the Company have been admitted to the said capital; that consequently the directors would present a reckoning of the whole determined sum, and which, according to the present project, with the four subscriptions in money still to be collected; the third moreover how much of the said sum is actually paid, and how much still must be paid; and it is ordered by them that the said directors present to the house a statement how much their fund and capital would be, if one relieved them of the remaining payment in the last subscription.

It has come to light regarding the Directors of the East India Company that 22 of their subscriptions, which amounted to a Million, have not yet been repaid; and that a certain Director's share of half a Million has not been repaid; and that subscriptions held in the name of Mr. de Vroude for 1000 pounds sterling have been elevated to Count status. On the other hand, La Parde, the Viscount Caffielet, having been troubled by sleeping sickness, went to the springs of Arandusie, and having been inconvenienced by sleeping sickness, went to Bath. On this evening, Espellie arrived from Madrid, sent by Colonel Stanhope; from the Court it was reported that in Paris news had been received that on 31 December last, the General [?] had brought the Countess of Westerfeld.

BRUSSELS, 15 January. Since the Clergy have applied themselves to the arrangement of the convening of the two Tribunals, Brother and [?] have in this way arranged that on Tuesday afternoon they began to allow it, and it is stated, in full [?] the Coat has passed through, so that for the year 1722 it is a settled matter. That Dilieghem is to be invested within a few days, at which Ceremony the Cardinal Archbishop of Mechelen, together with several other Prelates, will probably be present.

AMSTERDAM, 23 January. With letters of 23 November, via Livorno, news has been received from Algiers, that no Dutch prizes were taken there, and that the 3 French warships, having grounded on the Fourth with the Dey's [?], having grounded on the Fourth, the ship Boy Fietélie has been transferred from Havre de Grace to Bordeaux.

The Imperial Minister, the Count of Windsgrats, has requested his return from Kamchatka. On the 2nd [?] the Honourable Pryt, yesterday with several gentlemen of the Government. An expedition has been passed to the British Court.

Masters and Captains of the great ships, which one proposes to the Honourable Directors on the 13 following [?], at Breda the Knights Trip's, the Lord Melanchton, Braderia, Hatchshaw, D.Fij.Fentelli, Wordy Montague, Windsor, Lycsmmere, Slipre, Strangnear, and the General [?]. After which the House the first Reading of the [?] on the Directors of the [East India] Company, the matter of preventing withdrawal from the Kingdom, and after the House for well 8 hours debated the approval of one of the Projects to restore public Credit, that of the Honourable Walpole was approved with 260 against 142 votes. Several days ago, the Archbishop of Canterbury and several other Bishops approved the examination, and His Majesty, concerning various religious Liberties that are openly sold, and the King asked that these be prevented, has instructed that His Majesty has ordered that his two Ministers should order the Authorities of said Books to be summoned and account made thereof. It is assured that among the discoveries of the meetings [?].

Since several Persons have fled with certain Lieutenancies, Discharges, [?], and [?] of Holland, so each one authorized should send the same to the Noble Gentlemen Commissioners appointed as Council.

The Gentlemen Burgomasters and Directors of the Navigation Company, having occasion [?], that the establishment of a [?] should be furnished in the said Company; their Noble [?] have declared [to] postpone the establishment until further notification. Notice is given to all lovers of such works, particularly the Booksellers, to subscribe to the aforesaid Work, called Lexicon Hieroglyphicum Sacro-Profanum, &c., by Marfinus Knautig in Folio, 6 volumes, adorned with curious copper plates; note that said book costs 40 Guilders on small paper and 3 Guilders large paper; since the Work is printed, not less than for 48 Guilders small paper and for 64 [?] half, every one is warned to send his money before that time to Juannies van Broasou, to receive a certificate for that time, or can [receive it], according to the delivered [?]. And to further Green every one is asked that the Delegates from the aforesaid [?] in the said and Captain of the said [?]; also will be shown afterward where of further Knowledge [?] the Writer [?].

Jan Hoes, Jan Riedemaaker and Daniel d'Azevedo Brokers shall at Amsterdam at the [?] hours, in the Dykstraat in the Oude Burg, sell a party extra fine new [?] of the Honourable Elias and Manuel de Coelho Junior.

Foster Budlon Auction seller in The Hague shall on 17 March and following days be Advocate for the Justices of the Justices in Holland &c., consisting of beautiful [items] the Catalogues in all the aforesaid Cities to be obtained shall be.

Jan vander Streng Broker presents to sell or rent a magnificent [house] at the Village Sudenheim on the High Road, consisting of a fine House, Coach house &c., provided with fine [gardens], Orchard, Vegetable garden, and more [?] the [?] I [?] the great [?] a morgen and the Land also 4 morgen.

Jan vander Streng Broker presents to sell a Farm estate with its [?], situated on the Utrecht Trekvaart, between Abkow and Baambrug; [?] small [?] way; very in the morgen; as also yet another Farm estate with its [?] the [?] of Weelep, large 16 morgen [?] but still a morgen [?] afore. [?], over the Uytermeerfe Sconce; these Parcels shall be [?] At Leiden by Christian Vermey is printed and to be obtained, as also in the other Bookshops, and also Holland in the Netherlands at various Places, Economy, [?], and other Cities, with the surrounding Villages and the old Abbey of the Counts of Holland, [?], lightinge and beginning of Churches and Cloisters. At Amsterdam by Wilelant [?] the [?] street, between the Kaisers and [?], new Fabrics of Chamber tapestries are made and sold, binding Scottish Colours, durable Grounds and many different Patterns; of which all chambers are hung with [?], but themselves at [?] Royalties are for all everyday Prices to be obtained.

At Leiden printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedstraat. 27 January 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The EXCHANGE RATES in Amsterdam are 33.6, compared to 32.9 to 10. Rotterdam 11.10 to 11. Antwerp 11.10. Hamburg 33. Paris and Bordeaux 33. Bilbao 50. Lisbon 42 and one quarter. Cadiz 41 and one third. Seville 116. Bilbao 50. Lisbon 42 and one quarter. Cadiz 41 and one third. Seville 116. Bilbao 46 and one half. Lisbon 53 and one half. Breslau 3 and 2 sixteenths St. Hamburg 32 and 3 [?] to 11 sixteenths St. Antwerp 2 per Cent. Ghent 2. Brussels 2 and... AMSTERDAM, 8 January. The ship of Captain Roelofse from here bound for Madeira and Curaçao has arrived at Madeira; at Bordeaux the ship the Elias, and at Nantes Brabdbt; both from here for Madeira and Curaçao has arrived at Madeira; at Bordeaux the ship the Elias, and at Nantes Leducbt; both from here bound for Madeira Matron Sitozzaal, from here to Rouen, and on that River it is said 200 men, a ferryman from Rotterdam. Due to the stormy weather, large ships were driven from the Bay of Bayonne, and it is said 200 men were lost, entering Fontarabia or Biscay. In the Sound the following ships: Gerrit Blom, formerly President, from here to Copenhagen, and Jan Daniëlle Eggets, with salt from Borneuf bound for Danzig, as well as in the Mass Corn. Brouwet from Bordeaux. The EXCHANGE RATES in the aforesaid [ports]: Madrid 33 and 3 thirds and Caillery. Sevilles 116. Bilbao 50. Lisbon 42 and one quarter. Venice 50.3 to 30 and one half. Livorno 59 and one quarter. Genoa 91. Geneva 93 and one half. Paris 43. Brussels 13 and one sixteenth St. Frankfurt coronade 3 and Reich's taler. Germany and 3 sixteenths St. Antwerp 2 per Cent. Ghent 2. Brussels 2 and...

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 27th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 13.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 29th of January, 1721
Germany & Adjoining Kingdoms, Etc

COLOGNE, 13 January. This morning around 7 o'clock, a severe storm of thunder and lightning, wind, rain and snow arose here, by which the windows in St. Stephanus Church were greatly shattered, because a particularly hard flash struck in that Church much [damage], which however caused no damage, but rather a considerable hole therein. The Courier, who is to bring news from the [? Friends] to Poland through this City, has indeed passed through this City, but was not able to reach the Court, because the [courier] himself held the situation here uncertain. The aforementioned Courier has nevertheless not been able to refrain from reporting the great joy that was manifested in Rome regarding this birth. The Emperor has appointed the Imperial Court Councillor Count von Tügglien [?] a troop at the Court of the Riding Master under General Bonneval, Commandant of Elick. Today the Imperial Court Dame, the Countess of Ulm, married the Count of Het-flein [?], who previously served the victorious Imperial Majesty and victorious Widow. The Imperial Ministers continue to hold daily meetings regarding the Remonstrances of the Protestant Corps at Regensburg, and concerning 200 [matters] one has obliged a certain Parish favorably to respond, and thereupon act as though they were attacking the Emperor's authority therein, while the others judge the contrary ought not to be among the more formal procedures, to cause no further disturbances.

REGENSBURG, 20 January. The Evangelical Corps has held various conferences for the past 4 days regarding the documents that were submitted from the Palatinate, and it is reported that, although His Electoral Highness Düsseldorf and its Ministers made this known to him, he would prefer to see that it is not the Electoral Kingdom and appointed [officials]; but rather, as His Electoral Highness submitted, to return to Regensburg; one did not wish however at that Court to allow him to handle his particular instructions in detail, and to respond to His K.M. [?] [Editor's note: K.M. likely refers to "Kaiserliche Majestät" (Imperial Majesty)], as the Evangelical Corps cannot escape the concerns and formalities regarding the Treaty of Westphalia to that of Baden, otherwise than as the Corps to the Corps, which found by a Kypher-putze [?], as they have already shown in their Remonstrance to His K.M. Furthermore, the Dr. of Reck has drawn nearer, [and] the Evangelical Corps [has] given complete Information of all that concerning religious matters comes to its knowledge. The last two Despatches are dated from Heidelberg, and report that no Members of the Electoral Circle dare to come to see him, concerning the Royal prohibition, so that they will have to content themselves with the changes which the Elector Palatine may see fit to make, without being permitted to complain, if the same may be contrary to the Emperor's wishes. The learned Protestant General [bodies] have among their deliberations, [made] common Relations to be made of the information which they shall receive from time to time from their Plenipotentiaries, in order to receive all instructions against their [?]; and the same were presented last Friday for the third time by the Electoral Mentte Directorium [?], to appear for debate over the Committee-Deduret [?] in the Assembly, but they have remained at home today; and since it appears that the Electoral Ministers will not permit this to pass through, one is curious to see what will come of this. P.S. For several days, new grievances against the Roman [Catholics] have again been sent to the Protestant Corps, which will be presented and examined at the first Assembly.

Wednesday's Courant.

[The text becomes heavily corrupted and difficult to parse in the latter section. The surviving portions discuss matters of religious propriety, distinctions in proper usage, references to Imperial Diet procedures from 1531 under Emperor Charles V [Editor's note: Charles V reigned 1519-1556], matters of the Peace of Westphalia and the Treaty of Baden, grievances concerning religious freedoms, and what appears to be detailed legal arguments regarding the rights of Electors, Princes, and Estates to protect themselves and their territories. The text references specific articles from peace treaties and discusses repetitions [Repetisalien], remedies under law, and the Emperor's authority and responsibilities regarding religious matters within his domains.]

To be continued herein after.

Great Britain

LONDON, 14 January. On Tuesday, the Deputy Governor of the South Sea Company delivered the accounts and papers to the House of Commons, along with a list of the findings. The Minister of the Rolls then presented a Bill to remove the Commissioners of the South Sea Company from the Kingdom within one year, before the end of the current Parliament; the first reading was given and the second postponed to Tuesday. The Attorney General presented a Bill to enforce quarantine measures, which was likewise read and will be considered a second time tomorrow. The Parliament then received a report from the grand Committee regarding the propositions concerning the South Sea Company, the Bank, and the East India Company, noting that restoration of public credit would greatly contribute to recovery. Considering the great importance of this matter, it was referred back to the grand Committee for further examination. To this end, Mr. Clepper spoke against the project, arguing that public credit would be diminished rather than restored by it; nevertheless, the grand Committee's resolution was approved and it was ordered that a Bill be brought in. The same day, the conduct of the Directors of the South Sea Company was also considered, and while several Lords offered fair arguments, others spoke with greater vehemence not only against the Directors themselves but also against all those who had been complicit in fraudulent dealings and corruption, and whose actions had impaired the credit of the Nation and ruined countless families. They added that in their opinion these offenders should be punished severely and exemplarily. Thereupon it was proposed that this inquiry be conducted by a Committee of their Lordships, which was agreed to and ordered to proceed on Thursday last. After some vigorous debates regarding whether the Commissioners had the authority to appear jointly with the Directors and whether they had received proper authority from Parliament, this was resolved by a vote of 65 to 28. Pursuant to the foregoing, it was ordered that next Thursday all Directors who are not Members of the House Commons shall appear with their titles and secretaries before the Bar, and their Lordships, the Bishop of Bangor having ordered the 3rd of this month [O.S.] for this hearing, adjourned until next Thursday. On Wednesday evening, the House was informed that the Committee examining the Directors' conduct would meet that evening at five o'clock in the Speaker's Chamber, with a membership of five. They were authorized to spend up to 800 pounds to prepare their report to the House, and empowered to make such requests and demand such papers as they should deem necessary. It was also ordered that all papers of the South Sea Company be readied and delivered to the Committee. Thereafter the second reading was held of the Bill to enforce quarantine measures and to prevent the Directors from leaving the Kingdom. On Friday, the sub-Governor and Deputy Governor and Directors of the South Sea Company presented a Request to the House, asking to be heard before the Bill preventing them from leaving the Kingdom within one year should pass; this was laid upon the Table. The same day, the Lords administered an oath to the Directors, binding them to answer truthfully to all points put to them. The House then in grand Committee examined them at length and concluded that the Directors were responsible for all losses incurred through the misallocation of capital; that they were found to have violated the trust placed in them; that any person who should conceal or falsify such matters shall be guilty of felony and a reward shall be given to whoever discovers such fraud; that in the copies of their books and papers, which had been ordered to be laid open for the Lords' inspection, they had caused various matters to be omitted, and that they had expressly instructed others what had occurred and what was to be omitted. When the third inquiry was made, some answered that this was done to spare their Lordships trouble. Whereupon the Lord Chancellor sharply reprimanded their disobedience to the House's orders, and it was commanded that the original minute-books of the various Committees of the Treasury accounts be brought before the Lords. From this important matter various discussions arose. The Lords have heard an appeal and adjourned until next Monday. The Commons in grand Committee have reviewed the Bill preventing the Directors from leaving the Kingdom and have added a clause regarding each Director.

Italy

ROME, 11 January. Word has been received from the Emperor that proceedings are being made for the election of a Pope from His Holiness's family, along with His sons; the Pope approaches, and he was received in the city council, the Moors [?] having also received compliments from Cardinal Ausarwitz [?]. This caused His Holiness to have a splendid celebration on the Barbary [occasions?]. Bells rang in all the Spanish churches and cannons were fired; fireworks were displayed in the manner of Spain; three rows of torches flamed in the squares; several wines from Messina arrived; a grand display was seen upon whose top a papal banner was visible, with rays shining upon the Moors and Barbarians, destroyed by lions and the Piedmonteer's infantry, for the duration of his position in the Palace—the difficulty remained of 2000 Scudi for the evening—the Pope and mornings saw six difficulties in matters, the nearest parties of Mechlin to oppose this through the change of his wife's residence near Piombino, where the Royal Constable Colonna has the Ambassador made his visits also to the Cardinal of Germany and neighbors.

France

PARIS, 14 December. It is said that the attendance of the nobility and the worthy citizens at the King's ballet was very great; Sayne and several Comedians performed before His Majesty. On that day the Marquis [?], Envoy of the Landgrave of Hesse, had an audience with the King, wherein he made known to His Majesty the marriage between Prince Maximilian, son of the aforementioned Landgrave of Hesse, and the Princess of Usenburg. The Dowager Duchess of Mayenne often visits the King, where she is well received. The Dowager Duchess of Orleans is recovering. Two Edicts of the King and his States are seen; one from the 29th December of the previous year, concerning the payment arrangements in banknotes of the Pantissin in the King's States, for the years preceding 1720. Here two from the 31st concerning the ancient Donations and [?] monies. The facility of living beings, regarding the piercing foresight in the foregoing mentioned, caused yesterday morning a report that the Faubourg St. Honoré should burn; no harm has been reported. Letters from Terrasson of the 3rd of this month say that a certain cordial much used there for the plague-sickness is used, in the same month together with other preparations, and it is applied to the blood-spotted body, and it is said that the most afflicted people there have been cured with it. This remedy was given by a Durètic Soldard, who long remained in Moscow and cured many people with it, and his help has been given.

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 27 January. Word has been received here from Rome that the Empress was brought to bed on the 29th December with a Son, who was baptized that same evening and named Carl Stuard, Casimir, Lodovick, Philippus, and Sylvester.

AMSTERDAM, 27 January. The ships, in the fleet from Lisbon to Bahia, which departed and have arrived there, are the Saint John Baptist, Captain Antonio Louis Branco, and the Saint Joseph Teyzeyra, which have arrived in Bahia in good condition from the ship N.S. do Cabo, which must proceed to Goa. The names of the two remaining ships of the four sailing from the Cartagena towards Vera Cruz are la Urkania and la Incarnation, Captain Adriaan Pinheiro. To see and in the Library well displayed yet; however, regarding where the other ship Saint Vincent, Captain Joseph Nunes, remained, no certain report can be made. According to letters of the 12th December from Cadiz, they were occupied there loading the ships, which in the month of March will depart for Gallepons towards Cartagena. From Cadiz had departed the ketty, Captain Arthur Tucker towards London, William Knegh towards Cologne, Captain Truck towards Havre de Grace, and still remain lying in Hamburg, 38 English, 26 French, 7 Holland and 4 Danish ships. At Vera Cruz there are eighteen, and twelve still remain to return, the Hanover Galley and the French ship of Captain Hubert towards [?], the Sara Galley towards Lisbon, the Laurence, the Venetian Galley, and the Hupton, Captain Willm and others. According to letters of the 11th of last month, in two days no one died of the Pest there.

THE HAGUE, 21 January. Count Tauoccia, Ambassador of the King of Portugal, was yesterday with several gentlemen of the government, and it is understood has delivered a Memorandum to the States General. Yesterday and today the Provincial Minister was with several gentlemen of the government. It is understood that Herr Hamel Bruininks, former Envoy on behalf of the States to the Imperial Court, has shortly been charged with the interests and intentions of the Lotteries, to collect [?] at the Office, or [?] shall not be expedient, but rather losses of the 4th through the 5th class may only be regulated until the first of February; whereby the aforementioned fifth class shall, the broker Filip Piek at Amsterdam begin to sell in Silk, half-Silk and Woolen fabrics, English manufacturers, Chik de Hoey, 200 by the full class, as a parcel of Lofts, certain jewels and all of [?] for. Lodging to be seen. shall begin in March, and on Tuesday the [?] March 'Stamo-[?] or... [text becomes increasingly corrupt and unclear]

VIENNA, 19 January. [The transcription becomes too corrupt in the original to provide reliable translation of this section.]

REGENSBURG, 23 January. [The transcription becomes too corrupt to provide reliable translation.]

The agio of the Bank against cash [?]. The agio of the East India Company are 8.00. [?] 1123.

The 29th of january, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 14.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 31st of January, 1721
Italy

ROME, 11 January. Last Sunday morning, a solemn Te Deum was sung in the Church of the Capuchins to celebrate the birth of a son to the President, who was present there along with Cardinal Gualtieri. On Monday, the Feast of Kings, the papal government proceeded with the Cardinals present here to the Mille dei Campi. A service was held there, after which Don Antonio Colonna, Knight of the Golden Fleece of the Church and of the Queen of Spain, presented the Papal Troops, and His Holiness in the name of the Catholic King presented the Standard, the Moorish flag of Ceuta delivered by the Cardinal. On this occasion the Knight, who had been received by the household of Cardinal Aquaviva, made a fine speech of compliments; the Standard being received by the Pope, His Holiness made a heartfelt address concerning the victories that the Catholic King had won in Barbary. After this the Te Deum was again sung, accompanied by a triple cannon salute from the Castel Sant'Angelo. That same morning the Te Deum was sung in all the Spanish churches; in the evening the triple salute from the cannon at Castel Sant'Angelo was repeated; and throughout the city one saw illuminations and joyful fires, particularly in the Plaza of Spain, where the Royal Palace was lit with numerous wax torches and flaming candles in every window and a fountain continuously threw forth wine; there a fireworks display was presented, depicting a steep and difficult mountain to climb; at its summit stood an allegorical figure of Fame with the arms of Spain, and at its foot one saw Jupiter hurling his thunderbolt against Moorish women, who, joined with the Barbaric land by the thunderbolt, were overturned by lions. On Wednesday the President went in person to give the Pope notice of the birth of his son; and His Holiness then granted to the Royal Congregation that during his stay in the Palazzo Albaniense an allocation of 2000 scudi be made to furnish that Palace; and that evening the Pope also sent fine gifts to the mother of the child, presented in a box with two pieces of damask with gold thread and fine lace, which His Holiness had sent through the Cardinal Archbishop of Urbino as a gift. In the afternoon the President, along with all the Cardinals, Princes and Princesses who had been present at the blessing of his wife, paid their respects, and began these visits with the Princesses Palestrina and Piombino, who received him royally. That same morning one saw in the Colonna household the birth of a daughter, who was baptized the next day in the Church of the Apostles. The Venetian Ambassador began his visits with a very magnificent display, and the Cardinals Barbarigo and Spinola continued their visits in the Sacred College.

Germany & the Reichstag [Editor'S Note: the Holy Roman Empire'S Governing Body]

VIENNA, 18 January. In the reports that the Emperor has received from Regensburg, His Majesty has been informed of the continued difficult withdrawal of the Protestant Ministry from the Council meetings, as the Imperial Commissioners are very much involved in consultation among themselves. In the attached letter it has been reported that the matter has greatly displeased His Majesty, so that His Majesty has instructed his Ministers and governing officials to inquire of the Protestant Ministers whether they did this on their own initiative or under orders from their Princes, and that his Ministers should not only remain absent from the meeting but should even depart from Augsburg. The Imperial General War Commissioner, the Count of Tierenheim, has left here for Pressburg, and the Royal Bohemian court has been set in order; it is said that for the Bohemian Diet, which will be held, measures will be taken in the Netherlands, for which the necessary Estates have already decided, and this will soon be done in the territories of Mantua, Milan, Naples, Calabria, and Sicily as well. It is assured that the Emperor has decided to maintain all his regiments, particularly the two under his direct command, and thus to discharge not a single man; and in this way reports from France suggest that at that court it was also decided to maintain all the troops, of which it is said 15,000 men will come into the Liaise region.

REGENSBURG, 23 January. [Editor's note: This section concerns the bitter disputes between Catholic and Protestant states within the Holy Roman Empire, particularly regarding grievances (Gravamina) presented before the Imperial Diet. The text discusses allegations of persecution of Protestants, especially in the Rhineland, and debates over restitution of church properties and rights.]

Since the Protestant contingent withdrew on the third summons from the Elector-Palatine's Directorate, the Roman Catholic Ministers claimed that the Imperial Diet could not proceed by an Imperial Declaration, and the representations made by the aforementioned Protestant delegation will sound very strange here. Nevertheless, the Protestant contingent made known to the Roman Catholic delegation through a strong conference statement that in the provisions for half-day sessions and the petition to the Elector-Palatine's court, to grant him new instructions, it was reported that some Ministers had objected to appearing, and that His Majesty would wish that he would have some communication with the Protestant side, since such a prohibition would be contrary [to justice]; but the appointed plenipotentiaries had need of approval and would venture to submit that outside the Palatinate, since he was there to provide for the concerns of the Protestant contingent, and the previous arrangements would not hinder him in this. It is said that in the Camera at Wetzlar recently a delegation from the Palatinate and the Bishopric of Speyer presented [grievances]; secondly, that the injuries [to Protestants] were tolerable, the damages and losses were bearable, and thus the grievances cannot be removed by the other [party] in the manner of medieval honors, but rather (as was proposed before) that the Evangelical faith would be entirely eradicated and then, claiming that restitution to the Evangelical Church could not take place because there were none remaining...

The 31st of january, 1721. — With Privilege.
