No. 1721.No. 15.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 3rd of February, 1721
Portugal

LISBON, 31 December. Since the Count of Ribeyta has arrived here from Paris and Madrid, it is believed that the work of the company to be established will be greatly promoted. The Infante Don Antonio has returned to court from the hunt, which he held near Fischeiro and Palma, and has brought down a great many large animals. The Paracardinal [?] and Pillar, the Oidor of Moncarmel, was ordained on Sunday in the Patriarchal Church by the Patriarch, who was assisted by the Archbishop of Lacedemon and the Bishop of Fernambuco; and [he was installed as] Bishop of Guado Para, which ceremony was attended by their Majesties, the Infantes, the principal nobility, and the religious from all the communities. At the meeting of the First Academy of Ecclesiastical and Historical Studies, a second conference was held, in which the Marquis de Femsefey presided as Director of the Society; the laws to be observed in the said treatise were also presented and approved; thereupon the Count of Eticeyra distributed to all the members of the Academy the two types of histories, to be composed in Latin and Portuguese; and he praised the King's zeal for the protection of learning in poetry, exalting it to the highest degree.

The Indies

CADIZ, 6 January. Due to favorable weather, progress has been made with the loading of the ships from Vera Cruz and the advice ship from Cartagena, such that this will be completed within a day or two; as for the private merchants, although they still fear the loss of the silver and goods here, they eagerly await the ships from Recife [?], which will not arrive for some time; as well as the ship of Captain Ignacio de Noi [?] from Vera Cruz, and that of Vice-Admiral Antonio Serano from Havana. Information has been communicated regarding the fitting out of the ships that will depart in May as galleons toward Cartagena. From our forces before Ceuta, it is reported that the Moors were so strongly positioned in their encampment at Cassillos that further attempts after the apostasy would cost much blood.

MADRID, 14 January. The King, the Queen, and the Prince remain at El Pardo, where they frequently amuse themselves with hunting; however, their Majesties are expected here within a few days. On the Thursday last, the 9th of this month, His Majesty, through the Marquis Grimaldo, Secretary of the universal departments, dismissed Don Miguel Duran from his service as Secretary of War, of the Indies, and of Naval Affairs, though His Majesty has nonetheless given him a place in the Council of the Indies with a salary of 2,000 pesos. The King has appointed in his place the Marquis Castellis, named Intendant in the commission of naval affairs; much good is expected from this change. His Majesty has appointed the Lord Depe-des as Intendant of the Indies and Marine. [Editor's note: The text here appears corrupted in the original] Those of that department who were appointed have also been dismissed. The conferences with Colonel Stanhope continue daily, but what is discussed is kept confidential. For some days, no dispatches have been received from the Moroccan court or from Algiers, which causes some uneasiness, as one knows that the Moroccans are a volatile people. Private letters from Cadiz report that there was great controversy there, as some hoped that the arrangement of the Marquis de Leede would have been reconsidered, and they planned to attack the said Marquis for the fourth time. It is said that the marriage alliance of a Bavarian Prince is being negotiated here, to bring about some robberies [?] to a wedding.

France

PARIS, 27 January. Last Thursday a courier arrived from Vienna at this court, who delivered a packet of letters from the Emperor to the Duke Regent; however, little has yet been learned of the contents. His Royal Highness has for some days sent much money to various towns in the surrounding region for the payment of the militia, who are pressing hard for funds. For two or three days there was talk that a detachment of 15 men from the company of the French Guards might be sent to [...] in Provence, in order to completely cut off all communication with that province, as the plague is raging there with great fury. The Cardinal of Polignac has visited the Duchesse du Maine since his arrival, and made her several visits. The indisposition of the Archbishop of Cambrai continues to persist. For some days the prohibition against transporting war munitions and wagons from here to the Rhineland has been renewed in all the ports of the kingdom. Recently, several persons from Vilours, who had been at the lodging of the Dutch Ambassador in Caen, have been arrested and imprisoned in the Châtelet. From Caen it is reported that Italians there have put on a comedy that made sharp remarks upon the University, and that the University, foreseeing greater discontent, brought the authors of the comedy before the bench of its Rector, who, instead of students losing their studies, ruled that His Majesty's subjects had insulted their society, and that such presumption would be so impudent as to merit complete annulment and some further punishment.

Great Britain

LONDON, 28 January. When the House of Commons last Friday was in grand committee working on the bill to secure the under and deputy governors, the directors, treasurers, etc. of the South Sea Company, the said committee was ordered by the House to insert therein a clause by which all confiscations of real and personal goods shall remain at the disposition and determination of Parliament; after which the House in grand committee returned, went through the bill, and made some amendments; the report thereof was set for Monday, and the House adjourned until the following day. The King has ordered that all the directors of the South Sea Company who hold any office, whether in the Treasury or otherwise depending on His Majesty, shall be removed from their positions; in consequence whereof have been dismissed: Sir Harcourt, Master and Receiver General of this city; Thomas Reynold, Commissioner of the Office for the Victualling of the Ship Fleets; Arthur Ingram, one of the Commissioners in the Office of the Salt Tax; and Francis Hayes, Receiver of Customs. On Saturday a petition was presented by those of the Levant Company to the House of Commons, with a request that some of their members or their advocates be heard against certain clauses inserted in the bill for changing the laws to require ships coming from infected places to perform quarantine more strictly, before that bill might pass; which petition was referred to the consideration of the grand committee, with an order that the members of that company would be heard as deemed necessary; the House in grand committee returned; made some progress in the said bill, after some members of the said Levant Company had been heard; and the House resolved to postpone further consideration of the said bill until Tuesday, after which it adjourned until Monday. On Monday, Horatio Walpole, on behalf of the committee, reported to the House upon the bill to prevent the under and deputy governors, the directors, and treasurer, etc. of the South Sea Company from leaving the kingdom; it was approved, and the House ordered to put in neat form the amendments made therein; the House further ordered the committee, appointed to examine all the actions which the said directors have taken in execution of the act passed in the last session of Parliament to appoint a secret committee; then the calling together of the members of this House was postponed eight days, and on the following day work proceeded on the bill for taxing the incomes of lands. This day the House of Commons completed going through the bill regarding quarantine and the report thereof was set for tomorrow. [Editor's note: The text becomes increasingly difficult here] The Lords likewise ordered a bill to be brought in to render the under and deputy governors, together with the directors of the South Sea Company, incapable of directing any of the three companies, namely the Bank, the East India or South Sea companies; whereupon their Lordships ordered that Tuesday be appointed for the appearance of Mr. Webster the cashier, and Mr. Powell, clerk of the Treasury; together with Mr. Wegreandfeill, Mr. Tella, Strowd, Owen, Shaw, and Elias Faz, a Jew, who have been particularly employed by the directors to buy and sell their stocks and subscriptions, and they shall all bring their books to be examined; after which the Lords adjourned until Wednesday. The committee of examination met on Saturday at 5 o'clock in the house of the South Sea Company, when it adjourned until 7 o'clock and after having gone to the Puntak tavern, they returned to the said house and sat from 7 until 11 at night, during which time it is understood they examined Sir John Follows, Mr. Joye, under and deputy governors, and also Sir John Pont, one of the directors, and that they were required to sign the examination and bind themselves under oath not to reveal anything of the questions or answers; the said committee again met in the aforementioned South Sea house for further examination of various accounts. There has been drawn up a list of the lands which the directors of the South Sea Company have possessed, amounting to [?] per annum in income; etc. men are in hopes of discovering still more outside the capitals which they possess in the three companies. In the office of the South Sea Company duplicates have been made for the subscriptions and for the long and short annuities, to be brought into the Exchequer and exchanged in the capital of the said South Sea Company. The said directors have announced by advertisement that a special general meeting will be held in the office of the company on the [?], which will begin at [?] o'clock and end in the evening at [?] o'clock, in which meeting they will deliberate on the election of a governor [and] deputy governor in the said government, which on the [?] by advice declared that the company had forever forfeited all its privileges, and that it would forever be separated from the body of the universities. The prices of Bank notes remain equally poor; and it is thought that no considerable change is to be expected in them for the present. One again sees two articles of the King's Council of State: one of the [?] of this month concerns salaries, increases of salaries, and supplementary pensions. The other of the 23rd of this month, ordered that the receipts made by the directors of the mint before the publication of the edict of September 1719, and even those given for the accounting and bank of the tenth, shall be converted into perpetual rents on the aids and gabelles.

HAMBURG, 13 January. According to letters from Berlin, a prominent gentleman from France has arrived there, whose name and character, as well as the reason for his arrival, are not yet known. We note the opportunity of the winter season; work continues on the construction of the Royal Palace [Editor's note: likely referring to the Berlin Palace]. It is even thought that the King, the Queen, and the Crown Prince will spend the coming summer there. The situation regarding the Dutch General Count von Hempslch remains uncertain; the aforementioned Count has made a shipment of good wares to the French warehouse there, and given a sum toward the construction of the new French Church. Last Saturday the Baron von [?]

Director Copius hereby notifies those interested in the investigation of the shipwreck of the Klipde, and certifies that this has been concluded; however, the Klipde on the 16th. The Directors of the Navigation, Commerce Company, etc. of the City of Monnikdam hereby advertise all those who have neglected thus far to deliver their paid-in installments, and order them to do so by 13 February 1721. Likewise, payment may be made in their Actions at 4 per cent in cash (not Actions otherwise, and the balance in cash or with Actions at 4 per cent in cash; furthermore notice is hereby given for the aforementioned 15 February, that Actions must be registered in one's own name; this must be done or distributed, for the benefit of those interested, which remains committed.

The provisional commissioners committed to the affairs of the Company hereby notify that they, given the winter season and other inconveniences, have determined to extend the first delivery period to the end of April 1721; those who will supply [?] shall be guaranteed.

Jacob Magaus, broker, will sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, 3 February a good quantity of Sergies, Carlayen, Bayen, striped Flannels, Crownralls, and blond Calamancks, Etamlines, Sergies du Bois, Diptincees, Poplins, and other goods; the sale day from 8 o'clock in the morning to 4 o'clock in the afternoon at the aforementioned location. [?] Zomne, broker, will sell in Amsterdam on Tuesday, 11 February in the townhouse, fine paper goods and some good paintings, left by Pimenti and [?], Iron Caggel, and other goods; everything may be viewed the day before: Schellinger, broker, will on Wednesday, 12 February 1721 in Amsterdam sell Bonds and Lottery Effects, [?] and various General and Holland Lottery items up to 8 feet high; 1000 Palm trees from 2 to 6 feet; a quantity of Beech trees from 2 to 4 feet; 1000 young Lindens; 1000 [?]

It is announced for Saturday, 22 February 1721 at 5 o'clock in the evening, in the [?] belonging thereto, the aforementioned Noble Court estate, [?], at the aforementioned location, over the outermost fortification; these parcels will be sold. [Broker ?] intends to sell or lease a magnificent estate at the Village of Stielienum on the Main Road, consisting of a fine Gentleman's House, Coach House, etc., provided with fine Alleys, Tree gardens, Market gardens, and the beautiful [?] Court estate is approximately 4 acres, and the Land also 4 acres.

It is announced for Saturday, 22 February 1721 at 5 o'clock in the evening, [details regarding] the aforementioned Noble Court estate, [?] at the aforementioned location. The orchard, of which the mature trees are included; all around enclosed in its lands and [?], a standing Apple tree and meadows of the Court estate, similarly approximately 3 acres; lying and located on the Court estate, [one piece of] Land in the Low-land Polder under Koudekerk; furthermore 19 acres of similar good [property] and [?] of the quality of Holland.

BRUSSELS, 20 January. The Knight van Damme, brother of [?] from Diligignies, having been discovered in his position in France, has arrived here, requesting that he be granted justice and that his company in the Regiment of the Prince of Hollheyn Naiburg be restored to him; however, the Government has decided to place him in Fort Morette, until one receives orders regarding this matter; in the meantime, an examination of his conduct shall proceed, concerning the accusations of ongoing intelligence with the Ministers of Spain, though one holds him innocent in this regard. Last Sunday the Inquisition of the Abbot of Dillighem was carried out by the Cardinal Archbishop of Mechelen. The Count of Zwijnd, the Ambassador of France accredited here to the Court of Sweden, has arrived here, whose reception by the Marquis de Prie was accomplished at dinner, and he will depart for Paris shortly thereafter.

AMSTERDAM, 1 February. Today the Burgomasters of this City have elected the Ed. Ge. Agth. Messrs., the Gentlemen [?] thereof, Mr. Miguel, Freiherr van Wefterdykhorn, etc., as former Burgomasters; Mr. Jan Trip, Lord of Berkenrode; Mr. J. van de Coll; Mr. M. the Gentlemen Dirk vander Meer; Mr. Jan Hulft; Mr. Jen de Witt; Mr. Joh. Rondewulf van Collen; Mr. Pieter Kalcterom; Francois van Hemstede; [?] and Abraham Boddens; To Boddens have been appointed [?] and [?] Govkveld van Dinkelerken and Jacob Jabe van Embden; to Nantes, the St. Pieter Wilk, the [?] from the Maas; to Libourne, Paulus Ulrich van Bremen, [who arrived] from 17 January, 12 hours, towards Hans Breyden, and a good [?] has departed House Stiokherth, from Hamburg toward Cadiz; to Libourne, Hasmus Schut van Lubeck, and in other lands Laureus Geldens, from that city Hans Winkelman van Karelk[?], from Bijra hither has lain in Noosewegen, thereabouts Pieter Linlingen in a good state. The Ship, 7 miles from Danzig, has remained, named the St. Pieter Hendrik Vanzegeman. In [?] within three [?] from Nantes and Loyse Bolch van Bourdeaux; out to sea, the Fortune Frédrik Liland toward Kurfau; Simon Arendfz. Shipper Corambaneau; Shipper Colombeau toward France [being required].

The Rate of EXCHANGE today is: Madrid 91½. Cadix 119¼. Seville 116. Bilbao 90½. Lisbon 42¼. Venice 81 to 81¼. Livorno 89¼ to 90¼. Genoa 93 to 92¼. Geneva 93¼. Paris at sight 44¼. London 12 Shillings 7 to 8. Frankfurt current 29¼ Reichs. Danzig 250 Polish groats. Jelgau [?]¼ [?]. Hamburg 31 and 3 [?] St. Antwerp. 2 per Cent. Brest 1¾. Brussels 3¼. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 3 [?]. The Agio of the Bank 3¼.

THE HAGUE, 2 February. The Gentlemen Commissioners of the Wharf for the Admiralty, who were nearly [?], held an extraordinary meeting this afternoon, as well as the Gentlemen Deputies of the State finances. Request was made for the Finance Minister from Brussels to be brought back here this week. Mr. Volmiet appeared at the meeting of Their High Mightinesses.

HANOVER, 23 January. Today sad news arises here from Utrecht that the young Prince on the 22nd of this month has died, to the great sorrow of the entire Court there.

[Lands in negotiation being Lorraine, as the details of the Lord and the City of Monnikdam, with communication from the Gentlemen Burgomasters, the loaned parties are to be collected (according to the content of the [?] afternoon extraordinarily met, and with the Gentlemen Deputies of the States at the Comptoir, which conference shall be resumed tomorrow. It is assured that the Fiscal Nenny is expected back here from Brussels this week. Mr. Volmet appeared at the meeting of the High Mightinesses.]

HANOVER, 28 January. Today sad news arises here from [?] that the newly born Prince on the 2nd of [?] has died, to the great sorrow of the entire Court there.

[Further text regarding Lorraine and the Chapel, instructions regarding the Town of Monnikdam regarding paid parties and Actions, with notification regarding those intending to pay 2 per cent in Actions...]

[Auction notice for 9 February 1721 at 5 o'clock in the evening, in the Old Gentleman's Lodge, Greenhouses, and various other goods...]

[Auction notice for 11 February 1721 on the Canal by the Housework, by Jan Werden of Timoth and Hendrick van Streek...]

[Sale of approximately 10,000 Taxus Trees, and Palmhagen from 2 to [?] feet; 1000 young Spruces, 1000 May-Beeches, 600 Arbor Vitæ from [?] feet; 600 Hazelnut Stems, together with young Lindens; 1000 three-year-old Thorns...]

[Property listings for the City of Leyden, named the Hard Vloegh, with various descriptions of lands, houses, stalls for horses...]

[Further property descriptions with measurements in acres and details of improvements...]

Printed at Leyden by DE KLOPPER, on the Biecdelstraat, February 1721; With Privilege.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 3rd of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 16.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 5th of February, 1721
West Indies

SURINAME, November 6th. On the 31st of August, our Governor, Mr. Coctier, after being ill for several days, passed away; and his body was buried with full ceremony. Within 10 weeks, the following ships have arrived here from Texel, namely: the Queen Elisabeth, Captain Cardens; the Alfert Grunda [?], Captain Caritena; the Justouw Maria, Captain Henrie Ciephlo; the great Egidius, Captain Klaas Baker; the Muller, Captain Hendrik Blonk; the Barbara, Captain Hend. Kok; the Eendracht, Captain Kasper Janslie Mulder; Captain Adriaen Thomasse Smith; and Captain Cormis Schoon. These 200 [?] voyages encountered neither pirates nor storms; and as everything here is in good condition, it is believed that they will successfully be dispatched to [?] in March.

Inland Russia

PETERSBURG, January 13th. Prince Misersky, who had been here on commission to the Swedish Legation, has recently returned within several days as an officer, who it is said carries important commissions regarding the affairs between Sweden and this Court.

Poland-Prussia, Etc

WARSAW, January 20th. General Field Marshal Count von Flemming, who had departed on royal command, has unexpectedly returned and remains in attendance on the King's pleasure. Both deputy Chancellors, together with the nominated Folion, remain here; however, the latter are preparing for their journey to Great Poland.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, January 22nd. On Saturday, General Major Diemer arrived here as envoy from the Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel, who sought a private audience with the Queen; notably, the aforementioned envoy has not yet received a visit from the imperial ministers residing here, as is customarily done; but rather he has been frequently visited by the court, with hopes of speedy resolution. News has been received that the Czar is working with all effort on preparations for the forthcoming campaign. It is reported that the King will depart Sweden before the end of this month, and probably in the spring will attend the Diet.

Germany & Adjacent Realms, Etc

VIENNA, January 22nd. These days, by imperial order, it has been publicly proclaimed by trumpet that throughout the entire Lenten season, masquerades, musical assemblies, and balls are forbidden. His Imperial Majesty has received the remonstrances submitted by the Protestant Corps regarding the fact that the suspected contagion has not yet been expelled from the city.

VIENNA, January 25th. These days, by imperial order announced by trumpet, throughout the entire Lenten season the holding of masquerades, musical assemblies, and balls is forbidden. His Imperial Majesty has granted comfort through the Board [?], more favorably received by the Corps; and it is assured that the Emperor will return the mentioned remonstrances unanswered, to demonstrate thereby his care for the aforementioned Corps. The Court has laid aside the heavy mourning for the Emperor's mother and has assumed court mourning for six weeks. This past Monday, the young Councillor of Altheim assumed his office and the [?] Order. That same day the magistracy of this city and the Rector Magnificus of this university were honored in the customary manner, after which a free oration was delivered by the outgoing rector. For several days, rumor has circulated here that the Elector Prince of Bavaria may soon come, to continue his gracious marriage with the aforementioned Josephine [?]; and it is added that, should the bloodline continue, the government of the East Indian Netherlands might be entrusted to the Elector of Bavaria, under the condition that the aforesaid States would grant said position to the Elector Prince as his son; however, this requires confirmation.

DRESDEN, January 25th. The Court is in great grief over the sorrowful death of the young Prince, who died during the night at the age of 9 weeks and one day; preparations are already being made for the necessary arrangements; and on account of this death, all the entertainments of Carnival have been postponed for some time. For several days, various ships with cargo from Bohemia have arrived here, which has caused great joy among the people; this circumstance now hopes to diminish somewhat.

REGENSBURG, January 25th. The consistorial and joint assembly held by the Protestant Corps regarding religious matters in the Palatinate has been of such effect that, after previous deliberation, a petition was made by two elected representatives regarding the harsh proceedings which His Electoral Highness the Elector Palatine has undertaken against his Protestant subjects, that they should not forthwith obey the execution according to their religion, whether the same be complained of, and the community shall lodge complaints or make representations; one of the community memoranda was submitted by a minister of the Protestant Corps to the imperial principal commissioners, to be sent to an embassy [?], so that the aforementioned Elector may be urged by His Majesty to revoke such introduced laws, conflicting with the imperial constitution, and to restore them, so that the Protestant [?] may be able to address themselves in good order to the princes and electors, guardians of the Westphalian [?]; the other memorandum has been sent by post to the Palatinate to be presented by the Elector, and to exert all efforts to obtain the consent of the wise principals; where one must await the outcome: it is also said that a copy of this memorandum has been delivered to the attending electoral princely ministers. The royal-imperial envoy gives notice here at the imperial diet that the Elector his master has already begun to have the grievances of his Protestant subjects collected, in order that one may also have the Emperor's resolution upon all lands and sustenance, so that each might regulate himself accordingly; this accords with various letters which the attending envoys from there had received at the same time: it is hoped that the Elector Palatine will follow this example. The magistracy of this city, as a further precaution against the pestilential sickness, has ordered the gate at the great bridge on the coming Thursday to be closed, posting some day laborers thereupon to compel all foreigners to enter through the great gates of the city, so that they may be properly examined. The Protestant colleges have been summoned for the fourth time by the Roman Episcopal court to assemble for the examination of the previously issued imperial commission decree, but no one appeared: what shall come of this must be left to time.

### Regarding the Remonstrances of the Protestants

And to what extent they have proceeded, wielded, with arrests, imprisonments, reprisals, and other force of fact, without regard to the high imperial courts, and have thereby made a beginning against the evangelical testimony, the religious grievances of the evangelicals provide convincing proof [see appendix numbers 1, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 23, 24, 25, 26, 36, 38, 39, 40, 41, 45, and 46, with their appendices]; even as the Elector of Mainz, without regard to the known Camera Mandata and proceeding by force of fact, in the Cologne territories has acted as if in enemy land, just as also in December 1698 the then imperial court master, the Prince of Salm, when he sought to play the incorrect "compelle intrare" with the troops then under his command against the evangelical corps, the evangelical corps said that they should not regard this badly, and from neglect, that one sadly! in present times, from the often feeble justice, in the high imperial tribunals, even in clear cases one might hope for little relief. It is also known how that in the lower empire also, even among the Roman Catholics themselves, when they have something to dispute with one another, it is not unusual that the governments of the lands and revenues are taken into account until the matters are brought into agreement, as has happened between the Elector of Cologne in the ecclesiastical territories of Jülich and Berg: similarly, also for some time the Elector Palatine, when he had a violent dispute with the city of Cologne over the staple right, arrested the ships of the said city and made use of the same, by which they fared just as well as the evangelicals at present.

With much more right, Elector Brandenburg, when the House of Neuenburg, after the cessation of the Westphalian peace, began to oppress the evangelicals in Jülich and Berg, used against the Roman Catholics in Cleves and elsewhere [?] and reprisals, which at that time were confirmed by His Imperial Majesty and the Empire, and thereby the grievances were successfully redressed.

In the treaty regarding religion and ecclesiastical matters in the duchies of Jülich, Cleves, and Berg, between the Electors Brandenburg and Palatinate-Neuenburg, signed on April 26, 1672 at Cologne on the Spree, and on July 20, 1673 at Düsseldorf, it is expressly conditioned, as may be seen [by extract of article X, under letter F]: that if henceforth any contravention of the treaty should occur, the party maintaining [?] shall employ such retaliations, until the matter is properly redressed by the Empire, and that no one shall consider this an unlawful means; in accordance with which, it is now a [crime?], and the proceeding of the highest imperial authority, or indeed an unlawful violation of the [jura majestatis] was not considered; such things to do, reprisals or retortions, especially in religious matters and customs, would certainly [?] the said considerable house [would have made itself] guilty of punishment, and the imperial fiscal officers would not have rested, but indeed the execution would have been put into effect.

The Westphalian instrument of peace also carries in it the clear and explicit ordinances that such [agreements?] in religious matters, which have already been made or may still arise, not only be confirmed, but be of such force and [power] as if they had been enforced word for word in the named instrument of peace; and flows thence [with a certain logical consequence] that such a well-known and privileged imperial and circle [modus reformandi] in no way be held for anything right and becoming, much less for anything criminal and other harsh proceedings; especially as all the aforesaid efforts put into effect in matters of justice and counsel, and instructions made to the presiding courts for this purpose, have been unable to produce fruit; by which even some of the sent counselors and ministers have had to retire, in order to save themselves and their estates, and to maintain the dignity of their high principals.

Under the rule of the renowned Emperors Leopold and Joseph [Editor's note: Leopold I (r. 1658-1705) and Joseph I (r. 1705-1711)], it was also not taken up by the high circle examination office of the Saxon circle, nor referred to the godly prince and princely House of Brunswick-Lüneburg as a crime, nor to them as a violation of the high imperial [jura majestatis], when they in the year 1705 took up the grievances of the evangelical estates in the Hildesheim territory, and also showed in the still existing document of the high circle examination office, that such reprisals are becoming, founded on the principles of the Westphalian peace, and are to be allowed to all previously aggrieved parties, and what is more, which [text breaks off]

I appreciate the request, but I must be honest about the limitations here. The transcription you've provided appears to be severely corrupted or degraded - it contains numerous garbled passages, repetitions, incomplete words, and sections that don't form coherent Dutch sentences. This goes well beyond typical OCR errors from blackletter text.

For example: - "SCHULZE gedagte huize Kapels, ke Majesteyten zich in 266 valle hebben laten vervallen..." is incoherent - Large sections repeat fragmented phrases with no clear meaning - Many words appear to be corrupted beyond recognition - The text dissolves into near-gibberish in several passages

To proceed properly, I would need: 1. A clearer or re-scanned transcription of the original 2. Clarification on which passages are uncertain (marked [?]) versus which are corrupted 3. Access to the original document to verify doubtful sections

Would you be able to provide a cleaner transcription, or would you like me to translate only the clearly legible portions?

Course of Exchange

While the document clearly *is* a 1721 Dutch newspaper (Leydse Courant, printed by Felix de Klop in Leiden on February 5, 1721), with recognizable section headers (FRANCE, THE NETHERLANDS, ENGLAND, SWEDEN, ITALY) and some readable passages about Amsterdam exchange rates, Brussels military matters, and shipping news, I cannot in good faith produce a full English translation of garbled source material.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 5th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 17.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of February, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, January 8th. Since the 30th of last month, two English warships have arrived in this Bay: the Fortune, Richard Steffens, and the Newcastle, Eduard Valkinham; further the ship St. Philippus, Viden, and the Ceuta, Captain Henry, and the Constant Fortune, Captain Grafines from Genoa, Eftevan Velden from New England, Richard Gull from Gibraltar; and departing are Piet. Colomiege, John Mery and George Moun to London, Guillielmo Lando to England, Thom. Limbery and the crowned Galley to Bedford, Dirk Noland to Ireland, Ellevan Cavanajg to Warefort, the Magdalena to Buenos Aires, all English, and Ellevan Danhelmaar to Barcelona; so that there still remain lying a Hamburger, a Genoese, 26 English, 27 French and 3 Danish ships.

England

PETERSBURG, January 13th. Several couriers, who had been detained by the Cossacks, have brought to His Czarist Majesty the confirmation of the concluded eternal Peace with the Ottoman Porte.

Sweden

GEFLE, January 13th. The joy regarding the concluded Peace with the Kings of Sweden, Great Britain, Denmark and England was not so extinguished as great, as on the Thanksgiving and Joy Day held over it, most of the inhabitants of the surrounding Swedish regions and villages betook themselves to this city to attend the joy celebrations, through which this city was first provided with more people than ever before. In the morning before the sermon, not only several hundred shots were fired from the cannon that stood on the ramparts and city carriages, but also from the artillery at Frederick Schantz and various other places besides, among which were several whole and half cartridges, which were saluted by various units of the militia that were arranged in order: meanwhile one heard a continual sound of drums and trumpets among all: splendid harmonies on waldhorns: and a very pleasant mixture of voices, which were accompanied by all manner of instruments. After the conclusion of the sermon all of this took place in the presence of the principal gentlemen and officers, various times: in the second instance there was opportunity given for the lighting of a great fireworks display, which was placed opposite the iron and metal scales, and occupying go [?] lengths and eighty in breadth, which fireworks display, when lit and under the direction of the artillery personnel, was executed with such extraordinary cost and beauty as no person had ever seen before; for among all the varieties of fireworks that were sent off and ignited therein, there was an excessive multitude of fire arrows, of which a great number weighed 6 pounds: during the operation of this craft-machine one maintained a musical accompaniment of the previous fashion, mixed with the repeated sound of drums and trumpets, as well as the alternating harmony of the remaining voices and instruments, and altogether everything lasted in such wonderful order until 10 o'clock: beyond the aforesaid joy celebrations, all the houses were also illuminated and for the greater part decorated with splendid allegorical images and divisions, whereby the light was as if the whole sky, both within and around the city, had been converted into a single flame of fire, or a second Lima [?] had arisen. Among all these remarkable occurrences, very many splendid feasts were held, and it was not until 7 o'clock the next morning that people, in joy and gladness, parted from one another and retired to rest: but since the unavoidable roar of so many heavy cannon and other gunfire was scarcely felt in any part of the entire city, the joy was doubled, in that no person was harmed or any accident occurred, whereby everything was concluded by all to the utmost satisfaction.

STOCKHOLM, January 22nd. A courier from Copenhagen has arrived, who brings us word of the shipwreck guarantee concerning the French Crown that had arrived there, and which was subsequently evacuated as it now stood in danger.

GOTHENBURG, January 23rd. As the weather drifts on with excessive storm, no ships dare venture to sea, for which reason those of Fredrik Olden and Jacob Jurgens, etc., still all remain lying on this river and the shipyard. One finds here no people who have known such a winter as this; and since up to now no snow has fallen nor any ice in the rivers, all the meadows can still maintain the appearance of it being winter season, instead of it being nearly over (c 2 y).

Italy

NAPLES, January 14th. The servants of the Nunciature seized the Chaplain of a Dupri Regiment, because he had been in an osterly house, and brought him to confinement, over which General Caraffa expressed his great displeasure to the Nuncio, on account of the large troops outside the walls where the Nunciature is; thereupon the Nuncio released the aforementioned Chaplain back to his regiment through the servants of the Nunciature, but General Caraffa was still not satisfied, had the servants arrested and imprisoned, over which the Nuncio addressed himself to the Viceroy to request their release.

ROME, January 25th. Last Tuesday, Cardinal Altalli, Dean of the Sacred College, died at the age of 66 years, and his body was brought the same morning with the ceremony customary for deceased Deans to the Church of the Franciscans in this city, where he was buried in the grave before the church. It is generally believed that Cardinal Tanara, being the oldest Cardinal Bishop, will succeed him as Dean; but since Cardinals Ottini and del Giudice believe themselves entitled to this dignity, the Pope has appointed an extraordinary congregation to examine their claim before he proceeds to the nomination. Wednesday morning the congregation of the Holy Office lasted long, because the Mandate of the Cardinal of Noailles regarding the Constitution Unigenitus was examined there; but since it was treated with so much extreme bitterness, nothing could be ascertained from what was decided.

The Lord Patti, Secretary of the Congregation Buon Governo, died Thursday; and it is believed that he will be succeeded by the Lord Corsini. This morning a pontifical chapel was held on account of the feast of Saint Peter's Chair. The Lord Aldobrandini, Nuncio in Spain, has made known to the canons of St. Maria Maggiore that the King of Spain wishes to maintain his right of protection over that church, to which his Catholic Majesty will furnish them with a fund in Spain, to draw the revenues that they had obtained in Sicily.

GENOA, January 18th. Yesterday letters were received here from Madrid with news that at Cadiz 3 ships from Buenos Aires had arrived, whose cargo was estimated at 12 to 13 million, beyond the present that the King of Siam had sent to His Majesty; and with these ships confirmation had come that the fleet under Admiral Chacon had arrived safely in the West Indies.

MILAN, January 19th. The King of Sardinia, being discontented that this Government had forbidden trade in Piedmont, has taken away from a monastery an income of 10,000 scudi, which the monastery of Pavis in the Lomellinae possessed, and given it to another in Turin; and it is greatly feared that His Majesty will cause an edict to be published, by which all these properties, which possess goods in his territory, must come to dwell there, if they wish to enjoy the revenues thereof.

VENICE, January 25th. According to advices from Verona, the regiments of foot of Guido and Maximilian von Staremberg have marched toward Germany, whither the 2 regiments at Pavia of Newburgh and Lubcowitz have also taken their march through the Grisons. From Brescia comes news that the heads of the families Martinengo and Petrasso have been sent on high orders, the first to the fortress of Ossinuovi, and the other to the fortress of Felcheria; to remain there until the long-standing differences of these houses shall be terminated. Day before yesterday the Lord Law arrived here with his son, and keeps himself incognito under the name of the Knight du Jardin. [Editor's note: John Law, the Scottish financier involved in the Mississippi Bubble scheme] It is said that Cardinal Alberoni is also keeping himself incognito in a convent here. Recently the prominent Jews here also held a large banquet.

Germany & the Adjacent Kingdoms, Etc

VIENNA, January 25th. Wednesday evening came by express news that the sister of the reigning Empress, the wife of the Archduke Ferdinand Albert, Governor of Komorra and Colonel of an imperial regiment of foot, on the 1st of this month, and the wife of the aforementioned Archduke's brother on the 2nd of this month were delivered in bed of a prince, which news, since this court has been bereft of blessings, caused both sorrow and joy. Today one receives news from Mannheim that the Palatine's daughter, daughter of the Kurfürstin, has been brought to bed [of?].

The Palatine envoy resident here, the Lord von Frank, who has been raised to baronial rank, had audience yesterday with the Emperor, at which time he presented the credentials regarding his mission to His Imperial Majesty. With an express from Venice here, one has learned, among other things, that there a splendid banquet of 2 million was held at a prominent Jew's. The Archduchess Amalia finds herself somewhat indisposed. One is here in general pleased with the imperial decree that no masquerades or balls, but only the forthcoming weddings, may be held.

REGENSBURG, January 30th. It is learned that the high imperial principal commissioner and the minister of the Elector Palatine have sent an express to their courts with memorials given to them for this purpose by the Protestant corps; in which memorials that corps strongly complains about the sharp interdict published in the Palatinate, the contents of which were mentioned in the previous [dispatch]. One understands that the Lord von Reck on the 23rd of this week had audience with the Elector Palatine, received by better hope than before; since the power of the ministry so possesses His Electoral Highness that he does nothing but approve and confirm. The Vice-Chancellor had also returned from Heidelberg to that court and had reported on the examination of the Protestants who had spoken with the Lord von Reck; he thereby threatened that action would be taken against their boldness and disobedience [to] the Elector Palatine's ordinance, and it is feared that an adverse decision will be taken against those good people; meanwhile the Protestant corps takes all possible measures to protect them against all persecution, and will hold another confidential meeting over this. Yesterday an express from Count Coljet from Constantinople passed through here toward Holland.

France

PARIS, January 31st. Letters from the Crown of the 3rd report that daily from the harbors along the coast, ships loaded with grain, oats and other provisions are departing, and that at the aforementioned harbor there are collecting palace and transport ships, the number of which is already great, must alone wait heaped up at sea and freight from Bilbao, to convey this supply to Cadiz. Besides, still 11 ships, in which one [text breaks off incomplete]

Left Column

Director Copes advertises to the citizens of the countryside, and by order of the Board of Management, that certain properties will be auctioned on Tuesday afternoon, the 11th of February 1721, including land at Noordwijk [?] comprising 24 morning plots, which are presently being sold by Messrs. I. D. Zomer, broker, and will be sold at Uitdam on Tuesday the 11th of February. A fine house, good papers and several fine paintings, remnants of Simeon Jaquine, Tete Kogel, and other goods; all this by the aforementioned broker. On Wednesday the 22nd of February 1721, at Amsterdam, obligations and lottery effects from various General States and Holland.

On the 12th of February 1721, in the evening at 8 o'clock, there will be sold at Noordwijk [?] a fine house, furnished with many rooms and outbuildings, garden, stables, etc. By the brokers Jan and Willem Haring, of Amsterdam. On Friday [?] from Jacob Lemmigh, 18 Blacke Grond, sold on Tuesday the 11th of February. Bales of brown goods, situated in the Nes in the Blacke Grond, by the brokers Cornelis van Nes and Jan van de Sieng, of Amsterdam. Properties of South [?], at the Elf of Monte. Simon Raimond, a fine half house, together with white marble [?], children's items, wardrobe pieces, many antiquities; also fine [?] by the aforementioned brokers.

Elias de Matros, broker at Amsterdam, will sell on Thursday the 20th, at the Ketzen Krone, a pair of fine jewels, pastilles and round diamonds, and a fine flat [?], the day before the sale, in the morning at 8 o'clock.

There is offered for private sale a large, sturdy, well-appointed house under the Linden trees opposite the Swan, furnished with 2 fine appointed salons opening onto a large palm courtyard, along with 2 spacious kitchens, a fine entrance hall, on the side of the house a spacious entrance for coaches and horses, [?] a fine playhouse, viewing through glass windows onto the entire courtyard and through into the kitchen, wine cellar and scullery; behind it an extraordinary garden, 90 feet long in front with the most beautiful fruit trees and at the end again a fine fruit garden with fruit paths bordered by excellent hedges, of which compartments one is planted with apricots and nectarines; seeing altogether surrounded by yew and elder trees; where the aforementioned property Mr. Silvester van Antse presently resides.

By Stedouwer and Uytwest, book merchants at Amsterdam at the Rockin near the Three Pipes [?]. Sarendip, in 12mo, with figures; as well as the Voyage [?] 12, 7 volumes with plates. Etat present de l'Espagne by Mr. l'Abbe de Vayrac, 12mo, newly available from Messrs. Addison and Steele, second volume. Annotations upon various Collisions, durable foundations and many difficult passages; of which several rooms are hung with engravings, but even at the king's hangings are [?] for all manner of prices to be obtained.

On the 18th of this month, Monday the 22nd of February 1721, in the evening at 8 o'clock, there will be publicly shown and sold the elegant house and estate, with all the appurtenances belonging thereto, consisting of the fine estate house, [?], in a beautiful, well-appointed, covered [?], new fine dwelling farmyards, one of which has apricots of the exquisite family fruits fish-ponds; all around enclosed in its lanes and walks of ascending yew; a gilded garden of the lord, [?], over 7 morning plots; standing and situated on the Rhine, under the lordship of Koudekerk; also [?] morning adjacent to fine property, lying the first good land in the Lagewaardie polder under Koudekerk, in the county of Holland.

Right Column

ORLEANS, the 18th [January]. Through many friends Monsieur George, [?] had made the compliment, asked now if [?] in better thoughts than two good [?] and to the convenience [?]; the sick friend gave [?] of his testimony [?] that the Canon Georg of the Apple [?] still of St. Aignan, who [?] to my Uncle have you well verified [?] is ride that they a [?] Belief [?] at the Church [?] could be [?] until now to friendship science their single [?] Apple of the Constitution, [?] and on the Bishop [?] the conclusions of the joint testimony of all the French [?] the Rector, and over the [?] concerning an insulting matter through [?] or walls and curtains of the [?] University, that they there 200 well [?] and coming under their will be in particular forbidden in degrees in [?] of the [?]

PARIS, the 2nd of February. [?] made, to come of the [?] Volant at Rochelle. The Duke Roger given, and the Duke of [?] bread; but other dukes [?] decision taken: these [?] one appoints commissioner [?] all [?] in modified manner Patrimonial goods [?] services and other essential [?] for the first of July 1719; noted as a double [?]; and the attire of [?], placed; but one also finds this commission [?] finally [?] by the weighing word in the general is very much [?] the Mississippi Company [?] not yet complete, position [?] full-heartedly; buying [?] [?]. The Mister Van [?] a commission pleased [?] [?] a very fine and [?] given mild charity [?] at Rennes: The Naze through [?] who [?] [?] [?] disclosed on [?] of a commission pleased [?] holding [?] very well [?] Pensionaries. The Mister of the [?]; it is said that [?] this held there well [?] [?] well-turned persons [?] [?] during the [?] Fort toward St. Michael [?] is toward St. Malo [?] ships, at Boulogne [?] [?], which one [?]

PARIS, the 3rd of February. [?] mentioned [?] green [?] [?]; and [?] of [?], which the reading of Moorish rights, to [?] ancient place [?] some days after [?] and very numerous assemblies. [?] long deliberation granted [?] [?] [?] and [?], because they [?] interest of [?] bound, the Court has [?] what [?] [?] [?] [?] again [?] [?] [?] the other [?] of the doctors [?] [?] [?] [?] [?]

BY DE KLOPPER, on Breedstraat. The 7th of February 1721, With Privilege.

The 7th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 10th of February, 1721
France

ORLEANS, January 28. Yesterday our Bishop, accompanied by many priests and friars in ceremony, proceeded to the house of Monsieur George, a Canon of St. Croix, to visit him in his illness. After the Bishop had paid his respects, he asked the good religious woman whether he might not recover better by abandoning the wild way he had followed until now, and give glory to Heaven by returning to the said religious order, from which he had withdrawn himself. To this question the sick man gave no answer; whereupon the Bishop, grounded upon the principle that silence gives consent, wished to draw up a deed of his assent, so that the Canon would accept the Constitution and his named Appeal might be upheld. However, Mr. Buchart, the sick man's nephew and chaplain of St. Aignan, who happened to be present there, immediately said to him: "It is time that you declare yourself and give an upright testimony of your Faith." Upon which the sick man, in clearer voice, declared that his Priesthood had been served with all honor, and that his Conscience did not reproach him regarding any matter concerning the Appeal of the Constitution; and that he desired to die in that same faith. Upon this the Bishop departed in great confusion.

One sees printed here the Conclusions of the University of Caen, which, with the agreement of all the Faculties, has declared the Jesuits, by reason of their disobedience toward the order, the Mandate of the Rector, and the insults and insolences toward the University, to be excluded and deprived of the right and privilege of the Academy; ordering the University that they shall be separated therefrom, both they and their College and Schoolers, and that all henceforth shall not study there; and moreover each Faculty in particular is forbidden in future to confer upon any of them any Degree who shall have studied under them; further declaring the Jesuits in general forbidden the privileges granted to them by the Act of October 25, 1768.

PARIS, February 2. The King on Thursday afternoon began to learn from Monsieur Gondrin the playing with racket and shuttlecock, in order thereby to arrive at that exercise in the court. The Duke Regent has banished 1,000 [?], and the Duke of Bouffon himself those whom he possessed; toward the Duke of Antin he has taken no firm decision yet. This week 3,200 banished soldiers are being enlisted; and Commissioners are appointed to examine the rest and all the verifications, excepting those which shall appear serious. When the Duke Regent spoke to him about it, His Royal Highness advised him to show patience. Meanwhile the Police Lieutenant pursues his course, and has condemned the Duke to take the merchandise and cause his Magazines of Woad, Soap and other Spiceries to be taken; saying that he acts in the quality of Merchant; whereupon he appeals to the Parliament, and this matter will likely be of much concern to the Duke. His friends say he is ruined and now forced to engage in this business. The Resolution of the Council of Regency of the 20th of the previous month concerning the Arrangement of Paper Estates, both Banknotes as well as Stocks, is not yet known; this publication is hindered by two reasons: the first is that Parliament is unwilling to register it; the second that they wish to make few Arrangements to the advantage of Foreigners who hold them; one will find Paper Estates burdened, because the Ministers of the Emperor and other Powers solicit in favor of their Subjects, to obtain right and payment for their Paper Estates. There will be Offices established where all the Papers shall be examined, and those rejected which shall be found not properly signed or [?], and many of the placed ones, and these Procedures will encounter considerable obstacles. Meanwhile those whose means consist solely in Paper, which number is very great, are on the point of falling into misery. One also learns that in the various Council meetings held the past week, a reduction of all monetary species was resolved against [?] of this month, but the same has now been rejected. Also therefrom the Bank and the Company of the Indies shall be renewed; but upon the offering of a certain Sum by the Directors of the last Company to the Government, that was approved on Friday; the Stocks of the Company are now sold not for Banknotes but for constant Money. Saturday they were at two Seals 90, and new in the afternoon to 125, and a private letter that day of 1500 Livres in Gold in Stocks; regarding the same of the Banknotes (of which scarcely anything more is spoken), one sees nevertheless a Regulation approaching; and although the Watch applies everything to maintain order regarding the Bank, it is nonetheless driven there and in the public coffeehouses. One assures us that the Congress at Cambrai is to be held shortly; the Ministers Plenipotentiary of Foreign Princes, who are still here, prepare themselves to depart thither at the earliest, and the Count Corfini, Minister of the Grand Duke of Tuscany, shall undertake the journey thither this very week. The Knight Stair, Minister of the King of Great Britain, has already had the greatest part of his Equipment packed, but one assures us that he will remain here until the Ministers of this Court themselves shall be there.

One finds that the gold of the Gold Snuffboxes in the Mint of this City amounts to over 1,200, besides [text cuts off].

PARIS, February 2. The letters from Madrid of the 27th of the previous month give us account of the Battle mentioned beforehand; and those from Ceuta of the 3rd, say only that the Marquis de Leede of the Moors, whom a part of his Troops caused to fight at that place, is now better thereof. Some days ago, by virtue of a Lettre de Cachet, an exile and very numerous meetings in the Sorbonne were held, which after very long deliberation came to an end under a decision; the purpose of these meetings of the 12 Doctors was to restore them to their former position, who were previously removed from the Faculty because they too zealously advocated the Initial of Peace and the Constitution; there came a Lettre de Cachet to recall them [?], of which one cannot penetrate the reason, but one trusted that [text cuts off].

Great Britain

LONDON, February 4. Friday a Petition was presented to the King by those of the Lower House, wherein His Majesty was requested to give order that the Papers of the Officer over the Haven of Rye, which were authenticated by the King's order, might be laid open before the House. Whereupon the named and deputized Governors of the South Sea Company delivered the following Accounts and Papers to the House, namely a Supplement of the Money lent upon the Stocks above the Account of December 29 last given, an Account of the use made of the Money received for the Christmas distribution, and paid by the said Company in Obligations to Particulars; a report of the reasons that moved them to accept the 3rd and 4th Subscriptions in Money at 1,000 per 100, with the reasons that induced them to declare the Christmas distribution of 300 per 100, and the following 12 years not less than 50 per 100, together with the Calculations upon which they grounded their decision; an Account of the Stocks purchased for the service of the Company, and the Prices and times when; and the House, having read the reasons by which the Directors resolved to accept the 3rd and 4th Subscription at 1,000 per 100, as well as the reasons which induced them to declare the said distributions, ordered this named Accounts and Papers to be referred to the third Committee. The said House in grand Committee proceeded, and passed through the Land Tax Bill and made therein some alterations, of which the report was set for Monday. Saturday the Lords held the second Reading of the Bill of the Commons, to relieve the Directors &c. from going abroad of the Kingdom, and having laid it in Committee they read a Request from the Directors of the South Sea Company, wherein they craved by their Advocates to be heard upon the Bill, which shall burden them overseas for years &c., which Request after long Debate was rejected. Besides the before-mentioned Clauses named in that Bill, there was added thereto that to the knowledge of other Persons it shall be suspected whether any good of the Directors &c. shall be withheld or detained, or whether they shall be accountable or answerable for such Goods, or to the extent of their commanded being, and after expiration of [text cuts off].

Left Column

ENGLAND

Tulips are being traded urgently; those engaged in such trading shall forfeit all their goods and chattels, and the proceeds may be brought [to the Crown]: that anyone who holds any of their goods in trust, and does not declare them before 2 June next to the Barons of the Exchequer through deliberate concealment, shall forfeit everything and remain imprisoned for one year; and they receive from the Commissioners the first Bill thereof.

That same day the Commons passed the first Bill, declaring that the deputy Governor and the Directors of the South Sea Company [Editor's note: The South Sea Company, which had recently collapsed in 1720] are incapable of holding similar offices and a second [charge regarding] in one of the Company's positions; and the second having been honored, and they again the Bill to punish mutineers and deserters more severely, and it went into Committee for a period of ten days; upon which they passed the Quarantine Bill. That following day, the Lord's: [uncertain section]. The Commons then gave the second reading of the Bill of the Lords; declaring the deputy Governor and the Directors of the South Sea Company incapable of serving in one of the third Companies; and it went into Committee: after which Mr. Farret reported regarding the Land Tax Bill, that a deleted portion was approved in the old [form], the entire [matter] being postponed to another time: Thereupon Mr. Pengelly reported regarding the secret Committee, that Robert Knight, Cashier of the South Sea Company, had made himself scarce since Saturday, after being examined a third time by the Committee; whereupon it was unanimously resolved to present 2 Addresses to the King, one to request His Majesty to issue a Proclamation to guard against the aforesaid Knight leaving the realm, and the other to order the Ports and Sea Towns secured so that the aforesaid Knight cannot escape there; whereupon it was ordered that the Knights Chaplain and Lieutenants should maintain themselves in their respective places: after which the Comptroller reported that the King had given his orders, in accordance with the requests of the House: then it was ordered that the Gentlemen Trayley, Humphreton, and Clayton should seize all the papers of the aforesaid Knight, and also those of the under-Treasurer Surmond, and the Bookkeeper Grigsby; and it was also ordered that the aforesaid Surmond and Grigsby be placed under the custody of a Sergeant of the Guard: subsequently it was ordered that the Gentlemen Roll and Stoper examine the papers of the Gentlemen Trayley [and] Caswell; and also that Mr. Blund, Knight Lambert, and Mr. Fellows, deputy Governor, be placed under the custody of a Sergeant of the Guard; and it was unanimously resolved that the Gentlemen Sawbridge and Knight Theodore Janllen, Members of the Lower House and Directors of the South Sea Company, are culpable of a necessary breach of public credit and trust, and thereby have injured a very great number of the King's subjects and done great damage to public confidence; they were therefore expelled from the House, and placed in the custody of a Sergeant of the Guard; further it was ordered that Knight Chapin and Francis Evles present themselves before Saturday next in their seats in the House; after which the calling of the roll of Members was postponed for 8 days, and the House adjourned. Today the Members gave the first reading of the Quarantine Bill, and from the Brokers they received the Accounts of the Shares and Subscriptions, recorded and subsequently [submitted] to the Officers of the Treasury and the Exchequer; and beyond that compared with the other Brokers ordered a certain Song on the [?] further Family, in [?]. Today this is Cashier of the South Sea Company [?] were the Officers [?] strictly charged with [?] himself over Sea wishes [?] to interrupt; he [?] of knowledge to give [?] strictly charged him [?] to the end according [?] to [?] on the [?] clear [?] His Majesty [?] discover and for [?] [?] [?] a premium

Germany

HAMBURG, 29 January. [Concerns regarding] the disturbances in Braunschweig [unclear passage regarding necessary ordinances that the City [?] shall be laid, which now against would [?] had been shown they [?] in the coming [?] there [?] His Highness [?] but himself in Person [?] his interests [?] in Cassel for [?] which through [?]]

Amsterdam

Regarding silver ('t which to the owners has not yet been distributed) to the Mint &c., it is to be brought; against which the Consulate of Seville [?] had yet sent its Remonstrances to the Court, and thereupon received answer; also one still does not know what the [?] will be. With letters of the 22nd past, via England, one has tidings from Liverpool, that there 3 Ships have recently arrived: the Rijnders Schooner, the Christophel Jan Koffer, the City of Gouda Jan Zauw, the Christina Galiot Dirk Jansen Kuyper, and Anthony Overidall as [?] were anchored; as well as in Turkey Pieter Hansen and Andries Doenmans from the East Sea; in Plymouth Tiert Piet, the Wilde, bound from here toward Port à Port; on the River of London newly laden Jonas Hourlian from Bolemia in Norway in good condition; the 200 long-missing Ships and Sloops from the East Sea &c., bound hither, and that under Anke Gerits, Sweft Klaasjé &c.; the Harlingen Jan Hannes from Petersburg, and in the Vlie Boutes Symons from Bordeaux. Near Honfleur has been seen the Ship of Jan Jacobite Tabbes, coming from St. Maries. There have gone out of Texel about 20 Ships to Sea, and among them the Corn. Oums and Jacob de Jong toward Suriname; Jacob de Buyter, Wieke Ralf, Marten Tims, Jan Foryn, Pieter Appelboom and Bleij Tom. toward France, Jan Neean, W. Pion, and Take Olfets toward London.

THE HAGUE, 9 February. The Gentlemen Directors of the West India Company have, together with the Gentlemen of the Admiralty [and] the Gentlemen Deputies of the States General, yesterday had an ordering under Deliberation.

---

[ADVERTISEMENTS SECTION - Various property sales and auctions listed for Amsterdam, The Hague, and Rotterdam with dates in February 1721, including houses, gardens, trees, and household goods]

---

Right Column

STOCKHOLM, 29 January. The Expressman who [departed] yesterday has brought [news] that the goods from Great Russia at [?] a Ship for their transport over water [to make passage]. A certain Swede here [has] a merchant murdered and [?] likely a black [?] the arrested Resident Hopk [?]

Switzerland

BERN, 29 January. Beforehand one [?] over the States through the [?] an hour [?] fallen is; which one [a] similar, and thereafter [?] on the [?] this city situated [?] a Fire-Column perceived, which of [?] high without [?] great [?] in Fire-Globe thereby comes.

Germany & Adjoining Regions

VIENNA, 29 January. One [?] of the Protestant Princes to [?] [?] given is that they not the Peace [?] have they [?] in this Conversation that as the better over the Religion [?] promises for the Protestants [?]. One has found that in the [?] two thousand more people [?].

REGENSBURG, 3 February. [Regarding] this Imperial Diet [?] has of [?] establishing, that by the short way [?], and possibly the answer of [?] services of the Protestant Corps [?] the Bishop of Vienna [?], but from what thereof is [?] but one holds fast that the same in [?] of Reck [?] time the Fall is lower. [?] agreement of our Principals, [?] sent has been, and [?] is awaited.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 6 February. According to Letters from Cadiz, the silver ('t which to the owners has not yet been distributed) shall be brought to the Mint &c.; against which the Consulate of Seville had yet sent in its Remonstrances to the Court, and thereupon received answer; also one still does not know what the [?] shall be. With letters of the 22nd past, via England, one has tidings from Liverpool, that there 3 Ships have recently arrived: the Rijnders Schooner, the Christophel Jan Koffer, the City of Gouda Jan Zauw, the Christina Galiot Dirk Jansen Kuyper, and Anthony Overidall as [?] were anchored; as well as in Turkey Pieter Hansen and Andries Doenmans from the East Sea; in Plymouth Tiert Piet, the Wilde, bound from here toward Port à Port; on the River of London newly laden Jonas Hourlian from Bohemia in Norway in good condition; the 200 long-missing Ships and Sloops from the East Sea &c., bound hither, and that under Anke Gerits, Sweft Klaasjé &c.; the Harlingen Jan Hannes from Petersburg, and in the Vlie Boutes Symons from Bordeaux. Near Honfleur has been seen the Ship of Jan Jacobite Tabbes, coming from St. Maries. There have gone out of Texel about 20 Ships to Sea, and among them the Corn. Oums and Jacob de Jong toward Suriname; Jacob de Buyter, Wieke Ralf, Marten Tims, Jan Foryn, Pieter Appelboom and Bleij Tom. toward France, Jan Neean, W. Pion, and Take Olfets toward London.

[Further political reports regarding disputes between the Protestant and Catholic parties, with details of letters and proceedings, largely illegible in the original transcription]
