Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 12th of February, 1721
Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 29 January. The French Minister, Mr. Campredon, has received back the Express messenger whom he sent to his court some time ago. The aforementioned Minister has received letters from his court and intends to enter into certain conferences with the Czar of Great Russia [Editor's note: Peter the Great]. To this end, he has requested a ship from this court for his transport to Petersburg, which the Admiralty has graciously made ready at Walbohm. However, the approaching harsh season has prevented the French Minister from beginning his journey as yet; he intends to depart with the first appearance of favorable sailing conditions. A certain merchant in Sweden, who had already murdered a merchant and escaped justice, has now been apprehended and will undoubtedly have to undergo capital punishment. The matter concerning General Sweden remains in its previous state.

Switzerland

BERN, 29 January. Last Saturday night, around 11 o'clock, a fire-globe was observed passing through the sky above this city, which fell at a distance of one hour's journey. The following night, a fire-column was again seen above the nearby mountains to the west of the fortifications, which approached progressively over time, and finally disappeared in the darkness without producing any notable effect. Three fire-globes were then observed emerging from it, each taking a separate trajectory.

Germany & Neighboring Realms

VIENNA, 29 January. It is reported that certain ministers of the Protestant Princes at this court have told their friends in recent days that they have spoken several times with the Chancellor, and in those conversations had remarked most clearly that if the conduct regarding religious matters depends on him, matters would not conclude favorably for the Protestants. It has been found that in the suburbs of this city last year two thousand more people were counted than in the spring thereof.

REGENSBURG, 3 February. The second Imperial Commissioner at this court has now written to his friends in Vienna that he must return here shortly, and possibly bring the answer from His Catholic Majesty [Editor's note: the Holy Roman Emperor] to the aforesaid remonstrances of the Protestant Corps. If one is to believe certain letters from Vienna, the answer would not prove so disadvantageous to the Protestant Corps as the Roman ministers had given it to be understood. The aforementioned Protestant Corps has indeed seen fit to make inquiries regarding the latest dispatches from Mr. von Keck, but nothing of what was proposed therein was yet known. However, it is firmly established that the Corps has further decided to allow the aforesaid Mr. von Keck to depart for the Palatinate without probable consent of his Principals, upon whose approval he had been sent there, and that it will persist in pressing for a sufficient Resolution against the encroachments which he has encountered in his Mandate. Much greater satisfaction is taken in the conduct of the Elector of Mainz, since it is confirmed that he has not only given further orders for the restitution of goods, but that these are also being properly executed, with the addition that the Rhine Counts were to be presented with compensation for their suffered damage. The previous Friday, a conference which had been planned for the Elector's predicament at Conventus was postponed, of which the indisposition of the Elector of Mainz's Minister, who has the management thereof, is said to be the cause. One can well observe that the Protestant Corps is greatly dissatisfied with this, because it would not be competent; yet the same [minister] has not been willing to leave matters in this state, but rather that this Directorate should be taken over by another in such a case; and such changes are as favorable as before seemed good, for the Protestant Corps also secured provisions from His Majesty's remonstrance. One now sees the Memorial thrown over by the Protestant Corps to the Imperial principal-Commissioner and the Imperial older Ministers, and which they also sent to Mr. von Keck, to present the same to His Royal Highness the Duke of the Palatinate, concluded in Regensburg on the 22nd of the previous month, being of the following content:

[The Protestant Evangelical Corps, having received the Ordinance of His Royal Highness the Duke of the Palatinate dated 13 December of the previous year, whereby all his Ministers and Subjects are forbidden, under pain of cassation and removal of office, all communication, commutation, whether verbal or written, regarding matters of State, Government, and Religion, with whosoever it might be, had no other thoughts than that this prohibition was merely to prevent all correspondence between the Protestant Corps and the offended Majesty, and that the godless course had seemed to offer occasion for this; and His Royal Highness in his letter of the 2nd of this month had graciously promised to continue and maintain everything in accordance with the rule of His Royal Highness.

But since the Vice-Chancellor of Metsch had informed her Plenipotentiary, Mr. von Reck, that his person had become so disagreeable in the Palatinate, because one would that further correspondence and troublesome Subjects had been led, and spoken, the said Mr. von Reck had given thought to whether he would not do better to avoid this henceforth and always follow it, since His Royal Highness, having now attached himself to the Emperor and other matters, would not tolerate that others should concern themselves with religious affairs, the Evangelical Corps could not fulfill its full procedures; especially since the aforementioned Chancellor was not of the opinion that the day of these good people should be made to appear before him in the presence of a secretary from Megisburg, and them at length in his theology, and whatever their condition might be, as Commander of the Militia.

At Mannheim a report should be made, which had caused such fright among all the inhabitants of that city that even an apothecary, without prior permission from the Court, had refused to serve the aforementioned Mr. von Reck and to provide any medicines.

But as to matters of State and Religion, the Evangelical Corps was unwilling to interfere, and to hold no communion with those of the Roman Religion; but in accordance with the instructions to their Principals, their Plenipotentiary could not dispense himself, for the preservation of good accord between His Royal Highness and the Protestant Powers, to terminate all differences over Religion amicably, just as the public Laws and Edicts of the Empire oblige thereto with all forceful Remonstrances and Petitions against such unprecedented proceedings, so that the same, as contrary to the Empire's Constitution, might be revoked, annulled, and thereby no hindrances be caused to their Protestant Subjects, to remedy the wrong, which might be done to them in prejudice of the Treaty of Westphalia, and His Catholic Majesty, the Evangelical Corps, or the high Court of Justice at Wetzlar, to seek their aid and protection.

One could also say that this Edict is generally at odds with the authority of His Majesty; for that the same, taken to the letter, forbids him, according to the observed Capitulations, to exercise his Royal Office, and those who are wrongly handled against the aforesaid Treaty to obtain justice. The said Ordinance would also, if it were to take effect, be very harmful to the Protestants and all their grievances; the great burden to deprive them of the free exercise of Religion, and they, without being able to speak a word, wholly and entirely to acquiesce and yield; which would therefore give the Evangelical Corps great sorrow, that this work is the effect of the suggestions of the R. Clergy in the Palatinate: wherefore His Royal Highness is urged to consider that the instrument of the Treaty of Westphalia not only grants security to the States of the Empire, but also to their Subjects, as they are expressly comprehended in the sixth Article of the Execution, with these words: "and likewise they may be presented for restitution, but before retaining the right to complain thereover to the Directors of the Circles, or His Majesty, who must hear them, and in their representations or to Processes lend a helping hand.

As the 17th Article of the Treaty of Westphalia speaks in clear words, Terrarumque, omnes injus Traëtatus, tam motis omnis verlas & singularitate, inter Partes Ligae, contra quamcunque diviBendione, hujus protegeras; consequently it is not permitted to hinder the Fellow Contractors and Guarantors of the aforesaid Treaty, from taking knowledge of the conduct regarding their Religion, so that the Protestants should not be done Justice; and finally one can also conclude that by this Ordinance the Treaty of Westphalia and all the Treaties are made common and accounted for nothing.

The Protestant Powers have placed such trust in the equity of His Royal Highness that they would not have asked him to listen to pernicious advisers; for if the aforementioned Powers had wished to deal with their Roman Subjects in the same harsh manner, these would not hesitate to complain thereof to the Court of Justice at Wetzlar, to obtain Justice, which would also infallibly follow thereupon.

The Protestant Princes have for some time now had all too much reason to see that the Roman [Catholics], in order to promote their religion (although contrary to the Treaties of Westphalia and Rijswijk), have employed the Troops of foreign Powers against them in the Empire; the examples of Tewkesbury, the Rhine Counts, and others are notorious; wherein the Roman Catholic [authorities], although in another territory, through the military execution of the Elector of Mainz have been enabled to succeed. If the Roman Catholics deem it proper to maintain their right, their recourse to Powers of their religion to be taken; on what pretext then does one disapprove of the conduct of the Protestant Subjects in the same regard, and why will one prevent them reciprocally from addressing themselves to the Protestant Powers, who are Guarantors of the Treaty of Westphalia? His Royal Highness ought on the contrary to be obliged to join them in the proper remedying of their grievances; which not being found to be light, one cannot see otherwise, but that the evil Advisers of His Royal Highness have no other intention than to hinder the Protestants in their regions by all manner of means, and to prevent them from maintaining good intelligence with His Royal Highness; against which, however, in order to forestall all evil consequences that might arise therefrom, they will endeavor to contribute all that for the welfare of the Empire lies in their power, hoping in the confidence that His Royal Highness will not disdain these their Representations, since their duty requires this, and because it is conformable to the Treaty of Westphalia and the public Laws of the Empire; as also that His Royal Highness, the aforesaid circumstances and negotiations having been taken into consideration, will not only graciously permit his Protestant Subjects to have communication and intercourse with those of their Religion, but also to his Confederates, it is according to the Law of Nations, the exercise of his Committee and instruction unhindered to allow, since this conduct will not bring the least prejudice to His Royal Highness but rather will contribute much to the restoration and good trust between him and the faithful Evangelical Corps of the Sick.

Great Britain

LONDON, 7 February. The Members of the last Committee, which had been appointed by the House to examine all the accounts and dispatches of the Under-Governors and the disposition in betrayal [?], have...

[Text breaks off at this point]

Left Column:

FRANCE

of the Indies shall be held to provide a certificate from the Bank, which by Arrest of the 25th of February has been concluded for them; and under the same dating, it is ordered that all the Contracts of the Licenses; all the Receipts of the King's Treasurers, all the Receivers of the Tolls on behalf of the King's Treasurers, all the interested Shares; all the Certificates for Accounts in the Bank; all Annuity Shares; Times of the end and verified all the Bank Notes, within 2 Months must be presented before the Commissioners of the Council who are competent thereto, [it is] spoken by them to the Verification of the said Securities, to be done by them on the Verification from the beginning of January the Gentlemen Rochefort, Fagon, de Coullon, le Guerchoix, Bignon, Trudaine, de la Rochetart, Fagon, de Machault, Counsellors of State; and the Gentlemen d'Aufieuand, and de Machault, de Riguepeu, de Gourgues d'Aunay, de Morangie, de Malpou, Hebert de Beaujalan, de Beulle, Orry, Chavois, Pouchet, Knuille, de la Vigerie, Bettin, Vermon, Rouillé, d'Argental, de Felon, deVillayer, le Pelletier de Sivon, Palen, Bignon de Blanzy, d'Argenton, de Fébrantier, de Tallard, de Sivon, Palen, Bignon de Blanzy d'Argenton de Febrautier, [?]Commonet, Aubert deTourny and Finon d'Ayzur. Request for Execution of all of the 26th [?], concerning the Verification of all the nature of Securities expressed therein, [it] is proceeded. An Edict of the King, containing Creation of Payers and Controllers of Annuities on [?]. Issue of Notes on [?] of Controllers of Annuities on [?]. Issue of Notes on the [?] of the St. per Livre. By Arrest for the Continuation of the receipt of the St. per Livre. But this not from the Mr. Law for the Ground and Buildings of the Smeltery, which had been built, [he] is owing [?], [it] is thought everything has remained there ordered; [?] the [?] amounts to; everything has remained there ordered; [?] the [?] amounts to; everything has remained there ordered; [?] the [?] amounts to; everything has remained there ordered; [?] the [?]; the Crown Lands, which he for the most part in whole remained owing, now turns again toward their old Possessions. The Advisors have taken [action] these several days, and refused they were not on the Ltr. in Money; the melted Liv. and they are sufficient on account of [?]; up to 120 Liv. mounted in the Notes of 1600 they do around 64 Liv. in Money. On the Fair of St. Germain, which on the 9th [?] is opened, one demands many People, but few who hope for anything. On the [?] there is no Ballet held for the King, because His Majesty found himself not well disposed thereto. The Dowager Duchess of Orleans also finds herself still in her house completely confined.

PARIS, the 7th of February. One sees the Auctions of the King's Council of State: one of the 26th of January, ordaining that the Company. This Tuesday at 3 o'clock, being Wednesday the 12th of February 1741, the Gentleman Logement will sell Obligations and Lottery Effects, spoken of from diverse [sources] from the Ram and Willem Hartog Brokers, shall in Amsterdam on Friday the 14th of Jacob Lemmigh, in the Nes in the Broken Ground, sell a party of extra fine [?] Silver Notes [?], under the Legend in the Nes in the Broken Ground, where the same Cornelis van Nek and Jan van der Bije thereof in the Nes in Amsterdam sell grave by the City Horse-hall, on the Estate of Mosil, Zimow Riemer, an outstanding half of [?] [?] from the Herring Group, Images, Children's pieces, head-pieces, various beautiful wooden [?] decorative and other substantial Handled Images, Groups of Children later by the Notary thereof, which by the aforementioned Brokers are sold. Johannes Staats, Jan Lemmers and Hend. Linquer Brokers, shall in Amsterdam on [?] the [?] in the Burg, a party of good and wrecked [?] [?] [?], Linueus and Service-gold, a party of extra [?]

le Leyden [?]: [?] by FELIX DE KLOP over the City Hall. The 14th of February 17[41]

Right Column:

THE NETHERLANDS

THE HAGUE, the 11th of January. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland will meet tomorrow, toward which time the Gentlemen of the Admiralty Colleges would also be spoken with. Yesterday morning the Imperial Minister the Count of Windischgrätz was with the Gentleman Vegelm van Clacbergen, [?] the Meeting the Gentlemen States General concerning the Proposition of Viadelvelt upon the State Rentine [?] and his forthcoming departure toward the Congress at Cambrai, where Her High Mightiness through the Gentleman Agent in High [office] a fortunate journey have been wished to him. Yesterday afternoon the Minister prepared still [?] toward Brussels, and has [?] for this end procured a Memorias Passport for his baggage. Yesterday morning the Baron Phau Minister of the Duke of Württemberg, likewise attended the President of Her High Mightiness' Meeting. The Baron van den Kapelle, concerning the Province of Gelderland in the Council of State [?], is here delivered. The respective Receipts of the Zuyder-Quarter, which with the Gentlemen commanded [?] Belgians have been held, have again withdrawn toward Home.

MADRID, the 21st of January. Yesterday the Ministers and Grandes proceeded to the Patio; to compliment Their Majesties; on account of the birthday of the Infant Don Carlos, who was then in his sixth year to Compliment and kiss the Hand. The King has appointed the Brigadier Don Emanuel de Navarra to the Marquisate of Caneva, and to Don Nicolas Sangro given the order-Lieutenancy of the Guards of the Body of the Italian Company, and still many other Favors in high positions [have been] granted; as also of many Political intrigues. The last Letters from Corunna report, that that of that Month thus, that the Moors still held themselves in their Camp of Cadillus, where they continually [?] Convoys of [?] and Ammunition [?], and by daily Parties made to observe the Spanish Camp, wherein they by the High limits there were hindered; the same Letters say, that the Marquis de Le-[?] make those [?] the Moors have made upon the side of the City to lie-[?] thought, and that he with all strength and to build the way [?] way. And[?] and other necessary Works, to bring the Place in state of defense. This was further [?] to make: for which reason everything against [?] and the [?] Pitch not sufficient

ST. PETERSBURG, the 20th [?] At this Court [?] Minister [?] the second his [?] in his Co. N [?] and words. Likewise [?] from here toward Cadiz

ENGLAND

ROME, the 21st of January. [?], wherein the Pope, the [?] the Nine and opens the [?] of Cavrola [?] in before some days Rome [?] [?] [?] [?] in the Month [?] of [?] [?], concerning the [?] of Sicily.

CADIZ, the 22nd of January. [?] that we [?] tomorrow [?] [?] and the Marquis de Lesla [?]

DE KLOPPER, on the Jacquesstreet 14th of February 1721, With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**AMSTERDAM, the 10th of February.** According to Letters from the last of December from St. Thomas, the Ship Maria had arrived there from here, reckoned in August-gewere, and was indeed sailed toward St. Crifostel. At Lisbon the Warship of Capt. Hoofi had returned, who had convoyed the two Ships from Rio toward Port à Port, and Capt. Ch. Boyrillard had sailed from there toward Konigsberg. The Course of the WEISS EL is here [?] on Madrid [?] [?] one and a half. Cadiz 117. Seville 11; and one half. Bilbao 30. Lisbon 42 and one half [?]; [?]. Venice 81 and one half. Livorno 91 and one half [?] fourth. Genoa 91. Pt. Payre [?] and one half. London 32 Sct. 10. Frankfurt current 29 and one fourth Reichsthaler. Danzig 290 Polish gr. Breslau 35 and 3 [?] St. Hamburg 32 and one half [?] 7 selling St. Antwerp 2 and one half per Cent. Ghent and one fourth. Brussels 2 and one fourth. Zeeland 5 fourth [?] [?]. Rotterdam 3 [?] and one half. The agio of the Bank and one 3/4th. The Shares of the E. I. C. are 780 to 790, W. I. C. 115 to 116, both [?], and the Subscription [?] to [?] and 490.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 12th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 14th of February, 1721
Turkey

CONSTANTINOPLE, December 6th. The Honorable Emo, Bailo [Editor's note: Venetian ambassador] of the Republic of Venice, who arrived here on October 24th, held his first public audience on November 26th with the Grand Vizier and on the 29th of the same month with the Sultan. The pomp observed in this ceremony was among the most outstanding ever witnessed here. The following morning, seven months' wages amounting to 700,000 piasters were paid to all the military forces in the city. After this, the troops (70,000 complete cavalry) prepared themselves in march formation. Before him went the Janissaries in the livery of the Sublime Porte and many Chiaus on horseback, followed by 30 Slavonic soldiers dressed in red, accompanied by 44 lackeys, some of whom wore red clothing and others scarlet adorned with gold. Before these lackeys went 6 saddled horses with embroidered cloth coverings, along with the stable masters in livery on horseback. Then came the Bailo's great stable master with 2 chamberlains, and behind him 4 well-dressed pages with 6 or more young gentlemen who understood Turkish and Italian languages, along with interpreters dressed in Turkish fashion. This was followed by many Chiaus, and then the Bailo, accompanied by a Venetian nobleman between two secretaries, followed in turn by numerous military and civil officers, attended by a very great number of cavalry in rich dress on horseback. When this pompous company passed through the streets of Galata and Pera (where most Christians and the ambassador dwell), many cannons were fired from the Bailo's palace. Once the Bailo had entered the Seraglio, he was led by an interpreter of the Porte into a great hall, where the Vizier came to welcome him with great courtesy. The Bailo replied with great respect and fine compliments, whereupon he made known his rank and commission on behalf of the Republic of Venice. The Vizier answered that from his side he would contribute everything that could serve the preservation of peace and public welfare between the Princes. Thereupon the Vizier read the credentials of the Bailo, and at the same time coffee, sherbets, and worthy victuals were served, as well as the Caravan or long Turkish robes for His Excellency, and also for each of the nobles in his retinue. On the 27th, His Excellency had his first public audience with the Grand Sultan, for which the march was the same as before. The troops were then stationed in the Divan, where the Bailo was placed opposite the Grand Vizier. After he completed the midday meal with the other ministers, which took place in the location assigned to him, His Excellency, together with 30 other persons of his retinue, took a turn in the water in the Loggia of the Garden of the Grand Lord. The Sultan was in very splendid [...]

CADIZ, January 15th. The Commerce Council of this city and the Consulate of Seville expect within 2 or 3 days a favorable answer to their representations from the Court, as they have petitioned the King regarding what would be advantageous for the commerce of the Indies, namely that the much-needed silver should be minted. Daily various barks with provisions are sent; at Cadiz and Triana much provision is also being gathered to transport thither.

England

ST. PETERSBURG, January 20th. His Czarist Majesty has notified all the ministers residing at this court of the glorious peace which His Majesty has concluded with the Sublime Porte, and consequently the principal points have been made known in the name of His Czarist Majesty. For this reason, solemn thanksgivings have been held here in the public churches and cannon has been fired from the fortresses. Shortly thereafter, a solemn festival day is to be held. The King's birthday is being celebrated at this court with much ceremony.

Italy

ROME, January 25th. Monday morning a Consistory was held, in which the Jesuit Cardinal Giorgio Spinola [?] was present, the mouth [?] was opened, and thereafter the Cardinal [...] was proposed for the Bishopric of Catania in Sicily. Cardinal Aquaviva is much grieved that he was not brought to be present in this Consistory, though his offer remains that it was done in the name of the King of Spain regarding this Bishopric; disputes between the Roman Court and the monarchy of Sicily persist.

GENOA, January 27th. Letters have arrived here from Madrid reporting that the King of Spain, encountering difficulty in the matter of resisting the Moors, will order the monarchy of Genoa to [...] strengthen a garrison of 600 men [?] to be stationed there, and with the remainder of his army in Spain to return home.

MILAN, January 27th. Count [?] has been [...] by His Majesty

---

Friday's Courant

Sweden

Declared Prince in the Roman Empire and appointed to govern this Dominion for 3 years.

STOCKHOLM, January 12th. Pleasant news has been received that the troops of the King of Denmark have already opened a breach at Stralsund to our military; and on the 12th the entire city shall be evacuated. The Hanoverian troops still in Wismar shall likewise soon march out from there homeward and restore that city to the Crown of Sweden. It is said that the expedition, which the French envoy the Sieur Campredon has received from his Court, will also have brought considerable remittances.

Germany & the Adjoining Kingdoms, Etc

VIENNA, February 1st. It is said that Prince Dolgoruky [Editor's note: Czar's minister at the Papal court] is said to have departed for Petersburg in great [?], over which some speculation has arisen. Sad news has been received here that the young Prince of Saxony, after 12 hours of illness, died at Dieutschen. The sorrow felt here on account of this was moderated by the report, it is said, that the Electress is pregnant. The Dowager Empress Amalia has condoled with her daughter by letter regarding this death. The Carnival, which was to be held there on account of the birth of Prince [?], was transformed into a sort of very extraordinary illumination, and for this purpose booths and stalls were set up from the Gate of old Dresden to near the Zwinger, as well as around the council chamber and gallery up to the palace of the Elector; booths were also set up behind this. The remaining tents were placed in the great market and arranged in various other streets, where about 200 from the city and from the surrounding countryside brought goods to sell. There were also some boxes set up in which people amused themselves until late at night. Various evenings were spent in the apartment of the Queen, and there is talk of holding some further routs. The public prayers are continued here to beseech heaven for a male heir for the House of Austria. It is assured that the Emperor, upon hearing from Regensburg that the ministers of the Protestant states are withdrawing more and more, after holding a great conference resolved to send word to the Cardinal of Saxony to declare to the Protestant ministers at Regensburg that His Imperial Majesty's intention is to maintain good harmony between the Roman and Protestant ministers there, and that the aforesaid His Majesty will again write to the Elector Palatine in urgent terms and strongly recommend to him to remove the grievances of the Protestants from the way, and not to begin any new innovations concerning religious matters. And that His Majesty wishes to send Baron Kirchner to His Electoral Majesty before he departs for the Congress at Brunswick, to require him to comply in all respects with the Mandate of His Majesty. The supporters of His Electoral Majesty maintain that he has already largely complied with the commands of His Majesty, and that therefore it would also be necessary to send someone from here to the Court. However, the Protestants indicate the contrary, saying that one seeks to satisfy the Emperor and the Protestants only with words and not in deed, for that no grievance has yet been really addressed, much less settled. Some hold that the Protestants' grievances should be supported and presented at the Congress at Brunswick, which they say will shortly convene, because that place is far removed from Regensburg and the Roman Church does not predominate there, and because many turbulent spirits who are accustomed to sow seeds of unrest will not be found there. And much because no new institutions can be sent daily to the ministers there, by which matters are protracted and left undone. However, it has been noted that some individuals, willing to lose the aforesaid conveniences for the achievement of their aims, have opposed this with vigorous remonstrances, claiming that the city of Regensburg, having once been chosen to conduct the affairs of the Empire there, should remain unchanged. But it has nevertheless been intimated that religious matters can be settled in that place with the same accuracy and impartiality as in any other. Tuesday evening there arrived here a French monk who was taken by the corsairs of Tripoli four months ago and has been exchanged against other slaves from Tripoli who were at Naples. Wednesday the Nuncios Fonseca departed for Rome to rent a house for Cardinal Cenfuegos. The Sieur Jagomsky, having been decorated with orders from the Czarist court, came to this city on Thursday without waiting for Carnival to pass in order to return there, and continued his journey on Friday. [Note: F.S.] It is reported that His Majesty has not only made known to the plenipotentiaries Count Metternich that the negotiations between Saxony and Brunswick (where the minutes are kept) will be conducted, but also that he has sent Baron Kirchner, as commissioner at the Diet, along with all the documents and papers concerning religious matters, all of which he had thoroughly examined and for that reason was fully informed, and that he will send Baron Schast as second plenipotentiary with equal instructions.

Left Column

Ministers at the Palatine Court have come to understand that His Royal Highness has become increasingly dissatisfied with his sojourn, and that he has various reasons to believe that matters of Religion would be executed with less or greater effectiveness by reason of his presence; furthermore, that by removal from Memory (in which previous communities have newly been placed in Paderborn, that Roman ecclesiastical authority had been newly established in Paderborn, that one had newly seen to the handling of the republished Ordinances in the land, since His Royal Highness had no need of electors in his affairs, and was himself competent enough to know what he ought to do or refrain from doing, whereupon the aforesaid Ordinances of the Electors had declared that the republished Ordinances could not prevent the electors from having recourse according to the Ordinances of the Electors, recourse and protection: meanwhile one awaits with justice the body of the aforesaid Memory. Recently the Protestant Corps received further letters from the Lord of Maasheim, whereby it appears that the Ministers to the Electors have given him no other answer than to maintain peace and tranquility; through the execution of an Imperial Majesty's Resolution in favor of the Protestant Princes, as Guarantors of the Treaty of Westphalia. Yesterday the Ministers of Saxony and Hanover met, and as one is assured that His Eminence over matters of Religion and the refusal of the Protestant Corps to appear in court, has not been entirely exhausted, inasmuch as the aforesaid Ministers have yet made no report.

Continuation of Electoral Matters.

The dangerous and circumstantial learning also, how frequently in Spain and in the Netherlands, to the detriment of the Most Serene [powers], one ship here and another there is seized in ambush, also indeed at the yearly markets, and in particular among the merchants, an entire merchandise has been taken to loss; for there the circumstances of one member of the other are named. It will also be the question whether the Roman Catholic ecclesiastical authority is entirely abandoned, or whether it has no part in the persecution of the Evangelical and eternal affairs of the Evangelical Corps; and of the World all trust and faith being taken away, follows a matter in the care maintained, and thus the sole source-vein of all the dreaded unhappiness is, if not God and the same exalted Head, namely Your K.M. with emphasis that betwixt urged, and itself urged, and very much more reason, for which the Roman ecclesiastical authority, and therein chiefly the Jesuits (though we thereby understand the great hope and trust which these same orders, well, as also among the Jesuits some found would be, who have an abomination of such wicked deeds) through violence at various Courts have appropriated themselves to the aforesaid diversity to give the greatest occasion, and deserve from many lands to be driven out, because they through their entirely damned Morality which goes so far that even whole heads of their lives are not secure, teach the Youth and thereafter even the Regents themselves (though which through God's gracious enlightenment Your K. and M. never frequents) before[them], that one is precisely in their darkness not obliged in God's & [?] faith to rely upon gallows, but well upon the priestly [authority] of the forefathers. The glorious & Church increase against the heretics' misconduct may [be allowed].

Your K.M. may graciously see from the contents of what else in the Rijswijk Clause, as one of the principal grievances of the Evangelical State, how the Roman Court gave here the first occasion, in what itself [was] brought to his Majesty the reverently resting Lord Father in the evening before, as well to forget, in order to depart from the ordinances of the Westphalian Peace, and in what the most gracious Emperor to his Allies promised, to give up. And this Court (as to our Lords Principals is well known, and appears from the lately published Public Decree here to the E.) spares no trouble nor intrigues, to, if here possible, Your K.M's paternal disposition entirely and completely to withdraw from the same faithful Evangelical States and their Ministers, and to sow mistrust and malcontent, as alas! from some of the Religious Peace and in the 30 years' War occurred; that many examples show what the Evangelical of such under Papal Hierarchy certain armed Ecclesiastical authority might have ventured, and the question is debated, whether one can regard the same, as ordained and forbidden of Rome, well in all respects so innocent, and to hold that they have part in all things of the Evangelical community.

The proposed alleged Ecclesiastical freedom of [text continues but becomes increasingly unclear in transcription]

MARSEILLE

Elias de Marcos, broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Thursday the 20th at the Golden Crown, a party of made jewelry, pearls and fine diamonds and the fair [?]; all to be viewed the day before the sale, in the morning

It is proposed on Saturday also 22 February 1721, in the evening at 9 o'clock to hold a sale and to sell the noble house and estate, named Heyhim belonging thereto, consisting of the said noble dwelling in a fine and suitable manner, two rising small buildings, garden with fruit trees, of which one with arbours of the most exquisite French fruits, all surrounded in its lanes and walks of rising vines on either side of the riding path, along great over the Rhine, under the jurisdiction of Koudekerk, still in the morning on the estate, lying the first lands in the Low-lying Folder under Cold Koudekerk and the Community of Holland.

Right Column

Water-dike Privileges (which privileges in no respect the Evangelicals enjoy, but because thereof they are always themselves treated as Jews) can be no otherwise than with the Roman Catholic ecclesiastical authority; indeed not because they themselves have also made themselves guilty of that which hate, in [?] should not willingly say that to these ecclesiastics their privileges are not given and freed to that end, this is certain to be misplaced without committing sin, and this grace takes place in those cases under Cleric privileges.

That one deprives the Evangelical ecclesiastical authorities of their goods and incomes, so until the behoof of worldly matters is withheld and thereby not stirred, remains in unrest and hopes to be preserved, for example of the parceled in the year 1726 near Electorate-[?] by example of the parceled in the year 1726 near Electorate-[?] the second admiralty in the first has not likewise seen, of the second admiralty Evangelical conjugal authority Gaderstein, the incomes of the Evangelical conjugality to take up, and not again to permit according to; notwithstanding the [?] Royal Government at the second admiralty better [?] in repeated writings in date the 17 December 1706 to, all these goods, rents and incomes; and likewise are to the examination [?] of the Earldom of the second bridge; and likewise are to the examination of churches and schools, moreover their services; as in the years 1729, 1741 and 1744 the 3rd from the 4th year extended from the years 1729, 1741 and 1744 the knighthood-peace in the year 1745, and the innocent peace-instrument art. V., moreover from the altered capitulation instrument art. V., to be brought in; there with it lies also that noted law-giving, the only forbidden [?] and [?] of foreign subjects, which appears from the collection on that same statement "the subjects, which under proceeding have to know. In the territorial peace of the year 1748 part 1.; likewise that in that no one [?] another's subject nor to disunion of the wellbeing against the authority [?] from Zollich [?] order [?]-affixed of the year 1745 [?] and [?], that foreign subjects without their knowledge or will in protection not otherwise shall enjoy, than as for us aforesaid, the Roman Emperors and Kings of blessed memory of old have been &c. Here standing here m[?].

FRANCE.

MARSEILLE the 21 January. The travel and fumigation of the houses is carefully continued by the magistrates; daily all the kettles open to the goods therein, as also the beds to be aired, and all such goods are left to burn; this vigilance has helped us so much that the sickness now completely has stopped; daily come crowds of people from their country-places into this city for the purpose that is called daily greater; many shops that were closed are again opened, and one expects every day the publication of this city under the pen, also against previous health recovered is. Ships out of the Levant come, and which [?] quarantine have held, are loaded.

THE NETHERLANDS.

AMSTERDAM the 14 February. From the November to January one has learned that the ship of Captain Robert Hacton, and two others from Bristol were arrived; and that at the Spanish coast 2 or 3 English barks lay. From [?] of the Second Miss Johndah Cock from here arrived, and notwithstanding these letters; many days longer than the foregoing were, one there yet had no further news from Dirk Harmonie the [?] with the ship lying of Captain Thomas Karrels, was

AMSTERDAM the 12 February. By letters of the 9 November from the Levant were advertised; that the ship of Captain Robert Hacton and two others from Bristol were arrived; and that at Constantinople Spanish ships and English brigs lay. The 10 [?] of the ship the Miss Barbara Hendrik Cock from here arrived, and sits [?] not notwithstanding these letters 9 days younger than the foregoing were, one there yet had equal notification from Dirk Vrantenlie the [?] what many ships yet daily expected. Captain Jan Cadel with sailings lying ship of Captain Thomas Klarer, was Bilbao towards to depart.

THE HAGUE the 12 February. The Lords States of Holland and West-Friesland have been held in secret deliberation today. Yesterday was the Prince Kuraçao, extraordinary Ambassador of the Czar; and some Lords of the Court. The Electoral Minister the Count of Windiscraz, has at the same time departed to Brussels. The Lords commissioned for the Levant and East-end trade are also in competence in deliberation has been held.

Small-tithe house and [?] division of Jan Lot, purchased and sold to a [?], [?] and one and a half feet, with its brewed incoming [?], then elsewhere we, dwellings in and all the same in the [?]. Here lying in [?]

IX DE KLOPPER, on the Bredelsdust. the 14 February 1731 And Province.

Course of Exchange

**REGENSBURG, February 6th.** The Protestant corps has again received letters from their plenipotentiaries, who are in negotiation and have naturally followed the course of their [?]; the contents of these are not yet public, but it is reported that Baron von Sickingen and other [...] [text breaks off] The course of the EXCHANGE RATES on Madrid 90 and 3 fourths. Cadiz 317 to 16 and 3 fourths. Seville 112 and 1 half to 13. Bilbao 90 to 89 and 1 half. Lisbon 42 and 3 fourths. Venice 81 and 1 half. Livorno 91 and 1 half [?] half a fourth. Genoa 92 to 92. Paris [?] and 1 half. London 11 Principal Sch. 10 and 1 half. Frankfurt current 39 and 1 fourth Reich's Dollars. Danzig 290 Polish [?]. Breslau 33 and 3 tenths [...] for St. Antwerp 2 and 1 half per Cott. Ghent 9 and 1 fourth to Brussels 2 and 1 half to 3 fourths. Zeeland s. Rotterdam agile. The agio of the Bank 3 and one article. The shares of the O.I.C. [Dutch East India Company] are 790 to 800. W.I.C. [Dutch West India Company] 116. and the subscription 380 to 400.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 2 March 1721, one shall at Dordrecht in St. George's Guild-hall sell with one with 4 and the other; well-conditioned salt-pans therein, together with water-vessels, tubs, buckets and salt-barrels, salt-casks, great and small and well-appointed water-carriers, and other parties of aforesaid salt-works at Dordrecht; of which the sale conditions are to be found there by the gate, Barthol[omaeus]—

On Thursday the 10 February 1731, in the morning at 9 hours, in the Calves-market and red diamonds, as also a beautiful [?] of about 22 carats, in the morning at 9 hours, in the aforesaid Golden Crown to be seen. 9 in the evening at 9 hours, in the Lord's lodge of Brussels bettered on the Burg Rhine Leiden, in the and estate, named small Poelgeelt, with its addition and red puchon-estate, in a fine, great and well-conditioned [?] house, with departure-[...] door rooms, [?]-houses, stables for 2 horses, two well-shaped [...] door vineyards over 2 orchards etc. planted [?] [residents], two [?] rising type woods: as well as 2 [?] tented [?] Friesland and 2 standing lying on the Hope [?], one a half quarter cure from Koudekerk's 9 mornings like good and honey-bearing, as mowed meadow or hay-land, opposite [?] Folder under Koudekerk side thereto [?] lanes, in [?] chosen well-and St. John's Guild-hall open and sell, two forenamed Matize salt-works, besides of: Huylyen and other Leaverens of those, as weeds-backs, salt-works on the dike, under Hendrik-[?]-Ambursht: see also the title Dyk [?] district Electors of Gaildorp, in the Wine-street, [?] the house. ... on this place Langemanns, one part quan biert, as also a putty fine in mingled Lobbes, one part lappet cloth for clothing, lyde and [?] Cullen, Damaert, [?] Glffen, seeing estate, a [?] [house] Potpourris [?], its sold, less daily to smoke the [?].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 14th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 17th of February, 1721
Barbary

ALGIERS, 10 January. Despite the fortifications of His Majesty with cannon and artillery pieces mounted here recently, only Spanish vessels of little importance have been captured owing to the war between Spain and the Moroccans, with no more than eighty in total, because it is feared that the Spanish or Portuguese might attempt something against us. For this reason, large ships with manpower, provisions, and ammunition have been sent from [?] to Oran. There is also talk of fortifying other towns; work continues on the construction of the new castle; and it is said that about nine hundred men will depart from here with merchant ships, as in the previous year, to sail in the Spanish Sea under the guidance of some privateers.

Spain

CADIZ, 21 January. All the representations made by the Commerce Council of this city and the Consulate of Seville to the King regarding silver [in the Mint] and what means could bring quick relief to trade with the Indies have been to no avail, for it is said that the sent dispatches have brought back orders that the silver in the Mint is to be brought there to be coined. The departure of the galleons in March is eagerly awaited. From the West Indies, the three hundred long-awaited Buenos Aires ships have not yet arrived; also eagerly awaited are the great ship of [?] Isparta la Nobilissima, and that of Vice-Admiral Antonio Sarano from Havana. Since the duties on fruits and goods arrived from Vera Cruz are uncertain, indeed that matter remains still wholly unresolved to the merchants. Here they are busy equipping dispatch vessels to send the same to Buenos Aires at their own expense. P.S. At the closing of letters herein, an Express from Madrid arrived here with the joyful news that the King, by his decree, ordered that the silver arrived with the latest fleet from the West Indies shall be brought to the Mint and be coined there, whereby the Spanish [people] shall be provided again with new specie for their transactions.

CADIZ, 21 January. According to letters of the 17th from our army before Ceuta, the convoy with provisions, &c., which had been collected through the current efforts of the Governor, &c., had arrived there, which caused great joy in the army, since nothing could be obtained in the Moorish territory: furthermore, news passed through there of lodging, and because the Barbarians (who had been involved with recent skirmishes) had withdrawn to the land, the army kept itself confined to their encampment. In this harbor were stationed the St. Clime, Kamp. Tholod, Lodj, the Chlorinades and other Foyet, and the St. Ann, Anthony Sevya from Barcelona, the Salvatorio Jean Tillas and the St. Thomas d'Aquina, Capt. la Vigne from Alicante: in the army there were dispatches, the Vids Apl. [?] in action more than [?], the Robidert and Mary Dionis O'Brien toward Livorno, the Ailles Gally Geronimo Columbo and the Fortuny Richard Steffens toward St. Lucia, the Maria and Joseph Jan Lambert toward Ireland; and there still remain lying [in harbor] 1 Antelope, 3 Genoese, [?] English, 23 French, 6 Dutch, and 2 Danish ships.

MADRID, 28 January. The King, the Queen, and the Prince of Asturias are staying together in the Prado; the Infante grows better day by day and has recovered to his former health. According to letters from Ceuta of the 1st of this month, the Moors on that morning before daybreak were in motion with their artillery, and a third detachment, under the cover of darkness, had advanced to within musket shot of our encampment, being supported by a considerable corps of cavalry. In the engagement that followed, the enemy was defeated. [...] there would have been no loss, had not General Don Felicismo di Braccamonte and other high officers opportunely had the pickets of our cavalry advance and grenade forward posts, which both [forces] engaged, and made them retire in good order to the camp, in which retreat one subaltern officer and four common soldiers were killed; on the Moors' side the loss was much greater. Besides which, twenty cannon were spiked and their artillery remained in view of our camp, as they attacked it from the adjacent heights with their [guns], while from our batteries vigorous fire was made upon the land, whereupon the Barbarians, not daring to attempt a general attack, finally withdrew back to their camp; meanwhile, the force before Ceuta is occupied with the construction of entrenchments of the Moors, and then [?] of the [?] to the fortification of the city, where there are some sick in the army from dysentery, and it is spoken that the Marquis de Leede and young Counts of [?] will soon come to Spain, so that the fortified Ceuta shall be brought into a satisfactory state of defense.

England

ST. PETERSBURG, 24 January. The Gentleman Daelman, who on behalf of the King of Sweden holds his commission regarding the exchange of prisoners of war here, [it is reported] that he is returning to his court. The Prince Dolhorsky, Ambassador of His Czarist Majesty to the Polish court, has arrived here and will afterwards proceed to return. His Czarist Majesty, after the Prince Menshikov and other high persons have done [him] much honor and civility, have returned here.

France

PARIS, 30 February. [Editor's note: This appears to be an error; February has only 28 days in most years] With letters from Toulon of the 22nd of this month, one learns that the fear of plague there was occasioned thus, and at first with some apprehension, certain symptoms of this sickness were noticed; but one was extremely cautious regarding the consequences. Some days ago, the Prince of Conti waited upon the Duke Regent, by whom he was very well received. When the Duke of Belsac [?] on Thursday took his place in Parliament; the aforementioned Prince, with a large retinue and many nobles, also presented himself in the Palace; and it is said that this noble Prince between the two sittings of Parliament held some conversation with the Counselors of the Palace; it is said that he will keep his estate Mercœur, which brings in 45,000 livres of income, and that he will return his pension of 12,000 livres to the King, who it is thought will give it to his son the Count de la Marche. The gentleman Le Pelletier des Forts has obtained the reversion of his father's position as Councillor in the Council of Regency. The merchants and traders of Lyon, who have submitted a petition and requested an Edict that the Bank accounts be suppressed, have been refused; this causes great need in the trade of Lyon. There was a hearing in the Council of Regency upon a petition of opposition presented by the Company of the Indies against the Edict of 26 January, which ordered that the said Company shall render account regarding the management of the Bank. The King has fully recovered from his minor indisposition, but the ballet has nevertheless not been held. The Marquis Dangu, who by reason of the administration of his great house and inheritance had caused much talk of himself, is much inquired after; but he finds himself now recovered; the Duke of Vendôme has given him an Abbey with 1,000 livres income in Provence, and having had him take possession of it, they will, since he is married in England, make him a Knight of St. Lazarus. The Musketeers have orders for the whole of the month to be ready to send out a detachment to meet and escort the Turkish Ambassador. The Duke of Effiat has been dismissed from justice; it is said that he had the misfortune to involve himself in his duties, [?] to[?] with people, who fired upon and murdered [?] in his presence, who being captured thereupon, were executed at Besançon. At that time the Bank notes of 100 were no more than 1 livre, and there were few sold of 100 livres, and the hopes are 4 to 46 livres. The attempts and going of the stock market are not in favor of the agitators; they seize upon this as one says; and one anticipates the disappearance of the multitude, which could well happen, because some princes of inferior rank have interest in the transactions. The goods, which some days ago through the corporals of the merchants of this city were foreseen to be supplied—the Duke of la Bocca and other missionaries having claimed it—were partly sold. There is talk of a fabulous breach of the merchants of St. Malo, which [?]; in the transactions go particulars [?]

Great Britain

LONDON, 11 February. After those of the House of Commons on Friday passed the Land Tax Bill and sent it to the Lords, it was presented by the appointed Governor of the South Sea Company, the Account of the sold Shares, and all that had been given thereupon by the Committee of the Treasury; [it was shown] that the Company had never given any account of sold shares to the Court of Directors, and that the money obtained thereby was entered into the house on 17 December last; but after careful examination, the money was found registered in the Company's cash book dated 14 April last, and the House noted that the said account had been placed before the last Committee, and that examination thereof should be made. On Saturday, by unanimous resolution of the Commons, Knight Robert Champidin and Mr. Francis Eyles were declared as Directors of the South Sea Company guilty of notorious breach of trust, whereby very many of His Majesty's subjects have been ruined and the public credit greatly damaged; and it was resolved that they should be expelled from the House for these misdeeds; they proceeded without great haste to the Moneyers and Deserters, of which the report was [?] on Tuesday, until which time the House then adjourned. A list is being circulated of the names of 31 members of the House of Commons of the party, who it is said have received bribes from the Directors of the South Sea Company, during the time that the Bill, in order to settle the Company with the payment of the public debts and the appointed Fund therefor, was pending before Parliament, and it is really said that the Lords are also making a similar list with the names of 44 such members who found themselves in the early sessions of Parliament, and therein shall show those who have received bribes, which it is said they received in some proportion in the same time [?]; and so it appeared that the greatest part of them received about 9,000 pounds sterling in this way; also it is said that yet other matters would be discovered, which one expects in the coming week shall be able to be [revealed], before the House of Commons shall have made an adjournment, on which account every one there is eagerly awaiting account; the last Committee appeals with still [?] in order [?]

GELDERLAND, 16 February. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland will continue their Assembly this week. Yesterday the Prey [?] Minister Mr. Myndersh [?] gen was with some Gentlemen of the Regency. The Hr. [?] that today from Gelderland is to depart. There is an Express from the British [?] towards the North has passed.

THE HAGUE, 16 February. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland will continue their Assembly this week. Yesterday the Puy [?] Afternoon the Mr. Myndelaagen with some Gentlemen of the Regency. The Hr. van Heukclom is now to depart towards Gelderland. There is an Express from the British [?] towards the North passed.

Considerable discussion continued regarding the great changes in the Ministry that have taken place. It is whether the sight of the Project of Mr. Walpole depends on the information which the Directors give him would; it is maintained that that Company has only half Shillings of Obligations issued; and if Parliament further the underwritten Annuities to the Liege-ears should confirm. As again traded is continued, one held the Book-value 139, of Shares the Company ready in [?] diminishment. The Book-printer Cleerk, who in the matter of a Death of State was over the [?] of the own [?] the birth of the Son of the Pre-Resident, in Newgate imprisoned brought, and one believes that he for High Treason will be indicted [?], being Traitors of the withholding of King Charles the First; was in the manner of the Abbot of Westminster by the Bishop of Bangor the Upper House opened, and before the Lower House by Doctor Baker in St. Margaret's Church: that day the King was not in the Chapel, but has his Devotion performed in its Chamber in the Palace at St. James, as the Prince and the Princess of Wales in their House at Liefotet [?] held. Today the Company of the [?] Z. [Editor's note: likely South Sea Company] has elected the King to its Governor, the Knight John Bykes to Deputy Governor, and the Hr. John Ridge to Acting Governor.

[Extensive discussion of parliamentary business, including bills concerning wool and silk manufactures, and investigations into the South Sea Company directors regarding fraudulent trading practices.]

Monday, 17 February 1721, the Brokers Cornelis van Nek and Jan van de Stadt Paludtsdalt at the Yard of Mr. Simon Kramer, an outstanding Cabinet, together with marble Groups, Statues, Little Children, Bust-portraits, Vases, and various Antiquities: further also some Paintings of Heroes, Groups of Children's art, and Nature to be seen, which are to be obtained from the aforementioned Brokers.

This morning at 9 and this afternoon at 7 o'clock, being Monday, 17 February the Street at Amsterdam to be sold, on the Reguliersgraft by the City Horsestall with red Marble Columns and the flat half ditto, as well white Pots, Medals, [?]; being among these street goods many Antiquities: further divided Blue and Hanseatic Pedestals; as further by Night to be seen, this morning at 9 o'clock, at the house of Miss the Widow Abraham Durieux, on the [...] through to be put and sold a considerable party of Plates; of all kinds, earthenware and Split-ware; and 1 draaded and Muffles, item also a party White and Quinette, further the Equipment for the Pottery-Trade belonging shall be able to be seen.

One is of opinion on Wednesday, 19 February 1721, this morning at 9 o'clock, at Bredestraat over the School-lane at Leiden, in public to be auctioned and to be traded in White and Colored Barcannes of all sorts of Leather-ware; Silk and Colored Silk, Turkish and Sajette Yarns, Linens and Quinette, and further the Goods can be seen there the day before the Sale. Jan Rietmacker, Daniel d'Azevnio, Jan Hoffleyn the young and Rafael d'Azev. [on] 19 February 1721, this evening at 5 [o'clock], in the Dykstrant in the Old Castle, Housgrift over the former Jewish Church.

[...] the Tobacco-dealers, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday [?] in the Old Castle, a party of extra fine new Varinas Tobacco; lying on the

One is of opinion on Saturday, 22 February 1721, this evening at 5 o'clock, public to be auctioned and to be sold, the Noble House and Hofstad, called Klein Poelgezelt, with its Back-Leaseholds and further Foreign-Hofstad, with a beautiful, large and well-conditioned Gentleman's House, with various Farmworkers' Dwellings, Coach-house, Stalling for Horses, two well-built Stone Bridges, new variegated Garden-orchards, of which one with Fruit-trees of the most exquisite Family fruit over 7 Years ago planted is, Kitchen-garden, two beautiful Fish-ponds, all around enclosed with its Lanes and Walks of ascending Type Trees, as well 2 Fields of like good Meadow-land, against the Hand and situated on the Hogevaart, a half quarter hour from Koudekeik and morning of the good and well-equipped Meadow of Holland, opposite a Folder below Koudekeik, see two distinct Leases, to Leiden held being of the County of Holland.

Franco Condolus, Samuel Steenlack, Jan de Vryer and Gregorius Mees Brokers, shall at Rotterdam sell on Saturday the 22 at the Inn the Black Lion, a party of about 600,000 pounds guttated gum, by Cavelings of 2 vats ships.

One is of opinion on Monday, 24 February 1721, this morning at 9 o'clock, the Flower-market at Leiden, in public to be auctioned and to be sold an assortment of Mixed and White Barcannes, Balistade and Sajettes: item Old-blacks of 4 and 6 threads, all kinds of fine Noir & Fine woolen Damask and Cambrics; and some Equipment for the Pottery Trade belonging the Sale can be seen.

Course of Exchange

AMSTERDAM, 15 February. At Port are Willem Jansfe Welteyck, and at Bordeaux the young Jan Klaas Jac. Visfer from here; at London Magnus keelman from Danzig, Jochem ra-gelaot from Stettin, and Lubbert Roelle from here; however the ship of Michiel Oures, from Stettin bound for London, was booked but locked in the hold. From the River of London (where the ship the George, Captain William Hunter, from Gibraltar has sailed) there are [?] the opportunity; and in the Vlie Corn. Cornelihe Vos from Hitland not following otherwise; and in the Vlie Corn. Ponce towards France and Coenil. Blok towards Bilbao. The EXCHANGE RATES today are: Madrid 90 and 3/4 to 1½, Cadiz 116 and 1½. Bilbao 82 and 3/4. Lisbon 42 and 1½. Seville 171 and 1½. Genoa 94. London 32 shillings 10. Frankfurt current 29 and 1/4 Reichsthaler. David 50 pounds sterling. Bremen 35 and 5/4 at 5 per cent. Gemi 2 and 1/4. Bruges 3. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 1½. The Bank Agio 5 and 1/4. Paris exchange 43 and 1½. Geneva exchange on Paris 29 and 1/2 Lysel. Danzig 290 Fools' groats. Breslau 35 and 1/4 on the spot. Hamburg 32 and 2 self-same spot. Antwerp 2 and three agio on spot.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday, 2 April 1721, this morning at 9 o'clock, shall on the Gallery above the Doors at Amsterdam, be sold all the afore-mentioned Collections and purchases gathered together, consisting of Greek and Roman Antiquities, items very Indian Rarities, and some finely painted Portraits, strong and well-finished House, lying and situated within the Village North-[?] with fine hanging halls, beautiful interior Chamber and an extra hung Hall, with Cellar and a good appointed Barge in Wine-Cellar, above extra lodging for Coaches and Horses towards the Stable for the Day [?] and through an equal gate on the large Garden; here even [in?] the best [?] Scarborough; mentioned Garden is divided into 1 large [?] of which Plots one [with?] golden [?], and all for sweet-scent set with Type and Small Rooms; likewise belonging that in [study?] gotten on of House living, or addressed by the Mister Silvester van Anrac, in the same House living.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 17th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 22.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 19th of February, 1721
News From Vienna & Surrounding Kingdoms, Etc

VIENNA, 5 February. It appears that the mourning for the deceased Empress will soon flourish and come to an end, as their reigning Imperial Majesties showed themselves for the first time yesterday, since the aforementioned death, at the Italian Comedy, and this morning took their pleasure at the Italian Opera there.

REGENSBURG, 10 February. The Lord of Reck reported in his last letter to the Protestant Corps that the Baron von Deckinge, as Adjutant, had undertaken to present to the Elector the Memorandum of the 23rd of the previous month that had been sent to him, saying that His K.V.D. [His Imperial Majesty] had already been informed of it and was very displeased with it, based on the circumstances mentioned in the earlier Memorandum; but as [others] reported that the Ministers present here had learned with astonishment that one did not wish to respond to the Ordinary [requests], of which 200 concerned the Court of His K.V.D., on the pretext that they must first await an answer to the information already given to their Principals, whereupon the mentioned Lord of Reck found it unexpected that he was explicitly obtaining his Instructions; if in this matter there were to be some understanding with the Court or at least some indication: the mentioned Lord of Reck could not make it clearer that, upon consultation of that Court, further withdrawal of the [matters?] cited, or making no account of it, was because he perceived much displeasure among the Ministers and even with the Elector himself. There was still talk of the Reformed Church Council in Mannheim and what was taken there, and it has already been sent out from the Court whether a part of the same would return to Heidelberg, because Mannheim had not found sufficient lodging. The action of Reck showed itself as if impossible so that no difficulties would be made for him, and the Protestant [Corps] would thereby be damaged.

According to the letters from Vienna, the departure of the second Imperial Commission was delayed against the 1st of December. One expects that a Resolution of His K.M. will be provided to remove the cause of the interruption of the Company of the Protestant Corps, and thereby to confer good Religious [matters] among all the distinguished corps. It is still assured that the Emperor will not answer the Remonstrances except in the form of a Decree, that the Cardinal of Saxony as Principal Commissioner will attend the Imperial Diet. The Cardinal of Saxony held a Society meeting; nearly all the Protestant and Roman Ministers appeared there, His Eminence having said to some that he was not well satisfied whether high matters were settled without a generous and prompt Party.

Decline of the Demonstration

There is no doubt that no sound reasoning of law, consequently the same is harsh, being the secret Resolution of the royal concerning Kings.

There is no doubt that the second article here, consequently the same as in these Imperial Laws, regarding the ancient brought-forth customs and protection-rights is established, also applies to those who are regulated in the later and Neapolitan Laws, according to the Imperial Compilation; and that in the aforementioned 17th article of the Peace of Westphalia par. 5, 6, &c., an indisputable right is added to the collective pacts to implement all the ordinances of the same Peace and to protect those whose duty it is to enforce the same, of which in that part are to be excluded the principal ordinances of the same article, which concerning the Religion and Church-rights of the Subject openly was related, otherwise universal & singular pacts of this law and in whatever manner there & protector, if such protection and maintenance [were provided]. Against the Territorial Lords who transgress such Laws, no place exists, would not have caused so many residents as well as Territorial Subjects of different Religions [to suffer], for whom intercession from their fellow-believers was made, but without fruit? What then would help the two more words called out in anno Seventeen & 1624, and the ordinance that all subjects of the pacts will comply with it? And how long would such status of old declarations and the elected fundamentum of the Peace of Westphalia last, if in the Compromise nothing is held to? If the Evangelical corpus had sinned in anything, it is likely therein that it showed too great patience and no complaint; just as the example of the other Peace through the long removal under all kinds of plunder from far over 100 Churches, and more than half of the Incomes, which is no small Sum, but amounts to 30,000 Guilders. Whoever has read the Acts and Treaties of the Peace of Westphalia; although the Territorial Lords must in the above show the right to be able to hold to the Participants of the Westphalian Peace; and if one wishes to further regard the times by the Religious Peace, one will see that one of the great-minded is, and has given judgment, that the Territorial Lords did not wish to allow that other fellow-States would take upon themselves the foreign Lands and Subjects of the Empire, as one can see such matters widely related from the Acts politiques. [Citation: from publ. de Jure Religionis, under the name Rumours, in the eighth Chapter of the second Part of the said Piece; and just as the Peace of Westphalia is called generous; so that those who have wished to make new [ordinances] of the Peace of Westphalia, to the Lands and Subjects [would have to] confess that their rule is [not to accept impertinences] from another faithful [party]; a. has [duties?] per Provinces: but how can Subjects of the Provinces the easily [understand?] Imperial affairs with the Roman Emperor and, as daily pacts and ordinance teach, what [power?] years...

---

...um & liberties per prætoriis in causervatoria spiritualia, much misfortune has led to questionable such [matters], intercessions and protections, or judgments being prejudicial to the highest authority, which, like all equally [pacts], also 200 [reference unclear], the Evangelical Electors and States ex ipsi lege & ratione propria in vior [unclear] perpet, & irrevocabilis hold in hand. Would then the aforementioned Article of His K.M.'s election Capitulation, when the burdensome Protestation [of?], and all the Imperial Subjects, without application of internal and foreign assistance, equal protection in Religion and profane matters, against [the authority?], conducting themselves thereby at the Municipal and Superior [authority?] and other Imperial institutions, and such with that more for considered reason, because without such modification it seems that one would the legitimate guarantee, maintenance and intercession would [wish to] abandon from the compacts of the foreign powers; as from the remaining [Confirmations of the Peace?], and from the Electors and States of the Empire would wish to derogate, which is far from Your K.M.'s rightful intention.

This high [Peace?] [?]publicum thus obtained in the two justice struggles just as little against the Imperial highest authority & supreme power, as the per leges Imperium [?] the Imperial ordinances [?] church-descriptions [?] in [?] commissioner perpetual [?] opposition [executors?] [these?], and the cura & [?]odia [?] [?] pat[?] ut circulus could derogate or take away also not from the petitional Rights of the Territorial Lords and Authorities; but it is a prudent confirmation of those who, seeking nothing but to decline the Evangelical Corpus by Your K.M. and to portray it so gravely, make such a disturbance about it as here or that Your K.M.'s highest authority to the Evangelical is so attacked, and [attacks?] made on the other; whereby they then, upon their passionate representations of still never brought to effect [?] assistance and peace-known violence, against the literal sense and content of the Conclusion of the Evangelicals of the 21 Dec. 1719, [mandated?] [?] propose to execute in the Evangelical Lands, against the Imperial Reich-ordinances, a [resistance?] [?], by which against these Emigrants, after representations made, general satisfaction, with the Imperial Constitutions agreeing, can obtain; and also such [protestations & confessions?], which alone belongs to the highest Sovereign; with which indeed in all made Conclusions of Evangelicals very clearly has been alleged, which intentions in the Imperial Dispositions, just and in no way attacks the Imperial highest authority; yet this has he who has done it, the Evangelical Corpus hatefully to make, could not prevent; and as he may clean it the [?] of having been heard, he could not conceal that they brought it so far that the other Your K.M.'s highest name made [confused?], beside the newly...

---

LONDON, 14 February. Tuesday the 14th of this month, the first Reading of the Lords' Bill; after which the Brokers and other Defendants of the Z.Z. Company have delivered to their Lordships the Extracts of their Books, concerning the Actions for the Directors and Deputies, or for the [?] and of the Treasury and Exchequer bought or sold, and the Lords having ordered the same to be furnished, Mr. Aitell, one of the Directors, was called for the matter, who delivered to their Lordships [his account]; as the Directors, concerning the Actions which they have taken upon themselves, [they] drew up; after which the Title being read, the same was delivered to the Committee; subsequently the 2 diverse [?] were brought in, and one of them gave a letter of accounting; even as had been done by the Brokers; thereafter the House turned itself over the matters of public Credit in grand Committee; and Mr. Waller, husband of the Son of the Lord Aisleby, being called, was strictly examined against the Actions which he had bought and sold, as well concerning the Subscriptions, account of which he delivered; Mr. Wymandtell also being called into the House, was likewise carefully examined; and to these the Directors, through the diverse techniques which they had employed, had sold some Actions at exorbitant Prices; they subsequently examined him concerning the Convention which he had made with the Lord Aisleby or with Mr. Waller; and their Lordships being informed that Mr. Aitell was at the Door and had many matters of importance to communicate, had him come in, as when he discovered diverse particulars concerning the Subordinate Officers of the Company, which one has wished to examine. That same day the Lords in their Examination discovered that the Knight John Blunt and other Directors, the esteemed goods [?] of the Dividend of St. John [?], in January by arrangement had purchased for N. Schell, which Dividend they were [?] [unclear] to be 10 Pounds capital in Actions, which up to D. have been written, in this time when the Actions were at 1000 [unclear]. One suspects without doubt that the Directors under cunning [?] from the [?] which belonged to the Company, wrongly [abused?]; that evening the Lords came to the following observations: 1) That the Practices of St. John Blunt, Mr. Goorby and others, which they have practiced, a great quantity of Actions [?] over the Direction of the Company on that [?]; and in time [they] themselves bought Actions in a clandestine manner at the Company [?]...

The Netherlands

ROME, 24 April. The appointment of Cardinal de Rohan and his nephew as nuncios to His Holiness at Rome is now confirmed; the Cardinal has received 15,000 crowns in money for his travel expenses and equipment, and Cardinal Corsini has already begun preparing his necessary furnishings with diligence. It is understood that the Decree of 26 January has been executed with great vigor. The Turkish Ambassador is expected to arrive here at the beginning of May. The King will receive him at the end of the Tuileries, where he will be lodged, in the Louvre. There has been talk of the great soldiers at Toulon leaving the sick houses, which caused some concern, but it came to nothing. There is word that 1,200 bales of coffee have arrived there.

PARIS, 14 February. Tomorrow the King's birthday will be celebrated, as he enters his 12th year. Two new Articles from the King's Council of State are visible: one of 23 January establishing that beaver fur, of whatever quality, may not enter the kingdom except at the ports of Calais, Dieppe, Havre de Grace, Rouen, Honfleur, St. Malo, Morlaix, Brest, Nantes, Rochelle, Bordeaux, Bayonne, Cette and Marseilles. The other of 9 February concerns the taxation of shares, ordered by Decree of 7 November and 2 January last. The Notaries appointed to the Council as sub-Commissioners to inspect the shares have, finding this work tedious, demanded considerable compensation to withdraw from it. The filled shares are worth 745 livres in money, approximately [?] 89; and the bank notes of 1,000, which had been at 55 livres, have now risen considerably in price. Daily there are arrested those guilty of misappropriation; within 3 days two have been brought to the Bastille, whose goods, when inventoried, were found to exceed 30 million. It is reported that the Lord de la Houdan speaks confidently that holders of shares would do well to keep them, as they are expected to rise sharply soon. Madame has been told that she has dined in public since Sunday. The Princess of Wales has given birth to a Prince in the Hôtel. There is talk of making a canal from St. Quentin to Paris, which will have communication via the Scheldt with Holland. The King will attempt by reconciliation to restore the Doctors expelled from the Sorbonne on 22 [?].

BRUSSELS, 17 February. Saturday evening the Count of Windischgrätz arrived here from Holland.

ANTWERP, 17 February. Thursday morning around three o'clock there departed from Brussels a Major with a detachment of Dragoons to escort two Cadets [?] and [...] of the [Dutch East India] Company, who had been in Brussels for 3 or 4 days and departed Wednesday afternoon toward Liège, and were brought Thursday to Turnhout under guard of Dragoons in the Citadel [...] in security, where he is guarded by a [...] watch. [The authorities] await the orders of the British Court, to which notice of the confidential arrest has been given.

AMSTERDAM, 17 February. According to letters from Lisbon, the departure of the Fleet toward Rio de Janeiro took place in February as planned, and it appears that everything was provided for there as in the establishment of the Chamber of Commerce. Letters of 2 February from Cadiz report that on the ship arriving there from Madrid, a protest was lodged by the Commerce Council of that city and the Consul's office [?] of the Court.

The ship Jalon, Captain Hilard, has arrived at Martinique from Bordeaux; at Alicante the two Good Friends, Captain Lausdan from Ireland; at Dublin the Nees, Captain Mabbie from Cadiz to Bilbao; the Hamburger Fador, Captain Kolman to Bergen. Langevelt, which had to go from here to Drontheim: Hans Christian from Hamburg, and [...] Burg, where Mr. Pierre [...] on Red, from Copenhagen [?]. The ship Hidden Frederick and the Rieperwyk, Klaas Kool, both from here. From St. Maarten the ship Young Willem, Will. de Boer departed toward Honfleur; and from the Pillau Filip Bruns and Skipper Wojke. Through the entire autumn here the common trade in goods remained open, and there was also an unusual [...] Winter. The ship Hood, Mart. Bus., bound from France toward the Baltic, after having drifted at sea for 6 or 7 weeks, ran into Wieringen, where there was also a French ship from St. Malo bound for Gothenburg. Letters of 18 January from Drontheim report that north and west of the city there were wrecked ships and buildings [...], and there a great Fluyt from here bound for Fredrikstad, in which a Lady and her Maid were found dead. Day before yesterday various ships departed from Texel to sea, including Steven van Limdt toward Suriname, Klaas Swijgerman the Barber [...], Pierre Arnould toward Santa Cruz in Barbary, Matt. Drasco toward St. Thomas, Wigman of Dike and Kryn Krynte toward the Mediterranean. Piet. Gommers, Daniel te Velde, Jacob van Koe toward Cadiz, Klaas Duit, Dirck Goodtsthalk toward Lisbon, and others toward France.

England

BILBAO, 31 January.

---

Den 19 February 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES today:** Madrid 99. Cadiz 116 to 117½. Sicily 119 to 120½. Bilbao 89½. Lisbon 42½ to 43¾. Venice 51½ to 53¾. Livorno [...]. Genoa 92 to 93½. Paris 43 and 2/4 [?]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 19th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 23.
