Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 21st of February, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, January 24th. The Count of Egmond, nephew of Cardinal dei Giudice, has arrived here to take possession of the fiefs of Hillecede and Carigola.

ROME, February 1st. Last Sunday, Cardinal Corsini consecrated the Bishops Caisia and Loyero in the Church of St. Ignatius in the bishoprics of Fond and Umberatico. That same day, Cardinal Alchaan received an express messenger from Vienna, upon which his Eminence departed the following morning. He had an audience with the Pope; it is reported that his protestations concern the elevation of the diocese of Vienna to an archbishopric, for which reason the aforementioned Cardinal sent back his response with the same express messenger to the Viennese court that very afternoon. Tuesday morning, a meeting of the Congregation of Rites was held here, wherein it was deliberated whether Father Joannes of the Cross, Barefoot Carmelite, should be placed in the rank of the Saints. That afternoon, the matter of Cardinal Alberoni was heard. Wednesday morning, the mandate of Cardinal de Noailles was examined in the Congregation of the Holy Office. That afternoon, the prelatess Gemmainne for the third time gave her examination in the Catholic faith, for which she was complimented that evening by Cardinal Tanara in the name of the Sacred College.

LIVORNO, February 1st. Certain news has been received here from Cadiz that a vessel belonging to the English, under the command of Guevaria, has safely arrived there.

FLORENCE, February 1st. The Dowager Princess of Tuscany had a comedy performed here for the first time, which she had herself composed and arranged for her pages.

VENICE, February 8th. Saturday afternoon, the Doge went with the government to the Church of St. Maria Formosa, where they performed divine services; and the following morning, being the feast of Our Lady Cathalsmis [?], they attended the service held in the Church of St. Mark. The gentleman Law, who is residing here with his family, has built a magnificent palace to establish his residence; it is said that he expects his wife and daughter shortly.

Spain

BILBAO, January 31st. Yesterday, two decrees were published here; the first reads as follows:

D. Licentiate Don Francisco de Ucedo y Gilton, Councillor of His Divine Catholic Majesty, Criminal Judge in the Court of Valladolid, and Corregidor of this most loyal lordship of Biscay, &c., makes known to all citizens, inhabitants, and merchants of this city, that His Majesty's ordinance, dispatched on the 11th of this month by the Board of Health in Madrid, commands that ships coming from the North into this harbor, and laden with flax, flour, and other merchandise which they bring from there, without any goods from other places; also ships from Holland, England, and other provinces of the North shall be admitted only with the aforementioned merchandise, after first maintaining quarantine for 10 days in the harbors of St. Nicholas or Claviaga. That those ships bringing other goods without proper certificates from their factory or place of manufacture shall be subject to the most rigorous quarantine in the aforementioned places, where such goods shall be examined by foreign officials at the time of unloading. That free commerce is permitted with the islands of Majorca, Ibiza, and Formentera [?], being under the jurisdiction of His Majesty; likewise those of Minorca and Port Mahon, with all due consideration, that goods coming from the aforementioned islands and not being the natural produce or manufacture thereof shall be subject to the rules established concerning English goods. By order of His Majesty, dispatched by the Lord Dom Joseph Rodrigo, Secretary of Universal Dispatches; done in Madrid on the 19th hereof.

The second is of the following content:

NOT only shall persons of whatever condition seek to bring into these kingdoms, whether by water or by land, any goods or merchandise from foreign realms without health certificates and the observances prescribed by the Royal ordinances published in this realm; shall those [who do so] be required, if they wish to enter, to have their papers examined at the frontier or in the sea-havens by the police officers appointed thereto; [and it is decreed] that those who are responsible for or complicit in the importation of such merchandise and goods outside the aforementioned circumstances shall be punished with death and their goods confiscated; which punishment of death shall also extend to the soldiers who shall permit the concealment of such goods and merchandise, as well as to the officials who have been granted permission or who have not kept their records in order, and the commerce conducted by the guards is not to be left unguarded; and likewise the overseers of the guards, sergeants of the roads, rural and farm folk, and whoever shall permit such smuggling; provided that within six months justice shall be administered and the declaration of neglect or transgression shall be according to the rules and pleasure of the law; such trials shall be concluded without any appeal, and the sentence in all respects shall be binding as stipulated in the aforementioned ordinance. Therefore be it ordered and proclaimed to the aforementioned citizens, inhabitants, merchants, captains, and ship masters, and all others whom it may concern, that they obey, fulfill, and comply with His Majesty's order, under pain and penalty; and that the same be executed; and so that no one may pretend ignorance thereof, this shall be published in the public places of this city with drum and trumpet. Done in Bilbao on January 30th, 1721.

---

No. 24

Friday's Courant

Germany & Neighboring Realms, Etc

VIENNA, February 8th. Baron de Kirchner, through his favorable services, has so well gained the favor of this court that he has not only been honored with costly presents, but it is also assured that he has been appointed by His Imperial Majesty as privy councilor, and may subsequently be elevated to the rank of count; it is certain that through his impartial conduct he has won the favor of the Protestant ministers at Regensburg. And it is hoped that through his careful handling, the religious difficulties will be settled to the satisfaction of both parties. The British minister at this court has, as it is assured, presented a memoir to His Majesty regarding religious matters, requesting that the aforementioned Baron Kirchner be dispatched to Regensburg with the necessary instructions in writing; since he is beginning preparations for the journey there, and it is said that once his affairs at Regensburg are settled, he will proceed as soon as possible to follow Baron Keller at Brunswick, who has been commissioned to proceed there, so that together they may bring the religious matters to a conclusion. When the minister of a certain Elector was recently admitted to a public audience of His Majesty, it was remarked that his credentials were very well received, but at the same time he was given to understand that His Majesty did not wish to hear any more new remonstrances regarding religious matters, but that [such matters] should be subject to what would be established at the Congress at Brunswick. The Roman side has proposed that, if it may not be permitted every prince to send his minister there, at least it should be allowed that they jointly send someone there to look after their interests; and although it is assured that nothing has yet been resolved on this, it is nevertheless expected that this will be granted. It is said that the Papal [representative] maintains that the religious matter should be settled in favor of the Roman Church; but that he was answered that His Majesty would settle the condition of affairs in accordance with justice after the war, so that neither party would have further cause for complaint. That the Turks have dispersed their camp about Nisla to approximately 40,000 men is confirmed, wherefore orders have been sent to all commanders on the frontiers to put everything in a proper state and provision the magazines with what is needed; however, other advices state that it is only a certain Turkish corps that is said to be stationed thereabouts, of which more certainty will be reported shortly. The Count of Thierheim, general war commissary, having concluded the diet in Hungary, has returned from Pressburg to this city. It is understood that the Hungarians at that diet seriously petitioned to be exempted from the quartering of the 2 Imperial regiments coming from Italy, which request was not granted; just as it had also been refused to the Austrian estates when they sought exemption from the Kabutinie dragoon regiment, because the same had always been effective at their expense; but the Count [?] has given them to understand that they might renew their request should a favorable opportunity for the relocation of the aforementioned regiment arise, which matter shall be attended to in due course. It is understood that the Emperor has written to the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz at Regensburg to restore the general Imperial assembly, and shall send his chamber servants to Dresden to offer condolences to the King and their Royal Highnesses on the death of the late Prince. And it is said that shortly an express will be dispatched to Milan with a new plan of government, which shall be approved and signed by the Emperor, from which advantages are anticipated. The Emperor is also entertaining himself with writings. It is assured that a great change and dismissal is to be made among the chamber servants, because it has been discovered that these of late have drawn some millions for fees; from which the intended change will bring much profit and savings; moreover, in future the goods of the wives of servants, who have become wealthy from the funds of the land, shall also be subject to such confiscation, wherefore no marriage settlements of the wives of such servants shall have any force.

REGENSBURG, February 13th. Last Monday, the Imperial Commission decree was brought to the distribution, but the Protestant corps found no satisfaction therein; since in the same the decree of April 12th of the previous year is reproduced, and moreover the Emperor wishes that the Protestant corps appear at the assembly to deliberate whether the method of execution of the two principal articles of the Imperial deputation [should be adopted], and also the clarification of the eighth article regarding the voluntary financial contribution. Regarding the Protestant corps in their position as concerns the remonstrances which the Protestants made to His Imperial Majesty over the Commission decree of the aforementioned April 12th, in this decree no sufficient satisfaction is given. Last Monday and Tuesday the aforementioned Protestant corps conferred over this newly issued Imperial decree; and as far as one can learn, the same shall, at the convocation of the Roman college, also be brought to the town hall so that the propositions may be heard and reported to their principals, since the Protestants, as they have none of the other institutions, can come to no conclusions. It is assured that the chief commissaries believe that there is no change in these matters, that both parties are to settle them, which from before [tends] to satisfaction.

AMSTERDAM, 19 February. On the 21st of the preceding month, between the gentlemen Directors of the Dutch East India Company, there has been held at the residence of Mr. H. the Lodgings on the Burg within Leiden, in a well-situated and renowned estate called Kleyn Foelgeelt, together with its Back Lands and further property, in a spacious, large and well-conditioned Manor House, with several gentlemen's dwellings, Grain stores, Stables for 4 Horses, two flourishing French Fruit gardens planted for 7 years, Vegetable gardens, two rows of tall Yew trees; as well as 2 Fields of good Pasture land, Mills, and land on the Farm, one half of which from the Koudekerk morning equally good and well-maintained Pasture land, opposite the aforementioned fold under Koudekerk; being two distinct Leaseholds, held in fee—

Notice is given that on Saturday the 11 February 1721, in the evening at 5 o'clock, there shall be offered for sale and sold, the Noble House and Estate, together with all appurtenances, consisting of the said Noble Estate in a handsome structure, with suitable apartments, two stone drawbridges, fine Dwellings, Gardens, of which one is planted with Arbor vitae of the most exquisite Fruit trees, all thereof flourishing in its Lanes and Walks of tall Yew on both sides of the Estate, together with great Pastures and meadows by the Mountain, under the jurisdiction of Koudekerk; moreover 18 morgens of good Estate, the first two Fields lying in the Lagewaardie fold under Koudekerk and the County of Holland.

Notice is hereby given that the sale of Senegale Gum, which was fixed to be sold at Rotterdam on Saturday the 22 February 1721, shall not proceed as intended, but shall be postponed until further notice.

Notice is given that on Monday the 13 February 1721, in the morning at ten o'clock at the Flower Market in Leiden, there shall be offered for public sale a considerable quantity of Turkish and Sayette goods, consisting of: item old black cloth of 4 and 6 yards, all kinds of fine black cloth and damasks and canvas and some implements pertaining to the Goods office [?]. Brokers shall on Monday the 14 February 1721, at Amsterdam in the Out House offer for sale Stables, Barns, Sheds and Lands, one situated as a dyer's works, approximately 30 morgens, used by Gerrit Claadtz. Droft, and [one of] approximately 31 morgens, used by Picter Claadz. van Schalk, all further details to be obtained from the respective Auctioneers.

Whereas the Heirs under Benefit of Inventory of Daniël Garboriel, Merchant in Amsterdam in his lifetime, stand in accounting with all known Creditors for settlement; therefore notice is hereby given and published that they submit their claims or petitions within the said time in London to the Heirs office.

David Rabten and Willem de Vries, Brokers, shall on Wednesday the 26 February [sell] tables and chairs, English Office Cabinet with mirrors, Porcelain Cabinet, old Porcelain, and other rare articles, Silverware, Paintings by great Italian [masters]—

At Sichhouser and My Booksellers at Amsterdam on the Rockin by the Rue des Trois Princes de Sorendre, in 12°, with figures; as well as Description of Sr. i 23, 2 volumes with plates. Extract from the History of Spain by Mr. l'Abbé de Vayrac 12, [?] continued from the Lords Addition and Steele 8, second Part. Remarks on the Country of Louisiana, situated on the River Mississippi, in 8°.

In Leiden printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedelian above the City Hall, 21 February 1721. With Privilege.

---

Spain

CADIZ, 28 January. From the received letters from Court, and therein enclosed the Council and Cabinet memoranda regarding the arrangement of Commerce causing [?]. The armed Ships of [?] shall this be His Majesty's [?]. It is said that the fleet of Genio shall return, [and] it is thought it will be able to mentioned here in the end of March or in May here to the west coast [?]. On the West Indies they report that the [?] between the [?] [?] [?] shall be [?]; the Ships of the [?] Robinfon from London Simoon and Adriaan Leyen [?] the Rabatien, and the English the Leiamis, and on the other hand [?] Jeronimo Oneto toward [?] toward the Levant, and still [?] with him [?] [?] and 2 [?] Ships—

MADRID, 4 February. [Dispatches] from the King report that [on] Saturday evening past the Cardinal Bo[?], bishop of Lucon, in the presence of [?]. Sunday, their Majesties as customary at the Royal Chapel [?]; the King [?] on their elevated Place [?] [?] Breviary to the King [?] [?] seat must [?] Place of the Cardinal Be[?] [?] before the King [?] of the Cardinal. Mutte [?] [?], by the [?]; following [?] there was yet the Procession in which their [?] have [?]; His Eminence at the [?], also [?] that he [?] the ordinary Ceremony: Royal Chapel in the morning [?] of Ferdinand or was Don Andres del Or[?] [?] invested in the Caroli [?] [?] was, [?]. The King has [?] [?] disposed, until [?] of [?]; and then [?] Chancellery at Valladolid place to Don Nicola: Commander of the [?] da Montmamor, general [?] of the [?] Guards [?] Don Hieroin of the Regiment [?] [?] [?]. The Briefs [?] that one [?], and [?] their M[?][?] would be: to [?], and not [?] the [?] and... [?] appear [?] make [?] a great [?] on his high brother—

Gothenburg

[?] of Soldiers [?] 100 [...] sailing Galley[?], so that our [?] [?] have [?] or [?] already their [?] [?]. [?] there [?] Under Pieter Ka[?] [?] Ships toward Flanders [?] but the Swedish [?] [?], Orders toward [?]—

Germany

CORNLANDS Count Taube, who [?] was [?]—

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 2 March 1721, there shall be sold at Dordrecht in St. Jo— one [property] with 4 [?] and the other [?] well-conditioned Salt Pans therein; together with Root-vats, Tubs, Hoops, and [?], Drains, large and small [?] and well-situated dwelling houses, standing by and around the aforementioned Salt works at Dordrecht; whereof the conditions of sale may be found there with the Notary Bartholom[aeus]—

On Friday the 9 March 1721, there shall be sold at Dordrecht in the Rietzijdke Haven all Cables, Blocks, Topsails, and further all Equipment for a Warehouse ship, as they were in the Goods also to be seen in the Harbor beforehand with the Notary Bartholomaeus van Gelido.

Mr. Thomas Angelbaume, Surgeon, Operator and Hernia specialist at Amsterdam, on the Spiegelgraft at the White Sun, makes known that through his operations on Sunday evenings and some other times, children may be treated; being sufficiently experienced in my art through use; [?] [that] I treat all sorts of Venereal diseases and Accidents, so I heal all [?] and Medical [?], as I am myself [?] Medical [?], provided they first give me notice of their [?], then [I] will proceed with treatment, such being better, for which I have in my House various of the aforesaid remedies; through the aforesaid medicines are also healed many of the so-called sick—

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 21st of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 24.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 24th of February, 1721

Monday's Gazette

Spain

CADIZ, 18 January. Various meetings have been held here regarding the implementation of the latest Resolution from Court, and in response, several burdens on the Commerce Council and the Consulat of Seville have sent still stronger Remonstrances to Madrid to present orally to His Majesty what damage the transportation of silver to the Indies would cause to Commerce, and a more favorable reply is expected on this matter. Some of our merchants have received their silver from the most convenient ships from Vera Cruz, and when His Majesty learned of it, they were forced to return the same. It is said that the indult for the second consignment of silver will expire at one percent, and no further mention will be made through the most important letters. The departure of the Galleys remains fixed for the end of March or the beginning of April; and people here are already equipped with supplies for the Armada toward Buenos Aires. From the West Indies one has no news whatsoever; but it is understood that the Peace between the King of Great Britain and the King of Morocco will finally take place; which will expand Commerce between the two nations and make it flourish. In this Bay the following ships have been noted: those of Captains Bronfhorst from Dunkirk, Franciscus Robberije from London, Thomas Elveya from Scotland, Jan Rapp, Symons and Adriaan Leyen from Amsterdam, the ship la Victoria from the Rarantes, and the English warship of Captain Thomas Schot from the Levant; and the following have been drawn back in reverse: Thomas Uitenberg to Hamburg, Ieropoimio Oneto to Lisbon, Captain Mocaid to St. Lucar, Jan Hindman to the Levant, and Captain Cadenal to Marseilles: so that there remain still in harbor: 200 [?] one Genoese, 26 English, 27 French, 6 Dutch and 2 Danish ships.

MADRID, 4 February. Last Thursday the King, the Queen, and the Prince arrived here from El Pardo, and Their Majesties attended in the Evening in their Chapel; that evening the Cardinal Borgia in his Oratory, by the hands of the Bishop of Laresi, in the presence of H. Landi, performed the ceremony as was customary, whereunto the happy desire of Cardinal Muste is accustomed to take precedence. Sunday, the Feast of Candlemas [Editor's note: Feast of the Purification of Mary], Their Majesties took Communion after custom and were present in public in the Chapel; the Queen and the Infants took their elevated Places, whereupon H. Landi immediately delivered the Papal Brief to the King, which by the King, a notary called Kapar, was presented to His Eminence, seated in the ordinary Place of the Cardinals and affiliated with the Lord Landi, advanced before the King's hearing, which His Majesty had preceded with the Mufte ceremony, the Archbishops having been dismissed, some of whom were dressed Pontifically in Purple and Cardinal's Robes; subsequently the Wallstaten were blessed by the Eminent, and the Procession which took place in the Galleries of the Palace, in which Their Majesties and the Prince also participated; and after Emini., having come in order, he took possession of the Cardinal's seat, opposite that of the King, and thereafter performed the ordinary Ceremony of the High Mass, as Grand Master of the King's Chapel; it is said that a similar Ceremony took place during the reign of Ferdinand and Isabella. On the twelfth day Don Andries del Orbe was consecrated Bishop of Barcelona in the Jesuit Church, which Ceremony was performed by the Archbishop of Toledo, assisted by the Bishops of Laren and Tornel. The King has disposed of the two vacant Places in the Council of Castile, and given the same to the Licentiate Don Hieronimo Pardo, who is Chaplain and Auditor in War, and to Don Blafao de Orozco President of the Chancellery of Valladolid, as well as the War Council place to Don Nicolaas Manriques y Lara. His Majesty has given the Commandant of Villa Creida to de Haro [?] to Don Pedro de Menta-major, general of the Galleys; that of Africa to the Captain of the Wall Guards Don Georgio de Bay; that of Berga to the Colonel Dios Hipoiromis de Beiterne, and the Lieutenant Colonel place of the Regiment of Horse of Sicily has been assigned to Don Francisco Faudrus. The letters from Ceuta of the 20th of the previous month report that the evening of the rocky road there has been laid, one is also engaged in forming an Esplanade there, and one continues with all strength the progress of the Trenches and Blockade of the River: during which time the Barbarians appeared daily before our Army, and made a great Fire on the outer Works each time, which, since one could hear it well, was thereupon damaged.

Sweden

GOTHENBURG, 1 February. One is here strongly engaged in the recruitment of Soldiers and Sailors, both for the manning of the Fleet and for the strengthening of the Army: it is also announced that the right-hand toll in Västergötland will also be carried out, so that our Troops have already taken possession of Mainland and its Coasts [?]. Notwithstanding that it began to thaw here a day or two ago, the Rivers remain swollen, allowing outgoing ships [to depart], which include one under Pieter Barger, Dirk Sprceuw and another ship called Amsterdam, Captain Richard Robbinson and another to London, two brigs to Youghali, one to the Baltic, a Flute from Delft with the second Swedish ship of Olof Bundle to Lisbon, and as is said Fred. Oldeis to Amsterdam, lying at sail or have already departed.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms, Etc

COPENHAGEN, 11 February. Last [?] arrived here Count Taube, who ended his journey to Sweden, which [?] galleries Count [?], along with several other Gentlemen of [?]

France

PARIS, 17 February. Last Saturday the King was complimented by all the Princes, the Nobility and the entire Court, which was very numerous, on the occasion of his Birthday; and His Majesty entered into his 12th year of age; that day a Ballet was held, but the King did not dance at it, because the Maréchal of Villars thought that the numerous exertions might cause harm to the King's health. The Parliament the same day, having assembled in the presence of the Princes and Parisian [nobility], brought the case of the Duke of la Force onto the Carpet; the majority of votes were against him and especially the Prince of Conti; but the Duke of Bourbon, the Dukes of Noailles and Estrées were less severe and spoke in his favor; however it was decided to summon him against a certain time to be heard, as well as his Steward M. Bernard, and the Corps was granted certain [?] of the Orient, who has kept his Name for the purchase, and it was ordered that the purchased Goods should be forfeited, one third for the benefit of the general Hospital, one third for the benefit of the Hôtel Dieu, and one third for the benefit of the Merchants' Guild, as a loving gift of discovery. Among all the Discourses one hears on this matter, there are also those who maintain that he should be deprived of his Dignity and recognized as a merchant; which was established by an Arrest of Parliament; his strong Parties are the Grocers and Apothecaries; it is assured that he has engaged in 's a M. Millions in Paper Effects in Merchantship. The Magazine of Spices under the great Augustinians now under examination is estimated at only 12 to 15,000,000 Livres, besides which one has discovered more Merchandise in other Places, such as Walch, Spanish Soap, Indigo, Coffee, Tea, Porcelain, Handkerchiefs, Galangal and other Drugs, as well as Chinese Cards du Feu at 300 Livres each, besides yet a very great quantity of Coal, all of this being regarded as a scandalous Monopoly, because it is certain that these operations have been a great cause of the high prices. Meanwhile the investigation of further monetary Misconduct and like Transgressors is diligently continued; and the aforementioned case of the History of la Poree was yesterday brought onto the Carpet in Parliament. Gilleetus was also Councilor of Regency, but the Arrangement concerning the Company of the Indies to give it to give an accounting to the Bank has not yet been regulated, but it is hoped that the Arrest of 26 January will be renewed. Many capable Gentlemen speak strongly in favor of the said Company of the Indies, and especially the Lord d'Armenouville; who interests himself powerfully for that Company; it has even been rumored that the Accounts in the Bank will be reopened, upon which those of Lyons and others are pressing hard. Tidings have been received that the Lord Law will remain in Venice, and that he has purchased a Palace there for 50,000 Ecus. According to the latest Letters from Rome, a Congregation of the Holy Office was held there to examine the Mandate of the Cardinal of Nouailles on the Constitution and its legitimacy in Parliament, over which the Pope is not very satisfied, but no decision will be made on this until the Cardinal of Rohan shall have arrived: according to his own letters the Emperor demands a Cardinal's Hat for the Lord Alexander Albani, second Nephew of His Holiness. The Turkish Ambassador is not expected here before [?] and it is reported that he will make his Entry on Horseback. The Engineers, who are to have supervision over the excavation of the double Canal, of which mention was made before, have already departed. The Project to extend the Bank notes from time to time, and to do so by gradations of ten to 100 per Month, was presented to the Council but not adopted. A certain Monsieur de Vieth, who is a shrewd Financier, has also submitted several Memoranda concerning the Money troubles and Ambitions, to give them a fixed value, in order that they would circulate better and to revive Commerce; but it is feared that these Projects will also be rejected, because they do not tend toward the abolition of Paper debts. It is assured that the Lord de la Houlsay finds his Office of Controller General of Finances less and less [valued?]; and the standing of the Lord d'Armenouville is very good there. It is said that notwithstanding the proceeding on the part of the Court against the Benedictines of the Chaloris, the Prelates d'Axfeld, Cavoirier and de Rieu, along with many other Doctors, have renewed their Appeal to the forthcoming Council, the Parliament will not wish to be made green again, and one is not without fear that many Bankruptcies will then break out; one counts the Lyons along with as 4,000 Laborers, who from lack of work will burden the State; [?] no Manufactures for the West Indies are being founded, the delivery of the many Projects of the Fiercely to settle is the reason that the common National Natural [?]

[Much of the opening passage is severely corrupted in transcription and cannot be reliably translated. It appears to discuss Spanish financial matters and fleet preparations at Cadiz.]

Great Britain

LONDON, 13 February. On Saturday, the Lords brought before them Knight John Blunt, one of the Directors of the South Sea Company, and put many questions to him, which he excused himself from answering, saying that the matter had already been examined by the late Committee, and that he had answered their questions at that time, which he could not now recall from memory. He requested that their Lordships would excuse him, to avoid confusion with his previous contradictory answers. [Editor's note: This refers to the growing South Sea Bubble scandal.] He argued that the laws do not compel anyone to incriminate himself. The Lords, after deliberation upon this day's proceedings with the aforementioned Knight Blunt from Saturday, found him culpable of great contempt toward his duty and the good efforts of Parliament, declaring such conduct to be of very dangerous consequence for the Kingdom. This evening and yesterday, the first and second readings were held of the Bill concerning the incorporation of part of the South Sea Company's capital into the Bank, which the Bank and the East India Company are to hold in Committee on the coming Monday. This Bill, concerning the investment of 9 millions of the South Sea Company's capital in the Bank, and an equal sum from the East India Company, taken together with the Communities, has caused considerable unrest among the subscribers of the Royal Annuities, as they had imagined that, since this Bill was formed upon a plan agreed upon with a report of the Directors of the South Sea Company, the House would have relieved them of their subscription obligations. However, these interested parties, now considering another course of action, have employed all means to obtain relief. To this end, many of the principal subscribers have formed a Society to bear the costs involved, and have thereby engaged certain celebrated Advocates, who find no difficulty in freeing them from these obligations. It is thought that the secret Committee will make its report to the House on the coming Thursday, and then will be revealed the great Frauds, Irregularities, Villainies, and infamous Dealings, &c., of which nothing was heard before.

It is reported that the Earl of Sunderland has died, whilst he was in the House of Peers, where he had delivered a very temperate, yet fiery and widely applauded Remonstrance against the Directors of the South Sea Company. [Editor's note: Charles Spencer, 3rd Earl of Sunderland, died on 16 April 1722, not February 1721 as this account suggests—possible dating error in the original.] On Sunday morning, he was bled for the great pain he felt in his side, but the pain moved to his head, and he died that evening between eight and nine o'clock. His death is greatly lamented by all, and is mourned by the Nation. The King was so affected by news of his death that he retired to his Chamber and did not dine that evening. That same day, Thomas Vernon, Esq., Member of Parliament for the County of Worcester, also died, and is much regretted by the Public for his great legal knowledge. It is confirmed that the Knight [?], Treasurer of the South Sea Company, was arrested on the 3rd of this month whilst traveling from Brussels to Liège, and has been taken into custody in the Castle of Antwerp, where he is imprisoned and strictly guarded, along with his sons and certain servants. Yesterday morning the Post from Bristol and Gloucester was again stopped. The execution of 6 robbers, which was to have taken place yesterday, has been postponed until tomorrow, out of respect for the birthday of the late Queen Anne, which is being observed with the usual commemorations.

There is now to be seen in the London Gazette an Act whereby [?] made in the 5th year of the Reign of Queen Anne, and which has the title of an Act for better observance of Quarantine by ships coming from infected places, in order to prevent the introduction of contagion into Great Britain, Ireland, or the Islands of Guernsey, Jersey, Alderney, Sark, or Man; and to prevent the spread of infection, the said Act has been strictly implemented, though it is too lengthy to be reproduced here.

This evening at 5 o'clock, being Monday 24 February 1721, the Messrs. [?] at Amsterdam at the Lodge will sell two extra fine estates: one situated at the Ronde Hoep in the former lands called by Geest Claude Drot, and the other in the Helendregg Folder near Abcoude. Johannes Soars, Jan Lemmens, and Hendrik Linquer, Brokers, will sell this evening at 5 o'clock at Amsterdam on the Singel at the bridge: a parcel of 40,000 pieces of gray beaver pelts, 900 pieces of walrus teeth, a parcel of fine Muscovite mats, and other goods.

By Jean Frederik Reizaard, Stonemason and Seller of Religious Works at Amsterdam: Commentaries and Reflections upon every Chapter of the Old and New Testaments, by [?], which are useful for all who wish to read the Bible with profit.

The Church Deacon of the Hebrait Temple, Dirk Valkenburg, formerly Deacon of the Portuguese Jews at Amsterdam, [requests?] with proper documentation before the first of April [?], at the corner of the Singel and Tounsteeg at Amsterdam.

---

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 20 February. Letters have been received here from Madrid reporting that, after holding a great Conference at the Spanish court, which the King himself attended, it has been resolved to bring the troops back from Africa to Spain. The Marquis [?] had sent a personal letter to an officer of the aforementioned [?], in which he expressed satisfaction with his conducted operations. During some days the Spanish army had suffered a lack of supplies, which caused various misfortunes, but upon the arrival of a new convoy they recovered their spirits, and had already taken possession of the [place?] from the enemy. A certain officer writes from the troops before Ceuta to one of his friends that the Moors are making continual attacks, and their army before the Spanish positions [?] so that the troops are much harassed, which causes much sickness. [...] it was thought that the troops had [?] to the Battle [?]. It was feared that the troops would [?] daily with more [?] the troops, for which the Marquis de Liede worked with all effort to send further reinforcements and maintain order of the place, which had been much damaged, and the Moors had made their last attack. At Cadiz they were already beginning to prepare the galleons, but the great [?] of the Spanish troops could not [?]. The silver brought there by the galleons was for the most part sent to Seville and stored there. It is written from Castena that the Plenipotentiaries who are to attend the Congress there have begun to arrive in that city, but the precise day when the Congress is to open has not yet been fixed. Some wished that the French Minister, the Abbot and Bishop of that city, had delayed his journey there for some days, as it was imagined that the Pope would have wished to await the purple robes before coming, and not to appear in his Archbishopric; but others advise that this delay should have occurred only after the Peace was concluded there. But His [Holiness?] had caused Cardinal [?] to come for this matter. The case of the Duke of la Force made great commotion in Paris among the Princes and Peers of the Realm, and there was much uneasiness at court over it, as it seemed to give some trouble to the Duke Regent, since one had at first taken him for a merchant, and now through examination of the ships under which the goods had been found, it was discovered that all those goods belonged to the same Duke. The matter was taken very seriously in the Parliament of Paris, and those who held the goods in possession were brought to the Bastille or to the Château, to be further examined, and subsequently the goods were removed.

Letters arriving from Toulon and Marseille assure us that the contagious sickness there had been sufficiently arrested, but in the latter-mentioned city there were still some cases in the Hospitals; on the surrounding land the plague began to diminish. According to some letters from Vienna, it is claimed that Prince Eugene of Savoy might well come to Vienna this summer to attend to important matters, though his present occupation in high affairs there may not permit it. [Editor's note: Prince Eugene of Savoy (1663-1736) was a celebrated military commander.] Nothing else is reported concerning the ships that have departed from Ostend for China, as it is firmly held that they will have completed their journey [Editor's note: This likely refers to the Ostend Company's early trading ventures].

AMSTERDAM, 23 February. On the [harbor?] (where the river [?] had begun to freeze) no ships had departed, but at Calais Joris Boudewyns of Rotterdam; at Scheen in Norway Anne Boukes of Huijsberg; in the Sound Philip Pijck of Krombieghen for Elsinore; Jan Heffels of Danzig for [those parts?]; and Tomas Doplou of Danzig for Newcastle; at Bergen Christ. Vict. of Trondheim. From Vlissingen have departed to sea and thereabouts: one to the Strait [of Gibraltar], one to the coast of Africa, Jacob Japyn, Joost Cornelielie, and Capt. Wergow to the West Indies. On Friday came into the Roads Stephens from Norway and Klaus Jullien from the Goethees.

THE HAGUE, 23 February. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West Friesland are scheduled to adjourn here on the 1st of March. On that morning the Gentlemen Commissioners of the Admiralties departed for home, and the Directors and Bewindhebberen [shareholders] of the East India Company departed for Amsterdam. [Various other appointments and deaths are noted.] [...] that the Lord Goos, as former Envoy from this State to the court of Denmark, is to undertake the journey there again shortly. Mr. Ammema, formerly Counselor of [?], sometime Douanier of Nassau, &c., has been buried here these last two days. His remains have been transported to Friesland and are to be buried there with his ancestors. [...] from the woods on [?] 1721, the Brokers Hermanus Bienna, [?] will sell two extra fine House Dwellings, Stables, Barns, Sheds and [?] of [?] water, circa 30 morgen, in their use in [?] District [?], about 3 morgen, in their use with Pieter Claud[?]

[...] will be sold at Amsterdam on Wednesday 16 February 1721: [...] all kinds of vegetable gardens, a parcel of red, yellow and blue corduroys, a parcel of fine Muscovite linen, linen and silk goods, [?] extra coarse [?]

---

Printed at Leyden by PETRUS DE KLOPPER, on the Redelstraat above the Town Hall. 4 February 1721. By Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Course of EXCHANGE is at Amsterdam 33s. 9d. to 11s.; at [?] 33s. 5d. to 7d.; Rotterdam 34s. 7d.; Antwerp 34s. 10d.; Hamburg 32s. 7d. to 8d.; Paris 25 and ¼ to 25¼; Bordeaux no fixed price; Madrid 43 to 44 and ¾; Cadiz 42 to 43 and ¾; Bilbao 43½; Livorno 50½; Genoa 52; Venice 44½; Lisbon 5s. 3d. and ¼ to ½; Porto 53; Dublin 10½. The Stock of the Bank is at 141; of the East India Company 145; of the South Sea Company 188 (first subscription), [various other subscription prices follow]; Lottery Annuities 96½; Bank Annuities 99¼; Bank price C. 92; the 4 per cent Bank 150; Stock C. 93; new ditto 20; Heir Annuities 6¼; ditto L. 6; and York Stock 24½. The Course of EXCHANGE is today at Madrid 91; Cadiz 173; Seville 114; Bilbao 9[?]; Lisbon 42 and ¾ to ⅞; Venice 81½ to 2; Livorno 91; Genoa 91 to 2½; Paris 41 and [?] 93½; London 33 [?]; Frankfort current 90, [?]; ditto current 29¼; Leipzig current 38 stuyver; Danzig [?]; Breslau 3 and ⅝ st.; Augsburg 32⅛ st.; Antwerp 3 and ¼ pt. C.; Ghent 3½; Brussels 3¼; Zealand ½; Rotterdam 3⅛. The Agio of the Bank 3¼.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 24th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 25.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 26th of February, 1721

Wednesday's News

Germany & Neighboring Realms, Etc

IN the presence of the Emperor, King, and councillors, a meeting was held; the principal subject of this council session concerned the security of the Realms of Heilbronn Castle and Rhinefels, and it was decided to request the Landgrave of Hesse to cease hostilities in a friendly manner. It is understood that the Rhine General would maintain his troops in good order. Although a dispatch not from Milan, sent by Count Colloredo with various opinions regarding the Chamber Officials who have served there for some time and have been dismissed in these times, the courier has been sent back with the same dispatch, so that in short some great execution might be heard of there. Through several officers, as we understand, confirmed unanimously by reports that have arrived, the Porte is filling out its troops and providing the fortifications with all necessities; that besides the new warships and others being built there, a great number of galleys and munitions are also being prepared; and the Janissaries themselves had openly stated that all this was aimed at a new war; wherefore the Emperor has the border fortifications in Wallachia and Transylvania repaired in good condition, and work continues day and night to provide many places with new bastions and reinforcements.

Dresden, 16 February

Yesterday a magnificent sledge procession was held here; it was divided into four rows, each row distinguished by color—one in red, another in blue, the third in yellow, and the fourth in green. The King, the Elector-Prince of Saxony-Weissenfels, and the Prince of Württemberg each headed one of them. The trumpeters and musicians led the way, and the train was supplied with more than fifty hand grenades and horses. In the equipages and other items that the nobles had with them for those who would travel in the great court during the sledge race toward the King, an excellent spectacle was presented, and this afforded much pleasure to the court and spectators. It is thought that the King is preparing to travel to Poland in the coming month to attend a Council of State and to take measures to prevent the intentions of the discontented, who still seem inclined to cause disturbances.

Luxembourg, 16 February

Since the time to redress the grievances in the Palatinate has approached, and the 12th of May marks the expiration of the appointed period, the Protestant Corps has earnestly requested the Emperor's principal commissioner to send without delay the second part of the days [appointed] by His Eminence to his own address, with the request that the Elector Palatine be ordered to execute the redress thereof with all speed.

Remonstrance of the Protestants

We dare place these five conclusions of 22 December 1719 and 19 February 1720, as set forth here as Exhibit 1, openly before the whole world, and in particular before all discerning eyes of Your Imperial Majesty, being well assured that when Your Imperial Majesty shall deign to examine them and place them before the eyes of Your ministers who seek and care for peace and order in the Empire, [you will see that] there is nothing in these twenty points that is not a doctrine, well-founded scriptural proof, and confirmed through the Gospel, nor anything directed against the Empire, just as we have shown with clarity and conviction that the Gospel strives for nothing other than the clear content of the Peace of Westphalia, insofar as it affects so many Roman Catholic neighboring realms and the benefit of the highest Imperial Court; and if, in the preservation of this understanding and to avoid harmful and evil discord, the Imperial Estates could no longer support their discovered academies, the end would follow with the platina of Israel [?], unless Your Imperial Majesty permitted otherwise. To speak frankly and deeply: our highest Principals, bishops, and commissioners declare themselves so much and so firmly against the so-called Similaneum because they do not yet know with certainty at this hour whether all Roman Catholic co-estates have, in particular, recognized the doctrine of the Similaneum against the Peace of Westphalia and have changed their opinion, and are now for the Similaneum, and must tolerate the here and there aforementioned deviations and irregular insertions of the Similaneum, which cannot be governed by any rule. Otherwise one would hold it to be true that the Roman Catholics grant consent to so many other dubious principles and violations of the peace. And were the Evangelicals of a different opinion regarding the Roman Catholics in the matter of the Similaneum altogether enforced, which is not the case, then also the Evangelical party would not be prevented from remaining with the ordinances of the Peace of Westphalia, or from pointing out to it when the Roman Catholics undertake something contrary to it, what clear determinations the Imperial institutions provide, and in what manner one must proceed rightly in this matter, but not to lay hands on the protected custody of the Evangelicals, and to defend them with force and permitted means or to agree to and bind themselves and to take up their co-estates, which has never been contradicted for them by the deceased emperors, nor has ever been opposed by all Roman Catholic electors, princes, and estates. Much less then can one entrust to these the decision over the Evangelical Imperial part, as the proposers of the Commission Decree suggest, from which a circumscription of the entire Imperial constitution would rightfully follow.

The customary decisions of the Evangelical Corps for two centuries regarding general administrative matters, petitions, or ordinances valid in the Empire only touch the Evangelicals themselves and are therefore much less subject to revision, as they are directed toward each other and have always maintained firm bonds conforming to the Imperial laws, and from which have been duly drawn. In contrast, the grievances that have arisen for some time past have sprung from disparate representations, which are not new and were never heard of in preceding times.

Regarding the visitations brought forward with great diligence [detailed reference], to obtain everything that could be imagined, we respectfully made submissive representations to Your Imperial Majesty on 8 April last, and when this and other opinions put forward by the Evangelical Corps were subject to judicial sentence, as yet the Imperial institutions do not show, one ought, according to the known rule: Si qua pars altera in media fit, indicatur; & si iuste iudicatur, paerit, yet before any such harshness, nothing unheard should be permitted, and those shall have much to answer for before Your Imperial Majesty who have no other end or purpose than to bring their own partisan views to Your Imperial Majesty, not remembering the known rule that each is the best interpreter of his own words, and not observing the clear content of the resolutions and others, but making their own distortions and falsifications, which never entered the minds of the Evangelical Corps, and with which they nonetheless seek to charge them.

Copenhagen, 18 February

Last Saturday, General Scholten arrived here, and yesterday gave an account of his proceedings to the King. This afternoon, Mr. Brand Brynnerg, the King's [representative], arrived here, and as it is said, will shortly travel further to Stockholm, where he may remain for some time as an extraordinary envoy. Yesterday evening, the Swedish General Taube departed for the court. Justice Councillor Ambruster is now in such a weakened state that doubts are entertained regarding his recovery.

Hamburg, 21 February

According to letters from Berlin, the King and Queen had taken the pleasure of conducting the Bride and Bridegroom—the Countess of Görtz and the Privy Councillor of Kochau—to the bridal chamber, for which purpose the bride presented herself before the Queen and the bridegroom before the King unhesitatingly. This same ceremony was also performed at the wedding of the Privy Councillor of Podewils with Countess Cumbkausen. The following day, the King had traveled to Potsdam, where he intended to remain until the 17th. According to later letters from Stockholm, it is reported that the diplomatic proposals which were proposed to Mr. Hopken at Vienna by the minister of the Duke of Holstein were read in the Senate, and which it is said will be decided upon the following Thursday.

[The proposals are as follows:]

1. That the Czar has guaranteed to the Duke of Holstein the restitution of the Duchy of Schleswig, as well as the title of Royal Highness and the succession to the Swedish Crown.

2. That His Czaric Majesty shall give his daughter to the Duke in marriage.

3. And by virtue of that marriage, His Czaric Majesty shall give to the Princess as a bride-gift Finland, Estonia, and Livonia.

4. That His Czaric Majesty has conceded that these provinces shall subsequently come under the Crown of Sweden.

5. That the Duke of Holstein has likewise consented that the Estates of the Kingdom preserve their right of election.

6. But that if these propositions should not be implemented, it is not unlikely that he will join the Czar to maintain his right by force, &c.

The same letters add that these propositions were communicated only orally by the Holstein minister to Mr. Hopken, so that they did not find it necessary to deny them when they could; moreover, Mr. Hopken declared that he received a copy thereof at Vienna placed in the hands of an Imperial minister, and that this copy was taken from that of Mr. Japhetschisky, who did not according to the Holstein minister claim to have knowledge of these propositions to...

[Text breaks off]

PARIS, 21 February. Three articles are under consideration by the King's Council of State: one concerning trade with the provinces of Languedoc, Lyonnois and Dauphiné. Another concerns the audit of accounts and the Bank; and the third brings regulations for the prosperity of the Kingdom, of which the examination was held at Arcueil on the 26th and shall continue Monday next, and contains 12 articles. The Prince of Conti, the Marquis of Villars and the Duke of Antin, together with many other dukes and peers, appeared in Parliament regarding a trial between the Count of Crequy and the Marquis de Bailly, particularly concerning properties of the Count, in which the marriage contract of Daillance was greatly debated. After several days of hearing at the marriage tribunal, as the second guardians were not recognized, in which marriage the guardians are not recognized. The undersigned letters to one and the other have: recognized certain matters to the Count of Crequy in the enjoyment of goods in question, from the Count of Crequy, his inheritance officers, and even the Marquis a donation, according to the second marriage contract between the Count responded that the said second marriage should not be declared valid for several reasons: first, because the dispensation which the Pope has granted has proven acceptable; secondly, that the Pope's dispensation does not apply in France for such matters, and that one must have letters of the Prince for that purpose; third, that all formalities have been found lacking by Parliament, so that it deemed necessary for the Pope to investigate further.

There was a spectacle of a combat of 6 English roosters, which were armed with steel spurs in the third match on the field, one other had lost its eyes, and the rest were severely wounded: this pleased the King so well that His Majesty said he would wish to see the combat again. On the 17th a commissioner, by order of the Lieutenant General of Police, seized many goods belonging to the Duke of la Force, in a house which the said Duke had allowed; but the Duke's sister and servants being sent, refused the commissioner entry, saying that dukes and peers have no officers but those of Parliament, and since no commissioner had been properly appointed to the dukes and peers, he departed. On the 18th the Duke appeared in Parliament to be heard, but because he refused to surrender his letters without goods, and because he had not communicated his arrival to the dukes and peers, the examination was postponed. From the twelve physicians who renewed their appeal, they have sent word from Gachez.

PARIS, 11 February. As the King on the 15th of this month upon entering his 12th year, was complimented by all the great ones of the Court, the Marshal of Villeroy presented a letter to His Majesty, in which it was related how a certain person, named Jean Grapinjan, being 112 years of age, on the 3rd of this month at Nimes married a young girl, named Henriette Laufs, being a child, presented to him by the Pastor, who was to solemnize the marriage, the inequality of their ages being pointed out, the said bridegroom answered, why a young man of 12 years [?] having passed the number of 100 years [?] should not be able to marry a girl of equal years; upon which the marriage was concluded and consummated.

[No English entries in this section]

BARBADOS, 16 January. The previous Monday the English slaves were hastily freed, and it is said that where the great knots are, many were released. In these events there is an unbelievable commotion regarding the distribution and detail of the house troops or guards, here all manner of fishery is drawn into the enclosed grounds. It is true that the smiths with great loss of their lives, yet not in such a manner, the damage having been lost, for which reason the afflicted are not troubled, since they are greatly occupied. The laws of the city, with many great ones from a British warship to besiege the English.

ST. PETERSBURG, 3 February. Here Mr. Menshikov married his castle [?] with the daughter of the Vice [?], and yesterday with much state the Privy Councillor Ostermann with his lady was conducted by His Imperial Majesty, where a magnificent reception was given by the count Menshikov, at which the guests remained. The Swedish mission concerning some captured officers for 4 days was received in the [?] to make report. Although a general accord was made between Swedish and Muscovite commanders, [?] during his stay here much [?] he showed satisfaction, and since [...] Mr. Ostermann shall depart with a Swedish suite to conduct negotiations of peace, of which one hopes not withstanding daily news [...]

AMSTERDAM, 24 February. At the Canary Islands the ship Mary, Capt. Fitzgerald from Ireland has arrived; at St. Jago the St. Anthony, Capt. Jost Hoop; at Tenerife the Sirena, Capt. Richardson, from the Levant bound for London, at Passage through storm and contrary wind the ship of Capt. Louis de Fuentes, some days ago separated from his convoy afterwards broken at Hastilejo (thenceforth no plague sickness more reported, but still some work to be done cleansing the houses and kitchens) the 2 unloaded ships of Capts. la Troulte and Capt. Jan Buttildt sent from St. Domingo; at Bordeaux the Greyhound by Hendrik Willemis, and at St. Martin the Falcon by Janisse Valk, both from here to Rochelle the ship la Principelle from St. Domingo on the Thames the Capts. Henry Bendfort of the 2 Brothers Capt. Courter from here, Pieter Mobby, Will. Ham and William Barndilton from Sicily, Will. Wadion from Cadiz, Samuel Biggs from Malacca and James Haddilton from Jamaica. At Bahama-and there presently all the ships towards the [?] and the Meuse must proceed. The ship la Eydele, Capt. Martyn has departed from Nantes for St. Domingo; from Roiad Mertine Sloetard towards the [?] from the Thames Robbert Bream toward that city, Will. Benet to Middelburg, and most others. The four outbound East India ships, namely Dorodis, the Marianita, Bendigo and another from Torbay to the end of the Channel, on the 22nd of this month through contrary wind put into Portsmouth, along with various other ships and also at Cowes the outbound fleet toward the Virginias, comprising some 20 ships. Near Mandaal has come Capt. Kok, from Copenhagen to Drontheim with [?]; various untroubled Peter Hoorn; Jan Adams, and other Dutch ships were lying; Klaas Gerrritie; at Staveren Fredrik Bicker from Ahuys; at Delftzel through the customs brought sloops from Hamburg there under [?], Adeling, Egge Staype &c. Tichelen, Drontheim and Bergen in a smack, coming from Königsberg, being [?].

THE HAGUE, 25 February. Yesterday morning the British Minister Mr. Dayrolles was with some gentlemen of the Government. It is reported that a courier from the British Court to the colony Stantiope at Madrid has been dispatched. Yesterday a public coach with passengers from Scheveningen to Zealand departed.

Tomorrow will be offered until the receipt of the second lot [?] of their goods being sold, and the same to advantage this morning at 9 and this afternoon at 2 o'clock, at Amsterdam to be sold; an extra curious party of gold and silver fabrics, Moors; colored gold, blue and vermilion ribbons, corseted and white wool stockings, striped and flowered chintz, Bitamlieus, Serdes days before the sale to be seen.

Schooll, in his lifetime a merchant at Amsterdam, has [his] account settled and creditors established is now, that they lodge their claims or protests within the said time at the [?].

Some residents, of the known Treaty described by [?] of His F.H. House Maj. to the Ottoman Court, contains: [...] here, his clear Majesty, the [?], the [?] [...] and other of the undersaid nation policy very curious to learn.

His servants announce how Schiblets, living in [?] all and one in which some claims or protests shall arrive late from there and next April, given to address to the Registry of Amsterdam.

KLOPPER, on the Breeddelraat February 1721. [Address] France.

NAPLES, 4 February. Sunday in the usual manner observed, and a wagon [?] was delivered.

ROME, 5 February. Sunday, [?]; the Pope held chapel in the hall with the Sacred College and [?]. Subsequently the chapter performed the Te Deum sung; for the [?] this city of [?] in January from [...]. That same day was the church of St. Maria Major [?] and this afternoon several consultations in the Righelic Church were held, and were presented, and among other things on the nomination of cardinals as conductor by Pope Paul V. to Pope [?] granted) the bishopric of Tyren Rapulla [...] Rome King in Zevenbergen wide a [...] Glücke Preacher. That evening the [...] Arimantsme with the Pope, [?] [?] happened on the [...] D'Abbeguy a time. That evening held [?] from a Venetian, to [...] further to [...] the [...] at St. John brought, servant of the Cardinal de Nouilles, whose [...] for the cardinal [?] Crucisingius, Beagielsen; [...] until [...] Numtua to this not much [?] well [...] of His H. [...] is to Traentes [...] is the H. Sainprini [...] of [...] the Cardinal Aquaviva in [...] held on the lands the House Pavseli. England [...] Bass church and Holland, [...]; in [...] [...] for [...] [...] 1724. Mas is finished.

Course of Exchange

The EXCHANGE RATE today is: Madrid 90 and 1/4. Cadiz 89 and 1/2; Seville 171 and 1/2; Bilbao 90; Lisbon 44 and 1/4; Venice 81 and 1/4. Livorno 90 to 90 and 1/2. Genoa 91. Hamburg [?] 42 and 1/2; London 33 Sch. 1 to 2; Frankfurt current 29 and 1/4 Reichsthaler. Antwerp 29 to [?]. Mechelen [?] and 1/4 St. Hamburg 32 and 7/8 to [?] and 1/2 St. Antwerp 3 and 1/4 per C. Ghent 2 and 1/4 to 3. Brussels 3. Zeeland 1 and 1/4; Rotterdam 2 guilders and 1/2. The Bank Agio 5 and 1/4. The shares of the E.I.C. are 750 to 55; W.I.C. 110; Subscription 600; the English Bank 141 to 142, ditto E.I.C. 143 to 30, ditto S.S. 180 to 63, all current.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 26th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 26.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 28th of February, 1721

---

Tetuan, January 16

The deputies appointed by our Court to the English Embassy have accepted the proposed concession to travel to Miquenes, where English slaves are to be released. Reports also mention that large convoys have set out from there by sea, and that other fleets have been assembled. In this city and the surrounding areas, an incredible number of troops are encamped, consisting largely of Moorish forces and the household troops or guards of the Emperor, whose maintenance requires provisions of all kinds to be gathered here—since it cannot be established that the people of these lands have great resources. It is true that Spanish troops have forced our army to abandon its entrenchments with considerable losses, but it is equally certain that through continuous fighting they have also lost many brave officers and common soldiers, which is why there is no great concern for their further progress, especially since more troops continue to arrive at our army daily, in which, as also in this city, many courtiers are present. In this harbor lies a British East India ship to convey the English Ambassador to Gibraltar.

---

Russia

PETERSBURG, February 3

On the 28th of the previous month, Herr Mathaofyn Blumentx [?] was married, and on the 19th of the same month the daughter of Vice-Chancellor Baron van Schafirof [?] was married. Yesterday, the wedding of the secret councilor Alderman with the Russian Countess Tricdotch [?] was celebrated, with this last couple being led to the Church of the Holy Trinity by His Czarist Majesty himself. This was followed by a splendid feast in the palace of Prince Menshikov, where Their Czarist Majestys remained until late in the evening. The Swedish Adjutant Dahlman, having completed his commission regarding several captured officers here, departed two days ago. Although many doubt whether a general exchange agreement was made for the release of Swedish or Muscovite prisoners, it appears that he had more important commissions to undertake. The aforementioned adjutant received much courtesy during his stay here, over which he expressed satisfaction. It is said that Herr Ollerman may shortly depart for Aboa or thereabouts to confer with a second plenipotentiary regarding an upcoming Treaty of Peace and the Swedish question, the outcome of which we shall await. Nevertheless, work continues daily on the construction of ships, so that in the coming summer, should the Swedes prove troublesome, a confidential fleet may be dispatched to the Swedish coast.

---

Spain

BILBAO, February 7

Beyond the decrees published here on the 11th of last month regarding the entry of foreign ships and their goods, as well as concerning the locks, the following decree has since been promulgated for clarification:

Having received numerous remonstrances and petitions addressed to the Corregidor of this jurisdiction for the purpose of preventing the spread of a certain contagious disease, and considering that this has caused great weakness in commerce because ships carrying goods without certificates proving that each package, case, or parcel originates from lands where this disease does not exist will be subjected to rigorous quarantine; the gentlemen of this Consulate hereby order that, with respect to ships arriving from Holland or England with various merchandise, the captains thereof must prepare a general manifest of their cargo and present the same to the Spanish Consul, in order that he may certify under the said manifest that the aforementioned cases, packages, etc. are from the manufacture of those lands. As to watches, silks, and other merchandise coming from the North, he shall declare that the same have been admitted to free commerce in that land and that they do not come from infected places; and in harbors where no Consul is present, such certificates must be shown to the Governor or ordinary magistrate, in order that they may be given proper credence.

Letters from Madrid give great hope that the customs duties here [Aduanas], which were established during the time of Cardinal Alberoni, will soon be abolished. Several ships have recently arrived here, and due to continuing foul weather, the two ships bound for London and Schipper Claver must remain. It is also said that Captain Antonio del Barco and another ship loaded for the weigh-in are at the harbor.

---

Italy

NAPLES, February 4

Sunday afternoon, Carnival was celebrated in the usual manner, and a wagon of bread was distributed to the common people in the customary fashion.

ROME, February 5

Sunday, being the Feast of the Purification of Mary, the Pope and the Cardinal held consistory, and the customary cardinals attended the Sacred College, and a large multitude of other people. Subsequently, Cardinal Paracciami [?] performed the Mass, after which he kissed the feet of His Holiness and attended the blessing of the Archbishop, which occurred on the same day in the year 1709. That same morning the President's wife went to the Church of St. Mary Magdalene in the Church of St. Ignatius. Monday morning, the consistory was held, wherein, under the consideration and with the consent of Cardinal Althan, upon the nomination of the Emperor as King of Spain (according to the indult granted by Pope Paul V and Philip II, King of Spain), the bishopric of Ypres in Flanders was conferred upon Herr Joan Espatia Smet, and as Roman King the new bishopric of Focarij in Zerawbergen, being a Greek bishopric, upon Herr Joan Patriarch Critici Hisdich.

[Note: A memorandum from Cardinal Alberoni addressing the deputed cardinals, asserting that the said deputation has not the least right to make his proposals...] [Text continues but becomes unclear]

VENICE, February 15

Last Monday, Commendator Fra Camillo Grave of Pola, newly appointed administrator of Malta, made his public entry here with great magnificence. From the shipyard of this Republic, two new warships have been launched. It is said that Herr Lay, who already resides in his palace, might well petition to become a nobleman of this Republic, provided he pay a large sum of money. The day before yesterday, fire broke out in a coal merchant's shed near the Church of St. Mark and the Palace, and had the flames not been driven by the wind toward the sea, the Palace would have been in great danger of catching fire.

---

Spain (Continued)

[Following a note regarding Cardinal Alberoni's memorandum, asserting that the deputation has no right to prepare his reports...]

---

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

VIENNA, February 13

The wedding of the Prince of Lamemberg, which had been postponed due to an illness affecting the bride's mother, was celebrated last Thursday, as the land marshal's wife is now recovering. People here speak of nothing but the plundering of the domain of Rheinfels by the troops of the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel, over which the Landgrave of Rheinfels makes great complaint. It is said that His Majesty has sent directions to both Landgraves—namely to the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel to withdraw his troops, and to the Landgrave of Rheinfels to be wary of giving the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel any pretext; in consequence, the Landgrave of Rheinfels will remain quiet for 14 days, after which, if the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel has not withdrawn his troops, he will, it is said, request that a military control be established over the circle. It is assured that the Landgrave of Rheinfels accuses the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel of having erred in closing the Empire at his insistence.

REGENSBURG, February 20

Since the Protestant Body took up the Imperial Commission Decree of the 10th of this month for examination and to serve as a matter for imperial deliberation, no meeting of the Colleges has been held; it is hoped all will be concluded within 10 days, being postponed at the direction of the Cardinal, which shall be completed within 10 days.

THE RETINUE OF MOHAMET EFFENDI, Ambassador to the Porte at Court, consists of the following Persons: 1. The Ambassador's Son; 2. his Steward; 3. his Imam or Preacher; 4. his Ambassador's Son; 5. his Seal-keeper; 6. his Master Clerk-keeper; 7. his Provisions Master; 8. his Coffee-server; 9. he who tends the Ambassador's Pipe, and his Donkey, the same affair; 10. his Body-servant; 11. his Perfumers; 12. his Stable-keepers; 13. his Body-servant; 14. he who provides the Candlesticks; 15. he who provides the Ambassador's Court for Prayer-Castle; 16. the last Agas, serving as Chamber-servants; 17. his Scale-keeper; 18. his Ceremony-master; 19. the Ambassador's Body-Physician with a chief-Attendant; 20. Soliman Captain, whose position was made by the Maltese Force; 21. Soliman Captain, whose position is made by Sultan Foot-servants. Item those through his Excellency freely appointed, seven Foot-servants; 23. a Jaca or Kitchen-servant; 24. six Kitchen-servants; 25. two Stable-servants; 26. two Furnishers; 27. a Water-carrier; 28. two Cloth-makers; 29. two Water-carriers; 30. his Provisions Masters with two Servants.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 26 February. Letters from the 11th of January from Sophia in Barbary report that the Moorish Legions, numbering approximately seven thousand Men, having assembled with Swallows, small Tartans, and having been made ready. This noted that at Maidli one was led full of provisions, having been freed; what was at Muefti from the Red ones was helped. At Bayonne (where the Nation nevertheless received provisions, through Etienne Somblet &c., by avoidance of Weather and the Port lay) is Capt. Etienne de Barbés, and at Boulevard Mich. Colombeau, Corn. Wednesday morning the War-ships with all the Merchantmen toward Lisbon and S. Ubes went out from Hellsm Tiffell to Sea. Given and heeded one still more Paul the Sea on the Amstel led; also one goes over the V of this City toward the other side.

THE HAGUE, 27 February. The Lord Fiscal Nenny was today with several Gentlemen of the Government.

France

PARIS, 23 February. The Letter which previous Friday through the Parliament and all the Dukes and Peers sent was, was of the following contents: A Court Secret and in the Court of the King [?] His Maj and all his friends in the Parliament in the great Chamber, on the 22 ditto thereof in more of the Force is the verdurch over an Important Matter of the Spanish Great House yet another Procedure, because at, [?] of the Band of Falaise was done, against the Commissioners stood; his Lackeys summoned have, the Request in pieces tore, and his Secretary who the Abbot is, not on the Commissioner's request present in prison spirit, but he himself yet wholly un[?] become. Then named Duke organized in the Palace was come about interrogation to become, he, his Sword not good-friendly laid, after a Servant good great [?] otherwise [?]; and of the presentation made knowledge given to the Duke Regent, who himself there over today exerted in the Meeting of the Chambers of the Parliament is yet not the exit these important matter desired to meet. Previous Monday [?] himself the Duke of Noailles about his resolution in this matter, and give; the Duke and Mahl-light of William two his advice giving, made a great Praise-speech of the Noble Forefathers of the house of the Duke of la Force, who he hoped that himself would have justified: in the uncertainty and the willing and the ground of this matter are some of thought, that this whole work [?] Mirror-Court might remain and in the short by Authority set become through the way of bridging as the great Council. The Father Gourdan of St. Victor is enclosed deceased. The murdering goes also strong in practice, and one heard the Soldiers of the Guard therefore greatly suspected; yesterday is the Door-keeper of the small Augustinians through a One-hook of Vincennes the Briot cut off. According to the last Letters from Spain is one aware, that all the tidings from Africa say, that the Moors themselves more set about the Marquis de Lede to evacuate, as about advantages to obtain. The Emperor has the prohibition of Commerce with the Empire again withdrawn, and ordered that the French Goods again in the Austrian Netherlands received shall be, insofar as these necessary ones of good Certificates from the same from no determined Places come.

Directors of the Company of Commerce, Navigation and Insurance of the City Monnickendam, notify hereby, that quite the greatest yet man some few machinery, happening [?] they an equal what-the Month February shall have to do, as at neglect of that, same turn of the first to the red March 1721, 200 at Monnickendam as Amsterdam, of those Actions, of which already 2 per Cent with Actions is paid, yet have paid, and yet 3 per Cent in Actions want to furnish, can gain of Actions of [?] per Cent in Money furnished, and with promising-Bills; shall those who machinery on the set time to come, then has to repute. The Agent of Actions to recruit, as those which the Actions on his first name in the payment of a per Mille for the Company and 6 St. per Mille for the Poor has.

The Directors of the Commerce, Navigation and Insurance Company of the City [?] Month met engaged, and subsequently all Wednesdays and Thursdays for the middle and second Furnishment against 4 per Cent in the Year, on Ordinance of the Agent thrown out, to Wednesday until [?].

The provisionally appointed Directors of the Expedition Company of the City Zwolle, notify hereby, that the Term of the second [?], by important Reasons is postponed to further notification. Two Furnishments, which set was on the first Monday in March, by important Reasons New [?] Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 5 March 1721, at 5 o'clock in the evening, in the New Lords' Lodgings. St. Domingo Hides is laid as by Notary would be assigned, in March, at 9 o'clock in the morning, in the Old Lords' Lodgings, a party extra fine skins of 7030 Hides St. Domin, and a party fine common Linens, Silk, half Silk, and [?] Linens for Goods, Linen and common Chintz, Damask, Armenian, etc., some Household-goods, an extra beautiful [?] Louie, being a [?], which days before to see.

Filip Fiek Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 5 March, in the morning old Linens, of [?] and 3 quantities, as also one Woolen Stuffs, 200 inland as Foreign, a great party Lapp Linens for Clothes Linens, Calicoes, Ginghams, &c.; Jewels, Gold and Silver, some Household-goods, [?] examine beautiful pots, sugar, and Shoes of Coulours is; all days before to see.

Jan Pieterle Zomer Broker, shall at Amsterdam on Tuesday the 11 March 1721, in the afternoon at 4 o'clock precisely and the following evening, the sell, in the Doele-street by Nicolaas de Ruyfer [?], outstanding [?] of the both Netherlands Masters, as of Mites, Sadeler, Vischer, W. Koningh, T. Wijk, Hollyn, Schakken, Ruisdael, A. van Ostade, Dufart, Everdingen, Molijn, Rademaker, Heupdenberg, de Bray, de Stom van Kampen &c.; Mantegna, J. Romiano, [?], Perugino, Baroccio, T. Zucchero, Corot[?], Tintoret, Io., P. Telia, Pordel, Balen, Corpslet, Poly, Jouvenet, le Brun, Rubens, van Dijk, Muller, van van der Grijen &c. &c which Prints of Raphael of Urbino, [?], Corot[?], Tintorett, Coanus, Ferris, A. Curtas, Guido Reni, J. Angelo del Sar-[?]rubens, van Dijk, Muller, Goltzius, Blommert, Rembrandt, Sadeler, Latte, [?] Works; Zettler, Bouttats from oldest [?] Masters, extra fine of Print; many [?] further to see in the Catalogues, which to be obtained are by J. F. Zomer and [?] of all days to be seen.

One presents from the List of Builders a great, solid, strong and well-wrought solid, strong and well-wrought House, standing and located within the Door North-wick under the Lindens opposite the Swan, provided with 2 fair Ornaments, Beautiful, beautiful inner-Chamber and an extra hung Z[hall, out-lighting Seats, [?] view having on a great Palm Factory, beside 2 spacious Kitchens, a very good [?] cellar, and very good pleasant Provisions and Wand-cellar, above 3 extra beautiful [?] Front-chambers, on the good side of House [?] entry for coach and Horses toward the Coach-house, for Coaches, Carts and Chaise; there behind an extra beautiful Garden, so Hard [?], provided with beautiful Fruit-trees and at the end again a weir and through an equal number on the great Garden; here beside in the breadth Sections, on the Cross-paths and Elle Hedgings manned, of which Sections one is with Asparagus, who [?] Year for the [?] [?] in beautiful Garden, 500 feet long, with a beautiful middle-path and [spaliered], at the end again a fine [?] ['s enjoyed Garden in [?] a great one of which Sections one in full become, and one for Kitchen-garden, being wholly and entirely surrounded with [?] and the [?] 's besides one [?] the [?] the Lord Silverier of Arras, in his Side House dwelling. Someone having interest therein, please direct [?] dwelling.

The Executors of the Treasurer of [?] Dirk Valkenburg, in his life famous Art-Painter, and last dwelling [?], at there all and an equal who some advice or [?] to have thereof on one Steede, itself with suitable evidence, before the first April coming, please to address to the Newmarket at Amsterdam; before [?] in April coming, please to address to the Newmarket at Amsterdam.

---

THE KLOPPER, on the Breadth-street

28 February 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Exchange Rate today stands at Madrid 50 and 1 quarter. Cadiz 114 and 1 half. Seville 115 to 115 and 1 half. Bilbao 50. Lisbon 42 and 3 quarters. Venice 81 and 3 quarters. Livorno 91 to 90 and 1 half. Genoa 93. Paris 42 and 3 quarters to 1 half. London 68 to 68 and 1 half. [?] 29 and 1 half. Delft 29. Boumis and 1. Breslau 35 and 7 [?] St. Hamburg 3 and 7 sixteenths to 1 half St. Antwerp 3 and 1 quarter per C. Ghent 3. Brussels 3 and 1 to 1 half. Rotterdam per C. Ghent 3. Brussels 3 and 1 quarter [?] to 1 half. The Bank 6 and 1 quarter. The Shares of the E.I.C. stand at 735 to 35; W.I.C. 104. Sugar Shares 660 to 680; the English Bank 140, ditto E.I.C. 144, ditto Z.Z. 160 to 65, all according.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 28th of february, 1721. — With Privilege.
