No. 1721.No. 27.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 3rd of March, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 4 February. News has been received here that our deputies recently departed for Madrid have arrived at that Court, so that within 3 or 4 days the outcome of their Commission is expected. Nevertheless, there remains difficulty in bringing silver to the Mint, because certain merchants had prepared theirs in large quantities; however, it is hoped that everything will arrange itself satisfactorily. From the Welsh territories no news has yet been received; and at Ceuta nothing else has occurred. Into this Bay have arrived: Joost Dousart from Martinique, Jan Stokfliedt from Hamburg, Captain Solarium from Lisbon, John Winacoth from Málaga, William Butler from [?], George Coleman from Dublin, and Jacob Roos from Amsterdam. Among those departed were: the ship Thomas Erwil toward Barcelona, Will. Kurin toward England, Joseph Mynard toward Bristol, Philip Streng toward London, and Willem Danecote toward England. There were still to depart one Genoese, 12 English, 25 French, 6 Dutch vessels and two Danish ships.

MÁLAGA, 5 February. Last Sunday four inspectors arrived here, who the following day examined the fortifications of this place and found them in good condition. Tuesday morning they departed from the river, with the prospect that work will begin in the middle of the coming month. The sailors who are expected from Barcelona to man the 3 warships that will escort the merchant vessels lying here have not yet arrived.

GIRONA, 6 February. The Marquis de Castel Rodrigo, who had gone to examine the stationed troops in this province and also the magazines in this domain, having found everything in good condition, has returned here. There is still discussion whether the collection of duties at Vizcaya could be facilitated.

France

MARSEILLE, 4 February. For some time now no sign of contagion has been observed here, and everything improves day by day. It is planned to institute the general Quarantine very soon; and according to the orders of [?], laborers are being sought to prepare themselves against the illness that still persists somewhat in the city. The North Wind, which we have had for some time, has helped eliminate some of the remains of the sickness; however, against this some contend that the sharpness of the wind brings not as much benefit as one might suppose, since bodies, through the very cold air, breathe in the miasma, and thus retain the illness within themselves; for this reason there is concern that with the approaching warmth the illness might break out again.

[The text becomes increasingly difficult to parse in the original, with numerous transcription uncertainties affecting names and dates. The following sections contain significant portions marked uncertain in the original transcription.]

...the Sisters of St. Pierre at Châlons-sur-Marne, on account of their attachment to the Appeal, were dispersed, but this has proven false, because no one of them except Don Thierry was ordered by the Bishop of Châlons to leave his diocese. The Cardinal of Rohan has persuaded the Treasurer of the Chapter of Abbeville to accept the accommodation, but the Dean of this Chapter persists in his opinion. Much is spoken of a certain Letter or Request presented by the 3 Bishops to the King concerning the Decree of 3 December of the previous year, whereby the Mandates and recent Appeals are as if nullified, and it is further stated that said Letter or Request will shortly be made public. It is also said that a portion of the signatures of those who have renewed their Appeal and protested against the accommodation of Cardinal de Noailles will come to light. A letter is seen from the Bishop of Metz to the Bishop of Tarbes, wherein he states, among other things, to be extremely astonished to find his name among a certain printed letter bearing the accommodation, which he protests never to have signed. At present there is great activity with the furnishing of the Castle of Rambouillet in the Faubourg St. Antoine, and the necessary preparations in the houses of private citizens for lodging the Ambassador of the Grand Turk, together with his retinue. His Excellency arrived in Amboile on the 22nd, and may reach this city within a few days via Blois and Orleans, the King's household troops being under strict orders to hold themselves in readiness for his reception. The recent dispute between Prince Charles of Lorraine and the Duke of Noailles is being settled amicably, and to this end the friends of the Prince and those of the Duke came together Friday. It is said that the principal disagreement consisted in this: that the Duke gave 400,000 livres in paper securities in place of money in settlement of the marriage contract; and insofar as is learned, it will be found that the Duke will take back the paper, and now a portion of the marriage gift in money and the remainder in equal manner in installments.

In the old Louvre apartments are being prepared, wherein the principal office for verification of Royal papers will be held.

PARIS, 24 February. The King, who now finds himself in perfect health, grows stronger and sturdier day by day. The day before yesterday the King's Ballet was held with the customary ceremonies. The great comedians presented the piece "Jodelet Prince," but did not perform on the theater. Work is actually underway to erect a small stage for the King, so that His Majesty may be entertained during 2 or 3 weeks in the Valleys with suitable performances. At the Fair of St. Germain "Robinson Crusoe" is shown daily. The Marquise of St. Sulpice, having given a ball some days ago to various women of the first rank, went at the end of the evening meal to her cabinet to change her clothing, and having done so, had the misfortune that her garment caught fire—and no one knows in what manner; being alone, she did all that was possible to extinguish the flames, but being unable to overcome the violence of the flames, she called for help, whereby it was possible to aid her, though her clothes were already mostly burned, and she was found in a pitiful state. The fright from this unexpected accident has affected her so much that she has since been unconscious. The Duke de la Feuillade, who had a very splendid coach made, in order to use it in his embassy, has now presented it to the King, which is said to have cost him more than 20,000 écus. The Pope has declared Mr. Masfer as Nuncio at this court, and it is expected shortly that he will be appointed Archbishop of Albi by Cardinal de Biffy in place of Brydel. It is expected that the sitting of the Sorbonne in the first week of March will not conclude without great commotion, for it is said that the Court will then have the 22 [members] from that assembly restored to office, and that those of the Sorbonne will attempt in every possible way to prevent this reinstatement. The Turkish Ambassador is expected to arrive around the first of March, and will make his formal entry. The King's household troops will be ranged from the place called l'Estoile to the suburb of St. Antoine, where he will take up residence in a great house that has stood unoccupied for a long time, under the pretext of being haunted, by which it earned the name "House of the Devil," and houses for his retinue will be prepared. It is said in the gazettes that the lodgings are ready. The regiment of dragoons of Orleans, which has recently been newly clothed, will be quartered in the suburb to escort him the day of departure. Among other presents said to be presented on behalf of the Grand Turk to the King, it is said there is a skillfully and elegantly made golden crown, and fine horses white and black striped, and likewise hounds similarly marked in white and black stripes. When the Duke of la Force appeared in parliament last Friday, he did not have the patience to hear the reasons of the councillors, and when they rebuked him sharply, the ushers were ordered to lead him out, which also happened. Then the summons was issued for him to be heard by his advocate, and in a pleading petition competence will occur on the 26th and 27th, at which time the chambers of parliament will be convened to examine this matter thoroughly, and he will be questioned. [Text becomes corrupted here] The day before yesterday the Cardinal de Gerves won his lawsuit against the Jesuits at the court of the Palace. The Count of Argenson, second son of the Viennese [?], has been appointed to the intendancy of Tours. A military officer from Louisiana has arrived in Canada, reporting that there [text breaks off].

MARSEILLE, 14 February. For some time now no sign whatsoever of contagion has been observed here, and everything improves day by day. It is planned to institute the general Quarantine very soon, and according to orders, batteries are being prepared, to guard themselves against the illness that still persists somewhat in the city. The North Wind, which we have had for some 60 days, has by some means helped eliminate some of the remains from the sick; however, others contend against this, saying that the sharpness of the wind brings not so much benefit as one might suppose, since bodies, through the very cold air, breathe in miasma, and thus retain the illness within themselves; for this reason there is concern that with the approaching warmth the illness might break out again.

PARIS, 24 February. According to letters from Ceuta of the 2nd of this month, the newly-discovered way at that place became [text corrupted], the army of barbarians then approached to within a quarter hour of the Spanish camp, there making a battery on which they brought up artillery against the Spanish; the latter having made a camp, it was dismantled by the Spanish. The sentiments in the army under the Marquis de Leede [?] regarding whether the Moors and Barbarians would undertake a general attack; and others were of the opinion that they intended only to alarm the Spanish camp... According to letters from Cadiz, 24 merchant vessels with all manner of provisions had arrived there. From Bourges comes word that there, in very many places, carts laden with oxen have been seen.

PUBLIC NOTICE. We remind you not to [?] in the service against the Benedictines, and especially not to set aside the Communion from their hands; carefulness, solicitude, and circumspection, [text becomes corrupted]...

The Procurator General here having been made aware, brought his complaints the following day, and requested letters of ecclesiastical proclamation to counter this; but when the Official [?] would not sign it, the Procurator of the King, at the complaint of the Procurator General here about this incident, wrote. The same letters say that the aforementioned placards had not yet been properly enforced, for which reason [text corrupted]. Some days ago [His Lordship] de la Vieuville sent a letter in the King's name to the Provincial States [?], which, among other things, stated that, besides the reintegration of the doctors, all that, the faculties against the coadjutorship would be repealed, upon which, since this letter was not registered, and such could not take place without the consent of the entire Faculty of Canons, de la Vieuville held a meeting on the letter of [?] of the house of the Sorbonne, to have no voice in the [?] de Vieuville, in the person of Mr. Guerti, who has been appointed by Cardinal de Noailles as Curé of St. Paul. Against the anticonstitutionality of [?], there had been spread a rumor that all the religious Benedictines [text becomes too corrupted to continue reliably]

Left Column

AMSTERDAM

Ships are still being held back there by contrary winds. At Genoa the following are sailing: Jochem Barents toward Hamburg; Pieter Fuyk toward here; Capt. Thomas Hesselink toward Cadiz; and another English ship bound for London. According to reports from the Baltic, good news has been received from there regarding Danzig and other ports. The ship St. Pieter, Hans Bartels, coming from France and having reached as far as the Pilau, has been directed toward the roadstead of Danzig to be brought in there; this ship was also reported to be leaking at the aforementioned roadstead. Capt. Hendr., having departed toward Lübeck, had already sailed from the Sound. At Lübeck, [vessels] bound for St. Hubes and moorings had already sailed from the Sound. At Lübeck has arrived the Mandtmbrck from Danzig, with the ship de Zalm, Peter Hanflic, from Dronthen toward Königsberg, and the Liefde, Joure Okkes, from Friesland toward Christiania; and at Madouw the Agatha, Johan Dorp. Various ships that were expected from the Zuiderzee, owing to severe frost, had to put into the sheltered harbors, namely at Medenblik, Pieter Scholler from Königsberg, and others.

Before Riga: Gerrit Jansen from Vianen, Truykh Beger Tamming, Wiggert Bievelt Jr., and others in Mecklenburg; 6 to 7 ships in the Sloe; and Jacobs Jurjaans, coming from Gorinchem, was nearly stuck on the Scheldt. It is said that from [?] the stocks of the Zuiderzee would be laid up along the southern dike. The stock prices of the English [East India Company] are 710 to 35; [West India Company] 103; Subscription 650 to 680; English Bank 140; [Dutch] East India Company 143; [Dutch] West India Company 100. March 2, 1699.

GERMANY AND ADJACENT KINGDOMS

COPENHAGEN, February 22. The Prussian Councillor Amthor has died at Kifftel and Rosenburg, and was buried with honors befitting his rank; it remains uncertain who will succeed him as historiographer. The peace treaty concluded with Sweden is acceptable here. [The treaty] has been sent to the press in Dutch, German, and French in brief; and there is good hope that Her Majesty will soon be in good health. Some here are of the opinion that in the coming year some thousand men of the King's troops, together with 300 foot and horse soldiers, might be assigned to foreign powers; it is even rumored that all the regiments lying in Jutland and in Holstein have already received orders to hold themselves ready for marching, which time will reveal.

HAMBURG, February 13. According to the latest letters from Berlin, the King of Prussia, before his last journey to Potsdam, visited the tobacco factory of the Jewish merchant Cumperts, to inspect the spinning and processing of tobacco. The same letters report that General Count Rafpell [?], along with General Montargues, near the royal castle, is said to have purchased [property] for 1,500 Reichsthalers, and that the said Count was still in negotiations to purchase more properties at Lüneburg. Word has come from Königsberg that the Danish Envoy Extraordinary and State Councillor Westhaal has arrived there from the Court of the Czar and was detained some days by indisposition, after which, recovering somewhat, he continued his journey back to Copenhagen.

A few days ago, the Prussian Prince [?], brother of the King of Sweden, arrived here from Kassel, and according to report, His Serene Highness will sail toward Stockholm tomorrow or the day after. The Syndic Zander and other deputies, representatives of Stralsund, have recently arrived here from Sweden via Copenhagen, intending to return soon to Stralsund; it is believed that they had an audience with the King of Sweden, but were not successful in their commission. It is assured that the Duke of Holstein will await the outcome of the congress at Brunswick before going to Petersburg, and will for this purpose remain for some time at Breda, to gain the favor of certain German princes; it is reported that the Holstein minister at Petersburg is actively working to promote the forthcoming marriage of the said Duke with a Russian Princess; but recently this was deferred by the Czar, to see whether Sweden might sooner be brought to conclude the peace; the obstacles of the Russians to maintaining a house in Sweden [?] during the war are very great, and many believe this has already occurred.

The Burgomasters and Governors of the City of Rotterdam notify all their subjects that on Wednesday, March 15, 1721, [a meeting will be held;] the Directors of the Company of Commerce, Navigation and Alliance of the City have already received some shares at an earlier date, and some bonds, which have hitherto been neglected, shall be executed this month of February for the benefit of the Company.

It is hereby made known to everyone that the Directors from then on shall vacate their positions upon receipt of [dividends], in guilders, regarding the shares, those who have paid 4 percent in money on their shares and have done so between the first and eighth of March, shall retain their shares of 4 percent, which according to previous advertisements remain assigned; the benefits of the Company shall be forfeited in cases where individuals do not regulate themselves accordingly. The same applies to shares, namely those where the share owner has charged the share in his own name with the settlement of one per mille for the Company and 6 shillings per mille for the Poor.

The Directors of the Commerce, Navigation and Alliance Company of the City [of Rotterdam] on March [?], 1721, and henceforth all Wednesdays and Thursdays for the middle and third expeditions at 4 percent per year, on receipt of the owner.

The provisionally appointed Directors of the Expedition Company of the City [?] [?], which was established on the first Monday in March, for important matters [?].

Jan Pieriele Zomers' book collection will be sold at Amsterdam on Tuesday, March 11, 1721, in the Doelstraat by Nicolaas de Ruyde's shop, comprising the collections of half-Dutch Masters, as well as [works by] Miers, Sadelaar, Villet, W. Komyn, T. W. Everdingen, Molijn, B. Meelicher, Henghestraet, de Bray, de Stom van Kampen, etc., M. v. Teila, Toufik, Balzu, Coypel, Poilly, Jouvencet, le Brun, Rubens, van Dyck, Muller and various others of great masters; black art works, both finished works; further details to be seen in the catalogs, which may be obtained from J. P. Zomers, etc.

Right Column

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM, February 23. From Leghorn (where various ships have arrived), Pieter Reyelte Klopper has departed for Naples; but Skipper Klaas Hooft, who was to come here, was held back there by contrary winds. At Genoa the following are lying: Jochem Barents bound for Hamburg; Pieter Fuyk toward here; Capt. Thomas Hesselink toward Cadiz; and another English ship bound for London. According to reports from the Baltic, good news has been received regarding Danzig and other ports. The ship St. Pieter, Hans Bartels, coming from France and having reached as far as the Pilau, has been directed toward the roadstead of Danzig; this ship was also reported as leaking at the aforementioned roadstead. Capt. Hendr., having departed toward Lübeck, had already sailed from the Sound. At Lübeck, [vessels] bound for St. Hubes had already sailed from the Sound. At Lübeck has arrived the Mandtmbrck from Danzig, with the ship de Zalm, Peter Hanflic, from Dronthen toward Königsberg, and the Liefde, Joure Okkes, from Friesland toward Christiania; and at Madouw the Agatha, Johan Dorp. Various ships that were expected from the Zuiderzee, owing to severe frost, had to put into the sheltered harbors, namely at Medenblik, Pieter Scholler from Königsberg, and others.

Before Riga: Gerrit Jansen from Vianen, Truykh Beger Tamming, Wiggert Bievelt Jr., and others in Mecklenburg; 6 to 7 ships in the Sloe; and Jacobs Jurjaans, coming from Gorinchem, was nearly stuck on the Scheldt. It is said that from [?] the stocks of the Zuiderzee would be laid up along the southern dike. The stock prices of the English [East India Company] are 710 to 35; [West India Company] 103; Subscription 650 to 680; English Bank 140; [Dutch] East India Company 143; [Dutch] West India Company 100. March 2, 1699.

ROTTERDAM, March 23. According to letters from Cadiz, a gentleman from the court was sent there to collect the King's treasure, which arrived with the latest ships from Vera Cruz. For now, the city awaits with favor the return of the expedition with a favorable answer from the court. On the 14th, the Turkish Ambassador was conducted with an unusual ceremony to [?]; and from there was escorted in a most stately manner to Paris. The ship "Union," Capt. Arnould [?], bound from Bayonne to Lisbon, was wrecked near St. Andrews; the Captain and the second [officer] of his crew were drowned; and on the 15th of January, the ship of Simon Grooschoet, coming from Riga toward here, was also lost some miles from Bergen; by which voyage all goods were lost. At Bordeaux (from where Juriaen Arends toward this city, the ship Ebenezer, Klaas Kuyper toward Copenhagen, have departed) there had arrived Pieter Christiaen and Jan Everisde [?] from [?]; there were still [ships] in Scilly; much coin has sailed from [?], and Aris de Veet from Emden; at Morlaix, Anne Yabrands and 2 others from Copenhagen; the [ships] of the Heaven and Berkel to this city; at Copenhagen, Klaas Cornelisse of the Heaven; and Beykhyd and Thomas Barands from here; at Heillingen, Doome Jacobke from the Baltic, Klaas Gerbrandie from Stettin toward here, Heere Dirks, with wares from here toward Hamburg and [?], the Gilded Bleak, as well as English smacks and Matten Pieters' [ship]; at Swearingen, Dirk Dirkse and Jappe [?], with ships from Norway; and within the locks of Mardyk, Ary and Kl. Zander Lankhuysen from here, which ships due to low water could not be moved further. The ship of Jan Tomaslde Duyf, sailing from the Vlie toward Vlis to Hamburg, having returned there, had to anchor in the locks due to the frost.

GERMANY AND ADJACENT KINGDOMS

THE HAGUE, March 2. The day before yesterday and yesterday the Lords of the Admiralties met in conference with the Lords Deputies of the States General. The Baron van Renswonde has gone to Utrecht on business. Yesterday, the Marquis de Mor[?] [?] had only a few gentlemen; therefore they are making a like preparation for some gentlemen of the Government.

The Leyden pierced [?]. And [?] has died [?]; some business being matters of comedy and other [?] Beer and other [affairs] as at other [times] come under consideration at the Rhineland [?] day the Elector is at his residence.

One sees here the departure [?] well the [?] that it has brought: the illustrious Prince, it is said, from Breslaw [?] and is made [?]. [?] with the [?] Court the Count of [?] Echel and Czar and the King at Brunswick have left for [?]. The aforementioned [?] already with the [?] handled is very secret.

[Name] Envoy of [?] New [?] the Condition of [?] 12,000 Men at disposal [?].

RUSSIA

[Text too damaged to translate with certainty]

K. DE KLOPPER, on the Biccdelstraet. March 3, 1721. With Privilege.

Anno 1721.

Course of Exchange

**Exchange rates of the GUILDER** are: Madrid 90 and one quarter. Cádiz 114. Seville 113. Bilbao 91. Lisbon 42 and one eighth. Venice 31 and three quarters. Livorno 2 [?]. Genoa and one half. Geneva and [?]. Paris [?] one eighth. London 3s. 6d. Leipzig current 31 St. Frankfurt current 3 and one quarter Reichsthalers. Danzig 29 groats. Breslau 35 and one quarter percent. Ghent 3. Brussels 3. Zeeland, Rotterdam one half. The Bank agio [?] and one quarter.

Notices & Advertisements

On Friday, March 7, 1721, there will be sold at Dordrecht in the Church Court House a well-built and sturdy watership, 72 feet long, 10½ feet wide, with pulleys, pumps, and other small implements necessary for a watership; the conditions of sale may also be found with Proctor Bartholomew Geldorp.

On Wednesday, March 5, 1721, there will be sold at Dordrecht in St. George's warehouses the first May, the fourth and other months, well-conditioned salt pans therein, together with water tanks, tubs, stoves, large and small salt pans, and well-situated dwelling houses, standing near and around the aforementioned salt works at [?] Dordrecht, the conditions of sale for which may be found there with Proctor Bartholomew [Geldorp].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 3rd of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 28.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 5th of March, 1721
Germany & the Adjacent Kingdoms

VIENNA, 19 February. It was thought that Cardinal Althan would return to Rome shortly; but for more than 200 [days?] it is understood that he has written to the Court that his presence there is still necessary for certain other negotiations in which the House of Austria has an interest, and wherein he can demonstrate his usefulness. It has rarely been the case that His Imperial Majesty has considered whether reinforced troops should be marched to the Kingdom of Naples in the spring. Unless news is to be believed that the Papal Nuncio has actually acted as Knight of St. George, [and] should have written to the King of Spain on that Monarch's behalf, how greatly the ecclesiastical income is disbursed through the holdings of the said Knight; for as the Spanish Court has responded, it could not concern itself with the interests of the aforementioned Knight, though it was pleased that the Pope was doing everything to restore good relations between the courts of Madrid and France. It is said that whether or not new appointments have been made, or which Magnates riding thither to Rome regarding the present [situation] might be named; and that His Majesty will conversely contribute everything within his power from the settlement of rest and peace, and particularly in the Empire, where recently to Baron von Kirchner, in opposition to religious concerns, such institutions have been granted as will be agreeable to the returning adherents of the Church. It is hoped that the Landgrave of Hessen-Kassel will withdraw his troops in time from the Rhine region, so as to prevent all possible hardships and not allow those people, who are accustomed to seek evil where good is not to be found, to make fruitless attempts. Last Sunday the Consort of the Lower Austrian Feldmarschall, the Count von Haurich, died of an illness; and since the Countess's line of Thouolfsen expired with her death, this struck her Consort all the more, wherefore he departed for his Lordship's Brück an der Leche for some time. Yesterday the Imperial Chamberlain and Secret Referendary, the Lord von Palm, died at the age of 64 years, whose competence in that position was esteemed by all. With comedies and other entertainments, so too at the Imperial Court as well as among other Nobles, [things] continue, whereby not many matters of state are taken up; and most private persons on the path in the Rhine region, where it is (although it is reported that the Elector of Trier has proceeded more cautiously from Breitlauw [?]) very well and pleasantly situated.

News is spreading here of the details of the Project which Mr. Hopken has brought to Stockholm at the request of the Holstein secret council [?]; and Mr. von Cleeck, Counselor of Affairs to the Prince, it is said, has come to Breitlauw to learn what reasonings and opinions will be expressed regarding the aforementioned Project. [Editor's note: This section is heavily corrupted in the original transcription.] The Minister to the Swedish Court, the Count von Faytag, has sent new instructions regarding the mediation between the Czar and the King of Sweden, and also concerning the establishment of the Carolinade of the King of Sweden at the Congress at Brunswick, the latter being ready to depart from there. The Czar's Minister, the Count Jagolchinsky, continues to confer with the Minister of this Court, but what is negotiated there is kept very secret. It is expected that the Count von Thoring, Envoy of Baveria, is charged not only with conducting the marriage of the Crown Prince with Josephine [?], but also with making the conditions under which the Elector will maintain 12,000 men at the disposal of the Emperor.

England, 21 February

Word has been and is [being] received here: A Public Court on the Memorandum of the Protestant Corps, delivered by the resident Minister, the Baron von Zeller, has been communicated by him to the said Corps in a manner intended to be notable, whereby His Royal Highness complains that the aforementioned Corps, contrary to the Westphalian Treaty, has taken from him part of the function of his Imperial Grand Treasury Office, and has assigned it to the bench of Great Britain, as Elector of Hanover, inferring reciprocally that, since the majority of votes of the Protestant Colleges thereon will not suffer it, he may be allowed consideration. The said Colleges, which will not meet before next Friday, have been in conference in the secret council chamber to take up the proportions concerning Decrees at the first sitting of the Imperial Diet; and from which it is declared on account of the extension that they will guide themselves according to the means which in the done powers are extensively contained for the Emperor: against which it is expected that the lower Colleges will give their consideration again; the Ministers having been in the last Friday and the Belgians. It is wished to be assured that this College has dissolved its consideration, in order that their opinions be entered herein, in Worms and Spires, in the aforesaid the Palatine Court, the grievances of the Protestants being very deferred, to which still more must be paid: Recently the Bishop and Trier with about 40 Postmasters about this time have been placed at the Bishopric of Trier, not to hold themselves for some time with the Brothers thereof, the Bishop of The Hague.

COPENHAGEN, 21 February. Yesterday the State Councillor and Postmaster-General Weyle arrived here with the Royal Treasurers in his company, which has caused great joy, because in many years such a sum of money at once has not been seen brought into this State; it is said that the King will have all this money coined, but it is not yet certain in what form it will be transported. Yesterday the secret council gave another splendid dinner, at which all the Ministers of the Court appeared.

HANOVER, 28 February. According to letters from Berlin, the King had returned from Potsdam; His Majesty had arranged a small farewell with cannon and various small firearms in the great Orange Hall above the Main Guard for the Crown Prince, and although a guard was posted, everyone under safe conditions was allowed to go view it. The King had appointed the brother of General Grumbkow as Chancellor in Pomerania, in place of the deceased Lord Sommer; with whom the deceased Chamberlain Wallet should succeed was not yet known.

Great Britain

LONDON, 21 February. After the Lords on Tuesday took their decision against Knight Blunt, and examined the former Under-Governor, Knight Fellows, together with the Lords Joy, Eyles, and Knight Robert Chaplain as to whether they also wished that any gift or promise had been made to any Member of the Ministry or of both Houses of Parliament, their Lordships continued that examination the following day, and the Lords Gibbon and Halditch, Clelter, Hauws, Sawbridge, and Knight William Chapman and Theodonius Janilen were likewise interrogated, and the answers given by the said Lords and Knights, so much on one day as on the other, were approved and signed by the Examiners. The Lords Halditch and Gibbon further related that they had heard from Knight Knight that 5,000 pounds sterling had been offered for a certain great Lord, 12 or 15,000 for a person of merit, and 2,000 for a Member of the Lower House, whose name was not on the list, and that this had occurred in February 1719, when the Bill was pending before Parliament; that besides it is retorted that Knight Fellows should have discovered certain persons of confidence who have accepted bribes.

Left Section

[Note: This section is heavily damaged and largely illegible in the original transcription. The following represents what can be discerned:]

...part of which one half shall be paid in cash and the other half in [?], which matters concerning the Company shall be settled in gold. Yesterday the secret deliberations of His [Highness?] took place...

From this the knowledge reaches the House and Municipalities, that both shall be treated properly...

Robert Knight, formerly Knight of the [South Sea Company], has been arrested and [?], and this Knight was held in captivity at Antwerp; upon which one [?] all that belonged to [?]; whereupon an Address was made to the King to [?] and the publication was [?], to thank His Majesty for the care he had taken to have... has given his good orders to his Neighboring Ministers. Verily, as it is said, by the mentioned Knight it has indeed been arrested. So they proceeded...

His Majesty, to request from the said Minister at Vienna and Brussels further orders to be sent: the needful means and to cause Inflastatio to be held as [?], which for the delivery of the Person of the mentioned Knight, [?] all other [?] and [?] [?] having had their Papers and Documents, to the [?], which it is His Maj. of St. Simon.

To Belgium according to [?], that they made a Request...

Sergeant of Arms; which Address, first drawn up on the [?], leader of the House, who also of the [?] were by good [?] Leaders of the House, who also of the [?] fields Kaad are, was presented to the King; subsequently of Luxembourg...

[Remainder of this section too damaged for reliable translation]

---

France

PARIS, 21 February. An Order of the King's Council of State of the 23rd of this month is seen, by which the number of Commissioners, appointed by the Order of 30 January of this year for the verification of Securities, as [?] of the Order dated the [?] of the same month is to be translated, was increased with the Members of the Nation [?], the Directors, Regnault, le Cius de Luar, Moreau de Schellens, Hierault Lallemand de Levignan, and Dupuy, which are all appointed by the King for this verification. In place of the Lord d'Aguesseau, who has been appointed to the Intendancy of the Generality of Tours. On these days a Courier arrived from Rome with a Letter from the Pope to the Duke Regent, with which Letter they [?] in British [?] the Accommodation of the Bishops in France, the Body of Doctrine of Cardinal de Noailles, the Ratification in Parliament made, and generally all that in France has been done against the Constitution Unigenitus, on which matter and decision of the Roman Court every one here is at the highest [?]

---

England

LONDON, 25 February. On Saturday the King came with the usual Ceremony of his outings and arrivals, and His Majesty having returned from the House with [?] to Palace, the Lords continued to pursue the examination of the former Directors, until which day they adjourned themselves. Yesterday the Commons read the [?] of seven and a half million to the State, and after a very hot Discussion the Bills [?] that would not be rejected. After that it was proposed to make a Clause of the Capital of the [South Sea Company] in that of the Bank and the East Indies, to be sold by the Directors of the House, but this was with 21 [?] namely when the House Report of the Secretary Commission is published. On Saturday Colonel Churchill with 2 Deputies of State departed for Antwerp.

Secretary Craggs is very dangerously ill. Today the Lords the Gentlemen Grigsby of the Lower House have found the Bill until [?] in the Bank are 240, E.I.C. 144, S.S. 160, Afric. Comp. 30, ditto new 20, the Exchange [?].

---

Announcements & Auctions

Burgomasters and Governors of the City of Rotterdam notify all and everyone, that had 5644, [?] on Wednesday the 12th March 1721, but 14 days later on Wednesday, Burgomasters and an afternoon at [?] hours, being Wednesday the 5th March, shall the 2nd Lodging begin to sell a party fine new-modeled Leyden common Cloths and common Linen, ditto half-Silk and Wool Stuffs, 200 Domestic as Foreign Cambrics, Ginghams, Ginghams, Snuff-Tobacco and otherwise; Jewels, Gold, Silver, and some House effects; all these days the respective Halls for two days in The Hague, on the 17th March 1721 shall sell they have been able at Amsterdam by Ooserwyk and the Widow Derbordes, Utmost J. van Pool Delft Beeman, and in The Hague by mentioned Huffon.

Jacob Magnus Michelstra, being on Thursday the 20th March 1721, at 9 in the morning and at the house of George Meyer, Cashier in the Ouderyde, Lord Lodging, an extra curious new-colored and flowered Silk Damasks, new-modeled colored 1 [?], Silk Foreparts and Wool Stuffs, Egyptian Brocades and Linens, black Bursalens, large, blue [?] Satins, Serges, Flannels &c.; all days before the Sale to be seen.

Italy

Northern Europe

ST. PETERSBURG, 12 February. [?], Minister of France [?] is [?] at [?] of the Committee for the advancement of [?] and the King of Sweden is of [?]. Yesterday and tomorrow His Czar [?] departed, and the Posts [?] Fire shall [?] at the [?] to prevent [?] Yesterday His Czaric Maj. in [?] Script has [?] during 1722 in Czaric Maj according to Trade [?]. Amsterdam

Malaga come for Sale [?] Henry and Mar [?] are. As one says there still in the House at the [?] of Cadiz toward [?]... Cadiz [?]. These still well so [?] of some Galley [?] ever the [?] [?] East Sea departed Brouwer; as [?] Good [?] Lazarzy and in the [?] from here to Mattir Ko, toward Sieven Ship are [?] are in one [?] [?] of [?] Stockholm, and Co In Medemblik is [?] still well [?]

---

The Hague

THE HAGUE, 4 March. The Lords States of Holland and West-Friesland twice again over [?] days are of [?] the more recent [?], their ordinary Assemblies to take up, to which the Points of description to the respective Cities of this Province were sent [?]. The Deputation of Doelen has at the Assembly of the Lords States General for the Province of Gelderland itself taken. Yesterday evening a Pinkie with Passengers of Schooling departed toward Zealand; and a Courier has been dispatched from the British Court to Hanover.

The usual Ceremony in the House of Peers, and that of the Lower House have the same intention retained, and the King has again to the [?] House of [South Sea Company] and they have concluded on Tuesday the Commons the Request of the S.S.C. to exemption of the payment against rejected, was with 23 against 166 Votes [?] to make a Clause or particular in the Bill, to the [?] of a [?], to the Directors of the 3 mentioned Comp. in the future [?], but this was rejected: then requested Mr. to be released of [?]. In the same on Thursday the 27th departed from Antwerp to draw the Knight Knight hither. The forbidden [?] is concluded such Don [?] the Heurs Assurance 6½, and ditto London [?]

On 2 March, shall the Maledict Felix is Amsterdam in the Old [?] mixed Linens, of [?] Quaat [?], as well-made a party Domestic as Foreign, a great party Lapped Linen [?] for Clothing, Croyens, Ginghams, 200 pieces white Linen, some Bales Moueline from 1721 shall sell the Books of the Lord G. V. Hovius, Advocate for our Judicial, Historical and Miscellaneous Books; of which the Catalogues at About J. van Poolium to Crown. Doit of Braam, Rotterdam [?].

In the morning at 9 and in the afternoon at [?] hours, at Amsterdam shall sell [...] yet, an extra curious party of Golden and Silver Stuffs, Moors, [...] colored and second, blue and [?] to [...] and black Bourtettes, quilted and flowered Calameuksten, Bitammes, [?]; all days before the Sale to be seen.

---

Advertisements

DE KLOPPER, on the Bredelstraat 25 March 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**Exchange Rates:** Madrid 90½. Cadiz 115. Seville 112. Bilbao 91. Lisbon 42¼. Venice 81¼. [?] [?]. Genoa 45. Paris regards the 3½. London 33 Schillings 6½. Leipzig current 33 St. Frankfurt current 29 Reichsthalers. Ditto current 30. Danzig 291 Polish gr. Breda 35 7/8 St. Antwerp 32½ St. Antwerp 1¼ per Cent. Ghent 1. Brussels 1¼. Zealand 1. Rotterdam ½. The Shares of the Bank; and 3 [?]. The Shares of the E.I.C. are today 95 and 150. W.I.C. 105 and 105. Subscriptions 300. The English Bank 140, ditto E.I.C. 141, and ditto S.S. 162 and 162½ all current.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the two readings of the Bill to prevent the corruption of Judges were moved, and it was placed in Committee against Saturday following. Mr. Hungerford presented a Bill for better restoration of public credit and to prevent the infamous practices of jobbers in future, of which Bill the first reading was made and the second ordered; subsequently the House by instruction ordered the great Committee to add two clauses to the Bill prohibiting Indian painted linens, the first to also forbid cottons or linens that are figured, striped or shipped, and made in other lands; and the other to prevent that the Masters of the Silk and Wool Manufactures do not take too many apprentices: whereafter the House returned to great Committee, the advocates of the East India Company have been heard against the said Bill; and some progress being made therein, a Council was held at the Palace of St. James, wherein the King appointed the Viscount Townshend, in place of the deceased Earl Stanhope, as Secretary of State, of which office the Lord Woodale has taken possession, and declared that he would make no changes among those who had been placed in that office by the Deceased. The Duke of Buckingham finds himself much indisposed, the Earl of Cadogan is troubled with an inflammation in his leg, and the Secretary of State Craggs fell ill on Tuesday also with the smallpox; it is also said that the Duke of Newcastle finds himself somewhat indisposed. The celebrated advocate Thomas Vernon, who twice declined to be relieved of the great Chancellorship of England, which was committed to him, died last Sunday. For several days now work has been vigorously commenced on an equipment of the Squadron destined for the service of this year to the East Indies; it is assured that the same will consist of 25 ships of the line; under the command of Admiral Knight Norris, who under himself has the Admirals Holier and Hoblon; and it is understood that the sailors for this Squadron must be ready, and who for some time have permission to go to their houses or elsewhere, must repair at once to their respective ports at Chatham, Sheerness, Woolwich, and Deptford, at the expense of His Majesty and to be followed. The 2 ships which are being built about Limehouse for the Miscellaneous Company in France will be launched into the water within a few days. Mr. Rudby Ducey Moreton, son of Lord Moreton, has been elected Member of Parliament for the Borough of Cirencester in the County of Wilts, in the place of Mr. James Sawbridge, one of the Directors of the East India Company, who died in the Lower House some days ago. The Lord Chief Justice Pratt has laid down the office regarding the Chancellor's place in the Exchequer, in succession to the last Aisle, who has requested his dismissal. Yesterday at the East India Company, a general meeting was held, in which it was decided to present a request to the Lower House to, in consequence of bad management, have an exchange with the willing [parties?] of that [Company] and the Miscellaneous Midsburg Company, in consequence of the conditions of the Act passed in the last session of Parliament, which should be determined to the State. In the house which the newcomers [presented?], found by the Company there concerning the Company in which it finds itself, and that this was the main reason why the opening of the books was deferred longer, but they hope that the postponement of Christmas will fall well into the future, and that it will be not less than 1 to 10 [percent?].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 5th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 29.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of March, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, 11 February. Last Thursday the Viceroy proceeded with the court retinue to the church of St. Dominique, in honor of a solemn feast that had been celebrated there; after which His Eminence was carried in procession. On Sunday at the Cappelle Vizzihovuers, where some masked persons had gathered in a pleasant manner, an elaborate theatrical display was performed before a large assembly of men, and it was then brought before the Royal Palace and delivered to the court retinue. News has arrived of the Count of Schönbach, nephew of the Viceroy, and also of the son of the Prince of Trasalon, who have arrived here in recent weeks.

ROME, 15 February. Cardinal del Guidice has sent a letter to Cardinal Tamara, in which he makes known his opinion regarding the latter's pretensions to the office of Vice-Dean of the Sacred College; and advocates very earnestly in this matter before the religious authorities, demonstrating at the same time that Cardinal Ottoni, although absent, is capable of being appointed to the sub-decanship. Cardinal Albani receives with honor the visits of the Prefects, just as the Cardinals are accustomed to do at their first public audiences with the Pope. On Wednesday a Consistory was held, in which His Holiness the Pope appointed to the Bishopric of St. Jago of Cape Verde, Father Joseph of Santa Maria, and to the Bishopric of Nambin, Father Emanuel of J. Maria, both of the Order of St. Francis, and to the Bishopric of Cocius, the Jesuit Father François Vasconcellos; Cardinal Fabroni also was appointed to the Bishopric of Damascus; along with many others. The Lord Roxas Auantumia, a Paul Almodieniet, has died, and as many as ten thousand Scudi were found in his apartment, which he had not yet disposed of. The Duke of Mondragone, who had been called before the Inquisition and banished, and since then, not being permitted to leave and having gone to the Benedictine Monastery of St. Callite, has found a way to escape by flight; whereupon on Thursday the Congregation of the Holy Office ordered the officials of the aforementioned monastery, together with a Dominican, to arrest the Duke; and since the aforementioned Duke had posted bond for his estate, he forfeited all his holdings and assignments on the Public Debt or Banking establishment of that estate, from which he held a considerable sum. It is said that His Holiness Vicar of the Apostolic Camera, the Deputy-Curator and his clerical positions will be vacated, which offices have formerly been sold for two thousand Scudi. The Franciscan Order, which found itself in great distress near Pepin, now shows itself frequently in the Vatican, which is being newly renovated. Today Carnival has opened with masquerades, and the Batiste Faardcloon and Briosenne [?]. Cardinal Paribugi has taken possession of a new church. Today a highwayman, who was more than seventy years old, has been hanged in the Campo Vaccino.

Venetia

FLORENCE, 22 February. Dispatches have been received from various places, reporting that the Emperor intends to come to Italy in the coming spring.

MILAN, 1 February. News has been received from Turin that the Advocate Sicardi has received his final instructions to go to Vienna to defend the rights of his Sovereign against the pretensions of the Emperor regarding certain fiefs which Savoy claims to possess, and that the Governor of Casål and other places has made proclamations, and this has also been forbidden to those of Geneva, under the condition that they foot no forces in Piedmont.

It is reported that the Duke of Parma wishes to dispose of his estates without adhering to the Treaty of the Quadruple Alliance.

VENETIA, 22 February. General Count von Schulenburg arrived here in the latter part of the previous week from Vienna. On Thursday the ordinary diplomatic visits were held here.

The North / Russia

ST. PETERSBURG, 12 February. Word has been received that Mr. Camphilos, Minister of France at the court of Stockholm, has arrived here from the Emperor; publicly as an officer and furnished with his commission to further peace between His Czarist Majesty and the King of Sweden, he is expected to arrive from day to day. Yesterday morning His Czarist Majesty, according to reports, went to the Artillery Range to test a certain type of cannon, invented by Baron Bulaw, with which cannonballs were fired at a distance of two hours' travel [?] and it will be long before one can learn by speculation whether this invention of circulation will be successful.

Yesterday His Czarist Majesty granted free trade to the aforementioned merchants, observing that his subjects enjoy free commerce with the Kingdom of Great Britain, and that His Czarist Majesty will henceforth treat them as his own subjects.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 19 February. Mr. Dahlman, Royal Swedish Chief Auditor, has returned here from the court of the Czar of Great Russia, bringing word that His Czarist Majesty has reviewed the Ministry of the Officers and certain places near Abo have been arranged, and there concerning a style of weapons and ministries of this crown [?], some days [?] from the court [?] [?] [?] within eight or ten days they are expected to proceed thither [?]

---

Germany & the Adjoining Kingdoms

VIENNA, 22 February. The Great British Minister at this court has presented a strong memorandum to His Imperial Majesty, in which he complains most emphatically that the Elector Palatine, notwithstanding the Imperial order to allow his Protestant subjects to enjoy the Treaty of Westphalia, has done nothing whatever to remedy this, but on the contrary persecutes his Protestant subjects in the most rigorous manner. His Majesty reflects that the Protestant Powers had on his strong assurance withdrawn their complaints, and that the King of Prussia made no the least difficulty in immediately restoring the Dominican monastery of Hamerstetten in its entirety to the Roman [Church]; requesting by name of his exalted Principal that His Majesty would be pleased to take decisive steps, so that if the aforementioned Elector should continue to delay, [His Majesty] would deign by his high authority to bring this matter at last to a conclusion; all the more so because the Protestant Powers, through this delay, might come to regret their forbearance, and because they had obtained so little at Mannheim in response to their petitions, notwithstanding that they had on their part so promptly withdrawn their complaints; but since they could effect nothing contrary to all expectation through their own handling, and no remedy was to be hoped for in this regard, His Majesty ought not to take it amiss that the other party should resort to other appropriate measures, to bring the Elector Palatine to compliance with the tenor of the Treaties: which memorandum it is assured has been received in a satisfactory manner, and further action is expected in the near future. There is mention of a letter which the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz [Editor's note: Christian Augustus, Cardinal and Archbishop-Elector of Saxony] is said to have sent from Regensburg to this court, to inform it of the favorable turn which the matter has taken which the Lord von Keck, sent concerning the Protestant cause near the Elector Palatine, encountered there; the aforementioned envoy (as it is represented from the Protestant side) having been able to find lodging at nearly all the legations in Mannheim, and finally having sought refuge at a small inn; which sojourn has caused great trouble to the Protestants at Regensburg, and it was feared that the Protestant courts would take a poor view of it. Since the corps of troops, encamped near Nisfa, was daily being increased by arriving contingents, and since some Methodists [likely Moldavian forces] had committed excesses on the Emperor's territory and taken some Christians captive, the Governor of Belgrade sent an expulsion to the Pasha of Nisfa, asking him why such a large corps of troops was being assembled in peacetime, while complaining at the same time of the violence committed by the Methodists on the Emperor's territory, during a time of peace with the Ottoman Porte; that an actual invasion had even been made into His Majesty's lands, subjects had been carried off in peacetime, and unheard-of acts had been perpetrated against the lands; requesting the aforementioned Pasha that such excesses cease and satisfaction be given for the damage sustained; so that His Majesty should not be compelled to lodge a complaint with the Grand Seigneur, etc.; to which the aforementioned Pasha replied in an obliging manner to the expulsion sent to him, that His Majesty need have no concern whatever regarding the corps of troops assembled near that place, and that on his part everything would contribute to the maintenance of the existing peace treaties; that this assembly of forces had occurred only to hold a general review and to ascertain how many troops were present in that province and whether they were properly maintained in military discipline; but as for the excesses which may have occurred on the Emperor's territory, he had no knowledge of them whatsoever, but firmly maintained that such would have been done by certain malefactors to whom peace was unwelcome and who had not even spared the lands of the Grand Seigneur; and that those who on either side might have been taken prisoner should be summarily punished: which reply the aforementioned Governor of Belgrade sent on to the Imperial court; it is understood that a great council was held here regarding this matter, in which it is assured that it was further resolved not only to strengthen the fortifications in mobility but to put the lands in a proper state of defense. It is reported that the Elector of Trier has taken his journey from Breslau by way of Mannheim to speak with the Elector Palatine on the Imperial exertion regarding the upkeep of troops; and it is said that several Imperial legiments have received secret orders to hold themselves in readiness. General Seckendorf has arrived here from Sicily and keeps himself incognito; just as Mr. Law, who (according to the latest letters of Baron Schaffhauser, Chief Almoner of the Cardinal of Althan) is likewise expected here from Venice, has already made his arrangements in Prussia to then, upon the return of the King from the hunt, be able to appear before him: notice has been given to all four invited foreign ministers to inform their principals thereof. Word has been received that the French Minister, Mr. Campredon, had landed six or seven miles from Rotterdam, from whence he had sent notice of his arrival there to the court of His Czarist Majesty. P.S. Some report that a provisional accord regarding the maintenance of peace between this kingdom and Moravia has been concluded.

Regensburg, February 12

There is astonishment that the Protestant Corps here has sufficient grounds to justify itself regarding the redress of grievances, and it is also reported from that quarter that consideration is being given to whether some other representatives might be retained for the vote, since all the petitions from the Emperor's Capital have been treated. The Principal Ministers say that no reliance can be placed upon the issued Ordinance of that Court, and examples could be cited showing that the Holy Roman ordinances have remained ineffectual to this day. Moreover, it is noted that reports regarding the Falkenberg matter increase more and more. A certain gentleman from this city, of the French religion, had some time ago obtained lands in the Palatinate, and when this came to light at the mentioned Church, the said gentleman was forced by a prompt Decree to sell his considerable property, under the pretext that His Majesty did not wish to tolerate Protestant settlement in his territory. The matter of the Elector of the Palatinate has provoked such complaint that the Ministers of that Electoral domain have even been instructed regarding the Duchy of Zweibrücken to conduct an inspection concerning all the churches, monasteries, and revenues of the Protestant [communities], which have been taken from them without compensation by the Bishops' orders, and the grievances of the Lutheran and Reformed communities have been registered and examined by the Protestant Corps. Should the Bishops of Worms and Speyer act hastily in certain matters, the mentioned complainants will seek other means of redress. The Vice-President's Treaty between [?] and the Crown of Sweden has been measured here with the Dike.

France

PARIS, February 14

According to letters from Palermo of the 9th of this month, the new recruitments there met with little success, and a Regiment of Foreign troops was to be raised there; however, those who have entered Spanish service receive no employment. The construction of 2 warships and 2 frigates at Messina and 4 new galleys at Messina is proceeding, and from the mentioned places 16 vessels laden with tallow, rye, and barley departed for Naples. The letters from Madrid of the 11th report that in Raddix, Malaga, and other places preparations are being made to assemble transport ships to be sent to Ceuta. Since no conquests are being made against the Moors in Africa, the armies in Spain continue to find great difficulties regarding Gibraltar, by which the Congress at Cambrai shall not be surprised. The King has appointed the Count de Ronde to the Government of [?] and augmented his pension. Last Friday, Monsieur Pontcourte, son of the former President in the Parliament of Rouen, was received as Counselor in the Parliament of this city; as also the Sieur Hercol, whose father is General Receiver of Finances. The return of the 12 Doctors to the Sorbonne has not proceeded as peacefully and regularly as one might have supposed. The matters of the Constitution keep the Court in constant motion and continue to trouble the Gallican Church. One sees various new writings appearing, notably a Letter to the King, which is supported by a Memoir of the three appealing Bishops. Also a new Treatise on the Power of Grace.

Great Britain

LONDON, February 18

After those of the House of Commons, in pursuing the prevention of corruption among the governors, had approved and ordered in the first instance, the Controller reported that he had a message from the House, which had been signed by the King, and he handed it to the Speaker, who read it aloud, its contents being as follows:

GEORGE R.

His Majesty having received a Request from the Directors of the South Sea Company concerning payment due from the said Company to the Government, has thought fit to lay the same Request before the House of Commons, and to acquaint them that His Majesty will not oppose Parliament dealing favorably and appropriately with the said Company, as the House of Commons shall think proper in the matter.

This good news being delivered to the House, it was referred to the Grand Committee and subsequently the House ordered a clause to be added to the Bill to enable the necessary capital of the South Sea Company, and also of the Bank of England and the East India Company, to assist the Directors and Heads of the said Company in raising some money upon their effects. A sum of 100 per cent was approved, after which the House appointed a Grand Committee of Members to investigate the corruption, [details of proceedings follow regarding examination of accounts and reports]. It was found that the Directors had given gentlemen bonds for 500,000 pounds sterling in Africa without the Directors making payment; after lengthy proceedings, it was proposed to cause the aforesaid report to be printed, but this was not approved so that the Members of the House named in the said report might be heard and examined first. Thereafter it was decided to postpone further consideration to tomorrow, being Saturday; it was stated however that the report should be completed and made public by the press. The same day the Bill for preventing Emolument Credenda and Mayors was read and ordered to committee for the coming Monday. The questioning of the Members was also postponed for the day. Today in the Court of Chancery, a case was heard against the late Sir Craggs, whom some will maintain has profited from the advanced Directors of the South Sea Company. The body of the late State Secretary Stanhope was conveyed with great pomp to his country seat in the Province of Kent to be buried; for the procession about 500 men rode, from the Guards du Corps and the Foot Guard, and others.

Spain

CÁDIZ, February 11

The matters at Madrid continue without success, as his fleet suffers delays; against this, silver is brought from the King's mines. All recent advices from Gibraltar report that an Algerian pirate with Maldivian [goods] has appeared on this coast. Heard in Africa are the usual tidings; vessels from Havana recently arrived including the ships: St. Jago, Nicholas Dennis from Barcelona; the Doctor, Edmund, Nicholas Rowland from [?], Hallo and William Babbidge from Newcastle to Lisbon; also the San Antonio, Jacob [?], the Neptune, John Winscot, and which last by headwind [?] against Hampstead. Ships the Dragon, Capt. Scho [?] of that nation, 24 [?], 7 [?]

CÁDIZ, February 11

To the Owners [regarding] the King's order at the Mint [details concerning silver]: that our opportunity might work; [a] letter from the Court having arrived, it remained [unclear] as the silver [concerning] when the silver shall be wholly and firmly held for one or two years through continuing negotiations resolved, it was resolved [that] in the name of the whole Government Representation be made to the King concerning the silver remaining at the Mint, which shall be held at 3 percent, which has already been assigned to the Owners; yet the later loading proceeds so slowly that little prospect remains.

[At the Spanish Court with all ceremony it has been received] that the ships [...]

What through the Congress at Cambrai would not be satisfied; and that one occupies oneself with the Great Count of Ronde with the Government of Bapaume enriched and the pension augmented. Last Friday Monsieur Pontecorte, Son of the first President in the Parliament of Rouen, was received as Counselor in the Parliament of this city; as also the Sieur Fercol, whose father is General Receiver of Finances. The return of the 12 Doctors at the Sorbonne has not passed as peacefully and regularly as one might have imagined to publish. The matters of the Constitution keep the Court in continuous motion, and do not cease to trouble the Gallican Church; one sees undertaken certain new pieces, firstly a Letter from the King, which is supported by a Memoir of the three appealing Bishops. Then too a new Treatise on the Power of Grace, drawn up by one [author]. Then another Treatise on the Authority arising from the Pope, which in [volume] 12 is inserted [?]. Last Sunday the Circular Council of Government was held; and the following night the number of spectators at the Opera was very great, but the following [night] between the 24th and 25th few people were mentioned there. The Prince of Carignano (who is a great lover of the hunt) and the Princess make great expenditures. The Marquise of St. Sulpice [was] Sunday, pierced by pain, at her [residence] by accident of [?]. It is now reported that the Turkish Ambassador will arrive in this city toward the 6th of March next, yet it remains certain that he in the mentioned [?] House in the Street of Charenton shall dwell, until he shall have his public Entry; one shall see there, besides the already mentioned Regiment of Dragoons of Orleans, that of the King's Infantry and 6 general Cavalry on the [day of] the Ambassador's entrance take up position in the Palace of the foreign Ambassadors. The Brief sent by the Pope to this Court, and with which His Holiness countenances all that has been done here for his compliance regarding the Constitution, has been sent back to Rome in disagreement. The youngest of the Brothers Boivin, whom one held for a man of great learning, has been appointed in Paris [to fill] the position of the deceased Sieur [?], Bishop of Avanches in the French Academy; and the Sieur Landivillain, who had been given charge of the Opera, has been dismissed from it. As well as one understands, the Messieurs Ferrand and Paris have been those who pursued the matter of the Duke of la Force and also discovered it; the said Duke received eight days to settle account; one also wishes that his latest conduct was full of confusion; this day another assembly was held concerning his affairs; and the Advocate Terrellon stands [ready] to publish a work, by means of which he shall attempt to effect the salvation of that Duke; many are of the opinion that the said party will also depend upon a certain Patent, which granted to the Duke's advantages by eternal law, and by which all misdeeds of the descendants of that house, outside of felony, are pardoned. Besides the Request which yesterday was sent by the Company of the Indies to the King, so that the act of verification may be delayed, the Assembly has appointed Syndics. The verification of the Paper effects has not yet begun; and one says that such shall not be completed in 2 years, on which account those who are provided with many things in equal measure find themselves in a very desolate state. Moreover it is spoken of in the Gazettes, by which it shall be ordained that all papers in the Bank be brought in to verify them on the millions; and many are not without fear that once brought into the Bank, they shall never come out again.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, March 7

According to letters of the 2nd or 3rd of [?] of Saldé, there were then still but 2 years their boats at sea. At Port à Poil (where ships destined for Rio de Janeiro were being prepared) several merchant vessels had arrived, namely under the King George, John Perry, the John de Galata, Capt. [?], Bilbao, Antonio del Barco from Londonderry, all from London; and at Bordeaux (where the weather was extraordinarily violent) some 10 to 12 ships from here and the Meuse.

Madrid 90½; Cádiz 112; Seville 111; Bilbao 90¾; Lisbon 42¾; Verhenen 8¾; Livorno 93; Panos 42¾; London 33 Sch. 9; Leipzig current 30; Brunswick current 29 Reichsth.; Ditto current 30; Danzig 191 Poland gr.; Breslau 3; [various other rates] Antwerp 3¼ to 3½; [Amsterdam] ½.

Stocks: East India Company 760; West India Company 116; Subscription 800; English Bank 140; also O.I.C. 119; and ditto 16.

The Hague, March 6

It is understood that the Marquis de Monteleon has delivered his Credentials as Ambassador and Plenipotentiary to the King of Spain and to the States General; in which business a pinkship has been hired with Paligers toward England with [?], when again an Express in all diligence possible [?].

---

Printed at Leiden by X DEKLOPPER, on the Broad Street March 7, 1721. By Privilege.

LEYDSCHE COURANT, No. 1721

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES:**

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 7th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
