No. 1721.No. 30.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 10th of March, 1721

SPAIN.

CADIZ, 11 February. Our Deputies continue to remain in Madrid, pressing the King to permit the use of silver from the Mint, but with little success; since His Majesty has, by his most recent decisions, maintained his stance: on the other hand, the Commerce College continues to refuse to hand over the silver to the King; however, it is hoped that some change may yet be made in all this. According to advices from Gibraltar, on the third of this month near Felix, Malaga, and Miguelette, a copper vessel with 22 pieces, named the Koos, came drifting without a mast. Despite the daily storms on this coast, no damage is reported. Since the war in Africa has ended, the Fortifications have already received some troops from Ceuta by land, which will march toward their garrisons. From the West Indies, not the slightest news has been received, which is why the longing for the ships from Buenos Aires and other vessels bound for Havana is great. In this bay, the following ships have been cleared: the St. Jago, Captain Juan Garze of Alicante; the St. Jago Nicolaas Denis from Ireland; the Broderik, Philip Hore of Barcelona; the Delosus, Edmund James from England; and the Conception, Nicolaas Rolanden of Canary: meanwhile, Will. Mallo and William Babbidge departed for Bifaol; the English warship Vengeful departed for Lisbon; Andre Ponman for Bilbao and Will. Bullet for Ireland; the St. Antonio, Jacob Hendri Stap, for Alicante and Genoa; the Neptunus, John Winscot, and the Britania, John Everis, for London, the latter of which has suffered from contrary winds; so that we are still waiting for one Hamburger and one Genoese, the two English warships Dragon, Captain Schot, and the Loo, together with 19 merchant vessels of that nation, 24 French, 7 Dutch, and 2 Danish ships.

CADIZ, 11 February. Silver continues not to be distributed to the owners here, on the condition that they must bring it according to the King's order to the Mint. It is learned that our Deputies in Madrid have been unable to accomplish anything through their pressing efforts; indeed, an Express from the Court has arrived here this week, which reports that the King intends to have the silver coined; if this is certain, all the silver has already been struck into whole and half Reals, and with that well 300,000 or two years' work will be consumed: therefore several meetings have been held again, and in them long debates have arisen; to conclude, two of the principal merchants are to be sent to Madrid, to make more forceful representations on behalf of the entire Commerce College to the King, and to ask His Majesty whether the silver may be used from the Mint. It is said that the interest has been reduced to 3 per cent, as also of the goods which have already been delivered to the owners. The Court has also given new orders to repair the galleons, but the loading proceeds slowly, so that there is little appearance of their departure. They recommend themselves in Spain or undertake whatever possible efforts to obtain permission that the ships which are directed from France to the ports of this Crown, and those same [ships] of the green manufacture on a corner, might be admitted in the settlements of that Monarchy's sea; so it seems at the Court in Madrid they are not so quick about it, which does much damage to Spanish negotiations. [?] One wants it to be from Coëlly, that a certain nation has inflicted some hostilities in those regions, which it is believed will have occurred; and that there it was learned that the coast of weapons in Europe has been heard.

MADRID, 18 February. By the latest letters from Africa, it is [reported] that because the covering of people's [?] the [?] in people's [?] had been directed, the Marquis de Lecde decided with his entire Army to withdraw from his encampments into the city, which also happened the following night, without the miners having suffered any loss; the reason the orders to withdraw to the utmost edge [?] were so hastily obtained that the entire Army with all the Artillery and further necessities was in the city before a battle was engaged; [the Moors'] Field Commander found himself in great unease because they were expected by the critical positions, wherefore the Marquis supposed that the Moors would certainly undertake something; in order, if it were possible, to lure some few people from the fortress, and so it has also been sufficiently seen that the army in the field was placed in readiness, and it was certainly observed at the letter; in the meanwhile, a strong party showed itself almost under the cannon of Ceuta; but our soldiers believed themselves in great strength and that [?] from the company [?]. Below our army was ordered to [?], in this night of the engagement which was not from the [?], the first [?], whereupon some Moors were killed for a while, but the remaining Barbarians of this party [?] prompt and vigorous the hounds everyone; everyone the old community to [?] high and high the [?] fortified to be, which went so well that in the [?] all the withdrawal but 3 or 4 soldiers [?], but from the other side were many officers and community wounded. Meanwhile, by the other army on another part of the 3 battalions of the Spanish Guard was brought [?] Ceuta in Andalusia had arrived, which were [?] by the 3 battalions of the Walloon Guard, and daily the entire army had to be conducted to victory.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 1 March. Last Saturday, in the small theater in the King's apartments, the comedy called Arteganus Perrogat Enfans was performed; the ballets are postponed to the coming year, and it is said that the machines will be repaired and put in order. Work is underway to demolish the workshops which were in the palace of the Tuileries in the apartment of the Duchess of Ventadour, and also those which were in the great gallery there of the Marshal of Villeroy built for the lodging of his servants, to the improvement of the gallery of the Tuileries, at the end of which a throne for the King is being made, which will show itself very magnificent from a distance, and certainly from the end of the great staircase, which the Turkish Ambassador must ascend to his audience: this gallery will be provided with very excellent tapestries, in which all the great and memorable deeds of Louis the XIVth are depicted very vividly and at the greatest expense: the King's household will be exceptionally numerous, and His Majesty will entirely defray the Ambassador and his retinue during his stay, although His Excellency has offered to bear half the cost: a collection of very curious little horses has arrived here, and it is said that the Ambassador will arrive externally on the eighth or tenth. The Governors and other officers of Versailles and other royal residences have received orders to dress themselves all in the same manner in the finest, costly manner with gold trimmings, to appear dignified before His Excellency as those who wish to visit him: the battalions of infantry of the King's regiment, and the Colonel Generals of cavalry and the dragoons regiment of Orleans will also assist at his installation. There is mention of a marriage between Mademoiselle Law and an Italian Prince, and his marriage will bring millions. The Countess of Armagnac has retired to the convent of the Visitation, in the street du Bois, where she is received as a visitor from the Lorraine house; the Bishop of Bayeux and Mademoiselle d'Armagnac are discontented over the proceedings which her brother has followed in this matter; and work is underway eagerly to reconcile this Prince with his daughter. The Marquis de Lattre has a nearly similar case of separation with his wife, and has already left the house of his father-in-law the elder President. Madame des Aileurs, widow of the late Ambassador at the Turkish Court, died yesterday, and that day the Marquis d'Ambré also died at the age of 54 years, as also the Bishop of Nîmes in his diocese. Concerning the matter of the Duke of la Force, who was summoned to a Council Thursday evening, a decree was issued Friday to the Parliament which had assembled, in which assembly all the Orders of the blood and nobles were present; and after the contents were read, the Lords of Parliament deliberated that a deputation should be made to the King to very humbly request him to permit that this matter might be brought before His Majesty, whereupon the King's justices immediately took the order, which was announced Saturday at eight o'clock, and thereupon assembled the aforementioned judges of the chambers and some nobles, to draw up what should be presented to His Majesty, as that the Parliament requested, that the arrest of challenge, as well as that the Duke of la Force might be revoked: the nobles were divided in their opinions on this matter: the memorandum on one side said that the Parliament should not have drawn up so strongly that on the Duke his sword should be taken away to be held and that on a simple summons, where the councilors in such occasion had their robes, as also that the Parliament was not competent to proceed before all this had the King's command near provided, and at least for the preservation of the peerage, that matter should have been brought before the Council; the others sustained that one should let that matter go as it would naturally be presumed established. It is said that the Duke naturally became [?] and that the Duchess of Lothringen might next summer perhaps make a trip here, to visit the Duke of Orleans, father of the Duchess. According to the latest letters from Nanteuil, the pestilential disease there had been sufficiently arrested; and at Aix it also began to decrease. A servant of Monsieur Campredon, who had arrived at this court with some dispatches from Sweden, has been sent back in the mail coach, and is to go to [?], in the territory of the Czar of Great Courland [Editor's note: This appears to be a reference to a Baltic German territory]. The Cardinal de Noailles is daily in conversation with those of his party, to find means to continue the favor of the beloved [?] his house, which has been lodged very long in the Royal Palace; and it is said that an express has been sent to the Cardinal de Rohan to hasten his journey to Rome. The northern [?] goes here, notwithstanding all precautions, still continues.

GERMANY AND ADJOINING REALMS.

HAMBURG, 4 March. According to the latest letters from Stockholm [text breaks off]

BRUSSELS, 6 March. It is reported that the Imperial Minister Count von Windischgrätz, who is departing from London to proceed to the Congress at Cambrai, has sent a letter to Ostend to inspect the harbor and fortifications of that city. The Count of Aremberg has gone to Luxembourg as mentioned. Mr. Strelandt has been chosen at Leuven as one of the Aldermen of the city, in place of Vilserge. According to reports from Great Britain, several Ministers have been arriving daily there, but it was uncertain when the Peace Congress would begin.

Messrs. Cornelis de Roos and Isaac Schellinger, Auctioneers, will sell at Amsterdam on Monday at the house of George Meyer Kalylyn in the Old Nobility Lodge, various Obligations: one of 1700, one of 1700, one of 1700, two each of 1700, one of Amsterdam Bank, one of 1700, two each of 1000, all three in blank, at [?]; also for the Bank Comptoirs of Haarlem, two Obligations each of 6000, both in blank, at [?]; all which aforementioned matters will be further specified by the said Auctioneers.

There is presented for sale the fine Manor House of the late Baron de la Torre, situated in the Lordship of Coudekerik, an hour and a half from Leiden, [containing] Coach-Houses, Horse-Stables, Coach-House, fine Orchards with Fruit-Trees, all surrounded [by water] with tall Trees planted about, orchards on the Rhine [bank], and about 6 acres of meadowland; anyone desiring information thereon is requested to address [the agent], who will provide further details.

---

AMSTERDAM, 7 March. A letter dated 1 December was received there from Jamaica, from [?], reporting that several ships from here had arrived there and departed thence. On the 12th of the previous month, four ships previously mentioned departed from Genoa: Pieter Fock sailing hither, Jochem [?] to Hamburg, and the two English [ships] of Captains Harrison, &c., to London; and from [?] English [ships] of Captain Harrison, &c., to London, and from [?] English ships, all of which sailed from Cadiz to [?] under Captain [?]. From Bayonne departed Captain [?] and Captain [?] with the [?]; but the ships from Cartagena and Jamaica having previously arrived at Bayonne were the ships from Tynemouth and Yarmouth, Willem Gunnelly from London and John Godard from Barcelona; at Bayonne Captain Louis le Goudre, whose grenadoes received by the [?] were paid for in Bordeaux. The ships St. Peter Leonard, Vorw. the Corn. Sluyterts, Pieter Souw, the Hendrina Tys Cornel., Bätent Klomp, the Corn. Smyterts, [?] the Maas, of which some [take] 6 to 7 weeks and others July 8 to 9 days, Teyfel and the Maas proceeding nearly equally. From Paliaoje the ship of Captain Louis de Faguerre has departed hither. It is said that in the O.I.C. 145, and the same Z.Z. 156, no Negroes were shot.

AMSTERDAM, 3 March. On 3 February [a ship departed] from Lisbon hither, and [the Deputies] continue daily in conference with the Gentlemen Deputies of the Streets General in their sessions; a return was made home, but against the weakness [?] a delay is anticipated. The same is expected regarding Holland and West Friesland.

His Majesty. It is assured that a Squadron of Warships is to be sent to the Mediterranean Sea for protection against the Barbary Pirate States. From the Nether Lands it is understood that a Memorandum has been delivered to the Gentlemen States General. The Baron van IJselmuyden, who previously held this State, having paid his compliments to the Bishop of Münster and in Paderborn, is expected to return within a few days. The Baron Broekhuyzem, Lord of Doorn, having received instructions regarding Norway and the Province of Übersel, has departed thence. Some Utrecht Ministers were yesterday with Prince Kurakin, Ambassador and Plenipotentiary of the Czar and [?] of Great Britain. An Express [courier] has passed from the North to England. It is assured that the Congress at Brunswick will be opened in the forthcoming month of May. A Pinkie [small vessel] with passengers has departed from Scheveningen to Zealand.

of the lots of the 3rd [...] the Class in the sum can be seen as this entire running month of March, after which time no one will have any right or claim under the lots thereof. Much was asked besides.

At the Bookshop of Steenhouwe and Uytsaert on the Rockin over the Exchange, books are sold, of which new [ones] at stated Price [?] and [?] Bandelier and Information; all bequeathed by the late [?] that may be obtained in The Hague from the Widow Bouquet and Joh. [?] Fcollaim, and at Amsterdam from Steenhouwe and Uytsaert aforementioned.

the Goods, on the Burg within Leiden, to be delivered to the highest bidder within the said City: also a Farmer's Dwelling under Soeterwoude of Lands: further by affixture of the Notices expedited.

March 1721 there will be sold the Books of Mr. G. V. Houtis, Advocate, consisting of fine Juridical, Historical and Miscellaneous Books: [?] of the Catalogues at [?], Uytsaert, J. van Foolium and Kroon; Delft, van Braam; Rotterdam, Achett; and in The Hague [?].

721, in the morning at 9 and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, there will be sold at Amsterdam [?] and Silver Fabrics, Moors, newly fashioned yellow-colored and flowered Silk Woolens, costumed and dark blue Silk Linings and Woolen Fabrics; Egypt Calimancoes, Estamines, Serges of Bois, waxed fine Sateen, Serges, [?] to be viewed daily before the sale.

the house of Josias Carlier, on the Hoygragt near the Noble-bridge at Leiden [?] in English and genuine Brocatelles and white Brocades, Baldachin and [?] threads, Spanish Colors, [?], black and colored 4-thread, and [?]; also Cloister- and Lay-Goods, Turkish Yarns and Caniettes; as well as [?] necessary for the Grain-Measure [trade]; all to be viewed on [?] day before the sale.

Amsterdam on Monday, 24 March 1721, in the morning at 2 o'clock, there will be sold various Obligations, consisting of one of 4500, one of 1600 and 1200, and [?], one of 740, one of 500, all upon Names at the Comptoir [?]; the [second] Company insolvent, and a second brief of 5000, upon Name in blank, at the charge of the City of Groningen and Ommelands; prepared by the said [auctioneers].

the late Baron de la Torre, Lord of Valkenisse, &c., named the House [?] hour from Leiden, situated on the Rhine, having from his estate Fruit-Trees, all surrounded in the Water, and fine Avenues, among other things a meadow on the Rhine, with moreover about 6 acres and a half, [?] property; address the Marquess, residing in The Hague in the Fayetritr; willing that His K.M. [?] will be permitted We Drogues, and all such for [?].

The Imperial Commissioner and Reich [?] journey to undertake with advice [?] that the departure of the R. second Imperial [?] owing not fixed; and it is also known [that] Commissioner Count of Welz, by the Imperial [?], [?] over the hand, the [?] attendance of the Swabian Circle [?] one holds, wherefore one the arrival [?] with great expectation [?], in Hambury at this Court [?] there City for 2 years past [?].

And thereupon exceptional [?], in the sending of a Burgomaster payment of the stated Sum not at Hambury would be invoked to the said City in manner satisfaction to compel; there [?] least City Hamburg [?] men the first [?] Burgomaster to send, on pain of [?] [?], whereupon is been decided, with a [?] speed maintained [?]. [?] Kimbel to Rotterdam, wheresoever [?] state to proceed, it is said, that he order obtained would be journey ready to make, on Ministry of [?], the Free [?]; and since one out [?] as of the King of Sweden M [?] [?] means well might follow; and above all [?] King of [?] in the foregoing [?], in order thereby [?] affairs of the Hansedom, but North: one adds that [?] there [?] to [?]. One [?] inland [?] several Norwegian [?] against the exports of the [?], but one hopes [?] of the [?] Field therein that some Roman Princes [?] through new Laws [?] return of their [?] [?]. All the following out of Scandinavian [?] make, and that they already War-provisions provide; Order [?] come and Locks [?] Constantinople requested [?].

Notices & Advertisements

On Thursday it is also advised that the drawing of the lots of the 3rd class will take place, and that the distribution cannot proceed any longer unless the entire running lot obtains its delayed number, as has likewise happened with the previous concluded lot on 10 April following, which will then commence.

On Thursday, 13 March 1721, there will be held at Amsterdam in the Bookshop of Steenhouwe a remarkable collection of Dutch and French Books, of which many are fine volumes, including a valuable collection of Music Books and Instruments, the estate of Messrs. Johann Vischer and his son Adolf Vischer: the Catalogues thereof may be obtained at Wynands, Delft; Boiter, Leiden; Vivie, Rotterdam; Wyllis, Utrecht; Poolfam, and at Amsterdam [?].

On Saturday, 15 March 1721, among other goods, there will be sold at auction two large and considerable Houses and Lands, situated within the mentioned [area] and a similar dwelling place under Bodegraven, with separate parcels of Land; by Brothers Pieter Hudsons, Auctioneers in The Hague. On 17 March 1721, the respective Books of the Justiciary of Holland, &c., consisting of fine Juridical, Historical [works] may be obtained at Amsterdam by Oosterwijk and the Widow Desberdes; Utrecht by J. van F[?]; Leiden by I. Lageraert and Jansonius vander As; Delft, Beeman; and in The Hague, Jacob Magnus the Auctioneer. On Thursday, 20 March 1721, in the morning at the Old Nobility Lodge, there will be sold an extraordinarily curious collection of Gold and Silver Fabrics, Porcelain, newly fashioned colored, red, blue and black Silks, colored Borders and Linens, black Bombasines, striped and flowered Calimancoes, Estamiennes, Serges of Bois, a collection of English and Scottish Goods, &c.: all to be viewed daily before the sale.

On Monday, 22 March 1721, at the house of a fine gentleman, there will be sold a collection of Turkish Silks consisting of [?] and damask Satins with Silk, broad and fine Taffetas, black and colored threads, Spanish Colors, [?], heavy Damasks, and colored Caniettes: also Cloister- and Lay-Goods of various colors and Silk, and some implements necessary for the Grain-Measure [trade].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 10th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 31.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 12th of March, 1721
Spanish Correspondence

CARTAGENA, 19 February. Much effort is being devoted here to constructing a large number of flat-bottomed vessels, which are being assigned to the Cavalry and Dragoons from Africa and according to the latest reports. Thus, following the advice of Gregoris, the Court there has resolved to establish new Regiments in Murcia and elsewhere; and in accordance with these same reports, officers have already departed to conduct recruitment drives. However, according to letters from Badajoz, a considerable company of men from Portugal arrived there, seeking to enter the service of this Court; but it is said that the King of Portugal has forbidden this, [as] none of his subjects may depart from that realm without permission, the penalty for which would be quite severe.

Mantua

24 February. According to letters from Rome, the Pope is very displeased with the latest dispatches that His Highness of Parma has received, regarding which he has held several conferences with Cardinal Albani. It is reported that the Duke of Parma is seeking the resignation of the Duchy of Castro and the County of Ronciglione, which domains had to be ceded to Parma until the year 1649; but since those years Parma has kept them, and the same reports suggest that the Duke of Parma's pretensions to these dominions cause some difficulty with the Pope, as the said reports indicate that Spain and France are happily involved; however, Rome is of the opinion that this transaction would not occur without a very good equivalent.

Germany & Neighboring Realms

VIENNA, 26 February. During the last three days before Lent, several important festivities have taken place here. On Saturday, Their Imperial Majesties attended the opera with the Archduchesses, where Alexander in Sidon was performed; on Sunday, Their Imperial Majesties attended the masked balls and later enjoyed themselves at the opera, after which the Imperial Family held the evening meal and concluded the week's carnival festivities in grand fashion; [His Majesty] has ordained that during Lent no one shall be permitted to eat meat, except by express order for reasons of necessity and where the sick require it. The Imperial Commissary and Imperial Council Baron von Kirchner is today taking his leave to return to Regensburg. However, the date of the departure of Imperial Council Baron von Kelmayer, as second Imperial Plenipotentiary to the Congress of Brunswick, has not yet been fixed; nor is it yet certain whether the Imperial Commissary Count von Wels, Chief Chamberlain of the Archduchess and Imperial Council member, will undertake the journey to Ulm to attend the Swabian Circle diet, which is beginning its sessions, and the arrival of the said Count is being awaited there with great eagerness. Since the delegates from the City of Hamburg who are seeking satisfaction from this Court for the damages that occurred in their city two years ago have been pressing their claims, they have now been informed anew that if within the time prescribed by the Emperor's wishes a Burgomaster is not sent to make apology and payment of the sums promised is not made to the Emperor's Resident at Hamburg, troops stand ready to execute [the order] by marching toward the said city and compelling the Magistrate to make full compensation. The Court has sent [an official] to the City of Hamburg to notify the Magistrate to send a Burgomaster as aforesaid to Vienna, under penalty of execution. Thus, following the example of the Duchy of Mecklenburg, this matter has been pressed forward with a statement of the misfortunes that the Duke has incurred through his obstinacy. It is said that the Count of Goess, who has gone to Petersburg as Ambassador of the Emperor, has recently received new instructions, it is said, that he should order himself in readiness to draw up representations against the Turks, and to work with Monsieur Camprèdon, Minister of France, to advance peace in the North. And since reports indicate that 200 delegates from the side of the Czar as well as from the chambers of Sweden are authorized to negotiate the establishment of peace, one hopes that peace in the North may soon follow; especially since one receives reports that the King of France has given some order to his Court at Holland, and then to consult with the Czar, not only about the great matters of that Duchy, but also about the general peace in the North; it is added that the [?] and Poles can already be involved in the order. It is believed that the Pope is employing all his influence to dissuade certain Courts in the Empire and to help prevent the execution of the Emperor's mandates in favor of the Josephites; but it is also expected that there may yet come to the Holy Father some ecclesiastical difficulties regarding this matter. Nevertheless, it is said that some Roman peoples of the Empire are busily engaged in completing their troops through new recruitment drives, and it is said that some of them will increase their regiments considerably. All reports from Serbia indicate that the Turks are making considerable preparations at Nissa, and that they can scarcely keep all their frontier fortifications ready. Moreover, it is reported that the conversation of the Porte regarding Ottoman preparations at the Court of our Secretary at Constantinople has been preserved, for which reason we should have some cause for concern; nevertheless, one maintains new hopes and confidence in the encampment of the troops, which numbers are diminished and somewhat subdued. It is established that the Turkish Ambassador to Great Britain, who is expected at Constantinople, will be received with no less ceremony than any of his predecessors.

Wednesday's Courant.

Regensburg

5 March. Several private letters from Worms report that the joy among the Protestants there, owing to the arrival of Herr von Reck, was inexpressible. Regarding their transactions, no particulars can be reported, as matters are not discussed among the ministers of the Protestant Corps. The said Corps has for some days caused several grievances from the Lutherans and the District of His Royal Highness of Nassau to be printed, in order to use them as needed; and their Memorial in favor of the 3 prisoners at Speyerlingen, which the Austrian Government has sent there, was delivered to the Imperial Principal Commissary only 3 or 4 days ago. According to reports from Vienna, the Imperial Commissary should arrive here this week with a Resolution or Answer to the Remonstrances of the Protestants to the Emperor; which, having been sold out in its second printing, is now being brought to press for a third time, as everyone wishes to keep it for private purposes. The rumor that the Benedictine Commissary might be employed in the proceedings at Brunswick is not yet certain; and it is said that in his place, to offset the difficulties that exist between him and a prominent minister, another might be appointed to the Imperial Diet. His friends here contend that through the service he has rendered to the Roman Religion in his Commission at Vienna and nearly to the Roman Religion in relation to Roman interests at that Court, he has gained a very great rise in favor with the Emperor. Today the Imperial Assembly has been convened, but since it was requested that the Roman College not handle religious matters, nothing has been decided until Baron Kirchner arrives with the expected dispatches from Vienna. Reports have come from the Palatinate that the Government there, on the strong insistence of Herr von Reck, not only issued orders to return certain churches, over which there is no dispute, together with the same catechism to the Protestants, but also the time is beginning to pass, in order to execute the Emperor's commands.

Protestants' Remonstrance

The Council regarding Berchtesgaden is said to be a writ of arrest, and if the high Principal Lords wished to put this essential conclusion into effect, which has not been done until now (since His Royal Highness of Cologne, to his unforgettable honor, has already regarding some Berchtesgaden emigrants secured just treatment through his Berchtesgaden Government), then it would indeed be some justice to come to arrests, which would, according to what is provided, be lawfully and permanently brought about. For it would not have reached [them] interstitially, and nothing more has happened than what in the year 1705 under this Government was already received regarding some persons who had been taken into custody at Berchtesgaden for reasons of religion, regarding which evangelical fellow-captives were declared at other Roman Catholic [places] at the Imperial Diet verbally without title and unhindered. This has had such effect that the said prisoners then came from Berchtesgaden to Regensburg and gave thanks for their freedom. But that at the present time similar [matters] with which they were also dealt at that time would already have been completed, is as incomprehensible again as it is not clearly [visible] from [the documents]; much rather, on the contrary, it is incomprehensible how one can proceed so carelessly with matters that happen or do not happen, or can assess whether they happened or did not happen, and how one dares to accuse [us] regarding an unfounded and not yet executed ordinance for the benefit of the City of Speyer (as is much discussed in the Conclusion of the old [document] of February), as if one had [?] & [?] distributed, or as if His Serene Highness the Duke of Württemberg, on the command of his fellow-Estates, would have been moved to do so? These are all things which [?] cannot be conceived to bear so little relation to the Evangelical Corps, to move His Serene Highness to it, as to the Prince himself to do so, even if His Serene Highness had not otherwise declared something similar to his aforesaid embassy. Of the same character is that which was subsequently laid upon His Serene Highness the Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel.

[...] and [...] are indeed [...] as also the writers [?] indicate, but such previous preparations one will still find in [...] [...], [from] 1719 and 19 February 1720; but a more honest conduct by oppressed fellow-subjects in a manner conformable to the Imperial Constitution, with right, and if His Majesty and the Empire in the last [matter], and then by order of Your Majesty and the Empire on the last [matter] of 19 February, presses his Serene Highness of Württemberg, to approach the present Lord [?] in a friendly manner, to settle the ordinances of the said Government undertaken [with] dispatch, just as His Serene Highness has never gone further than through friendly and natural persuasion for the oppressed city to his Serene Grace the strong assistance and intercession, which on the part of His Serene Grace has been received and answered in the most friendly and natural manner: this is all that is to be said from the matter of [...] and [...] through [...] which are gathered together and in time, grant [?].

But if it already went further to the speedy institution of the City of Speyer by compulsion, in truth it would hold very well against this grievous complaint of Your Majesty, [though] necessarily [provided], so would we in these days [?]

---

[Editor's note: The final section breaks off mid-sentence in the original transcription, and several passages contain lacunae ([...]) where the transcription could not reliably render the blackletter text. The document appears to be a lengthy Protestant remonstrance regarding religious grievances and territorial disputes involving the Duchy of Württemberg, the Palatinate, and the city of Speyer.]

Left Column

[Note: The left column of this transcription is severely corrupted and largely illegible. The OCR/transcription contains numerous errors, false word divisions, and corrupted passages that do not form coherent Dutch or English. What follows is a reconstruction of the most legible portions:]

A matter regarding the Reich, in accordance with the Land and Religious Peace, but continuing to hold matters of the inheritance distributions as before, and there as they remain in matters of inheritance distributions, as they are authorized to be, according to the ordinances of the college of 8 May and 6 November 1703, [?] the Corp [?] (and indeed emphatically), when [?] the Powers might involve themselves [?] from Württemberg, and that to His Royal Majesty of Prussia was requested Württemberg, and that His Royal Majesty of Prussia was requested, not to hold his hand thereto, and powerfully not to yield; not being aware that one took such a thing so ill [?]...

[The majority of this corrupted left column section is untranslatable due to OCR errors and cannot be reliably reconstructed.]

HAMBURG, 7 March. From Holland it was reported that the Nobility frequently gathered at Kiel to devise means for the restoration of Church governance and the fallen Credit, and it was said that some remarkable and beneficial resolutions might be taken there. According to letters from Stockholm, in the months of April a considerable Squadron of War-ships from England was seen coming into the Baltic Sea. The latest letters from Prussia say that the King of Prussia still held himself at Potsdam, and that the Queen amused herself on the appointed days with the Court Ladies. The Margrave Christian Ludwig found himself at Berlin, and the neighboring Margrave Albrecht held himself with his Court at Friedelsfeld. General Sido has handed over the Government of Curtain, which was entrusted to him by the King, to the Lieutenant Kinkel, the interim Commanding there, on condition that he be retained for him. According to letters from Copenhagen, the King's young son of Fredricburg had returned to Copenhagen, and His Majesty had ordered General Schulten to restore the Dikes in Holstein completely during the coming Summer; whereby the General would employ soldiers from as many Regiments as he deemed necessary.

France

PARIS, 7 March. Two Decrees of the King's Council of State are seen: one of the 2nd February, ordaining that the Schedules, Certificates and Memoirs, which are to be furnished by the Monasteries and Owners of the Effects which they bring to Pledge, shall be only on Paper that is not sealed. The other of the 4th of this month concerns the old Currency-smelting mentioned therein. Daily so much weight of Silver is brought to the Mint that they have been forced to support with Props the Floor where the melted [metal] is laid, and whereby the melters had already sunk so far that one feared a complete collapse: it is assured that a general smelting will be done of all the Coins, and that the same will be worked anew. It is still well assured that the Brief which the Duke [?] received these days from Rome not only breaks and condemns the Marshal of the Cardinal of Noailles, the Accommodation, the King's Declaration and the Registration; and one has the more reason to hear that this will not happen, because the Court has sent prima facie to the Sorbonne, whereby that Assembly could be troubled; and hereby the Anti-constitutionalists begin to flatter themselves that the Pope's decision will completely remain in their favor, whereby they will be able to defend the Liberty of the Church much more freely. Notwithstanding the Plenipotentiaries of this Court have already betaken themselves to [?], it appears that the conclusion of the Peace, which is to be struck there, is not so near as one had imagined: the departure of the Knight Sutton from here to that place is still quite uncertain; and many well-meaning persons there ascribe this delay to the untimely death of two [?]

---

Right Column

ENGLAND.

[The partial text discussing English affairs is too corrupted to translate reliably.]

FRANCE.

PETERSBURG, [?] March. [The text] writes how one sees there various things happened, whereby one is very [?] in dealings with [?] that the mutual grievances must be [?] meanwhile one holds the Sinus of the Noranites [?] to distribute to our [?], newly [?] the Recipients, who will have to deliver their [?] and other [?], and one will have new [?] on Thursday, which the Admiral and the King's Son on Thursday new will establish; Doubtlessly [?]; the Commissioners will begin their Assembly on Monday; and one trusts that from the [?], which after verification well chosen will be, new [?] well shall [?] on the general Farms, which [?] needed, in ordered [?] which good and proper shall [?] the Trade of the Remitters and a part in Annuities and a part in Rents shall divide. This must part good [?] must [?] by tomorrow [?] [?] [?] and a great part...

Although the Princes of Foreign [?], which by Parliament [?] and Paris, the [?], which by Parliament [?] and Paris, Parliament [?] day gives, very quietly [?], thereupon very many Goods [?] to take, continued to hold in few days for more than 100,000 [pounds] sterling and Alicante and Wines and other Liquors [?] more Wines and other Liquors attached [?] [?] the Duke of [?] the unhandled Smiths in Paris, Preceded Monday in the [?] of Spain eaten with Miss [?] [?] of [?] both employed [?] of the old Families in this Kingdom.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 10 March. [The text] writes that [?] various reports from Cadiz are very [?] various reports from Cadiz are very [?] reports from that City, which report the aforementioned Silver need not be brought to the Mint, if the Monday on the [?] should have [?] none with [?] over [?] Day they it from Antwerp from said City is settled; so that one shall take the certainty with the nearest Letters from Cadiz. Say the great Letters from Cadiz shall take. Say.

From Madrid were from Cadiz a Million and 200,000 Pesos from the Kingdom's Treasury [?], which for the gold [?] [?] [?] [?] [?]

[The remainder of this column is too corrupted to translate reliably.]

Notices & Advertisements

On Saturday, 13 March 1721, will be sold at public auction, among other Goods, at the Burgh within Leiden various houses and lands standing within said City; likewise a Farmer's Dwelling at the Soeterswyde and another dwelling below Bodegraven, with separate parcels of Land. Pieter Hutson, Bookseller at The Hague, on the 17th March in the respective Courts of Justice in Holland &c.; consisting of [?] Juridical, Philological and Miscellaneous books by the late L. Hoquius, Advocate for [?]...

On Monday, 24 March 1721 and following days, Joannes van Braam, at Dordrecht, at the house of the late Adriaan Meis, in the Brewery "The Aster," will sell his fine and well-conditioned library, consisting of a large assortment of Theological, Biblical, and Mathematical books, many Mathematical Instruments, Telescopes, Magnifying glasses, Bowls and Glassware, and on Friday the 23rd of the same month and following days, the following collection of very rare and valuable books, to be sold; [?] Graevius & Gronovius's Thesaurus, and many Genealogical & Heraldic works of various Heralds in the United Provinces, Haarlem by Keulen, The Hague by Hondt, and [?] and Natural [History], [?] A. [?], Leiden [?], by Deutzinger and Lugmans, Ghent, Gorinchem by Wijk, Utrecht by [?], Leeuwarden by Halma, Nijmegen by Patou, Breda by Voetius, and [?] Mercks, Anti-[?], Villingen by Maynard and Willeghaerts, and in other bookshops and with Booksellers; all of which shall [?] before the Sale [?]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 12th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 32.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 14th of March, 1721
Russia

PETERSBURG, 17 February. His Czarist Majesty, who recently departed from Petersburg, has elevated General Prince [?] and Chancellor Counselor Osterman to the rank of Baron; it is reported that these gentlemen will proceed to Novgorod this very week in order to enter into conference with the Swedish gentlemen, who are being assembled there. On the 14th of this month, the ice in the River Neva was broken over a great distance, which afforded the distinguished Architects the opportunity to have it brought to water in pieces; furthermore, the birthday of the elder Princess Anna was celebrated most splendidly with the holding of a grand Ball, illuminations, the display of fine fireworks, etc.; and in short, there are still three ships, one carrying 96 [?] and two others carrying 72 [?] each, which will depart from the harbor.

Italy

NAPLES, 18 February. By the post-felucca, which arrived here Wednesday from the east, word has been received that the wife of Don Antonio Pignatelli, third son of the Viceroy, was formerly named Paterno. On that day, the Jutte de la Torella, who had arrived at his house, received an order from the Viceroy to repair to the prison of Catania. Whereas on Sunday [?] with blood and [?], and without the 3rd [?] with fish for the Royal Palace was given over to the populace for plundering, there followed with German Wild and Venetian [?] through the street of Toledo before the Royal Palace, where the same was replanted by the people, in which occasion the citizenry with various troops [?] adorned.

ROME, 22 February. Last week there arrived here a courier to Rome with a letter from the Emperor to the Pope, requesting that his Majesty's nephew Alexander Albani be elevated to the Purple, and it is believed that His Holiness will comply with this request. Cardinal Alberni has sent a written notice, well received, to all Ministers of this Court, wherein His Eminence protests that the Congregation of appointed Cardinals has no competence in his matter; that it belongs to the Pope himself to confer upon him, since this is a papal obligation to appear in person, though such may be accomplished through his representative; but notwithstanding this protest, the said Congregation proceeded to draw up a process, and it is thought that the sentence will soon come to light. It is said that no one shall be appointed by the Signori Gentili as Secretary of the Congregation of Good Government, and it is said that Cardinal Nicolini Spinola shall be appointed as Legate in the Province of Urbino. On Wednesday, Cardinal Aquaviva dispatched an envoy to Madrid with notice that the Pope has dismissed him, on account of the infractions which His Catholic Majesty has committed regarding the restoration of the Fiefs of Castro and Ronciglione.

France

EXTRAORDINARY Letter from an Outlying Place, circa 24 February.

It is hoped that the sicknesses and entire misery will cease, and thus the joy of its subjects in many respects is elevated; for while many people [?] are scarcely affected, more than 6 or 8 people of [?] now note that those who dwell elsewhere [?] daily in 4 or 5 leagues practice [?], and since from this freedom the good order which has been imposed upon all matters is maintained; yet so long as it shall not be much relieved, it would be well to be cautious, and so long as this will remain uncertain, in order, as much as possible, to unite new measures. As one observes in the city and citadel of [?], that in the coming days if no new sick appear, one will begin the weekly quarantines; for during the past month the citadel has been most carefully guarded, and until we are not so infected as healthy. Our principal hospitals, where 1,500 to 1,800 people lie, would have needed good hygiene, or if some measures of guards had been sent for, they empty themselves of necessity, so that scarcely a day passes that many are not sent from there to the recovery houses and thence again to the quarantine stations, while in truth one could at present reckon with still [?] sick to be burdened. During this sickness many [?] and sick attendants, etc., have perpetrated many faithless and inhumane deeds, with the object of robbing and murdering the afflicted, and it is much to be lamented that one presently, regarding the gruesome crimes of the plague, sees daily different executions of these unworthy [?]. At Toulon, where one has heretofore had only the imagination of dread and fear of evil, the authority has now become truly vigilant. It is hoped that Arles and Tarascon will protect themselves through their great precautions, and especially that the evil may not extend into Languedoc, which sends us great anxiety, and where nevertheless, not being without effective means, all measures of prevention are being employed.

The Netherlands

MILAN, 12 February. The collectors of taxes have received orders to advance 2,000 Philippine [coins] of the Rhine within a year, notwithstanding that they have not yet been fully paid, and these collectors are even forced to render account of the public debt, so that the finances may thereby demonstrate the municipality.

VENICE, 21 February. Tuesday evening the Carnival here ended with all manner of festivities, as is customary, and we have had the good fortune that no inconveniences or accidents have occurred.

Germany & Adjacent Kingdoms

VIENNA, 1 March. On Thursday, the marriage was concluded here between the Margrave of Baden of the princely house of Swabia and the daughter of the supreme Marshal, Prince of Swabia, by which forthcoming marriage the last heirs of the two houses will be united in one; at this marriage bond were present the widowed Margravine of Baden, the Bridegroom's mother, and the Prince of Swabia, father of the bride, as well as the Imperial Supreme Court Master, Prince Anton of Lichtenstein, and nearly all the principal Imperial ministers. Today the aforesaid Prince of Swabia has set off toward his estates in Bohemia, whereto he is to be followed on the coming Wednesday by the widowed Margravine of Baden with the princely bride, in order that there, having received the dispensation from Rome, they may celebrate the marriage. Regarding the war, all here is very quiet and nothing noteworthy to report. In the outskirts, recruitment has indeed begun with the drum, and to make the regiments up to full strength, this is to be undertaken likewise in the other hereditary lands. The Emperor has commanded General Baron von Seckendorf [Editor's note: likely Johann Christoph Freiherr von Seckendorf, Austrian diplomat] to be elevated in his eyes concerning the office of the Gravenstrand, and has caused the diploma thereunto to be prepared in the Imperial Chancellery.

REGENSBURG, 6 March. The day before yesterday, the Baron von Dotzenberg, minister of the Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel, in a lengthy audience before the Imperial principal Commissioner, protested the injustice done to his master through hasty procedures regarding the quartering of troops in his lands against the communication of the pestilential sickness, and this solely upon the unfounded information of the Prince of Hesse-Rhinefels, as if thereby judgment of one of his states would be made, to such a degree that His Catholic Majesty (to whom nothing more was lacking than the publication of the Reich and Artillery ban) in order to have his troops deloged, would have arranged and ordered the Rhine Circle, to the satisfaction of the said Prince of Rhinefels, to have their contingents for the auxiliary troops march with utmost speed, and withdraw with violence, to which end this minister also delivered a lengthy memoir for justification of his Master's conduct to [?], to be sent to the Emperor: this matter makes not much movement among the Protestant ministers, and they have given notice thereof to their principals, to know how they shall conduct themselves therein; some are of the opinion that this work could well have consequences, in that one is little accustomed to the need of a prominent [?] upon the state of the land, without having heard him, with such haste has one proceeded. The plenipotentiary of the Protestant Corps, before his departure toward Zweibrücken (where the committee was to have presided) wrote that the Elector Palatine has finally issued a mandate to restore all copies of the catechism which his Protestant subjects would have taken away, to them again, and to place them likewise in the vicinity of the church at [?]; whereby at some places already a beginning would have been made. Tomorrow all the Rhine assembly, which convened the past Monday, will gather again, in which undoubtedly the execution of the latest decree will be resumed, and proposals regarding the redress of grievances since the Treaty of Westphalia will be made back and forth; also some further grievances are put forward for the benefit of the small subjects of Elector-Mainz, in order that these may be made public through the press and [?]. The French Circle [?] a new intention to make an end of everything. [?] to prevent their share in the Reich taxes, maintaining that none of all the circles in the recent war have been so much burdened nor suffered; yet others do likewise, also the Bishop of Lübeck; which could cause some difficulty. P.S. From the Palatinate it is reported that on the 17th and [?] of the past month two more mandates were issued; the first concerning the institution of the catechism, also the other of the Elector's [?] and the words [?] of the circle, and the second regarding [?] of the churches, [?] of the church, and according to his religion, in the lands' reformation on holy days in the cities and not in the countryside with locked doors shall be permitted to work; except as [?] is held, [?] must come to the weapons; to which [?] they may know the said ordinance on the other [?]. The [?] of Delt stood upon his departure toward Zweibrücken, in order to execute the orders of the high body.

Great Britain

LONDON, 4 March. Saturday of this [week] the [?] reported back from the committee of the whole house regarding the Royal message of Wednesday; which report [?]

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM, 12 March. At Batavia there arrived the ship St. Jan, Captain Willem de Koning, from East Zealand, and before the bar the ship De Jager in the Red Sea. On the 20th of February ships departed from there. The ships St. Jacques, Captain Bernardus, from Ijsselmonde, and two others, all three of which have now arrived at Batavia; and among these the ship Het Witte Paard [The White Horse], Captain Gerrit Gondeaux, also Margaretha, Leendert, De Goede Zeelung on the Maas, the St. Anna, Captain Willem Muys, and others, all from the Red Sea. Several ships from here, and two vessels were still in the open [water]. At Nantes there was much ice on the river, so that ships could not load, and some had therefore been driven ashore; meanwhile the ship St. Jan, Captain Pieter Meun Bernardus, also got out to sea and on the River of Rouen there is likewise much ice, so that around 200 ships cannot proceed. According to letters of the 18th instant from Stockholm, the following has been noted: and many ships, both at Hamburg around 200 and elsewhere, lie frozen, and one hears that the Cessation of Arms with His Czarist Majesty will soon be concluded. At Kiel and Königsberg it is reported: due to continuous ice, no ships can enter the Sound; and at Hamburg around 200 ships lie waiting. At Copenhagen, the Governor of Norway &c. has arrived from Elsinore. In Norway various ships lie, among them Christopher Jansen from East Hamburg bound through, which also [ship] Vilstein and some others, of which Captain Schelhorn on the 14th instant set sail for Lillieshon. The ship of Marin Braun, which had gotten 3 to 4 feet into standing water in the Vlie, was again drying out, and lay in the Sloet, where 11 other ships lay, and among them in good condition Jan Tomacte, Duyf, Marin Pieterse &c. The last-mentioned ship of Jacob Spijkerman from Gorkum bound thence, and also Nienderbijck got caught in the ice; it has since been run aground in deep water, yet it is hoped the ship and goods will be salvaged.

Madrid 50. Naples 3¾. Seville 3½. Bilbao 91. Lisbon 42¼. Valencia 82½. Livorno 91⅛. Genoa 93¼. Paris current 42¼. London 31s. 9d. Leipzig current 38⅛. Frankfurt current 29 Reichsthalers. Ditto current 3%. Danzig 290 Polish groats. Breslau 3¼. Hamburg 32s. 7d. Antwerp 3¼ per cent. Ghent 2¼. Brussels 3½. Zealand ⅛ to 1⅛. Rotterdam ⅛ to ½. Bank agio 3¼. Shares of the O.I.C. [Dutch East India Company] 179 at 4%. W.I.C. [Dutch West India Company] 165. Subscription 600. Bank Annuities 141, ditto O.I.C. 145, and ditto Z.Z. 165, all current [prices].

THE HAGUE, 13 March. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland held a meeting today. The Spanish Ambassador, the Marquis de Monteleon, was yesterday at some conferences with members of the Government. Last night a dispatch sloop departed from Scheveling for England. An Express from the British Court has proceeded to Germany.

The drawing of the lottery from the 1st to the 1st Class will now again take place everywhere, as soon as the current month of March passes, after which time no further drawings of these Classes will take place; the drawing of these will commence.

The Commerce, Navigation and Insurance Chamber of the City of Monnickendam, has submitted to the Gentlemen Five a proposal to contribute one per cent in money from their fellow-interested parties at the time of their accounting, though under express stipulation that the long-outstanding accounts of their Africa ship of 1721 remain in the sale of books of Mr. G. V. Hoving, advocate for [?], Utrecht, Historical and Miscellaneous Books; the catalogue of which is in The Hague by said Hutlon.

[Various notices of book sales and auctions follow, including:]

At Dordrecht, in the house of the late Mr. Adriaan Mels, an extensive library consisting of a great number of theological, historical, juridical, philosophical, mathematical, manuscripts and printed books and rarities. And on the days following, the property of Mr. Simon Muys van Hulst, in his well-equipped house, contains all [...] as well as very fine and rare books in all faculties and sciences.

Catalogs of both libraries may be seen at Haarlem at Kefsel, The Hague at De Brood, Nieudorp and Neaulme, Delft at A. van Damme and Lugman, Gouda at Staal, Gorinchem at Vuigen Wa[...], Willingen Paaysaar and Willemslijn, and in other cities by the principal lovers of books.

Further, a sale of fine and common [...] and convenient thread, Spanish dyes in cloisters and pepper-gardens, Turkish and other goods. All on Saturdays before the sale, as well as Old Style through the consent of His Czarist Majesty, sent to Archangel in the month of March [?] were [...] from [?] from the time of [?] to the return [?] were found.

Printed by FELIX DE KLOPP, near the Town Hall, 14 March 1721. With Privilege.

---

ENGLAND

LONDON, 7 March. [The original transcription becomes severely damaged here. The text appears to discuss Parliamentary resolutions regarding financial matters, including capital, payments in April, and various monetary transactions, but the corrupt state of the transcription prevents reliable translation of the specific details.]

Course of Exchange

**Exchange Rates of the Wisselbank in Amsterdam, 12 March:**

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 14th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 33.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 17th of March, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 18 February. Everything here remains in much the same condition, and the greater part of the silver remains still undistributed to its owners; the same is being sent soon, so that it may be broken up at the beginning of the month, and it is hoped that the two deputies who departed with such instructions have gone forth in such a manner that one expects a favorable answer from the King regarding this. On the other hand, certain private individuals had brought matters concerning the silver before the courts of the civil authorities, in hopes that, if it pleased his Majesty, they might recover the same at some point. The owners of the fleet are in such a hurry that they have fixed the departure against the last of March, so that the same, whether loaded or not, shall have to put to sea at that time; yet nothing is being loaded in the meantime, and everything awaits the answer of the King to the deputies last sent. In this bay, certain troops returning from Ceuta have not yet arrived, and one has received no news whatsoever from the West Indies. The ordinary current here is presently brought to the hospital. The ship Charles Madrier, having been unable to obtain leave from the court to depart, has sailed to Havre de Grace.

MADRID, 25 February. The letters from Ceuta report that the Moors are engaged in constructing a line of circumvallation to keep the city blockaded on the land side, as if they were of a mind to besiege it in that manner: but the Moors on the night of the 13th made a detachment of 300 horsemen sally out, supported by 2 companies of grenadiers and commanded by Colonel Don Manuel Ibanuus, who, with all the military skills at his command, had made very vigorous sorties to secure his retreat. These 300 horsemen, with furious zeal and terrible force, attacked the works of the enemy, and in the strength of their attack broke through and drove them in rout; whereupon they pursued these barbarians all the way to the fortified camp of the Spanish lines, where the camp of the Moor's army was lying, where considerable fighting became necessary. The Moors in their retreat suffered real losses and lost some horses: since this sortie the accounts say that much damage has been done on both sides. Furthermore, one has received word that their camp remains in the same condition, having sent out no troops, and that they remain entrenched in their camp.

Great Britain

LONDON, 7 March. On Tuesday the House, pursuant to the order of the day, reviewed the Report of the Secret Committee, and thereupon adopted the following Resolutions:

I. That the entry into the books of the South Sea Company of £574,500 capital, which is claimed to have been transferred between 4 February 1719 and 12 April following for millions of £215,375, made in the name of those persons for whose benefit the shares in the Company were received at excessively high prices, be annulled.

II. That all petitions and [shares?] received or broken up since 4 February 1719 until 12 April following, without genuine value being paid for the same, or sufficient evidence and acceptance of the paid shares having been given, those shares shall be considered part of the above-mentioned £57,450, which is claimed to have been sold for account of the Company, shall be entirely payable to the Company by all those who received or obtained such shares as aforesaid.

III. That the addition of £25,000 to the first subscription in cash, after that subscription had reached 1,371½ millions, as well as the reduction of £50,000 to the second subscription in cash, after it was declared that would reach 1,371½ millions, appears to be a corrupt deed, tending to corruption, and most prejudicial to public credit generally.

IV. That a Bill shall be brought in to compensate those who have been ruined by the false handling of affairs or the directors of the South Sea Company.

Whereupon it was announced that the said Bill would be drawn up by the following 10 members, namely: Dr. Lechmere, Jalhry, the Chancellor Lechmere, Lord Molesworth, the Attorney General, Jekyll Master of the Rolls, Smith, Ward, Horatio Walpole, Lowndes, Rolle, Lord Barring, and Pogely the Solicitor General. Finally, it was ordered that they present the same at the next sitting in accordance with the above-mentioned company and articles such as from the previous Saturday, and on the following Saturday of the coming month they will deliver their report thereupon. On the same day Mr. Conrad Gold was elected, with equal votes, as Treasurer of the South Sea Company, and Mr. Samuel Clark was appointed as Solicitor of that Company. Also on this day before the Court of the Exchequer at Southwark a case was heard concerning the Duke of Marlborough and the disputes regarding the building of Blenheim House, which final payment was due from [?] of his Majesty, and which belonged to [?] of the Crown. The court has rendered judgment in favor of the Dukedom; but it is expected that the House will appeal this decision. On Wednesday thereafter a Resolution was presented to the Lower House, one on behalf of the Mayor, Aldermen, Burgesses, and other representatives in the Wool Manufactures, occasioned by the export of wool from Ireland to France without payment of any duties; which petition was laid upon the table; and subsequently the petitions of Messrs. Sawbridge, Theodore Jansen, John Fellows, John Lambert, and Robert Susman were considered, requesting that by providing surety and payment of costs they be released from arrest, which was granted to them: thereafter Mr. Farren reported on behalf of the Committee of the whole House regarding the proposal to transfer a portion of the capital of the South Sea Company to that of the Bank and the East India Company; which report he having read at his place, and the Bill with the amendments being laid upon the table, those amendments were read, and the Bill, after one further amendment had been made thereto, was approved by the House, and the amendments were ordered to be written into the fair copy. The [matter] of Gilleon was not particularly discussed by those of the Lower House: but the Lords gave the second reading of the Bill to prohibit the use of painted linen, and kept the same in committee for the next Tuesday, when the advocates of the East India Company against the [?] representatives for the same Bill are to be heard. On the same day there came before their Lordships a notable case, which has hung for many years between the Duchess of Hamilton, who is the appellant, and Mr. Fleerwood, a Roman Catholic, concerning a dispute over the succession of a great estate in the county of Stafford, which the Duchess claims as the next of kin; but because the differing opinions thereon kept the sitting of their Lordships extended until an unseasonably late hour in the evening, the judgment cannot be delivered before next time. Today the Commons postponed their consideration of the Bill to better establish public credit by preventing the infamous practices of certain merchants until next Monday; and approving the Report of the Bill to punish mutineers and deserters, ordered the same to the fair copy. The secret committee, which continues to examine [evidence] daily, today examined Messrs. Lambert, Blackwell, Hawes, Susman, and Mr. Dunkley, one of the clerks of the South Sea Company. On Wednesday morning at 2 o'clock the Duke of Rutland died of the smallpox; it is said that he leaves both sons and daughters, and he is succeeded by his elder son the Marquis of Granby in all his goods and dignities; and by this a place in the Order of the Garter becomes vacant. From Oxford it is reported that the Duke of Beaufort is also severely afflicted with the smallpox, and that one of the daughters of the deceased Duke of Rutland has also been attacked by this sickness. On Friday the printer Miff was again placed in the pillory at Charing Cross, [?] which the Commons felt injured by, although he was struck with a stone by a sailor, who was soundly beaten by the crowd therefor and placed in Bridewell. This afternoon the Duke of Buckingham died in his advanced age of about 80 years. Robberies are running rampant here; yesterday morning a certain gentleman was accosted by a highwayman on the public street with a pistol held to his breast, to compel him to surrender his purse; he having handed it over, the robber forced him to proceed and follow his way without looking back, on pain of being shot should he turn around. Tomorrow 3 addresses are to be presented to the King: one by the members of Parliament in the county of Hereford, which will be introduced by Lord Townshend, Secretary of State, and since one has already heard the King speak favorably of the contents, it is hoped that this address will be very graciously received by the King; it is also said that the members who have supported it have been received and appointed by the Mayors, Aldermen, and magistrates of the towns in said county of Hereford, assembled in a general council. The third concerns the Reverend Lieutenant, [?], and other prominent gentlemen of the boroughs in the said county of Hereford.

Amsterdam 34 5s 6d, sight draft 34s 2d. Rotterdam 34s 9d. Antwerp 34s 10d. Hamburg 33s 2d 8/4. Bares, Bordeaux no price. Madrid 48 to 47½. Cadiz 48 to 48½. Bilbao 47¾. Livorno 51 to 50½. Genoa 52½. Venice 44½. Lepanto 44¼. Porto 5s 8d. Dublin 12½ to 13.

Stock prices: Bank shares 138; East India Company 142; South Sea first subscription [?], second [?], third subscription [?], ditto for cash [?], 4th subscription [?], annuity subscription [?] no fixed prices; Million Bank 128; Lottery Annuities 96¼ to 98; Bank Annuities 96½; ditto 4 percent 94; African Company 37; ditto new 16; Insurance 7 to 3/8; and Dock Building 22½.

France

PARIS, 10 March. Today the Chambers of Parliament have assembled; the Princes and Peers are all present to take a decision on the King's response to the Remonstrances of Parliament, as well as to judge the case of the Duke of la Ponce, inasmuch as the King has revoked the Decree by which his Majesty had drawn the case before his Council and disqualified Parliament from proceeding in the said case; now it is hoped that the Chamber of the said Remonstrances of Parliament will be drawn up in very forceful and convincing terms. The Lieutenant General of Police sent all those who had appealed to a recalled Councillor before him, to hear their statements. The King has removed from the Faculty of the Sorbonne by Lettre de Cachet the following 5 doctors, Messrs. Shuevet, [?]...

Monday's Gazette.

THE NETHERLANDS.

AMSTERDAM, 15 March. According to letters from the eleventh of February from Lisbon, there had arrived there the day before from Oudenarde the British Frigate. It is said [to have arrived] with the concluded Peace Treaty between the British Nation and the King of Great Britain and of Morocco; immediately [this] had an effect on the Exchange, much [?], which he at a certain [rate] would be convinced, said which [ship] and sailed toward England Fleet. There arrived at Lisbon the Ship the Maria, Captain Pieter Pieterse van Hua; there arrived the Ship the Hazre from Madrid, likewise in the [?] at the further [?] the letters of the 2nd [?] from Madrid, likewise in the there come [?] of Cadiz, b in and [?] as well as 200 [?] with [?] no Audience by the King would have been given in general notification in a Ship Hardat Ludis Alosfe le Bordeaux would have arrived, [?]; but well ie St. Malo le Bordeaux would have arrived, [?]; but well ie the Ship of Dirk Poppes, ie Morlaix in British Hardat Tigt Brittanya in Vlieland, ie Lucelle Sicke Tonnie and at Bordeaux [?] and Fietter de Haad, which Transit, and at Bordeaux [?] in Piettereeld in the Vlie lie in good state the Ships of Minoe Andrielle, Michael Wa de e Petty; Henct, F. Kaif, Jac Siecde Jaegh and four others still.

CURRENT AFFAIRS in the Market. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West Friesland, who next Tuesday will hold their Meeting, have for Friday brought the Orders with his Majesty's this [?] out Dispatch Burgomaster against the Reunion General of [?].
