Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 19th of March, 1721

GERMANY AND THE ADJACENT KINGDOMS

REGENSBURG, 10 March. Last Thursday, just before the Post's departure, the Imperial Commissioner arrived here, having been absent from Vienna for over four months. It is reported that he has also brought with him a response from His Imperial Majesty to the Remonstrances of the Protestant Corps, which were presented last month on the 16th by the Saxon Agent on behalf of the aforementioned Corps. However, it is said that hopes are not as favorable as they might be, though there is much suspicion that the Cardinal of Saxony will [?] communicate on this matter. It is also known that what was received was not a formal Answer, but merely a compositional Disposition, as the aforementioned second Corps [?] has received since his arrival the visits of [?] representatives of the present Ministries. It is also assured that matters concerning the Protestant Corps remain financially uncertain. [?] The election and appointment of a Reformed [?] minister at Rheld in the Gulick (which appointment fell to a certain gentleman [?] from Germany and was previously approved by [?]) has been revoked by order of the Freedom of [?], who holds jurisdiction over the aforementioned place and claims therefore the patronage; through a rescinded Mandate, whereby confirmation was forbidden on [?] and religious [?] was made null, something that was procured with great effort and expense for the neighboring three kingdoms Brandenburg and Bleuburg, [?] and has not been implemented. His Royal Majesty of [?] has written to the Elector Palatine about this, [?] concerning the dissolution of the aforementioned Community Council, but the latter sought to excuse himself, advancing that the matter is beyond his power and has been referred to the Emperor on all sides, so that he cannot do otherwise than to seek grace. [?] It is feared that the aforementioned Herr Steenbergen of Rheld must soon leave, should no redress follow. Whereas the [?] Commissioner of Conibanks, of whom we have read the [?], [?] that they do not hold positions derived from the magistrate religion, but those of the Quakers; yet the Independent minister of this city, who [?] through contrary testimony declared that he had found them to be no less than such Sectarians, but Orthodox Christians of [?], and had marveled at the liberty [?] answered all the questions put with such [?], wherefore the Protestant Ministers protect them with zeal, [?] them from the spiritual detention at Sphinckum, particularly saying that they shall in Germany have their full religious freedom in [?] concurrence. It is continued here still, with [?], [?] discussed for the Emperor. Before [?] the validity was [?], so that it is a

Mannheim, 17 February 1721. CARL Phillip, Hallberg.

The Subscription was for the Electoral Palatine Religious Commission, concerning the further distribution of the Catechisms to the Book-children.

The following was also made public in the Palace.

[The text becomes increasingly difficult to parse due to transcription corruption, but continues with similar content regarding religious matters and administrative orders]

At Betenthal it was charged that they do not hold positions consonant with the magistrate religion, but those of the Quakers; thus the upper independent official, as he [?] it, through contrary testimony declared that he had found them to be no less than such Sectarians, but Orthodox Christians of [?], and had marveled at the liberty [?] answered all the questions posed with such [?], wherefore the Protestant Ministers protect them with zeal, [?] from the detention at Spijkman the [?], and it is reported that they shall in the territory of the Duke of Wittenberg with [?] concurrence have religious freedom. It is continued here still, with [?], [?] to recruit for the Emperor. For [?] no church meeting was held, so that a [?] was, as also because the Council Ministers with the others in [?] harmony were, no less of their resistance against the [?] which they have received from their Courts regarding religious matters. [?] have also been with Baron Kirchner, to [?] orders at the supposition of the Emperor [?], after [?] the conference of that day henceforth, where after [?] [?]; it has been read a Letter from the [?] [?] (who on his visit travels to Zwelingen) [?] reports that the Booksellers had not yet been given back their copies of the [?] Catechism, and that the Electoral [?] [?] in certain lands, the orders to the [?] and costs had not yet [?], but that he hoped such would be managed.

Here follows the following Mandate of the Elector Palatine.

to whom the Religious Declaration shall be assigned. IV. All Religious grievances shall be settled according to the contents of the Colloquy's issued Mandate, and all like Disputes shall be held in the previous state after the Council Peace Treaty; the aforementioned publicly made Religious Declaration shall be observed in all respects to prevent inconveniences; wherefore the Councillor Cuntz has not only published this to the ministers of religion in the Church of [?], but also to each Community in particular, to regulate themselves accordingly: furthermore charging the upper Office without fail within 4 months to report on the issuance. Heidelberg, 20 February 1721. Electoral Palatine upper Office there SCHUMAN.

To the Bailiff it was also earnestly made known, to publish all the foregoing Religious points to his subordinate Communities, but also to each minister of religion, through the Church Writers to share a copy thereof. Leiden, 23 February 1721. Cuntz, Councillor.

FRANCE

PARIS, 14 March. According to letters from Madrid, the King, the Queen, and the Prince of Asturias entertained themselves with the Carnival festivities; and the King had an Ordinance published whereby Beggars are obliged to betake themselves to the Royal Hospital, where His Majesty has ordered that those unable to earn their own living be provided with lodging, food, and clothing; those who shall be found capable shall be employed in public labor and in manufactures. The following Monday the Princes of the Blood, together with the Dukes and Peers entered Parliament, inasmuch as the Duke of la Force had been imprisoned; in the afternoon he appeared, clad in flannel without hat or sword, which he had handed to his servants, and was interrogated for about 4 hours. The King's Declaration, whereby the Arrest touching this matter was brought before his Council, has been printed well enough, but the Duke has obtained from the Regent such that it shall not be made public. The procedure shall take place before Parliament, but the Sentence shall remain in abeyance until the King's majority, which sufficiently shows that the Duke has hopes with the passage of time; the matter shall therefore [?]. Approaching Sunday, Celebi Mehemer Effendi, the Great Lord's Ambassador, made his public entry into this city, and according to custom shall lodge in the Palace of the Ambassadors, to have, on Wednesday as is hoped, his first audience with the King; but whereas the equipages of his Excellency were shipped in Bordeaux hither without any news of that ship having been reported so far, all the gold and silver fabrics with Herr Boucher the merchant in this city have been gathered to furnish them for his Excellency's retinue. This Ambassador has a pleasing air, and his manner showed the gentleness of his disposition; his son showing no less agreeable marks of courtesy. On the day of his arrival the Duke Regent sent his first Equery, Herr de Biron, to welcome him in His Royal Highness's name. The Ambassador was clad in scarlet, which was folded with the same rich fabric as his Veil; afterwards Madame Biron with her daughters and 2 sons greeted this Ambassador, who received her very well, and his Excellency had chocolate, coffee and tea served to her, after which the ambassador presented her also orange and aloe water; when she rose to depart, young Effendi offered Madame Biron his hand to conduct her to the coach; and since then all the courtiers have visited his Excellency and have all admirably marveled at his kitchen. The previous Monday the commissioners appointed for the security of the Royal Effendi began [work], which was continued on the following days; but so little was brought them that on several occasions they had nothing to levy, but got only [?]. Tuesday the letters were at 7½, but have since risen; the second stamps are about 30 and those of the [?] government 90 Livres. The Arches Company in The Hague, which previously consisted of but 60 men, is now increased to 150; and after the previous Tuesday they received drums and trumpets; they have also been given a Standard. The Duke of Orleans held a review of his Regiment of Dragoons, which had escorted the Turkish Ambassador, and it is found that, excepting 200 of the Marshalsea's extra-dressed [?], as the horses, it may be called one of the finest [?] ever seen here.

GREAT BRITAIN

LONDON, 14 March. It is perceived that among the Commons over the discharge of Charles Stanhope there is not such good unanimity of votes; (which is reasonably justified) and it is said that General Rols, member of the late committee, after the aforementioned Stanhope was declared innocent, would have said that the aforementioned committee had employed all possible means to bring the matter to light, but the committee being found guilty and by the House authority being curtailed, thus the procedures and the investigation of the committee could not be hindered. In the meantime the hearing of Herr Aisleihe is postponed until next Tuesday, the Commons having asked the Lords for Herr Wallet, his son-in-law, who was in the care of the Pope in [?] and [?]. The Knight Blund has finally offered bail, through another request to be discharged from the custody of the Lower House, insomuch as he is found in like case with the Lords to fall [?], thus o so that well well be peaceful.

England

The house of Sir George Caswell, [?] of the same, upon his [?] man [?] will be held before [?]; after which the Bill on 9 Millions will be [?]; in order to answer today to that which the [?] of the [?] from the Capitals, as well of the Bank as of the East India Company in and [?], [?] was read, which after a long Debate with France passed with 177 against 93 votes, and was subsequently sent to the Lords [?]. That day the Lords heard John Eggleton, a case brought against the Gentlemen Ferry and Byam, 2 East India merchants; the Norwegian interest of the Place [?] thereby, that here [?], has been [?]; and by the saying of the Nation brought to profit, was very [?] strongly demonstrated, wherefore they request, that if the East Indian Cottons might be bound, and Clause for the conservation of the Indians the Bill would be withdrawn. Today those of the [?] have continued the Examination over the report of the secret Committee, concerning the extension or removal of this Death court [?] by Sir George Caswell, him declared guilty to the [?] and having promised these Procedures to be continued next Monday, to appear on Thursday in his place; when one will consider the charges brought against him regarding the £5000 which he has undertaken and which his colleagues, the Gentlemen Turner and Brickridge. The Actions of the Bank are 15 shillings and a half, East India Company 141, [?] 170, African Company 37; ditto March 26, [?] African 7, and [?] and ditto London 7.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 17th March. Regarding England, one has received Letters of the 16th February from Lisbon, which report that the Algerian [?] shows distress due to the heavy Storms on the Portuguese Coast, which came to Lisbon [?] Ships within; and the Fleet would have sailed first on the second day of Bayonne to [?] the Ships [?] 20 December from there but in 10th January could depart. On the [?] Captain Dominicus de Irrgoyes and the St. Jan Captain Jan Bordeyn, [?]; has arrived the Ship the Sara Captain Pichant from Transylvania [?] at Gibraltar [?] Barks from Barbary and Tetuan; [?]

The Directors of the Commerce Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn, requesting [?], to appear in the new Doelen of the aforesaid City on the 2nd [?] in the morning at 9 o'clock, and to help deliberate on matters of Importance, [?] On Saturday, being Thursday the 20th March 1721, one shall sell at Amsterdam in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, a fine Household goods, consisting of Ladies' Porcelain Cases, Tables, Mirrors, and other [?] Clothing, Floor-[?] Carts of the most foremost Masters, namely of Raphael of Urbino [?], Bartolome, of Belen, Frans Mieris, two Portraits of Holbein, Anibal of the [?], left by the late famous Gentlemen of Beuningen; as well as the Furniture [?]. State [?] Wolieskuyl Maklaarts, shall (according to appointment of 1721, in the afternoon at 4 o'clock, at Amsterdam in the New-syde's Gate Lodging the [?] undamaged hard blue Elbing in the year 1721 and following days, shall Joannes van Braam [?]

One is in arrangement on Monday, the 24th March 1721, at the house of Jolix [?], to sell a party Turkish and Syrian Grains, consisting of super fine Bracelets, Satins with Silk, [?] and final Polomytens, Notis, Fillis and threads, [?] threads, black Damaits, and coloured Caycnts; item Cloister [?] various colours of Silk, and some Necessaries for the Grain-Business. Cornelia de Boom and Iliaak Schellinger, Maklaarts, shall at Amsterdam [?] at the house of George Meyer Kaselyn in the Old Gentlemen's Lodging, sell one of 1200, one of 1100, one of 1001, 4 pcs., two each of 1000, one of [?] of Amsterdam, one of 1000, two each of 1000, all three in blank at the Office of Haarlem, two Obligations each of 6000, both in blank, [?] the Notice can be seen, which by the aforesaid Maklaarts can be obtained.

Philip Steen, broker, shall on Tuesday the 8th April 1721 and following days [sell] a fine Cabinet of Rarities and Merchandise, as a Silver Gilded [?] with various Types, together with a fine Cabinet of Silver, and [?] a Royal embroidered [?] and a ditto Japanese [?] of 13 pieces of Gold Japanese Stuffs, [?] with Gold and Silver Stripes, Armenians, [?] Pearl, an extra Cabinet with Horns, Shells, Sea-[?] and [?] costly Green Tea and Tea-[?], a [?] of 10 ells long and 6 [?] Porcelains, Lacquer-works, and other Merchandise more: which Goods [?]

Filip Pick, broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 23rd April in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, a party coloured Cloths, of 8 and [?] quality broad, [?] more, a party of 200 pieces of Cloth for Clothing, Silk, half Silk and Woolen Cottons, Nettlecloths, a good party Colds, a party Green Tea and Tea [?] with Brazil [?], and Havana Snuff-Tobacco, and other Goods; all [?]

At Leiden by Jab. Arn. Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 21st of March, 1721
Russia

PETERSBURG, 21 February. It was last Monday, the 17th of December, when Mr. Camptedon, Minister of the King of France, arrived here from Sweden via Revel, bearing a commission from His Highnesses [Editor's note: likely referring to Russian officials]. He gave notice of his arrival at the Church Court and to the diplomatic ministers present, who have since paid him their compliments. His commission consists of nothing more than what has been made public; however, it is understood from the said ministers that the mediation of the King of France will be offered in order to make peace between His Czarist Majesty and the King of Sweden; and that first of all a sufficient armistice must be firmly established. There is also [?] being brought into the water from the German dock. The time that this same nobleman must depart from Riga has been [?] by the committee of the aforementioned Mr. Camptedon (who has already held conferences with the ministers present at court), [?].

Russia's minister to Constantinople has departed, with whom certain documents were found intended for the Imperial Resident residing there, in order to lodge complaint with the Ottoman court concerning what occurred in the district of Nissa in the past month, to request sufficient order regarding this matter, and to learn to what end so much military force is being gathered on the frontiers and magazines are being set up. There is great eagerness to know what answer the Turkish court will give thereon. General Flemming, who has traveled from Poland to Saxony, is said to be preparing to return here to discuss matters of importance, as well as concerning the movement of the Turks on the Polish frontiers. It is assured that His Majesty is very strongly pressing the maintenance of the Brunswick Peace Congress, to finally end the war in the North. It is said that following the conferences between the Czar's minister Osterman and the Swedish ministers Lilienfeld and Strömfeld at Nystad regarding the regulation of the preliminaries to the general peace, the remaining matters at the said congress shall be concluded under the mediation of the Emperor and King of France. Meanwhile, it is reported that the Duke of Holstein has let it be known that he, for the advancement of his affairs, intends to travel via Abo [Editor's note: Turku, Finland] to Saint Petersburg to complete his intended marriage. News has been received by express from the Eastern Netherlands that the Marquis de Prie, at the request of the King of Great Britain, the Knight [?] and treasurer of the South Sea Company in London, who had fled, arrived there by land and was imprisoned in the castle at Antwerp, requesting orders from His Imperial Majesty. A plenipotentiary of the King of Sardinia is expected shortly at this court to regulate the differences regarding fiefs in Italy; and some believe that other important matters might also be in commission. New orders have been sent to the State of Milan to put financial affairs there on a better footing, and to punish those who have since some time been guilty of bad administration as they deserve, and to recover as much as possible from their goods, since the financial chamber has been greatly burdened by bad management. It is said that the Pretender has nominated a certain archbishop to be elevated to the purple by the Pope. The Nuncio Albani applies himself greatly at this court to bring the work of [?] to completion before his departure; however, he has been given to understand that at the present time it is judged that nothing could be decided upon this matter at first, but that the necessary orders concerning that affair would be sent to Cardinal Alban at Rome. Meanwhile, a rumor has arisen here again that the Pope has been seized by his old ailment in the region of Benevento. Consequently, the departure of the Emperor's minister [?] is accelerated; he has been sent back there again, as it is assured, with orders to his Excellency to inform [the court] what business the Turkish ambassador, who has come to France, might have at that court in commission. It appears that the two frontier cities of Fontarrabia and Saint Sebastian remain a very great obstacle regarding the progress of the Cambrai Congress. It is now again commonly said that the Empress might go to the spa at Carlsbad in the spring to take the waters. The treasure, which some time ago was dug up in the earth in the kingdom of Sicily and had been seized there by the clergy, has by judgment been awarded to her, because it has appeared that they had merely buried it in the earth in the aforementioned district of the realm, as protection from plundering by soldiers. It is assured that the journey of Prince Eugene of Savoy to the Eastern Netherlands, for which some time ago money was disbursed, is generally off; however, some military forces from this realm might still be called there. The Elector of Bavaria still makes considerable applications at this court regarding the arranged marriage for the Elector-Prince, his son. The Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel has made such sufficient representations at this court regarding the positioning of troops near Rheinfelds that no apprehension is felt. It is reported that the Imperial minister at the Swedish court, the Count of Fylag, has received an answer to his previous memorial, and [?]. With these letters, news has also been sent to this court. With these letters there has also been [sent] news of the arrival of the French minister Camptedon near Revel. It is considered that the consort of the Elector-Prince of Saxony will, to the great joy of this court, be aunt [?]. Recently the Emperor's privy counselor Count von Wels, upper court master [text cuts off]

Italy

NAPLES, 25 February. Last Sunday afternoon the shopkeepers and fish sellers likewise honored the glorious wedding of the [Bruno?] with a triumphal wagon laden with all sorts of types of fish and pasties, which was carried through the Toledo Street in front of the royal palace, where it was presented to the court of the king, who was in attendance, and received, just as those mentioned in our previous report, in honor of this triumphal wagon. Before the triumphal wagon went a great troop of sailors dressed in the Turkish manner, and a large multitude of shopkeepers, whose houses and shops were hung with flags and banners.

ROME, [?] March. Notwithstanding that the weather here was very raw last Saturday afternoon, in the evening there was a procession through the streets with triumphal wagons, representing all manner of figures, upon which and around them were many masked persons, and among them the most splendid was that of the family of Althan; on the back of the said wagon was a beautiful figure on the same field, representing a German Hercules, with Fame trumpeting between his hands; the wagon was drawn by eight beautiful horses, before which elaborately dressed persons on horseback blew trumpets; behind the wagon followed a coach full of disguised noblemen who played on all manner of wind instruments, and the last triumph, in which the Triumphal German procession was completed, was borne by fifteen German noblemen in very costly garments. When in the evening all the triumphal wagons had gone home, that of Althan returned with a very large number of men carrying flaming torches, which gave a wonderfully fine sight, [and returned] to the above-mentioned street, where they, under a great concourse of people, danced several times to great music and other amusement, and finally went home. That same evening, after the opera and comedies ended, the Ambassador of Portugal gave a magnificent ball to the assembly of nobility; which, however, felt obliged to position itself at the entrance to the palace, and the crowd was so great that the musicians had to be divided into three separate halls. The evening ball [was attended] by his Excellency and a great multitude of all ranks, and the entertainments to all were presented. And everything passed without any discomfort. Sunday morning the Venerabile [celebration] was held in the German [church?] of fourteen cardinals at the Jesuit church of Jesus in front of forty guards in the middle and upon a specially made machine, which was illuminated by a very large display of torches. Monday afternoon the Pope embarked in his carriage, and the court followed as well as the German singers on horseback to the aforementioned church to perform the solemn prayers there, after which His Holiness returned to the Quirinal. Tuesday afternoon there was a great concourse of masked festive people in the above-mentioned Corso street and through the common city; in the evening the aforementioned Roman Senate went with an innumerable multitude of people to the aforementioned church of the Jesuits of Jesus to witness the celebration of the Venerabile. After which the Ambassador of Portugal again gave a ball as just mentioned. Wednesday morning the Sacred College held Pauline Chapel in the church of Saint Sabina [?] of the Dominicans, where the church's pope and Cardinal Molli as grand penitentiary were found present. Thursday morning a congregation of the Holy Office was held in the Pope's chamber. Before the library were the bulls [?], those which had been held back there so long, which expedition is now completed, because it has been ascertained with satisfaction that His Catholic Majesty has received the [?]. Friday morning His Holiness, not having proceeded to the college of the [?], which was held in the apostolic palace.

Germany & Adjacent Realms

HAMBURG, 1 March

The letters from Stockholm of the first of this report, that one there great hope had that within few days a Stillstand of Weapons between the Czar and the Crown Sweden should be closed; the Messrs. Liensfeld and Stromelvelt the straight toward Nicobid were drawn to the negotiation, that the Preliminaries but alone should reach, with the Czar's Plenipotentiaries to regulate; after which the Peace at the Brunswick Congress under [?] and at Delft, France is supposed to be broken.

Course of Exchange

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 24th of March, 1721

SPAIN.

CADIZ, 25 February. Just arrived in this Bay after 66 days from the Island of Cuba was an English ship, bringing news that the Ship N. S. de los Rios of Cádiz, which had likewise set out three months ago, had arrived there, and that the same would depart again under the escort of two naval frigates of the Admiralty, which had brought the aforementioned parties to the Islands. That same day there also came into the Bay the warship of Captain Serrano with tobacco for the King from Havana; as well as the merchant ships arriving in 2 months from Buenos Aires and in 3 months from the River [Plate], carrying among broad cargoes some 2 million pesos; having altogether been laden with 9 million, but because news of the French had arrived at Buenos Aires, much cargo was left behind: Their captain was sent back there: this arrival has caused great joy here. Regarding the remittance of the silver, one remains still in the previous uncertainty, since the Audience had been held with the King and one does not yet know the outcome, [and] it will not be begun to load the galleons.

CADIZ, 27 February. Finally, on the 25th of this month, there arrived here the 2 ships from Buenos Aires, namely the Sint Jozef and N. S. del Rosario, having departed from there on 4 October; with these ships came various navigators—those ships which sailed from here four or five years ago—one does not yet know [their fate], since only 2 navigators came ashore; however, it is said that the registered gold and silver amounts to 9 million pesos. Since these ships, which had long been held back by heavy contrary winds, had only a moderate market, because on the South Sea there were still several French ships. The warship, which had suffered leakage in Havana, departed from there on 4 January, laden with tobacco for the King: before its departure, Admiral Crux brought news that the Flotilla in Arrilia appeared to be in order, having arrived there on 10 October. Into this Bay came Adriaan de Boer from St. Lucas and Jan Baptista Rempo from N. S. [?]; and departed the three English warships of Captain Schol, John Teller and George Ponerot toward Gibraltar, together with the British merchant ship Captain George Coleman toward the Levant; so that there remained lying in the harbor the large [ship], the Dutch [ship], and among them Aube Volkera, Gerard Durcomby and Gerardo Buys, by the Wind [?], a Genoese [ship], a Hamburg [ship], 2 Danish [ships], 22 English and 24 French ships.

MADRID, 22 March. Last Wednesday, being Ash Wednesday, the [...] of here and the King and the Prince attended in the great Monday Courant.

[The text becomes increasingly difficult to parse; the following sections contain significant transcription uncertainties]

FRANCE.

PARIS, 17 March. All letters from Spain speak of nothing but the mustering of troops and new recruitments. A courier from London to Madrid passed through here. The King of Spain still insisted on the restitution of Gibraltar, which the courier found it was not hastening; and according to reports, the King continued to attempt to bring the silver of private persons to the Mint. The ministers of the Emperor and Rome remain in the King's City, and nothing has yet been settled; and according to common report, the ministers of Spain, who are at Camernik, will shortly depart from there, if the military powers do not find [grounds] in the few days [remaining]. Recently the Turkish Ambassador made his public entry into this City; the same was very magnificent and well arranged; and the King, being desirous to see it, betook himself to the Gate of the Place Royale to observe his Excellency as he would pass; before the Train of the Household went the Inspectors of the Police and behind them the handhorses of the Companies Huxelles and Estrees; followed by the Dragoons with their Officers and Bayonets on Horseback, and thereto the named la Cornelle Blanche with Sabres drawn in Hand; after which the People of the Ambassador, and he himself in a Golden [carriage], and his Tolls at the lower end and the Turk was accompanied by the Cords of the Court, the Nobility, Lords of distinction and the Marshal's Guard: the Duke Regent and the Princess and Princesses of the blood, the King presiding, watched the entry. [Further details become unclear due to transcription difficulties]

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 18 March. The House of Commons, deliberations from the preceding Friday over the Bill for broader Friday and public Credit, in the prevention of harmful practices in the Stock trade, postponed this until this day; thereafter the House heard the overrulings on the report of the Committee and Sir George Caswell, upon taking his seat, three examinations of Daniel Watkins, Elias Turner and Jacob Sawbridge were conducted by the said secret Committee; and after J. Sawbridge, D. Watkins and John Clark had been examined separately by the House, and Sir Caswell had taken his seat and left the House, it was heard that it had become clear to the House that Sir George Caswell, one of the Affidavits of Turner and Company and Member of the House, around 25 December, after the House had begun to investigate the conduct of the former Directors of the [South Sea] Company, had encouraged Daniel Watkins, one of the Book-keepers of Turner and Company, to alter the name of Stanhope to Stangape in various places, as he also did at that order, whereby now the accusation of Mr. Stanhope comes to be dismissed. Said Sir Caswell, being called to his place by the preceding [matter], was presented with another question, which he defended, requesting time to vindicate himself by testimony before the Bar of the House; whereupon it was promised that said Sir would appear Thursday at his place; subsequently [the matter] was read with the continuation of said Report Monday. Saturday the Commons in Committee passed the Bill, forbidding the use of printed Indian linens, and the merchant of Norwich, mentioned in the preceding, defended himself very strongly with the arguments of Eggleitun &c. before the Bar of that House, saying and maintaining that the assertion of Eggleitun, that the price of that Cloth had fallen from 21 to 15 stuivers per ell, according to the vouchers he had from the sellers of those materials, was untrue, adding that, instead of forbidding Indian linens helping Dutch linens, such would on the contrary only further... [text becomes too corrupted for reliable translation].

ENGLAND

The House, in acquiring the apprehension of the Commissioners of Trade regarding the prohibition of Indian goods, whereby the common Commissioners have been compelled to arrange a certain manner of distribution, as was shown to His Majesty's Address on the first of February; this Resolution being approved, the House proceeded to consider the matter and ordered the Commissioners from the Report which on the 28th of January they had previously presented to the Commissioners of Trade, concerning actions taken against Indian Trade.

The Speaker makes the speech of the Lower House determine that the Lord Chief Baron of the Exchequer, who had been with him that morning and had delivered to him a Copy of the Inventory of the goods of Patron Eylen, one of the former Directors; which inventory was ordered laid upon the Table, to be examined and reviewed by the Members of the House; after which, according to the order of the House, opportunity was given for the examination of the general report of the Lords Commissioners concerning the prohibition of Indian goods, in Committee to be considered, which was there drawn up, and the matter adjourned to the following Friday evening. Subsequently, the House ordered that the Copy of the order to make one Million, the Memorial whose Letters, for the account of the said Company and the Memorial which nine views over the said Letters by way of lending to the said Company, as [?] the certain mentioned Company there being and against expenditures of Money to [?] for put forth, and also various Books of Money to [?] for the House would gain; and that Knight John Fellows, Charles Joye, John Blunt, Richard Holditch, Francis Hames, Edward Gibbome, Robert Chefer, Theodor Lardeni, Edmund Blackwell, John Gore, Robert Chapim, William Adfell, Robbert Surman, Matthew Weymouth and Chapim, in Lingfield, Singleton and Daniel Watkins, should appear before the House tomorrow; Thereupon those of the Lower House with 17 against 70 Voices approved the election of Mr. Moreton as Member of the House for Criclade; and the Inventory of the goods of Mr. Eylen, delivered to the Lord Chief Baron, amounts to approximately [?] of State appointed in place of the deceased Mr. Craggs.

On the coming Thursday a general Meeting shall be held by the said Company, where it is then likely that it will declare what it intends to do in regard to the Holders of Petitions, as much concerning the redeemable Annuities as Money, for that [?] alone this day in the Government of the Directors of that Company have decided that Plan to communicate. It is thought that the Company in that regard will follow the Project of the third Walpole, not concerning it giving 155 pounds Sterling, wherein is contained the 10 per Cent; Sterling for the last St. John's distribution; as also the increase of approximately 41 p. in that Project named whereby [?] p. ready Money demanded of the Company; but what the incorporation concerning 1st Millions, the one half in the Bank and the other in the East India Company, that of is the Bill still before the Parliament, which the choice can at the first Money obtained Company to preserve the same, where lay the said last mentioned are thereof to proceed, and that the said Company shall will it: it is also expected that the said Company on Thursday shall declare and [?] of the money which to them in Money for the various 6 Months from St. John to Kermis [Editor's note: Candlemas] 1720, whereby the Circulation of Money shall be powerfully restored.

The Minister who on behalf of the King of Portugal at the Court of France was stationed, with the present at this Court being, here came, yesterday had his farewell Audience with the King, and was led by the new Secretary of State Milord Carteret. The Duke of St. Albans is said to be [?], as also that the Daughter of the Count of Berkeley has [?] the Chickenpox—

ADVERTISEMENTS & ANNOUNCEMENTS

Leyden Courant, by FELIX DE KLOPP over the City Hall. The 24th of March 1721.

AUGSBURG, 16th March: Yesterday the Cardinal of Koban came with a suite from Strasbourg, [?] of the allied force [?].

ROHRENBURG, 17th March: It is reported that the Protestant Corps here over the good disposition of the Duke of Deux-Ponts regarding the Redress of Religious Grievances is well satisfied.

FRANKFURT, 19th March: The Mr. von Reeck, who Wednesday Evening arrived at Deux-Ponts, had his first Audience with the Duke, was mildly received and kept at Table. His Proposal, although the common [?] thereof was [?] by the [?], decided in the redressing of Religious Grievances without any withholding shall execute; to which already a Mandate had been proclaimed, and the Samples were with the Redress actively occupied.

There shall by the Brokers Cornelis de Roon and [?] Schillinger at Lodgements sell various Obligations, namely one of 4500, one of 1000, one of 800, one of 740, one of 500, all on Names [?] those in Blank and Counting-house book, an Income Letter of 1500 and [?] in Blank, the charge of the City of Groningen and Surroundings, together are.

[?], shall on Monday the 31st of March 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, a loading extra puyks purk new Virginia Leaf Bay-Tobacco, of the [?] on the Newbales in the Warehouse of Mr. Bastiaan Molewater.

on the 23rd of April 1721, in the Old Manor Lodgement, in the morning at 9 and in the afternoon [?] white various sorts, and other domestic and foreign Linens and Stuffs, English Manufactures, Fine and common Chriien, Caroenes [?], at Bosoms of [?] and [?] pounds, a party extra puyks pulk Brazil [?] the day previous to the Sale to be seen.

The useful Useful Dutch Gardener, instructing how barren Trees fruitful shall make, and now well [?] 1000 become and to be cured; as also a Description of all sorts of [?] and the ancient [?] Cloisters as 300 years hitherto [?]; [?]; renewed and enlarged beside eyes and Deer.

become time is, as also of the lofts of Vineyards, Fruitles, [?], &c. &c.; where also added [?], concerning the lofts to make, with and through [?], as also a second examination of the Mosaics, instructing the ways Culture of all the [?], since the continuation of the new precise Dutch Gardener [?] together strange sharp selections until Soul-arrangements [?]

D. Gentlemen Governors of the City [?] &c.

D. KLOPPER, on the Breadstreet

March 1721. Ad vi Privilegia.

Notices & Advertisements

At Leyden by Jan. Ann. Langeraij, now printed for the fourth time; here in this Land under this Climate, a Garden with its Parks, Plantings &c. well shall be arranged, built and maintained; in what manner barren Trees can be made fruitful; also the easy and certain manner to prevent them becoming sick and to cure them of Diseases which spoil the Flowers of Fruits: all by reason with necessary fig. 1721. Also printed for the second time by the same: renewed and improved at The Hague, also with all the said [?] the design to make Type, such as Apricots, Plums, Cherries, Figs, Mulberries, Walnuts, Palm-kernels, also Tree-falls, and wild Plantations for Gardens to make (the healthy [?] of exercise and proper treatment concerning the cultivation of Moor-Plants which until the Crown-flowers, also to the shady and the same in an early time 1721; as also Carolus Stephanus: Mensel-Davy, and Zider-Lanes over manifold selected Crops.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 24th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 26th of March, 1721

SPAIN

CADIZ, 25 February. Notwithstanding the fact that the two Buenos Aires ships that have arrived have been away for 5 years, and seeing that the return shipments are not arriving as before, they appear nevertheless not to be as rich in silver as one had imagined, because the registered silver from [?] Barco, which reportedly contained a million in 19,000 pieces of eight, has not yet [?]. The ships on board carried 21,000 hides, 40 bales of Virginia wool, etc., and because it is said that the reason for their departure [?] the Silver indult and [?] pieces of eight has been regulated, one hopes that the same [will be maintained for] the future. One expects here daily an advisory from Cádiz with details of the arrival of the Fleet. At Tarifa some military from Ceuta has arrived.

GERMANY AND NEIGHBORING KINGDOMS

REGENSBURG, 17 March. It is understood that His Electoral Majesty of Bavaria, in his Electorate, has all foreign marriages, especially those for the Emperor, placed under bond, and since he appears to intend to augment his Troops, the chief officers have already been appointed to raise some new regiments in Germany.

HAMBURG, 21 March. From Copenhagen comes word that the Queen of Denmark, to the great sorrow of His Majesty and all the subjects of the realm, died there on the 15th of this month.

ENGLAND

LONDON, 21 March. On Wednesday, Mr. Kelfall, in accordance with orders, delivered to the Lower House a copy of the Memorial of the South Sea Company concerning the Exchequer Bills issued by way of loan and the obligation made by the Company for the million aforementioned Bills and the indemnity thereof; after the titles were read, the House ordered them laid upon the table to be examined by the Members: thereafter the Secretary at War was ordered to bring before the House on Thursday morning Rudder John Bluet, who is in his custody; it was also ordered that the following should appear: Robert Surnam, James Telland, Joffeu Spaebura, Edward Poultier, Daniel Watkins, John Clerk, John Mount, Edward Owen, and Will. Bowles: the House ordered that the journal book of the [South Sea Company] from February 1682 to 1719 be delivered to the House, and that John Falcoubtdge, bookkeeper of this [?] Weymouth, should [?] for the House; at which time it was also ordered that the Usher of the Black Rod should [?], and it was ordered that the book containing the accounts of Knight [?] Francis Hawes, one of the former Directors of the South Sea Company, which were given in the second report of the third Commission, be at the same time delivered to the House. On Wednesday the Members examined [?], by Mr. Aislaby and the Commissioners of the Treasury and [?] (Lower House), and finding that all, and some more than once, the delivered copies, etc., had been read and examined, and Aislaby himself had been heard in his place, after which he withdrew and was petitioned. It came out before the House that between the 3rd and [?] December 1719, for 22,000 pounds sterling in Adieu [stock], were purchased by the broker Knight on account of Aislaby, who was Chancellor of the Exchequer and one of the Commissioners of the King's Treasury; that from 30 January 1719 to 4 March, 70,000 pounds in Adieu were sold by the broker Weymondhold on account of the aforementioned Aislaby and delivered on 12 February 1719 by order of the aforementioned broker Knight, by the under-broker Rob. Surman, for the benefit of the aforementioned Aislaby at 150 per 100, were handed over to R. Abenctan Weymondsgld; [and] to Aislaby, who received a portion of the 70,000 pounds as aforementioned; that by the same Weymondsgld a further 3,337 pounds 10 shillings, being another portion of the 70,000 pounds, were by order of Aislaby sold; that by the broker 12,000 pounds in Adieu were taken between [?] and Aislaby, even as [?] the South Sea Company had presented its Proposition; that the taking of the same 20,000 pounds by Knight, for the benefit of Aislaby, is a notorious [?] [and] corruption in Aislaby; it also became clear to the House that between March 1720 and the following 11 November, an account was held between Turner and Company and Edmund Waller, son-in-law of Aislaby, which amounted to 7,644 pounds 8 shillings sterling, and that there was also a dispute between the South Sea Company and the aforementioned Turner and Company with Waller; in consequence of which Turner and Company [are indebted for] 77,600 pounds sterling, which sum is contained in 2 obligations, one of 44,600 pounds for the benefit of Waller, and the other 33,000 pounds for Thomas Waldioll, for the benefit of Aislaby; that it appears that the aforementioned Aislaby, without authority, has been able to subscribe and receive the million Exchequer Bills on the shares of the South Sea Company; that likewise by Aislaby [?] has been able to receive the subsequent subscription at 900 per 100, and to encourage the same, [?] money has been taken, which together amounted to 7,344 pounds in the aforementioned Aislaby the fraction of the subsequent [?] of the dangerous and in hand deceitful Project of the South Sea Company continuing and promoting, nothing other than an extraordinary and excessive dealing wholly intended, and joined thereto the executed Bills, their course in their continuation and in the dangerous Projects to ruin good, true subjects of the King, and to strike down the public credit and commerce by the neck; whereupon the House ordered that the 200 [?] Aislaby, for his aforementioned reprehensible crimes, be expelled from the House and brought to the Tower in prison, to which the Speaker was appointed to expedite the necessary orders; then it was also ordered to bring in a bill to ordain that the aforementioned Aislaby, within a year or before the end of the next sitting of Parliament, should not leave the House, to discover his goods and effects and to prevent their sale and alienation; and since it had already become very late and even an hour into the night, the House adjourned until Friday morning: [It should also be noted] as some wish, in the late part of this Session, in the examination of Mr. Hawes, former Director, to the astonishment of the House it appeared that they had burned the book of transfer of shares between them, which was ordered to be brought before the House. Yesterday the South Sea Company held a general meeting, in which it was resolved that from the received monies of the first and second subscriptions, shares shall be made at 300 and the other at 400 per cent, with the St. John's distribution of 10 per 100 in shares; and after a long debate it was resolved that from the paid monies for the 3rd and 4th subscriptions, likewise shares at 400 per cent with the said distribution should be made, whereupon the distribution of St. John was deferred until Christmas, at 5 per 100 in cash money. Today those of the Lower House declared Sir George Caswell guilty by 237 against 92 votes of accepting 50,000 pounds in shares; whereupon the House ordered that he be expelled therefrom and brought to the Tower, and that, just as in respect of Aislaby, a bill should be brought against him. There is a general satisfaction and contentment among the people over the cordial procedures of Parliament regarding the aforementioned matters and bad direction of the South Sea Company; it is generally thought that this wholesome method will cause credit and commerce to revive. The inventory of the effects of Mr. Read, one of the former Directors, amounted to 263,000 pounds sterling. On Monday in the [?] House in the writing room of Knight, there was found 181,000 pounds sterling in obligations and 135,000 pounds sterling in notes, together with some keys, which were not then opened. It is said that the Marquis Pozzo Ducno, Minister of Spain, has presented a memorial to the King. Execution is now actually being begun on the charitable gift of Mr. Thomas Guy, formerly a bookseller in Lombard Street, and to establish a hospital, the ground in the forecourt of Southwark, next to the hospital of St. Thomas, having already been purchased for this purpose, and according to the plan this building, in which well 400 sick brethren can be maintained, will cost more than 150,000 pounds sterling. Yesterday an order was published by which the apothecaries, in accordance with the decision of the doctors, shall distill their waters and oils and make the various compositions. Word has been received that the ship the Nute Galy, commanded by Captain Richard, coming from New England to Lisbon, was taken by the Turks and brought to Salé. On Monday the postal letters arrived from Ireland; they report that the government in that realm, being informed that some evil-intentioned persons in the county of Waterford and other provinces in that realm were occupied in recruiting soldiers in the service of the Pretender, [Editor's note: James Francis Edward Stuart, the Jacobite claimant], on the 25 February issued a Proclamation, by which all officers, both civil and military, were ordered to apprehend all those who should be found recruiting, or who should already have engaged themselves in the Pretender's service, with a promise of 50 pounds sterling reward for each person who shall be convicted of having done such recruiting, and [?] for each who shall be convicted of having engaged themselves in the aforementioned service; and at the end of this Proclamation all and sundry are prohibited from becoming any soldier in that kingdom in the service of any foreign power.

LONDON STOCK PRICES

The shares of the Bank closed at 3:10; 140 books; of the East India Company 142 and 1 half; of the South Sea 16 fl. before opening, the first subscription, 2nd ditto, 3rd subscription, ditto for cash, 4th subscription, and subscription of annuities at no fixed price; the Million Bank 131; Lottery Annuities 97; Bank Annuities 96; ditto 4 per cent 93; Assurance Company 34; ditto new 23 and 1 half; the Stock Exchange Assurance 7 and 1 quarter to 1 quarter; ditto London 7 and 1 quarter to 1 half; and York Building 12 and 1 half.

FRANCE

PARIS, 21 March. Two articles of the King's Council of State are seen; one of the 14 March ordering the execution of that dated 26 December 1720 and 22 January of this year, containing the suppression of the bank notes and the increase of their rank; the other passed on 26 May coming [?] the delay of the marines, noted by the article of 26 January last concerning the verification of contracts, papers, and other documents mentioned therein. Day before yesterday the King heard the office, which was performed by the Abbé le Pervoit, canon of the cathedral, in the chapel of the Tuileries. The Abbé Fleury, the King's great-uncle, has requested and obtained his dismissal, after which this position was given to the Abbé Virgins, who has just come to court, the office henceforth being observed by someone else. On Monday Mr. Schaub arrived here for the King of Great Britain, but it is not yet known whether he, during the [?] diplomatic visit [?]

BRUSSELS, 24 March. Mr. Hop, Lord of the Mogul, Ambassador to the Court of France, arrived incognito in this city on Wednesday evening, and His Excellency is lodging with Mr. Jacques Liborne, Director General of this city, on account of the death of Count Santini, the father of the aforementioned [?]. He receives no visits, although the gentlemen of quality have had their condolences conveyed to him. On Wednesday, the Feast of Saint Joseph and the nameday of the Marquis de Prié, His Excellency was sumptuously entertained by the Marquis. On the same day, his second son, Count of St. Maurice, took command of a Company in the Regiment of Baden, which, newly clothed, paraded in review in the Park of Sablons. Regarding the matter of Damme, Captain in the Regiment of Dragoons of the Prince of Holstein and brother of the Baron d'Audegnie, who is accused of having maintained correspondence with Holstein and his brother, a court-martial is to be held.

THE HAGUE, 25 March. News has been received that the late advices from Mr. Hendrik Naerdi, Burgomaster and Aldermen of the city of Delft, delegate regarding the said city to the assembly of the States General. [?] regarding the deceased of Delft of the Company. The Lords Directors of the West India Company at the Chamber of Trier are hereby requested to consult regarding matters concerning the said Company.

Colonel Stanhope dispatched a dispatch from Madrid to the British Court, and conversely another has sailed from there northward.

The sale of the lottery tickets from the 3rd to the 3rd class is set for the coming Monday at 30,000 guilders, after which time no one may claim his tickets; note that the drawing of the 6th class will take place under the auspices of Mr. [?], Lord of Weede, Dom-De Lem, etc., etc., etc., for various gentlemen in York, notable on account of their own coat of arms with 5 lilies, a large heraldic composition with 4 feet, and a coat of arms with a ring and a [?] coat of arms, where the 3 last [?] in another coat of arms as the 6 lilies and [?], will receive a premium of 25 guilders for honor. All those with 10 shares or more in the same Company are requested to assemble on Wednesday, 1 April next (being the first Wednesday of that month) regarding the interests of the said Company, so that with the authorization of the Aldermen, they will [?] in their Church, with the Church [?], the same for the first time before the aforementioned Church, residing on the Church Square there, and trusting [?] on pain of absolute forfeiture [?].

At the bookshop of Johannes van Braam are sold the outstanding house effects of Holk, to the Honorable Gentlemen Aldermen and heirs of the estate, with rare and fine books in all faculties and languages, among which several on heraldry; the catalogues can be obtained from the book merchants. On Monday, 31 March 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, [there will be a] sale of extra choice new Virginia leaf bay tobacco from [?] at the Nieuwehaven, in the warehouses of Mr. Baltiaen Molewater. On Wednesday, 2 April in the evening at 6 o'clock, [there will be a] sale of extra choice fine Virginia leaf bay tobacco, with parcels as they will be laid out on Thursday, 3 April 1721, at 5 o'clock in the evening, at the house of George Me-ergias: new-fangled colored druggets, sarges for ladies, crown embroidered goods, fine calamancas, damasks, half-silk ribbons, durayes, satins, etc., and the lodging of the morning buyers, from 3 to 4 o'clock in the afternoon for viewing.

[Advertisement for] The new and precise Dutch book of trees, in which are shown [?] and which trees should be planted, making unfruitful trees fruitful, and now well-bearing, so that they can be used for beautification, and likewise said description of all kinds [of trees] with reason and experience of more than 6 years' usage in those areas, renewed and enlarged: register of most all kinds of [trees] [?], as well as of the kinds of vineyards, [?], willows, etc., etc., to which are added, [?] kinds of gardens [?], as a third renewed, [?] will be 3000 second [?] of cultivated kitchen gardens, instructing the proper cultivation of all [?] so that one can have them at an early time rather than under ordinary [?], enlarged with new precise Dutch garden [?] [?] spiritual [?] [?] [?] [?]. Lords Regents of the city of Leyden, etc., which will be sold on 2 April 1721 at Amsterdam above the Exchange [?] so that in the cities of our land, with the most and principal [?] under the printing press [?] named treatises of [?] and [?] for which [?] a cursive precise [?] where the seeds are sown and preserved, described in English, as well as in the natural medicine of [?] [?] [?] The treatise is published by him in service of the general [?] in light.

LEYDEN.

CAPELS, 4 March.

Regarding the contagious sickness and its spread from the strong possessions, [?] the trade in the sea between the fiscal or [?] are placed; and [?]

A chapel in the Vlie [?] of Mario Chiggi with [?]; and efforts are made to [?] the Scudi [?], to give to [?] good and also of Easter, which his Uncle wishes to retain, although he has [?] Easter in a secret [?], and His [?] has [?] the costs of the Princesses fallen, said Princesses, on pain of 30,000 Scudi [?] say the Prince [?] the Princes [?] should do [?].

Mr. Fr. on Saturday evening a very [?] that his Church affairs were by him led good in one of his exits. Regarding [?] to be [?] in his government [?] of Albania particular audience [?] of the bishopdom at [?] on [?] in a congregation of [?], was accorded [?]

GENOA, 8 March.

The said sickness is sufficiently [?] however daily still [?] and Arles remain still [?] and [?] in his former houses thrown in, as well as medicinal remedies sold [?], and one has the care to hold [?] or the [?] at the open country is excessively very strict to be and a very [?] some hope that such [?] other mean and the sickness.

VENICE, 13 March.

[?] the Count Charles of the Emperor and the Duke [?] via Rosa Gonzaga Troutti. Having departed from here, as is reported.

REGENSBURG, 26 March.

The King of Prussia [?] Prussia propositions in the Imperial Diet of the Reich [?] the aforementioned [?] there alone aforementioned [?] still so swiftly not at [?] the hand a sort of answer [?] and submission of this [?] like answers [?] that the best of the [?] [?] soon understood in a conference over the replica of the Emperor on the Corps, who [?] there [?] [?] accused, is his [?] to have read [?]

PARIS, 21 March. The [?] will [?] the third afternoon [?]; to which are well [?] and thereafter via [?] repairs back the [?] Muette not [?] the street St. Honor to [?] house in the [?] turns; Madame and [?] these places. [?] Marquis de Bully, and the [?], still today we the [?] still much that [?] [?] and [?] is for that place. The City [?] has been [?], and even his [?] have an ash Madame de St. Street going, all time when, from the [?] [?] Mde night of the [?] so one [?] to be [?] outside houses thrown in, as well as medicinal remedies sold [?]: displaced, and one has the care to hold [?] the first [?] at the open country is excessively strict to be and one [?] hoped [?] called the other [?] there the sickness.

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES** The course of exchange is on Amsterdam 34, 4, sight 33, 1 fl. Rotterdam 34, 7. 6. Antwerp 35, 10. Hamburg 33, 4. Paris 24 and a quarter to 1 half, Bordeaux no fixed price. Madrid 27. Cádiz 48. Bilbao 47 to 6 and 1 half. Livorno 51 and 1 half to 2 and 1 half. Genoa 53 and 3 quarters. Venice 46 and 1 half. Lisbon 5 to 5 and 1 quarter. Porto 5 to 4. Dublin 13 and 1 quarter to 13 and 1 half. AMSTERDAM, 14 March. According to letters of the third from Madrid, there, upon the oral and written remonstrances made regarding the circumstances, a decision was taken at Court, and it is feared that His Majesty intends to leave the silver in the mines. At Genoa (where such things can happen), news from Livorno reports that a ship bound from here to Amsterdam, which had already lost all its masts in a storm, put into Madeira. Letters indicate it was expected to sail toward Genoa. Letters from Riga, Danzig, and Königsberg report that on account of unusual water conditions at sea, a ship from Medemblik in the Voigt Fleet, which had been there for three weeks at sea by Christoffel Rudders, has arrived. Much damaged at Copenhagen came the ship of Miss Willemina Martien Cornelide, and at Bordeaux Jan Adriaantie de Boer, both from here; on the Thames John Deen from the Sound, George Boer from Bremmatan of Venice, Thomas Philips from Rotterdam, etc., and according to letters from London and Plymouth, several Dutch ships which lay there in quarantine [?] and quarantined. That the ship of Jan Harinck Rott from the East Indies will arrive in the Vlie, continues the letter from London, with exchange rates from Madrid 90, Cadiz 114, Seville 112, Bilbao 90 and 1/2, Lisbon 44 and 1/2, Venice 83, Livorno 91, Genoa 93 and 1/2, Paris 44 and 1/2, London 33 shillings 90, Danzig 290 [?] 1 gr., Arnau 93, St. Hamburg 12 shillings 90 and 1/2, St. Antwerp 2 and 1/4 C., Ghent 2 and 1/4, Zealand 1/4, Rotterdam 1/4. The Bank agio is 18. The shares of the East India Company are 780 to 90, West India Company 100. Sugar 0.85. Gum.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 26th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 38.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 28th of March, 1721

LEYDSE FRIDAY COURANT.

ITALY.

NAPLES, 4 March. Since reports from Provence indicate that the contagious disease now diminishes, then increases again, and spreads from one place to another, it has finally been decided to conduct a quarantine at Quingye, in order to regulate the entry of persons and goods, at the expense of 1,300 Ducats to the Fiscal authority and arbitral correction. All idle workers are being sent away here, and only Hamonen and the Galion are permitted. On Sunday the Sacro Collegio held ordinary chapel in the Quirinal; at that time was also published the marriage of Abbot Don Mario Chigi with Donna Falbina, daughter of the Duke of Jaguette, and the Peace was arranged to secure that Abbey, which brings in 1,000 in revenue, against a young Son of Prince Chigi, which was permitted to proceed, because Don Mario is authorized by a Bull from a Pope, who was his Uncle, to be able to satisfy the Church pension, illustrating His [?] Stowaed to come to settle. However, His Holiness has proposed various Bishoprics in a recent Constitution; and His Holiness, regarding the difference in the Carnival between the Roosts of the Puesteeten Piombuigh, Runggh and St. Angelo for distinguished young Princes, together with all their blood-relatives, on pain of 10,000 Scudi, has ordered arrest in their Palaces; but the departure of the Prince and Princess of St. Angelo to Naples received it yesterday. Mr. Falconieri, Governor of this City, suffered on Saturday evening a very severe attack, which since then has been such that the Church regulations have already been applied to him, but yesterday he was found outside, to the inexpressible joy of the whole City. Whether the Purple elevation will take place, and Mr. Vincenriini Nuncio at Naples succeed him in his Governement, remains to be seen. On Tuesday the Cardinal of Althan had a private audience with the Pope, and again obtained the confirmation of the Wisdom at Wecentot as an [?], which on Thursday in a Congregation of Cardinals and Prelates, convened for this purpose, was graciously accorded.

GENOA, 8 March. According to reports from Provence, the plague had indeed been cured and ended at Marseille, but nevertheless some people still die there daily; Aix, Tarrascon, Cannes and Arles remain very afflicted; some villages appear to be completely depopulated; at Toulon there were already 43,000 inhabitants, as well as the shops, excepted those which sold foodstuffs; the Magistracy would no longer meet there, and one could not find the money to maintain the Quarantine; from the first of this [month] to begin: the practice in the countryside is excellent, but since the cold continues to be fierce and a great many locusts have fallen, some hope that this would completely purify the air; however, others fear that the disease will thereby be increased.

VENICE, 15 March. The day before yesterday the marriage was concluded between Count Chiaraxda, son of the Emperor's Ambassador at this Court, and the Daughter of the Marquise Donna Maria Roza Gonzaga Trotti. Today Mr. Law departed hence with his Son, it is said towards Italy and Rome.

THE HOLY ROMAN EMPIRE.

REGENSBURG, 20 March. Since both the assembled Roman Catholic and Protestant Envoys say they must await further instructions regarding the mutual propositions, on Monday the Assembly of the Empire did not break regarding the matter of the Election; meanwhile the former made a neat list of all their grievances to deliver to the Cardinal of Saxony; and in general Assembly only the matter of the Chamber at Wetzlar was undertaken, which is still not brought to so speedy a conclusion. One sees here in confidence a sort of Response from the Prince of the Rhineland, as a refutation of a Letter from Dietz; but the Author appears to have had little liking for such responses: nevertheless there is news that troops have already been withdrawn with the present Radical [forces].

P.S. This evening it became known that the Cardinal of Saxony-Zeytz in a Conference with the Minister of Saxony handed over a Reply of the Emperor to the known Remonstrance of the Protestant Corps, which the latter would also refute in orderly fashion, and the Protestants are accused by His Imperial Majesty of having violated his highest authority in that document and gone beyond their duty.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 21 March. The Turkish Ambassador Celebi Mehemt Effendi says that this afternoon he will have his public Audience with the King; for this purpose 1,500 Men in Arms will conduct the Parade, and afterwards the Review will be held for His Excellency: the Court has made some arrangement in the Route, just as at this ceremony in the Museum with regard to his place; the Ambassador has been ordered to proceed through the Street of St. Honoré to the Gate where the Ambassador's, the King's House in the Hamas will be arranged with the Front towards the Disteyburg; Madame and all the Princesses of the Court will go there; this magnificent Train comes from the Haton. It is thought that the Marquis de Bully and Mr. Carteret, members of the Company of the Indies, will still be released from the Bastille today. From Provence comes news that the contagious disease at Marseille and Cannes is abating; but it is also said that the poor condition does not favour the Duke de Cue of Calais, who was a great constitutionalist and was even banished, is here deceased; at his funeral a very great number of Appellants [?]. On Friday Madame de St. Surpret became ill.

...dignity of the Peace of the Realm, therefore reassembled; and the Petition of the latter submitted to the King, is very well drawn up. The Pope has the Mr. de Beauveau Archbishop of Toulouse held half the costs for his Bull as Archbishop of Narbonne; as well as the Abbot Nanti, for the Abbey of Miormontiersz. At present many great Doctors are separating from their E.F.ncoën, and some do not wish to pass this as a new French Mode. The great Doctor, who recently as an Appellant of the Continurie appeared before the Mr. baurdi, was found to be Mr. Sacquart, who was also interrogated, and firstly asked whether he had his name on the [?] friendly; whereupon he answered to recognize the same with much pleasure, and that he would always with joy embrace all occasions that might present themselves to him to give witness to the Truth, for which he had made a promise, even his blood to shed; secondly the matters are grave that keep him being interrogated; an Appeal once more to renew; whereupon the same answered, that the necessity to again protest against the Continurie, which perverts the truth, the essence of Faith, the noble duty; and in particular against an Accommodation, so disadvantageous to Religion, which was made against all orders, without the assembly of Bishops, and even without the Bishops, who had the most interest therein, being heard, has greatly harmed ecclesiastical rectitude and sincerity: which also introduces dangerous methods into the Church, to mix truth with lies and yes with no and nay, contrary to the obedience which one owes to the Pope and the Church. To the fourth question, regarding the Declaration of the King, he answered, that His Majesty would become the most faithful subjects among the Appellants, that he was brought up in a high School, where he had learned to respect and defend the Holy and Sovereign Right of Kings, but that the subordinate respect which he feels for the Royal Authority, has with great sorrow shown him the injustice and violation done thereto, with these in force in such a terrible time-critical moment, wherein one has forced the young Monarch a Declaration, contrary to his sentiments and interests, which instead should have supported his piety and the foundations of his Reign; that one has so visibly made him transgress the determinations which the Almighty has set upon the laws of the Magistrates; that this Declaration, authorizing an essential evil Bull, and an Accommodation, which brings nothing but new evils, which the Bull has caused, nor increased; and that he in this occasion was forced to hold to the words of Holy Scripture, that one must obey God above men; fourthly, when asked regarding the signature, he answered that it was not necessary for one to encourage him thereto; since he followed his own inclination, understanding, hatred and conformity, as well as the general opinion of the Believers against this Constitution, as soon as the same came to light; and that for the rest he will account himself patient to suffer and die for the truth. On Monday an extraordinary Council was held regarding the Bank Accounts, where it is said Mr. de la Houdaye continues to oppose; and some think that the same, being [?], he has decided to withdraw; against which it is said that this Mr. should have told the Duke of Bourbon not to oppose him more in his principal concerns for the Company. Meanwhile the Advies have somewhat fallen, those of the 2nd Seal are 84, those with the heathers 120, and the Bank notes of 1700 are 66 Livres. It is said that the Duke of Bourbon has declared himself party for all those who shall pursue against the Company of the Indies; also it is said that the Court on the question of the Swedish doubts, regarding the damage which their Subjects have suffered in the Paper trade, gave a negative answer. From Toulon the news has been received that a Mas, which had 300 Bread to feed, by one of his blood-relatives in the eighth Degree, lost all the further Heirs [?], 40,000 Livres have been left.

THE NETHERLANDS.

AMSTERDAM, 26 March. The French ship le St. Helena, Captain Jaques Mesle, has sailed from Alicante towards here; and the Ship de St. Jan Bautitia, Captain Louis de la Fuente, from Bayonne and last setting out towards this City, has put into Camanque in Ireland by [?]. It is not yet certain whether Captain Guinct, sailing from Alexandria towards here, or another Ship [?] de Matta has come in. At Bilbao has arrived the Anna Michiel Timmetmaa and at Kendres the St. Jan Cornelis Will from here; at Bayonne the Hope Willem Groen from the Meuse; at Bordeaux the Juft. Catharina Gent; at Rouen the Gezellbcrs Corn. Clok &c. from here and the Naas at Rodi. Pieter Jnullen from Zeeland; at Martinico the Iafon Captain Nolert from Bordeaux; and at Nantes the Juft. Willemina Will, Buyk &c. [?].

THE HAGUE, 27 March. Mr. Grysheuken, appointed by the Lords States General as Inspector of the Revenues and Lietenants in Zeeland, regarding the City of Vlissingen against Their High Mightiness as Expediter, recommended by Their High [?] at the lower [?].

Left Column

According to the many times made agreements, the time of the doubtfulness of the L. Monday the 1[?]. The peace and peace expedient, 200 advertised here by the Director Co. the further arrangements and will be administered, as then the right of Loren is to be certainly begun on the 1st of April following thereafter.

At night registered the 1st [?] December 1st to Uysfeght in the House of the Lord well-booted violent mouth through housebreak [the] following items, and sundry Spoons and V and a small Florelbe son that above and Tenalls is to Uysfeght, and a Duckiel thereon with a half thereon; then a Silverdo y and j Handles, and a Cover thereon with a [?] neat did have; someone whose Goods lies [?] by the Silverwares Guild [?].

Directors of Commerce and Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn, request all who wish to ship in the new Doctor of the common City on the 2nd of April current, the morning at 9 by the Tower, to help deliberate over matters of importance, the same Company Merchants and Cornelis Bulkn here convened, and shall sell at Amsterdam on Monday Nieuwwerdt-three Logs; 100 Green Brandstills; Rayns silk and part Dame; from 60 to 50. Green Likers, of length 24 to 28 feet; 50 part Inland Deeds, ten and one and a half [?] [?] inch, from 50 to 160 feet long; 100 in Inland Deeds and Prussian Deeds, ten and the other Beams and Deeds and Planage by the Brugeysle; in the Rapenburgergraft, and the other Zotten as will be further announced.

3rd March 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Exchange Rates are today at Madrid 90 to 95 and 1 half. Cadiz 112 and 1 half. Seville 111. Bilbao 90. Livorno 91 and 1 quarter. Venice 88 and 1 half to 3 quarters. Livorno 91. Genoa 93 and 3 quarters. Paris sight 41 and 1 half to 41 and 1 quarter. London 31 shillings and 8 and 1 half. Leipzig current 18 and 3 eighths. Frankfurt current 29 Reichsthaler. Ditto current 19. Danzig current 20 Florin. Riga current 20 eighths. Hamburg at 60 and 1 half [?]. Antwerp 2 and 1 quarter per 100. Groat 2 and 1 eighth [?]. Brussels 3. Zeeland 1. Rotterdam 1 eighth to 1 quarter. The Aqua of the Bank 3 and 1 eighth to 1 seal [?]. The Shares of the D.E.I.C. are at 810, W.I.C. 106 to 108, [?]. 980 to 800, the English Bank 141 to 140, ditto D.E.I.C. 141, and ditto [?]. 105, all cash.

Notices & Advertisements

On Tuesday the [?] April and following days, shall be at Amsterdam in the Doele-street [?] assemblage in noteworthy Dutch and [?] bound Books, Hot [?] conditioned [?] in long Years by [...] [...] proceeded and [?] by a renowned Lover [?] Amsterdam by Addt. van Damme and J. Oolderuyk, Haarlem M. van Lee, Leyden H. van Briel Foreman, Rotterdam N. Kotte, Dord van Braam &c.

On Monday the 21st April 1721, shall at Amsterdam in the Old Hot Lodgment, in the evening at 7 hours, Houses, Stalls, Cows and Wagon-house, Houseman's Dwelling, Stalls for Horses and Cattle, and that for the River the Vegt, under the Lordship of Nigtevergt, large 51 morgen [Editor's note: morgen is a Dutch land measurement, approximately 0.85 acres], and there yet 7 Acres located Billets can be seen and purchased.

On Wednesday the 16th April, shall be in the Bookshop of Johannes vander Linden, sundry new and some unused Books, consisting in exclusive Theological, Juridical, Botanical, Medical, Latin, Antiquarian, Lexicographic & other Miscellaneous. The Catalog in the stores, to Rotterdam the Widow N. Bos and Remand, Dordrecht, and in the further inland Cities by the principal Booksellers.

At Gouda by J. vander Kloes are still to be obtained some few Exemplars of the Remarks on some passages of the Holy Scripture, principally the Language and Nature of the Gospel at Moordenert, in Quarto.

Mr. Thomas Angelholme, Chirurgeon, Operator and Bleacher at Amsterdam, on the Singel, to him has been delivered a Philological Mercury, which performs its action without [?] so that the same unafraid of pregnant Women and young Children may be used. Amplifying, which by their use, in a neat and easy manner heals, however old or malignant they may be, without that anyone needs to abandon their affairs or circumstances. Patients can help themselves through the aforesaid Medicines, provided that they beforehand [?] some knowledge, which daily oversight and own treatment seek, such better and speedier healing to effect hope, as yet how there and verily the healing sought be, will we (the Patients beforehand question, but to full ease thereof by accord accept; through the aforesaid Medicines powerful been can; nor is much here to note: incapacity having clearly Justices &c Accidents, too much to note here: Incapacity having a Sick [?].

At Leyden by Joh. du Vifie Bookseller are still very many Exemplars to be obtained [?] W. Heys Catalogue of his Expedition the Mister Great Coiter, Ambassador of her K., the [?] much created matters in the Ambulant put forward, by the Great Lord, s.to Clazis of Sweden Charles the [?], the Republic of Venice, and other high [?] and wine [?]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 28th of march, 1721. — With Privilege.
