No. 1721.No. 40.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 2nd of April, 1721
West Indies

CURAÇAO, 1 January. On the 6th of the previous month, the young Daniel Jan Bontekoe arrived here, bound for the Coast, and on the 12th Pieter Loethouver, both from Amsterdam; as well on the 29th the Great St. Jago and Paulus Chevallier from the Coast, bringing word that several more ships were still lying there, and that without the Zeeuwe of Capri, Matthias [?], as well as that of Wil. Houtkuysens and myself were also at Tucacas. Britannicus [?] is sailing hither, and at Laguayra 2 large Spanish ships with cacao bound for Vera Cruz. Having arrived there at Laguayra is the Spanish ship from Curaçao, Vicente Orias, which was again to sail from there. The 2 aforementioned Spanish ships and the Captains Domingue Louis de Colombo and [?] having already departed for Vera Cruz, were driven mast-less here by storm, but as soon as these are repaired, they will continue on their way thither with haste. It is said that some of our barks have retreated from Laguayra to the Caracas Coast, because by order of the Viceroy of Fez the Governor and some merchants of Laguayra were asked [?]; and likewise at Rio de la Rache and about Porto Rico 2 barks would have been conveyed; as well by the English privateers the rich brigantine of Capt. [?] returned to Curaçao, the Captain having already departed for Jamaica to reclaim the said brigantine. At St. Maria two ships are lying; from the following islands some barks have arrived here. The 4 ships of Jacob Black, Hendrick Tyman, Warnaar van Staade and Paulus Chevallier are to depart from here for Amsterdam at the end of this month or the beginning of the next.

Spain

CÁDIZ, 10 March. The Marquis de Leede, having arrived here from Africa, reviewed the Garrison of this city, and then departed with various other high commanders for Madrid; but in the evening before the general's departure, news was received from Málaga that into the harbor of that city from Ceuta had come 34 transport vessels, also bringing the 2 battalions of Aragón, the old regiments of foot from Granada, Murcia and Córdoba, those of horse from Montleon and Guadalajara, together with all the equipment and baggage of the said troops, and also many cannon pieces and ammunition. Here they are engaged in equipping 6 warships or frigates to escort the fleet, which is to depart for New Spain on the 15th or 20th of the coming month, for which purpose the galleons and other ships and valuables shall be prepared; they continue here in the arsenals to manufacture a considerable quantity of ammunition, and shortly many damaged cannon are to be repaired. In the newly formed troops many new regiments are being established.

[The remainder of this section appears severely corrupted in the original transcription and contains numerous illegible passages. The text mentions arrangements at Rome involving a palace donation to the Knights of St. George and some story involving a woman and creditors, but the narrative is too fragmented to render coherently.]

Germany & Neighboring Realms

REGENSBURG, 24 March. The reply of His Imperial Majesty to the remonstrance of the Evangelical corpus, especially its ministers, has greatly displeased them, because the expedients to which His Majesty has resorted are quite rigid, and that corpus handled itself in a manner resistant to the implementation of the Imperial decrees; among other things it is stated therein that the said corpus has not been ashamed to say in their remonstrances that however just the orders of His Majesty might be given for the amelioration of their grievances, this through the stubbornness of the Roman Catholic princes and peasants will hardly be viewed as satisfactory; that the grievances of the Protestants, though indeed not extended to the utmost, could not be compared with those of the Roman Catholics, which might well have more to complain of; that the said corpus has also not hesitated in its remonstrance to call into question His Majesty's respect and supreme authority, and that through various immoderate passages, although very much [?] evangelical conviction, His Majesty is assuring them that he will do justice to all their rightfully grounded complaints. It is assured that the rescript will also be printed in order to make it public like the remonstrance of the Evangelical corpus. This foresight had emboldened the Roman ministers for some time, such that they particularly imagined that on their part everything had been accomplished without reproach; but this inherent arrogance has been clearly shown since last Friday, when the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz communicated to the Elector-Ministers the Emperor's rescript to the Roman ministers, and it falls to them to communicate the same to the said ministers; wherein His Majesty shows himself greatly displeased with their conduct and forbids them in the same to pursue the harmony, peace and tranquility of the realm, and [text becomes illegible] [?]; for that His Excellency moreover will not allow such [?]; but on the contrary feels keenly about all that they have undertaken against tradition and established laws and daily inclinations; and that they must be assured that although His Majesty himself is Roman Catholic, he will not favor the religion in prejudice of the Protestants, but will respect the courts without [?], according to the high [?] [illegible]; and since this demonstration has greatly vexed the aforementioned Roman Catholic ministers, they have employed all means to prevent [text becomes corrupt and largely illegible].

Word is heard from Osnabrück that the King of Poland is there and intends to return to his realm [remainder illegible]. Word is written from Rome [remainder illegible].

FRANKFURT, 26 March. It is understood that the Count of Wels, who at present represents the Emperor at the Swabian Circuit assembly, has also betaken himself to Salzburg, Munich, Württemberg and Kassel; that the Count of Metz, who for some time was at Hanover, is to depart there toward the courts of the circles in the Netherlands, Westphalia and Münster; and that Baron Kellier, before betaking himself to Brunswick, is to discharge some commissions at the courts of Bamberg, Würzburg and other states: to which add that Baron Kirchner is to go once to the court of the Palatinate and that of Speyer; and that all the Imperial military authorities absolutely have orders to involve the said courts in a peaceful settlement of religious grievances, thereby to speedily restore tranquility to the realm.

Great Britain

LONDON, 28 March. Tuesday, Mr. Hutcheson presented to the Lower House a bill for the expulsion of Knight Aislaby from this kingdom within a year and before the end of the next sitting of Parliament, whereof the first reading was given, and the second was ordered; and the House, which then stood in grand committee according to order to consider the representations of the Lords commissioners of commerce, concerning the prohibition of the use of Indian fabrics and the manner proposed to effectively secure trade to the East India Company for the benefit of the kingdom, postponed the same until the following Thursday morning: after which the Sergeant at Arms having marshaled all the members who had been loitering in the hall of the House as well as in the Court of Requests, it was proposed to read the aforementioned portion of the report of the second committee, which was then done; but as this concerned a matter of great importance, it was deemed good that consideration thereof should be postponed to the following day, and thereupon first to examine more closely Knight [?] and Fellow, formerly sub-Governor of the East India Company, and the former directors Joye, Edward Gibbon, Robert Chester, Richard Holditch, as well as the under-cashier Surman, together with Knight Blury [?], who appearing before the House the following day, were all examined at length; after which the House having entered into deliberation, very heated debates arose concerning the question whether the former cashier Knight had taken 50,000 pounds for a certain member of the House or not, and the debate having continued until 8 o'clock in the evening, the accused was declared not guilty by 233 against 79 votes; and further consideration of the report of the secret committee was postponed until the following Friday. The same day the House received the petition of the clothmakers' apprentices, in which they requested through their advocate to be heard by the House against the bill to regulate their daily wages, which petition being read, was referred to the committee on general bills. That same day the Lords gave the third reading to the bill [?]; which was somewhat to incorporate a part of the capital of the South Sea Company into the Bank and the East India Company; after which their Lordships also read for the first time the bill for better regulating the juries, and postponed the second reading thereof. From the 19th to the 25th of this month, His Majesty's gold in the customs house has been entered for transport to Holland and elsewhere. The aforementioned Monday, the Lord Abergavenny and Baron of England died at his house in the county of Salop, being succeeded in his titles and goods by his minor son. Yesterday evening about 10 o'clock, Sir James Craggs, Postmaster General, died of an apoplexy; so that the charges against him by a secret committee, which were to be examined by the Lower House today, are ended by his death, and it is said that perhaps a bill to legitimize his estates might be brought in. Upon complaint concerning the insolence of the servants and their disobedience to the orders of the House, it was ordered by the Commons that the Sergeant at Arms should forthwith take into his custody all the servants he should find in the corridors leading to the House on or at the stairs, since the access of members was thereby much obstructed, and that none of those in attendance should be released without a special order of the House. Then the roll call of members was made, and it was noted that those who were absent should appear at their places after eight days; after which consideration of the report of the Lords commissioners of commerce regarding the trade of the East India Company was postponed until the following Tuesday, when that report shall be considered in grand committee. That day was held a hearing in the house of the South Sea Company concerning the proposition made at the last meeting to deliver receipts to the holders of the 2 last subscriptions, and the votes being taken today, this was carried with a unanimity of votes [?]; the Lords thereafter hearing an appeal, and those of the Lower House, on a proposition by Sir [?], presented an address to the King, beseeching His Majesty to grant [?] to the House concerning a matter which had been [?] [remainder illegible].

PARIS, 28 March. Since the severe winter season has kept us confined with strong winds and heavy rains, we have been occupying the King with walks and various exercises to amuse him from time to time; and as His Majesty finds himself in his own palace, he is continually occupied with all praiseworthy studies. Last Monday the King intended to go fowling, but was prevented from doing so by the weather in the forest. Since the publication of the Decree by which paper securities, not verified within the set time, are to be destroyed, the flow of people to verification and testing has increased, for which 230 commissions are employed, and it is reckoned that they make little progress, although they expedited 140 documents on Monday. There was debate as to whether the Company would retain its privileges or lose them; however, the final decision could not yet be determined; but Mr. Landiviàu said to the Directors working with him last Sunday that there seemed little prospect of the negative outcome. For account of the said Company, some ships laden with goods have arrived at Saint-Malo; on Sunday the two street robbers were captured, who by displaying notices had robbed people of their effects. One hears here talk of thefts and highway robberies, and since such clever schemes are discovered daily, they give the victims themselves cause to laugh. One sees here a book in quarto much sought after, bearing the title "Fraudeurs Bill-wygraard in the Printing House, Bill-board of the Church regarding the Church and the Constitution and Settlement," and many find it singularly well composed; meanwhile, it is said that Mr. de Baudry continues with the induction or appellants, whom one wishes to reduce in number before long. The Abbé Massay, Agent of the Pope, was on the 23rd by Cardinal de Bissy consecrated in the principal church at Meaux as Archbishop of Athens, and as the said ceremony was completed on the following day, the Minister for the first time took on the 25th the character of Nuncio in an audience with the King; and those who know him well say that, though he is very polite, he may go further in time. Since the Turkish Ambassador had audience with the King, his son has amused himself by visiting the Fair in the Faubourg Saint-Germain; and scarcely had such festivities not ended, when he and his court took in hand a tobacco pipe that was a good ell long, tasted by 400 Swiss or mercenaries. Previously the said Ambassador went with a great gift to the Archbishop of Cambrai for audience; it is said that his Excellency will this afternoon take a meal with the Count of Toulouse, and will do the same subsequently with the Marshals Villeroy, Villars and other gentlemen. Yesterday his Excellency was fetched in three carriages with the Archbishop and taken to the Opera, where all the places were rented out, although one paid a high price for the first box; and upon leaving the Comedy last evening, it is said that the said Ambassador will take supper with the Duke Regent, which was followed by a ball. It is afterwards said that Mr. Charramount, who was formerly in embassy at the Porte, and also the Knight Sutton, previously would have been charged with the audience of that day, the supper entertainment. On the day [of] the Ambassador's [visit], elegant furnishings were displayed in the Royal Palace; among these the ladies prepared at table various dishes, Ragouts, Pilaus, Coffee, Chocolate, Tea and Liqueurs, which table was surrounded by ladies of song who served the same, or rather gave a charming illusion through this delightful entertainment, which was arranged for the amusement of the distinguished persons present. The Ambassador is to go to the French Comedy this afternoon and tomorrow to the Italian Comedy; and tomorrow also a printed account of his entry and audience is to be published. They are busy furnishing the rooms in the Bank to hold there next Sunday a general assembly of the Directors and principal shareholders, in which the Duke of Bourbon will preside. The shares of the second seal are at 78, and those of the third proportionally. Works are being carried out to furnish the cathedral, open the gardens, and construct the buildings to make the waters play, and the same is being done at Marly, Meuty and Trianon. [Dispatches] from Catalonia and Navarre show that the descent was vigorous under our troops.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 31 March. Letters of the 25th instant from Lisbon report that there a great assembly for commercial purposes was held, and in the same it was established the opening of a Commerce Company, which would consist in the slave trade to Angola, Macao, etc.; however, it is feared that the King granted great privileges to the said Company; but one wishes to know the further circumstances; since the registers for subscriptions would first be opened on the 10th or 15th of this month; but it is said that this would be reserved only to Portuguese inhabitants, who, however, could subsequently sell their shares, without stating in what each party would consist. From Cadiz one has that the navigators from the Buenos Aires ships have all now come ashore; and that one reports there that the two said ships contained more wealth than one had previously thought. At Malaga has arrived from London the ship the William Robbert Hamilton, whose ship began that he has in the name of an Algerine. The ship the Adriana Gerr Blom has departed from Rochelle towards the Maas, and from Bordeaux the 2 messengers Cornelien and Ven theirwards and the St. Joh. Roelof... towards the East Sea. To Ter Veer and Middelburg have arrived Bartel Taarling and Audi. Ketelaar from the Caravanserai Coast, as well as Corn. Bank of Quincy and a blockader from Saint Eustatius. In the Vlies has entered brown Jan Koekemoer from Bayonne.

THE HAGUE, 1 April. The Baron Godema has arrived here. Mr. Vauden Waerelt has departed as Provincial of Friesland. According to letters from Kampen, the third States of the Province of Overijssel were assembled there.

The Directors and principal shareholders of the Company Commerce and Assurance in Gouda hereby advertise to all the gentlemen Burgomasters and governors of the aforesaid [territories], who have approved the aforesaid Company, to make known that, in so far as 2000 Guilders per share were to be paid on calling up, henceforth only 1 per cent per share is to be paid, following the previous payment of 2 per cent per share, proportionally. The transfers of shares shall therefore be discontinued from this 4th April of this year 1721 until the 31st of the said month of April.

Jacob Magnus Broker shall sell in Amsterdam on Thursday, 3 April 1721, Wednesday the 3 April 1721, in the evening at 9 o'clock, at the house of George Meyer, upholsterer in the Old Whole Lodging, a good lot of English Sorties, new-fashioned Diogenes, Sarge for Ladies, Coronalifes, Imperiales, and diverse Norwich fabrics, consisting of striped and plain Calamantkins, Damacels, half-2nd Kinipaten, Satinettes, etc. and other colors. From the Lodging in the morning at 8 to the afternoon at 4 o'clock to be seen.

The Brokers P. Outelboek, Joannes and Dirk Bontekoning, wish to sell in Amsterdam on Monday 7 April 1721, at the Swan on the Nieuwmarkt, 30,000 Copperwood, Swinebone, Crustanfe and other timber of various kinds, from the Flatkerie and Madonia Batchers, short and long, each kind, 1000 Spars and Justers, diverse lengths and kinds, 4000 beams from Langecour, Madouw, Flatketten, Duskat, Holms-Holt, Copperwork [?] of [?] 20, 24 feet, 100 Hamburg beams, 300 Copperwood, 300 Copperworks and other 12 parcels, 400 Speckled, 10 parcels, and other timber more.

The gentlemen Jan van Baalen and Jan Wittig Brokers shall sell in Amsterdam on Monday 7 April 1721, in the afternoon at 1 o'clock, in the Free Yard, a good lot at the Lodging on the Haarlemmerdam, a lot of fine and other wools from Moetentse to Leyden. Manuel Koerloburg and P. Okkelberg Brokers shall sell in Amsterdam on the Nieuwendijk in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, 175 good Berlin beams, long 40 to 65 feet. Amsterdam shall sell on Monday 9 April 1721, at the White Swan on the [?], precisely 4 drums, 100 common Crook and Knees, etc.; lying outside the Sawmills, [?] 17 feet, in France cut planks, the thickness of 2 and the gates of the sawmills at Raamjuuri, at the H. Nicholaes the scraps...

Filip Fiek Broker shall sell in Amsterdam on Tuesday the 1st [recte] 16 April 1721, (in place of on Wednesday the 16 April) in the morning at [?] and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock precisely, at the Old Whole Lodging, a fine lot of paintings and excellent pictures by the foremost masters, Flemish, Haeyael, Elginni, Wouweinan, Rubens, van Wyk, van Lint, van Wyk, van Kint, Verburg, Ruisdael, Paolo Bril, Goltzius, Hugo [?], de Heem, Guilfrt, Teniers, and a great many other foremost masters; curious seals, musical instruments and other [items] may be obtained from the said broker.

[Items] all days prior to be seen; and the catalogues at the offices...

In Amsterdam and here on Tuesday and Friday, being 17 and 18 April next, [on] 16 April next, the booksellers sell the deceased scholarly library of the late and the Mr. and Mr. Adriaem Buys, in his leather books; consisting of excellent theological, miscellaneous, juridical and [?] books, among which are distinguished the Tutustus Romanorum and Graearum by Gro[tius?] [?], by the said widow by Mekin at the Exchange, by the H. and Wolte shall be in the house of the learned [?], by the [?], Leyden J. du Vivie and H. Thering, Printing Vis, and in [?] of the booksellers Hilorg Klokman, where the catalogue, as well as in the [?]; Rotterdam J. Hof, The Hague J. de bond, Haarlem W. van Kellen, Leyden J. [?] with the foremost booksellers.

Printed in Leyden by FELIX DE KLOP over the Town Hall, 4 April 1721.

Course of Exchange

The exchange rates of the DAY are as follows: Madrid 98. Cadiz 113. Seville 112. Bilbao 90 and 1 quarter. Lisbon and 1 quarter. Venice 1 and 1 quarter. Livorno and 1 quarter. Genoa 94 and 1 quarter. Paris sight 41 and 1 quarter. London 33 Shillings. Leipzig current 28 and 1 half St. Hamburg current 28 and 1 quarter Reichsthaler. Ditto current 28 and 1 quarter. Danzig 27 and 1 half Polish gr. Strasbourg and 1 quarter. Hamburg 21 and 1 half st. Antwerp 3 and 1 quarter per C. Ghent 1 and 1 eighth [or] 1 quarter. Brussels 2 and 1 quarter. Zeeland 1. Rotterdam 1 quarter. The dividends of the Royal Bank 3 eighths [to] 1 setting. The shares of the East India Company are 311 to 313. West India Company 109. Subscription 800.

Notices & Advertisements

It is made known to everyone that henceforth from Utrecht a post-wagon shall run every morning at 6 o'clock via Gorinchem and Cappel to Breda, to arrive that evening at Breda, from where each day thereafter an equal post-wagon departs for Antwerp and returns the following day, to come as quickly as possible to all the quarters of Brabant and Zeeland; whereby one shall begin on Monday the 7th April and continue until Saturday the 8th October; said wagons shall depart from the aforesaid places in the evenings outside the Toll Gate, before the Inn the Swan.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 2nd of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 41.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 4th of April, 1721
West Indies

CURAÇAO, 2 January. News has been received here from the West Indies that in December of the past year two Zeeland vessels were at the blessed coast, and furthermore in the West Indies there are still 3 Dutch ships. It is also reported that the ship Sara Galy, Jan Valk, was attacked near Toulon by 2 privateers, one of 28 guns and 180 men, a brigantine with 150 men and one with 110 men. After nearly a two-hour engagement in which the Captain suffered only 3 dead, they repelled [the privateers] with great loss. Afterwards, the same [privateers], together with the ship de Hoop, Captain Jac. Everste, having arrived at the Island, drove off 2 piraguas there.

England

PETERSBURG, 10 March. Yesterday Baron Waldecker ordered the ice in the river to be broken open, since the day after tomorrow two warships from the fleet will be launched into the water. In the meantime, daily consultations are being held with the Grand Admiral and other naval officers, regarding urgent matters. Various foreign ministers have, according to orders, congratulated His Majesty on the advantageous treaties concluded between himself and the Ottoman Porte. As soon as news is received here that the Duke of Holstein is on his way, it is assured that His Czarist Majesty will depart to Riga to receive and conduct the said Duke, whereupon it is assured that the mentioned marriage will be publicly declared. The French Minister, Monsieur Campredon, who confers daily with our ministry, has sent an express with important dispatches to his court. The said minister has entertained various foreign ministers and nobles of this court at His Majesty's [residence]. One still cannot say with certainty when the negotiations at Neustadt between the ministries of this court and the Crown of Sweden will begin.

Italy

ROME, 15 March. It is said that the severe arrest of the Princes Piombino and Ruspoli, together with all their blood relations, on penalty of 5000 scudi, arose principally because the President recommended certain matters to His Majesty, and even excused himself to Princess Ruspoli for the occurrence. As this princess felt herself most offended by it, Princess Piombino, not having wished to heed the President's counsel regarding the sight-line, the Holy Father became so disturbed at her stubbornness that by the said arrest he wished to banish her as well as all who maintained her from this place. The Duke of Adriania, who was in the carriage of Princess Ruspoli at the time the occurrence happened, is likewise under arrest. However, the daughters of Princess Piombino have been exempted from it. Cardinal Althan has written about this matter to Vienna, and Cardinal Aquaviva from the court of Madrid has written [similarly], and the latter Cardinal shows himself very dissatisfied with the ministry, as he believes that the Pope should have removed the Captain of the Guard, His Grace Count Capizzucchi, brother-in-law of Princess Ruspoli, by reason of the little patience that Count showed toward His Eminence Aquaviva's nephew. The said Cardinals Althan and Aquaviva have each in the name of their respective courts proposed a successor to the canonry of the church of San Michele Maggiore, vacant through the death of Signor Bonaventura. But since the incomes of this canonry come from Sicily, it is believed that the Emperor's arms will prevail over the rights of the said church, unless the King of Spain grants the church an income in Spain equal to that which comes from Sicily. On Monday Cardinal Aquaviva had an audience with the Pope, and His Holiness there requested that the King of Spain might be pleased to be freed from the clergy of his monarchy requiring a new contribution to finance the war in Africa. Signor Falconiert, Governor of this city, grows better day by day, and his complete recovery is expected shortly.

VENICE, 21 March. This afternoon the Government received an express from Rome, sent by the Ambassador of this Republic with news that the Pope [?] last Wednesday the 19th inst.; heard early in the morning through 200 cardinals, that His Holiness thereafter lost his voice, whereupon the doctors abandoned him. It is said that His Holiness, seeing the end approaching, received last sacraments. Just at the arrival of this express, two other expresses have arrived here with confirmation, one of which was sent by Cardinal Althan to the Emperor and the other by [?] to the King at Vienna.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 19 March. Yesterday the Prussian minister had a private audience with the King and Queen. It is assured that His Imperial Majesty has received a letter from the Count of Finck, and that His Royal Highness wishes to pay a visit to the court of the King of Denmark, and there to lay down certain commissions from his principal's court. Our plenipotentiaries are both here, awaiting an express from the plenipotentiary minister Herr Campiglou from Petersburg, to depart thence to Neustadt.

---

Germany & Adjacent Kingdoms

VIENNA, 22 March. Work is underway here to arrange the necessary matters to put the new archbishopric in order. It is also feared that daily differences arise among the imperial ministries, which could cause some change among them. The work of the bank is making good progress, since on Thursdays many good bills have been paid. War matters continue to be brought up daily, and according to the resolutions thereupon, the regiments of foot have been made ready. But efforts are also being made to equip the cavalry regiments and put them in order. Some days ago an imperial edict was issued, whereby officers are forbidden to enlist any people who have previously served His Majesty. This command has also been sent to the Viceroy of Sicily, so that the natives of that kingdom who served the King of Spain under the Marquis de Lude in the last war shall not be employed in the Emperor's service. A certain Jew from Frankfurt, who had petitioned to be baptized here and requested the priest Eugenius as his godfather, a request to which His Highness had granted permission, was scheduled for this morning. However, the said Jew was seized on Friday by a severe fever, and therefore at his request, being of sound mind, was immediately taken to his bed. Afterwards he was brought to the church and last rites were administered to him. However, he died that same evening. In his will he left to all the Christian poor Jews residing here a legacy of 1000 florins. Not long before his baptism, a Jew [?] who had committed a theft was saved from the gallows by paying compensation for the theft.

DRESDEN, 27 March. Although the departure of the King of Poland with our Elector to his kingdom was fixed on a certain date, it is understood that it has been postponed until next Tuesday. It is believed this occurred because His Majesty yesterday wished to celebrate the feast day of Josephus in the Roman Catholic chapel morning and afternoon, as well as the birthday of the Elector Princess here, which was celebrated most splendidly. The Royal baggage and court retinue have already been sent ahead to Warsaw. His Majesty held a cabinet council, in which the King introduced his son, the Electoral Prince, as President of the Privy Council with the customary ceremonies. It is also assured that a seat in the Privy Council and chancellery, vacant through the death of the Count of Wertheim, was given to the Vice-Chancellor Herr Bunau, though who would obtain his late position was still entirely uncertain. The Polish General Flemming is likewise still here as crown marshal. However, the minister has returned to Poland via Breslau.

REGENSBURG, 27 March. Last Monday the proceedings at the Imperial diet were concluded. Because the Crown ministers said they had no authority to declare themselves upon the proposals laid before them on the 11th of this month by the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz in the Emperor's name, which proposals concern that the Roman Catholics might consent to moderation regarding the 4th article in the Treaty of Ryswick, and that the religious council and the religious differences with the Protestants be settled amicably. His Imperial Majesty deemed this could be done with respect to the three mentioned points without contravening the imperial laws. His Majesty also granted the commons the authority to examine whether the alleged contraventions, of which the Protestants complain, have occurred. At the same time the Protestants were given to understand that His Imperial Majesty could not approve that they themselves, insofar as in this as in other occasions, would rely upon their veto power to oppose the effect of his commission decrees, but that they should, like the crowned heads, vote head by head to establish the decision thus by majority vote. And since the ministers of the joint colleges would have to take their measures in order to handle this in an orderly manner, it is foreseen that little progress will be made there before Easter, but during this time only other particular matters can be deliberated upon, and especially those concerning the Chamber at Wetzlar. Meanwhile various imperial cities continue to petition for investigations into their imperial privileges. Along with letters that have come from the Palatinate, other reports have been received from Zweibrücken, which state that the Duke steadfastly abided by his decision to have all the grievances that could readily be settled examined to see if everything was done correctly according to the Emperor's intention. Those same letters also say that some ministers, who were principally the cause that the Lutherans were prejudiced, are beginning to show other signs. Herr von Beck still wants to see what he will do or whether he will return from there to the Palatinate court. Meanwhile the Roman ministers held conferences to declare themselves upon the Emperor's proposals, which one will not be able to learn about before [?]. The ministers of Hanover and Hesse made a trip to Augsburg but expect to return within 3 to 6 days. The supposed baron who is imprisoned here and is also found to be a student of lowly birth has been examined over several days by the magistracy of the city on various charges brought against him and found guilty of many crimes, whereupon his trial will be drawn up, which may result in loss of life. Now newly printed and published here is a copy of the imperial rescript to the imperial commission at Regensburg, dated at Vienna on 11 February 1721, though the same is without general permission, and consisting of eight pages, is too large to be found at this place.

COLOGNE, [date uncertain]. Yesterday morning at six o'clock [text breaks off]

Papal Affairs

The Apostolic Nuntius, the Count of Archinto, and the Express [messenger] from Rome— or rather less frequently than one might expect, since it is well known that the Pope on 19 March [issued] IV. Of the General Council, first concerning this news to the Papal... the Company, then the second-mentioned Council with this news to the Papal Internuncius, which was communicated by Mr. Sautini at Brussels...

France

PARIS, 28 March. The Gentlemen of the Council have refused to accept the office of Viceroy, and they were offered [seats] at the Table of the Tuileries, each with a great Seal. The Ambassadors proposed this, which remained with the other Troops and it was followed; they accepted nothing but the Honors alone, one hundred [?] [?]; this refusal gives cause for people to say that the King or rather very much values the heart, like those whom it is proper for others to serve as an example. It is assured that the Court has commanded by Terms that the Commissioners for the business shall grant no grace regarding the matter of foodstuffs; it is also wished that the gentlemen have no Letters of 1600 Livres and afterwards, so no account is passed; and it is said that the Holders do not regard themselves bound by the three last-served roads that were laid out for them by the King, whether the account or affairs are settled. The Duke of Emées has a Process against his own... Gremelli sought, because he sought Livres from the Marriage of their House-portion at Aix-gift at the Town Hall, holding, in place of the Debts of the failed Term of House of Studies therewith. [This was] according to the Marriage Contract that had been stipulated in writing. The second Process is against the Intendant Bignon touching payments of the House. The Knight Process in the Parliament at Rouen, who [is] 23 years old, has had 40,000 Livres income from his Lands. The Estates and Companies of Languedoc have drawn a Deliberation against a certain [matter] of six Months [duration], to obtain the availability of Money there, the wages whereon [depend on] the Don Gratuit for the King. The Bishop of Bordeaux [?], who [is] near the Sea, has ordered the Mendicant Brothers and Capuchins at Calais to be expelled, because they disturbed the Constitution. All the Chambers of the Parliament on the 21st have, by Arrêt, suppressed all the Listrenes.

Memorials concerning Appeals to a future Council, with VIII. Which in a Brief of the Procurer General of the King concerning information [containing] deliberation upon the same against those Printers and Distributors: the Parliament has also [made] representations to the Regent on these matters, to the Court, on account of rights heretofore granted... [?]ation of the Interrogations of the Ambassador IX. Who [appealed] to the Appeal of the 4 Bishops, that Body was received favorably in this Negotiation and heard with great End, and the said Lieutenant received thereupon the Faculty to bring his rectifications of the Appellants to the Registry. [?]

The Comedians, who have made themselves very bold through the presence of the Turkish Ambassador, are quite dismayed that Mademoiselle Desmarés will have left the City of Gold [and] allowed them to sell [?] to bring their goods to Java quietly. The Dominican Father Jerome, who was a great Preacher, has deceased.

The Netherlands

GOUDA, 8 April. The Directors and Head Participants of the Company of Commerce and Annuity within Gouda shall, by the Guarantee of the same City of Gouda, conduct a Negotiation, whereby the Interested Parties shall be able to draw and enjoy Annual Annuities of six to one hundred, to one hundred and one hundred, upon the following Conditions.

I. Any one who is competent to bind himself may take part in this Negotiation by subscription.

II. There shall be five Classes of Subscription, namely: a Class of two hundred fifty Guilders subscription per Person, a Class of five hundred Guilders subscription per Person, a Class of one thousand Guilders subscription per Person, a Class of two thousand Guilders subscription per Person; and in all Classes shall be admitted all Persons without distinction of age, provided that in each Class per Person only one subscription may be made.

III. From the subscribed and paid Capital Sums, the Company shall make no Interest, but the Company shall instead provide to the Persons thereunto existing the annual Annuities following, namely: six per Cent in the first year after the subscription made.

Seven per Cent, when of the one hundred Persons in each Class seventy-five Persons shall have died.

Eight per Cent, when of the remaining seventy-five Persons twenty-five more Persons shall have died.

Ten per Cent, when the remaining fifty Persons shall have died.

Fifteen per Cent, when of the remaining thirty Persons ten more Persons shall have died.

Twenty per Cent when of the remaining twenty Persons ten more Persons shall have died.

Twenty-five per Cent, when of the remaining ten Persons three more Persons shall have died.

Fifty per Cent, when of the remaining seven Persons three more Persons shall have died.

One hundred per Cent, when of the remaining four Persons two more Persons shall have died, and that so long as the two remaining Persons shall live, and all this according to the advice of more [?]

IV. From the Class of Persons, after subscription has been made with the Company, shall be made and furnished Letters of Verification and Magistracy, whereby the Subscribers shall be found proper Copies for their verification.

V. The Payment of each Subscribed Sum shall have to be made in Silver Money, Guilders and [?] twenty-fourth parts thereof being in gross Silver Money, Guilders and [?] twenty-fourth parts thereof being in gross Silver Money, as far as concerns the Class of two hundred fifty Guilders and of five hundred Guilders, by the Intermediation of the subscription or at the latest fourteen days after the subscription made, and the following Terms of fifty per Cent.

Commissioners Dirck Nikkels of Engel and Jan Wittig Brokers, shall at Amsterdam and Marien Koelenburg and Pieter Onkelboer Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam in the White Hall on the Nieuwerdyck, 175 Pieces of Pine Beams, long 40 to 65 feet per piece, the Eenhoorn and Samson.

THE HAGUE, by the Bookseller Daniel d'Azerdo, David Pimentel and Raphaël d'Azerdo. Wednesday the 16 April 1721, in the evening at [?] hours, in the Dykstraat in the Old Castle, Pieter Onkelbueer, Johannes and Dirk Bontekoning Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam a Fine Lordship Lodging, an extra fine lot of 200 Rix-dollars, [?] and Nerva [?]

On Monday the 31 [?] April 1721, there shall be at Amsterdam in the Old Lordship Lodging, in the evening [a sale of] fine Houses, 5 [?], Courses and Wagon-houses, Houseman's Dwelling, near Bidde the Vegt, of the late Herr Jacob van Nigtevegt, measuring 31 morgen [approximately 62 acres], and there a posted Bill can be seen, and from Broker Lucas Antonio de Schepper [can be] learned. Antonio Brafanus, Cornelis de Koos and Johannes van Uppen at Amsterdam [?] all posted Bills can be seen, and from Broker Lucas Antonio de Schepper [can be] learned.

Amsterdam

Shipping: Today the ships the 3 Brothers Dirk Harmense Veth and the Isabella under Hendrik Kock were [recorded]; and among the ships that in the year of March of this year hereabouts were found to be spoiled, there were Corn. and Jan Caritiens, H. Schallie and H. Blonk. At Gibraltar a Ship with Sugar and Cacao from Nantes has arrived. From [?] it is reported that Capt. Offem the fourth [?] had purchased and his ship [was] to be put [?]; in the Harbor lie the 2 Spanish Ships, the Maria Catarina Capt. Franc. Pannotige and the St. Joser Francisco de Revilia, both heading hither. At Havre de Grace Conn. W. Joannis toward Norway, and Simon Messert hither. At Bayonne has arrived the St. Anna Cornelis Valk from the Meuse; at Bordeaux the Sara and Maia Capt. Bernarie Bosbius, at Havre from Cadiz. Lunelen from the Baltic, and Gerrit Blom Etc. from here; at Nantes (from where the St. Jost Capt. Filipo sailed toward Martinique) Leendert Vos from the Meuse, and Volkers Nannings from here; at Rochelle about 12 Ships, and among them Leendert Pieterls, Marten Buyk, Jan Adams, Pieter Jacobde Etc. from here, Zeeland and the Meuse; at St. Martin the 2 Friends Pieter Roelofs and Jentnie Adriainste; on the Elf about 30 Smacks from here and Friesland; and in the Zuidzee a small Ship, [?] of Lierick, with report that there had arrived Klaas Dorninegh from Hamburg, and loaded English from London; at which time the Fleet from Lisbon to Rio de Janeiro was not yet open. The missing Ship of L. Eylander, from here to Stockholm, lies [?]

Madrid 90; Cadiz 113 and one quarter; Seville 112; Bilbao 90; Lisbon 4; Venice 84 and one quarter; Genoa 94 and one quarter; Paris 30 [?] half; London 33 Sch. 9 and one half; Leipzig current 36 and one half Sturry; Danzig 190 Polish; Breslau 36 and one quarter St.; Hamburg 32 and one half St.; Antwerp 2 and one quarter to 2 per C.; Ghent 2; Brussels and one half; Zeeland 1 3; Rotterdam 1 quarter. The Agio of the Bank 3 and one eighth.

THE HAGUE, 3 April. Yesterday was the Key-Palaces, where a Russian Expedition returned again toward Persia.

[Various property and goods sales notices for Amsterdam and other cities follow]

**To be sold at Amsterdam on Monday, 7 April 1721, in the afternoon, a lot of Fine and other sorts of Moscow Hemp;

Wednesday, 9 April 1721, in the afternoon at 2 o'clock on the Old Castle, a lot of over 200 Bales of Hair Snuff-Tobacco.**

**At Amsterdam to be sold on Friday, 18 April 1721, in the New Court and 1000 [?] and [?]; lying at the Lands Warships, at the Lodging, in the evening at 6 o'clock, [a property] to be sold, the conveyance for 17 Bushels Nk., named Welgelegen, with all [further details] according to the regulations tomorrow under Account, &c.; as all [further details] at Amsterdam to be presented for sale or rent, Bills at the House, [which has] a fine making on the Trading [?] and other Arts, Kitchen, Cellars, and for 5 Horses, a charming small House, Well, Fountain &c.; opening onto the Achtergalt with a Garden, at [?] breadth

---

At Leiden, printed by FELIX DE KLOPPE over the Town Hall. 4 April 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES [as of this date]:**

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 4th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 42.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of April, 1721

MADRID, 11th March. News has been received here from Madrid that various meetings have been held at Court, and it is said that in one of these, in which the Presidents of Finance and War were present, long debates have taken place establishing that all the silver, both from the Vera Cruz and Buenos Aires ships, together with the little brought by the registry ship from Cuba, in the warehouses still in whole and half reals and half shillings, shall be melted down at the same time, which will require considerable time; so that our [affected parties], after having demonstrated how disadvantageous this decision would be to commerce in this Kingdom and the West Indies, and to the division of the King, now expect it to return here shortly; this resolution will probably hinder further negotiation, and the departure of the galleons, for which more men are still needed, is postponed at least until May. The registered silver in the Buenos Aires ships would amount to nearly one million and 800,000 pesos; and the unregistered, which has already been brought ashore, amounts to just over 2 million; yet it is said that the case must not be altered, nor an express sent to the King. Through the storm of last week, the Dutch ships of Auke Volkers, Gerrit Colomryn, etc. have not yet departed from here for Amsterdam.

PARIS, 31st March. Last Friday, the Archbishop of Cambrai received an express from Rome with news that the Pope died on the 5th of this month, whereupon, after holding a great council, the cardinals were informed; shortly after receiving the same news, the Nuncio also received a courier: and it is said that the Pope's nephew, Cardinal Albani, who is clothed with the dignity of Camerlengo and is much beloved by Roman nobility, will exercise all authority during the Interregnum. One of the cardinals has conveyed the news to the King's officials, the Duke Regent, and other magnates alike, urging them to proceed to Rome with all speed; and the Cardinal de Polignac took his leave of His Majesty in like manner; the Cardinal de Bissy will also follow as soon as possible; however, Cardinal de Noailles will not go there either on account of his advanced age or for other reasons. By this death, the Anticonstitutionalists began to take fresh courage; some are already saying, in a manner of speaking, that the Conclave will more resemble the body of a deceased Pope than anything else. It is said that His Holiness, being troubled by his old complaint in the intestines, found himself somewhat better for a time. Because within a few days the symptoms returned; he was attacked by fever two days before his death, became delirious the following day, and died on the 19th about midday.

On the 17th, a great audience was held by the Pope, and His Holiness thanked the envoys of foreign powers among other things for papers recently presented to him. It was assured that the Conclave for the election of a new Pope would begin on the 30th of this month. Wednesday saw a great crowd of people in Parliament over the judgment of a certain case in favour of the Duke of Bourbon, which regarding the alliance in the beginning of the affairs of the Duke of la Force would be pronounced. The following day several Paris gentlemen were present there on account of the Abbot Taulion, who sought a certain arrangement.

His Excellency was not prevented, in the middle of the service at his usual place of prayer before sunset, from retiring with his son to a separate chamber, where he performed his devotions for about a quarter of an hour, after which he returned to the service, which had meanwhile continued, to watch it finish; he had been given a free passage to come and go as it pleased him. The clothes which the King wore at the audience were displayed on the throne for three days to show the extraordinary richness thereof, together with the costly jewels. Saturday, Monsieur Boivin the son was received as a member of the French Academy. Monsieur de Contade, Marshal of the Camp, who died on the 30th of this month, is mourned for his singular merits. It is said in this city that the Marquis de Maulevier, Envoy from this court to Madrid, has received orders to depart from there; if this news proves true, as many assure, farewell then to the Congress at Regmuelt. It is expected, although the present state of affairs seems to allow it, that there will again be a rupture; but on this side we are in a good posture to maintain it. It is also said that Spanish troops are moving toward the Cart of Nuitbilion. The King has issued a Declaration by which His Majesty declares that the annuities and pensions of the Duke Regent shall remain on the same grounds as they have been before; and that they are not included in the reduction by the various decrees issued a year ago against individuals in the Kingdom; and this in conformity with the great services which His Royal Highness has rendered to the King in the public affairs of the Kingdom.

In these days the following has occurred in the High Council; Madame de Tourville married to the Count de Tourville, eldest son of the Marshal of that name; having willingly separated from her husband after her marriage, was confined to childbed of a first son, who was christened as her son under the name of Adriel de Dorps: some years later Madame de Tourville returned to the house of her husband, and after some time was confined to childbed of a second son, who was claimed by the Count de Tourville as his eldest; Michel du Desert was brought up and educated with him, and also ate at the Count's table as Monsieur de Tourville. After Madame de Tourville's death, Michel du Desert was excluded from those tables, and regarded in the house only as a good and honest servant: the Count de Tourville came to die, and he who bore his name and was supposed to be his heir found himself in possession of the goods of the house which lie in Normandy; but this unknown brother, having received information of his birth, wished according to the laws to enjoy his birthright, and thus having caused his younger brother to be summoned before the Parliament of Rouen, a decree has been issued.

BRUSSELS, 3 April. Last Sunday the Internuncio of the Pope received express news that Pope Clement XI [Editor's note: Pope from 1700-1721] after two days of illness died on 19 March, at the age of approximately 94 years (having been born 22 July 1649); this interregnum has now brought considerable notice to the Marquis de Prié and the Cardinal Archbishop of Mechelen: with the same express it is reported that all the Ministers who were appointed at that Court, just as they have been dismissed, and similar dismissals shall be made. Under the Auditor General of the Knights of the Order of the Garter, with the presence of Buckingham and Monsieur Holland there, the General of the Post Office [?] shall be filled by the Sovereign in a Chapter. It is said that Captain Walpole is to become Director General of the Board of Pumps, in place of the deceased Mr. Cleggt; Mr. Clark has become Secretary of the Directors of that Office, in place of Mr. Marshall, who has been dismissed. Regarding the holdings in this Realm. It is reported that a Bank in Onlislow shall be closed. It is reported that the Three Credit [?] payment consists of 17,000 pounds sterling, 10,000 pounds in the Bank and 10,000 pounds sterling in the East India Company. Today the Municipalities shall; 3 houses with insurance of the trade of the East India Company will shortly sail to the East Sea: and the Admiral's flags are already hoisted on the masts. The shares of the Bank are at 1-20, East India Company 740 and one-half, South Sea Company 135, Millions Pitt 135, A Flit. C 36, ditto new 27, and York Building 19.

AMSTERDAM, 5 April. On 17 January the ship of Pieder Koelofle arrived here from Curaçao, and on the 15th of the previous [month] from Lisbon, Captain Klaas Duyt, Dirk Goudfchalk, Jacob van den Rade, and Jan Groot sailed on 15 February from Texel; likewise an Andi. Harmense from Danzig, Benjamin Hop from here, and Thys Bioma from the Meuse [River], at St. Malo Captain Harmen Toddevin from Alicante heading homeward; at Nantes la Fidelité, Captain Bovet from Guinea; and in the Downs the English ship the Princess Anna with a cargo of some 22 [?] and in the Coffee [trade] in Mecca, whose [?] the Rovers had taken and also in the Downs within the Hanover [Company waters] from Bombay, and yet certain letters report that the Zeeland ship Sandenberg, Captain Koda in the Spanish sea was still dispatched, and most of the crew along with some cargo were lost. Yesterday news was received from Zeeland that Captain Pieter Koelofs on 17 January had arrived here from Curaçao. From the Vlie this week departed for Sea and Guadeloupe. And news from St. Hubes that the Dutch Fleet there is before the coast.

THE HAGUE, 6 April. The Lord Fiscal Nenny has departed for Brussels. The expedition from London to the North has been seized. Tomorrow a beginning shall be made with the drawing of the sixth Class in the Dutch Lottery.

VIENNA, 24 March. Today is celebrated the Birthday of the Archduchess Maria Magdalena, in her 33rd Year, being celebrated splendidly from the Court. This morning early couriers arrived here from Rome, those of Cardinal Albani to his Nephew here, with news that His Holiness arrived on the 10th of this [month]; and [?] by the side through the Councils; [?] dispatched by the Councils of Althan, brought news that the Holy Father on the 19th in the evening at 7 o'clock had departed, in the 711th Year of his Age and in the 31st Year of his Reign; this sudden death brought consternation to this Court. Last Monday the Fourth Afternoon was received, having left his Widow and three Daughters with more than a Million.

REGENSBURG, 31 March. According to letters from Tvebruggert of the 21st, the Lord von Reck, very satisfied with his negotiations, departed from there to Mannheim.

HARWICH, 1 April. Two prominent Jewish Bankers G... and B... having departed Sunday night from here, subordinate officers were sent to fetch their baggage; it is assured that they have been arrested, and that they have sought satisfaction from the Government over these proceedings, as they would never have intended to depart secretly; but their intention was now to do something; however, wagons have been placed in their houses, and the coffers, chests, &c. have been searched, but it is assured that nothing of consequence was found there. P.S. It is said that a considerable sum of money, which had been hidden in the earth, has been discovered, and that they themselves shall be brought back here this evening or tomorrow.

HANOVER, 1 April. Two prominent Jewish Bankers G... and B... having departed Sunday night from here, officers have been sent after them to apprehend them; and it is assured that they have been arrested, and that they have sought satisfaction from the Government over these proceedings, as they would never have intended to depart secretly, but their intention was to do something: however, wagons have been placed in their houses, and the chests, crates, &c. have been searched, but it is assured that nothing of consequence was found there. P.S. A considerable sum of money, which had been hidden in the earth, is said to have been discovered, and that they themselves shall be brought back here this evening or tomorrow.

---

Regarding the meeting of the year 1700 of the Ordinary Assembly of the Insurance Guardians, Assembled in the Magistrate Leuven changed: where the election of a new Pensioner shall now follow. Monday Captain Slater was brought before the Auditor General, to answer to the questions regarding the difference that he had had with his Commander the Lieutenant Colonel Ofarell, and was [?] brought back to his detention: it is said that no punishment shall be held on account of this. The detention of the Knight Knight, who in the Castle of Antwerp is still narrowly guarded, seems to change into a serious matter of State, because the Authorities [?] Province of Brabant have risen up against its delivery into the hands of the Great Britain Resident here, proceeding with the assertion that such driving conflicts with the Name Privileges of Great Britain &c.

It shall be sold at Katersburg on 2 May 1721 O.S. to the highest bidder: goods to be delivered monthly in 50 ton lots, to be paid with Royal security, and those who pay promptly shall receive one percent, which Colleges of His Czarist Majesty, where one can address oneself. Offering for sale 3 64 tons of Wood-Ash, to be paid in Archangel in the nearest [?] with Reichsthalers within 3 Months, provided for honest prompt payment, which shall be done at St. Petersburg in [?] address.

Marten Kocisenberg and Pieter Onkelboer, Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam in the White Swan on the Nieuwendyck, 175 excellent Barley Beams, measuring 40 to 65 feet and one-half to 2 [?]. 1700 common Crooks and Knees &c. 3 Lying outside the [?] the Unicorn and Simfoon. [Amsterdam shall sell on Wednesday 9 April 1721, in the afternoon at 3 o'clock Beams, measuring 40 to 63 feet, 150 Upperland excellent trimmed Planks, thick [?] and outside the Sawmills and Window-frames, at the Mills the Sheep.

Sigt Friits [?], Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Tuesday 13 April 1721, at 9 and in the afternoon [?] precisely, in the Old Lord's Lodging, a fine party of Paintings, namely by Brueghel, Felgiun, Wouwerman, Rubens, van Wyk, van Linf, van Daem, [?], de Hy an, Giffuns, and a great number of other Masters in Rocker; and various Birds, [fine in colors, [?] and sound] being everything viewed daily by common arrangement to be obtained. On 15 April 1721, (in place of on Wednesday 16 April) in the morning at 8 o'clock, three charming and pleasant Paintings by the prominent van Wyk, van Lint, Veiburg, Ruisdael, Paolo Bril, [?], and other celebrated Masters; curious Mirrors, Music Instruments and [?]; being everything to be viewed the day before; and the Catalogue at the Lodging.

Nicholas Lemmers, Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Thursday 17 April 1721, a fine Collection of [?] Pieces in the Doelhall, a party of important Jewels Vessels and finely made Silverware, consisting of a Cooler, Lamps and Vessels, Coffee Pots, Tea [?] and Tea-Tables, Candlesticks and Basins, Arm-Brackets, Saucepans, Sheen Tea-Pots, Spoons, Forks, [?] &c. &c. 3 besides an extra fine Gold Clock. Sales in the morning at 9 and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock to be viewed. Thursday 17 April 1721, in the morning at 9 o'clock, in the Colvenera [?] Paulien; and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, a good party of new Hotel [?], Coffee Pots, Tea Kettles, [?], Hat Boxes, Mirrors and further [?], Saucepans, Serving Boards, Oil and Vinegar Sets, Tobacco Containers, fine Gold [?]; being everything in the aforementioned Doelhall to be viewed the day before.

At Amsterdam shall one on Thursday and Friday, being 17 and 18 April forthcoming the late Mr. and Mrs. Adriaan Buys, in their lifetime Advocate and Notary, possessed of rich Books, among which stand out the Thelautus Romanorum and [?] of Grey, the Distribution and legal sale shall be in the City Hall of the Deceased, by these the [?] of [?], [?], Rotterdam J. Hofhoui, The Hague J. de Hond, Haarlem W. van Keffel, Leyden J. du at the aforementioned Booksellers. And on 18 April forthcoming, shall be sold the bequeathed fine Library of [?], standing out in Theological, Miscellaneous, Legal and Historical works, Gracorum of Gravius and Gronovius, 25 Volumes, and other important Works, by their bequeathed Widow on the Rockin by the Exchange, through which it can be obtained in the [?], as also at Dordrecht by J. van Keifel, Leyden J. du Vrie and H. Theuring, Utrecht Vitt, and in other Cities.

Pieter Onkelboer, Joanne and Dirk Bontekoning, Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam [?] Lodging, an extra fine party of Deals, Muscovite and [?], on [?] sale party of fine black Molen-Rods, and 1000 Hay and Go. Ells at Amsterdam to be sold on Friday 18 April 1721, in the New [?] and [?], thick 14, 16, 18, 20, 24 and 25 palm [?], 200 [?], and 1000 Hay and [?]; lying behind the Country's East-Houses.

One shall at Utrecht on 21 April sell the outstanding and well-arranged in his lifetime Professor in Holy Theology there: the Catalogues are to be obtained at [?] and the Widow Bes, Leyden Logmans, The Hague de Hondt, Haarlem van Alica, in [?]. And the arranged Library of the Lord Melchior Lerdecker, [?], at Amsterdam by Boom and Wefthern, Delft by L. & Jurramanu, Rotterdam de Hondt, Haarlem van Keifel, Middelburg van Heeke, Leeuwarden [?], by which last-mentioned is printed, Lampe Hall. [?].

Antonio Badrevasfus, Cornelis de Roos and Johannes van Logea, Brokers, at Amsterdam [?] pleasant and modern Houses, called Country-Meening, provided with [?] fine [?] and of [?] breadth an [?] a fine Couple on top of the House, having [...] [...] being situated on the [?], between the Weesp Pier and the Ruysdael Island, [?] of 255 feet, [?] all else can be seen by the Interested parties. Previously at Amsterdam, [?] to sell or to rent, a [?] on the House, having an outbuilding, very convenient, where [?] on the house, having an outbuilding, very convenient, where [?] on the house, having an outbuilding, very convenient, where [?].

Course of Exchange

The EXCHANGE RATES today are as follows: Madrid 92. Cadiz 112 to 112 and one-quarter. Seville 94 and one-half. Bilbao 90 and one-half. Lisbon 44. Venice 84 and one-half. Leghorn 91 and one-quarter to one-half. Genoa 94 and one-quarter to one-half. Paris outgoing 41 and one-half. London 13 February 10. Geneva 94 and one-half. Leipzig current 38 and one-quarter St. Frankfurt current 38 and one-quarter [?]. Danzig 299 red [?]. Breslau 36 and one-quarter St. Hamburg 32 and one-half St. Antwerp 2 per cent. Breda to St. and one-quarter. Zeeland one-quarter. Rotterdam one-quarter. The Bank premium stands at 5 and one-quarter.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday 30 April, one shall in the Bookshop of Johannes vander Linden, [?] the first and second Books: consisting of outstanding Theological, Legal, Botanical, Latin, Antiquarian, Lexicographical & other Miscellaneous works. The Catalogue is to be obtained, [?] Keffel, The Hague de Hond and Kitto, Delft Beman, Rotterdam the Widow N. Ros and Beman, [?] and in the further in the supplying Cities by the principal Booksellers. [?] vander Linden, shall sell all of its well-arranged [?] Logical, Legal, Botanical, Medical, Philosophical, Historical, Greek Authors & to be obtained at Amsterdam by H. Strik and J. Boom, Haarlem [?] Booksellers.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 7th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 43.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 9th of April, 1721

Wednesday's Newspaper.

GERMANY AND NEIGHBOURING KINGDOMS.

VIENNA, 26 MARCH. The reports that come from time to time from Turkey concerning the Porte's preparations in the East are of importance; it is now said that in the Divan it has been resolved to take up war against the Emperor; and to push Fools into his territories; and that this was all done at the instigation of the Prussian [Editor's note: likely referring to King Frederick William I] in Poland, who far from having obtained the approval of the Great Lord for this, was found near the Frontiers to encourage the Discontented to take up Arms: it is hoped that shortly a certain [?] will be assured that the Emperor has not only resolved to complete the Regiments, but also to raise six new ones, namely 4 Infantry and 2 Cavalry.

IN ROME, 29 MARCH. On Tuesday the King departed for Warsaw, accompanied by the Counts of Waldorf, Manteufel and Witzthum, though General Flemming remained here. According to letters from Constantinople, Prince Rákóczi was there with credit, and has been given command over a portion of the Ottoman Army on the Hungarian Frontiers.

REGENSBURG, 29 MARCH. Although the Roman Ministers were appointed separately by the Emperor's Commission for the Diet to handle the matters of Religious Grievances, and each point was placed under the four decreed Clauses, it appears that they have not yet accomplished this, notwithstanding the various Conferences held thereon in the Monastery of the Minorites, at the instigation of the Ministerial Committee, which has been taken up with weight: nevertheless at the Diet held Friday by the said Ministers, no mention was made except that some of them would there have [?] over the common Fund [?] of the Princes to meet before the end of the Easter Recess; which result, as one is assured at the Emperor's Commissariat, has not been received well.

The Matter of the great German Order, concerning the complaints of the ecclesiastical authorities of the Marquisate of Cleves, and the Counties of the Mark and Ravensberg, to make common cause, and the same very extensive, yet well founded, and causes no wonder here, because daily pieces of this kind are issued. The Bavarian Commandant of the Vanguard here has herein made a good arrangement of Troops on his Court's orders, to prevent the Bavarian [?] that was to happen there, from being harmed. The levying of the Emperor in this City is still being continued: the Officials who must furnish 1500 Men from the Marquinate of Belgrade, have thrown [?] at Nuremberg, which from here were conveyed to Vienna by water. In the last held Diet Preliminary College, again placed in [?] by

SALZBURG, in that it was surprising that the Emperor's Commission-Decree of 12 April last had in the appended brief brought matters which it had already brought into proper Proposition on 14 February of this year, yet the time then to come the deliberation thereon being deferred, it was proposed to the unanimous Council, whether it would not soon be pleasing to take up the Votes thereon and to record them in the Protocol: whereupon the inquiry having been made, the first advise was by

SALZBURG, that one should humbly thank His Majesty for His Imperial Patrimonial care contained in the common Emperor's Commission-Decree, and whilst the wish and desire of his Principal is directed and practically experienced to maintain the necessary bond of unanimity between the Head and the Members, and that such should be appropriately maintained under this sweet [?], to provide each State in the slight possessions, and that none may follow from the Imperial Constitution any seat belonging to the Westphalian Peace, whereby it still conducts itself to the Imperial deputation of the Year 1704, and also in the forthcoming matters to a general bond; [?]: but concerning the Clause touching Religion, comprehended in the 4th Article of the Imperial Peace, one relied upon that which had already been proposed at various times by His Majesty; nothing more rough or more should happen in practice for that, which His Majesty could, to which one would from his Principal's side contribute everything that might be possible, with respect of each one's right.

Those of AUSTRIA said that it was known that His Majesty had devoted the time of his reign to his chief care the preservation of peace and unanimity between the Head and the Members, and that notwithstanding this, unlawfulness was caused by Religion under those pledges, as had appeared circumstantially in the Commission-Decree of 12 April of the previous Year; and that the matter comprehended in the Clause of the Imperial Peace was, from 12 April of the previous Year appeared circumstantially; and but that the matter comprehended in the Clause of the Imperial Peace was.

SALZBURG CONCLUDING wished to review this Protocol and observe what was necessary therein.

The Settlement of Religious Grievances was awaited within the time prescribed by the Emperor; and since these consist in deeds, it was awaited that they should form a Process or time issue, by way of Execution themselves might be carried out, either by the Circle-publishing Princes, or by the nearest adjacent Circle where the Building-offices have been carried out; or if the offended Party should request it, by appointed Commissioners, with the observation of co-empowerment, with respect to Religion, to examine the Facts thus and to decide without Appeal; and if the matter should be of too much weight, one could according to the Constitution address oneself to the Diet. Concerning the use of the Clause in the Imperial Peace, all the particular Points had no other meaning than that the Protestants, in all that by virtue of the Westphalian Peace came to them, should be left in equal Possession, as such by France understood not otherwise, but only for it is proper; that the Churches in Germany, at the cost of the Roman Catholic authority, at the time when France held those Provinces in possession, therein only the Roman Catholic Religion was fostered, as is actually been [?] or the Peace: besides which, France by the Utrecht Peace has renounced the Point in the Imperial Peace, whereupon the Roman Catholic relied, and against which the said Power has accorded everything according to the terms of the Westphalian Peace; from which it clearly appears that one had no right to bring the already decided Clause of the Imperial Peace [?] Treaty of Breda onto the Table; which was the cause that the Protestants protested very strongly against it, wherefore it was then asserted that the Baden Peace cannot harm the Protestants, and that one should accordingly only conform to the Westphalian Peace; but if from the Roman Catholic side any further and better means to settle all the differences can be shown, one is on the Protestant side willing and ready to negotiate thereover in the Corpus and to give justice in all its full place. The following Protestant Votes have partly conformed themselves with the above-mentioned and know Palts,

Luneburg and Wimar, Saxony-Eisenach, Saxony-Coburg, Saxony-Gotha, Saxony-Altenburg, Brandenburg, Culmbach, Brandenburg-Osterburg, Bruns- wick-Lüneburg, Brunswick-Calenberg, Brunswick-Grubenhausen, Hanover, Schaum- burg-Lippe, Mecklenburg-Gustrow, Osnabrück, Pomerania-Vorpommern, Pomerania- Hinterpommern, Wrangel, Hesse-Kassel, Hesse-Darmstadt, Saxony-Lauenburg, Minden, Waldeck, Moers, Diehl, Wittenberg, Svebia, Raichsberg, Montbéliard, Nassau-Diehn, Dienburg, Dietz, Waltstein, Wrangel, Hesse-Kassel, Hesse-Darmstadt, Saxony-Lauenburg, Minden, Waldeck, Moers, Diehl, Wittenberg.

HAMBURG, 4 APRIL. According to letters from Berlin, there on the first of this month a Royal Patent was published, by which all Deserters were given freedom to return; with promises and conditions to remain, and sharp order to the Officers, on pain of three Months' Pay, to let them pass unhindered. The King, who had departed to Potsdam, intended not to stay until Passau, His Majesty having had the Son of the Cap-Cleves baptized there; also the King had his Troops quartered there perform their Exercises: the same letters say that at Frustad on the Borders there had been a skirmish, and one of them remained on the Spot. From there it is also written that Mr. Daniel Schönemann, Preacher at Geltau, who lived a week and underwent Tests in Vacijen, in the presence of the Court, was heard, whereupon four learned men [?] the Elector had the de la Croix [Editor's note: likely a scholar or theologian] present matters that had been set for him, namely the first good intention of Julianida Apostles, and its last words spoken shortly before his death; the Tyranny of the Emperor Caligula, for the [?], Solmeist [?] through Salodinas; likewise whether Conservation is a new Creation, and such-like fine and curious Pieces moreover; which was carried out: His Fluent Majesty had placed a certain speedy Writer beside him, to write down what he thus spoke in Vacijen; but it was impossible for him to follow in his utterance: he had been asked to hear the Dedication in the Capital afterwards, and to hear it in Vacijen from the Queen afterwards, and it was done in this manner.

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 4 APRIL. The Duke of Rutland was introduced into the House of Peers, where having taken the Oath, he took his seat in the place of the deceased Duke his Father. The same day the Controller of the King's Household communicated, by order of His Majesty, to the House of Commons a letter whose copy was taken and following was retained, that he upon his return had had Audience with the Emperor, and having been received favorably, did not doubt of obtaining an Imperial command: that same evening Colonel Churchill was received from Vienna; having returned here, and has informed the King [?] of His Majesty, so if one will here in this place, that His Majesty is already prepared to deliver the mentioned Knight, provided the States of Brabant consent thereto, and that His Majesty hopes the mentioned States will do so: Tuesday the Earl of Burlington was married to the eldest Daughter of the Marquis Halifax. Saturday the Viscount of Lisburn died, and was succeeded in his Lordships and Dignities by his eldest son. According to letters from Jamaica of 31 October of the previous Year, there [?]

BRUSSELS, 7 April. The Head-Bailiff passed away some days ago, and his office entails many precedents. Due to the vacancy, the person of Mr. Santini as Internuncio to this Court has arrived Wednesday evening; and has had a formal audience with the Marquis de Drie, with appropriate ceremonies; however, he will not receive the full rank of Internuncio, nor will Mr. Santini proceed to his Nunciature in Cologne before the election of a new Prince. [?] with his Genealogy arrived here last week; and proceeded to Castle Bellenit, which belongs to the Prince de Ligne. It was said that the Count of Windisratz would add a portion of [?] to the Castle, which had not yet arrived.

A proclamation has been published against piracy, and particularly against Nic. Brown and Clifford Winter, with promises of a reward of £500 for whoever shall apprehend the first and likewise for the second. Gillerton is in a general meeting of the British Fleet determined to make a distribution of what shall be due for the half-year ending the 25th of this month. Sir George Saunders and Sir Britwell Fisher are appointed Commanders of the Fleets, in place of [?]. Gillerton was moved to introduce a Bill, and gave his Royal permission [?] the King in the capital of the [?] to incorporate into the Bank and East India Company, &c. in those respective matters, ordered also the use of Indian [?], deserters; and also in [?] Athens; after which the opening of the Members in the proceedings of the Lower House [?] and Mr. Charles Parker was appointed [?] of Heavy Goods [?]; and the distribution was deferred, but has not communicated his negotiations relating [?]: but it is expected to be made known to the following Monday: and as the House after the Report approved the Bill to prevent improper practices in the Asian trade, and ordered it to be put into effect, it has thus been deferred until now. In the House of Peers, Lord Cowper has proposed that, since the King at the opening of this Session said that the previous year had been favorable for commerce and the current one promises to be advantageous, M. should by Address request the Account of the Product of the Toll from the year 1714 to 1720, with the exception of the Products of each Year, to be presented. The Cargo here of the Ships Hanover from Bombay and Princess Anna from Mocha, for account of the East India Company, consisting of 300 pieces Bajutpauts, 2700 rupees Blue Byrampauta, 100 rupees Boral Chawdeas, 180 feet Challoes, 1200 rupees White Ponabguzzees, 1000 feet ditto Doorguzzees, 1000 feet ditto Cundy, 400 feet Guinsete Stokes, 4410 feet ditto Imaile, 1300 feet Lemunees, 1100 feet second Niceanmees, 1440 feet ditto Imaile, 300 feet second Tapuëls, 34700 pounds Carmenia Wool, 741000 pounds Coffee, 5600 pounds Cowries, 12000 Pepper, and other Goods for Batavia. East India Company 140 and one half, West India 150, Africa Company 34, likewise 27, Bourse Alfur. 7, ditto London 7. Mill. Bank 139.

PARIS, 4 April. The King, while enjoying the Glorious Palace and other diversions, had in recent days swallowed a small ball, which His Majesty retained for some hours without being able to expel it, despite the remedies applied; but having gradually expelled the same, the Marshal of Villeroy and the entire Court, including the Guard, have represented to His Majesty the danger in which he had been placed. It was proposed that the King go to Vincennes, but this was prevented by an unforeseen circumstance. That day a long Cabinet Council was held: and it is believed that the matter, which the Stock cannot resist, will still be handled without much deliberation. The Princes amuse themselves as usual: the Prince of Charolois appeared on Sunday in a suit of Green Velvet most richly embroidered. At table one spoke of [?]. When the Turkish Ambassador had audience by the Archbishop of Cambrai, the Prelate received His Excellency on a cushioned chair, and the ceremonies of the audience by an Ambassador to the Grand Vizier who reclined on a Sofa; they have simply conformed to the ceremony that took place in the year 1661 in the same case, when Mr. Lysianes was Secretary of State, and the Porte sent an Embassy to Louis the XIV, on account of the interest that Prince took in the War of Succession in Spain. Although the Emperor invoked the prohibition of Commerce, it is believed that at Frankfurt no Pauluses nor their Goods shall be admitted until the Easter Fair. The Governor of Provence reports that the Sickness is severely felt in Aix, Toulon and Terrasson. The first news of the Pope's death came by a Courier from Parma, who [?] on the 28th at [?] presented the matter, upon which the dispatch of the Bishop of Eifferen from Rome and an hour after was followed; by its bringing were of the same content, to wit that His Holiness [?] the 2nd third of this month for 24 hours was ill and deceased: he was born 31 July 1642 in Pedrara in the Kingdom of Urbino, and made in the Secretariat of Briefs by Innocent XI; on 13 February 1692 by Alexander VIII created Cardinal, and after the death of Innocent XI, on 12 February 1700 raised to the Papal dignity; he was the 243rd Pope and the 45th after the restoration of the Roman See, or the Siege of Rome. One finds here presently not clearly whether the spirit of Charity and Religion of our Lords Anticonstitutionals [?] on the Pardon of the Martyrs: said to have handled over Sunday by force over the rest of the Deceased. Some days ago, however, Rome the Regent said to Knight Surson that the English, insofar as they were very prominent in the Adieus diplomatiques, could expect reward. It is said that few Cardinals and Solemnities are on the way, to [?]

AMSTERDAM, 7 April. The Directors of the East India Company in the Assembly of the Seventeen in Zealand have arranged [?] in the selected chambers to rebuild, 2642 bales brown Pepper, being all that the Company [?] 10000 pounds white Fouly, 4000 pieces male Nutmegs, 425000 pounds Cinnamon in House Bardeelfan, and more Goods, namely at the chambers of Zealand on 13 May, at Delft on 26 May, at Rotterdam on 23 May, at Hoorn on 6 June, and at Enkhuizen on 9 June; the Pepper, Fouly, Cinnamon and male Nutmegs shall be sold with a one-year credit; and upon delivery of the Nutmegs at 75 Guilders Bank-money shall continue until the first March 1722. In the month the Ships Bleyenburg, Ouwekerck, Kokkingen, and the Hookers the Cape and Gouda. On 21 January lay the Zealand Ships of Captain Zacharias Zechthuise, Piet. Verschaale &c. at the Caraques [?], of which the same could sail to the southern coast.
