At Bristol various ships from Jamaica have arrived, among them the Violet Captain Harlion, the Doughty Captain Williams, Captain Breuton &c. the Fort Frigate to London, and 3 or 4 others to Ireland; with said ships news is that on 7 February at Jamaica 3 or 4 barks from Porto Bello and Cartagena had arrived, which were chased by Spanish [?], but subsequently were retaken by other ships; and that at Jamaica or thereabouts there were no rovers; the ship the Hope Harmen Piet., from Copenhagen bound for Bordeaux, has come within the river of Gothenburg, and on the contrary the ship the Hunter Zacharias Handsen departed therefrom to Nantes, as likewise Christopher Burmans from Danzig have departed. At St. Maarten arrived the Elisabeth Corp. Pieterfe from here, [...]

THE HAGUE, 1 April. The Spanish Ambassador the Marquis de Montileone has visited several gentlemen of the government. This morning the Guard at [?] in the Wood was sworn in by General Count [?].

HANOVER, 4 April. The 2 Jewish Bankers Gumperra and Isack Berens, who (according to my last) last Sunday were still fetched in Hildesheim in the [?] and were pursued by several under-officers in various places, have further been fetched in Hildesheim in the [?] and have been brought by the government to Hildesheim in a secured place; and as the same the Generals on request most earnestly wish regarding this investigation [...], as Bildsheim of Hildesheim, the government of [...] has sent knowledge by Express to his K. V. Grace. [?] some [?] have been sent to Hildesheim to [?].

The Directors of the Company of the City of Hoorn, notifying the Company, whereby 5 per cent interest has been paid, complain that in accordance with the resolution taken on 1 April 1721, such 5 per cent on the 2nd [?], and continued to the end of said month, on [?] 21, and on Saturdays from half [?].

The wood merchants Pieter Onkelboer, J. and D. Bontekoning, shall on Friday the 11 [?]; 3800 Miscellaneous beams, at Boskuem, short supply; 2800 Oak; 3400 Long beams; 1 Lodge, an extra fine batch of 110 Rigade, Moscow and Nerva Timber. 2; 2800 [?], in which also a batch of fine Mill-timbers, and 1800 [?] Goodinia. To the lovers of Wood shall be announced [?], of which all [?] Listed these [?], and Huberrus Dekker Medical Doctor, handled in extra custody from [?], Smyrna, Antioch &c. shall be sold in the said June, with the auctioneer shall serve [?] to obtain catalogs shall [?], further

The City of Hoorn hereby notifies all those who [?] in [?], and this shall also be shown with Letter or Tickets on their own [?], shall begin to [?] from [...] of the execution of two per [?] month, on [?] Tuesday, Thursday and Friday, in the morning from 8 to [?] Friday 11 April 1721, at Amsterdam in the Swan on the New side [?] and Narva parts; [?] [?] and [?], [...] [?] and other and [?]; 400 Long [?], Nerva and [?] Beams, Post-beams, &c. And Amsterdam sell on Friday 11 April 1721, in the New Exchange [?] and Goods lying behind the Land [?].

The estate of the late gentlemen Frederick Dekkins, [?] and [?] and Mrs. Bargetten, as also the beautiful [?] of the same shall be, further through the Courant shall be made known.

---

DE KLOPPER, on the Heeidelstraat 0 April 1721. By Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Courant of the EXCHANGE is today on Madrid 96 and 1 quarter to 1 half. Cadix 113 and 1 quarter to 1 half. Seville 112 and 1 half to quarter. Bilbao 90 and 1 half. Lisbon 43 and 1 half. Venice 84. Livorno 91 and 1 quarter to 1 half. Genoa 94 and 1 half to [?]. Paris 41 and 1 half. London 11 Shillings 10. Leipzig current 38 and 11 [?] guilders. Frankfurt current 21 Rigsdaler. Ditto current 18 and 5 [?]. Danzig 200 Polish guilders. Breslau current 1 [?] guilder. Hamburg 32 and 1 half guilder. Antwerp at per Cas. Ghent 1 and 1 quarter to 1 half. Brussels 2 and 1 half. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 1 quarter. The Agio of the Bank 3 and 2 [?]. The Stocks of the East India Company are 320, West India Company 108, Subscription 160. [Editor's note: This newspaper contains commercial intelligence, shipping news, exchange rates, and political reports typical of early 18th-century Dutch gazettes. The transcription quality deteriorates significantly in the latter sections, particularly in the Hanover and Hoorn notices, where multiple passages remain uncertain or garbled in the original. The death notice concerns Pope Clement XI, who died 19 March 1721 in Rome.]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 9th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 44.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 11th of April, 1721
[Leaden Mercury/News Digest]

SAINT PETERSBURG, 17 March. Yesterday another warship, which will carry 80 guns, named the Vredemaker [Peacemaker], was launched into the water from the slipway, and at the end of this week another warship will be launched as well. So that His Czarist Majesty, if peace with the Crown of Sweden is not concluded this summer, will have a considerable fleet at sea, for which the necessary orders have been given. His Czarist Majesty has recently met [with envoys], and letters and conferences have advanced; they remain here waiting only for the return of the courier, which the French Minister Herr Campredon has sent, and once that arrives, one will be able to determine when the aforementioned ministers will depart for Nieuwstad [?], to begin negotiations regarding a peace treaty with the second minister expected there. On the fifteenth day when His Czarist Majesty is to retire to Riga in a comfortable manner, everything has already been made ready; as soon as word is received of the arrival of the Duke of Mecklenburg there, the journey in that direction will be undertaken.

---

ITALY

NAPLES, 13 March. Orders have been issued to march idle soldiers to Manfredonia, and to have them proceed from there to Fiume and subsequently to Hungary.

ROME, 22 March. The Pope, having felt indisposed for some days, his condition manifested itself through spitting of blood and bleeding from the nose, but this was not so severe that His Holiness was forced to keep to his bed; however, his condition called for a consultation with the doctors regarding his state, in which it was concluded that the bleeding and discharge of bloody fluids had its origin solely in the head, caused primarily by certain congestions from continuous bleeding.

In the afternoon around 2 o'clock Cardinal Albani as Cardinal Camerlengo [Chamberlain] arrived in a violet robe, accompanied by the chamber clerics, who wore black vestments, disposed with the Pope, and the faithful Penitentiaries, to prepare the body of the Pope; approached him [?] by his bedside, and having declared him dead, he had the same announced by means of an Apostolic pronouncement; thereupon from the Holy Cross, the Pope's chamberlain, the [?] of [?], which has the papal seal, which he subsequently delivered over to the aforesaid head of [?], with an obligation to bring it to the [?] with the date [?], everything being done through Apostolic pronouncements, and his venerable [?] Cardinal Palace of the Quirinal, gathering [?] of the cold, before which the Captain of the Pope's body [?] to [?], and the Swiss who usually accompanied His Holiness went thereupon, to proceed thus near his [?] Palace of the 4 Fountains: when this procession began, the great bell of the Capitol was rung, which is not heard except in such weeks of occasion to announce the death of the Pope throughout the whole city; and subsequently the [?] and all the Grenadiers have been heard, along with the [?]. When His Eminence arrived at their palace, he held first a chamber congregation, which is continued daily. Thursday the Cardinal Tanara [?] of the Sacred College held a congregation with the three Generals of the Orders and the Cardinal Camerlengo, which congregation is also all days and [?]. That evening at 5 o'clock the body of the Pope was brought from the Palace in the following manner to the Consistorial [hall] to the Vatican; the lowest servants of His Holiness went ahead with unlit wax torches in hand, and behind them came the light horses or guards of the Pope, their lances dragging along the ground and the standards rolled up, with the church trumpets and trumpets at the head on which only trumpets were made: and the guard following [?] stalwarts, with burning torches in one hand, and on the other hand a led horse with black vestments, trailing to the ground, hung [with ornaments]; behind these followed the body, which lay on a, with black crepe covered, litter or carrying chair, and was carried by four horses; the same was clothed in the patriarchal vestments, the stole about the neck, the ecclesiastical cap and hat on the head, and alongside the same went 6 Penitentiaries of St. Peter, who each held a burning wax candle in hand and sang psalms; these were surrounded by the Swiss guard on foot, with their four [?], which sat just behind the bier on horseback; after which the rest of the horses and carriages followed; and the march consisted of 6 or 7 pieces of cannon, which followed the train. The body, thus brought to the Vatican, was laid in the chapel of Sixtus: the following day the canons of St. Peter, together with the Sacred College, brought the body of the Pope in the church of St. Peter to the chapel of the Holy Sacrament, where it was placed on a bed of [?], and [lay] for 9 days displayed for all the common [people] who wished to come kiss the feet of His deceased Holiness through the railing, against which the same lay exposed. Yesterday morning a congregation was held in the priestly vestry in the Vatican of 17 Cardinals, because Cardinal Sacripanti and Mariscotti did not appear through indisposition, in this congregation there were read the Bulls of many Popes concerning the election of a new Pope, and the aforesaid Cardinals confirmed under oath to observe the same: and then the Fishermen's Ring was broken; the Lord Falconieri governor of this city was confirmed; the Lord Rupoli declared governor of Borgo during the Conclave; Father Lucchesini appointed to give the funeral oration; and the Lord Mari Bishop of Aleria for the oration concerning the election of the new Pope, on the day when the Mass will be said [?] to celebrate: thereafter the Cardinals Barberini, Altieri and Corini were appointed as superintendents to prepare a new Conclave, to which the utmost speed is being applied. Finally it was concluded in the congregation to summon the Admirals of Noailles and Alberoni to the Conclave, and to the latter for all security a free safe-conduct to be given, so that he could come and return safely. Today the Cardinals have assembled again in the church of St. Peter, where the Mass was sung and the funeral service for the repose of the deceased Pope's soul was performed: subsequently a congregation was held in the sacrifice, where Prince Don Agostino Chigi [Editor's note: possibly Agostino Chigi] the [?] as marshal of the Conclave deposited himself here. The conservators of the Roman people requested, as is customary, that they be provided with a new Pope; and Cardinal Aquaviva requested the Sacred College on behalf of the Catholic King of Spain to support in the maintenance of the honors of the same College: Cardinal Gualtieri, minister of the President, made a similar offer in the name of his Principal, saying that the aforementioned unfortunate state [?] finding itself, and not being in a state to offer the College his military force, he nevertheless through his prayers will seek and endeavor to obtain a happy success in the maintenance of the honors of Heaven: the aforementioned Cardinals Aquaviva and Gualtieri have offered some compliments of condolence regarding the death of the Pope to the [?], and made some offerings to him.

---

MILAN, 28 March. It is said that a considerable number of soldiers from this duchy are to return back to Germany.

---

VENICE, 29 March. The day of Mary's Annunciation, patroness of this city, was held in the church of St. Mark according to custom in the presence of the Doge and the magistrates. The Lord [?] having received news of the death of the Pope, has returned here with his son. Cardinal Barbarigo has arrived here, but having received news of the Pope's death, has packed his baggage again in his wagon, intending to return [to Rome] with his household. Count Ferdinand de Tallis, general of the Venetian fleet there, has died, and his eldest son has succeeded to the office. Also [?] that Cardinal de Koumbia has been [?] by [?], and that he intends to pursue his journey to Rome at the first opportunity. There is already talk of more than 12 Cardinals who would come into consideration for the Pontificate, and it is assured that the Spanish interest will not be without [weight] [?] As one has seen on many occasions, at the time when that see is vacant, the interest of the [?] is again awakened from good [?], so if one [?] of the others one that was placed on the tomb of the Pope's chief physician [?]. Begun in the words [?], [remainder of text illegible or uncertain]

England, April 1

The Roman Catholic Ministers held a hasty conference during the previous week at the Dominican Monastery, without reaching a conclusion on the preceding Wednesday; the principal orders established by their Courts must await confirmation. In the meantime, the Cardinal of Saxony [Editor's note: likely referring to a German ecclesiastical figure] has consulted with the Catholic Ministers; discussions have been held on these matters, but little good has come of it. It appears that in these circumstances, few want the negotiations to proceed lukewarmly and merely as a formality; and some affirm that the Catholics, not wishing to yield to the Protestants, seek to obstruct matters, to which the Evangelicals prepare themselves as much as possible to resist. Likewise, the agreement on the Imperial Proposals and the Easter period have caused that the negotiations have not proceeded; [?] Right will be chosen. The Parisian plan has received considerable support in these negotiations; the aforementioned Baron, of which I made mention in my previous report, has been marked for punishment. Here it is wished that the Ministry's Troop be increased to 1500 Men, and the recruitment of the three regiments destined for this has begun. On Tuesday the Countess of Eck passed away here. According to letters from the Mr. van Keek of the 29th, he intends to make a journey, and that notices of today's business will be sent there presently for [?] 100, which one suspects will differ considerably from what the Minister there reported. The aforesaid Mr. van Keek is expected to return shortly. In the meantime, the conduct of the Protestant Ministers here has been well approved by their respective Courts, which the Imperial Court will likewise acknowledge.

France

PARIS, April 4

The King has granted the Coadjutorship of Saint-Pierre Brochasfer, of the Order of Cluny, Bishop of Saint-Flour, to Madame de Beaumergies de Mongon, and the Archdeaconry of Saint-Toul, in the Church of Verdun, to Father Degete, in place of the Mr. Hierbet, who has requested his dismissal. These days, before the Council of State, a considerable lawsuit has been decided against the Marquis de Blanc, whereby he not only remains deprived of the King's Domains which had previously been taken from him, but furthermore loses a Sum of 2000 Livres which he had raised for the service of the late King. The Abbot Bignon, Librarian to the King, accompanied by Mr. Bignon de Bianzi, Intendant of Paris, and the Abbot Raguet [?], presented a visit to the son of the Turkish Ambassador, who received them with great courtesy, offering refreshments and coffee; and their requested portrait of the Ambassador was [?]; meanwhile the Ambassador himself entered the room, and the principal discussion was upon the difference between Eastern, French, and Italian music; the Ambassador, with good grounds of the matter, declared himself more charmed by the melody of ours, and the Abbot Bignon offered to show his Excellency, at the nearest opportunity, that our Ministers in church music were much more perfect and elegant than the Oriental; subsequently, several refreshments were brought forth; which the Messrs. Bignon did not wholly accept, but they could not refrain from the perfumed waters, to which they dipped their hands, in order to have them poured therewith. It is now certain that the gratitude of the Grand Seigneur, conveyed through his Ambassador to the King, the following week brought a casket inlaid with gold with Prunes and a Ring, two small Turkish horses with Turkish saddles, Jennain, a Salamander, two Rhinoceros teeth; a Persian [?], six Gourds, Syrups and various Saffrons. The rumor is, as if the King of Spain has [?] according to which the inspection of the Acts of the Company of the Indies shall not be done in this way as hitherto happened; the W.I.C. [Editor's note: West India Company] subscription shall soon perhaps know the truth of it, as likewise whether that Company will not be diminished as it is today.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, April 9

At Fort à Port lay still the Ship-fleet from Lisbon; the ship bound for Goa, and the Fleet likewise sealed from Lisbon. At Bilbao people were very busy preparing to sail. Francisco Sarnotega lay there ready to sail. From Majonne departed Pieter Klyn toward Zeeland, and Captain Willem Beyn toward Rochefort, and Saint-Job, Pieter Laurens toward [?], and the Hope Jan Gerritte de Jong toward Honfleur. At Bordeaux arrived Teums Andriette, the Falcon Lammert Jande, and others here and elsewhere at Nantes (from where Jan Adams and three others departed toward the Netherlands, &c.), the Saint-Laurent Gertrit Thom. from the Meuse; at Portsmouth Pieter Valk, Lecad. Stroobo and others, from here and the Meuse toward Nantes likewise, as well as the Ship the Co-etboy in Beneden and at Stavanger Eelke Gertris from here.

The Hague, April 10

Yesterday morning, the Baron Ulfeldt, Minister to the Electress Palatine, spent some hours in conference with the lords of the Council of State; at the same time, certain important Ministers were with Prince Kurakyn, Ambassador and Plenipotentiary of the Czar of Great Russia. A dispatch from the former British Minister at the Court of the King of Denmark has passed to London. After the Easter days, the gentlemen Commissioned Councillors of the Northern Quarter shall be convoked here.

---

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES** The Course of Exchange is today: Madrid 90 and 1/4. Cadiz 91 and 1/4. Seville 92 and 1/2. Bilbao 90 and 1/2. Lisbon 91 and 1/4. Venice 84. Livorno 91 and 1/4 to 1/2. Genoa 94 and 1/2 to 95. Paris 41 and 1/2. London 33 shillings 9 and 1/2. Leipzig current 38 and 1/2. Frankfurt current 38 Reichstaler. Danzig 290 Polish groats. Breslau 38 and 3/4. Hamburg 32 and 1/2. Antwerp 2 per Guilder. Ghent 1 and 3/4. [?] 2 and 1/2. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 1 and 1/4. The Premium of Stocks [?] and 2/18 to [?]. The Premium of the E.I.C. [Editor's note: East India Company] is 2110. W.I.C. 1 o 8. Subscription 3 ro.

Notices & Advertisements

On Sunday His Holiness felt some cold chills, but on Monday morning, having risen, he assisted at Mass; but shortly thereafter he was again seized by some chills, followed by a slight fever, whereupon sweet almond oil was immediately administered to His Holiness, which had no effect whatsoever; upon which the doctors prescribed rising lye-oil; but the effect that the Holy Father experienced from this was an increase in fever with continual fainting fits, from which continued deterioration the doctors concluded that the end of His Holiness was near, so that formally in the afternoon the Sacrament was given and in the afternoon at three o'clock the last anointing; at four o'clock the Pope's chest began to swell, and the fever doubling, brought him into great distress, such that Cardinal—as the Pope's uncle—deemed him at the uttermost, summoned all the Cardinals, who by His Holiness were raised to the Purple, and the Cardinals themselves hurried to come before the bedside of His Holiness; and when they had appeared there, this was made known to the Pope when he had recovered sufficiently to his senses from the fainting fit, when the Cardinals Albani and Paolucci, along with some others, requested that a consistory be held and the promotion of the 2 vacant Cardinal positions be made, but the Pope answered them that it was not the time to think of other matters, and having given them his blessing, he said farewell to them all; after which Cardinal Albani alone remained with the Pope his uncle, whose Holiness requested his petition to promote his blood relatives to the Purple, upon which the Pope regarded him for about 2 minutes intently without speaking, and subsequently answered GOD MAKE YOU HOLY: in the evening around 7 o'clock the Cardinals were again called out from the Apostolic Palace, [?] that Cardinal Salsemo alone, along with the 4 Generals of the Orders remained with the Pope, and in the night at eleven o'clock the Penitentiaries of St. Peter were called to His Holiness, [instructions following] for proclamation in all churches, upon which various criminals in the Castle of St. Angelo imprisoned themselves, to obtain pardon at the election of a new Pope according to custom, and thus were released and [?]. Wednesday morning and around half past eleven o'clock, His Holiness feeling that he was dying, thanked his personal physician for the help rendered to his weak condition, and asked all his servants to forgive him whatever he might have wronged them in; subsequently, what occurred was that, with great fervor, he asked pardons from all Christian Princes and generally from the whole world, and about an hour thereafter, around half past one o'clock, he expired on his deathbed. One found in his estate no more than 1200 thalers and 3 schillings, since he had distributed very much in alms, and it is reckoned that His Holiness during his Pontificate, above the customary gifts made through his almsgiving officials, from his own money a million and a full thousand [?] to the poor. [Editor's note: This refers to Pope Innocent XIII, who died on 7 March 1721]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 11th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 45.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 14th of April, 1721
Spain

CADIX, 19 March. Finally, our Deputies have turned their backs on Madrid with the latest Resolution from the Court, requiring all debased Silver to be brought to the Mint, which has caused great consternation among the Merchants, so that nearly everything has come to a standstill; the Silver that has arrived with the Buenos Aires Ships must also be brought to the Mint; and it is said that the Spanish Court would not be satisfied merely to freight the galleons with the various kinds of Spanish goods and manufactures, nor to ship anything further from this Territory, and that no other manufactures or merchandise may be loaded therein except those with which these Kingdoms are not yet provided, and moreover an order has come from the Court to have the Galleons depart at the end of this month or the beginning of the next; and although the Spanish Court still takes on both, it is to load their goods therein; and because the foreign merchants are likewise keeping their Warehouses closed, it is settled that the Galley goods shall also remain between Cadix and Seville until peace is concluded, or whether the aforementioned Ships will be able to sail out to Sea. People here await with longing the News from Vera Cruz. The Ships of Jacob Barba and Friend Christian Lubben, which arrived in this Bay, have received permission to unload likewise; also the French Ship of Captain Tassio has entered unobstructed; further, arrived here the Swift François Bateman from Vlissingen, the Rider Wydt, Islander of Ostend, the Anna Maria Wigman from Dietseren bound for Barcelona, and the St. Urbin Pietré Taffy bound for Genua and Livorno, both from Amsterdam, having carried through Soedum [?] Poleate, a Sailcloth and Wheat cargo; the Dove Year. Anthony Merd from Ireland, the Samuel and Isabella Wilk. Skelton, and the Harle Wilk. Harle, both from London; in part waters departed Adr. Loyes and Pietre Hendrik from St. Louis, the Ships from Amsterdam resident, still lie in this Bay, and Wigman from Duyckeren likewise, to depart tomorrow for Barcelona if the Wind be favorable.

MADRID, 22 March. General Marquis de Leede, having finally arrived the other day, was immediately admitted to Audience with the King, who received him most graciously, and His Majesty immediately granted permission for the Expedition in Africa, etc.

France

PARIS, 7 April. This Court cherishes great hopes regarding the abilities of Cardinal de Rohan, as well as of the Duke of Bourbon: nevertheless one observes that the Appellants are troubled, for the Court is looking to Billy and striving to obtain the Edict upon a Constitutional basis. It is said that the prospective Election of a new Pope might be so delayed that it could fall upon a Cardinal [?] allied to the House of Austria, as being the strongest in the Conclave. Father Jesuit Gaillard, who possesses particular talents for relieving the burdened consciences of persons of rank, has been engaged at the outset to set upon the right path a certain Marchioness, who has not been to confession in 30 years. On Friday the King was hunting in the Wood of Vincennes; Mademoiselle de Chadollo and la Rothe Sir Ton accompanied His Majesty, besides other Ladies. The day before yesterday there was a meeting of the great Council, which finally resolved to suppress the Company of the Indies and establish a new one upon different principles; it is said this was done by order of the Lords of Paris. The Duke of Bourbon is reported to be troubled over this suppression, because he was the Protector of that Company. In the handling of the Regent Bishop much is being undertaken, and much good is expected here from the Example of the Proceedings in England, and the Constitution among the Ministers is undoubtedly [?]. Monsieur Coppel, one of the King's capable Painters, showed to the Saturday and the Regent Duke an Affectation, which shall serve as model, with the Carpet that was ordered to be made to decorate the Audience for the Turkish Ambassador: the same Ambassador brings the sacred vessels of the Greek Church, besides 2 Arabian Manuscripts for the King's Library; the Son of His Excellency is to go this week to the renowned Apothecary, Claudii Cordova. From the Academy of Sciences and his own Cabinet of the History of Nature to be occupied. The Parliament [?] by two Distributors of a certain Man, who has a Shop in the Palace and there upon by [?] of the Appellants did the loads velociter, as also certain other Writings making the Constitution, all the Copies were removed, without otherwise molesting him. The Process of the Duke of [?] Force was postponed by the Parliament and it is understood that it is proposed: to pay the Smith. 2,200 Livres for a sixth great and buen-valued [?] Ice-Kit, which he had received from his considerable fortune at that Man's order and had wanted to continue.

Great Britain

LONDON, 3 April. Last Friday, Colonel Brickley, Member for the Commons from the Knights, Justices of the Peace, principal Burghers, and other Communities of Gloucester, presented a Request to the House of Commons; wherein were set forth the lamentable consequences which the Trade of this Nation, and particularly the Woolen Weaving, has suffered, through the faithless and bad treatment held by the former Directors of the South Sea Company, of which the greater part, together with their Associates, has thereby brought the State into discredit of the Nation, requesting that the House, according to its great wisdom, should proceed against the guilty parties in a fitting manner; and after this Request was read, it was ordered that it be laid upon the Table: it is expected that this example will be followed by several other Counties. Saturday the University of Oxford formally thanked the Earl of Nottingham through the Vice-Chancellor, together with the Gentlemen Bromley and Clark, for His Lordship's praiseworthy defense for Christendom, concerning the matter of Mr. Whitton's Letter written to him regarding the Directorship. Yesterday the King communicated to the House of Commons through the Comptroller of his Household various Letters, both from here, Brussels, as well as Vienna, concerning the surrender of the Knight Knight to Great Britain; as well as the answer which the Emperor gave thereupon to Colonel Churchill, who had been sent to Vienna to him with the opening; this answer is in very obliging Terms, and His Imperial Majesty assures the King that it will be a pleasure for him to serve His Majesty in this matter, as well as in all others, in such manner as His Imperial Majesty shall give necessary orders to the Marquis de Prids; and that the said Knight shall be delivered up, as soon as this can be done; without bringing great satisfaction to the States of Brabant, since they claim that their Province has the right of protection: but however obliging this answer of the Emperor has been, it nevertheless displeased many Members of the House of Commons, among others Milford Molesworth, who thereupon declared himself sufficiently ardently, and as well as Mr. Hutchinson, a fellow-Member of the said Committee, proceeded to present an Address to the King, to request His Majesty to issue a Proclamation, whereby the Knight Knight be relieved of the Suspicion incurred in departing from the King's realm within the time determined by the Act, to invite it thereby to the like welcome; hereupon arose heated Debates, whereby some proposed to read and examine the said Letters once more, but they agreed to postpone this until Wednesday morning. That day His Inventory of the Goods of the former Governors and Directors of the South Sea Company was received; the same consisted of various Volumes in Folio, for upon the checking of their Goods it must appear that these Gentlemen have wholly adhered to the Letter of the Parliamentary Act, particularly of all the Transports which they made in nearly a Year, and giving an Account of the Receipts and the Expenses of the whole Year, at the End of that, the Balance was not settled; wherefore the House resolved to appoint a Committee of 13 Members to examine the same and report thereon to the House. Yesterday evening the King held Chapter with the Knights of the Order of the Garter, wherein the Duke of Grafton and the Earl of Lincoln were received as Knights Companion, in place of the deceased Dukes of Buckingham and Rutland; it is the King who hangs the new forbidden Knights the blue Band, the Prince of Wales gives them the Garter around the Leg; and the remainder of the Ornaments, as the Cross, the Mantle, the Star, etc., are not given to them except at the Ceremony of Installation, which always takes place in the Chapel of Windsor Castle. That same evening there were brought before the King two young African Princes, whose elder Brother possesses a Kingdom in Africa, lords over the Island Madagascar; these Princes were maintained here by the East India Company, and are to be sent back in short to their Fatherland, from which they had set out in new accord to visit Europe, and particularly England; by they have met the misfortune that the Captain of the Ship, in which they had embarked, had the faithlessness to sell their father, together with others in [?] Naples, as Slaves, notwithstanding all kindness and good treatment shown by the King to their Brother who had given them the same. At the end of the previous week a Secretary of the Tickfaurie of Greenwich Hospital was hanged, which appears to have occurred because he the following day [text cuts off]

It is understood that the formal establishment of a camp has been largely concentrated near the border; 12,000 of the Pomeranian Army will be assembled, as the King has also directed. It is reported that the King has been in Holland. The Ministers have not been to Germany this summer. There is mention of a court matter. There is mention of a public hearing, to expedite certain matters through the East India Company to Parliament, whereas such [matters] in the year 1727 [were handled differently], with the Government granting the Company the requested discharge. This will be presented by the King to Parliament. Today, regarding the dispatch of Queen Anne, concerning the employment of manufactured goods, the Report of the Bill is approved for further consideration, etc., passed in the fifth year of the Reign, with the Actions of the Bank at 110. Regarding Thursday's hearing, the Actions of the Bank are 110. East India 134, West India 130, the Million Bank 125, Africa Company 13, the 10 new [shares] 24, and the [remaining] contribution.

THE HAGUE, 13 April. Messrs. departed yesterday with the Lord Teffers, Resident for this state at Brussels, with some gentlemen; it is expected that they will shortly return thither. Before the Council of States the following gentlemen have taken their oath: Hans Willem van Groenendael as Lieutenant Colonel, and Syned Johan van Wynbergen as Captain. It is reported that the Lord Swerns de Landas, H.I. of Baa-icloot, Receiver at Renogenbdoich, has deceased.

The Dykgraaf and High Lords of Rijnland will on Wednesday, 23 April 1721, in the morning at 10 o'clock, at the house of Zwanenburg, halfway between the Spaarndamden Dike, in public auction sell the delivery of a large quantity of Axes, Nuts, Stone, Lime, Cement, Seaweed and Tallow Tar, also the Arbes and the [...] materials of Rijndam and at Halfway.

Tomorrow at 9 in the morning and at 2 o'clock in the afternoon, being Tuesday, 15 April 1721, the Lord Logement will sell a fine party of extra costly and fine Paintings of the foremost Masters, curious Mirrors, Music Books; together with some Bird [paintings], School of colors, Sprays and golden Dinettes &c.; all of which may be viewed today; and the Catalogs on the Loeysgraght at the aforementioned Hendrik Keibyl and G. Schoeminaket Broker.

In Amsterdam will sell on Wednesday, 16 April 1721, in the Nés in the Barke Ground, a party of Cotton Linens and Silk Stuffs, namely 1225 pieces long Gerasse, 700 ft. short ditto, 241 ft. rough ditto, 440 ft. raw Cotton, 222 ft. fine Guineas, 190 ft. Salempoaties, 633 ft. Emi thys, 693 ft. fine Batten, 101 ft. striped ditto, 193 ft. Tirintagres, 1920 ft. Coracaw, 280 fl. [and] Picter Onkelboer and other Goods more.

Johannes and Dirk Bontekoning Brokers will in Amsterdam sell at the Lord Logement, an extraordinary party of 800 Rigals, Moscovill and Nerts of 800 Rigals, Mosey skins and Nerts of 100 black Mole-Furs, and 100 black and Gray Mice.

Johannes Staats Broker will in Amsterdam, on Friday, 18 April 1721, in the evening at Haasttemanda, sell a party of over 300 Barrels blue English [goods], some Kikaente, Cafoergh, etc., Daurtzigte, and 60 Bales Tripoli Weed-Ash; the same being the day before and [?] Hendrik Jonas van der Zee, authorized Broker, (having commission from his Principal) is of the intention next Saturday, 12 April, in the evening at 8 o'clock, at the Burg within Leyden in public to sell a Life issued 11 January 1721, at 6 per cent per annum, on two lives, namely Maria Cath Willemina Ambrozia Roemaalen, then aged 25 years, the Purchaser himself retaining the Guilders, of which each year the half thereof on 11 July and 11 January.

To the Lovers of Flora it is made known that on Monday, 21 April 1721, in the finest condition of Hyacinths, Yellow, and other Crown Imperials, with variegated [leaves], remainders will be sold in the garden Game-Yard, located outside the Roepoog of the City of Leyden and Vrakeeroogg of the Hyacinths, Jonquilles, Anemones, Auricula, Syringe and Linden Trees, belonging to the Lovers, will by following Courants and posting of Tickets be made further known.

Pieter Eldens Broker will in Amsterdam sell on Wednesday, 23 April 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, in the Nés in the Brakke Ground, a party of extra fine St. Thomade Caramels; lying as by Notice is indicated.

Jan du Rien, Jan Wils, Jan van Bjanken, Joben Brouwer and Pieter Eldens Brokers will [sell] in April 1721, in the evening at 8 o'clock, in the Nés in the Brakke Ground, a party of extra fine Tobacco lying as by Notice is indicated.

Pieter and David Rukert Broker will in Amsterdam sell on Wednesday and Thursday at the corner of the Bantemer and Schipper-street, a neat Household [furnishing], with good Items, Carpets, Floor-Tapestries, Mirrors &c.; a standing Clock that runs 8 days; [items of] old Crack-Porcelain, large and small; some Paintings of foremost Masters; an old 8-seat Sleigh &c.; all of which may be viewed today in advance at the Sale-house.

Jan Pieterls Summer Broker will in Amsterdam, on 25 April 1721, sell the estate of brave Masters, as of the deceased Dirk Valkenburg, with all the Painters' Equipment, Model Drawings and Prints; all further printed in the Catalogs, which may be obtained at J. P. Zomer, Gysbert Bel and van Leeuwen House; tomorrow at 9 o'clock, in the Long Church-street, the adjacent House of the Leid-street; at Leyden at S. Luchman's will on 28 and 29 April 1721 be sold the estate of His Lords Mederts Dielars and Professor in the Academy at Leyden, to be made fine, finer by the Catalogs which may be obtained at Dordrecht at van Braams, Rotterdam Widow Boes Werkeius and Boom, Utrecht van de Water, The Hague de Hondi, and in other Cities more. By m. priim lb DISSERTATION on Theological Prophetic Necessity, that which perpetually in the Church U of Bauuus, God according to the Thread, and the 2nd Chapter of Matthew, by Jacobus [...]. At Leyden by Abraham Kaljeweyt has been printed and in all Cities at the Booksellers may be obtained.

Funerals & Sepulture of the Pope; Comm. Anglii an Exact Relation of the Conclave of the Election 40: [?] is with the eighth the Dopra; being an account of the Seventh; Good, cost and Report of the Conclave, of the choosing of a new Pope, and of all the same circumstances. Also Jacobus Alderkerk, Chirurgeon at Zoetermeer, or properly the Juridical [office] of Zegz at Wedde is firmly attached to the Hook Shop, which in the active House for long years has not [?]; having once been reduced, offers himself to the aforementioned Widow at Zegvuaurd, or at Leyden.

VEENEN, 4 April. Saturday evening two Couriers from Rome have arrived here again, the first sent by the Cardinal of Schönhausen, to invite the Cardinals and Conclave, and the other from Cardinal Albani to his Brother there to come to Rome. Yesterday the Cardinal Genfuegos took leave of the Emperor, who presented him with a fine Cross set with Diamonds. They have hastened this journey to Rome: the Cardinal Czaski prepares himself to follow soon, but has found the Cardinal of Saxen-Zeyts at Regensburg to pass, and that his presence there is deemed more serviceable than at Rome: likewise the Cardinal of Schönborn Archbishop at Speyer will be dispensed from that journey: meanwhile the new Chancellor Count of Kinsky has been appointed to take up the office as Imperial Intendant to go to Rome, and there the interest [to] go to Rome, and there the apostolic Count of Coloredo, who at [...].

FRANKFURT, 9 April. The Elector Palatine has appointed the Earl-peltin of Sultzbach as General Wachtmeester. The Imperial forces are being strongly pursued hereabouts, but with the remonstrances of the Cavalry no beginning has yet been made. The Imperial Minister and the Countess has the Count of Stollheim still at Ulm. The Swabian Circle has been invited to always maintain 3 Regiments on Foot and 2 on Horse in peace-time in service.

DÜSSELDORF, 11 April. Friday the Gulich and Berg Estates are to assemble in alliance.

COLOGNE, 11 April. Today the Bishop of Münster and Paderborn, together with Ministers and Officials, are to make the arranged journey to Rome. The Elector of Cologne is expected within a few days to visit the City to give said Bishop a visit. The day before yesterday the village of Wattingen, lying near this City, was burned down by Heidens' son.

Course of Exchange

AMSTERDAM, 11 April. At Lisbon have been captured: Hendrik Schelborn of Lübeck, the Ludaine William Tuin of Rotterdam, a German ship of Holl[and], the St. Michael, Captain Nath. Cieag of Liniöe, the Revival John Huges and the Wright John Charter of London; at Pharos the Young Jan, Jan Buysdan and the Young Chaufsa Bayonne, Francois de Rebella of Bilbao, Siewert Adriaanie, besides an unknown ship of Honfleur, and before the City Anne Tobi. The Course of the EXCHANGE is today at Madrid 90 à 19 and three quarters; Radix 113, Seville 112, Bilbao 90 and 1 half, Lisbon 92 and 1 half, Genoa 91, Geneva 94 and 3 quarters, Paris sight 41 and 3 eighths, London 33 Sch. 9 and 1 half, Leipzig current 13 and 3 quarters St. Frankfurt current 11 and 1 half, Danzig 36 and 5 sevenths Sch. 9 and 1 half, Hamburg 12 and 1 half à 9 sevenths St., Antwerp 2 and 3 quarters per O., Ghent 2, Brussels 2, Zeeland 1, Rotterdam 3 eighths. The Actions of the Bank 9 eighths.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 14th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 46.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 16th of April, 1721
Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

VIENNA, 2 April. By the latest letters from Constantinople, it is reported that a conspiracy by Prince Repofian against the Grand Turk has been discovered there. From this one could clearly see that the undertaking of Fuzuli would have been for the benefit of the said Grand Turk, had it not been discovered in time by a Janissary. Also, news has been received that the Ambassador in Moscow has fallen afoul of the latest ordinance of the Sultan, in that he is in improper correspondence with certain [?] officials, wherefore it is presumed that the Emperor, the Grand [?], in his audience has instructed the Czar, his Majesty, to request peace with the rebel Raseziby, which [?] to give him any aid, one that he himself alone [?] with the [?] and the weapon magazines [?] his garrison in a full war, whereby this [?] still reaches a document to the Great Majesty's Divan, from which certain [?] to send and the rumored line Plenipotentiaries in London at the Congress at Brunswick, a Church of Peace with Sweden may have been postponed. III. That His Czarist Majesty of Russia (Wilhelm David) has also from Paul Magneten and Versilles, the said Russian Ministers returned from here to Petersburg last Monday.

BRUNSWICK, 4 April. On Wednesday the Duke of Blankenburg arrived here, and it is said that he will go to Kurelbad at the beginning of the coming month in order to negotiate with the reigning Princess. The Baron Keller, second Imperial Plenipotentiary, is expected in this city within 4 to 5 days.

DRESDEN, 3 April. Since the King has lodged with the Count of Nottkie, nothing else will have arrived in Warsaw except the troops and gendarmes of Courtland [?].

REGENSBURG, 9 April. After the last negotiating session for [?] was held last Friday, the Roman Ambassadors declared themselves ready to answer the proposed points and propositions of His K.M., to settle and resolve the difficulties concerning religious matters, saying that they would await orders from their high principals in this regard, and would declare themselves at the first meeting on the 21st of this month. Accordingly, it is understood that the chief Imperial Commissioner [?] some Roman Ministers are not well disposed, because [?]. The printed list regarding the present redress of religious grievances, issued from the office by the Minister of the Elector there, contains no less than the orders given by his Master, and which have not yet been executed on account of the stubbornness of certain oppositions; the Protestant deputation being strengthened with one piece in the German [?], but through the weakness of the Elector Palatine [?].

Great Britain

LONDON, 11 April. On Wednesday those of the Lower House took under consideration the letters regarding the surrender of the old [?], handed down to them by the King on Monday; and, wherefore the gentlemen gathered there, it was striking that one of the Members was so [?] that he believed that we have done so much through the King and the Nation for the Emperor, and these which have cost such considerable sums, that His K.M. should refuse to deliver any knight, so that he might return hither and be surrendered to the Public; that one could see that this refusal proceeded from bad prejudices, and which reasons being more and the conveyance to the said Knight not having been overcome, and he therefore thought that one must present an address to the King to request His M. to undertake negotiations with the Emperor or at Rijswijk; and that if His K.M. should persist in his refusal, [?], yet this hereunto has not been formally ordered and not supported, has not come to any adoption; the House having turned in great committee, and the following day, it approved an address to the King to present to His M. to thank him for the resolutions and corps to placate [?].

Wednesday in the Courant.

The Report of the Committee, to which the Bill for fixing the day wages of the Tailors' journeymen was submitted, and the changes which the Committee made therein, being read, was well approved by the House, and others approved: after which the Bill was ordered to be put in proper form: subsequently the House ordered that Mr. Milnes, one of the Members, should also be joined to the Committee, also to the inventory of the seventeenth under, appointed Governors and Directors of the East India Company being put in and the third reading of the Bill against the unlicensed graduates of the Air ships would be postponed to this day, because the King had made known that the House, that His Majesty would be set to confirm the same in the Palace of St. James, according as the House has also appointed a body to present its resolution to the King, such that the Speaker was followed by more than 300 Members. It is expected that the Inspectors of the Goods and Effects of the said Directors of the East India Company will be made common with the Press, so that these might be examined by each [person]. The said Company has begun at this time to issue its orders to receive the transferred [?], but those who have old stock, seeing that the Company has added to this the annuities which they have subscribed and which are converted into new stock, have now found it reasonable to accept these orders, because they all [?] the execution of the proceedings, which several interested parties have undertaken before the Exchequer against the Directors, and of their having been well governed, is what one still expects will be cleared in time. Yesterday a general meeting was held at the Bank of England, and in it Sir Thomas Scawen was elected Governor and Mr. Aston as Deputy Governor for the coming year. Mr. William Bateman, son-in-law of the Earl of Sunderland, was elected in place of George Caswell as a Member in the House of Commons; as also Mr. John Aislaby for the Borough of Ripon. Wednesday the Duke of Hamilton returned here from his foreign journey. Yesterday the Daughter of the Duke of Dorset died of the smallpox. Today the Commons for the first time read and passed the Bill for preventing harmful practices in the East India trade; whereupon the Speaker read His Majesty's answer to the aforesaid Bill, presented by the House yesterday, being of the following import: I am pleased that my Governors have favored the instances which I have made regarding the Knight; I have received my wishes to be able to give sufficient provisions for the accomplishment of my secrets; and I hope it will be vigorous. It is understood that the Company of Stocking-makers has ordered their obligations with the East India stock to be discharged against 100 per cent.

France

PARIS, 11 April. Yesterday, being Maundy Thursday, the King heard the sermon of the Abbot de Lamothe Lamyre; after which came the adoration of the Holy Cross and the absolution, following which His Majesty washed the feet of 12 poor people and served them at table, with which service the Count of Bourbon did not precede [it] at the head of the court table; the dishes were brought by the Duke Regent, the Count of Chartres, the Counts of Charolais and Clermont, the Prince of Conti, and the principal officers of the King. After His Majesty had remained in the church gallery, the King on the following Sunday gave [?] in a chapel except towards Elizabeth fields, but because the evening hours remained cold, His Majesty returned early to the palace. On Monday a fire broke out in one of the cellars of the King's palace, which however had no consequences. On Tuesday there was also a fire in the palace of the Envoy of Parma, and in this circumstance one observed a great tranquility of good conduct in this Minister, who left the table with his servant in hand to investigate the cause of the disturbance in his house, and having investigated, he, without any change being perceived in him, gave the necessary orders to prevent the misfortune, which therefore also had no consequences. Subsequently His M., accompanied by his Governor the Count of Villeroy, went to the Church of the Capuchins in St. Honoré Street; before the King's coach went the Guard of 100 Swiss, the banner turned; the Garde du Corps marched around the coach, and after His Majesty had enjoyed the music there, the King participated in the procession, and was in the evening in the mourning which was held in the chapel of the Tuileries. One now sees the copy of a new project that has been proposed to the government and is very much welcomed and approved, bringing some change to public affairs, consisting in the following articles. 1. All royal duties to be discharged. 2. The cities not to have the power to tax. 3. From the debt which holds millions, keep the rents, and distribute them to the holders of liquid effects. 4. To regulate the rents on this city at 3 per cent, and to accept the new at 4. 5. To clear oneself of the goods below of the Million collector. 6. Direct overturn of Parliament to declare again [?]. 7. A Den Graauli of equality to [?]...

Brussels, 14 April

Last Wednesday, the new Jesuit General had a Mass celebrated by the Dominicans for the repose of the soul of the deceased [on the 12th], and the following day the Fathers of the Convent made him Keeper of the Keys of the Holy Sepulchre. The youngest Cardinal and the Count of Rouffy are dangerously ill. This day a Prince of la Tour, Captain in the Regiment Vehlen, departed this morning from 's-Hertogenbosch and is being conducted by Their Highnesses toward France; he is being followed by the greater part of their Court; it is believed that the old Prince will return here within a few weeks. The Cardinal Archbishop of Mechelen has departed from this city toward Rome; some suggest that he may have been sent with some order from the court in Vienna.

Amsterdam, 14 April

Letters from the coast confirm that the English, not only regarding the Buenos Aires ships have sent a new expedition to Madrid, but also copies of the contracts made with vigorous and humble remonstrances, so that one may yet hope to gain some [recompense?] concerning the remuneration of the silver from those ships. Due to rough and unseasonable weather, Lisbon—neither the ship to Goa nor the fleet to Rio de Janeiro could depart from there and go to sea. According to letters from the coast, the bells were rung there for two days over the matter; the Cardinal Archbishop Biesco [?] departed on the 2nd toward Rome; he is to be followed by Cardinal Alberoni, who would bind himself in the Genoese territory. On the 1st of March several English ships lay at Gibraltar, among them the Elisabeth (Thom. Bradly) and another ship, along with the Susan, [?] needing to go to the Mediterranean. Captain Bernard Argus sailed there from Bononia. From Bordeaux have departed the l'Heremitage (Captain Chapeaux) toward Saint Domingo, and the Susanna (Henrikson) toward Jamaica. At Limerick was entered Jan Douwde Kramer, and at Cork the Hoop (Gerrit Muys), both from here; in the Downs the St. Jan (Captain Antonio del Barco), from Bilbao toward London; the Parker (Captain Smith) and the Britade Adventure (Captain Darphon), both from Seville needing to go toward London; on the River of London the Bruin Gaily (Captain Hilladit) and several other ships from Jamaica; at Ostend Agent Groen within eight days from Nantes; and at [?] several ships.

The Hague, 13 April

The Electoral Palatinate Minister [?] visited several gentlemen of the government. A courier has departed from London toward Stockholm, whereby it is understood one wishes to give notice that the British fleet is to depart toward there in the late part of this or the beginning of the next month.

---

The Magistrates and Professors of the Rhine, shall on Wednesday the 12th of April 1721, in the morning at 10 o'clock, at the house on the Zwanenburgh, [?] opposite the Spierdam, publicly auction the lease of a large quantity of oak and [?] wood, stone, cement, water and [?], likewise labor costs; the bells are [?] within the boundaries of the Rhine at Sparendam and at Halfwegen.

The Directors of the Commerce and Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn hereby notify all those who hold shares in the said Company, upon which five per cent deposit has been paid, and who shall also show the same with letters or bills at their own risk, shall begin to receive until further distribution of two per cent on the 4th of that month, and continue until the last day of that month, on Tuesday the 11th, and in the afternoon from half three until five o'clock.

At Dordrecht has been stolen a silver timepiece with a twisted silver case, made by Paulus Kramer, with a minute and day hand, the chain made of thick silver and likewise, front and back with silver clasps, and a small key therein with initials B. L. W.; whoever brings the same to Dord at the Red Lion shall receive 10 guilders as a reward.

Pieter Onkelhoer, Joannes and Dirk Bontekoning, brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam on Friday the 18th of April 1721, at the New Lodging, an extra fine party with 200 [measures?], Muscovite and [?] materials, and 5 to 10 feet and a party of 200 grey fine curried Muscovite [?], lying behind the Land's East Warehouses.

Notice is given to lovers of flora that on Monday the 21st of April 1721 in the morning at 10 o'clock, at the bookshop of Johannes vander Linden, shall be sold an excellent collection with 200 [measures?], Muscovite and [?] materials, and other Crown Imperials, with [fine specimens?], left by the late Professors and Doctor Dekkers, in fine lots of hyacinths, [Gelders?], and other Crown Imperials, with the ephemeris of the City of Leiden, in the Bloom collection, under [?]; and green and lemon trees, [?], and the remainder belonging to the late [?], shall be made known by following newspapers and notices of sale. The register of all the collections in Gelderland can be obtained at Leiden from Mr. Pieter Pla.

The excellent and well-conditioned library of the Honorable Melchior Leydecker, formerly professor of Holy Theology there, shall be sold at Utrecht on the 21st of April; the catalogues can be obtained at Amsterdam from Boom and Wefleyn, Dordrecht from Outman, Rotterdam from the Widow Res., Leiden from Lugmans, The Hague from Floosz, Haarlem from J ma, Arnhem from Reggers, Deventer from Wyk, and Utrecht from de Water and Paddenburg, by which last-named [it] was printed, L. Lampe Hill. Ecclesia.

House [?], shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 23rd of April 1721, at the Old Herengracht, a party of colored cloths, of 8 and 9 quarter [width], [?] of various qualities, and other imported and exported cloths, silk, half-silk and wool fabrics, English manufactures, fine and common chiffon, [?], nettles, a party of linen, a party of [?], a cabinet with extra fine beautiful gold and silver antique medals, a party of green tea and tea [?], in packets of 4 to 10 pounds, together with a party of curious extra fine pique Brazil muslin and heavy spiced tobacco, and other goods to be seen the day before.

Johannes, Jan Lemmers and Hend. Linquet, brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam on Thursday the 24th of April 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, in the Weigh in the [?] Ground, a party of 200 grey fine curried Muscovite [?], the same server linen, and the same gauze, beaver hides, [?] furs, and other pelts.

Jan Pietsefe Zomer, broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Friday the 25th of April 1721 [the collection] of the late [?] Valkenburg, with all the painting implements, models, drawings and prints; all more fully described in the catalogues, which can be obtained from J. F. Zomer, Gysbert Hol and Leeuwen, [?] in the long Church street, the eighth house from the Leiden street, in the morning at 9 o'clock; all to be seen the day before.

At Leiden from Abraham Kaileweils has been printed and in all cities is to be obtained from booksellers, Treatise Curious concerning the Illness, Death, Funeral Rites & Burial of the Pope; Also an Exact Relation of the Conclave for the Election of a New Pope & of All the Foundations, [a description] of the illness and death, funeral rites and burial of the Pope; as well as a careful collection of the [medical] foundations of the Illness, Death, Funeral Rites and [?], in quarto.

Job Hugenburg, renowned battle painter in The Hague, [announces] that he has [engraved in intaglio] with various representations of the battles fought by the Most Serene Prince Eugene of Savoy, [published by Mr. du Mont, Counselor & Historiographer of His Imperial and Catholic Majesty]; the same is dedicated to the said Prince, and the prints are made after the paintings in large format, to be had [through the following booksellers]: at Leiden from Joh. Arnold [and] Langerak, Dore [from] Braam, Rotterdam [from] Beman, and Delft [from] Ad. Beman.

(Notice of a life annuity loan on behalf of the Generality, held on 31 January 1722, in the evening at 7 o'clock, to be sold publicly at the Burg within Leiden, at 5 per cent per annum, on two lives, namely Maria Catharina Roejczen, now aged 28 years, and [another person?] containing an annual life annuity of two hundred guilders, [payable] on the 11th of July and 31st of January.)

Course of Exchange

The course of EXCHANGE is on Amsterdam 34.4.1., regarding 31.11. Rotterdam 24.15. Antwerp 34.10. Hamburg 34. Paris 24 and half. Bordeaux no fixed price. Madrid 40 and half. Cadiz 47 and half. Bilbao 47 and half. Livorno 11 and a quarter to it and half.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 25th of April, at the bookshop of Johannes vander Linden, shall be sold all well-conditioned bound Latinos, Antiquities, Lexicographies and other miscellaneous works. The catalogue can be obtained at Amsterdam from Jb. Smits and J. Boom and at Haarlem from [?] and Bemain, [?] from Braam, [?] Staal, Utrecht from de Water, [?].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 16th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 47.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 18th of April, 1721
West Indies

CURAÇAO, 18 February. Various French barques from Martinique, Saint-Domingue, etc., and 13 to 20 English vessels from Barbados and other British islands have arrived here, together with a ship from New York, as well as Dutch barques belonging to J. Bonk, Pieter Klassis, Will. Vermey, Jan Thomas, Jan Nek, Andr. Kok, Jan Somel, Pieter Huyls, Brend. d'Widt, Pieter Bernier, Will. Lamothie, Bend. Vos and Rio dela Rache, &c., [bound for] la Guayra, and many others, without encountering any Spanish pirate vessels; and according to their reports, some French privateers are said to have been sent around Martinique, for which reason the Governor of that city had dispatched a warship with a brigantine to sea to seek out and drive away the aforementioned pirates. The Spanish warships which convoyed the fleet from Vera Cruz with Radix, and lay for several days below Spanish Saint-Domingue to take on water, have sailed back to Vera Cruz. On the Canary Islands (where Balthasar Wiggers arrived after his boat and 7 men had been lost), 3 to 4 Zeeland ships are lying, together with a large Spanish registered ship sailing for Spain; and the 2 Spanish ships lying at la Guayra will depart for Vera Cruz in 3 days, whereas 2 other laden ships already sailed from here on 30 December. The two Zeeland captains Zacharias Zach. and Piet. Vermale have, departing from the aforementioned coast, and early this week departed for the lower coast, where Pieter Bernard had arrived; as well as in this harbour Pieter Loethouwer of Amsterdam, and Alex. Mills from Iceland and Madeira, still late on 1 March, and Wernaar van Stade and Hendrik Tyman on 1 April [?] to depart for Amsterdam, towards which Captains Paul. Cheval. and Jac. Black are also sailing.

Spain

CADIZ, 13 March. In this bay lies a Spanish warship sailing for Havana, and 2 others of the same [class] are being careened, which it is thought will serve as convoy for the galleons, which [ships] are only just being built, and thereabouts in May the one—a different Holland ship, the city of Amsterdam, Captain Bellym—and the other—20, 24 to 30 [?]—will [sail]: on the 14th of this month the loading of these ships has begun, but it is not believed that they will depart fully laden, and what has been said.

England [?]

PETERSBURG, 24 March. Yesterday the French minister, Monsieur Campredon, after having taken his farewell audience with His Czarist Majesty, departed again for the court of the King of Sweden; having previously dispatched an expedition to the French court and another to Sweden with important dispatches: regarding what the aforementioned minister has negotiated here, people here only guess at it otherwise. His Czarist Majesty, who in 2 or 3 days [is to depart] towards Riga, yesterday took a ride with Prince Dolhoruski to Kioosilot: Her Czarist Majesty is also [to follow] to Lydia, for which reason her court retinue has departed thither: it is assured that His Czarist Majesty will stay there for several months, the court minister Herr Stambke having already departed there in advance to receive His Majesty for the occasion.

Italy

ROME, 29 March. The Pope's body, having lain for 3 days on a bed of purple and gold, was [displayed] for the people, who have come in very great numbers to kiss his feet through the iron grating which serves to separate the chapel of the Holy Sacrament in the church of St. Peter, [and] on Sunday evening was placed in a leaden coffin, on which his name and title were written in the presence of Cardinal Albani, nephew of the deceased Pope, and all the cardinals who have been elevated to the purple: Cardinal Albani placed a cord in the coffin [?], and a band over the eyes, and Monsieur Raspoil placed therein a purse with golden and silver medals, on which were the image of the deceased Pope and the names of his predecessors; and thereafter the coffin with the corpse was buried in a chapel of the canons of St. Peter, under a public act made by an Apostolic prothonotary: meanwhile the funeral services will continue for 9 days on the 3rd, during which the Apostolic Chamber has at its own expense erected in the middle of St. Peter's church a magnificent chapel, which is illuminated with a very great number of wax candles, and the [chapel itself?] is decorated with exquisite symbols and inscriptions, besides all the arms of the Deceased; and the Sacred College has participated gratuitously in these services, which tomorrow will conclude with a funeral oration delivered by one of the [clergy?] at the chapel. Meanwhile the cardinals gather daily after the conclusion of the chapel and hold congregation in the sacristy of St. Peter, to take all necessary and proper measures, both to ensure that certain offices of the Church benefit do not remain vacant during the vacancy of the Holy See, and regarding the forthcoming conclave. The Prince Famelico Confalonieri, Gonfalon[?] of Rome, has sent the banner that was in his custody to the Capitol Senate; the Herr Folgones who is confirmed in the government of this city, with the confederates of the Roman people and the heads of the 14 captaincies of the citizenry of this city, called Caporioni, have submitted their respective college to the Sacred College and offered the same all the help and assistance which is within their power, and at the same time requested permission to carry the civic arms, which has been graciously granted to them, so that the forthcoming pontificate the next day with their assembled citizens may go to their quarter [of the city]; the three days [?] [?] during which the see will be vacant. Meanwhile many cardinals, all the foreign ambassadors and ministers, all the bishops, and the greater part of the nobility have armed and warned people to guard their palaces, as is customary when the see is vacant, and whereby each palace seems to be a quarter of soldiers, and since each [is] to enjoy freedom of movement in his quarter, measures are being taken to prevent all disorder, but since some ambassadors have made it known that the civic patrols should not patrol their palace yards at night, there has been a congregation before the conservators of the Roman people and it has been resolved that the aforementioned armed patrols shall go not only by day but also at night past the palaces of the ambassadors, and thus as often as it is deemed necessary; of this resolution notice was immediately given to the Sacred College. The cardinals, heads of orders, have had Monsieur Peisgi, president of the Orfagna or the council over the grain stores, summoned to conduct himself in such a way that one has no reason to complain about his administration. The Herr Falconieri, governor of this city, has forbidden all sorts of games under severe penalties and has issued a stern decree against all sorts of mockery, libels and pasquils, on pain of the galley and even death. The Prince Chigi, marshal of the conclave, had also first taken measures to police it for the security of the conclave, and also presented his assistance to the Sacred College, which has already chosen a governor for the conclave, and likewise the doctors and surgeons who will attend the cardinals during the time that they will be occupied with the election, together with a confessor and an apothecary, to provide everything that may be necessary, and generally all the officers which are accustomed to be employed during the conclave. In the congregation held last Wednesday, the lot was drawn regarding the cells which each cardinal is to occupy in the conclave; and those marked in the plan in the margin of 68; the first that was drawn was Cardinal Albertoni, which gave rise to various reasonings; in that same congregation all the officers and clerks of the church were confirmed in their respective positions, and it was ordered that by the courts of the Rota and the government no one, whether to death, to the galley, or to any other punishment above the value of 20 Scudi, should be condemned without first giving notice thereof to the Sacred College. Cardinal Tanara, as dean of the said college, also holds congregation daily on the affairs of government with Cardinal Camerlengo, the cardinals heads of orders, and the cardinal priests and deacons. At the same time the Sacred College gave audience to the ambassadors of the various powers and republics, who presented to this gathering their compliments of condolence, and having been assured of the good will of their masters regarding the Holy See, they offered their military forces to protect the freedom of the conclave; their excellencies and the ministers were received in the royal hall, where they made [?] according to the law of custom, and as if the Pope were present at the same time; for the cardinals in that occasion say that although the pontificate is not yet filled, nevertheless the Pope is among them, and why this distinguished college receives the second obeisances, as if the Pope were known to be in the camerlengo; Cardinal Tanara answered on their addresses as dean in the name of the Sacred College. Herr Albani, brother of the cardinal of that name, by order of the congregation of the cardinals, heads of orders, has been recalled from Vienna; and the necessary provisions for the journey have been sent to him. Finally, circular letters have been dispatched to invite the cardinals de Noailles and Alberoni to attend at the forthcoming election, and for this a special safe-conduct has been offered to Cardinal Alberoni for the time which he will stay at Rome, with an ample time specified to return in safety after the election; upon which the cardinal sent an express messenger to the procurator Braide Antonius Ferrari with letters in which he appointed him his [?] agent, and the procurator in the said capacity has requested a safe-conduct from His Majesty [?] for the benefit of Cardinal Alberoni from the Sacred College, which has referred the matter to the special congregation of the cardinals of the orders.

BOLOGNA, 1 April. Yesterday evening quite late Cardinal Alberoni arrived here, accompanied by a pilgrim, took lodging at the Hotel of the Polish [?], but went instead to stay with the Senator Marquis Monti, where he is presently still residing, and it is not certain when His Eminence will continue his journey to Rome. The Herr de Chaviguy, envoy of France at Genoa, has also arrived here three days ago, but as yet the reason for his coming has not been able to be ascertained.

France

PARIS, 11 April. Last Thursday the King went out early in the morning to hunt in the park of Vincennes, being accompanied by the Misses Charolois and Rocne tur Yon in Amazon dress; the hunt being ended, His Majesty dined, which was presented to him before the hunt by the hunt-men. Much is spoken of a review of the troops, but it is not yet believed that the populace will happen so soon. The Syndic of the Sorbonne opened last week's congregation with a speech, which is deemed offensive, [one] which principally pressed the doctors to celebrate a solemn funeral mass for the repose of the soul of the deceased Pope Clement XI; and the votes being taken, they all confirmed themselves in that of the stipend, being paid, which said that this matter, outside examination, should have been rejected; the Herr la Pitie added thereto that it would have been more proper and better had it been done otherwise.

AMSTERDAM, 16 April. Captain Francisco de Farnotega has departed from here for this city, and Bernard Argus lies at Bayonne awaiting departure. The ship of Pieter Wootmie sailed from here to Svendorp. [The text becomes unclear here regarding vessel movements.] At Genoa, Jacques de [?] has arrived, and on the Weser, Hildburgh. The following vessels have put to sea: Joachim Corn., Corn. Corn. &c. to Bergen, &c. Ruyn to Kiel, Theod. Bertrand to Suriname, and la Varga to Bayonne, Corn. Jantje, Hend. Piet, and Pieter sta to London, Anke Brouwer, Jacob Rynders, Symen Jantzien &c. to Flanders, and other ships to the Strait of Davis and elsewhere. The cargo consists of 1677 Ducats, various goods, 900 Rixdollars ditto Blue, 1610 ampourie, 44,500 p. Cowles, 76,800 p. Pepper, 29,100 p. Red-s, 23,200 p. Saltpetre, and other merchandise for private [parties].

GOUDA, 17 April. The Directors and Company of the Commerce Company at Alum sitting within this city, [text unclear] also out of their negotiations of Lisbon on the 13th of this month; whereof the conditions and notes of Lisbon in the [?] by the aforementioned Book have been conveyed and received.

THE HAGUE, 17 April. It is understood that the Mr. Envoy from the King of Denmark to this State, acting as his deputy, [text becomes corrupted] the President of the General [text unclear] Commission of the King of Denmark [text unclear], a relative of the King, has been conveyed here, whereby His Majesty gives notice of the death of the Lord Straaten [?]; His Majesty [text unclear] by the aforementioned gentlemen States has been conveyed. The said Majesty [text unclear] that to the Outer Council notes &c. has already been given thereof, and the heavy [?] has arrived. The senior Father, first of this State at Brussels, has departed thither. The gentlemen Council of States have appointed the Mr. Gerard Schroucken [?] the gentlemen Council of the preceding day at 's-Hertogenbosch as member for the Freedom of the City of 's-Hertogenbosch, in place of the Lord Fredrik Hendrick Zweerts de Landas. The continued Council of the North Quarter have come here, and have been in conference with the gentlemen Continued of the Council of the South Quarter. It is expected that a courier from London with a letter from the King of Great Britain to the Mr. [text unclear] has been dispatched. The Spanish Ambassador, the Marquis de [?] has been with some members of State. Although letters from Paris suggest that some Spanish troops were being set in motion by him. Nevertheless, it is believed that this occurs for another end than to change the garrisons and make way for troops which are to be conducted in their place [text unclear] Africa without themselves. There was an express [messenger] from Britain, Minister Stanhope from Madrid to London, arrived. It is thought that a certain time for the matter of Congress is fixed to be opened in good health soon. The Lord of Eden has departed the Province of Gelderland. This morning the General Count Hompesch, in the presence of some gentlemen of Dilbug, and the Guard of the Horse have reviewed.

COLOGNE, 17 April. Yesterday in the forenoon at eleven o'clock the Cardinal Bishop of Mechelen arrived here to proceed further to Cologne: that afternoon he went, through his High Dom, the Bishop of Paderborn with the Dom Chapter at the Cologne Court to a meal entertained under the [?] of Paul &c.

The Dike Magistrate and High Regents of the Rhineland will, on Wednesday the 23 April 1721, in the morning, at the halfway point of the Spaarndammen dike, in public view the Free Air Harbor [text unclear], Chace, Weir and Windows [?], stone the Abbey [text unclear], the property and its mediation of the Rhineland at Sparendam at halfway [point].

It is announced to many that on the coming Tuesday, which will be 22 April 1721, at the most unique, high Towers [?] Sanctify, absolve and Tariffs, midshipmen an Antique Theater, a [?], Tacher and Pottan; as also some [text unclear] and [text unclear] (chapter) to see at the N.H. Wieringe, on the south side of the new Ferry-side, and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock on the [?] over the [?] and Ferry [?], Governhage.

The Court of Freedom Oetterwijk in the jurisdiction of Den Bosch, lying near Tilburg, on 15 May 1721, by authority of approbation by the Committed of her Ed. Mo. the Lord Ed. [?], dated 29 July 1718 previously, for all Men and sealed and [text unclear] should stand in haste, for himself and wife, who [?] find there at the Hotel; the second day condition is that the Copper [?] Lumality [?] shall have to provide security, the judicial [?] of the Council for one month and then the bell there at the place shall be delivered, which shall also be obliged [?] The communal [?] of the purchase of the commerce of the city Belum are requested to appear midday, at [?] hours, in Amsterdam on the Gingel in the Burg, to help deliberate about [?] to the Lovers of Flora it is given notice that on Monday 21 April 1721, in the morning, there shall be sold in their manner [?] the Hirching, Yellow, and other Crown-Imperial [flowers], with their blooming catalogues in their Play-House, lying outside the Copper Gate of the city Leiden, the Sales-day of the Bayberries, Jonquilles, Anemones, Auriculas, Citron and Lemon-Trees [?] complete, as the present Courant and the list of notes further [?] to be received at Leiden by Mr. Pieter Pla.

The Brokers F. Onkelboet, J. and D. Boniekoning will sell at Amsterdam on Monday 21 April 1721, at the Swan on the [?] in the [?]; some [?]; 3000 Copper [?], [?]; 1600 Flakkersh, 10 [?], 9 at Hamburg, 14,000 timber beams, copper beams, various beams and rib-knots, and other woodwork, etc.

Hendrik Aries and Jan de Kaastere, brokers, will sell at Amsterdam on Tuesday 22 April 1721, in the evening of 4 vats of [?], 4 vats of [?], together with a party extra Grand [?] in the Brackish Ground, a party extra wig [?] Seine-leaves.

At Leiden under the [?] and Day, on 21 April 1721 the Library of the late Lord F. de Cloux shall be sold, consisting of [?]; the catalogues are known by [?] and this Suede over 1000 old and new well-conditioned medical and other books; the catalogues are [?] The brokers J. Onkelboet, J. and D. Boniekoning will sell at Amsterdam on Monday 1 May 1721, on the N. [?] in 80 to 60 feet long; 128 pieces of [?] and curves; 132 pieces [?]m thick; 1180 green planks of 18 to 24 feet long, a plank around and by the Maid Pom. the Wild [?] and Early flowers, consisting of 250 planks; daily books and [?], of 30 to 50 feet long, and 1 half to 1 and 1 half dym thick, 1600 [?]s and 1 half dym thick, and diverse other woodwork more; lying in the Place [?] around and by.

It is announced to many that on Wednesday 22 April 1721 at Rotterdam [?] is to be publicly [sold], and [?] the broker Janus van der Leg, [?] Broker, (as if on behalf of his Principals), in April; and then at 2 o'clock, at the Burg within Leiden in the ditch [?], notes for a Life [policy] on 11 January 1722, with interest at 3 percent 3 January, on rude Lives, namely Maria Gakhart Willgemina Ambresia Kitesumken, then being of age 21 years, being the [?] itself, containing [?] Colleen, which on all half her [?] on 1 July and 11 January.

Notices & Advertisements

On Monday 21 April 1721, there shall be at Amsterdam in the Old Lord's Lodging, in the evening [?] some Gentlemen Houses, Stalling, Coaches and Wagons, newly [made], Housekeeper's dwelling, [?], at the tuition of the Fair, under [?], measuring 8 acres, and then another 7 acres [?] Capes, and to be procured through the Broker Lucas de schepper.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 18th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 48.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 21st of April, 1721
Arabian News

MOCHA, February 13th. Regarding what has occurred on this voyage of the Dutch ship Sandenbergh, bound for Ceylon: the same has endured many storms and hardships. There also lie here still 2 other Dutch ships, namely the ship Rotterdam before the Cape, and the ship Leugtenburg for the Rotterdam Chamber, together with 20 English, French, Portuguese and Moorish ships. The aforementioned ship Sandenbergh, having been restored of all things, shall depart hence for Ceylon, and the 2 ships Leugtenburg and Rotterdam, to depart for the Strait of Gaalen; as some English and French ships shall also do shortly.

Sandíaz

CADIZ, March 22nd. All the remonstrances from the owners of the 2 Buenos Aires ships having likewise proven fruitless, so that all the silver must be brought to the mints.
