From the silver arrived from Vera Cruz, new struck specie has been distributed to diverse private individuals who have made use here of the mint, which is to be likewise distributed to other owners in turn, without yet knowing whether the weight of the new specie shall differ from the old. We have received advice from Madrid to the Commerce College, requesting from the same to lend to this King one and a half million pieces of silver, for which they have already placed obligations against 3 per cent interest until amortization; however the Commerce Council has not yet convened for this purpose. Notwithstanding the galleons are ready to depart, it is said there are almost no loaders, so that it remains yet uncertain when they shall put to sea. The warship of 50 guns that must go to Havana lies still in this bay by contrary winds; also it is testified from the advice from Vera Cruz that she should have passed the bar. The ships of Pieter Fuyk and Klaas Hoofi, bound from Italy to Amsterdam, lie still before the Bay, without being able to enter. Thus situated, the Hope capt. Boelman of the Canaries, the 3 Dutch warships with the merchant vessels Gerrit Stadegaart, Cornelis Viller, Corn. Martens, and Gerrit Schaap, all of Amsterdam, the Jonsfie capt. van Hoiten of Hamburg; the Grillo capt. George Silinger from Ireland, and the Richard Jean Francis Rich. of Plymouth; and there departed the Jacob Philip Jones to Dublin, the Isabella Sam. Torrington to Naples, the Packet John Smith and the Mery Tom. Poke to London, and there arrived the Anna Maria Wigman of Dunkirk from Barcelona; so that there still lie here one Hamburg ship, 22 English, 12 French, 7 Dutch, 2 German and 2 Genoese ships.

Madrid

MADRID, April 13th. On the last Tuesday, the feast of the Annunciation of Mary, the King and Queen heard Mass in the Church of Saint Hieronymus; and heard the sermon delivered by Father Petrus d'Equirola, of the Order of Saint Francis, ordinary preacher to the King; in place of the same there remained the Cardinal Belga, and having taken formal leave of the King and the Prince, he has since departed to his bishopric of Murcia, to observe the church service of Holy Thursday. The King has given the Commandery of Zagra and Zentis, of the Order of Saint James, to the Field Marshal Count of Zuebesgtken; the Lieutenant Colonel's post in the [?] and his [?] to the Captain of the Grenadiers Don Pietro Posaifagna; and that of the Infantry of Tuscany to the reformed Lieutenant Colonel Don Juan de Seller. His Majesty has had Don Alonso Tavalya, who has recovered from his illness, resume his offices in the Council of the Indies: the King has entrusted the posts of [?] and [?] in the Council to the Marquis of Almodar; and the Deaconry of the Church of Granada to Don Melchior Hertera Flores, Archbishop of that same Church, which Archdeaconry has been given to D. Nicolas de Silya.

A few days ago the dish at the Ambassador's table was a butter cake, which was poured over with a sauce of honey, saffron and other spices; the second was a meat pie and a common pastry; and the third course consisted of diverse small dishes, of which among others one with pilaf was [served], without further complaint. The young Celebi is experienced in mathematics and literature, and since he is second secretary to the Grand Vizier, he has had much difficulty obtaining freedom to make this journey; he is being instructed in the French language by a member of the Academy of Sciences, but not without great difficulty, because there is scarcely any correspondence between the French and Turkish languages: the Ambassador himself was formerly Aga over 500 Janissaries, but is presently in the finances as Tessterdar, or High Treasurer of the Turkish Empire; and since he is a very talkative man, he bears the name of Celebi or courteous. His son has no elevated place nor [?] in his apartment, but the Ambassador well knows how to maintain his gravity, both in receiving viziers as outside. One sees an ordinance from the King, by which the construction of 4 new guard houses is commanded, to prevent the gathering together of lackeys, tradesmen, and idlers. Many people gather daily here, and it is feared that such will still increase, if the heat continues.

The letters from Marseille of the 3rd of the previous month, say that health there increases more and more, and that those who had hitherto remained at its country estates all come back again to that city. The renewal of the Company of the Indies brings general consternation in the great cities; the session of the Parliament of the Saints at Lyon is postponed to May 19th; the Parliament of the King, and the debates which were wont to take place on Easter are behind schedule. They have traced [stolen money] by the gold louis, and by another [case] for 25,000 livres. The council meeting over the renewal of the Company of the Indies lasted until about 9 o'clock in the evening, and the hall of the court was crowded with people; the new Company, which comes forward in a respectable form, shall profit for a time from the remnants of the previous, and the gentlemen Samuel Bernard, Croizai, Paris, and some prominent men at Saint Malo, shall have charge of the same.

France

PARIS, April 14th. According to letters of the first of this month from Madrid, in Spain they were busily engaged in bringing all the regiments to full strength; and there was even talk of whether they would resume the expedition against the Emperor of Morocco, as soon as the season had advanced favorably: but other letters say that one should be content first to make all the regiments complete, and incorporate the weakened ones, which have suffered much in Africa, into other regiments. The Marquis de Leede, who continued his conferences daily with the King and the ministers of the court, was first to review all the troops in the Spanish garrisons. It is observed that all those who had a hand in the Mississippi finances now meet with little recognition for their alleged services, when they rely in some lawsuit or court proceeding on the acknowledgment of others; [witness] the sentence pronounced against the Lord Panier d'Orgeville, Master of Requests, who has been condemned to pay to Madame de St. Pont, widow of the Attorney General before the Grand Council, the sum of 170 thousand livres in specie, and this for his office of Master of Requests, which he had bought from her for 18 months to pay the bailiffs, which payment then did not succeed because her creditors opposed it at that time; besides which it is to be noted that such offices in the most flourishing times have not been sold for more than 60 thousand livres. It is said that Lord Law has had himself assured [?], and also the sum of 12 millions which he would have in England; also it is assured that he holds an equal sum in the banks of Venice, Genoa, in Holland and in Germany. The arrangement of the paper effects consists in two separate points; by the first the Company is liquidated and also the Bank destroyed, and in order to relieve the shareholders who have acted in good faith, a new Company is established to restore their loss as much as possible. With the examination of the paper it is now necessary to proceed, and now it is not easy to overlook the conduct and dealings of some English Mississippi speculators, who do not even shrink from threatening it. The court, taking the state of affairs in Provence very much to heart, has ordered the Hôtel de Paris to send 2 millions in specie to the cities, which have been most affected by the pestilential disease. The Marquis of Beringben, first Equerry to the King, has obtained the survivorship of his office; [?] his son, together with the government of the citadel and fortifications of Marseille. Much is heard here of suspicious deaths, and many hasten their end by gluttony, which is very common here: the sicknesses increase daily; but [they] diminish in Provence. One has posted malicious notices found in diverse places of this city, that all those who had some business to settle with the office of [?] and Lamrufin, could address themselves in the archiepiscopal palace here at the [?], and that those who saw concerning the religion in some doubts arising, should have reported themselves to the Lord de [?], general of the police. One sees widely printed here the interrogations of the Abbé d'Asfeldt, of which much is made: if the Pope had still lived, the same would certainly have been sent to Rome, in which case one from others would have expected something other than the non-arrival of excommunication on one side against the appellants, and on the other the [?] of 2 cardinals and changes before the court. The King had forbidden the debates at a halt.

One sees 2 articles of the King's council of state, both of the 7th of this month; the first rejects the opposition stipulated by the Company of the Indies in execution of the article of January 21st last, and orders that the same shall be bound to give accounting of all receipts and expenses, both regarding the Company as against the director thereof attached. By the second the lords Trudaine, Fagon, Ferrand and Machault, councillors of state, are appointed to draw up a process-verbal, the registers, papers and effects of the Company, as well as those of the Bank to be examined. One no longer sees the matters of the second constitution with the same eyes as before; the matters are exhausted of, the business of the King's chapel, and one receives news of the employments which they held before [?], and that they have appealed from the accommodation; the King was at the Capuchins at the [?]. The operation on the fistula of the Cardinal de Medelli did not succeed well despite the intervention of the King's chief surgeon, who supervised the said operation; the operation took place without the ceremony, and they will be forced to undertake the operation again, to which his eminence has consented. And [?] the well most noble blood, in service of the youngest ambassador, a young girl having sold [?], is before the council of his excellency to be [examined] and condemned: to great expense certainly will be [incurred], and the said ambassador, although being dismissed, to be and on my account remain: the mentioned ambassador, who through this profit is honored, keeps a table, for which k cooks are [maintained]; the midday meal is held by him, and it consists of the table spoiled with privies, at the second his officers, at the head of whom is his steward and [?]; the remainder of the said retinue one at the third table.

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 17 April. The Countess of Windischgrätz departed from here yesterday in a fort-chaise; 300 [soldiers?] wish to go to correct the Castle of Brugelm, and others believe [she is going] to Mons. Mr. Jeffers arrived here yesterday evening from The Hague. The Regiment of Bonneval, which has been in garrison here, departed this week, and the Regiment of Württemberg from Charleroi is expected to take their place; with the garrison [?] others have continued here through this month.

AMSTERDAM, 13 April. The aforementioned Spanish ships, which leaked 30 miles below the Spanish St. Domingo waters and sailed from there to Vera Cruz, are 2 of those brought from Basiliero, and one from Havana. Further letters from Sheepmen, [?] Ferreri & Co., [arrived] at the end of March from the shipping [vessels].

Via Livorno, one received letters from 7 March from Algiers, which reported that there is no collerie there. From Tripoli well [?] leaked, and shipmasters [?]. In [?] have arrived within [?] Valkereis, Gerriet Buys and Getr. Colombpzn from Cadiz, etc.

AMSTERDAM, 13 April. According to letters from Gibraltar, the ship Moscovite Galley, Captain Jacob Santvoert, entered on 13 March, and on the 17th the Neptune, Captain Kirn Kynslie; both are to proceed to Livorno and Smyrna; said two ships experienced such heavy weather that Captain Kirn Kynslie was forced to throw overboard part of his cargo and sea, and [is] again nearly [?] around; if the wind remains good, that evening or the following day [they will] depart for Livorno and Smyrna.

At Bordeaux have arrived the Miss. Hr. [?] Will. de Jong, and the guilds Floeg Harmen Pieterse from the Baltic in the last [?]. In Poland at Keikin, [?] by Blaet and Kurafau, having departed from there Mar. [traveling] incognito, to visit the Bishop of Munster and Paderborn.

The Magistrate of the City of Breda, having investigated that in the previous newspapers certain cloth [?] was found by merchants [?] not to be as glazed as [?] to the city it brought; therefore the aforementioned Magistrate here [?], [?] examination is to be conducted, and to provide a remedy for the future so that the cloth here shall be such glazed [?] as the lot with the city's scales shall bear witness [and?] to present; a very neat instruction or ordinance [?] for [?] both sides [?], which [?] also with the print [?] commonly made [?].

---

Advertisements

The Directors and Principal Shareholders of the Commerce and Insurance Company in Gouda hereby advertise that they have continued their negotiation of the Life Annuities until the 1st of May of this year 1721, and no longer; and that to that end the aforesaid 1st of May will be the final day to come, namely in the morning; are out of the negotiation for the poor company, [commencing] the 18th of October, announced the 9th of October; regarding this for the settled 1st of May they must declare.

The Directors and Principal Shareholders of the Commerce and Insurance Company in Gouda hereby advertise to all those who have taken entry in the negotiation of the aforesaid terms to be paid; that they must pay the same without burden utterly by the 24th of May of this year 1721, or else they, in accordance with and by force of the 1st Article of the conditions on which the aforesaid negotiation was published, from the entry payment and stated furnishing monies they shall be forfeited.

An auction at [?] on Tuesday, being 22 April 1721, will be sold to the highest bidder a quantity of fine hyacinths, early tulips, auriculas, anemones and narcissi, likewise an articulated theater, a large leaden statue, some gilded pedestals; [?] and [?]; as well as diverse melancholy chairs and tournament cloaks by [?] in T. Wieterij, on the outside of the new Ferry, and in the afternoon 1 and 2 o'clock opposite the Flowers in The Hague.

Pieter Meleemdeen shall sell again on Wednesday the 23 April 1721, in the Old Lord's Lodging, a quantity of cloths of all sorts, domestic and foreign; silk, half-silk and wool manufactures; men's coffins; diverse sorts of chiffons, and among them silk chiffons and [?], Malmsey with gold flowers, satin fabrics with gold flowers, gold brocades, Allepuzelles, and silks with gold and silver stripes, [?] and other stripes, satin linen, [?] and cornelian, [?] linen, baffas, saleganes, cashmere Bengal, agate porcelain, extra fine [?] items and rarities, a quantity of Pedro Cordial, [?] with gold buttons, tea, snuff tobacco, household goods, made clothes, a carved cabinet without equal, which by right could be placed in the chamber of a king or prince, as well as some gold, silver and other medals: all viewable the day before the sale.

Pieter de Lange, T. de Harde and W. Otterbeck, brokers, will sell in Amsterdam, on Thursday the 24 April 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, in the Kalverstraat in the Kaiser's Crown, a cabinet of 1718 and 1719 extra curious Mosel wines.

At Leiden on the [?] on the [?] shall be sold on 25 April 1721 the library of the late Mr. P. du Cloux, consisting of very rare old and new well-conditioned medical and other books: catalogues available; the catalogues are to be obtained at [?] and in other places.

Jan and Jacob de Bruyn, brokers, will sell in Amsterdam on Friday the 25 April 1721 at 6 o'clock in the evening, in the Nes at the Broken Ground, a quantity of extra fine [?] of 26 [?] and 12 hogsheads of makers' sumac, and 42 bales of Madeira [?]; lying on a schuyt in the Herengracht by My Glass runs fast.

The broker F. Onkelboer, J. and D. Bonrekoning will sell in Amsterdam, on Monday the 5 May 1721, on the Nieuwendyk in the White Seevau; a quantity of timber, consisting of 217 whole planks, from 15 to [?] flanks and beams, from 20 to 50 feet long, 216 corner knees and curves; 247 corner flanks and beams, from 20 to 50 feet long, one and 1.5 [?]; and 1.5 inches thick; 1 to 2.5 inches thick, 1700 great planks; from 18 to 45 feet long, and various other wooden goods more; lying in the plantation around and by the Muyder, [?].

Eijak Louch je Faber, broker, shall on Wednesday the 7 May 1721 and following days, in Amsterdam in the Old Lord's Lodging, sell a quantity of East Indian merchandise, consisting of Tonkin lacquers, the cabinet-quality chiffons, with white as well as other grounds and net linens, together with a quantity of uncommon stiff Japanese and Chinese porcelain, and many other goods more; [to be] viewable the day before the sale.

Joh. Hugienbuim, renowned battle painter in The Hague, makes known to all curious lovers of painting and histories that he has in stock les Batailles gagnees par le Sérénissime Prince Fr. Eugene de Savoie, the painted works & engraved in copper engraving, with explanations by M. Du Mont, Counselor of His Imperial and Catholic Majesty; the same is dedicated to the aforementioned Prince; and the Princes are made after the paintings in the large, for His Highness [?] in the large so [?] in the large painted by the [?] house Harenburg; this work is printed on large atlas paper, and are to be obtained in The Hague by P. duffoetin, in Amsterdam [?] and Joh. Arnold, Langtijk, Dort van Braam, Rotterdam, etc.

of [?] Dissertation on Prophetic Theology [?] perpetual in Church [?], in 4to and in long folio, concerning [?] the light of Magnus, the first [?] and the same chapter of Matthew, by Arcadius Kaim.

Printed at Leiden by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedestraat. 23 April 1721. With Privilege.

---

Vienna

VIENNA, 9 April. Whereas one has news here that the king of Poland from Saxony has arrived at Warsaw, and he meanwhile here with new arrangements [?] at the Imperial [?]. The Count von Flemming, the envoy, has arrived; likewise also an express [messenger] with dispatches from Erdödy sent forth; subsequently also a courier with important dispatches from the court of the King of Portugal. Count Kinsky has not yet [?]; on Monday [he] departed for Rome, and the young Count [?] on Monday departed for Rome. It is pleasantly noted that the election of the Pope came to fall upon Cardinal Pignalelti, Bishop of Camerino, as good East Indian news. In the last held consistory medicine is believed that here the Emperor [?] might be this spring going to the Carlsbad, for which everything is being prepared. The field marshal Baron von [?] in the year [?] of old age died.

---

Regensburg

REGENSBURG, 14 April. According to the latest missive of Mr. von Reck, [there was] held by the Protestant [?] over the field here presented to the Protestants on the 13th of the previous month, the Protestants who did not wish to kneel being forced to do so by the [?]; whereupon the Elector [?] is informed that directly [?] regarding his orders: and not [?] it seems there one wishes to murder the grievances rather than do [?]. The corps, assumed [?] said Mr. von Reck still is judged [?], must assure [?] the Augsburg-Master after sufficient [?], [?] the Prussian religion, which the longer the more [?]. The answer of the [?] of his [?] is here publicly become accustomed, while there [?] we imperial writ without compulsion thereto directed, as [?] count, under [?] of court [?] to make odious; [?] to see, to save diversion. One cannot [?] of [?] yet [?]; to give, which no [?] of the simple [?] ordinance states, from [?] [?] to [?] [?] [?]. What further in the [?] this in [?], [?] the patience the opening of the new [?] from the 21st [?].

---

Frankfurt

FRANKFURT, 16 April. Whilst the fair approaching Monday will be held, one is preparing here against the entry of persons and goods of diverse nations.

---

Hamburg

HAMBURG, 18 April. [The college of] the soldier's bond having prevented [?], since there [?] there, regarding which one what aid, has the King [?] of the N. [?] in place of these [?] city his Mr. Nicolas Stampe.

---

Düsseldorf

DÜSSELDORF, 18 April. The Counts of Nelleroth and Frankenstein have arrived here from Mannheim, to settle the dispute [?] and mountains [?].

---

Cologne

COLOGNE, 14 April. Tuesday morning the Cardinal archbishop of Mechelen departed from here toward Rome; and against [this] the Prince of la Tour and Taxis from Brabant has arrived here, and that forenoon he came to Cologne [?] His [?] to travel incognito, to visit the Bishop of Munster and Paderborn.

Notices & Advertisements

At Leiden by S. Luchtmans shall on 28 and 29 April 1721 be sold the newly released library of the late Fridericus Dokken, in his life medicine doctor and professor in the Academy at Leiden; consisting of [?]; catalogues are to be obtained in Dordrecht by van Braam, Rotterdam widow [?] and J. D. Bemans, Delft A. Bemans, Amsterdam, and other cities. By the aforesaid S. Luchtmans is printed Vol. I.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 21st of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 49.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 23rd of April, 1721

GERMANY AND NEIGHBOURING KINGDOMS

VIENNA, 9 April. The departure of Her Imperial Majesty to Luxembourg has been postponed to the 26th of this month, and the Empress to the spa at Karlsbad on the 12th of the following month. For some days now there has been talk here as if negotiations at Cologne with Saxony are about to commence in the Conclave; and that the Count of Windischgrätz, President of the Austrian Council, shall become Imperial Principal Commissioner at the Assembly of Envoys. The King of Poland has sent an Envoy to learn reliable information concerning the Turk's future intentions regarding war, of which high envoys have arrived at Vienna from his Imperial Minister at Constantinople, with the report that the Poles are to cross the Danube in order, according to the plan agreed upon with the Cossacks and Count Berezin, to march toward the borders of the Republic: but this news is not believed at this Court, because His X.M.'s Minister at Constantinople makes no mention of it whatsoever. Meanwhile, the Venetian Doge has ordered old fortifications near Sevenbergen to be inspected, and a watchful eye to be kept on Turkish movements.

REGENSBURG, 14 April. The courteous Reply which the Protestant Corps directed to the Duke of Treviours, requesting him to remedy all the grievances since the Peace of Westphalia in that Principality, has been received with approval by the voices of all the Princes of the Imperial Diet; this has been transmitted to the Duke in the name of the aforementioned Corps, and there is no doubt that he will be satisfied with the request, especially as the entire Corps is becoming increasingly orderly due to the lack of instructions for their Plenipotentiaries; whose recently transmitted Reports from the Palatinate concerning what was concluded there in his presence and what will be written by him, shall come out with the Press any day or tomorrow; from him much other and more ample Information is still expected: meanwhile, the attending Envoy from Hamburg, at the request of the Protestant Corps, has undertaken to write to His Britannic Majesty that the aforesaid Mr. von Reck shall for now remain in Paris, where he was still needed, until it shall be learned whether the assurance of the Envoy at this Court [from the Duke], that everything shall be redressed to his Master's satisfaction, shall take effect, or whether the Protestant Corps shall be able to obtain proportionate measures in religious matters, which are becoming increasingly disputed.

FRANCE

PÉZENAS, 10 March. The Diocesan sickness has newly appeared again in certain places of Provence, where it had been believed the plague had been completely overcome. In this city also a very great number have swelled, and in one house five people have died within a short time; however, the Government has caused the cause of their death to be examined most carefully, and upon opening the corpses, it was found that all five had died of the Plague. The Baron of Roquillane, having been officially informed by the Government, was not satisfied with the common declarations of our Doctors and Surgeons, wherefore he sought information; they for their part were unwilling to have any communication on any terms with these afflicted persons, and did not enter the city until they were assured that certain suspect individuals had been confined in the general Lazarhouse, which they inspected in all respects. Here and there one remains exceedingly vigilant to learn of anything that might bring some new virulence, and so clearly is the attention of the Court in the other provinces of this Kingdom engaged.

MONTPELLIER, 2 April. After the Gout, from which a certain Bishop had long suffered in considerable measure, had left him, within a few days he was seized by a severe Colic in the stomach, and indeed so violent that fears were held for his life; but that malady, to which he is frequently subject, having passed over, he finds himself in reasonable health. A beginning is being made here to follow the ecclesiastical zeal of those in Paris in the matter of appealing against the Accommodation; many Canons of our Cathedral Church, Curates, Priests of the Oratory, and other Clergy have signed an Act of adherence to the pending Appeal of our Bishop and three others, to a general Council. The Syndic General of the States of Languedoc died during the night between the 27th and 28th of the preceding month, being much beloved by the community on account of the great services which he had rendered the Province for many years; and his office is now filled by his second Son, because his eldest assumed the ecclesiastical habit eight or ten years ago.

MONTPELLIER, 7 April. The pestilential sickness reigning in Provence shows very different manifestations; and at present all places are unaffected, with the exception only of Toulon, where the sickness still makes itself somewhat felt.

PARIS, 15 April. Last Sunday, the first of Easter, the King confessed to his Grand-father the Abbot Fleury and afterward attended the great Musical Mass, which was celebrated by the Archbishop of Albi; in the afternoon His Majesty heard the Sermon of the Abbot [?] and likewise Vespers, which was also conducted by the Priests; and after the King had performed his devotions, His Majesty rode out, to take the air, with the Marshal of Villeroy toward the Elysian Fields. On Monday the King went, accompanied by the aforesaid Marshal his Governor, to the Convent of the Virgin Mary, where His Majesty assisted at the Church services; and the King, having made himself agreeable to the Sisters of that religious community, assured them of his protection; after which the King amused himself by riding out. Yesterday His Majesty went with the Duke of Villeroy to Vincennes for fowling. One sees an Edict of the King's Council of State of the [?] of this month, concerning Bank Defaults and the accounts of parties involved. There has been the greatest wonder here to learn that the Princess of Modena with the Prince her Husband have both most unexpectedly withdrawn themselves from the territory of the Duke of Modena, and first traveled to Strasbourg; and notwithstanding the orders sent by the Duke of Orleans to them to return to Modena, they have nevertheless traveled to Lunéville in Lorraine, where they have already arrived; it is not certain whether the Duke Regent, her Father, will permit them to come hither: but it is assured that the aforesaid Princess declared she would never return to Italy. If the reduction of the Troops continues, as some still wish to contest, it has been established that the same shall consist in the strength of twenty Men per Company of Foot and fifteen Men per Company of Horse; the reformed Officers, who were counted on half pay, shall be reduced to one quarter. There is talk of reducing the wages of the Officers of the Household [?] to one third. Great preparations are being made for the holding of the Ball, at which the King will also entertain the Turkish Ambassador. A certain Officer who caused himself to be called Mustapha Aga, and claims to be of the retinue of the Turkish Emperor's palace, who had been in the service of the Republic of Venice and in His Majesty's Troops, and through his merits had been honored with the Cross of St. Louis, with permission to have it displayed in his house, came these days to the lodgings of the Turkish Ambassador to pay him a visit, and the aforesaid Minister would not even look at him, and had him told that he well knew what respect he owed to King Louis the Fifth [Editor's note: likely Louis XV], otherwise he would have him thrown out the window; the Turkish Ambassador complained about him at Court, and it has been deemed proper to have this presumptuous Sultan sent away to Pierre Ancile. The King has given to the Marquis de Gondrin, grandson of the Duke d'Antin, thirteen years old, the Reversion of the Superintendency of the Ships of His Majesty, which office his Grandfather holds. The Duke of Bourbon is at Chantilly most dangerously ill of a Pleurisy, which seized him on the road from Anet; he is permitted to leave this place, and finds himself in great danger for his life. Some time ago the Turkish Ambassador was received in audience by the Duke Regent, after which he paid his respects to the Archbishop of Cambrai. The common Minister was entertained with some Preserves, Confections and Liqueurs. The Knight de Beringen has come from Flanders to this city, and is about to depart to his Regiment in the Limoges. The Lord de Chamaillard, who in the time of the deceased King Louis the Fourteenth had caused himself to be much discussed in financial matters, died last Monday. According to Spanish reports, orders have been sent to Cadiz to make all possible speed with the Infanta in the Galleons, since the King of Spain wishes that the aforesaid Ships, whether with or without cargo, are to depart toward New Spain by the middle of this month. It is rumored that a great Promotion among the Spanish Officers in Phalachen shall take place, and one saw an unofficial notice that the British Minister Stanhope has not for some time been received in a friendly manner by the Spanish Ministers, as well as before. P.S. Although the Duke Regent sent the Abbot Philbert to Lunéville to persuade the Prince and Princess of Modena to return to Italy, it has nevertheless proved fruitless, and reliable advices assure that they shall shortly come to this city, and will take their residence in the Palace of Luxembourg, as the Margravine of Hanover [does].

PARIS, 14 April. The house being [text incomplete in source]

The Universities are eager to depart for America with speed. The trade in Paper Securities continues to grow ever more favorably. The administration of the new Company proceeds apace; one also sees a plan of the new Company with no greater certainty than in previous arrangements. It is expected that within a few days a new Decree will be published, containing articles that will be binding. Last Tuesday evening there was a violent windstorm here, by which many chimneys were blown off, trees uprooted from the ground, and windows broken. [?] still possesses great vigor and has relocated to the Canals. Some days ago there was a rumor here that a Swiss Baron of The Hague's son intends to marry a lady of the March family. It is said that the Treaty of the Abbé d'Aveld, the Bishop, will take effect in the coming [?], regarding the opportunity for [?], requiring the cooperation of priests from their dioceses and the entire clergy from their spiritual direction. Some believe that the Syndic of the Sorbonne hangs over a sharp precipice; and certain sharp expedients, which were employed in April, have gone so far as to potentially cause damage to the [?]; and all are of the opinion, and openly maintain, that it was his right and he had reason enough to go further still. The King has rejected the order of mourning from the Queen of [?] on account of a difference in ceremony between the two Crowns; but the House of Orleans and a [?] of the House [?] have done so. Much is spoken here of the forthcoming arrival of the Prince and Princess of Modena; it is said that neither the air nor the manner of living agrees with the departure of the Princess; and although some believe this is merely a pretext to return to France, others assure that preparations are being made, and [?] to receive these Highnesses here, who will lodge in that part of the Luxembourg Palace which is presently unused. It is reported that this Princess is provided with all necessities in the most generous manner, and that she possesses very many jewels and precious stones. Regarding the accounts of the Bank, which the Company must render in accordance with the Decree of the 27th of the month, the Syndics have demanded a written accounting from the Regent Duke of the 26 million [livres], which were withdrawn from the treasury by his order; and some add that His Highness, on account of the unfortunate turn of affairs, greatly desires the King's clemency; while others believe that the Prince will obtain, through negotiation, said clemency by means of a Declaration and request, so that he may thus be recompensed and thanked by the King for his administration. It is reported that Mr. Law has surrendered more than 16 million [livres] in lands or fixed properties to the Crown. There is a strong rumor that the Duke of Chartres will go to lodge with his aunt at the Bank. The Regiment of Champagne has departed these days.

The representatives of the Free Lordship of Oost-Weyck in the Meuse region near Tilburg, presented on the 20th of June 1721 with the approval of the Commissioners of their Excellencies, [announce that] all men who wish to sell goods there may present themselves from that date onward; the conditions are that the buyer must provide sureties, [and that] the judicial [delivery] and the Clock at that place will be delivered within [?] month from date; and also [?] The sale of the Commerce Company at Edam is postponed until [?]

Johannes Staats, Jan Lemmers, and Hend. Liguet, brokers, shall sell in Amsterdam at [?], in the night in the Black Ground, a parcel of 141 packages of Muscovite [?] and Muscovite Linens and Grey Stuffs, 30 packages of fine Yarns, 15 packages of Beaver Yarns, and also to be viewed the day before.

Jan Pietersse Zomeo, broker, shall sell in Amsterdam on Friday the 25th April 1721, the paintings of the deceased Dirk Valkenburg, with all his painting equipment [?]; the catalogue [is available] three days, three hours from J. F. Zeemer, Gyabert Hol, and van Leeuwen: they will be sold on the long Linen-street, the [?]th house on the Linen-street: [?]

The brokers Joh. and Dirk Bontekoning shall sell outside Amsterdam on Saturday at the Poot, the cool-named, at the Comb- and Saw-mills Poot, all of a parcel of Hamburg, Rice, and Nordic beams and planks, and a parcel of [?]

The brokers P. Onkelboer, J., and D. Bontekoning shall sell in Amsterdam on May Swan, a parcel of wood goods, consisting of 2300 Christian, Fredrik Königiberger pieces and Truffle planks, 150 Zwego smooth Hamburg beams, a parcel of Spruce, [?], Measuring-beams, Bartlonas, and other wood goods, etc.

On the 19th of April, one shall [?] in the book collection of Johannes vander Linden, and certain unbound books, consisting of exclusive theological, juridical, [?] Latin, antiquarian, lexicographical, and other miscellaneous works. The catalogue is to be obtained [at?] [?], and Delft [?], Rotterdam [?] Wed. N. Bos and Bieman, and in the principal booksellers of foreign and domestic cities.

The guardians of the minor children of the late Johannes van Nossem, and proprietor of Woerdijk, on the 21st of May 1721, by public auction shall sell [?] Smiths' Shop [?] the same house with 90 years [?] much success done the Smithery or the Smith [?]

The [?] F. Duburgh, J., and D. Bortekoning, shall sell in Amsterdam [?] the White Swan, a parcel of wood goods, consisting of 237 corner beams, of 9 to [?] planks and birch wood, of 20 to 50 feet long, [?] and 1 and a half to 5 and a half inches thick, [?] and 1 and a half inches thick, and all three wood goods more; lying in the Plantation [?]

Alfred Jacobus Albertus, surgeon at Zoetermeer, or properly the jurisdiction [?]; the widow will sell in the house at the shop, which in the same house is [?]; anyone disposed finds [?] to direct himself to the aforesaid widow at Zoetermeer, or at the [?] Leyden printed by FELIX DE KLOP over the Town Hall. The 23rd of April 1721.

---

AMSTERDAM, the 21st of April. It is the ship Schoonenberg which entered Mocha on the 23rd of June, and on the 15th of August, together with the ships Rotterdam and Lugtenburg, departed thence again, intending to go to Frede-burgh. In 9 days, 1000 English drovers had arrived on the river of Lisbon, and among them 16 or 18 from London; the Fidelity ships have arrived toward Rie (or Plata) [?] the 5th, but yet still on the same river, and [?] 200, it was said only after those ships from Danzig [?], because the East Indian ship had already sailed. At Danzig several ships had arrived, and among them Pieter Geerts from here, Jan Glassemeester from Lübeck, Zacharias Futs from Stettin, Hans Jurgens from Copenhagen, etc.; at Königsberg Pieter Thomsons, etc., Woutes Falies from Bordeaux, and Mathias Burmeester from Danzig; at Gutenberg the Hope, Zacharias Müller from Nantes, the Johanna, skipper Peteren from the East Sea, though Jaaft Berggen and the Maria, Captain Robpion, with grain from England; at Hamburg two recently sailed ships from Lisbon, and more others at Bolton the W. and Maria, Captain Perimae, the Fort Merchant, Captain Marsvelt, both from Port à Port, and the Thomas from Lübeck; on the river of London the Spanish ships, the St. Joseph, Nicolaas del Barco, and the St. Laur., Juan del Barco, both from Bilbao; the captains François Willems and Ursio from Brünn, James Omer from Stockholm; the Haven, Jaad and Govee Gallis from Danzig. From Königsberg, 7 or 8 ships have departed, and among them Siwert Imkes and Klaas Jonket have undergone [?]. At Stockholm lay sailed Benjamin & Bro from Danzig, Mathias Stroman toward Stockholm, Jacob de Ruyter, Corn. Boender, etc., toward here: also on the fore-said Friday the Greenland whalers had set out on the Elbe. The ships of Mantuan the Veide, Corn. Baame, Jan Hend., etc., from here toward Rochelle and Bordeaux, are bound, have on the 15th of this month passed the Heads in good condition. Likewise some of the East India ships lying in Delft have set out to sea, as also various ships bound for France, etc.

The Lords States General have appointed the Colonel Verschuur to the command of the Citadel at Namur, in place of the deceased Brigadier Baron van Rijpperda. Yesterday was the Foreign Minister the Mr. Grys with the Lords of the government. The sixth class of the Holland Lottery having ended, one shall renew a drawing in the sixth class of the great cannery until the last of May next coming, the drawing of the 7th class to be certainly on Monday the 9th of June accordingly.

[Multiple auction and commercial notices for Amsterdam and surrounding towns:]

---The representatives of the Lordship of den Bosch, near Tilburg, are of the opinion to be able to sell the same lordship, moving from their Excellency's, the Lords Counselors of State, on the request to their [?] [to] sell a large fine market ox, weighing 1456 pounds, which going the high [?] shall be sold the other day thereof, and the price [?], the judicial [judgment] of the Council at the Leenhove of Brabant [subject; that the [?], who shall also be helped with the [?] to satisfy there.

[?] shall in Amsterdam sell on Thursday the 24th of April 1721, in the evening: [?] packages of Muscovite [?], 8 packages of Siberian [?], 10 barrels of [?], [?]; 15 packages of Beaver skins, bear hides, and other furs in [?]

[?] on Friday the 25th of April 1721, the bequeathed household goods, together with the extra future painters' equipment, models, drawings, and prints; in [?] in oil and by van Leeuwen; the household goods shall in the morning at 9 o'clock in the aforesaid street [?] the [?] service every day before at [?].

[?] Amsterdam on Saturday the 26th of April 1721, at the mills of Monsieur Adriaan Poort, shall sell all manner of sorts of sawn and unsawn wood goods, as: [?], Spanish, Barksawn, and other wood goods more.

[?] Amsterdam on Monday the 28th of April 1721, on the Nieuwedijk in the White [?], [?], [?], [?] and [?] pieces; good Hamburg beams, 1000 stock-beams and fire beams, and [?] wood goods, etc.

---At three o'clock precisely, at Geertruidenberg, at the house of Jan Condonel, shall sell 2 houses, with an extended and well-situated garden behind, and further [?], all bequeathed by the late Miss Anna Habella van Calthorn.

---[?] Johannes vander Linden, shall sell all the same well-conditioned [?] [?], juridical, botanical, medical, philosophical, historical, ancient Greek [?] is to be obtained at Amsterdam by H. Sink and J. Boom, The Hague [?] N. Bos and Bieman, Dort [?], Gouda [?], Utrecht [?]; [?] booksellers

[?] by public auction shall sell a house and land, together with the equipment for [?] dwelling on the Jodenbreestraat; the establishment of smithery or the smith [?] smithery or the smith [?]

---At Amsterdam shall sell, on Monday the 7th of May 1721, on the Nieuwedijk in [?] beams, of 40 feet long; 216 corner knees and crooked [?]; 317 corner and [?] in the plantation and by the Muiden gate.

---At Amsterdam, on the Spiegelstraat in the White Sun, makes known that by working by would still some other [?] to cause, [?] may be used; being conditioned according to all duties and [?] in an easy manner heals all sorts of Venus-sickness and [?] from intercourse with people needs to abstain; [?] [?] [?], which prevents [?], for such have I in my household [?]; and so that each of the aforesaid medicine [?] shall be instructed, and their healing with all [?], provided they [?] me not [?] shall give; so that their [?]

[?] shall be instructed, and their healing with all [?]; wealthy people with some [?] [?] have.

[?] [?] [?] jurisdiction of Zegwaard, has come to die, presented [?] house [?] with very good success has been done and still [?]; [?] Zegwaard, or Leyden by Matthys Alderkerk, Mr. Surgeon.

THE CLOP PRINTER, on the Bredelstraat, the 13th of April 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The EXCHANGE RATE is today: Madrid 89 and a half. Venice 51 and a half. Seville 11. Bilbao 90. Lisbon 93 and 3 halves. Venice 34. Livorno 93 and 1 half. Genoa 92. [?] sight 42. London 33 Sch. 1. Leipzig current 38 and 3 quarters St. Frankfurt current 36 Reichl. Leipzig at Polish [?]. Berlin 8 and 3 reaching St. Hamburg 32 and 9 selling St. Antwerp 2 and 3 quarters per C. Ghent 2 and 3 halves. Brussels 2 and 3 quarters. Zealand 1. Rotterdam 2 quarters. The Bank shares at 1 [?]. The SHARES of the E. I. Company 279. W. I. C. 100 to 101. Subscription 370 to 9to. The English Bank 142, ditto E. I. C. 145, and the [?] 145 and 146, all cash.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 19th of April 1721, in the afternoon at three o'clock precisely, at [?], a house [?] with furnishings, [?] accommodations for 6 horses, [?]

Mr. Thomas Angelhome, surgeon, operator, and rupture-master in Amsterdam, [?] has been invented a philosophical mercury, which does its work without [?] 260 [?] use by pregnant women and young children [?] Condemned or malignant, no matter how severe, without any person needing to abstain from his affairs [?] can help themselves with the foreseen medicines, provided they [?] the chamber perfect, so that each may lodge therein according to his satisfaction, so that [?] hastier working may prove effective; if we (the patients [?] sufficiently recommended for grave illnesses and accidents, too many to detail here: without [?] 231, bought by me or not helped, provided they have not [?] Venus Sickness

The 23rd of april, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 25th of April, 1721
Italy

FAELS, 1 April. Cardinal Pignatelli was improving day by day, but Cardinal Caraccioli, Bishop of Capua, has been troubled by a swelling of water [dropsy].

ROME, 3 April. After the funeral services for the deceased Pope concluded the previous Sunday, and the funeral oration was delivered by Hr. Lindeling, the Sacred College assembled on Monday in the Church of St. Peter, where they attended the Mass of the Holy Ghost, which was sung by the Canons of St. Peter. Upon its conclusion, His Excellency Matti, Bishop of Alicata, delivered the Latin oration exhorting the Cardinals to choose a person worthy of the Pontificate. The Cardinals then proceeded two by two, according to their rank, into the Consistory, accompanied by the Pontifical Guard and a great multitude of people. Meanwhile, the Veni Creator was sung by the musicians in the Papal Chapel—a hymn to invoke the influence of the Holy Ghost. Once the Cardinals had entered the Conclave, they proceeded to the Pauline Chapel, where the Bulls concerning the election of the Pope were read. Cardinal Tanara, as Dean of the Sacred College, took the floor and presented to this illustrious assembly the matters requiring compliance with the Bulls. Following due deliberation, the Cardinals swore to observe the seven following Articles:

I. That he who shall be chosen to the Papal dignity shall endeavor as much as possible to maintain peace among Christian Princes and encourage them to turn their arms against the Turks, heretics, schismatics, and other enemies of the Roman Church.

II. That the Holy See shall not be removed from the City of Rome to any other place, in whatever city or land it might be, unless for an extraordinary or necessary occasion for the Holy Roman Religion; provided that such removal be first approved by general law and authorized in the Consistory by the votes of the greater majority.

III. That he shall ensure that all servants of the Church and State, when their term of service expires, shall give an account thereof in the place where they have served, and remain there some days so that it be made known to the people whether they had any objections to raise.

IV. That he, as future Pope, in the creation of Cardinals shall have regard to persons of good life, good reputation, and learning, and furthermore shall carefully observe the decision of Pope James [?] confirmed in public Consistory on the 21st, that no two brothers, on any pretext whatsoever, shall be elevated to the Cardinalate at the same time.

V. That the future Pope shall endeavor with all his power to preserve the properties of the Roman Church subject to the Apostolic See, and shall not alienate them in any manner without the consent of the Consistory. After the necessary expenses have been paid, the remainder of the revenues shall be set aside in a special place for use in grave necessities in service of the Church.

VI. That it shall in no way be permitted to the future Pope to declare war against any Prince or people, nor upon whatever pretext, without first giving notice to the Sacred College of Cardinals, who shall then be obliged to express their sentiments by secret ballot on his proposition. Should the majority of votes concur with him, he may then proceed with such war. Under the condition that once war is declared, he may negotiate for peace or a truce, nor shall any decision concerning this be made by anyone without the special approbation of the Consistory obtained by secret ballot.

VII. That he who shall now be elevated to the Papal dignity shall be bound to preserve the dignity of the Papacy in all its splendor and shall not permit any Cardinal to be deprived of the Purple without special consultation, just as all matters concerning money must be collected in the Consistory, which shall have authority therein, excepting in case of death, when one shall be obliged to follow the rule of custom—though this shall remain subject to the Consistory's understanding.

After these preliminaries were concluded, many Cardinals immediately went to their cells, which had been assigned to them. Some took their midday meal in their private chambers, for Cardinals are permitted to leave the Conclave for one day, provided they return in the evening; therefore the Conclave remains open the entire day, and the Ambassadors and other persons having business with the election remain there until evening, going from cell to cell to gather votes in favor of their candidates. When all who had no right to remain had withdrawn, the five Ceremonial Masters visited all the cells. The door of the Conclave was then locked and guards posted. The Cardinals were dressed in simple gray cassocks (or gowns called Graecae). This dress is always used during the Conclave by Their Eminences.

Having begun the opening of the Conclave, all the Consistories and officials of the Conclave were visited to see if they were in the correct number and quality according to the Bulls. After good communication was established under the hand of Cardinal [?], the scrutinies began. Each Cardinal, having cast his ballot in one of two chalices placed for that purpose on a long table on either side of the altar, caused the following to occur in the Conclave:

At Rome, Titus Crispus, who has been called here [?], I wished to speak cordially to Christ concerning this difference; and I shall do the same in the next scrutiny. In this scrutiny Crispus lacked but one vote of the two-thirds required for election—Cardinal Paolucci would have been Pope, for at that time there were but 27 Cardinals in the Conclave, and the aforementioned Cardinal had 14 votes. On Wednesday in the next scrutiny he had even more, which caused great astonishment among all the other Cardinals. They thereby saw that Cardinal Albani, who strongly supported Cardinal Paolucci, had already acquired considerable support in his favor, and he might well have succeeded had not Cardinal of Althan opposed him strongly, saying that such a speedy election would be injurious to the Emperor, that one should wait for the arrival of the other Cardinals who were en route and had not yet been able to arrive, and that since they had received news of the Pope's death, one should at least wait for instructions and answers from Vienna so as not to expose oneself to electing a Pope who might exercise the [French] exclusive. Furthermore, such agreements had in earlier times been preceded by [consultation with] the King of France. Cardinals of the Imperial [faction] and Corradini set themselves strongly against these pretensions of the Cardinal of Althan. Cardinal Corradini delivered a fine oration in defense of the freedom of the clergy, arguing that this freedom should be preserved independent of all potentates, and that the election of a Pope should be made according to the motion of conscience and not otherwise. Nevertheless, the Cardinal of Althan sent letters outside the Conclave pressing the Cardinals to enter, and the Secretary of the Nunciature sent an Express about this to the Viennese Court. However, the party of Cardinal Paolucci became stronger with the arrival of Cardinals who reside in the ecclesiastical states and depend on no crown. Cardinal of Rohan, who arrived here on Monday and had decided not to enter the Conclave before Tuesday evening, came the same dawn, and everywhere he is said to have been inclined toward Cardinal Paolucci for the Pontificate. On Tuesday morning very early he entered the Conclave, which the French Cardinals facilitated for him. Since then there have also entered the Conclave: Cardinals Benivoglio, Legate of the Romagna; Spada, Bishop of Osimo; Pico, Bishop of Sinigaglia; Origo, Legate of Bologna; Buoncompagni, Bishop of that city; and Cardinal Patricci, Bishop of Reti. Cardinal Scotti, being indisposed, did not enter; nor did Cardinal Marscotti, on account of his advanced age. The Cardinals who at present have the best chance of being chosen to the Papal dignity are His Eminences Paolucci, Corsini, Scotti, Paracciani, and Orsini. Cardinal Albertoni is expected in this city either today or early tomorrow. His Eminence has provided himself with the domestic servants, horses, and retinue of the deceased Cardinal Altaili, and is even renting the Palace of the late Count Paulus Spada. Work is also underway on a very fine livery for the Cardinals. Fire was discovered in the roof above the chambers over the cells of Cardinal Salviati, but the fire was extinguished in time. The Cardinal Heads of the Orders caused the Pretender and his wife to be complimented, and offered to them the Swiss Guard for their security before they entered the Conclave; but the said Pretender, together with his wife, thanked them politely for this courteous offer. Since the Chapter of St. Maria Maggiore has not yet been recognized by the Court of Madrid for an annual income of 4,000 scudi, as that Chapter drew in Sicily, the said Chapter has caused the arms of the Emperor to be hung above the door of that Church in place of those of the King of Spain, Philip V. The difference between the Princesses Dumbini and Ruspoli has been settled amicably, and they have been released from arrest.

MILAN, 5 April. Orders have come from Vienna for the regiments of Zumjungen and Lobcowitz to march to the Palatinate toward the Rhine. On Sunday, Cardinal Odeschalchi, Archbishop of this city, departed for Rome, whither he is to be followed on Easter Sunday by Cardinal Culani.

VENICE, 11 April. During this week the ordinary councils have been held, in which the Doge and the government have participated. Last Sunday an envoy of the Cardinal of Albani departed from here toward Vienna. Cardinal Koran is to begin his journey to Rome on Easter Sunday, to which place Cardinal Barbarigo has already departed. It is reported that Cardinal Alberoni, who went to Rome via Bologna, is staying in an Imperial fief in the territory of Lunigiana.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 9 April. The King arrived in the city last Saturday. Our Plenipotentiaries are still in Griefswald, but will depart as soon as possible for Neustadt. The French Minister Campidon is expected shortly from Reelburg. This court has assumed mourning for the deceased Queen of Denmark.

Germany & Adjoining Kingdoms

VIENNA, 12 April. Count von Sinzendorf has departed for Rome with commissions, having been followed there yesterday by Cardinal Gacchi. Further advices have arrived that the Cardinal of Albani sends important letters from Rome to His Emperor.

I must be honest: the transcription you've provided appears to be severely corrupted or contains significant OCR/transcription errors that make meaningful translation impossible.

The text becomes increasingly garbled after the opening sections, with nonsensical phrases like "kiim worde daer reede Christaen wel afgeranskt" and repeated, incoherent passages that don't form valid Dutch syntax or meaning.

The readable portions are:

---

AMSTERDAM, 21 April. We have received reports this week from Picardy and other places that the enemy has taken to the field and is bringing together large forces. No important dispatches from the army have arrived, except that the campaign is proceeding with much caution and good order. The state of affairs appears entirely uncertain, and therefore one must be gracious for the present, to make no poor suppositions, for the sake of Mr. Roos, and for propriety and regard of the commons. It is further believed that forces will take the field before much time passes. Yesterday evening here shopkeepers and merchants closed their shops about two hours earlier than usual because of [...]

From GERMANY, 21 April.

[Remainder too corrupted to translate reliably]

---

I recommend obtaining a clearer source document or re-scanning the original, as the transcription quality prevents accurate translation of most of the text.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 25th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 51.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 28th of April, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 1st April. The Commerce College established here has not yet reached a decision to present to His Majesty the petition concerning half a million pesos at eight per cent; however, it is reported that new orders have arrived for 200 men to agree to the aforementioned sum. Daily new obligation certificates are issued to the creditors, and work continues with the silver from the Vera Cruz and Buenos Aires ships being brought to the mints. Despite new orders arriving for the transport of goods to the galleons, the loading of the same proceeds slowly, so that it is firmly believed that they will not be fully loaded. The Spanish warship of 56 guns has left this Bay, 200 men say bound for Havana, and likewise two English warships. There is no news from the West Indies. The Dutch warships continue cruising in general. Arrived in this Bay: P. Fuyk from Genoa, Pierre Flipion from Alicante, Jacob le Grand with cargo, Juan de Canna from Bilbao, Captain Giraud from Amsterdam; the English ships of Th. Erwin, Jacob Pearson, Will. Carlton, Th. Miller, Th. Furral, Th. Browns, and Robb. Williams from England and Ireland; and departed for Mâcon: Captain Grafinds toward Saint-Malo, Captain Soares and Captain Marion toward Genoa, Ant. Mead, Will. Walters, and Adr. Boem toward St. Lucar, Rich. Harley toward Livorno, Captain Brac toward Ostend, Nic. Denice toward Ostend, Jacob Revis toward Malaga, Andr. Raimundo and Edmund Jemmes toward England, and Captain Murokin toward La Coruña: so that there still remain lying 5 English, 3 French, 10 Dutch, 2 Hanseatic, 2 Danish, and 2 German ships.

France

PARIS, 21st April. In the hunt which the King held on the 17th at Vincennes, as mentioned in our previous report, the Princesses appeared again in Amazon dress, and the Turkish Ambassador, who had been brought there with his Sultan and King [?], made use during the hunt of His Majesty's horses, and was thus as well as the King surrounded the whole time by his people on foot: his son, who is already beginning to speak some French, entertained the Princesses: when the King betook himself around half-past six to the chamber to return to the Palace, the [?] retired to the side in the wood with his attendant, and went with his son and Imam to say his prayers, with the carpet spread out on the ground in full view of a great multitude of people that followed him. Yesterday afternoon the [?] Ambassador to the Lords States-General of the United Netherlands paid a ceremonial visit to the aforementioned Ottoman Ambassador, with which His Excellency was greeted with distinguished honors. These days the Cardinal de Noailles has renewed in all the churches and ecclesiastical congregations a prohibition against saying Mass any longer according to the plan of the closed afternoon, so that through this the people on these last feast days have not heard Mass.

The Duke of Bourbon, who had gone to enjoy the feast days at Chantilly, had a great accident there yesterday; so that he was judged to be in danger: the Duchess his mother was naturally away from him during this accident, but a certain person came to bring news of the Duke's condition at the request of the accident, and returned immediately: the next day one found that the Prince was better, and returned to this city yesterday. Also he has at Fontainebleau again begun his new Palace in the open field, which will be great and magnificent, and at least 300 men will work on it. Madame the Duchess is taking great care with the arrival of the Prince and Princesses; that the Prince and Princels of Modena are at the court of Lorraine is confirmed. With the latest advices from Toulon one hears that matters there are going from bad to worse, and that day after day things worsen for the people. And yesterday the [?] is daily better, and people are waiting there only for the latest orders and passports from the court to send different ships with merchandise to America: the city of Bordeaux is presently noted as that in which commerce in this kingdom flourishes most: confidence is there for the most part restored and money begins to circulate again; daily foreigners arrive there, and there are many rich people. One is assured that money in this city and other principal cities of the realm is also plentiful enough, but the great mistrust divides the general conditions and best difficulties. One continues to speak of impending increases and decreases of specie, but as yet without effect. The previous Thursday the decree of the sixth Directors of the generous Company of the Indies was annulled: the following morning the commissioners and syndics began to inventory the books and property of the Company, and the members who were Directors bore much fruit for it; the further Directors of the [?] pursued more aggressively.

PARIS, 21st April. According to letters from Strasbourg, one hears there that troops are being gathered on both sides of the Rhine near the Rhine [?]; some suggest the reason was to send the same to the Netherlands, and others to recruit the regiments. [?] d'Antigu, Tuesday and yesterday [?] of the Champagne, who was a green partisan of the faction of the Jansenists, passed away these days. The pigeons, which the Curé in the old Faubourg St-Germain [?] and the soul reader [?], have under the [?] this in that faith hand [?] and one printed [?]. The Lord de Montpensier, formerly Commandant of the grey musketeers, passed away on the 15th of this month at the [?] years, as also here Notary [?] of the papers of the diary of Lyon.

England

LONDON, 3rd March. The last Committee has been adjourned until next Wednesday, but that for the Inspection continues daily to examine one or another of the former Directors of the South Sea Company, and those who have rendered their accounts clearly; the said accounts are found very milder, and particularly one article in that of the former [?] Surman, regarding which one has found that he received for India for himself sold 1642 23 p., that he has lodged with the Company for 13000 p. India, 12000 p.; at another time for 4000 p. India, 2400 p.; and at another time for 1000 p. India, 7000 p., and the like for 196393 p. fixed goods lodged. In the account of the knight Blunt one has found that he made 277164 on the sale of 48963 pounds India, and came over such 238603 p.; moreover it is assured that those against the present Directors of the South Sea Company, regarding the aforementioned former ones, would have discovered that a sum of more than 300 thousand pounds was not brought into account. The widows whom Getchy the Company loaned, under pledging of their India at the rate of 400, and which have already transported to the Company, find themselves greatly satisfied by the threats which the Company's opponents have made to them, to hold in levy their further India which they still possess in their ownership, and therewith to compensate themselves for the difference in price from 400 to 100 and less, where the India lent at the pledge have decreased; over this the Individuals have consulted with the renowned Advocates, who unanimously assert that the Company has thereto not the slightest right, since they at that time made a transport of that whereupon the Company may compensate itself, which cannot be regarded otherwise than as a real sale, as happens daily between Individuals, and so much the more, because the Company has set the price of India according to its own will at 400. The published list of the inventories of the goods and effects of the 21 former Directors amounts to no more than one million and 50 to 60000 p., but those of the knight John Fellow, Theod. [?], Lamb, Blackwell, and Hascourt Matter are not included therein. One is assured that the Committee, appointed to examine the aforementioned inventories, will have discovered such [?] questions, which according to the Acts of Parliament deserve death. One says that the new Directors are making preparations to send ships to Greenland and the State of Davis; which by the discharges and insurances through the court, the mentioned [?] of institutes, and the allowance of the combined goods of the former Directors to the Company.

LONDON, 22 April. The King's Fleet has set sail again, after the Yachts and several other ships have been repaired. The Heir Prince of Modena and three Cardinals have arrived on horseback at Edelnuyden [?], where a customary solemnity was performed; the Prince of Münster and the Prince of Nassau attended the city; the Elector received them from a gallery.

Here the Governess of the Prince of Wales is no longer deemed necessary for her post. Government and [?] have for some time heard much concerning Roman subjects. It is feared, namely, that the newly converted Roman nation shall suffer thereby; [text heavily corrupted] the affairs concerning Kirsener have gained more weight, apparently her dispatch shall soon follow. The Heir Prince [?] has on the side of the Heir Prince his Princely [authority?], concerning the [?] in her place to be his Princely [?], the Director has concerning this matter [?] and previous [matters?] [?] under-Earldom has [?]. That to his Princely [authority] and other [matters?] [?].

REGENSBOURG, 27 April. The Gentlemen Burgomasters Drewin [?] and Gockinga yesterday morning took the oath before the Council of the Admiral General; the first-mentioned as Councillor of the Admiralty in Amsterdam, and the last-named at Hatlingen. The Admiralty Gentlemen of Sommelsdyk, here [?] and taken. Yesterday afternoon, having [?], departed for Amsterdam. Arrived yesterday from London.

VIENNA, 16 April. The Emperor has elevated Baron von Mikolck [?] for his particular merits to Count, and it is said that a patent shall be given him gratis. The journey of the [?] Diploma that shall be given him gratis. The journey of the Diploma toward her soon Carlsbad [?] is now first to be, because the [?] of the Empress in the Palace of the Marquis [?] Rofiano shall lodge. For 200 [?] one expects, the Count of Welszyn Commission could not fully [?]. One has in this Expedition from Rome, that it is but three [?], though the affairs since the gift have changed.

BASEL, 21 April. The Court of Turin would [?] the [?] of Paul [?], the Duke of Parma in the restitution of the [?] of Spain, the Duke of Parma in the restoration of the [?], which he from Pauline [?] demands, support.

REGENSBOURG, 21 April. The Papal [affairs?]; [?], is [?] shall the gentlemen again for the first time [?], wherein both Roman and Evangelical have appeared: one gathers [?] [?] the Cur-Minister the presentation has [?], but in the same [?], and the Religious affairs therein [?] in [have been] handled, one has hitherto not been able to agitate. Our [?] Palatinate has also begun [?], that since the List [?] specific [?] other various [Grievances?] would be redressed, of which the Gentleman [?] Keck to his Principals [?] in date the 15th hereof, further [?] miles [?] shall give information, or possibly even [?] bring.

MANNHEIM, 22 April. Our Elector amuses himself at Kaiserslautern about hunting, where the Electress of Trémont is entertained.

FRANKFURT, 23 April. Here some 1000 Ducats are collected for the journey of the Cardinal of Schönborn to Rome. A Prince of Modena, who with his [?] at [?], a Suite of 30 persons, and three Carriages with six Horses, at Joli-Challes, and various Noblemen on horseback, proceeded via Strasbourg to Luneville, it is said that shortly he must continue on to Paris.

COLOGNE, 25 April. Today the yearly great Procession was celebrated with customary solemnities, wherein participated the Bishop of Maastricht, the Papal Nuncio, the [?] of Cologne, the Prince Nassau, various other Counts, Prelates, and the Magistrate of the City: the Elector, who arrived this morning from Bonn, made a [?] with two [?] galleries.

AMSTERDAM, 26 April. One sees here Letters from the first [?] of Kidix, wherein the Commerce Council [?] of the [?] of the King; to have one and a half Million [Pesos?] against eight per Cent [?] could have [?], after proper deliberation and consideration of their [?], had decided. With younger Letters from Tunis [?], it is confirmed that at Algiers no Dutch Ships were captured, and that the [?] and [?] on ship St. Lamb equally sent to Naslan.

[NOTICE] Hereby notified to all those who have any Obligation running on the said Chamber, and not greater than 1000 Guilders. Holders of same are hereby requested at the due date on the Capital, together with the interest accrued thereon, until primo January shall be paid, but the same shall be held as discharged.

The Couriers is known as such, as there in Rotterdam some black [?], as here that hanging [?] with the Arms [?] of the same [?], which to discover the aforesaid Fact through the Ju[stice?] to coming report to place, that the Fabrics there shall be [?]; it is also therefore the advantage, [?] or subordinate for the sworn Stalemasters on the one hand, as for the [?] etc. shall be made.

The Magistrate of the City Breda [?] have that in the previous Court, concerning the better [?] Tables by [?] would also [contribute?]; therefore 200 [?] the afore. Magistrate hereby and equally, that [?] here carefully examined is, and for each for the future gratis [?] on collateral, as it so with the City. Wagons all drawn as well [?] is [?] privilege, a very neat [?] or Ordinance, so for the sworn [?] [?] is [?], which soon also with the Print made shall w[?]

ROTTERDAM, 12 May 1721, O. Style [Editor's note: Old Style dating] to the medely [?] Te Aichaugel delivered to be with [?] Tons [?]; the payment must take place in [?] prompt payment, or [?] [?] Caution that in 3 Months shortly shall be paid to the Gentlemen College of His Most Gracious Majesty.

[??] 710 Tons Pot-Ash, which in the following Month July must be delivered in [?] Imperial Rixdaalers, with [?] per Cent [?] shall be paid: and one can address oneself there to [?], at Geertruidenberg, at the house of Jan Condonel, sold and, with a very large [?] Tuy there behind, and further [?] left by the late Justfrow Anna Elisabeth van Calvioorp.

One shall on Wednesday the 30 April 1721, in the afternoon at 3 o'clock precisely, at Geertruyden [?] Dwelling with or without Furniture, together with a Stable for 6 Horses, with a very large [?] of Houses according to the Tickets. Being within the said Breda; all [?] by the late

The [?] over the [?] children of the late Johannes van N[?] [?], and [?] of Werden, the first May 1721, by public sale shall sell a House [?] Smith; Shop; which same House and Estate within the City Woerden in the Hofstaat [?], and the Estate House and by [?] with much [?] made; the [?] in Smiths [?] Nolem, shall, under [?] of Schout and Aldermen of the City sell a house and Estate, with [?] of the Judicial [?] to a [?] in the Hofstaat over the City's Inn, next to a [?] Wagonmaker [?] [?] the Smiths [?].

Jacob Magnus Broker shall in Amsterdam sell on Thursday the 15 May 1721, in the morning at [?] and in the afternoon at [?] o'clock, at the Old Beer's Lodgement, a party new-model colored, blue and Black Fabrics, and a[?] Fabrics, and other Wares, as flowered Silk and plain Linen, [?] the Rots [?] and black Crown-measurements, [?] [?] [?] the Baguette Bank, [?] [?], Damasks, dyed Lyke Sayeu, White Chalons, London and other Calamander, Eltamines, cochineal Sergies, Coulls, Silk Wool Stuffs, and [?] Wool Stuffs, and other Goods; all days in the morning and [?] o'clock.

Cornelis Watairis Broker shall in Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 14 May 1721, in the morning at [?] and in the afternoon at [?] o'clock, the Rest [?] in the Crown Crown, a party White prepared dyed Fabrics, [?] Silk Fabrics, consisting of Red, Blue, Green, Black, and diverse other Colors [?]; Silk Damasks and banded Stuffs, Wool ditto, Atlasses, [?] three, [?], Atmozenes, diverse Chintz and [?], Netting-cloths, White bleached Linen, and diverse other Goods more; as daily for the Sale days for the Sale can be seen.

[?] Wilt, Jan van Blaeauw, Joan Brouaer and Picter [?] Brokers, shall in Amsterdam [?] in Amsterdam sell on Monday the 12 May 1721, the Wednesday [?], m.o. [?] by Brick [?], a party extra fine Souchonlie, and [?] Panbarie, and other tones of Wool.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 30 April, one shall in the Bookshop of Johannes vander Linden, sell all the same well-conditioned bound, and some unbound Books; consisting in outstanding Theological, Juridical, Botanical, Medical, Philological, Historical, Greco-Latin Authors, Antiquarian, Lexicographical &c and Miscellaneous. The Catalogue is to be obtained at Amsterdam [?], The Hague de Hond co Kitto, Delft Beman, Rotterdam the Widow N. Bor and Deman, Dordrecht Béman, Dordrecht van Braam, Gouda Staal, Utrecht vande Water [?] and in the further domestic and foreign cities by the principal Booksellers.

On Tuesday the 6 May 1721, one shall at Leyden in the Bookshop of Andries Dykhuyle the Gentleman Wilhelmus van Opizzet, in his [?] Lawyer before the Court of Holland, be [?], of which the Catalogue is to be obtained at Amsterdam by K. and G. Wellkun, Haarlem [?] and Andres Dykhuyle, [?] sell all the remaining Books of Rotterdam Mackporp, and in the other Cities by the principal booksellers. Court of Holland, consisting of dignified Juridical, Historical and other [?] and G. Wellein, Haarlem van Lee, The Hague de Hond, Delft R. Boijer, [?] buyers.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 28th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
