Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 30th of April, 1721
Germany & Neighboring Territories

REGENSBURG, 29 April. The matter of the Chamber at Wetzlar is being pursued more vigorously by His Imperial Majesty to bring it to a conclusion; however, it is observed that the Evangelical Corps will not readily consent to the reduction of the new offices, and as long as they see that the Elector Palatine's arrangement of these Chancellery positions is not divided equally and fairly between Protestant and Catholic subjects, they refuse to hear of it. We learn that a newly arrived Russian Minister yesterday dined with the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz; and that the Court Commissary Baron von Kirchner was in conference with His Eminence from 5 to 6 o'clock, apparently to bring the Imperial Commissarial proposals, concerning the delayed points, into effect. Here we see the reply of the Crown Prince of Hesse-Cassel to the Imperial Exhortation, being of the following content:

[The remainder of this lengthy German territorial dispute concerning the placement of troops in Hesse-Cassel in response to plague precautions from France, containing various legal and jurisdictional arguments regarding the Crown Prince's rights and the complaints of the Landgraves of Rotenburg and Homburg—a highly technical legal document whose technical language is partially obscured in the original transcription.]

Great Britain

LONDON, 25 April. It is reported that this Court has come to agreement with Spain regarding the surrender of Gibraltar, and that the opening of the Leiden Congress is now absolutely determined by this expedient in several points. Yesterday the Committee for examining the inventories of the goods of the former Directors met at Westminster; it is expected that the same will charge one of these Directors with misfeasance, and this not only in the inventory of his estate, but also in the supplement. The King has given the Government of the Bahamas, vacated by the death of Lord Irwin, to Lord Belhaven, a nobleman of the Chamber of the Prince of Wales. Some days ago a man was imprisoned in Newgate Prison who has married women, having married in the year 1703 in various provinces of this Kingdom under different names.

From the provinces of Kent and Sussex it is written that there, in the past week, a troop of 2 to 300 smugglers of incoming duties, enticed by 40 to 50 well-armed men who had come from a French ship, have gone along the entire coast from place to place, where the officers of the Customs stood and were shown; and if they should come to apprehend any of their troop, they would come to burn them in their houses, and that the magistrate who might assist in the apprehension shall likewise be punished; these smugglers are daily escorted by French ship's crews in the delivery of their brandy, even 12 to 15 miles inland, without any Customs officer daring to resist thus far; but now the Government being informed thereof, it is said that prompt provision will be made. The merchants there have reported that some of their ships encountered a severe storm near the Gulf of Oporto, and that the ship called the Unity [?], commanded by Captain Gristlain, went down with all hands. Since the equipment for the squadron to the East Sea is now complete, it is firmly believed that Sir Norris will today give orders to set sail. It is assured that the warships Dolphin, Hector, and Richmond will be equipped with dispatch to sail toward the north; also on the previous Monday orders were given to the warships Nailsea, Ipswich, Ellex, Totbay, Yarmouth, Breda, Windsor, and Deptford to cruise along this coast and maintain good vigilance. The warship Falmouth was damaged by a squall in descending the river toward the Rendezvous. Since my last report, 2,700 ounces of gold have been entered at the Customs for transport to Calais. Some days ago the King promoted Nathanael Gould, Member of the House of Commons and one of the Directors of the Bank of England, to the title of Knight. The Earl of Isla has become keeper of the Privy Seal in Scotland, in place of the deceased Marquess of Annandale, and Lord [?], a nobleman of the King's Chamber, in place of Lord Carteret, now Secretary of State. *My Lord Folwarth, who was sent as envoy to the Court of Denmark, has not yet returned this evening, and although Baron Soënthal, Envoy of His Danish Majesty to this Court, has not yet arrived to give notice, our Court will nevertheless adopt mourning on Sunday. Today the Commons were again assembled, and have chosen the two newly elected members Ailesbury the younger and Mr. Pelham to be received among the members; whereupon the Speaker was ordered to send out orders for the election of a member for the borough of Berwick in Devonshire, in place of Sir Carteret, who has become Postmaster General; and after the calling over of members, which was postponed for eight days, the House received and read petitions presented by the Mayors and Aldermen of the cities of Rochester and Worcester, as well as by the burgesses of St. Albans, New Sarum, and Shaftesbury, all complaining of the loss of trade brought about by the conduct of the former Directors of the South Sea Company and their accomplices, requiring the House to see that the fullest justice be done, and it was ordered that all these petitions be examined together with those from London and Gloucester: whereupon the House adjourned to the coming Monday. Mr. Walpole is in poor health. Doctor Stanley, Archdeacon of the city, and some other clergy, were yesterday examined by the Bishop and divines regarding the writings of Dr. Whiston.

France

PARIS, 25 April. After the King heard Mass on Wednesday, the Count of la Mark, Prince of the Blood and brother of Louis, Duke of Anjou, Prince of Conti, performed the ceremony of baptism, with [His] Highness being held by the Bishop during the King and Court [participation]... [text becomes illegible/incomplete in original]

The Duke of Orleans, and the child was named Louis; the Duke of Orleans attended this entire ceremony at his court, which was performed by the Bishop of Metz, in the presence of the Curé of St. Germain l'Auxerrois. It remains firmly established that the King on the 3rd, together with the long trains of his court, will perform a ballet on this great theater, and that few spectators will be admitted beyond the Ambassador of the Mussulmans, for whom our most respectable gentlemen offer greetings. The Lords have departed to St. Cloud, where they will remain throughout the entire summer. It is assured that the Prince and Princess of Modena have departed from Luneville, and that on a certain day their appearance suggested they had returned to Modena. That the Knight of Beringhen returned here on Tuesday, one wishes to attribute to his connection with the powerful Duke of Bourbon; by the Duke Regent. The Duke of Bourbon, who finds himself considerably better, is nevertheless not yet fully recovered from his indisposition, which some attribute to the frequent bloodletting of his blood. Furthermore, on Sunday, upon the conclusion of preaching at the palace of Knight Suirou, a man and woman were seized and brought to prison, because they had been present to hear it. The preceding Monday, the Parliament took up again the case of the Duke of la Force, and that day and the following day alone was occupied with it: several Dukes and Peers presented themselves there at that time, and I noted Paris and Ferrands, who went there as commissioners, left the Palace very late. The aforementioned Duke is generally regarded as the author of the collapse of the Mississippi [scheme]; the buzz about this matter in Paris grows from day to day. The repetition of the same Ephemerides proceeds ever better here with more facility than in the Provinces, where the Intendants, the holders or the learned in matters of paper spread much concern. The old shares in this city are held very much, whereas the new is greatly feared, that they will fall with the majority of the King's age, because the court, as well as the epigones of curious private individuals, know the grounds from which the Mississippi goods have been produced. Monday the theater of the French Comedians opened with the tragedy of the Maccabees: on Tuesday the Academy of Inscription opened and displayed beautiful letters with Redeyvoringa, for the edification of ancestors, who, having died, left holy monuments; was also presented to the Sciences, with a magnificent design concerning faith, which was supposed in certain letters. At about four o'clock in the afternoon the Ambassador of the Porte paid a visit to the Duke of Noailles, where many gentlemen of the first rank attended; besides the friendliness of the Duke himself, his Excellency found no greater pleasure than in the Library, where, among other things, he found the Quran, which he kissed out of singular respect for his religion, not without some reluctance; and after he had finished viewing the library, his Excellency amused himself by walking in the garden; where he then went to the apartments of the houses to view them, and in one of the chambers he found a splendid collation that had been prepared for him. The state for reforming the troops has been taken up, and great measures proposed, but not yet approved; and since such matters have been advanced until this point, without the drawings following, one will await them, as that day of Saxony is, to provide some details here without immediate arrival. According to the latest letters from Madrid, the common there can still [?]

The Netherlands

NETHERLANDS.

AMSTERDAM, 28 April. From Lisbon one has news that the fleet from Bahia in June was seen arriving there. Since the Duna at Riga were open, ships went out to sea: Sea-skipper Hanifaes to Lübeck, Mathias Holferman to Danzig, etc., as well as several ships hither; of which Jacob Hopkes and Gabba Cornelis have already arrived in the Sound. From Danzig and at Kaneu troops had already passed. At Bayonne lay sailing [ships]: Captain Martyn Vigoureux bound here, and Captain Pierre Reynout to Havre de Grace; at Stockholm 4 to 5 ships to this city; and among them skipper Lely and others; however, the English squadron was much desired at Stockholm, because it was known that the Russian fleet would put to sea early; and since the waters are entirely free of ice, 10 to 12 ships had been fitted out, and a third others to Danzig, Lübeck, etc. sent out; as well as Jacob Schryver, Jan Schelvis, Wybr. Pjeters, and others, who had lain so long before Copenhagen, [sailed] to the Baltic. On the Elbe (from where all the Goeland merchants went to sea) were fitted out: Dirk Fredriks from Gothenburg and more others; at Gothenburg Jan Pieter from here; 3 ships, and among them Thomas Bradly from England; at [?] Adolf Sargbree; at Lübeck at Aalburg Sietje Volkers and Jelle Janfen, the [?] Folkeren Jantlen, and at London the Miss Maria Corn. Jantfen, all from here; and at Schiedam Fedde Imes from France.

GOVERNANCE, 29 April. Tomorrow council following the ordinary yearly custom by the Company of Gunners of the Orange-Bianje-Blue banner, of the Lord Vroelschap and Captain Hendrik Goufet, through the Lieutenant Johan van Ceulen (the younger), the May-Trees will be planted with the following inscriptions.

For the Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland: CURA NON IPSA CONCEDUNT PACE QUIETEM, that is, Cares do not themselves grant him rest in peace.

For the Gentlemen States General: CLAVUM VIGILES, ET SIDERA SERVANT, that is, Wakeful [hands] attend the helm, and stars keep watch.

For the Prince of Nassau, Stadtholder of Friesland and Groningen: FULGET PATRIÆ VIRTUTIS IMAGO, that is, Here shines the image of the father's virtue.

For the Princess of the same Modesty: NOBILITATE POTENS NEC BONITATE MINOR, that is, High of birth, and no less in goodness.

For the Magistrate of The Hague: PLACIDO MODERAMINE GAUDENT, that is, They find pleasure in a mild governance.

For Colonel Alfenidis: NON TOGA COMEDIE SAGULO NON ARMA TAGATO, that is, Neither jacket nor weapons at the table do justice admit.

The Lord van Effen is returned from Gelderland, the Baron van Renswoude from Utrecht, and the Lord Ockerkle from Zeeland, and have appeared at the assembly of the Gentlemen States General. The Lord Mya has been from the government. An expedition from the British court has been directed here toward the North.

To be notified hereby to all those who have any bonds, common chamber running, and not great of the Gis. the holders of which are hereby requested at the due date to pay on the interest, but the certificate will be held in abeyance.

Now again is proceeded to the exchange of the lots of January and 13 Clause, positive the [?] of June following will begin. Deficiency of their shares falls away and the same for the benefit of

The Directors of the East India Company at the chamber in Delft notify hereby by this advertisement of the years 1696, 1697 and 1698, to the charge of said chamber have run, coming to mature on Tuesday the 3rd of June next, that the holders thereof will be paid by me; and that those in default will receive no further interest, the 6th to 7th class; that mature upon the right of the transferred lots, and that the drawing of the 7th class, per company of commerce and assurance of the city of Dordrecht, will take place on [...] and that at all occasions of the city of Dordrecht, will take place on Tuesday the 17th of May 1721, after which all occasions of their company will be declared void.

Reynier van Grieken and Jacob Coelraart, brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam on Tuesday the 17th of May, in the Nether-Casteleine on the New Side, Lord's lodging, a ship for the city; as well as a party of 3 to 10 hundred Lisbon [goods]; by parcels of 2 to 300 salt [measures]; the samples of the salt will be at [?] flowered Calamauken, Essamine, Sergies du Bois, Damask, Damastene, and much more [...]; Saturday from 8 in the morning to 4 in the afternoon to be seen, as the Norder is running; Jacob Makei shall sell in Amsterdam on Thursday the 13th of May 1721, flowered Calamauken, Essamine, colored Sergies, Kersey, Silk full Sergies, and on the third Saturday in the Mayery of the Boelch, near Tilburg, and there [...] the company made Dictionary Eastindic and Comparts d'Est at Marseille, external of the same location and arrangement, submitted to the Knight and the Gentlemen Bieffyn Chicquenaut, Verit and Boulier, and those [...] as an appendix to the Treaty of Pest, described by Mr. Richard [...].

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES OF WESEL:** Amsterdam 14, 2, 1; regarding 33, 11, 14. Rotterdam 13, 8, 1. Antwerp 34, 11. Hamburg 33, 10. Paris 34 and 1/2. Bordeaux 34 and 1/2. Madrid 46 and 1/4. Cadiz 47 and 1/2 to 1/4. Bilbao 48 and 1/2. Genoa 51 1/2 to 1/4. Venice 45 and 1/4. Lisbon 5, 4 and 1/2 to 4 and 1/2 to 1/4. Dublin 12 and 1/4 to 13. Bank shares 113, East India Company 142, South Sea 143, Million Bank 133. Lottery Annuities, Bank Annuities and 4 percent South Transport, 2 10, 19; ditto new 21 and 1/2; Stock Insurance 7 and 1/8; ditto London 7 and 1/4; and York Building 18 and 1/4. The EXCHANGE RATE today is: Madrid 89, Cádiz 110 and 0½ to 110, Seville 110 to 109 and 1½, Bilbao 89 and 1½, Lisbon 43 and 5 agio. Venice 83 and ¾, Livorno 92 and 5 agio to ¾, Genoa 95 and 1¼ to 1¼, Paris sight to 1, London 170 shillings 8 [pence], Leipzig current 38 and 7 agio, St. Danzig 48[?], Poland gt., Breslau 36 St., Hamburg 32 and 5/16 St., Antwerp 3 and 1¼ to 3 and 1½ per cent, Ghent 3, Brussels 3 and 1½, Zeeland 1, Rotterdam 1¼. The agio of the Bank 5 and 3 agio to 1¼. The agio of the E.I.C. 770, W.I.C. 101 to 102. Subscription 290, the English Bank 141 to 142, ditto E.I.C. 148, and S.S. 160, all cash.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 30th of april, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 53.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 2nd of May, 1721

ITALY.

NAPLES, 1 April. Cardinal Caraccioli, Archbishop of Capua, is recovering better from his indisposition following his death, and it is hoped that he will still undertake the journey to Rome. The Nuncio Vicentini, dressed in deep mourning, presented the formal Condolences regarding the death of Pope Clement XI to the Viceroy; after which the Palace of the Nunciature was reopened.

ROME, 12 April. Fray Arcanio, a Dominican monk and Spaniard, who had been retained at the court of Madrid concerning matters affecting the Crown of Spain, is taking up residence at this court for as long as Cardinal Aquaviva remains in the Conclave. The aforementioned Father has been installed in a Palace on the Spanish Quarter; where he is served by Marchionesses of the aforementioned Cardinal Aquaviva. The Cardinals, Heads of the Orders, have had brought to the Castle of St. Angelo 130 thousand Scudi in gold, on account of the 300,000 Scudi which the deceased Pope had drawn from there in the year 1709, and what still remains to be received to complete that sum is already calculated in bonds and assignments. Last Sunday a courier from Vienna arrived here who, after delivering a packet of letters for Cardinal Althan, continued his journey to Naples, where he brings to Cardinal Schönbach the Imperial Instructions concerning those whom the aforementioned Cardinal will leave in his place as Viceroy during the time he remains in the Conclave. Monday morning the P.P. Affitian Minorites arrived from France and the courier from Paris with news that the French Cardinals, excepting the Cardinals Givry and Noailles, have set out for Rome to attend the election of the new Pope [Editor's note: following the death of Pope Clement XI in March 1721]: that same evening the courier from Vienna returned here, who had been dispatched there by Cardinal Althan, and with this courier came word that Cardinal Cenfuegos is already on his way; that the other Cardinals were preparing themselves to enter the Conclave; and that Count Kinsky had been appointed by the Emperor as ambassador to this court, so that he might be present at the forthcoming election of the new Pope. Monday there arrived here the Cardinals Piazza and Albertoni, who made a formal public entry in one of their carriages, attracting a very great crowd of people, who accompanied His Eminence through all the streets that he passed along, both in front of his carriage and at the sides: this Cardinal went that evening to the Palace of the House of Albani, where he went to pay some formal condolences to Donna Theresia and Don Charles Albani, after which the Cardinal returned to his carriage and then went to the house of his agent the Abbot Ferrati.

The following day, being the fifth of this month, around three o'clock, His Eminence went in his carriage to the Conclave, accompanied again by a great multitude of people eager to see him, and was led according to custom to the entrance of the Conclave, where he was received by the Cardinals, Heads of the Orders, and Cardinal Albani the Camerlengo [Editor's note: the papal camerlengo was the cardinal who administered papal finances and temporal affairs]; His Eminence made a very polite compliment to the Cardinals, Heads of the Orders, and repeated it even more warmly to Cardinal Albani, but the former only made some courteous bows without answering him a word. It has also been noted that his compliment to Cardinal Aquaviva was received better. It is also said that the number of Cardinals who are presently in the Conclave amounts to 42; meanwhile more arrive in this city daily. Cardinals Barbarigo, Ruffo, Cosadini and Borsoffi have already arrived; the latter will enter the Conclave tomorrow evening, where Cardinal Scotti has also entered today. It is reported that Cardinal Albano, having been given the task by Cardinal Paolucci in the name of the Emperor to propose for the Papal dignity the Cardinals Tanara, Lini, Tolomei, and Paracciani; but since the greater part of the Cardinals presently in the Conclave are creatures of the deceased Pope, and consequently of the party of Cardinal Albani, the party of Paolucci is very strong, which for this reason did not yet find itself entirely without hope of success, and although it is certain that they were ready, in case they lacked votes for the election, [they suspended] their zeal for his elevation, in order to wait for the courier whom Cardinal Althan had sent to the Emperor, which was received on Wednesday evening: in which he again obtained the votes; but it is reported that beyond the exclusion given to the aforementioned Cardinal Paolucci, some other potentates have also done so, and other Cardinals, for when opening the ballot boxes, instead of finding the name of a Cardinal on the ballot, they found the word "Nomi" [names], and this occurred in about 28 ballots in each scrutiny, from which one sees that this Cardinal Albani will not be chosen by anyone. Some say this is being done in order to win time, because Their Eminences wish to wait for the return of the couriers from various Princes, so that the election might take place in a united fashion.

This same day the secretary of the Imperial Ambassador arrived with a courier from Vienna, bringing the appointment which the Emperor has made of the Prince Borghese to the Viceroyalty of Naples, in place of the Cardinal Schönburg, who was already greatly involved in the Conclave these days, now it is being asked whether this Prince will be charged with bringing these tidings to that Prince, or whether it will be sent with the dispatches of His Imperial Majesty to Spain. The Prince and the Abbot Oudenat of the Castle of St. Angelo and some members [are involved].

Friday's Courant.

The Citizens' Guard continues daily and armed to patrol through the city, watching to prevent disturbances in their respective wards, and for complete prevention, the Conservators of the City have stationed 300 soldiers in the Capitol, so that they might shoot if necessary. The Venetian [ambassador] has had on Wednesday a great retinue of prelates and nobles at audience with the Cardinals, Heads of the Orders, to whom His Excellency delivered the dispatches of the Senate for the Sacred College. Yesterday Mr. Ruspoli, Governor of the Conclave, made a present of a ring to Cardinal Alberoni, and it is reported that he daily makes gifts to one or another Cardinal. Monday afternoon, when an officer of the court tried to pass on the side of the Conclave guard, near the Place of St. Peter, he was ordered by the sentinel to choose another way; but when this officer answered the sentinel brutally, it caused the posted soldier to begin to threaten, whereupon the officer drew a dagger on the sentinel, who was thereby slightly wounded, but the other soldiers, being so quick, shot at him so that he could not escape by flight; they fired 3 shots at him without hitting him, and having finally brought him down, they beat him with their swords so harshly that he died from the wounds received, and he was thereby spared the public punishment that had already been prepared. It is reported that there is discussion about closing the gates of St. Sebastian and St. Pancras, because this causes too much difficulty for the nobility and citizens who wish to attend the vigil. The Holy Office of the Inquisition has requested that the properties of Dom Phillip Mellini be placed in prison for the second time. The Abbot Martelli is waiting for 20 mules from Florence, which the Grand Duke sends him, so that he might present them to the new Pope. The Abbot Chiaponti, Master of Ceremonies, having become ill, has departed from the Conclave. A servant who had performed various tasks in the Conclave was brought to the Castle of St. Angelo, because it was discovered that he had been conveying letters outside the Conclave for some members.

GENOA, 12 April. Reports from Provence indicate that the sad sickness would increase again in Marseille, and that at Toulon (where it was growing worse daily) about 300 people had already died, besides a great number of the sick who lay in the galleys. Last week a general's servant shot one of the servants of the envoy of Great Britain with a musket in the chest, and having been caught in the act, he is reported to be facing a lengthy trial. By a packet boat from Port Mahon, word has been received that the Moors had resumed the siege of Ceuta with 40,000 men, and that on the 14th of the previous month they had approached the city with their ships. Here have arrived the Lion galley, Captain Gionata Kappel from Lisbon, Pietro Pauli de Simoni and Gregorio Mordegha from Alicante, and departed the Gianna galley, Captain Babel Nyx for Amsterdam.

VENICE, 19 April. With letters from Dalmatia came word that uprisings had occurred in Cairo and Aleppo. Mr. Law with his son is staying in a country house near this city. Mr. Alban, nephew of the deceased Pope, passing through here on his way from Vienna to Rome, has [passed through].

GERMANY AND NEIGHBORING KINGDOMS.

VIENNA, 19 April. It is believed that this court is giving more attention to the election of the new Pope than was initially thought; and it is openly said that the Cardinal Paolucci has received the Emperor's exclusion [Editor's note: a formal veto], notwithstanding the strong following which has declared itself for this Cardinal; it is assured that this is altogether owing to the ill will which he showed toward the House of Austria when he was Governor of Rome, and that his partiality went so far that satisfaction was demanded of the Pope on this account, who indeed removed him from his governorship but afterward made him Cardinal; and it is certain that this court would have been inclined to elevate the Cardinals Pignatelli or Odeschalchi to the Papal dignity, but that in these days there are still some difficulties in respect to other nations. The third departure of the Empress to Carlsbad is said to remain postponed, because all necessary preparations are being made and sent there; and it is said that Their Imperial Majesties have decided that once they return, they will make a pilgrimage to Mariazell in Styria, and spend the rest of the summer hunting in the Favorita.

REGENSBURG, 24 April. It is reported that the Nuncio of Saxony-Zeitz's request to be allowed to travel to Rome and there present his credentials at the election of a new Pope has recently received, through a dispatch from Vienna, the Emperor's answer, in which His Imperial Majesty excuses himself, saying that his patience, in view of the various religious conflicts, is greatly tried here, and therefore cannot grant his request. Some servants of the Marquis of Welterlbo, who were arriving from Vienna, have traveled through, making it known that the aforementioned Marquis will also soon set out. From the Palatinate it is reported that the Elector has instructed one of his principal ministers to examine the validity of the existing contracts, and to arrange other matters concerning the judicial restoration. The Lord Castell, Minister of Dorneburg, is reported to have departed for three weeks to his country estate.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 21 April. A nobleman who arrived yesterday from Chantilly reports that the Lieutenant of Bourbon is well and that the administration of His Grace's affairs has been arranged well [text incomplete in original]

FRANCE

[The] Lord de Chamilart, whose death was feared, has left a pension to his children upon his death. For him this was a great position in the household of the Duke of [?]; this pension is divided into 2,000 livres to his widow, 5,000 to his daughter the Marquise of Dreux, and 4,000 to his granddaughter de la Croix Cavaor, Marshal of the King's Household; and the remainder reverts to His Majesty. The death of this minister was compared to a reimbursement of 500,000 livres. [Following] the grand funeral, a woman from Montpellier, who had gone to church in that city some time before to hear the sermon, cried out loudly when she saw the priest come before the altar, that all the clergy and the Church ought to withdraw, because this priest, as had been established, was numbered among the most schismatic Appellants. This unheard-of occurrence in the public church caused the Bishop to make a forceful visitation concerning such disorder and to punish it; though this was without question dealt with harshly, in that the aforementioned worthy prior of the innocent troop was rewarded with [?]. Nevertheless, now order has been set that such disturbances will be prevented in the future. Recently, Madame Turgor, wife of the Intendant of Soissons, passed away. The Lord Colbert of Teron is to marry Miss Gaufetl, who comes from a distinguished family in England; and the Marquis de Kanti [is to marry] with Miss d'Ateprenry, who possesses considerable estates. It is rumored that the Heir-Prince of Modena and the Princess his wife will not come to France; moreover, some claim that these marriages with the [?] are settled, as has been said alone; but many people find reasons to be satisfied with this proposal, and wish without objection that the [Duke] of Modena and his Excellency [may come]. The young Prince of Meffiac de la Rochefoucault, who was one year old, passed away last Sunday to the great sorrow of his parents and family. [A new] broker presented himself to go inspect the forests at Versailles; His Excellency [?], in order, if the King [travels], to position himself on one or another place on the route to [meet] His Majesty, [?] which he, without leaving the carriage, frequently [does]. One still does not hear when the said ambassador will depart from here. On Friday, the Lord Bernard, Intendant of the Harlock of La Force, was interrogated again; and interrogations without interruption have continued. [One sees] some printed memoranda for the justification of the Lords Bernard and his brother du Parc, which have been compiled by the advocate [?] and it is believed that they are incomparably well written; but since the matter is treated only generally, such [memoranda] will certainly be no convincing proof of their justification. Meanwhile, the [wife] of La Force, it is said, departed to Lansoeuden in Normandy on Monday, to remain there during the coming summer. The First President, who is held in honor by the Generals, is having his country estates put in order. Although it is generally believed that the Congress at Cambrai will be opened within a short time, one finds almost no encouraging reports, which gives confidence that matters will not be long delayed. It is assured that since the Emperor gave the exclusive [right] to Cardinal Paolucci, the Court of the Pope [is] joined over the Cardinals Paracciani and [another] [?] years old, d'Avia and Scotti; indeed, one finds people here who imagine that within eight days word will come that the declarations and three other Cardinals [?] that Cardinal de Billy is now unavoidably in Rome.

---

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM, 30 April

At Bilbao (where no ships had arrived), Captains Blok and Warnart Hofman lay ready to sail homeward. From Bayonne, Captain Pierre Reymoud [sailed] to Havre-de-Grâce and the hooker St. Jan Gerrit [sailed] to Zeeland; likewise from Dieppe the Doris and Maria, Captain Durfel, to Hamburg. At Bordeaux has arrived the St. Jacob, Cornelis Gertitie from here; at Nantes (where the Tekke Wibele Brouwer still lay) the St. Peter, Christoffel Selmer, from the [?]; on the River of London, Joshem Beeckman and Hendrik Worms, together with several other ships from Hamburg; and at Schiedam, Paulus Kluyt from Bayonne. At [?] some ships have arrived; and the East-Indiamen lying there and those on the Maas and other havens will depart and, with the first favorable wind, put out to sea.

---

THE HAGUE, 1 May

The Prince Kussin, ambassador and plenipotentiary of the Tsar of Great Russia, who had once been in Amsterdam, is being sent back today. The Palatinate's minister, Baron Ulrer, [has been?] with some letters from the government. It is said that the British squadron under Admiral Norris from the English coast would have departed. At this time, the Lords Silvius and Schage have taken their seats in the Assembly of the Lords States General on behalf of the Province of Holland, being introduced by the Baron van Wielderen. At the same time, the Lord of Wellem has also taken his seat there on behalf of the Province of Groningen, introduced by the Lord Idinge.

With the Lords Councils of State, the Lords Bleskengstraf and Geidersmidem have been nominated for the leasing and inspection of the magazines and fortifications of Walenburgh; and the Lords of Weede and [another?] Wayten for the leasing of the dikes along the Maas. Also, the Lords Trip, on behalf of the City of Groningen, and Pickart, Lord of Slogteren, on behalf of the Ommelanden, have taken their seats in council, and likewise the Lord Valk on behalf of the Ommelanden has taken his seat in the Joint Chamber, having been appointed there by the aforesaid Lord Idickinge. The Baron Godenau, minister of the Elector of Cologne, was today in conference with the Lords States General. The Lord Heimen, privy councilor of the King of Prussia, [has taken a seat?], and this afternoon, together with the Lord Mindertshasen, minister of [the aforementioned...]

---

STOCKHOLM, 16 April

News has arrived that the plenipotentiaries of this crown [have departed] from Greece to Nystadt.

---

HAMBURG, 25 April

According to letters from Petersburg, the Lords secret chancellery-councilors Osterman and Tolstoy departed from there to Nystadt. Letters from Hanover report that there they had examined part of the affairs of the two imprisoned Jewish bankers, since which time they have been in close confinement, which is regarded as a bad omen for them [and their] faith.

---

COLOGNE, 28 April

This morning the wife of the Prince of la Tour and Taxis arrived here by post from Brussels and departed further toward Frankfurt.

---

AUCTION NOTICES

The Directors of the East India Company at the Chamber in Delft hereby notify all those who hold any bonds [of the] common chamber, outstanding in no greater sum [than...], of the holders thereof, by these [notices] are relieved of the obligation to repay the mortgage, together with the interest accrued thereon, until the first of June. Andries Dykhuizen will sell all remaining books from the collection of [?] of Holland, consisting of juridical, historical and other works... [?]

[Multiple auction notices follow for various goods, properties, and merchandise to be sold in Amsterdam, Leiden, and other locations on specified dates in May 1721, conducted by various auctioneers]

---

Printed in Leiden by FELIX DE KLOPPER on the Dressselstraat May 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**Exchange Rates of the WECHSEL:** Madrid 80½; Cadiz 110; Seville 109; Bilbao 80; Lisbon 42½; Venice 83⅔ to ½; Livorno 92⅞ to ¾; Genoa 95¼ to ½; Paris at sight 41½; London 33 shillings 8 pence; Leipzig current 98⅞; Danzig 93½; Poland [?]; Breslau 36 pence; Hamburg 32⅔ to ½; Antwerp 3⅛ per hundred; Ghent 3; Brussels 3½; Zeeland 1; Rotterdam ¼. Bank shares 3⅔ to ¾. Shares of the East India Company 970-775; West India Company 101-101½. Subscription 990; English Bank 131; likewise East India Company 142; and [?] 150, all cash.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 2nd of may, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 54.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 5th of May, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 8 April. On account of the plague still raging in Provence, the authorities here are now redoubling their efforts to inspect the arrival of French ships in port: work continues on transporting the silver to the mints; and since the loading of the galleons is proceeding so slowly, they will not be able to depart before May.

France

PARIS, 28 April. The re-petition of the Duke of Bourbon has come to nothing, and that Prince hopes he will be in a position within a few days to hold the Assembly of the States of Burgundy. Some days ago an unusual incident occurred in the King's Chapel, where during Mass a certain Prince, who is a blood relative of the Dowager Duchess of Orleans and in service of this Realm, wished to kneel in the choir but was raised up, whereupon the Marshal of Villeroy sent him orders to bend his knee or depart, and choosing the latter, he left the church. Last Monday the Turkish Ambassador paid a visit to the Marshal of Villeroy, to whom he delivered a letter from the Grand Vizier, enclosed in a large sealed silk bag according to the custom of the Ottoman court; the following day the same Marshal, who with his illustrious family remains much beloved by the public, returned the visit to this Turkish Excellency. It continues to be said that the reform of the King's Troops will proceed well there, and that it will be considerable, with some saying it is ordered at 50,000 men; but others believe it will amount to only 18 to 20,000, because many companies of foreigners are found complete. The Lieutenant General Claude de Marcilles has received the Grand Cross from the Marquis de Maupertuis, and the Marquis des Clazelles his Red Ribbon. The Marquis du Plessis Bellière weds Mademoiselle de Chalinies. The Duke of Chartres amuses himself very frequently by walking in the Tuileries. The rumor that the excise duties would be increased has caused everything that pertains to human sustenance to rise greatly in price; the butchers say they would not wish to commit themselves to supply meat in the months [ahead] at 20 sous per pound. News has been received that at Toulon more than 100 people die daily. At Vliet and at Amelia several counterfeiters have been found and imprisoned. From Rome there is news that it lacked very few votes for Cardinal Cornaro of Venice to be elected Pope. It is still being said that the Emperor, the King of France, and the King of Spain have all united. From Madrid of the 3rd it is reported that Cardinal Borgia has departed toward Alicante, from where he is said to embark for Italy to attend the Conclave; he is to be followed there by Cardinal Belluga. According to letters from the 8th from Barcelona, a review of the troops in that Principality was held there some days ago, which were found in good condition and had returned to their quarters; work was also being done there to establish large magazines of provisions and gunpowder in the principal places, and from Pamplona it is reported that the troops from Navarre defileed toward Bilbao to pass in review between that place and Saint Lucia. Argument was heard before Parliament in the case of the Baroness Albreo and the Count d'Etoyelo, householders, regarding the estate of Tancarville, which the former claims and which was purchased by the latter from the Marquis Law: the Advocate Chevalier made very sharp accusations against the Marquis Law on this occasion, and this pleading will be continued next Friday. Since much discussion has been made about the case of the Duke of la Force and continues to be made, one therefore finds the best examination in the following

[Legal Proceedings Regarding Monopoly Accusation]

Memoir by the Sieur Bernard, Secretary of the Duke of la Force, against the Merchants and Overseers of the Community of Spice Merchants of this City and the Procurator General.

The Sieur Bernard never feared anything more than that one might accuse him of having conducted the slightest commerce; and he is also entirely uninvolved in the cited merchant partnerships; which partnerships the seller does not deny are goods and the buyer to whom he suspects they were claimed; a notarial deed of purchase and sale clearly reveals the lawful and present heir. All the clarifications necessary to the decision lie openly before the scrutiny of the judge. Meanwhile, one must ask whether the merchants in spices, or those who lend their name to that, must one reject this real matter in order to proceed to imaginary propositions; and thus let the body give way to the shadow.

The Sieur Bernard's interest must not be mingled with the interests of the other accused; he must content himself with what concerns him personally, although in his particular detention matters arise which serve the general justification; and these matters are so precisely and naturally developed that, surrounding the circumstances with care, one must ask where the crime lies, which has caused so much talk and brought the people to such great agitation. If the Sieur Bernard had direct or indirect part in the purchase of goods, which were later found among the effects of the great Augustinians; he would not know in what manner, or by what means the purchase which he might have made could be named a monopoly. In general it is admitted by law that individuals among the other inhabitants may lawfully acquire such merchant goods, which are necessary or useful to human sustenance, and sell them as they think good to such countries and under such conditions as they please. And then, for example, he who would buy up all the grains and other provisions found in a Province to raise them to an exorbitant price, and thus profit alone from the dear-bought necessity of the poor, would undoubtedly deserve strict punishment; that is, all murderous monopolies would tend to deprive the people of their sustenance or to make it excessively difficult to obtain. But to establish the character of monopoly, two things are required; the one that the goods which form its foundation are of absolute necessity, or of indispensable utility for the public; the other that the hoarding which has been done is powerful enough to strangle commerce, to the detriment of the welfare of the State. The goods in question consist of Chinese porcelain, porcelain ware, tea, and some dried goods which are of small utility. Now let it be left to judgment whether goods consisting of luxury or ornament, which serve only to satisfy the curiosity of a small number of people, interest the public enough to treat those who bring such things from other lands as monopolists. Regarding quantity, the said goods are valued in the growth of the people at several millions; and yet it is certain that there is no more than for 157,500 livres; and in all there are no more than 6,000 livres of goods which concern the trade of the druggists. Moreover, maritime commerce is kept open by the ordinances for all sorts of persons, and has never been subject to the laws established for things which originate in the Kingdom, or for goods which are privileged there.

But one has the freedom to demonstrate the falsity of the fact which serves as pretext for accusation, as if that fact itself were untrue. The goods in question were sent to Paris for the account of the ship-owner commissary, the Knight Landais, who had them brought from China; and to whom they were rightfully assigned after the return of the ships; they were addressed to the Sieur Bernard, because several persons of confidence, having interested themselves in a privilege in Louisiana, had found it good to admit the Knight Landais into their society to make the necessary purchases for this establishment; and by a deliberation, it was found good to admit the goods to the Sieur Bernard, factor of the society; so that the ship, the Count of Toulouse, anchored at Saint Malo, the Knight Landais, having received full orders to send the goods from that ship to Paris, directed the same to the Sieur Bernard, which he sought; the costs of transport to be paid from the monies which he then had to remit to him. For the rest, the Sieur Bernard received them without unpacking them, without seeing them, and in a word without any other care than to pay the handling and freight costs, and to find a suitable place where they might be kept until the Knight Landais should arrive in Paris.

Meanwhile, although these goods were not of such a nature as to constitute a monopoly, although they were addressed to him in his capacity as commissary, and only to have paid the costs of transport from the monies he then had to remit to the Knight Landais; although at the time he received them he recognized no other owner than the Knight Landais, who he knew would later sell them to the so-called Orient [Company], yet a prosecution was brought against him by an arrest of the 15th February 1721, to which he willingly submitted on the last day of that month; and since that time he has been held prisoner without either a lack of proofs, which is always a painful point for the accused; nor the declarations alone which he gave at his interrogations, and which he has justified in writing, having been sufficient to restore his freedom to this day.

It is true that monitions have been published, and that this publication places the interests in a state of uncertainty: but besides the detention of the prisoner usually being founded on clear proofs, and not on those which might appear; one can confidently say that the monitions could bring little advantage in this case; because there are no witnesses who can dispute the Knight Landais, and the capacity which he had as owner of the goods, by force of a regular adjudication, nor to the Sieur Bernard the capacity of factor, which was given to him by an express deliberation. There is also no testimonial proof which might be drawn from the sale of the goods by contract before notaries to the Sieur Orient: so that the formalities of the monition can do nothing but make the procedures and the misfortune of the accused more lengthy, without giving any new light to justice.

The Sieur Bernard has, however, too much respect for the authority of the court, and is too convinced of its equity, to complain of the unusual slowness in the instruction; he blames the mutual fatality of the circumstances, which having given rise to diverse abuses, the suspicion of monopoly has likewise easily been produced, possibly by the facility which there was to commit it. But it is hoped that this zeal for the public good will right the rights of the innocent, and that in all the fruitless investigations into the discovery of an imaginary crime exhausted, the fault will fall on the instigators who maliciously brought the accusation.

It is not as principal author of the pretended monopoly that the Sieur Bernard is accused by insinuation; they do not wish the goods in question to belong to him, nor that he brought them to Paris on his own account; they only blame him for having executed the orders of the Duke of la Force, for having had the goods brought, and for [...]

THE NETHERLANDS

At the Cloister of the Augustinians this season... Their proceedings have been conducted, for presentation according to the discourse, which was directed against the Lord. [?] not yet concluded. the Chamber [?] has been resolved. As a Mediator [...] Proceedings will be expedited, the Chamber [?] concerning some matter. In the criminal case, [?] in the second that the implicated in our [?] the Proceedings convince that there is no deed in the criminal case and had Spanish Cardinal. If the Duke of la Force, would be the mentioned Cardinal Tuscher-[?] [...] the Ship Is. Goddeken here was full of public Transport— would have Monopoly, and where he [?]. [...] and this might; Time, the [?] By order of the Duke of la Force, would be a second from London; to [...] bring Regarding Mr. Bernald, which were to know whether Capt. around Alicant he, Dismissal of mentioned [...] of him following, a [?] criminal case good could have committed in the receipt of Merchant— around Martini [...] one for the Duke, of which the decision for him unheeded which to Mari the third to Mart [...] hi tart delivered, against the Duke not, he [...] et to [...] nor indirect any part in. the declaration which he there of in two is gathered [...] Interrogation given has, it by no confusion proof weakened or is not done: it is him well clear and open of the Corps of Limbik: on This there is, deryt it [?] on the presumption of the Jof Corn., Roc Judges within royer, in the Person of the Duke of la Force; here John Nic this judge in Fact, which with him much burden brings, zy [...] ding this the St [...] without that 'et cerds to Cadene must serve convincing against him in; and the ira. For all had the Mr. Bernald everything in his advantage that an Ec-guilty one is between has, which is commanded of o es—rusing; but he has still not the advantage, to in the Trial equally a diadem on prove to overcome: the first in, that the criminal case which inside him Complicit presented, is not assorted by the principal Accused, nor included on the side of the Knight: it, Pleinipertorin goes Hoon itself green, as the Corpus Directed: not con— [...] of the [...] why would the Mr. Bemard the trans—sen-should have been paid, has, in Express Commission, an Enquist —le. bound him, the Goods to receive from a Common— [...] that the Knight Landais in way admitted was become, and the which in this [...] that by, by the Society charged being of the Monies ann status Generaal of the Knight Landais to remitter, on those Learnings paid the the Questions of the Goods which to him from his 'w were sent to the sides, her Registry of the Disburdens is in these Broad Commis—mittier: Ua exaggerated. The second is, that one, well far from the Duke of la Force as Owner of that Merchandise in a or arrange, in certain broken has, that they the Knight Landais to—belonged, all him to St. Malo by public purchase being assigned, and and it remains also by a Notarial Act, that the Knight Landais this sich maintained has acknowledged to the iter Order Merchant and a Party, and Zoan [?] of a Merchant in dire same City. The title of Owner, that the Rid—der Landais by deliberation lost, and still in the Contract of which d'er of the Eigendemain was been, nor the Contract of which where by of the Eigendemain was been, nor the Contract of which

In the quenti he is unround that the Hr. Bemard yrch in diese navor—searching in lay, it is said one zenen opugicht, that by one consequence of zyn Commission, and by previous Engagements of all their in of the Duke to the sensitivity.

Lechm bequeathed | Leiden of the H:

AMSTERDAM, May 3rd. From Bayonne has among other things departed the Ship Pigouteus; and from London John Michiels toward Hamburg, and with Admiral Norris various Merchant ships toward the Sound. At Smyrna have arrived, the Alice of Capt. Tima, the Tendering Capt. Loons, and the Dolphin Capt. Hörtevel from London; at Barcelona the Barcelona Merchant Capt. Davids, who came from London there by an Alg. Pirates had been. By 2 Pirates was around Africans by an Alg. Pirates had been. By 2 Pirates was the chelle toward Mario sailing. Pieter Fuyck had at Cadiz sailed toward the Biscay-guard, and St. Ubes Etik Ingenberg toward the North. At Boure him Simon Klaafsen from Dublin; at Beigon the St. Jan Zach. Winter harbor of Limdt 3 on the River of London Jan Vermey, John Lewis, Roo—der lof Corn., Roelof Claafé, the Elisfabet Warnart, and 1 other from here John Noel and Tomas Miller, recently sailed from Port à Port, with ding of the Ships from there toward Rio de Janeiro departed were. The 2nd ille before has at Ostend within come the Ship the Concordia Capt. Joseph de Gezelle from the East Indies and lastly Brazil in erbij the Servier Klaas de Ruyter and the Wolfgang Jan Vaik.

THE HAGUE, May 4th. According to the latest Letters from London, the Princess of Wales on April 27th in the evening was happily delivered of a Son, to great joy of the entire City, with which pleasant news Expresses toward Germany &c. have been sent. The Mr. Kempenaer has received Direction of the Eight States General as Counselor to the Admiralty on behalf of the Province of Friesland at Harlingen, and the H. de Bey as Receiver and Convoy-Master of Scherenburg.

VIENNA, April 23rd. This forenoon the Imperial Court and the Princes thereto present have been gratified by the Wedding between the Count of Rosenberg and the Courtlady Countess of Hohenfeld. The day after tomorrow toward 2 [?] the Emperor to Karlsbad held against mid-May.

REGENSBURG, April 28th. Since the Rhine-Main Director finds himself indisposed and continues to remain so, on last Friday [?] held against mid-May.

Care toward [?] Weert given, and one doubts whether both will also be helped. Yesterday the Baggage of the Mr. Haml Bimming, Envoy of the State of the United Netherlands at the Court of the Emperor, from Vienna here arrived, and that shortly to be followed by the Ministers. These days were the Lord-Ministers and false Ministers concerning Religious matters in conversation with the Cardinal of Saxen-Zeitz, and his Eminence would soon in the Emperor's name with such hard Expeditions over the stubbornness of their Principals, in the redressing of the Churches, have addressed, that the first there thus of relieved is, that one such as cause of his indisposition to be: there and against has his K.M. the Protestant Ministers assured, that the Emperor their grievances at first shall cause remedy to be done, and has thereto the List of the redressed sought, to send to his K.M. and to that end who can see in what manner one his old Preachers.

FRANKFURT, April 30th. Yesterday afternoon the Landgrave of Hellen Darmstadt and the Count of Hanau with his entire Court here arrived. One wishes that the debate on the Mint in the Mint-hall is too small. One says that the Prince and Princess of Modena again from Luneville toward Italy have hastened.

EUROPE, May 2nd. Yesterday morning the Bishop of Munster at Paderborn took a trip to Bonn, and in the evening returned again to attend the Procession this morning.

BRUSSELS, May 1st. One has news that an East Indian Ship, which about a year and a half has been out, and sufficiently looked to when the Magistrate of the City Breda having found that in the previous Companies have been delivered Linens in buying would be found such not to be placed; has also brought; therefore so advertises the afore. Magistrate here also an equally, whistle all-here and offering examination will be done, and to a rule for the future thus lived is as the Loor with the City Seal will testify; as to the Verwerin to prevent, a very new Instruction or Ordinance; so for the sworn other side erutalieren in, of which [speedily] also made with the Three greens.

Afore Couranters is made known, as that at Rotterdam some black to be it blown, as that thereto hanging Luol with the Wipen there more also an equally, that to the mentioned Fact to discover by the Judges the most needful would be placed, that the Linens here will be in will testify; al, also the for the foremost. Fraud or uncouth 200 for the sworn Stalmasters on the one side, as for the Receiver to the common place will be.

AMSTERDAM SALES

Herd. Kerkhoff Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 7th May 1721, at 4 o'clock in the evening, in the Nes in the Brakke Ground, a party Cologne Linen goods and Silk Stuffs; consisting of 1120 pieces white Selempouris, 240 fl. white Guineas, 600 fl. Colourgres, 200 fl. Gerendeel, 140 fl. Cada Beegale; 180 fl. Douibalien, 200 fl. fine Nillas, 120 [?]. Gre Catagie, 120 fl. Moulis, 140 fl. Malmolines imported, 140 [?]. Cali 100 fl. Ackelas Gingangs, 100 fl. elie Buflen, 200 fl. Piniateurs, 100 [?]. Chailbalts, 120 vias, 263 fl. long ditto, 120 fl. coarse ditto, 100 fl. white Caroen, 200 fl. Atchiabants, and 100 fl. Chailbaries, 120 fl. Alibances, 240 fl. Gollingas, 300 fl. fine Nicnas-fl. Atchibaays, and more other Goods; [?] on the Sale day in the Vilhein, in the Inn the Seereis Lion, sold are of diverse Goods more; as through the W

Johannes Rafter Broker, shall on Wednesday the 7th May 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, buy 64 Chests extra payks Tea Boey and Congoe, eight Chests Green and 8 Chests extra Bleum, with preceding on the Keyzer-graft, over the Gold Chain, where the Jacob Magnus Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Thursday the 8th May 1721, at 5 o'clock in the evening, in the Old Broad Lepenoant, a party Caluayen, long Baayen, North Linens, Sergies, Sergies de Dames, et. long Holmines, Sergies du Bois, Durances, Dinamden, Paagnen, and other Northern Stuffs; as finished and Fulten on the Sale day can be seen; and a Sale day from 8 in the morning to 4 in the afternoon to see, and the Patterns from mentioned Broker to get Money, unless of Papers also brings.

Hen. Willingh Broker, shall on the 9th May 1721, at 4 o'clock in the afternoon precisely, at Amsterdam in the Burg on the Claegel, sell a party curious Cornelians, Cutting-stones, Billets, Clater-Cond, Sack Mirrors, and many Fiandle, English and New [?]: Mentioned Broker, on the Princes-graft by the leguiliers-graft, shall come, seals, [?] Cornelians and Stones, Venetian and German Coral, Scissors, &c., Fornisbe and Nuremberg Wares more; 200 as daily before and at the Sale can be seen.

Joh. Staars, Jn. Henmer, D. Pluskard, Jn. Willet, shall on Friday the 9th May 1721, at 4 o'clock in the afternoon, at Rotterdam sell a party extra fine Hand-[?]erninge, curious fine Tea, consisting of 45 Chests Congoe, 46 Chests, seals, and many Fiandle; being day to see, and the Patterns from mentioned Broker to get Money.

Cornelis Wachels Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 14th May 1721, at 4 o'clock in the evening precisely, a party White prepared and dyed Linens, consisting of Red, Blue, Green, Black, and diverse other Colors more; Silk Damils and banded Stuffs, Wool, Atlalice thicks, White bleached Linens, and diverse other Goods more; Honey [?], Barrels, Linens, Yarns, and other Goods, which daily before the Sale can be seen.

At Leiden printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breede-street. May 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**Exchange rates of the FLORIN today on Madrid 80 and 1 half. Cadiz 111. Seville 110. Bilbao 90. Lisbon 43 and 3 agide. Venice 83 and 1 fourth 3. Livorno 92 and 1 half to 1 fourth. Genoa 95. Geneva 94. Paris 90 agio 1 and 1 fourth. London 31 2d. 9 to 8. Danzig 287 Poland 287 and 1 fourth.**

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 5th of may, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 55.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of May, 1721

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 2 May. On Tuesday the Lord Mayor and Aldermen of this City went to the Palace of St. James to congratulate the King on the birth of the young Prince, whereupon His Majesty was pleased to give the following answer:

"The zeal and affection which you have demonstrated on all occasions for my Person and Government assure me of your participation in my joy over the happy increase of my Family. I cannot let this occasion pass without assuring you that I am deeply grieved over the calamity which the second blow of the affairs of the South Sea Company [Editor's note: the South Sea Bubble financial crisis of 1720] has brought upon you. I have, however, this consolation, that you are not alone in misfortune, for the middle of the Realm has shared in your affliction. Nothing shall give me greater satisfaction and contentment than to see you relieved of your present difficulties regarding your Trade, and the public Credit restored.

The same day, the gentlemen Humphrey Pardos, youngest of the Aldermen, and William Billers were chosen, as younger royal companions, to wait upon the City on this occasion. The Prince of Wales has been pleased to give a sum of money for the support of the Foundlings in the Westminster Parish of Westminster. On Wednesday two Petitions were presented to Parliament: one on behalf of the County of Chester, and the other on behalf of the County of Wilts, detailing the lamentable, pernicious, and disastrous effects of the evil conduct, greed, and excessive Ambition of the former Directors of the South Sea Company [Editor's note: Directors implicated in the South Sea Bubble scandal], their accomplices, subordinates, dealers and Projectors; and lamenting the decay of Commerce and the loss of public Credit; likewise on behalf of the King's high Credit which has been damaged for London, obliging the wealthy to make restitution; and on the continuation of efforts to uncover the Scene of injustice, to take such further measures as shall be deemed necessary to end the confusion, bring the truth to light, revive Trade, restore public Credit, and do justice to an injured Nation; and when the reading of these Petitions was done, they were laid upon the Table. Mr. Broderick on behalf of the Committee requested assistance, noting that the same Committee deemed it necessary to examine Sir Caswell, prisoner in the Tower, whereupon the House ordered that the Lieutenant Governor of the Tower or his Deputy should bring the said Caswell before the Committee. When this was done, a second Reading was made of the Bill to relieve the unhappy Sufferers of the South Sea Company, which was committed to Committee for the following Tuesday; and the Speaker reported the King's Answer to the Address of Congratulation which the House had presented to His Majesty on the previous Tuesday on the birth of the young Prince, in the following words:

"I thank you for this Address, which is a new proof of affection for me and my Family. You have always had the Protestant interest in these Kingdoms at heart, and I doubt not that you will also apply yourselves with steadfastness to building a firm foundation in this matter.

Yesterday a Petition was presented against the former Directors of the South Sea Company on behalf of the City of Shrewsbury, and the House ordered a Bill to be brought in regarding the Import of 2 Shillings Scots on every pint of Ale and Beer, which shall be sold in the City of Dundee: thereafter the Earl of Hereford reported that the message of Congratulation according to the custom of the House had been delivered to the Prince of Wales, and thereupon the former Committee's Report was brought to table again, to which His Royal Highness was pleased to give the following answer:

"The contribution which the House of Commons shows on the occasion of the birth of a Son is a clear proof of the continuation of your loyalty to the King, and of the generosity of this House, which you have so consistently demonstrated in all your actions; I regard this happy event as a particular token of your affection for me, and I thank the House most sincerely for it."

Thereafter the House and Committee continued to examine the Petitions of the Officers of the Regiments of Wood, Douglas, and Hamilton, which had been in service in the late War in the service of the Union according to the prophetic fulfillment which was ordered, that the Inventories of the Goods and Effects of the former Directors should each separately be printed; and then the English gentlemen, Richard Steeky, Will. Athill, James Edmondson, and Will. Tillis, the former Directors, each presented a Petition, requesting the House to take the same into consideration, and with them in their said Company regarding the decisions made and business transacted, these were laid upon the table. Mr. Broderick made known to the House on behalf of the second Committee that the Committee since their last Report until now had no new matters: in order to some consequence, and that it was also obtained that from the House report should be made when it is ready to receive it; which Report was fixed for this day; as the House also fixed the rise for this day until eight days hence: by way of the usual great [?], and the reading continued, the second Reading was made of the Bill to banish Mr. Aislabie from the Kingdom, and one should establish lights for the unhappy Sufferers of the South Sea Company to be helped, whereupon the appointment of the session was deferred to the following Monday eight days hence; and the assembly of that day proposed some Debates about that of the Money Bill also, so that it might be concluded before the middle of Summer and an end made of this session by that the previous [?] would be able to proceed quickly.

---

Wednesday the Courant.

[continued:] Thereupon Mr. Modenck replied that he had 200 Georgics to learn at Delft of the Air and the Land, provided at least that it would not be hard for him to remain in service of his Fatherland until further order from mid-Summer in the City. Yesterday George Camel was examined by the first Committee regarding what the Postmaster Crags with the Company's clients in the matter [?] of money. Yesterday the House the first Reading was adopted of the Bill to make the River Mercy from Liverpool all the way to Manchester navigable, whereupon they adjourned until Wednesday. The former Directors petition Parliament each for a certain Sum to be allowed them from their submitted Duplicates, but it is believed they will meet with refusal here. The first Committee has held hearings regarding the 30,000 pounds Sterling that have been given to the old Mr. Crags without any payment being made for it; and also regarding the dealings of the sick Maccabees, Wilmerson, Shaw, Strood, Owen, and Grandmor, who as Assistant-Helpers of the Directors are to be accused before Parliament. The first Committee has consulted with the learned Advocates in Brussels regarding the delivery of Sir Knight the same, to which it appears that this privilege belongs to the Landsgemeinde, but not to a Vice-Officer, and that this is particularly evident from the aforesaid Article. Mr. Robert Walpole, being recovered, was again in Parliament on Wednesday.

GERMANY AND THE ADJOINING REALMS.

HAMBURG, 2 May. This morning Mr. Schlem was appointed as Secretary of this City, in place of Mr. Koch, who has become Proto-Notary; and Mr. Wijkerna as Archivist, in place of Councillor Stamgeel. The gentlemen Deputies from the Diocese of this City are preparing to undertake the journey to Vienna, and it is expected that this embassy will have a good outcome. From the Street-Watch one learns that the gentlemen Barons and Counts Lt. Strömfeld and Wulfrad, together with Major Barneke, who the same previous [time] under the tolling of the Cannon from there have departed, have by the continuing headwind not been able to come further than Glückstadt.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 2 May. An Order of the King's Council of State is seen, containing that the Holders or Buyers of Annuities, 200 perpetual, Life Annuities, or on the Tailles [Editor's note: a form of French taxation], may in their payment make use of the Annuities for which they have deposited the Receipts in the King's Treasury, and this even though the Contracts and Receipts of the Finance have not yet been expedited: and that at the Town Hall no agreements shall be concluded until the Holders have presented the said verified Contracts. The Duke of Bourbon being continuously disposed in no condition to take the right place in the Assembly of the States of Burgundy, which shall soon convene; the said gentleman has taken it upon himself, but has not further taken the Bishop of Châlons on the Marne as Adjunct with him. In some days all the Stock Jobbers and Dealers in the Bank were sent away, to go negotiate in the long Street Quincampoix, where they, according to permission, may conduct business; but in the Royal Papers more losses than profits are made; the Shares with the Seal are at 44 and those with [?] at 333; the Notes of 1000 Livres are about 70 Livres, and the others in proportion. One was at the beginning of this week greatly astonished at the Order whereby the [?] the beginning of the Order, whereby the conclusion of all [?] on the Town Hall: one is no less amazed that in the respective Provinces the Receivers do not [?] with all rigor in advance collect the Money and Effects for which a considerable augmentation regarding the Money-species is later expected; this finds the more credence because one, whoever it may be, and even the King's Troops are not excepted. It is reported that the Count Bielke, Minister of the King of Sweden at this Court, would have suggested that one would greatly oblige His Majesty if the King would send the Swedish Troops, which are in the same service, to return home; but one does not know what answer was given to this. The first of these two Concerts were held with the young King; the first at the home of His Majesty, and the second when the King was seated at table in the afternoon. The same day the Duke of Orleans was complimented on behalf of the Court. The young Monarch will tomorrow in the Plain of Sablons, on horseback, review the French and [?] Guards. One doubts not a considerable reformation of the Troops, it being expected that the Inspectors and Officers according to custom would depart to their Departments; and one is obliged in the Royal Printing-House already busy printing the Regiment, on what footing everything should be established. The King has granted a Lottery to the Hospital, where the Foundlings are used, whose profit having been some time meagerly managed. One takes in the meantime this week the good [?].

BRUSSELS, May 1st. The Illustrious Isuaren of the Bishop of Ghent, called by his great [?], have placed the Frislaan outside the city.

Jacobus Bastekus, tobacco dealer in The Hague, who for many years has lived daily at 4 o'clock on the Groenmarkt, directly opposite the Poortkuyst, where these [?].

This evening at 3 o'clock, being Wednesday, May 7th, 1721, the broker Jo will sell a new fine selected Tea from the Boer and Corange. At the Great [?] of the Gosd, but now newly arrived from sea, lying on the Keyzersgraft, over the Goudden; the terms for money in the Nee in the Brekke Ground to be obtained.

This same evening, being Wednesday, May 7th, 1721, the broker Ginad will sell a quantity of Carmine Linens and Silk Fabrics; consisting of 1120 fl. Ouliers, 100 fl. Grisfaen, 300 fl. spo Carjes, 167 fl. Mauris, 140 fl. Malmolens 7 vie and. Froe Nlles res 6. Turkish Gingham, 100 fl. effe Balten, 200 fl. Pinaschoes, re fl. Niegannaize, 340 fl. long ditto, 120 fl. coarse ditto, 100 fl. white Cotton, 200 fl. Atel melded Brekke Ground to be.

Jacob Magnus broker will sell in Amsterdam on Thursday, May 8th, 17[21] a good quantity of Curlaven, long Baayen, Northern Woolen cloth, Sergies, Sergies of the Dam bound Calamanten, Edamines, Sergies du Bois, Durances, Damalien, Popelyncne, purchasing day from 7 in the morning and 8 to midday at 4 o'clock to be viewed.

Franco Condeloelo, Jan de Viyer and Greq. Mees brokers will on Friday the 9th at the Wynstraat, in the Heidelerg the Black Lion, sell an excellent quantity of fine Gold Carmine and the State's Buyers Tea, now just arrived from sea; and this day to be viewed, and those Menflurs may obtain money from the said brokers.

Cora. de R.sos and Isaac ichtielings brokers will in Amsterdam on Monday at the Old Gentlemen's Lodging sell a Bond of 9000 gft. at the Amsterdam Office, and a Lottery-letter, worth 1300 Gft., also in name, at the Gouda Office.

Jan Wolfers broker will on Tuesday, May 13th, 1721, in the afternoon at 4 o'clock buy a quantity of extra fine hand-Roitingen, curious Cornalyne Hegies, large Con. Smyfdoolen, Spectacles, Clarex-Gold, Pocket-Mirror, and very many French, English and No [?] sold by the said broker, on the Princegraft by the Reguliersgraft, shall [?] on Tuesday, May 13th, 1721, in the evening at 4 o'clock, a party of Caslon Linens and Silk Fabrics, consisting of 200 Ruksje Chipeuiers, as ditto, 400 fl. Garlains, 320 fl. raw Pejskallen, 200 fl. garep Roemails, 200 fl. Chiffon ditto, 240 fl. Rognelans, 100 fl. Turkish Gingham, 340 fl. Malmolens; 360 fl. Casli eo fl. Hamans, 200 fl. Rol-Armozyen, distributed in diverse sorts of fine Colors; on Thursday, May 20th, 1721, in the afternoon at one o'clock, shall be sold in the Blerke and Bygrraaden, 3 Tu'pe-Tents with their Sails, and repaired Baguettes, English and Luylkle Aurioles bolkea, and bequeathed by the Gentlemen Freidenicus and Hubertus Dekkers at Leiden, [?] Commails of Duyit, Dierven and Jan Wittig brokers will in Amsterdam day at 3 o'clock, in the New Hiere Lodging, on the Haarlem [...medydyk, a large St. Petersburg Hemp, with gadders 70 Bouden Spiegelbarits Touk, It is claimed many fine loaded [...] sold dourtinmeft hept, strong and well cured, Loden and further Tuyp and Spelhuye contained therein. Standing and situated at Uordrecht, bequeathed by the late Mr. Goovert van Welel, in his [?] life Sel wyda sunken, address yourselves to Franpois de Vries on the Wynhauen at Rotte.

IN AMSTERDAM, May 5th. At Lisbon the ship of Captain Adrenio da Fonseca from St. Michael has arrived; and news was had that: the sea freight [?] knew not from Bahia (which considerable and here java there might have of S... good &c. on board) well in May there might sailors of Holland O... from Lisbon to Goa departed, and there one in that city the Holland O... from Lisbon to Goa sent, and there now one in that city the Holland O... were there some cargo in the neck-ter at St. Ubes saying ships, well in Engelbert their cargo in the nec-ren, in April seen at Gibraltar well o at 12 Engine[?] Zittena audi of the wili. Walter Moxton, the Engel[...]hard Brom, the Zittena audi ... of the wili. Walter Moxton, the Engelhard Brom, the Engel audi of the wili. Bistuus Capt. Th. Hauged that himself the Czaar and Halop Brough Sc. Ostend Captain [...] Rigsa [...] that himself the Czaar and Halop Brough [...] Zittena audi [...] overtake yet 200 Kaanen. from farmers come, also [...] Danzig lay of the rengenwaid Flora Black, Douwe Lüeke then St. Hoeward Han[?] rengenwaid Flora Black, Douwe Lüeke then St. Hoeward Han[?] through naming theme the r other. On the Ree of Danzig lay and [?] rengenwaid Theame of the r other. On the Ree of Danzig lay through rengenwaid and [...] Nieks and there towards St. Milo and Brest must the French ship. Zittena audi in the berg of February at Algiers we loaded not that in the berg of February at Algiers we loaded not well es Oil. At Roemer has arrived Kosmei Likles from Boorn; to well es Oil. At Roemer has arrived Kosmei Likles from Boorn; to well es Oil. At Roemer has arrived [?] Dirge the Great [?] an-well es Oil. At Roemer has arrived [...] Likles from Boorn; to the Greete the Graete the Catharina Jan Koeyman from Hamburg; at Londel of the St. Francisco Captain Nicolaes . Stockholm (where from the Scheeren dres Olofie from Strengzoent; at Stockholm (where from the Scheeren dres Olofie from Strengzoen; at Stockholm (where from the Scheeren wul and Scotsmen there herewards, Königsberg &c. golden [...] the at Kareckrom of the Kareckrom from Lisbon and Genoa; at Bremen a d Koopen-the 2 Kareckrom from Lisbon and Genoa; at Bremen a d Koopen-raardens from the East Sea, and at Helmslad [...] berleden Jacq Gortembung. raardens from the East Sea, and at Helmslad [...] Pietertle from Gottemberg. Zittena audi still itself of the hope Zacharia Louder towards Nantes sailed is. Zittena audi still itself of the hope Zacharia Louder towards Nantes sailed is. moreover the Tuesday and Thursday are all the ships lying in the Vlie towards Norway the East Sea from [?] gone out.

IN THE HAGUE, May 6th. Since the arrival of Prince William of Hes [...] altjmet, the same has been received by the General Count Home-petich and Gentlemen of the Government. The Gentleman Bentingh, Director of the Prince &c., has arrived here from Overcffel, and appeared before the Assembly of the Gentlemen States General. The Gentlemen of Haarlem, of Zwolle, and de Bruyn Bentinck have taken their seats away the Province of Overijssel in the Generality's Chamber of Accounts. The Gentlemen High Water-Boards of Rhineland gathered here yesterday and this day.

No. 1721.No. 56.
