Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 19th of May, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 21 April. It is worth noting that news arrived in this Bay within 11 days from Vera Cruz, reporting that the Fleets departed from the Harbor of Veracruz on the 26th of August according to ordinance, growing in the most favorable manner, and in the Land [?] in April made the usual movements, to be here in September, which is considered a regular trading schedule. It is reported that Vera Cruz was again closed [?] from Cadiz on its journey to the Harbor, along with the Ship of Captain Diego Tanschco Żac., as well as a Ship from China, which [?] the trade goods from Granada, from here to Cologne also brought Merchandise in the Goods. In that time to dispatch here [?], where the Governor from Mexico also [?] to prepare a Market, which was duly arranged through both parties, whereof the affairs and many Goods, [etc.], were noted, such that upon the Remonstrances of the Commerce College in Mexico, to the [?] Orders it again made the Market hold the necessary [?]. Now news arrived concerning Silver [?] and yet another on Board, and that thus would also have to be brought to the Mint: this happened before as a Relief, yet none as yet [?]; to Kitus Catholinhe, 21 Sacks Basilica, 210 Sacks Jalappes, and other Goods.

The Court has issued new orders that the Galleons, as before and in keeping with strict measures against [?], were surprised. From the 1st Pier. Fuyk, along with several other Ships, sailed toward Amsterdam.

France

PARIS, 12 May. Following the Letters from Madrid of the 29th preceding, the King Don Francisco Carlos, the Queen Prudence, and the Government of St. John of Ura in New Spain were assigned to Brigadier Don Guillermo de la Vieira, Colonel of the Regiment of Galicia; and the Government of Curaçao to Don Feliciano [?] Don Christobal de Salor, Lieutenant Colonel of the Regiment of Lisbon. Word is sent to the Court regarding work on a reform of the Troops; but in Antwerp again the [?] the Wrestlings [?] in Britain, to have the Regiments in the Month of June complete. Yesterday there were festivities held in the [?] Balls, though the King did not return there without Mr. Eualon placing himself in the throng, which was good, wishing to save [himself], jumped over a [?] with [?] Knob upon it, where by [?] fell, and raising the struck Lower [?], turning he could however with Blood of the Trusty Ambassador held himself there as well as in the [?] Combat: yesterday Ball was held again, where said Ambassador also lightened, where yet in the Opera of Amsterdam [?], finds much diminished in the arrangements, and the Music. Yesterday the King appointed the Duke of Chartres as Colonel General of the Infantry; which Prince since the death of the Duke of [?] has not been in favor. Friday we said that we became aware of the [?], and cried there [?] Elizabeth [?] wide again. The Tuileries are daily well [?] with Princes and Princesses before, who go for walks. The [?] of the [?], which since the [?] of the [?] has in part been [?] in the foundation of Secretary of State of the [?], is on the [?] of this Month dismissed by the King. Those, who this [?] again show, has in the Department of Mr. Le Blanc, to great [satisfaction] of the Troops. The Knight de Breteuil has [his] Company in the Guard, as well as two Companies of the Artillery [?], so one will to great [?] all [?] from the Kö-nacht, [?] with Mr. d'Orlay, who also Captain in the Guard is, upon order of the Cardinal and Navailles [?] of Mr. d'Argenton, upon which the full midday [?]; the Funeral Procession was then [?] to be, various Troops [?] stored with the common People, where in that great multitude [?] great [?] with Bottles and Glasses in hand, scolding and cursing the Deceased, who [his] second Son to universal [...] has found [?]. The Chancellery equally [?] at Pontchartrain found itself [?] has sent [?], lay in the [?] of the Church by the Dutch Ambassador were arrested, his [?] was [?] called. One shall then two [?] Congress at Karamesk at the end of [?]. One shall then two [?] Congress at Karamesk at the end of their [?] half the Favorites of the Court to be, themselves they give unmistakable signs of their standing Honor, their [settlement?] to Forelands are Mr. of Mainne, the Marshal of Villeroy, the Princess de Ventadour, the Bishop of Prieus, the Cardinal of Rohan and other Lords of Rank. The Cardinal de Mailli is so far in health recovering, that, if he could obtain permission, to assume the royal Army. The Abbot d'Autels de Forteille [?], and his Brother has nothing else but the change of the Place of his Banishment [been able to obtain]. Regarding the [?] Rome in the Conclave opening, runs yet the [?] and Laborer [?] say that the Cardinal [?] the [?] Party in the Conclave holds, and also much is spoken of the Decree that the Cardinal Pamphilij has proposed, for the purpose that the Pope in the future no [?] could publish in [...] of the undertaking of the Sacred College. The Conclavist of the Cardinal Salerno is placed outside the Conclave, because he his work made at the door of the [?] to maintain, what is the same handled. One says that the Count of [?], who has arrived there, his House-work from Germany hither [brings].

Great Britain

LONDON, 11 May. The Directors of the S.S. Company decided on Friday to give Dividend against the Hundred in payment, for the holders of Shares, and for each Share 13 Shillings and 6 Pence in value, if the Millions to the Company would be restituted.

The Negotiation of 400,000 Pounds Sterling on the S.S.C. is still not complete. The Investments are becoming mighty and with many difficulties lifted, and especially in the Provinces, where some against the Collectors of Taxes say, that the S.S.C. here delays everything, until they the Interest of their Annuities as before receiving, which Money in hand get. That same day were regarding the City of London, by the County's Petition against the usual Directors of the S.S.C. presented to a Lagehouse: that according to the order of the day in consideration took a portion of the Report of the last Committee on the 1st previous [?] in, as also a portion of the Report of the 1st Formal Craggi [?], that pertained to the Lords Nuuon and Tuffusis, sons-in-law of said Lord Craggi, Members of the Court, have placed themselves, and by their Advocates to be heard, before the House any Resolution took, was resolved that they on [Friday?] would be bound. Upon the complaints, heard the 27th previous Month in the Council, and in the Streets London and Westminster, and other Places thereabout, by young People were held the most unjustified and Godless gatherings, where the Divine Almighty himself was heard, was Friday Council held, wherein the King was present, and the Lord Chancellor received orders swiftly [...]

England

Religion being hindered; and a certain Lady, who called herself the Holy Maid, claims that, being full and drunk, the Spirit possessed her and she spoke in tongues. At this time Postmaster Craggs was being examined in the House of Commons. The examination of Postmaster Craggs in the House of Commons was concluded on February 11, 1719, in which it was established that the aforementioned Knight had offered to bid him farewell; that on the 17th of the month 30,000 pounds sterling from the Company's capital was named, and on March 28, 1720 was lent to him, and that on March [?] 1720 a further 10,000 pounds in addition was given to the said Mr. Craggs; whereby it was noted that one of the Company's agents of Knight and the Governors and Directors had been [?], concerning the reasons and protocols of religious matters [?] regarding which he had obtained since December 1, 1719, and this should come to affect the heirs of the unfortunate sufferers in the South Sea Company; and this was included in the Bill with a clause concerning the heirs of the South Sea Company, which passed with 104 votes. Today the Lords held the second reading regarding religious matters [?], and the House adjourned until Tuesday. The Commons have taken consideration of public Credit in hand, and together passed with 104 votes to come to the aid of the unfortunate sufferers in the South Sea Company with 7 million pounds. The stocks were: Action 133 [?]. [?]. 1745. Million Bank 135. Lottery and Bonds 97. African Company 40, new ditto 31. J.B. 18.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, May 16. With the ship Endracht from Suratte, which had sailed from Eastern Austria [Ostend?] three to four weeks ago, [and] which had lain poorly for some weeks in the Bay of All Saints, letters were received in London from the said Bay with news that the Lisbon Fleet with its return ships had entered there in January; and that the departure of the said Fleet toward Lisbon from the Bay was announced for March 20. The Commerce Committee there had fixed the price of sugar at 1500 Reis per arroba, and because there was an oversupply of sugar, tobacco, etc. in that Bay, it was firmly established that the Fleet would certainly depart at the appointed time. Recently some ships have gone out to sea from the Vliet; and from Texel one ship for Hoorn and one for Enkhuizen bound for the East Indies.

DORDRECHT, May 17. Arrived before this city: Abraham van Coppel, Conraad, Boelman, and the Hope Jan Lugien van Dorp; and departed: the William Salomon Chapman, the Thomas and Catharina Will. Lewis, the Joseph and Henry, Henry Wood, the Exchange Christoffel Foy, the Oweria Goodwill John Boor, the Mary Golwith Warton, the Sparrow Ingram Chapman, all for England; the Liefde Gerr. Rynders for Hamburg, the Moor Jan Scnuyten for Bruges, and Maliham van Capell for Dunkirk.

AMSTERDAM, May 17. The ship St. Louis, Captain Rolland, which was to go from St. Domingo to Havre de Grace, was forced by storm to put into Martinique; also a ship that had departed from Bordeaux thither was attacked by an Algerian pirate with sharp cannon fire, after which they, in the attempt to board the ship, charged the crew with cutlasses; the Algerian pirates cruised off Bordeaux and Rochelle, wherefore the Dutch ships lying there and at Nantes had received orders not to depart.

At Bordeaux (where Jacob Jacobte Rokje is) there arrived the [ship] Siden for [?] Tyme from here; at Coudres the Paper Mill Corn. Broer-dey de Mas; at Rotterdam Alex. Thomson, Samuel and James Melville of Aberdeen; and at Texel Lambert Jorisville from the East Indies, Gerrit Luytsds from Dunkirk, Corn. Koeffer, Corn. Groot and Brother Aiken from Norway; as well as for the city Fied. Pieterse from [?], and Pls Groos from Riga.

THE HAGUE, May 15. The Lords States of Holland and West Friesland, continuing their assembly on Tuesday, have appointed the Gentlemen and Nader Heibert Diel, etc., to the position of High-Heemraad [water board administrator] of Delfland, in place of the late Mr. Adriaan van der Goed, Lord of Naties, etc.; likewise their Excellencies have appointed the Lord Adriaan van der Duyn, etc., also as High-Heemraad of Delfland, in place of the late high-born Lord Wilhelm Frisch Baron van Wassenaer, Lord of Ruyven, etc.; regarding the lottery (down to the 7th Class) that the drawing must take place, at the latest before the expiration of the right of the undistributed prizes, and that the drawing of the 7th Class is noted. Since the ticket No. 64930, in the name of E.G. Snoukaert, has been laid down at 1, it is further announced that the same at the Office of the [?] has been received. Notice is hereby given that again in the Barony of Wassenaer on the last Monday of this month, May, being May 27, 1721, there will be a free horse market; therefore all those who own horses of ten years or above are requested to appear promptly; also all who have a pair of horses will enjoy one night's free pasturing or fodder.

Nicolaus Ismering of Alkmaar will sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday, May 21, in the Doelstraat, a fine collection of rough diamonds, 200 both large and small; the aforementioned gems to be seen.

Johannes Verduyn and Philip Piek, art dealers, will sell at Amsterdam (according to appointment) various fine and pleasant paintings, including several with Turkish heads, all to be seen.

J.F. Zomer and Gyabert Hol. Meyer, selling skillful and capital paintings, including several works by Vorbius, [?], especially to be seen beforehand; the sale is to take place at Meulder's, on the Stukke and Oakhofhoorn, in the Guildhall "the Golden Maiden," on the High Newstreet, [with?] a beautiful collection of fine tulips, consisting [of?] Here Jan Wilip, [?] on the South side of the new Veertiade [?] and subsequently in the Garden, at 's Gravelthuys.

---

Petersburg, April 25

It is suspected that Her Czarist Majesty herself will arrive on May 1st or 2nd with the entire Court at Reval, at which time the other Ministers of His Czarist Majesty will likewise proceed there; it is supposed that the foreign Ministers will now follow the Court to that city. The Danish Minister, Mr. von Westphalen, arrived at Riga some days ago. Mr. Tietholm, Legation Secretary of the King of Denmark, has informed the foreign Ministers here present of the death of the Queen of Denmark. Word has subsequently been received that His Royal Majesty, recovering in health, [?]; it is supposed that the Duchess of Holstein will [?], as it were, because Prince Ferdinand von Wirtemberg had arrived there from Hungary, in order, as one hears, to carry out the marriage with the Widow Duchess of Courland; and some reports will say [?].

Vienna, May 9

Sunday evening the Imperial Express Courier Belcroti returned here from Rome with letters for the Court, from which it is understood that matters in the Conclave proceed slowly, as the election may well be delayed until all the Cardinals on their way have arrived there. Monday there was a great crowd of people in the streets, because the half-moon and a great star appeared very clearly from west to east. Yesterday ten more wagons with supplies were sent toward Carlsbad, whither the Empress will follow on Monday.

Regensburg, May 12

Friday in the Council meeting, owing to the indisposition of the Electoral-Mainz Minister, [proceedings?] were postponed. The Protestant Ministers are preparing a list of grievances to be submitted to the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz. According to reports from Vienna, His Royal Majesty would have determined to have all the abuses that have crept in regarding religious matters corrected, thereby setting an example to the Italian States. Meanwhile a mandate from King Augustus has arrived, who is lodged with the Cardinal of Saxe-Zeitz.

Frankfurt, May 14

All the princely and other estates [?], the meeting having ended, [?].

Cologne, May 16

Many prominent persons from Frankfurt proceed daily here by royal authority toward Aachen. The Bishop of Münster [?] to be at Liège.

[This section of the transcription is heavily corrupted in the original.]

---

Course of Exchange

**Exchange rates for the day:** Madrid 99 to 85. Cadiz 110 to 109. Seville 108. Bilbao 89. Lisbon 41 and 5/8 to 3/4. Venice 126. Livorno 95. Genoa 94. Geneva 94. Paris 41 and 1/2 to 1/4. London 33 shillings 10 pence. Danzig 127 Polish groats. Bremen 13 and 11/16. Hamburg 12 and 1/4. St. Antwerp 2 and 3/4 percent. Ghent 1 and 1/2. Brussels 2 and 3/4. Zierikzee 3 and 5/8 to 1/2. Rotterdam 3 [guilders]. The Bank premium at 5 and 1/4 to 5/8. Guilders; the stocks of the East India Company were 735, West India Company 152 remaining, Subscription 1060, the Bank 132, East India Company 138 to 40.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 19th of may, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 61.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 21st of May, 1721

WEST INDIES

CURAÇAO, 19 March. Since the last report, several ships and barks have departed from here to the surrounding islands, and conversely some others have arrived; also on the eleventh of this month the ship Providentia, Jan Roelofs from Amsterdam, arrived with news that Marcus Stadema and Willem Stoel had arrived there safely. Captain Christoffel Tym lies in good condition at the Boyer coast; from where the Sara Cally, Jan Valk and Captain Jacobje had sailed to other places; and for this city several Zeeland ships from the Caracas coast are being observed approaching. So also many ships under Warnard van Staele, Hodt-drink Tymen and Alexander Mils are heading to Amsterdam; however the ships expected from there have not yet arrived here.

SWEDEN

GEFLE, 3 March. From this harbor more than 100 to 200 large and small galleys have set out to sea, after some vessels had reconnoitered two days before to determine if Russian ships were at sea; the half of the aforesaid galleys will remain at Karleby, and the other half under Colonel Faber for further orders at Loofstad; where they will be reinforced with 26 large galleys, which are newly [built] in the Northern cities. Last night new orders were received from Stockholm that all regiments in those quarters—both foot and horse—under Colonel and General Count Hamilton must encamp near this city for the continent; the aforesaid regiments, which together with the retained peasants, who have been drilled throughout the entire winter by officers on half pay, are presumably 19,000 men strong, and furnished with 29 cannons and several field pieces; so that one is not concerned here about the enemy's undertaking; all the more so because our garrison of eight regiments on foot and horse will be reinforced; the new Fredriik-Schans will be improved and soon planted with guns, so that no ship can pass by it; and since the King has the main army around Stockholm and the warships and Karlskrona are already in a state to be able to put to sea, it is hoped to block the enemy in all places.

DENMARK

COPENHAGEN, 13 May. According to letters from Stockholm, the French Minister, Monsieur Campredon, was very often in conference with the Swedish Ministry, though one could not yet finally determine what might consist of the report of the Commission on Great Russia; but it is said that the Second Court would not be satisfied with it. The Regiment of Dragoons that in Scania had refused to serve on foot and had been abandoned by their officers, themselves appointed officers from among their own; they maintained good order and discipline among themselves in the quarters and gave assurance that they were always prepared according to their duty and ready to risk their bodies and lives in the service of the King, if one would let them serve on horseback; the newly formed regiment, on the order of the commanding general there, drew themselves together, which general came to offer them a pardon, but they refused to accept it and said they would serve only on horseback; and they announced they would march; but the general seeing that all gentle means were in vain, let the Life Company of the mounted regiment of Nordskonen advance, and immediately sent word to have their baggage seized, and afterwards the rest of that regiment followed; the aforesaid general thereupon ordered these dragoons to be surrounded on all sides on foot, and subsequently to be shot; which indeed succeeded in such manner, and many suffered thereafter. They will have been forced to resist for some time and subsequently surrendered on the trains; of the aforesaid Life Company 9 or 10 men remained dead in the first discharge which the rebels fired; and some were wounded; and the rebels had scarcely been disarmed when one had the fifth man hanged. The ringleaders, who have been taken into arrest, are now being brought to trial. This morning the King, together with the Queen [text unclear] returned from somewhere. Her Majesty will tomorrow begin to busy herself with the regiments lying in garrison here; and it is said that the King will perhaps make some promotion among the officers tomorrow morning. At four o'clock yesterday, the British Admiral [?] saw fit to depart with the fleet; at six o'clock it was completely under sail and disappeared from view; and although the wind yesterday changed somewhat, one does not doubt that the aforesaid fleet will advance to around Dornholm to conjoin with the Swedish; but it is said differently what commission the British Admiral Norris might have. It is believed that soon the 2 aforesaid fleets will make reconnaissance, after which they will sail along the Hanseatic coast to prevent the departure of the Muscovite warfleet as much as possible. P.S. It is said that a further express from the Second Court to their Plenipotentiaries at [?] has been sent; with further instruction as ambassador for the peace work including the aforesaid throne of the Czar. The French Minister Monsieur Campredon would also have sent an express with important dispatches to his court, whose return the aforesaid minister would remain in Stockholm.

GREAT BRITAIN

LONDON, 16 May. Tuesday evening the young Prince was born and named William Augustus; for godparents were the King of Prussia and the Duke of York, who were represented by [?] the Duke of Grantham and Lord Lumley, great steward [?] of the Prince of Wales; the Queen of Prussia being treated as godmother by the Duchess of Dorset; and it is said that the young Prince will have the title of Duke of Lancaster. That same day, on behalf of several cities and counties, eight petitions were presented to the Lower House against the late Directors of the South Sea Company, their assistants and confederates, etc.; which being read were laid on the table. That very day very many persons came who have annuities in the South Sea Company. In a coffee house nearby they assembled and resolved to present a petition to the Treasury and also to the Directors of the South Sea Company, requesting that relief be granted and reparation be made for the excessive losses they must suffer. Wednesday morning those [persons] who were to sign the petitions did so and handed them over yesterday. Monday the Lord Chancellor, pursuant to an order of the Council and to expose the infamous plots, dispatched a circular letter to all the justices of the peace of the cities of London and Westminster and the county of Middlesex, wherein them strictly ordered to make careful investigation, according to the aforesaid order, whose copy was read to them, so that such unheard-of societies might be suppressed. The Princess Caroline at the Palace of St. James has been seized with chickenpox. Wednesday the Prince of Baden-Durlach arrived here from Holland; having greeted the Queen at St. James, he also proceeded to the Prince of Wales at Kew House. That day petitions were again presented to the Lower House against the late Directors of the South Sea Company and these being read and laid on the table, Monsieur Haldane introduced a Bill to continue the excise on every pint of ale and beer sold in the city of Dundee; and with the revenue thereof to pay the debts of that city, the first reading of which being done, Mr. Farrer reported the conclusion reached the previous day concerning public credit, in our previous report; "after a lengthy debate [it] was approved with the following determination: "that for the restoration of public credit, relief to the amount of 1,563,100 pounds sterling, 4 shillings and 11 pence, the South Sea Company shall be given, in respect of the payment of the Millions for four and a half years, and one year's purchase of further annuities and other national debts; and finally the Company, in order to [?] its profit to provide finally to the holders and those interested in the same Company, such compensation shall be given to the public and such relief to the holders as the House shall think fit to determine." Yesterday the Lords postponed the debate concerning the rebuilding of the Sleeping Chamber at Westminster for eight days; after which their lordships heard the advocates for and against the Bill to determine the daily wages of tailors' apprentices, and further debated it in Committee on next Tuesday. That day another petition was presented to the Lower House against several of the aforesaid late South Sea Directors, which being read and laid on the table; and Sir David Dalrymple introduced a Bill to charge every pint of ale and beer sold in the city and liberty of Jedburgh with a tax of [?] shillings, toward the payment of the debts of that city and other necessary purposes, the first reading of which was done. The House being informed that the brokers Samuel Straud, Edward [?] and Joseph Shaw, who in the Report of the Secret Committee made on the [?]th of this month are accused of many evil practices, and being ordered to be examined by the House on next Monday, they in the meantime dispose of their goods and effects to evade the justice of the House, ordered that the aforesaid brokers be placed in the custody of the Serjeant-at-arms; after which the examination of the members of the House was postponed to next Monday and the House in grand Committee considering the Bill to relieve the unfortunate sufferers in the South Sea Company and to exclude Monsieur Joshua Aislaby from the kingdom, a discussion arose concerning many fixed goods which the aforesaid Aislaby as jointure for his wife had purchased, bringing in 2,500 pounds sterling annually, besides moreover much money which he has settled upon his children, and it appeared that all this had been done since his pious practices; and on some progress being made in the Bill, further consideration was postponed to next Saturday, when it is hoped to hear that the aforesaid goods and moneys will be declared [forfeit] for the benefit of the unfortunate sufferers in the South Sea Company. Yesterday evening an express was sent to Colonel Stanhope to Madrid. The House today in grand Committee considering the public credit has read a proposal of Monsieur Hutchinson and after a debate lasting 2 or 3 hours, the following question was put to the Committee: "that the loss caused by the bad management of the affairs of the South Sea Company has the same proportion as what the Company owes to the public credit, as well as to the holders and those with subscriptions [?]," which proposition was passed in the negative; whereupon the Committee on a proposal of Monsieur Lechmoun came to the following resolution: "that it is the opinion of this Committee that for the restoration of public credit and the satisfaction of the minds of his Majesty's subjects, the several holders of shares and those who have further interest in what concerns the South Sea Company shall be placed in a state of security, and it is hoped not to proceed further on next Tuesday. It is said that one of the members of the Treasury has been removed from office. It is reported that the son of Sir Knight would have come into a post [?] that his father is ready to come to England, but he is being asked that he be pardoned. The shares of the Bank are 113; East India Company 117; South Sea 143 and 1/2; Million Bank 132; Lottery and Bank A[ction] 97; Allepol Company 49; [text unclear] 11 and 1/2.

Year 1721.

Leydse West India [News]

JAMAICA, 17th March. There arrived here recently one of the merchant ships dispatched from London in 1710 with glass cargo, and brings us good tidings that ships of [?], the remainder having encountered much favorable Divine providence, the remaining vessels from the Gold Coast had taken; the remaining prospects.

MANY recently fallen upon this coast. O Goods of P. Jann [?]...

CURAÇAO, 19th March. [...] with much care to the watch of the [?] where the Admiralty presents itself, concerning [?] towards [?] has shown. Monday the Nuntios of Venice Zeebrügge authorised by

ROME, 1st May. On Sunday the disease in the Church of H. T. Aldin was met [?]: Thing Considerene, as it was ended at Bandissen. The small

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 13th May. On Thursday the youngest Count of [?] with his Consort came forth. Market in the Cape of [...] Marquis van Wettelino, who nearly concerning in to us sent in this city in short his Consort the Countess [?] and hanging Dragoons, some Lands more... [text continues with damaged/illegible portions]

AMSTERDAM, 18th May. [?] to happen [...] pursued, and among other things through the bringing of our Golden Cape. Peal. van Chios coming thither, is as other more Birth mentioned. At Gelbuen, is Captain [?] Captaine, Harquist [...] the [?] to Monument. the summer Cape. Zands to Barcelons already on Coast Cape. Mottle and Zamen al Aleks al Akse past through intrigue Prussian trapped, in that of the great [...] growing [?].

At Leyden from the Printing House of FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedeltrraat [?], the 21st May 1721. With Privilege.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 21st of may, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 62.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 23rd of May, 1721

No. 61.

Year 1721.

West Indies

JAMAICA, 17 March. Several of our merchant ships have arrived here from Porto Bello and Cartagena with a brisk trade, having acquired much cargo from Swedish vessels. The Spanish had posted guards along the coast. About 200 men or more, in fact, were devoted to two large English privateers that plundered the valuable Spanish merchandise from the south side, which had come from silver, gold, and the finest goods. The voyage at sea was troubled, and the dangers were so great that the Viceroy of Lima did not dare send silver to Panama, because one of these privateers was operating near the half-way point around the city. Here are the large privateers Betty Cape, Kith-Eliot, the Mary, Peace, etc., from London and Bristol, hastily sailing back to London with extensive and valuable merchant goods. Near Martinique, 2 [privateers?] [?] also operate in this region.

CURAÇAO, 29 March. Off the Caribbean coast lie the merchant ships of Commodore Barbiers, Jan Bontekoe, Matt. Regemse, Bash-uis, Wiggins, Will Boughtylen, Bertram Vermoylen, etc. Arrived in this harbor: Alexander Fenix from New York. No reports are heard here of pirates, and good merchant ships [are secure].

Spain

CADIZ, 22 April. A merchant vessel from Gallipoli has arrived here in 47 days, bringing news from there to the harbor, and with the advice having come over from here, which report the Viceroy of Mexico (who was here sailing in 1712) after lengthy debates had delivered, [regarding] the royal mint, as before, to be established in Mexico; and that in the arrangement of goods much has changed. Because the ministers are withholding the distributed money and provisions, all private individuals are very eager to have their silver brought to the mint.

Italy

NAPLES, 19 April. After Prince Borghese, our Viceroy, had paid a visit, entertainment was undertaken. Friday afternoon, Cardinal Pignatelli, our Archbishop, was with the Viceroy to speak briefly, after which His Excellency yesterday morning undertook the journey to Rome. That day, the feast of Saint Mark on the nameday of the Viceroy, a gala was held. Monday, the Nuncio Vicentii and Signor Vicent, Resident of Venice, had public audience with the Viceroy.

NAPLES, 29 April. After Prince Borghese, our Viceroy, had by intermediary delivered the compliments regarding the advancement of the Admiralty, His Excellency did not hesitate regarding the matter of the settlement. Friday afternoon Cardinal Pignatelli, our Archbishop, was with the Viceroy to take leave, after which the Cardinal yesterday morning undertook the journey to Rome. That day, the feast of Saint Mark and the nameday of the Viceroy, a gala was held at court. Monday, the Nuncio Vicentii and Signor Vicent, Resident of Venice, had public audience with the Viceroy.

ROME, 3 May. Sunday the cardinals gathered in conclave in the Church of Saint Peter, whose ceremony His Eminence had attended; thereafter scarcely enough [text unclear], in this Conclave [had passed?].

ROME, 9 May. Sunday Cardinal Althan arrived here in the Church of Saint Ambrose, where His Eminence is lodged. He had yesterday had the cardinals return to the Consistory, where Cardinal Parraciani had the previous day departed from the Church of Saint Ambrose. Signor Albani and Vicentien, his brothers, who had stayed several days in Siena, also arrived Sunday in this city and immediately went to the Consistory. Don Charles, also named in the cathedral, Imperial Ambassador at the Holy See, also arrived here, having been received by the Imperial Secretary in the state [manner]; Monday the aforementioned Ambassador gave notice of his cardinal authority regarding the orders. The aforementioned cardinals, having been complimented, Wednesday their first audience was sought from him, whereupon the cardinals prepared themselves accordingly, and His Excellency all the facilities, the Roman [officials?] and foreign clergy attended; [text becomes increasingly unclear and fragmented]

TURIN, 10 April. A rumor circulates here that the Prince of Piedmont is to marry the Infanta of Portugal, and that Count Ricomi will within a few days embark at Genoa to further negotiate the marriage.

TURIN, 30 April. A rumor circulates here that the Prince of Piedmont is to marry the Infanta of Portugal, and that Count Picconi will within a few days embark at Genoa to further negotiate the marriage.

LIVORNO, 1 May. According to reports from Provence, the city of Marseille is now completely freed from the pestilential disease. Toulon was still similarly affected by this plague. This week arrived here: Lazaro Stei from Bristol, Will. Grumly from Genoa, and Benjamin Clee from Falmouth. The ships of Captains Kiyo Kirjole and Jacob [?], which had departed on 19 April for Smyrna and were blown back by contrary winds, are now ready to sail again. No Algerian pirates are reported here.

FLORENCE, 3 May. Our Grand Prince is confirmed in [?] 200 [vessels?], though [text unclear] on the mainland it is [?].

VENICE, 10 May. Wednesday Count Cremens, Resident of the House of Lorraine, had his first public audience with the Senate. General Schulemburg, who has been our commissioner in Terra Ferma, is expected back here any moment. Last Saturday, Signor Bourbo-Morofini was appointed by the ordinary channel as ambassador to the court of France.

France

MARSEILLE, 4 May. This city is now sufficiently freed from the pestilential disease, though [text unclear] outside the city on the land it [remains concerning, and] good money [is needed], wherefore not even the third time shall this sorrowful plague be detected again. At Aix it has been in a poor state for three weeks. At Toulon the pestilential disease is necessary in the city and on the ships, as it will last in the months of August and September. This city has been violently afflicted; such that one may reasonably say that affairs there are in the utmost distress.

MONTPELLIER, 9 May. Thus one learns of the sorrow of Toulon, that some inhabitants of that city, forced by lack of provisions, attempted, with the intention to seek their livelihood on the land, to leave, but were stopped by the militia posted at the barriers; and the city is properly fortified; so it is said that in this miserable commotion some of these unfortunate persons have died by the plague, and it is feared yet more cases will be heard of.

PARIS, 16 May. Monday the King dined at the home of his Steward of the Princes of Vaudours; His Majesty took his meal there, and after dinner amused himself with a small fireworks display. Yesterday evening before dinner the King entertained the Council at the Tuileries. That day the Duke of Chier presented himself in the Ministry as Colonel General [and was received] by committee, and it was silent regarding what he had formerly presented before the officers of the Chamber. When the Ambassador Efendi, some days ago, made a visit to the Marquis de Caulies and Captain Lieutenant of the second company of the King's musicians, His Excellency was received by Monsieur de Cavillac according to the custom of his country, and thus in the hall was received [?], to not spoil the atmosphere of the court proceedings or [?], where he was brought with great ceremony; such that the collation was served with refreshments at the same time; such that the Ambassador, having spent 2 hours altogether, departed very satisfied with Monsieur de Cavillac—who is held in high esteem for Lieutenant Gerardin, who was ambassador at the Ottoman court; one may learn how long [text becomes increasingly unclear and fragmentary] [?]

The chief Postmaster of the Republic, also Knight and Lord of Brederode, etc., and of the foremost College of Posts, has made it known that he is very inclined toward the lawful establishment of a general postal ordinance, by which commerce and correspondence in these lands could be greatly furthered.

AMSTERDAM [date uncertain] The couriers between Brussels and Breda, and [?] traveled with Captain Joost [?], and on that order the [?] Junties, etc., sailing [?] Dijk from here is [?] toward they sail lower [?] Commerce of St. Do[?] Guadeloupe, the Maria [?] from Portsmouth. In [?] the, and in the Vlie or [?] Jan Thom, German & [?] go, and there under [?]

---

Germany & Adjoining Kingdoms

REGENSBURG, 10 May [1721]. When the arrived envoys on last Monday were at the City Hall to establish their deliberations, it was found that the verification will have to take place upon the expiration of the right that undoubtedly [?], and that the drawing of the 2nd Grand Direction of the Company of Commerce, Navigation and Admiralty, by City [?] of the Lords Burgomasters and Governors of the same City, of which so [?] the dismissal of a per cent of the respective [?] in the same Company, from which [?] the per cent the payment which of this year 1721 might be demanded; whereas by the usual [?], everything according to resolution of the Lords States-General [?], likewise the Council [?] on which the Company is based, and properly [?]

It is announced hereby that again in the territory of Wallentaar there will [?] the Linden houses of the same month May, namely the 17 May 1721, the same will be all [?] in [?] to be seen and give which a couple bring, together one night [?] Oil [?].

Johannes Stans, Cornelis vanden Heuvel, Jac Lemmens and Hendrik Linquet Marshals, 12 May 1721; in the morning to estimate, on the Singel in the Burg, a party extra [?] heavier Pig's Breach, [?] Linens and Servetgord, Red and [?] Alspio Cordage and other goods more.

Abraham van Breda, Pieter van der Belch, Adrianos Kamerbeek and Abraham van Dorselia fortieth the 1 June 1721, in the morning at 8 o'clock, on the Singel in the Burg, a party Head Cabbage, a try Leaf-top, some 1000 Leaves loose Bilk, and a party Walden, using Philip Jick Mackerel, will on Wednesday the 4 June 1721, in the morning at 8 and afternoon Burgat the Hope, sell on the Singel in the Burg, some Leaves loose Bilk, and a try Wale, Saffies, white Linen, Tea by Roll, Jewels, Paintings, and some Sculptures; as we the Hope, the [?] Holland situated on the [?] Sir Christian Riemen, with an Outside [?] of the Household [?] [?] Meta-Toys, [?] hom and Fy[?], [?] [?] [?] morning [?] morning, [?] [?] land much much [?] in dwelling all full, [?]; the [?] '[?], and Jan [?], [?] on the afore. Frans, can be learned.

In Leiden will be on Saturday in the house over the City Hall. The 23 May 1721.

---

Spain

CADIZ, 19 April. In this harbor has arrived Thomas Miller from the North Sea, and has there caught many whales. According to letters from Amsterdam, Williamsburg and other places, that the English East India Company would have established its Warehouses there, and that the City at that time willingly requested to proceed; but since the same goods were found outside places toward Africa the aforesaid [?] did not succeed, the merchants and citizens here in the Land said that such cannot take place. It is said that a certain Minister from Germany, who earlier made his residence in this City, and was a much beloved Citizen, now still dwells there, and that the same for [?] English Residents Spina, and [?] third or seven of the journey to the Hague is enticed away.

---

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 21 May. Because various Algerian robbers were floating in the bay, the ships of Damian Gonzales, Walford Hofman, and Blok, lay under escort [?]. At St. Domingo was garnisoned the ship la Mañana Captain Filippo, and at Martinique Pieterscaptain Juan, both from Nantes, at [?] and at [?], and over there the Young Corn. Leendert Druys of Schiedam, Marena and under there the Young Corn. Leendert Druys of Schiedam, Materna [text repeats corruptly] without robbers seen; at Rochelle (that at [?], Petr. Reutzen, etc. hither [?]) Teun. Klip of Hickel, l'Aimable Maria Captain Keetnou from St. Domingo, the St. Jodgen Captain Jean Romain from Guadeloupe, the Maria Captain Dupont from Cayenne, Jan Piersele Moni Laalit from Fortefonde, at Texel the letters found; Jan Verrery from Loeven; Hendrik Sprouw, Jan Thorn, Duof Kck. from Hamburg; Andt. Corn. Et je Piereth, and another from Norway. From the Vlie out cautious Ships in the going, and that without Balliaan Geerblaan toward Bergen. The Croix of the Wifzich, in the [?] at Midburg [?] Rad is of Seville [?] and a fourth [?] Rio. Bilbao is a half. Lisbon and a [?]. Venice [?] and [?] half. Livonia [?] and [?] half. Livonia and a half. Granada is and Malvode, London yes! Sch. 5. Danzig [?] Foo'ie [?]. Riga [?] and a [?]. St. Hamburg [?] and [?] fourth St. Antwerp [?] and [?] fourth St. Goethe [?] and [?] fourth; Brussels [?]; Zealand [?] half. Rotterdam [?] fourth. The Action of the Baak [?]; and [?] fourth [?] The Acres of the O.I.C. [?]. Tall. W.I.C. [?]. Sublet, 1550, the Eng. Bank 135. O.I.C. 144, and Z.I. 143, all current.

---

France

PARIS, 19 May. Some days ago the Viscount de Sûns, the M. de Charières and the Vermeulen. Chief Charières, Grand Lord of the Order of the Invalides was; and showed King Henry IV which the [?] was. England that of proceedings that the Secretaries of the King in communication heard would also well suspicion in the Government and Commerce of England to have. On Monday next, will be sold in Amsterdam on Wednesday the 21 May 1721, in the morning, a party extra [?] of the Kilns [?] Tea Boeg and [?], a try arrived fetched the [?] over the Gold chain, that the Poles on the Friday Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday in the [?] Ground for Money to be obtained.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 26th of May, 1721
No. 67, Year 1721

---

Spain

CADIZ, 19 April. Into this Bay have arrived various vessels from England: the warship Chesterfield, which is returning; Thomas Miller heading to Málaga; Pieter Fuyk and Ybrand Pool bound for Amsterdam; William Baal for Barcelona; Robert Will for Camarica; Captain Amelingen for St. Malta; Jan Franciscus for Plymouth; and an English warship for Gibraltar.

MADRID, 6 May. On the first of this month, all the Grandes of the Court and the Ministers of State visited His Majesty at Aranjuez to pay their respects. It is reported that His Majesty has ordered that an olive tree, brought by the last departing ship of the Año [?] from Vera Cruz, shall remain unmolested and unbesieged, as the Spanish wish to deliver it; this has caused some rejoicing in the city, as it is hoped that provisions will arrive soon with the old [supplies?]. By the same Año ship, news has come from the Indies that all foreign nations settled in America have been ordered to withdraw from there, and that they have had to pay large sums for tonnage, which displeases merchants here in this land greatly; and although the said goods from the Galleons and some cargo must depart, it is hoped nevertheless that the Spanish, after receiving the aforementioned silver, will not prohibit goods in the same vessels. It is spoken of as if the King's promotion will do something regarding the high officers lately stationed in Africa, and that some weak regiments, which have not withstood most [trials?], will be reinforced. It is said that a certain Minister, who has held himself incognito near this Court in order to propose a marriage, has unexpectedly departed from elsewhere in retreat, possibly having broken off his objective there. Nothing else is spoken of in this city but that a marriage is being arranged between the Prince of Asturias and the Duchess of the House of Orleans, by which one would then have to conclude that the old friendship between the two Crowns was sufficiently tested; however, the most likely [outcome] regarding Sebastian, Fontarabie and Gibraltar cannot be reported with certainty. Some foreigners have arrived here from Brussels.

---

France

PARIS, 19 May. A few days ago the King bestowed the Order of Colbert upon 4 Edicts in the form of Euro, namely the Viscount de Surat, the Sieur de Champion, the Marquis de Calvière, and the [Venteuil?]. On Tuesday the Duke of Chaunse, Governor of the Orders of St. Lazarus, together with many members of the Commanders who accompanied him, was honored in the service, which took place in the Church of the Capuchins in memory of [the 250?] of King Henry IV, which [order?] he had greatly privileged. Yesterday the King's Ballet was performed again; and on Thursday evening, after the King had amused himself, he will leave this divertissement with his Court in the great hall of the Philistines for some time. The King's fine words, after his years of preparation, shine forth so powerfully that much is expected of His Majesty. Some of those who have knowledge of the Streets and Pensions granted by the King could tell well of his wisdom, and that such will also come from satisfaction. On Thursday the Duke of Bourbon with his magnificent retinue made a visit to Madame Law in the Strand de Colebert, where she is lodged. The business of the Leidse will be adjourned from the 26th of December to the 17th of the coming month; yet this is only being announced by the arrival of Billettes [documents?]. Among these Millions and Millionaires in the Indies, [certain persons?] have arrived; the said Sieur has purchased many linen goods in Languedoc and other Provinces. One sees from Articles of the King's Edict of State; deliveries to the Marines; dated January 1720 and following years. Another of 23 March concerning the Controllers of Rents, confirmed by Edict of January of this year, for certain Juries [?], and for certain Juries to purchase in the guilds, the wages of the Millionaires shall be paid; by Edict of January of this year, for certain Juries [?], they shall purchase [?]; and another of 30 February of the coming month, signed by the Sieur Nicolas, Director of the Office of Propositions, and to the Sieur B. are given the new Advices or old [documents?] with the modification that in repair, and to the Sieur B. are given the old fulfilled Advices, as well as the 330 Subscriptions, which have been brought to [?], on which month [?] and the Bernis of Dodevan, who have purchased Rents that are encumbered by Ground-rents, shall be supplemented by Algebryes et Redres [?] France Botelers. The Sieur Dieux, formerly Councillor of the Great [?], has retired to Montueilen. It is assured that the Duke of Chatsière will live in the House of Colbert, which the Duke the Marquise de Biron, sometime Head Equerry of the Duke Regent, is now named [to oversee?]. The Cardinal [...] elevated [...] was raised upon the death of the entire [...] Conclave [?], and his Excellence is acceptable with good judgment to the Roman Catholic Powers: he has been competent in Paris [...] one holds him to be a very honorable man, for which the Pope, who elevated him to the Papacy in the year 1707, [?].

The said new Holiness has had for some time a great Sickness, from which subsequently a Sleeping Sickness developed; on the advice of his Brother (who is said to be endowed with much understanding and other great qualities, and possibly on his behalf has betaken himself to the Bath in the Kingdom of Naples) his health has improved, although he remains still somewhat weak, and has been weary of chagrin: one assured that the Imperial [authority?] has in this matter drawn a line for him; one also speaks of the Sieur du Bois, Archbishop of Cambrai, who might now be elevated to the Purple, and that Don Alexander Albani, who has been Dean of the Cardinal College, also comes into consultation; however, it is firmly stated that the Pope's Brother will certainly obtain a Cardinal's hat or Hood. The peaceful disposition of this new Pope brings some suspicion to the Anticonstitutionals, who have troubled themselves [?] since sealed Letters have been sent to various of them, by means of which the Abbé d'Asfeld has been banished to St. Florentin, along with the Sieur Tabourn, Superior of the Community of the College of Lisieux, and others, who have been sent into Exile to various places of the Kingdom. It is said that the new Pope is not among the number of three new [candidates?] regarding Cardinal de Noailles, and that thus the Cardinals have come to a decision and have refused an Abbé; that the new Pope, who is to be chosen, will not decide the great matters of the Church except in a Congregation of such a number of Cardinals and as many Officers of the Pope, as have [?]; and each to avoid the Schism into which the Church has come under Clement XI, [who] saw all efforts directed at such a Direction; without giving the new College any knowledge thereof. Forty Protestants, who some time ago were captured in the Cévennes for exercising their Religion in public places, and were transported to the seacoast, have, through the powerful intercession of the Ambassadors of Great Britain and Holland, obtained permission to depart for England. The Government of Ham, vacant by the resignation of the Count of Spienga, has come to the Count of Grammond, who had the reversion thereof. The Bailli de Mesmes has offered to the King the Valleys, which the Grand Master of Malta is accustomed to present to His Majesty annually. Since the death of the Sieur Desmarchi, the Duke of Luxembourg has been negotiating to purchase the House of the Minister, for which his heirs are asking 900,000 Livres. The Sieur de Pelletier de la Houslaie, Controller General, has requested from the King for the Sieur de Pelletier, his Nephew, the Abbey of Hamvil, vacant by the death of the Abbé de Mesme, who, besides that of Vallery, also held the Priories of Daupont and St. Peter of Abbeville and of St. Denis des Estrées, which latter Benefice has been enjoyed by his Brother, the Ambassador of Malta [?], which brings in good Livres in rent, and in his Lordship the matter is also to be carried [forward?] of St. Denis of the Abbots, which the History of Malta sets [forth?]; the Sieur de Belly de Mesme has also received a Pension of 1000 Livres from the common Treasury of [?]. By order of the Directors of the Mail Coach here, a certain [person?] called Constantine, who has since called himself Chabannei since he became a Gendarme of the Watch, [has been arrested?]; several circumstances have made him suspected of being complicit in the last thefts from the said Mail Coach, although he is one of the Key Figures who were found there, and a third appeared, and one took 37 Gold Louisd'Or [coins], 3 bags of Watches, and one Gold Snuffbox. From reports from Provence, the bad Sickness has raged there so fiercely of late that more and more one is concerned about the consequences; there are even assertions that at Toulon six people have died in one day; however, whether the Sickness has spread to the other side of the Rhone is not uniformly reported. The son of the President Boulanger died while he was embracing his Wife. In order to provide remedies for some diseased animals, a Royal Ordinance has been published these days at Versailles, by which all Ferms are forbidden to keep Mastiffs, Dogs, Horned Beasts, Pigs &c.; a Commissioner has been stationed to bring this Ordinance into execution; and it is reported thereof that in this city there are many sick dogs, a good Health Measure has been taken to kill all that come before [the authorities]. The Murders are multiplying again; every day bodies are found in the River Lyres, wretchedly cut and mangled, and especially in the face, so much so that the being is completely unrecognizable, and also many of the Thieves in entire Troops. It is noted that the Thieves in the Months shall be brought to their old value. The Abbé Fyot, formerly Almoner of the deceased King, Henry, Abbé of St. Elden at Dijon, has died there a few days ago. It is hoped that the Abbé d'Orleans will be appointed Coadjutor of the Archbishopric of Cambrai.

---

Great Britain

LONDON, 12 May. Friday the House of Commons, [by order?] of the Commissioners of the Tax, ordered the investigations into the Coal and pressed Paper [taxes?], a total accounting of the revenues since last year to Christmas 1720 to be delivered to them; they have also ordered [?] and likewise [to investigate?] 200 years of revenues regarding the imports of goods from the East Indies. Saturday the Commons had Dr. Hailey summon[ed] on 10 May regarding the accounts, which is when the arrears of the Royal Family shall be settled. That day the Sieur Guggsby, who is in the keeping of the Sergeant of Arms, by [?] requested to be discharged from an Article as having performed [service?] [continues in garbled text, final entries too corrupted for reliable translation]

I must be honest: this transcription is too severely corrupted to produce a reliable English translation. The source text contains:

1. Extensive OCR/transcription errors throughout, making numerous passages unintelligible even in Dutch 2. Partial or missing text marked by [...] in critical places 3. Garbled words that don't correspond to recognizable Dutch vocabulary 4. Structural breakdown particularly in the Germany section (Duytslandt), where entire passages become incomprehensible strings of repeated fragments

While the opening sections are partially recoverable (England, Netherlands, Doornik), the quality degrades significantly through the text, and the final German reports are largely illegible.

To proceed responsibly, I would need: - Access to the original printed source or a higher-quality scan - A retranscription of the corrupted passages, particularly: - The latter half of the England section (stock prices/financial data) - The entire Germany/Regensburg section - The Hamburg closing

Publishing a translation of this corrupted text would introduce historical inaccuracies and misrepresentations of what the 1721 Leydse Courant actually reported.

Would you be able to provide a cleaner transcription of the most important sections?

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 26th of may, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 64.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 28th of May, 1721

Wednesday's Courant.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

FROM Warsaw of the 14th of this month, it is written that on account of certain troubles, His Imperial Majesty having been informed of Turkish movements on the Frontiers, would have discovered the true intentions of the Porte, and that the Emperor [and] the King of Poland cannot help but condole with the said Kingdom; it is even reported that the Court has given orders to its Resident at Constantinople to make known this Resolution to the Ottoman Porte, in order to turn it away, if possible, from the execution of those [plans] which were directed against Poland, in the faint hope that the Emperor would go along with this; it is also said that His K. M. has refrained from [allowing] the Czar [?] his Troops to withdraw from the Polish Borders; however, the Minister of His Czarist Majesty in a [?] noted that the aforesaid matter is whether the Czar and his [?] would have any obligation with the Polish Court for reasons of the [?], [which is] completely false and beyond likelihood; His Imperial Majesty will conduct himself during the storm of enmity; His K. M. [and] His Imperial Majesty will, through [?], jointly celebrate the Feast of St. John [?], who through the Gregorian Month by the reigning Pope shall be declared a Saint. Some days ago there was published here a Notice for a Lottery, which is worth altogether one hundred Millions; and what the Interested Parties will draw is 117 Millions; the same is divided into 100 Lots, of which all who [have] a Monetary share, and just as all in former years are now drawn.

BRUNSWICK, the 14th of May. The most distinguished [persons] of Hamburg, who arrived here on the eleventh of this month, were the next day also visited by Herr Melchior Maniault on behalf of the Emperor, and on the 13th of this [month] journeyed toward the Western Court. Our Historian could not [?] their last [visit] of 200 [?].

GOVERNMENT in the Ley. The same [proceedings] of the Elector's Minister and Officers of the Church Assembly continued, and by means of a Missive from Friedland on the preceding day no meeting was held by those Ministers at the Town Hall. Just as the Protestant Corps in a Conference [were] engaged, jointly with His Cesarean Majesty and [?] still high still [?] the redress Grievances in the Palen March, in [?] still high still [?] the redress Grievances in the Palen March, and also in various other Petty States, where a Conference regarding the Treaty of Westphalia has been occasioned, and in order to give notice of this to the Cardinal of Saxen-Zeyt, the same was [?]. This [report] comes from there, and that the King his Brother in the requested Collect, to the charge of the same Latinic Ordinance of [?], has [?]; so one reports Several in this Conference also [?], to the Heidelberg Imperial Councils at [?], that they may not [?] a further Conference matter to bear, so that the [?] to the Emperor to Regulate a first [?] for truth have been done. Furthermore are some Public Receiverships for the [?] City chosen, and also [?]; and although various Princes [?] shall be reformed, so those [?] from Turkey continue. Troops shall make, so those [?] from Turkey continues [?] and says not acted, they hope in this Victory and Consolidation, has [?] much [?], and that the related [?] shall be with compassion.

From Barstratfle in Germany, they Troop-gen also actually Huguenots; [?] against the Protestant[s], their preparation [could] not [?], has Huguenots [shall be] necessary and declaration[s] means to be considered. As the Magistrate of this City some days ago to no resolved [?] Bers [?] concerning his [?] examination, he has confessed, that [?] Music of a Nobleman [?], he confesses that setter Music of a nobleman [?], and [?] house [?] (which in [?]-Service [is], and so one understood [?] in of a Chancellery-Service, of [?] of the City [?], [?]), her often from home [?], and [?] has [?], which against [?]-undertaking not shall be [?] gives.

That neighbor [?], a [?] has become, and he very that the rulers [?] on his rests [?] dy Majesty the situated

The Elector-Prince, who [had] gone [to] Pietsch in order to visit the King his Mother [?] requested the Right of the reigning Heiress [?]'s Nameday, which yesterday was celebrated at Wolfenbüttel. The [?] dwell, but have they legitimized themselves and [?] a with come, because they their journey [?] [could] not [?].

ENGLAND. Schoenewick [?] Treaty of the 22nd of May. The Heiress and Heiress of Brunswick Blauenburg, here [?] are, and the Count of his ...

SWEDEN. A favorable Wind toward Revel [they] had journeyed; that the Czar his [?] not on Sweden [?], is not all[together] from the Letters of Stockholm of the 14th of this [month] to be concluded, but those of Petersburg confirm indeed that the Preliminary Peace-points between the Czar and Sweden would be as good as settled. Letters from Grodenland from Vienna report that the Cesarean Generals [?] had received orders to betake themselves to their Posts and to draw the Troops to them, and the Poles, if they might be attacked by the Turks, to assist.

France

PARIS, the 23rd of May. The Prince Delhomniki, as Ambassador on behalf of the Czar of Great-Russia at Court, is having a costly Levee made, wherefore one believes that the said Prince is conducting his public Interest there. The Hr. Camus, first President of the Court of Aides, sold the Blue Ribbon to the Lord de Breteuil, Master of Petitions and Intendant of Limoges, for the Sum of 30,000 Livres, on the following condition: that the same shall be approved by an Edict of the Council; that the Hr. Breteuil shall pay to the Hr. Camus within 3 Years after receipt of the Commissary the Sum of 30,000 Livres, and the remainder placed in 4 Terms, to wit all 6 Months one equal fourth part, until the entire Debt shall be paid, and this all in ready Money, at the rate of 22 Guilders [per] Mark Sterling, which is currently valued at more than 30,000 Livres; at the price [?] Money as now in value is: the said Blue Ribbon shall be worn eight days by the Lord Felelier de la Houllaye, Controller General of the Finance, because he, having the money handed over, shall do this all his life long. The Hr. d'Argenson, Councillor of State and Intendant of Maubeuge, has obtained the opportunity for the Office of Chancellor of the Seal of the Military Order of St. Louis; he will wear the Red Ribbon eight days long, for the same reason as the above-mentioned Hr. de la Houllaye came to do the Blue Ribbon; subsequently the Hr. d'Argenson [shall serve] as Chancellor of the Order. Besides the Office of Bishop of Perpignan, which the Abbot Vaulony Canon of St. Chapel holds, he has also obtained the Abbey of la Reale in the same Bishopric. One sees an Edict of the King's Council of State of the 18th [of this month] containing the prorogation of [?] of the Courier-[?], Papers and other [?], ordered by Edict of the 18th of January of this Year. The day before Tuesday afternoon a certain Chevalier de Faasd rode through the Street of St. Honoré, and encountered two Carriages which were so close together that he with his Horse had to be pressed between the fitted [?], wherefore he, after one of the Coachmen a threat has made, because he had not wished to yield; from the second a blow with the Whip in the face received, whereupon the said Cavalier drew his Sword and him forced [?] that he fell down from the Seat onto the ground, riding away thereupon on his way without being pursued by anyone; one thinks that a number of the King's Gardes du Corps is, although he his Regiment's [?] had not had. One speaks there of two new Offices to be created, namely one of Master of Camp General over the Infantry, for the Marquis de Bijon, Lieutenant General of the King and of the same, and for the Duke of Orleans, and the other of [?] Stallmaster of the Duke of Orleans, and the other of Commander General, for the Marquis de la Farse, Marshal de Camp and Captain of the Guard of the Duke Regent. One says now whether or the [Bishop] of St. Albin, and not the Abbot d'Orleans as was mentioned before, [is] soon to be nominated to Coadjutor of the Archbishopric of [?] and other reasons: that the said Archbishop wishes to divest himself of the same [in his entirety], in favor of the afore-mentioned Abbot, in which case the Hr. du Bois would be said to receive a Cardinal's Hat; but this report requires further confirmation. It appears that in neighboring Powers at present that some [?] of the Fort Jacques [?] begin to express themselves about the [?] [?] concerned in affairs [?] begin to express themselves about the [?] [?] concerned with the Turkish Ambassador at this Court [who] has sent [?] and this more so, because they a very great confidence between our Ministers and his Turkish Excellency. Three [?] of this world a certain Ambassador by the [?] and Duke well [?]. [?] at Table was received; the Hall, where His Excellency was well conducted, and was greeted, [and] Beautiful Flowers garnished, and on the Board of the Minister was an outstanding fine bouquet [of] Flowers which he, being a great Lover thereof, [?] held in hand: in the [?] of the Cabinet was His Excellency well satisfied over the splendid [?] of the Invalids [?], where an Infant of the Opera was served: which by the best voices and Instruments of the foremost [?] was conducted, and there [?] himself on order of the [?] of the Mother of the foremost Nobility [had] betaken; in which an Angel, all had been completed well.

BRUSSELS, 16 May. Last week the Count of Windischgrätz made a journey to Ath to attend a hunt, to which the Count of Vehlen had invited him, where His Excellency is reported to have stayed for several days. On Saturday, a Finance official with the Major of the Court Household was at the Castle of Tervuren to prepare lodgings for the Venetian [?], which were being furnished for the Count of Windischgrätz, who has decided to remain there until the opening of the Congress at Camera [Cambrai] is confirmed. The magistracy of the city of Tournai having been changed, the same is now to be done in the other cities of the Barrier and Flanders. On Friday, the regiment of the Count of Ghent, which lies in garrison at the Castle of Antwerp, passed through here toward Mons, and that of the Marquis of Bougainville, coming from Mons, marched toward Ostend in garrison.

AMSTERDAM, 26 May. According to letters from Curaçao, the ships of Captain Jan Akerman and [?] sailed from there on 11 January toward the lower coast. With letters of 30 April, news has arrived from Cadiz that four French ships were in the South Sea, and it was still uncertain whether the silver arriving by the advice-ship would need to be brought to the Mint; since the same arrangement had not yet been widely distributed to the owners, although some goods had already been received, but it is not known how great the amount of silver and goods will be. The galleons lying there receive so little cargo aboard that it was thought they would have to depart with two-thirds ballast. News from that city also reports that word had come from Gibraltar of the ratification of the treaty between the King of Great Britain and the Emperor of Morocco, so that now the vessels to Salé and other Moorish harbors will be able to travel safely to and fro. From London to Barcelona came the English ship Medway, Captain John Richardson, which had aboard near Alicante the Algerian corsair the small Sun with 30 guns, which in five weeks had taken only one Genoese bark sailing from Mallorca to Alicante. The said captain was on 20th of last month at the Roads of Barcelona near three Maltese warships heading to the Strait, of which one carried 65 and two carried 52 guns. At Martinique has arrived the ship St. Jacques, Captain Pierre Normandy from Bordeaux; at London (from where John Lewis and the 2 Wolves [ships named] with Roelof Klaeten have departed hither), the Neptune fluyt, [?] and the Merslooper Reinadt [?] from Bremen, [?] Bell from Amsterdam; at Wight the Hopewell from Carolina hither, and at Plymouth the Granada City from here toward Barbados. Ships departing from Lisbon and St. Ubes by Captains Hendr. Netscher and Hendr. Schelhorn [?] were expected at Lübeck. At Wyborg [?] on 12 March toward Petersburg, the river was completely open, whereby it was thought that a part of the Russian fleet was already at sea. From ships wrecked near Iceland, various dead persons came ashore, and it was thought to be a fluyt from Suriname. According to letters from Hamburg, Stralsund, Wismar and other places, the peace between the Czar and the King of Sweden is thought to be very near.

THE HAGUE, 27 May. Mr. Hymen has been delegated to Cleves. The gentlemen Sylvia Nuyen and Veillon van Oelslberga, who have been nominated by the States General to undertake the reformation of the magistracy in States Flanders, will depart there at the end of this week or beginning of next week. The Spanish ambassador, the Marquis de Montléon, has been with several members of the Government, as has the British minister, Mr. d'Arulien. This morning the Baron Ordinance [?] Müller, envoy of the Elector of Cologne, was in conference with several members of the Council of State.

LONDON, 14 May 1721. This Wednesday two more petitions were presented against the former directors of the South Sea Company; that day the commissioners delivered the accounts in order to the House; and after a petition presented by Mr. Edward Eliot and his wife, daughter of the deceased Mr. James Craggs, had been read and it was proposed to lay it on the table, the House went into grand committee against the bill to relieve the unfortunate sufferers in the South Sea Company, and appointed 9 commissioners to sell the effects which were confiscated from or hastily taken from the former directors of the South Sea Company, which monies shall come to the benefit of that Company. The said commissioners are Knight John Pyes, deputy governor, Mr. Rudge, deputy governor, Knight Thomas Croft, Mr. Lade, Mr. Hopkins, Mr. Roberts, Mr. Malloy, Mr. Hudson and Mr. Lant, of which 5 gentlemen are in the Lottery. Captain James Walter and Thomas Big, charged with being organizers of the scheme, were arrested. Yesterday three more petitions were brought against the former directors of the South Sea Company, and one concerning the borough of Beralston, complaining of improper elections for said borough, and after some discussions a fourth petition became a commission.

Martin Capris, Director of the Land Lottery, advertises that the current drawing [?] the third to the seventh class; that the drawing shall take place at the latest for the expiration of next March, namely the drawing of the seventh class.

This evening at 6 o'clock, Wednesday 18 May 1721, will the broker Joh. [?] follow on sale extra [?] [?] of 62 [?] current tea from the East and Congo, arrived the [?] on the Emperor's canal over the Golden Quay, where the auctions have been held [?].

Abraham van Deth, Plaatsen den Bosch, Adrigenus Kamerbeek and Abraham van Dore, Tuesday 3 July 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, at the [?] in the Burg, a [?] racecourse; some 100 [?], some 1000 sheets of loose tin, and a [?] watch...

[The remainder of this section is too corrupted in the transcription to render reliable English.]

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES:** Madrid 88. Cadiz 111. Seville 110. Bilbao 89. Lisbon 43 and 4 sixteenths. Venice 81 and 1 half. [?] and 4 fourths. Genoa 93 and 6 fourths. Paris rose to 91 and 9 sixteenths. London 33 shillings 9 pence. Danzig 290 Polish groats. Breslau 5 and 1 half shillings. Hamburg 12 and 1 quarter at 7 shillings each. Antwerp 2 and 3 fourths at 19 sixteenths each. Ghent 2 and 1 half at 3 sixteenths. Brussels [?] and 1 half. Rotterdam 1 fourth. Bank agio 3 and 4 sixteenths. East India Company shares 343 at [?]. O.I.C. 138, South Seas 140, all cash.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 28th of may, 1721. — With Privilege.
