No. 1721.No. 65.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 30th of May, 1721
No. 65, Friday Edition

---

Italy

NAPLES, 6 May

After the Viceroy heard Mass on Thursday morning in the Chapel of the Palace, His Excellency proceeded to the Royal Church of the Carmine, and from there to the Cathedral Church to view the relics of Saint Januarius, Protector of this Kingdom, in whose honor the beginning of the Feast of the Given History was celebrated. That same morning, the Deputies of the People presented the customary gift to His Excellency, consisting of flowers, fruits, preserves, and beautiful maidens. The procession of the blood of Saint Januarius and the relics of the saint was conducted with great ceremony from the Cathedral Church. Three times the blood was found to liquefy in the presence of the Viceroy, as well as the nobility and a considerable crowd of people, which liquefaction of the blood is held as a matter of great significance.

ROME, 10 May

The previous Saturday, the Sacred College decided that the new Cardinals, after the election of the Pope, would not be obliged to pay the customary visits to the old Cardinals; rather, the visits given in the Conclave would be deemed sufficient. That same day at 4 o'clock in the afternoon, Cardinal Pignatelli left the Conclave, which had been accompanied by a very large crowd expressing joyful exclamations in memory of Innocent XII, who descended from the house of Pignatelli. Soon after this Cardinal entered the Conclave, Cardinal Gracci also entered it. Monday, Cardinal Botia Aquabitegheo [?] arrived in the city in a carriage drawn by 6 horses from the house of Rospiliochi. That evening, Cardinal Carraccioli arrived from his Archbishopric of Capua, making his entry into the palace of his uncle, Cardinal del Guidice, who had sent his carriage with 6 horses to meet him. After His Eminence visited the Church of Saint Peter, he entered the Conclave on Tuesday evening at 6 o'clock. That evening, the Cardinal of Schönbrunn also arrived, having been met by a carriage drawn by 6 horses from Cardinal Althan, and went to lodge at His Eminence's palace. Wednesday morning, it was published that the Cardinals had convened to elect a new Pope. The elected candidate was Cardinal Michael Angelo Conti, Priest of the Title of Saints Dionysius and Grace [Editor's note: likely Saints Sixtus and Dionysius], not yet 66 years of age. On Thursday of this month, the Feast of the Apparition of the Archangel Michael, the Cardinals, who numbered 55 in the Conclave, unanimously elected the aforementioned Cardinal Michael Angelo Conti to the Papacy—a Roman who is somewhat less than 66 years old—as Pope. He accepted the Pontificate and declared his intention to take the name Innocent XIII. He was then adored for the first time by all the Cardinals in the same Chapel where the election had taken place. Subsequently, his cell was plundered by the servants of the Conclave, as is customary. Around midday, announcement was made from the balcony of the basilica from which the Pope gives his blessing to the people on Good Friday. Cardinal Pamphili, as the first Deacon, accompanied by one of the Ceremonial Masters who carried a cross, also assisted in giving the blessing. The Cardinal then published the election of the new Pope and cried out in loud Latin words: "Annuntio vobis gaudium magnum, habemus Pontificem"—which means, "I announce to you a great joy, we have a Pope." This was followed by great rejoicing in the city, accompanied by the firing of cannons. Following the investiture ceremony in the Church, the same was done by the Conclavists; afterward, the English lords and ladies, who had come with the Pretender [?] and his wife, were admitted to pay their respects. The Pope was then led to the apartments of the Vatican. Meanwhile, preparations are being made for the Coronation, which is said will take place a week from tomorrow. It is believed that His Holiness will not appoint his ministers before that ceremony is performed. The Cardinal of Rohan was entertained by His Holiness's Majesty. Cardinal Aquaviva has received a gift of 14,000 scudi from the King of Spain, regarding the Archbishopric of Toledo and the Bishopric of Cuenca in the Kingdom of Sicily. Last Thursday night, Cardinal Farnesio was suddenly seized by a new accident and died, being 75 years of age, by which death the sixth Cardinal's hat has become vacant. After a courier arrived from Genoa, a new rumor arose that Cardinal Fierchi, Archbishop of Genoa, has died, and that Cardinal Marini might do well to resign from his position to apply himself to married life.

[THE NETHERLANDS]

[Amsterdam, 17 May]

Saturday evening, word arrived by express from our Ambassador in Rome that Cardinal Michael Angelo Conti was elected Pope by unanimous vote, having taken the name Innocent XIII. This news was published Sunday morning with the ringing of the bells of Saint Martin's Church. On account of this election, a solemn Mass was sung in the cathedral, after which the bells of the other churches were also rung. These celebrations of joy will be repeated for three days. This Government will shortly appoint four extraordinary ambassadors to go and offer congratulations to His Holiness on his elevation to the Papal dignity. Word is given that the countryside about the Po is full of locusts; the magistracy over public health has sent people there to exterminate them. Word has been received that the Imperial troops in various places near Mantua are performing military executions, though the reason for this is not mentioned.

---

Muscovy [Russia]

ST. PETERSBURG, 6 May

Word has been received that His Czarist Majesty has postponed his journey from Riga to Reval until the 20th or 21st of this month. The Duke of Württemberg, who was still held at the Imperial court, has gone over to that of the Czar and has been appointed by him as Generalissimo of the Russian troops. The said Duke will follow the court to Reval. According to the same letters, His Czarist Majesty's plenipotentiaries in the Holy Wars are still at court; but they are now to undertake their journey to the Congress at Neustadt to confer with the Swedish plenipotentiaries there—concerning which very good reports are heard here. An officer of the King of Sweden, who came to the Russian court, is again departing from Riga to Stockholm. Work continues without interruption on the repairs of the fleet in order to send it to sea at the first order. Regarding the negotiations of the Duke of Holstein, nothing certain can be reported.

---

Spain

BILBAO, 9 May

On this day departed from here the ships of Captain Damian Gonzales, Warner Hofman, Cocnaert Blok, etc., bound for Amsterdam; the Spanish ship Saint Joseph, Captain Marin Morales, for Cadiz; and 2 English ships for London. It is hoped that our deputies sent to Madrid will obtain the abolition of the old customs duties at court. Word has been received that henceforth silver coming from the Spanish West Indies will be minted with greater care, and that an order was sent from Madrid to Cadiz to distribute the silver that came with the last advice ship to the owners.

---

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 14 May

His Majesty, who recovered sooner than was expected, owing to indisposition from fever, has now sufficiently recovered from his illness. The Senate convened yesterday as well. Word has been received by express that the British fleet has arrived in the Sound and advanced to the Dardanelles, which has caused great joy here. The French Minister, Mr. Camppedon, awaits the expedition sent by him to the French court, which may return one day, at which time the said Minister may perhaps be sent once more to the French court. Word has been received that our plenipotentiaries have arrived at Neustadt; but that the Russian plenipotentiaries would also arrive there soon remains unconfirmed, though they are expected daily. It is hoped in this city that the negotiations will have a good outcome, and that an expedition with further instructions to our plenipotentiaries at Neustadt will be sent. Some servants of the Imperial minister, the Count of Eytag, have come from Copenhagen; his Excellency is expected to return here himself by the 18th or 19th.

---

Germany & Neighboring Realms

REGENSBURG, 22 May

The memorial that our court has sent to the Emperor to inform His Majesty how his order regarding the grievances of the Princes has been received, and it is said it will be answered shortly, if no further reports come from the Palatinate indicating that the Elector has still further grievances. The said memorial will be accompanied by certain detailed accounts. Our plenipotentiaries will continue to support the matter, and an order regarding the handling of religious matters [?] will be obtained; meanwhile, some Russian [?]...

[Text becomes fragmentary/uncertain at end of this section]

The Chief Popish Minister, it is said, does not see everything clearly in this matter. He has not been able to give us further investigation of affairs, nor how the complaints made thus far might be settled to satisfaction. The previous Monday there was a dinner given here by a Scott, when Christ [appeared] with Dismas and [?], by which an Armorer decided [?] at table, [?] had [?] was sung on the head, Ladies & [?]; and the Council of Satan; in the Wood of Biel outside the City, his [?] we [?] Society, helped, by which the same Company, being forced to retire into Confusion here as well as in the surrounding area of the Basile Cloister. The Chief Minister is nearly finished with the Publication of [?], though only much at the Assembly of States much unexpected has come, it remains uncertain. The Minister of State of the United [?] can [?] nothing Netherlands, has various notable Roman and Protestant [?] over the matter, which [?] at the Walyel [?]. day when the Turk is not fixed. Moreover the Aziens on the Louvre Commissions in [?]

On the other hand one sees clearly from Rome that [?] the Cardinals, as a [?] of the H.G. must be accepted, and in that circumstance the Answer is repeated that Cardinal Scotti gave, when the [?] asked him which of the Cardinals by order deserved the title of St. Peter the best, which answer was, If he there the H.G. garred würde, shall it certainly [?] Purple if he then the German [?], shall it be there appropriate and plintzlyk, and if [?] the D... moved, can it no one else point out but si Eminendis which not. The subsistence of the Jubilee over the joyful elevation is here asked with much impatience. Regarding the number of the Banished for the new Appeal nothing certain can be reported, except that the Anticonstitutionals over the matter, which makes much commotion, are very contracted. The day when the Turkish Ambassador shall go to Versailles is not yet fixed. The day before yesterday the Banbuisins of 1600 were on 17, and the Azigs on 17 Livres; there were 14 Commissions in this Comptoir, and in each Comptoir is a President to facilitate the increasing mobilyzetden. The Pelt continued the [?] in Provence. Since a few days ago 5 Thieves have been captured here, as 4 poor children have been brought, besides a Brother and a Cellarer, who in the garment and caparra have been [?] on the Law. The day before yesterday in the Faubourg St. Germain was found the corpse of a young Female person, which the Deya and Bortius [?].

PARIS, May 23rd. The previous Sunday the King gave the Bishopric of Perpignan, vacant by the death of the Seigneur de Flamanville, to the Abbé de Vausor, Choir-master and Canon of the Holy Chapel in that City, with the Abbey of la Keelle, which is in the same Bishopric. What precautions were employed to satisfy the pious intentions of the Holy Courtenaux would one wish to see in the Chamber, to bring it to Montreuil, without [?]. In payment to the Curé of Anei le France, there it is lent. There the Mademoiselle d'Aluye, who is about one hundred years old, and was Favorite of the Dowager Duchess, passed away. Mister Law, who had gone to live in a [?] Chamber, is greatly desired, because she did not appear on the Street without being insulted by the People: it was cried throughout this entire City that her Man [?] by the Heir, but [?] to the confirmation: Will. Law is still in Fort l'Evêque. The Peer is Roy, attacked by various [?], which have made the Couplet on the Codharge, by the [?] Vial of Raudlbeer in the Châtelet, which he must rid himself of: of the Sections of the Duke of la Force, who has studied him well for a long time: by those of the Academy of Inscriptions, where he was requested to leave, because of the multitude of the [?] which he had in Toulouse and in the French Academy and likewise by the gallant [?] of the brought Agioteur, has [?] his other [?] inspect, in which verification he also [?] the public [?], in more e. hansbiteren year of 1000 L.D. against the door of the Duke of la Force for the appeal of the hereupon and Zedenierung which the Duke has done, seven he in the French Academy and: Secretariat, the General of the Augustines, noting that all the Sums du tri and t haie. To the benefit of the Duke, Conti were, has written from Rome, half a's agitte. And have to a pair pair equally: When are very good Family, great upon the [?]. F Bodd Thamoift, in which likewise daily has heard say, that; in the midst of [?] in the Chapter had: b) the Cardinal Fabriani in the Chapel St. Aogis

AMSTERDAM, May 25th. On the 29th of March the Ship of Captain Nicolaos Timcut still lay at Salve, and about 300 men with it [?] the departure; from there some Privateers had gone to Sea, but no prizes were brought in. At Bayonne one looked with longing toward the meeting of the [?] Ships reported there, and under those of the Captain la Farga, du Dieu d'Aigus &c.; there the Ship Polithoora Hend. Bartente from the Maas was [?] at Brecht [?] Lampe of Bassen at St. Hallo Janjorite Boller of here; at St. Marrde: Good Friends Tymen Pieterck, and the Hope Marren Bryn of Honficar; at Bordeaux the S.Schcreen, and thereunder Haas of Llingetien, Leed, Kruyk, Will. de Jong &c. of here and the Maas, as well as the St. Laurens Jochem Klok from the East Sea, and [?] those Ships have gone to Sea. At Camaret near Brest 16 Ships coming from Rochelle have entered, and thereunder Skipper Karecns, Laurens Janfleu, Gerit Muyz, Jan Janileu Dom Sec. of the Maas; and at Bordeaux and elsewhere as needed; and at Borneuf P. Groen of St. Marreo.

THE HAGUE, May 29th. The Seigneur de Brodes, extraordinary Envoyé of the King of Poland to this State, has been visited by several Gentlemen of the Government, as well as the Seigneur Konin Minister the Baron Godenai. The Seigneur van Heckeren has departed to Gelderland and the [?] Koelaan to Utrecht.

It is ordered, that in view of the ordained Fast and Supplication Day, on Thursday and Friday the 12th and 13th of June 1721 shall be held, promptly shall we again [?] the Continuation of the Lottery of [?] for the expiration of the present Month of May, on pain of the 2nd Clause, positive the 9th June following shall commence.

and the following days, at Amsterdam, in the [?] High Lodgement, [?] Tonquinese Lacquerworks, as Cabinets, Writing-desks, and [?] Articles, together with handsome fine Japanese and Chinese Porcelain [?], in the morning at 9 and in the afternoon at 2 o'clock, on the Kolveniers-[?], [various textile items and goods], [?] Paintings, and some [items]

the 21st of June 1721, on the Prince's Graft, on a Property, according to the usual Brown [?], under which four, high 5 and a half feet, ditto Portbeelden [Statues], ditto Portbeelden, under which the four Prunes [?], over [?], [?] Marble Blocks, of very great size, and some [?] instruction by the said Brokers to be obtained.

[For sale or] to rent a magnificent Manor House, with its Land [and] a fine Manor House and Gardener's Dwelling, [?] Pasture, [?] all with the best sort of Fruit Trees, and [?] Vines [?]

[Exemplars] to be obtained, of the well-known Treatise described by the E. A. Hague May, at the Ottoman Court, governing [?]

A very pleasant Manor House, with its fine Manor Buildings, [?] provided with fine Flower Gardens, Fruit-Wine and [?]

[and] moreover still about 2 and ½ morgen extra fine Meadowland, [?] still another Manor House with its Buildings. [?] for Pasture etc.

[?] Overvep, approximately 4 morgen large; 2 [?] of that Place [?], and at Jac. Schoon in the Heiberg de Letuw, over the short Sidestreet.

[?] soon beforehand to be able to view, and all [?] Manor House-orders Everything, Stairhouses &c.; likewise a modern [?] House with [?] for 14 Pasture, Coach House and Gardener's Dwelling, approximately [?] large, [?] still about 2 morgen good Meadowland lying thereby.

[?] with its Land lying thereto, [?] and [?], approximately [?] [?] House, with its Manor Buildings, Stables for [?] Pasture, Coach Houses [?] and fine Fruit Trees, under Everything, Stairhouses, [?] words.

[suitable,] to read the [?] Leiden, Schywen, Arithmetic, Singing, [?], Land-surveying, Wine-measuring, and other Mathematical Exercises [?] [?] can address themselves to the aforesaid Schoolmaster.

Course of Exchange

The Course of the EXCHANGE is today: Madrid 17½ half; Cadiz [?] and ½ half; Seville 110; Bilbao [?] and ½ half; Lisbon 43 and ½ half a [?]; Venice 51 and ½ half; Livorno [?] and 3 quarters; Genoa 95 and 3 quarters; Paris [?] and 2 quarters; London 5 Shillings 9 [pence]; Danzig 190 [?]; Breslau [?] and ½ half st.; Hamburg [?] St.; Antwerp 3 and 3 quarters a 5 per C.; Ghent 4 and ½ half; Brussels 1; Zeeland ½ half; Rotterdam ¼ quarter. The Agios of the Bank are [?] agulte ailf. The Agio of the E.I.C. 745 à 750. W.I.C. 104, [?] reliant, Subscription 108f.

The 30th of may, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 66.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 2nd of June, 1721

SPAIN.

CADIZ, May 6th. Rumor has it that silver arrived on the latest advice ship and will be distributed to the owners, and it is said that some have already received their share. The old indult and goods are already being handed over to private individuals, as well as silver returned from the mint from the Buenos Aires ships. Despite the loading of the galleons proceeding slowly, it is hoped that they will depart within five or six days, waiting only for dispatches from the court. Three days ago, some value was placed on Maltese warships in sight of Malaga, and it is reported that two Algerian pirates, having used them, sailed home without prizes. Within two or three days, three Dutch warships with their merchant vessels will depart from here for Amsterdam.

ITALY.

LIVORNO, May 10th. At last, for the third time, the ships of Captains Jac. Santfoorn and Kiya Kryutie have put to sea. The Nia, with a large French bark, has returned again; unable to enter because its mast, damaged by storm, had to be repaired at Marseille; and since the bark will not enter here for this reason either, it will have to return to Marseille; the captain reports that on his return to Algiers roasting ships lay ready to put to sea, as well as three French barks heading here, because there were no prizes stored then. Letters from Smyrna of the 1st conveyed news that the English convoy with merchant ships from there had arrived at Constantinople, from where they were seen heading back to Smyrna; there lay several Maltese barks there, which after the taking of Granada were to sail home. From here various English and French ships have departed for Sicily, the Levant, and London, including among others John and Mayrkiliam Bondy, and Kich. Hudkinlop.

FRANCE.

PARIS, May 26th. A certain officer among the King's troops, who for a long time had solicited a pension without being able to obtain it, yet wishing to approach the Lord Regent in a convenient manner to present his petition, found himself one morning in the antechamber of the Duke Regent, whom the young Marshond [Editor's note: likely a young nobleman] greeted, with whom said Duke occupied himself by having him pass through that chamber, and this officer, taking advantage of the opportunity, hung his request on the collar of the dog, which being permitted into the cabinet of his Royal Highness, the Duke took the said request from the collar and read it, and granted this supplicant an audience. Now it is said His Highness was so well pleased that he granted the requested pension to the said officer. The Turkish Ambassador was received at the Comédie: when His Highness a few days later received him at a meal hosted by the Marshal and Duke of Villeroy, the following persons were present there, who were seated at his table in the following order: namely the said Ambassador, who was seated on the side of the fireplace, having at his right side the Marquis of Villeroy and the [?], next to him; then the Marshal and Duke of Villeroy, the Duchesses of Vandadura and la Ferté, the Marshalsess Bouffiers, the Bishop of Frejus, Preceptor of the King, the Agent and Ambassador of the Lords States-General of the United Netherlands, the Marshal de Tallard, and the Duke of Luxun; on the left side of the said Ambassador his son was seated, then the Marquis de Biron, the Knight d'Asfeld, the Prince of Tingry, the Lord Cely Raudschert of Saarti, formerly Intendant at Metz, and the Lord de la Baume, ordinary nobleman, who accompanies the Ambassador; the Marshal mentioned above was gracious, and the table was blessed and served several times with exquisite variety, over which the said Ambassador expressed great satisfaction, and His Highness enjoyed all the dishes served in a pleasant manner with spoon and fork, in the friendly manner; he drank the health of and the healths in the entire company, and each in turn, using nothing other than cider, while his son availed himself of beer; after the meal his servants brought dishes to the windows, and His Highness dined well and found [?] on the windows. Some days ago, the Count of [?] presented to the King a globe with all the attached fortifications and maps; to this a certain Lord of the States-General of the United Netherlands, the world bent, which is made by an ape that shaved a fool, and at the same time displayed a whale light; now it is opened by a common vicar through His Majesty with a [?] has been gifted, and upon hearing still with His Highness Holl, which pastime we around Gibraltar, but is then the campaign with the Spanish ministers Baron of Leuenberg and others mentioned, and in 2000 men will also [?] generations, [?] led. Previous during [?] in knowing few people to the bad sickness used; through letters the opening of is late seen; through letters not in the opening of Galbracter, is late seen at Toulon the plague begins to diminish there, as also at Aix the Marshal de Villem has letters from Fluvidia; but has seen and Friday a fire broke out near the hall by a roaster, and those who were the cause of this misfortune were consumed by fire. It is certain that the Abbot of St. Albin has been appointed coadjutor of the Archbishop of Cambrai, and that the Abbot d'Harcourt has obtained the coadjutorship of the archbishopric of this city. Word comes from Vigo of the 12th that the fleet bound for New Spain was overtaken by a severe storm and forced to put into a Portuguese harbor to provision itself; in Galicia some 1500 recruits had been raised; in the harbor of Vigo lay 47 ships laden with all manner of provisions, ready to depart under convoy of a warship and two frigates to St. Andrews; and from Havana 15 well-laden ships had departed for Spain, of which the Commerce College at Cadiz has been notified. According to letters from Naples of the 6th, the Health Magistrate there had strictly forbidden the entry of all manner of merchandise, which, though they might be from the entire world, not even excepting those goods destined therefor, which Verona also regulates, concerning which the Well-disposed. Yesterday the Envoy from Parma departed from here with dispatches. Daily persons are being seized by the neck. Today the stocks are at 49 to 50 and bank notes of 1000 at 18 livres.

The communication restored between Provence and the nearby provinces, cities and towns of this kingdom is noted as premature and of bad consequence; there is mention of drawing a line from Dijon toward the side of Lyon; and the custom of certain of the city, which suffered only from deficiency of provisions due to its regulations and daily market meal, now yields to the approaching plague. One must nowadays provide oneself with health certificates to leave this city and proceed from France elsewhere. There has been a rumor as if the physicians and surgeons here would have received orders to show themselves at Leverendat and elsewhere. It is also said that the King intends to betake himself toward Flanders, to remain there until the contagious sickness ceases, and that preparations are being made for lodgings for His Majesty, his household, and other persons who may follow. The postilion who brings letters from Rome to the Rhine delivered them at Pont de Beauvoisin. Last Friday a person was seized on the island of Notre Dame on suspicion of being an accomplice of those villains who plundered the post-house at Lyon. The Abbot d'Armagnac, now [?], who has been imprisoned a very long time in the Citadel of Rysell for renouncing the Roman religion, has been released and come into the city after he had again adopted the said Roman religion. The Lord de Bullion, Provost of this city, having died, his son the Marquis de Perraqves inherited the governorship of Mayrie, and has come to an agreement with his brother himself, Knight de Bullion, regarding the succession to the office of Provost of this city. The King has granted letters to the Lord Sequin du Breuil as Councilor of the Parliament. During the night between Thursday and Friday a fire broke out near the hall by a roaster; and the person who was the cause of this misfortune was consumed by fire. It is certain that the Abbot of St. Albin has been appointed coadjutor of the Archbishop of Cambrai, and that the Abbot d'Harcourt has obtained the coadjutorship of the archbishopric of this city. Word is brought from Vigo of the 12th that the fleet bound for New Spain, when it sailed out, was overtaken by a severe storm and forced to put into a Portuguese harbor to provision itself; in Galicia some 1500 recruits had been raised; in the harbor of Vigo lay 47 ships laden with all manner of provisions, ready to sail under convoy of a warship and two frigates toward the Audiencia, and from Havana 15 well-laden ships had departed for Spain, of which the Commerce College at Cadiz has been notified. According to letters from Naples of the 6th of the previous week, word was brought that the Health Magistrate there had strictly forbidden the entry of all manner of merchandise, which, though they might be from any part of the world, not even excepting those goods destined therefor, which Verona also regulates, concerning which the Well-disposed.

GREAT BRITAIN

LONDON, May 27th. The court has had printed at its expense the inventories of the goods and effects of the former directors of the South Sea Company; it has also already been found that Lord Chester makes himself debtor in the sum of 36,000 pounds sterling to a certain [?], which has been publicly assured to be false; that it is indeed true that Chester made him this sum owed, but that he has also paid it. Saturday the Lord Ward (witness of the [?]) presented a bill in the House of Commons to bind the goods and effects of Sir George Carwell, the Lords [?] and Elias Turner to the satisfaction of 25,000 pounds sterling to the South Sea Company. The House in grand committee turned itself to examining the bill regarding the unfortunate losses in the South Sea Company and that to prevent the Lord Aislaby from leaving the kingdom within the space of a year, as well as to pledge his goods and effects and prevent further disposition of the same; some progress having been made in this bill, further consideration was postponed until today. Yesterday the Lord Trviseel presented a bill to the general House to provide the cities of London and Westminster and neighboring places better with fresh water, which bill being received and the first reading thereof made, the third was not withdrawn from the House. The first reading was also made of the bill to bind the real and personal goods of the Lord of the Good Sir George Carwell, the Lords Jacob Saachridge and Elias Turner to make good a sum of 25,000 pounds sterling to the South Sea Company; and it was ordered that the second reading might be done; and it was ordered that the Knights Will. Courtency, Will. Blacket, Harvey Elves, [?]. Williams, Charles Kaypéut go, because they have neglected to verify it; it was ordered the removal of the same against today at [?] days; and all absent members as then will be placed in the custody of the Sergeant at Arms; and the Council was asked to report upon it; on coming Friday morning; this report made May [?]; on coming Friday morning; this report contains before a meeting between the absentees [?] and Shaw (which under escort of the Sergeant at Arms [?]), which meeting is [?] impossible [?]

[ENGLAND]

...been conducted, and the payment of Shaw in particular concerns the fact that some 30 pages were taken from his Books, which contained the transactions between him and Knight. The Secret Committee continues its investigations daily; today George Cnapoll, summoned from the City, was brought before it for examination. Yesterday a certain housekeeper named Pierpoint, found near Grays Inn and Dinanni, was brought in with approximately 2,000 pounds sterling, but it is understood that he has since gone to the King.

The previous week a woman, who is 120 years old, went to the Bath at Tunys, and remains in good health with a lively Memory; she said she was never bled, nor used any remedies in her entire life; and that she had lived as Knight did before the death of King Charles the Second. Yesterday the Earl of Grantham, Chamberlain to the Prince and Princess of Wales, went to Richmond to arrange the new Apartments, on which all three will be enlarged, setting aside 300 [units] so that everything will be completed by the beginning of next month, since their Royal Highnesses intend to spend the Summer there and proceed thither after the celebration of the King's birthday on the 28th of this month. The young Princess Carolina is reported in good health. Saturday afternoon the King and the 2 young Princes gave a dinner for the Princess of Wales. The previous week at the Toll there were registered 1,040 ounces of Gold and 2,005 ounces of Silver to be transported to France, and 600 ounces of Gold and 1,300 of Silver to be transported to Holland. Colonel Hamilton, having requested to be relieved of his Government of the Leeward Islands, has the King's permission for Colonel Hart, who was formerly Governor of Maryland, to take his place. Those of the Lower House, today in Grand Committee over the Bill, to prevent the King's Subjects from engaging in trade in the East Indies under foreign Commissions, and to secure the lucrative trade of those Lands, have made some progress therein, and resolved to continue the matter next Thursday. This afternoon a man in Calehouse was brought in imprisoned, because he cursed the King.

At Plymouth and Falmouth there have arrived from China the Ships the Success and the Mioney, whose Cargo is not yet public; it is said they were at the Cape, where according to loose rumor 12 Dutch Ships lay; it is expected daily from China the Ships Batavia and the Satellite. The Prices of the Bank are 129, E.I.C. 136, S.Z. 134, Mill. Bank 120, Lottery and Bank Ann. 96 and a half, Afr. Comp. 18, the same new 30, Exchange Assurance, the same London 6 and 7, and York Building 11 and a half.

...would have been able to pass, of which the unfortunate [consequences], which are also felt in the second [instance], through severe inundations, the resulting Knowledge, diminution of Commerce, Trades and prosperity, and other difficulties, are able to serve as proof, as also as a warning of heavy Flaggings and Judgments, unless they be mitigated by Divine mercy, it is [resolved] that the Gentlemen States General of the United Netherlands, to give God the honor for the good which this dear Fatherland has experienced, and to implore his further blessing in power, durability and necessity, have deemed it fit, and accordingly with immortal will, approved, to appoint a general Day of Thanksgiving and Prayer, throughout all the united Provinces, and for the occasion of these Lands; the form and conduct whereof, on Wednesday, which shall be the 29th of June next coming, so that on the same day in all the Churches of these Lands God Almighty shall be praised and thanked with unity and from the heart for all his benefits bestowed upon this dear Fatherland up to now by free grace, and in particular also for the calm and peace which it has enjoyed for some years and still enjoys, and likewise to make confession of all the Land's high and continually increasing sins and iniquities, and with a worthy contrition and repentance henceforth with courage and fervor to pray and beseech, for the gracious forgiveness of all the same Sins and iniquities, and against his Enemies and their servants against the State, and over the good inhabitants thereof, so that they may be preserved in their cherished Freedom and Religion, and the precious Peace, in God's grace, may be further continued, and that all deserved Plagues, and in particular the Pestilence, elsewhere so terribly raging, may be turned away from these Lands, and that all the Wars, in other parts of Europe continuing, may be changed into a wished-for calm and peace, and all the severe difficulties with which the Protestants are oppressed, through the all-powerful might of the Lord, by the furtherance and recommendation to the true reformed Christian Religion, confederacy of the Land's beloved freedom, and Our Soul's salvation, &c. &c.

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 31st May. On the 29th of this month the Pilot Wilh. Wilken of Bleyeh arrived before this City, on the 30th the Happy Retorne under Antony Smith, and on the 31st the Christoffel and Mary under John Mattinson, both from Newcastle; on the 30th the Blind Ezol Batham departed for Delft Church.

AMSTERDAM, 31st May. Via Smyrna and Verden letters have been received at Livorno from the 10th October from Suratte, in which City news was that the Portuguese Ship which departed from Lisbon the previous year has arrived at Goa. At Lisbon have arrived the Ship St. Maets under Captain Lancdornet of Bayonne, the Hope under Abraham Winter of Topsham, and the Freedom under Jeronimp Prexecre of Genua; at Nantes the 3 Goedsbergs under Jacob Groen the Younger of [?], and another richly laden Ship from Cadiz via Bordeaux under Jan Slenrer of [?]; at Jan Marcaid of Dordrecht; at Kochelle Matten van de Velde of [?] and Ventura de Sachs of Bilbao; at Bergen Dirk Klaarffen of Hamburg; at Bremen Warnart Diejes of Dublin; in Friesland Dirk Harings from Norway; and at Copenhagen (where all the Iceland-bound vessels still are) one saw Rynd. Swet of here meeting them. In that the Hakkebot found on the Iceland-bound Ship a small Maiden with a Book under the Arm, one concludes that the same was painted by can. Luyije Gernis.

VIENNA, 11th May. The previous Sunday a Courier arrived from Prague with news that the reigning Empress had arrived there safely on the 7th of this month. That day the noble Count Dizzent of Fellentihel was married to the Countess Bajol Chamberlain of the Empress Dowager Eleonora, in the presence of the entire Court, at which gathering went to the Maiden Convent. Monday the Imperial Councillor Count of Henemy arrived in this City from Prague, also bringing a personal Letter from the Empress to His K. Majesty, in which she notifies of her arrival in the Capital of Bohemia, and likewise of her journey to Carlsbad, without [details], as it is hoped daily to hear of her arrival there, whence she goes to the aforementioned Maiden Convent, remaining there the entire day with the Empress Dowager, and toward evening in the Imperial [residence] returning. Notwithstanding the diversion of the Court hunt at Luxembourg, His Majesty's Imperial [presence] [is] daily in the common Conferences, to put the Camerial and Financial affairs on a better footing. The Emperor has received a personal Letter from the newly elected Pope Innocent XIII, in which His K. Majesty is notified of his elevation. The report suggests that Cardinal Piazza, who previously held the Nunciature at this Court, would be appointed as [?] and Secretary of State by His Majesty, which is as pleasing as the elevation of the Pope.

REGENSBURG, 26th May. The previous Friday the Electoral Envoys again excused themselves from the Assembly, through those of Bavaria and Cologne, declaring they could not come due to indisposition, so nothing of importance was transacted in that Assembly. The Report, drawn up by the Protestant Corps in defense of the Reformed inhabitants of Weerd, has come out in Print, and is much praised.

MAINZ, 28th May. His Serene Highness the Margrave of Ansbach arrived here by Water with his Consort, to proceed further to the Ems Bath. The Kasselle Regiments of Foot destined for Pomerania, of Ranck, Rau, Denhoff, 4 Companies of Grenadiers, one Regiment of Dragoons, together with the taken-over Darmstadt Regiment of Foot, continue their March by three different routes toward Pomerania, and the other nine Regiments are to follow shortly, but it is not known to whom the upper Command over these 7 troops is entrusted. The Landgrave of Hesse Cassel had appointed the Gentleman Peter Romanus, born a Hollander, as Lieutenant over the Buildings, Gardens, and Fortifications.

FRANKFURT, 28th May. Yesterday evening the Margrave of Ansbach arrived here, and today with his Consort departed for the Ems Bath.

COLOGNE, 29th May. The Papal Nuncio Count of Atchinto held festivities on Tuesday and Thursday for the elevation of the new Pope through Felleys, and heard all the bell-ringing [?] and from his Palace; within 6 weeks intends to depart thence as Nuncio to [?].

THE HAGUE, 1st June. The Proclamation of the convening of the general Day of Thanksgiving and Prayer, which according to the ordinance of this Province has been sent out, reads as follows:

Honorable, Powerful, Wise, Beloved, Gracious, Gentlemen: As we have taken into consideration the present state of affairs, and in particular have reflected how through God's infinite goodness and forbearance this dear Fatherland has been preserved to this point in its Freedom and Religion, and for the present still enjoys a wished-for calm and peace, as it has done for some years, notwithstanding all the abundance of Sins and iniquities, which increase rather than decrease, and which would have been able to turn away the blessing of God Almighty from the Land and diminish his wrath, and even bring severe punishments upon it, of which the unhappy [consequences], which are also felt in the second [instance], through severe inundations, the resulting Knowledge, diminution of Commerce, Trades and prosperity, and other difficulties, are able to serve as proof, as also as a warning of heavy Flaggings and Judgments, unless they be mitigated by Divine mercy; it is [resolved] that, since the appointed Day of Fasting and Prayer is on Wednesday the 11th June 1721, the Magistracy in this City will hold it on Thursday and Friday the 12th and 13th June 1721.

The Auction, which by the Broker Filip Pick, at Amsterdam IN THE HOPE ON THE COLVENIERS BURGWAL, on Wednesday the 4th June and Tuesday, shall be held of Dutch and Foreign East Indian Articles, Silk, half Silk and Woolen Stuffs, Chintz, Kersey cloth, Calico, Paintings, and some bedding, Linen, Rol-Armozynes, white Satin Damasks, white Silk Kersey, Linen, Cotton Satin, white Linen, Tea in Rolls, Jewels which extended until Thursday the 13th June. Hope: all being viewable for 3 days, and the Catalogues by said Broker Abraham van Bezd, Pieter van den Hotch, Adrianus Kamerbeck and Abraham van Dorteillaer Brokers, shall be sold at Amsterdam on

Tuesday the 9th June 1721, at 6 o'clock in the evening, at the Gingelhall in Royt, a quantity of Cordage, about 150 Slaves, about 1,000 sheets loose Tin, and a quantity of Walch, belonging to the wrecked Ship's Fortÿm under Captain JanPier. Thursday the 12th June 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning, in the Colveniers Hall in the Citadel, a quantity of Copper, consisting of Red Sheet, System Plates, and some Building and Meadow Land, all to be seen daily at 9 o'clock in the morning. These 3 new [items]

Elias de Marios Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Thursday the 12th June 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning, in the Jewish Street, a quantity of finished Jewels and Rough Diamonds; all to be seen daily at 9 o'clock in the morning.

1. The Knight's Manor, with 16 morgen of arable and Meadow Land, and Orchard. 2. The Ambaght's-Manor, with tenants' rights, Tenant's Dwelling, Orange House &c.; further the Plantation with Orchards, Parterres, Alleys, Shrubberies, Fountains, and Fish-pond, also a fine new Chicken House with all conveniences, and some Building and Meadow Land with the arrangement of Barn, Sheepfold, Secretary's Office, Road, and further rights pertaining &c., wherein included the Tithes belonging to said Manor under the Field and Meadow, also under Field lying. 3. Also 36 morgen of arable Building and Meadow Land, similarly lying under Field. 4. A part of 26 morgen of arable Building [land], Parcels in whole or in part to be purchased wholly, otherwise inquire about said Manor, or with Pieter Sjps in the Kaivestraat by the Mint at Amsterdam.

Course of Exchange

The Course of the EXCHANGE is today on Madrid 87 and a half, Cadiz [?] and a half, Seville 88 and a half, Bilbao 55, Lisbon 43 and a quarter, Venice 31 and a half, a quarter, Livorno 91 and three quarters, Genoa 91 and three quarters, a half, Geneva 94, Paris view 42, London 93 shillings 9, Danzig 215 per hundred, Breslau 3 and a half st., Hamburg 22 and a quarter st., Antwerp 1 per hundred, Ghent 2 and a half, Brussels 3 and [?] quarter, Zeeland 5 [?], Rotterdam 1 [?]. The Prices of the E.I.C. are 104 [?], I.C. 104 and a half, Substance 1010.

Notices & Advertisements

On Monday the 4th August 1721, there shall be sold at the Place as shall be further advertised, the magnificent and very famous estate Heemitlee near Uitgeest, which shall be divided on the following partition:

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 2nd of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 4th of June, 1721
Persia

ISFAHAN, 10 January. The King of Persia, who is governing from Macholt with great care, has not only had the good fortune to defeat the rebels at Tlaan, relieve the siege of Casshan, and drive out the insurgents in the mountains, but has also uncovered a great conspiracy against his person and government by several rebels. The chief minister of this conspiracy, Artemal Diopiaan, who favored the rebels' cause, has had his eyes put out for this reason and subsequently beheaded—an execution of such a prominent person as has rarely been seen here. According to letters of 16 October from Surat, several English ships were lying there.

The West Indies

CURAÇAO, 29 March. Since the 15th of this month, various barks and other vessels have arrived here, including ships belonging to Jan Pieterfs, Jan Somel, Jan Boent, François Pereyn, Willem de Vos, Pieter, and about 20 others, from Tucacá, Coro, Maracaibo, and the Cumaná coast; also a Spanish bark and two others from Saint-Domingue; three French [ships], together with the barks of Abram Vilfer, Jan van Hoorn, and an English ship from Río de la Hacha; two barks from Jamaica, one from Bermuda, two from New York, and several others. At La Guaira lie two Spanish ships ready to sail, and likewise at Puerto Caballo two other [ships] of that nation, all bound for Vera Cruz, together with another ship bound for Cádiz. The ship Madera, belonging to Pieter Rocloffs, was to depart from here on 15 April for Amsterdam, and was to be followed thither by the Curaçao galley Pieter Loefhouwen.

Bohemia

CARLSBAD, 18 May. This afternoon the Empress arrived here safely with a retinue of more than 300 persons, besides the nobility of this land, who have also joined her Imperial Majesty. The Duke and Duchess of Brunswick-Lüneburg, father and mother of Her Imperial Majesty, have also arrived here.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 21 May. The King, who has completely recovered from his indisposition, has declared that he will inspect the army in person, and the troops that comprise it have already arrived in camp. Our fleet has put out from the straits to sea and will join [forces] with the Great British [fleet], which has already arrived on the Gothenburg coast, as well as the ships that have sailed from Karlskrona.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

BRUNSWICK, 21 May. Several Catholic regiments, which were previously reluctant, have now completely accepted the peace aforesaid, and one will soon depart for the Congress at Cambrai.

France

PARIS, 20 May. Letters from Rome report that the Pope has appointed his brother, the Nuncio of Tuscany, as Grand Penitentiary, and Cardinal George Spinola as Vicar in Rome. Monsieur de Languet, Bishop of Soissons, has been appointed to the French Academy in place of the deceased Monsieur S. Argenton. The court had indeed resolved to increase the poll tax in all the provinces of this kingdom to compensate for the losses suffered in Provence, but when the general receivers expressed their concerns, they resolved to spread this advance over three years in order to prevent the subjects from being burdened further, and to await the time when His Majesty shall see fit to restore it to them. They were presented to the King by the Marshal and Duke of Villeroy, who on this occasion reasoned with such force and eloquence that it was admired by all. The aforesaid receivers have already sent out orders to provide what is necessary. Monsieur Guinet, Intendant of Caen, has received orders from the court to prosecute the officers of the Admiralty who have allowed goods to be unloaded that came from Provence. Monsieur de Bonas has departed toward the Duke of Berwick, who will send some militia to Languedoc and will fortify the barrier there. The Count of Rossi continues to be ill; his friends have worked to have him transfer the government of Provence, which he holds, to his son the Marquis de Roye, who has refused this generously, saying that it should go to his uncle the Count of Blaste, who has the reversion of it. It is also said that the Marquis de la Vilière would have refused the reversion as Secretary of State for his son, because he is too young. Four decrees of the King's Council of State are to be seen: one of 17 May, establishing the four percent on bills of exchange. Another of the 18th of the same month concerns the annuities which have been placed in the provinces. Another of the same date ordains that the receipts which have been delivered by the directors of bank accounts in the provinces for the value of bank notes of 1000 and 10000 livres, which receipts were to cover annuities and income payments at Tours, shall be verified by the commissioners of the council. The fourth of the 20th commands that the holders of lottery tickets who have received gifts from the Company of the Indies shall be held to have them verified. The annuities have fallen to 51 and one half, and the bank notes are presently at [?] livres; notwithstanding these low prices, the number of those who are demanding the securities is very small, and no buyers are to be found; and although the fishing is now proceeding vigorously, and the operation is being undertaken by Messrs. Paris, it is not possible that everything can be completed within the appointed time, which is why it is believed that a further delay will be unavoidable. The dearness of foreign goods, which is already quite excessive, shows no sign of abating. During the night between Monday and Tuesday the cold here was so great that many vineyards froze, and since the following nights also had extraordinary cold, it could be observed that there was some shrinkage in the wines, as many people wish to provide for themselves and fear that the crop might be spoiled. It is further reported that the Abbé Mornay, Archbishop of Besançon, ambassador of the King to the court of Portugal, has died. It is learned that this duke has little freedom to return to this city. It is also said that the Duke of Berwick will also make a journey to Montauban to give the necessary orders for the correction of the new roll of those who have renewed their appeal in this city and other provinces, which creates much discussion, and people are very attentive to see what course the court will take in this matter. We shall not alter [this report] and it will be that their punishment is imposed; in the new roll of this city there are more than 530 persons who have renewed their appeal, among whom are more than 100 curates of this city, of which the number belongs, and 80 doctors of the Sorbonne; the number of those in the provinces who have renewed their appeal amounts to over 400 persons, besides the Chapter of Tours, which have all, save two or three, renewed their appeal. Some days ago a woman was apprehended on suspicion of leading an evil life; she rented rooms in a poor house, which she used for [nefarious purposes]; nearby at Courville there was a large band of brigands who had their meetings; it is reported that this infamous gang numbered over 100 members, including soldiers, deserters and companions, as well as over 200 well-dressed merchants from this city; it is reported that she has revealed everything, and that several members of this band have already been apprehended; some say that it was they who planned the robbery of the coach on Lyon. Monday evening the foot and mounted guards in the suburbs engaged in a skirmish with these fellows; they were coming out of a house and going out into the public streets with pistols in one hand and swords in the other; the guard fired on them, and as one of them fired on the guard, the guard was wounded, and he together with several others [were wounded], but because the guard [continued], they were pursued by 400 men, one was taken into custody but many [escaped]; the others were captured, but the fifth managed to escape so well that he has not yet been found; although it is reported that he cannot be found; although measures have been taken with torches and soldiers to search for him.

The 4th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 6th of June, 1721
England

[Note: This section heading appears misplaced in the original; the content is actually from Italy and the Papal States]

NAPLES, 13 May. The Prince Borghese, our Viceroy, has with the advice of the Collateral Council forbidden the importation by sea or land into this Kingdom of any goods brought by the Quinaire, even though they come from places with which trade is not forbidden, in order to take the necessary precautions. Word has been received that 600 soldiers from the corps of Manfredonia, who were being transported to be sent thence to Hungary, rose up against their officers on the march and took refuge at Benevento, whereupon the Government sent some cavalry there and wrote to Rome for orders that they might be conducted from the Ecclesiastical Territory hither. Friday morning the Viceroy received news that Cardinal Conti had passed away at Easter, upon which time all the bells were rung, the cannon of the fortresses discharged, and public rejoicing was conducted for three days. The Duke of Celano, who is married to the Princess Ispoliti and nephew of His Holiness by customary right, betook himself, together with various other Neapolitans, blood relatives of the new Pope, by post to Rome.

ROME, 17 May. Last Saturday, Pope Innocent XIII admitted for the first time in ordinary audience the Marquis Frangipani, Senator of this City, with the Conservators of the City of Rome, the Falconieri, Governor, Cibo, Auditor of the Apostolic Camera, Geligola, Treasurer, and other Ministers, who kneeling down made their compliments and sought from His Holiness confirmation of the state of their offices; the Pope received them in a majestic manner and answered in brief terms, desiring that they should continue to observe their duties with the utmost exactitude. His Holiness also confirmed the Lord Giud[?] in his office as Keeper of the Apostolic Palace, and on this occasion it was remarked that His Holiness demonstrated the majesty of his Papal power and authority by ordaining that the Pelotry [?], which shall come to the household of the Prince, may have permission that they themselves shall not go outside this City and the Ecclesiastical Territory to await the outcome of their process in another jurisdiction, and it was reported that His Eminence declared he would not depart from Rome; however, he would have to await the outcome of his process at Castel St. Angelo; and meanwhile a rumor was spread that in the said Congregation it was decided that the process of this same Eminence was sequestered, with the archives of his process [to remain in] Castel St. Angelo. Sunday, a solemn Te Deum was sung in all the churches of this City to thank Heaven for the election of a new Pope; and on this occasion the bells were rung for two hours in the morning. That same morning the Cardinals Tanara and Orfini, Decans and Vice-Decans, had audience with the Pope; and the following Sunday all the Cardinals were summoned to give an accounting. That evening the funeral service for Cardinal Paracciani was held in the Jesuit Church of Jesus, where all the clergy kneeling down, priests and all who had not received the necessary supplies participated in the general public funeral. That evening the Pope conferred the Vicariate of this City, which he had taken upon himself, to Cardinal Paolucci. Monday the Cardinals Cordini, Orighi, and George Spinola had audience with the Pope; but Cardinal Paolucci, with His Holiness and the penitentiary worked in the inner chamber. After the audience, His Holiness appointed Cardinal George Spinola as Secretary of State; Cardinal Cordini as Proto-datary, Cardinal Orighi as President of the Conclave, and the Lord Doria as Chamberlain of His Holiness, the Lord Ruspoli as Secretary of Memorials or Petitions, the Lord Riviera as Secretary of the [?], with the provision that he shall also retain his secretary and auditor positions; the Lord Pallionelli as Secretary of Latin Letters and Chamberlain, the Advocate Accoriamboni as Under-datary, the Lord Beccanini as Under-agent, the Lord Marceliotbi as Auditor of His Holiness, the Lord Seglion as Secretary to the Princes, the Abbot Fernestuni as Almoner; the Abbot Tasca as Master of the Wardrobe, the Abbot Minicense (who before His Holiness became Cardinal) as Chamberlain; the Keeper [?] remained; and all the other Nobility of His Majesty were declared as first Chamberlains Patrimonial; His Holiness also appointed first the Father Firmilian of the Order of Capuchins and Minorite Brothers; as his [?] Physician Doctor Michael Angeli; and the Marquis Maidalchini as Body Physician. Hereupon the Lord [?] was appointed as Legate of Benevento to his first Chamberlain. Cardinal Buzaiti was confirmed as Secretary and declared as Keeper of the Seal, and the aforesaid official was continued as Secretary in the bureau.

MILAN, 21 May. Our Governor received news that Cardinal Conti was elected Pope by 25 votes, and thus by all the Cardinals in the Conclave, excepting His own vote, which he had given to Cardinal Tanara. The French believe one ship on the domain of the Pope struck a reef, which was carried by a corps of soldiers on the ground of Piedmont, where it had formerly been wont.

VENICE, 24 May. Last Saturday the Senate appointed the Lords Knights and Procurators, Alvise, Fiffani, and Andrea da Lezze, above the Knight Jean Francis Morolini, as extraordinary Ambassadors to go and compliment the Pope on his elevation. That evening the Prince and Princess of Modena arrived here incognito, heard Mass on Sunday in the Church of the Religious of St. Lawrence, and visited various other churches in the afternoon: the retinue was quite large, and among them was also the Abbot Filli, who some time ago was sent from France by the Duke Regent. That same afternoon an express messenger came from Rome, who having delivered a packet of letters to the Nuncio, proceeded on his way. Monday morning the Nuncio gave notice to the College of the election of the new Pope. Thursday, the Feast of the Ascension of Christ, the Doge, accompanied by the Government, went in the Bucentaur [Editor's note: the ceremonial state barge of Venice] to marry the Sea according to custom, on which occasion the concourse of gondolas was extraordinarily great; the Prince and Princess of Modena were also present in a vessel that was splendidly equipped and adorned: after the ceremony was concluded, the Holy Doge received the Government at a splendid banquet, which is customary. There is a rumor here that the Prince and Princess of Modena intend to return to Modena shortly.

LEGHORN, 17 May. Word has been received that the ship la Madonna de la Guida, departing from Lisbon for Goa, was lost near the Philippine Islands.

GENOA, 17 May. All the advices from Provence confirm that the rest [of the plague] continues to increase more and more, and particularly at Toulon, where over 300 sick are reported to be in the Hospital of the Holy Spirit, which contagion is so great that any sick person who approached it was immediately infected, wherefore it was decided to abandon those sick without further concern, such that the others who come to be sick should be brought outside the City in pitched tents. At Aix and other nearby places the sickness continues also to make great ravage; and word has been sent from Paris permitting vinegar to be consumed by the people, because the air there is so befouled that not only the inhabitants but all who approach that place come to become ill. It is said that the plague should also be at Boccaie and Eres; there is a rumor that this scourge has penetrated to Carcassonne in Languedoc, and that already a family of 12 persons has died there; also there was great fear that the sickness should continue to creep forward, to Arles, from whence reports are received at Marseille.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 21 May. Word has been received that His Highness Bruise, Plenipotentiary of the Czar of Great Russia, has arrived at Neustadt, and the Lord Oosterman, who was sent there all days, is also expected; to begin negotiations with the Ministers of this Crown; it is not yet known what resolution was taken in the Senate or what was determined against His Majesty the Czar, and that it will be decided only on the proposals. A further express has been sent to our Plenipotentiaries at Neustadt. Meanwhile everything necessary for the preparation of war is being done more than ever; the negotiations over peace have made no progress [?]; to be in a state to offer resistance to the Muscovites. The French Ministry from Constantinople [?] the garrison of Confide, continued [?]; [uncertain passage] His express, which he sent to his court some time ago; whether it will be possible with more caution, or whether said Minister shall go once more to the court of the Czar, or go directly to Neustadt. The king is reported to have recovered [?]. No news has yet been received [?]; regarding certainty the recovered state. Word has been received [?]; that our war fleet, together with that sent under Admiral Norris, is in [?] still remains placed under contract [?]; however, this is considered [?]. The high [?] [uncertain passage] has been set at liberty. The Minister the Count of Frygg, is expected every moment from besieged Copenhagen in this City; word has been received that some Catholic troops have marched to Pomerania in March.

France

PARIS, 16 May. Now that for some time efforts have been made to discover the hiding place of a certain Thief, who stolen by night in the streets of this City [engaged in] murder and stealing in safety, and that by day the approaches from outside [section breaks off incomplete]

Left Column

It has come to light that a woman named la Piemoa, who sold galanteries [small luxury goods] along the houses, committed these crimes, notably in a house that stood before a shoemaker's; this occurred at [?]; in order to further next Carnouche, who [?] for 3 months, and withdrew from the Fort [?]; the water of the salt in the [?]-ring led this scoundrel to be discovered on Tuesday the 10th of this month by the Archers, in the garden of someone near Luxemburg, where he was with four of his associates; but when he became aware that the Archers were pursuing him, he sent one of his company to the town of the garden, to [?] with the constables; the named Carnouche [?] with the pistol on the breast in contest; [text becomes increasingly unclear] ...the Archers, with a lamp, [?]; coming to the designated place, but his troop [?]; and after much effort, around the small Augustinian cloister [?]; the Archers, [?]; and eventually they came above, shot at him, and in the confusion on the chamber [?] the brother of Carnouche appeared, who still fought quite long after that discovery, and he himself through the [?] down the chimney, calls out to one of these scoundrels; and he falls dead; we [?], but this young man here thereupon, and [?] in a [?], whereupon he came out of the chimney, and one of the other 3 [?]; they opened the small Augustinian cloister, to search for Carnouche, but he was not to be found. According to letters from Rome, the Pope has found [?], but we know better. The Parliament, which convened on the preceding Wednesday with all chambers [?] against the subversion of this character, which then after sufficient proof [?], not having conformed itself to the [?] exercises, [?]; and most [?] [?] and upon the Civil [?] and Parliament, although they follow the Civil [?] and Parliament, [?] [text becomes too corrupted to reliably continue]

---

AUCTION NOTICES AND REAL ESTATE LISTINGS

Cornelis de Roos, Broker in Amsterdam, shall on Saturday the 7th June 1721, at three o'clock in the afternoon, publicly sell a fine and pleasant [property], [?]; with attached notices to be seen, and further information from Abraham van Brecht, Jan vander Burg, Jan Straatsman, and M. de Bries.

Franco Cerdelo, Jan van [...], and Gregorius Mees, Brokers, shall in Rotterdam at the inn the Black Lion in Wine Street, [sell] a curious party of Cavalier Pekko, recently arrived from sea and unloaded, on Monday the 5th June 1721. [?] Oudhehorn, J. and D. Boschekonning, Brokers, shall on Monday the 12th June 1721 sell a party of wood goods, namely 50 second Hamburg barrels, of extra fine quality; 1100 flanks, long and short; as well as 2 hogsheads of ordinary bones, a party of spars, [?], and a party of barrels of the [?] beasts.

Pilp Pick, Broker, shall in Amsterdam sell on Thursday the 15th June 1721, in the room at the Collegeburial, a party of Irish linens, wool, silk, half-silk and wool fabrics, [?], needle-cloths, cotton sorts, with some small [?]; silk [?] 2 pages to be seen, and the catalogue by needed [?]; Nathanl Jacoel, and Corn. Veilude, Brokers, shall in Rotterdam at 6 o'clock, in Wine Street, at the inn the Black Lion, a curious party of [?]-tobacco, dated 10 October 1720, and [?] as the great houses of the [?] [text becomes corrupted]

Cornelis de Roos, Broker in Amsterdam, shall on Wednesday the 11th June 1721, in Schager and French linen, in the afternoon at [?] o'clock, publicly sell and trade Schager and French linen [?]; broader by the notices to be seen, [?] and Caed de Roos, Brokers, shall on Monday the 12th June 1721 [?] of the Eagle, sell a fine cabinet of [?] beads, [?], and values, as well as white statues, marble values, [?] of painted marble, further some fine [?] and marble [?]; all to be seen 2 days before the sale, and further instruction [?] [text becomes very corrupted] ...it is notified to all notice-holders that on [?] June 1721, one shall begin [?] by the late St. N. Chevaliers; and after the end of the sale medals shall be [?]; whereof a separate catalogue shall be made [?] the goods can be seen on the gallery [below]

Cornelis de Roos and Jan van de Streng, Brokers, intend to sell or [?] situated by the Lord's Road, a fine country house with free avenues, fruit trees, gardens, [?], all fresh, about 4 morgen [acres], and the land with 4 morgen.

[?] and van de Streng and Dirk Nagel, Brokers, intend to [?] or buy [?], provided with beautiful orchards, and the best fruit trees, further avenues, fine [?], provided with nice [?] as well as stabling for 14 [?] 2 morgen, situated in the Bante van Velzen in Driebuys, [?] Middelen, [text becomes fragmentary]

Jan van de Streng, Broker, intends to sell a homestead with its land [?] on the Looij [?] Toll-house, on the Leyden [?], named Klein Berneyel, with house and garden-man's dwelling &c.; the homestead provided with fine orchards, fish-ponds &c.; as further seen, and by said Broker can be learned.

At Leyden printed by FELIX DE KLOE over the Town Hall, the 6th June 1721

Right Column

DORDRECHT, 4th June. Yesterday arrived before this city the ships the Utrecht and Elisabeth Joseph Davidfon, and today the Margriet from Charm Matihew [?] Edw[ard] Robinson, the Content Will. Eftly, the Chariot from Peterjou, the Eflington, the John from the St. Francis Creiphaidt, all from [?], the Edinburgh Gilthorman, the John from [...] [...], all from Soeeterijland; the Ouenick Goodwill John Boot from Newcastle, [?]; and against this [?] from Jup Abraham Poft from Ostend, and against the city Dordrecht [?] from Meppelen, [?]

AMSTERDAM, 4th June. The ship from Lisbon to Goa arrived, is named N. S. de Cabo and St. Pedro de Alcantara. Arrived at Sint Marien the St. Anna Marten Will. from here; to Nantes [?] Crooper; 4 ships, Syimes Gerrits, Jan Pauw, and others; to Crooper 4 ships, and to Bordeaux Marien de Boer, Pieter Bendt, and others, all from here, and the Mass, of which some, as well as Symen Janfle, Jan Nobel &c., which had entered at Bournemouth through [?] have been unloaded in the [?] and cast out [?]. The ship [?] came the East Indian ship Loendereen, as when the ship [...] banner Doen [?] Ankerman before this [...] Bleithuven, in [?] before this Ankerman Anker [?] which of the ships, the Samariüaan for Zealand, the Spertgeleda for Delft, and Groode-gewaart for Rotterdam, [?] for [?] speediest [?]; the said ships departed on 30 October from Batavia, and in February from the Cape of Good Hope, whereby the cargo amounts in the ton of gold, and some [?]; the cargo thereof is not yet public, but it is said that they brought 350,000 pounds of tea [?] the different ships to Fatherland [?] Batavia were [?], it came [?]. With more named ships [?], [text becomes corrupted] ...it is said [?] ships [text very fragmentary] ...in January [?] in Batavian [?] the ships Purmerland, Arensburg, Barnevelt, Remiweik, Ambroze, Saortelijckbeidis, the Johanna, Borslelen, the Arendboerg, the Alkvier, Fruitehurg, and the Elizabet [?] in the East Indies from the Keronie, which [?] from the East Indies [?], in 12 ships would be, including drawn the 6 great ships [?]. The cargo of the said East Indian ships the Elice and Montague, consists of 50 bales of [?], 734 B. Geloorgas, 812 B. Gorgoutona, 120 B. Gotthes, 1200 B. Jujubes, 150 B. Pollet, 1938 B. Palmitat, 11,300 pounds of sago, 1,200 p. Tea Bing, 133,000 p. Tea Bos, 101,000 p. Tea Congoe, 8,300 p. Tea Singlo, 1,300 p. Tea Byfilon, 2,700 p. Tea Clüftele [?], and 1,015 [?] Bumantle

April, Rotterdam [?]. The [?] of the Black [?] and [?], with advice of the O.I.C. [?] [?]. Saturday to [?] The Hague. The English Bank 110, O.I.C. 131, and [?] 110, without trade.

THE HAGUE, 3rd June. This evening or morning depart the Gentlemen Shuis, Nuyen, and de Vriesen of Glandum, as [?] [?] Their Mog. to the aforementioned to States Flanders, until changing of the Mog. brass-setting there. The Lord Postulate of Town, Aldermen of the Knights-ship, [?], to the [?] of the latter States General the [?] as receiver of the commune Lands Middle, and the [?] of Haarlem and the Great Zijl and the Niue-hoevg [?], and the jurisdiction of the [?] of the commission has. The British Minister the Lord Davyrolles, [?] with some gentlemen of the government. Have meanwhile [?] the Guard [?] behind the old court, before the General Count Hompeich, and other gentlemen, their foot exercise.

IX DE KLOPPER, on the Broad Street

The 6th June 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

Notices & Advertisements

On Tuesday the 13th and Monday the 12th June 1721, [?] on the Cingel over the Hunt.

For sale: an extra well-running white paper mill, having been used by Klaas Cornelielie [?]; to be inquired of by the widow herself.
