Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 9th of June, 1721

Monday's Gazette

Barbary

ALGIERS, 16 April. Despite various losses of our privateering ships at sea, two frigates have nevertheless been brought in here; some of them, which had been at sea for a long time, have returned from Vidauquaire. From this anchorage, [ships] have newly set out to sea, and it is said about 100 [are sailing] to cruise in the Strait of Gibraltar.

India

CADIZ, 12 May. On account of the pestilential sickness in Provence, and particularly at Toulon, which continues to some degree; nevertheless, all possible precautions are being taken from the ports and neighbouring stations of this kingdom. The Governor of the Ships is heading to [?], and from French southern ports [ships] have departed; our sailing galleons are receiving some cargo, and it is said [?]. The Holland warships are ready to depart tomorrow with their merchants from here, among which are to be found the St. Nicolaas Gerlaard and others; the [?] of the galleons continues still, [and] it is possible [they] will remain lying [here] until the end of this month.

France

There are Holland warships under Captain Reys, lying at anchor several times before [?], along with those of the Grondvelt. After we came with the Holland warships, under Admiral van Somelsdyck, to the general Rendezvous by Heyland, and we proceeded forward into the Bay of [?], having experienced storms and bad weather underway; we have arrived here about midday, but have received no news up to now; and we are assured that next month 2 ships will be sent here to cruise in the bay, and that the same will proceed with the remainder towards the coasts of Portugal and Spain; it is generally said that German ships [?] will remain here a month.

PARIS, 2 June. The express [messenger] from Rome arrived here, bringing [news] of the 60 Infante [?] to the Queen, through [?]; this [?]; the elevation of the 3 Cardinals Conti to the Pontificate; but [?] news, that the Pope has [?] the Chamber office [?] [?] to another. The Duke of Antib, having arrived at his estate of Petitbourg, was [?] and it is said he finds himself much pleased; the Abbé Anselme, who was his Preceptor, is also [?]. The Gentlemen of the French Academy held a [?] in the Church of the Godshouse over Monsieur d'Argenson, and it was entrusted to the Marquis of Villars to come forward appropriately; whereupon, that which depends on Justice is in motion to carry out this [?]. A search was made in the street Calonnette for a Pensioner, but he was not at home, [and] Justice appears to have been evaded. The Mr. Rigby, formerly third Ship's Captain in service of this Court, and since one of the Directors of the India Company, has been brought to the Bastille. The actions are finished, and the Bank Commissioner is [?] to vigour [?] the effects of the Controller General and President of [?] the Key into Commission of the great Book [?]; this one has held this post [?], which the Commissioners [?] have noted, and he has not been able to excuse himself, considering his library and visit of Monsieur de la Villietre, where he received authority; his Excellency has not yet been to Versailles. The table, which Monsieur de Bannes is to hold on the King's expense in the Palace of that Ambassador, is praised; the same is of five and twenty [?], and the King's Interpreters in the Eastern languages have [?] to dine there; [which] having been worked, on the grounds of the general Company, alongside the Ambassador's Officers, [we have] been shown [?]; our Reader in helping, has the son received so quickly the tea so quickly, and the other the, at Liège, whereby we were drawn father to himself came, as when each early in the morning what heat he followed he came to himself to run; whereupon they calling father to themselves came, as when each early in the morning what heat he followed: whereupon they answered, that the animals themselves did not come to the Company is a Cult and; and that appears from it, that there are counter-[?], which one cannot [?] to see this case, it is said that the Mr. Ambassador [?] considering his library Doctrine of a Pastry-baker, on the Place Dauphinie; and one adds thereto, that his Excellency has had some proportions made [?] the little maid, who is about the table old, with which he would lead her to Constantinople; which proportions intimate, that he is of good selection; for it is said that [?] several maids, who manifest themselves through their [?] above other diligence, wherefore they are worthy to be brought into the Serrai of the Great Lord. Here a Courier has arrived from Spain, whereupon a certain express [messenger] was sent to London, to give that Court knowledge of his arrival, which [?] [?] according to the Letters from Toulon of the 17th past, there had been these days before in that city a procession held in honour of the Virgin Mary, protector of the city, and of Saint Roch, through whose miracles it is said that the sickness in the said city began to diminish, as also according to those letters, [by] day no more than 10 or 20 people [had] to [die]; at Niçois and [?], however, there were still some sick [?].

PARIS, 3 June. The express [messenger] from Rome arrived here, bringing letters from the Nuncio to his Majesty; also brought [?]; to this Court the notification of the elevation of the Cardinal Conti to the Pontificate; but news, that the Pope has [?] the Chamber office of the said Monsieur [?] still to his [?] [?]; this Lord [?]. One has [?] news, that the Nuncio of Antris, having arrived at his estate Petitsbourg, was [?] overtaken [?]; and that he finds himself much pleased; the Archbishop Anthemus, who was his Preceptor, is also [?]. The Gentlemen of the French Academy held a eulogy [?] in the Church of the Goodshouse over Monsieur d'Argenson; and it was entrusted to the Marshal of Villars to come forward appropriately in the name of the Academy; also [?] and [?] for the correlation Nicollet held in the Church of the Capuchins; the tapestries and hangings are costly and [?], and the display is by [?] the magnificent effect. One of the commanders at the Châtelet, has these days imported a great quantity of Spanish wines and other excellent liqueurs, in the house where the Councillor [?] has; these were to be [?] through a [?] in this [?], for the account of a private [person] at St. Malo, as one believes one knows that they belong to a whole other person. Monsieur Segais, President à Mortier in the Parliament of Bordeaux, married Miss Gainnaint Bouffé; the Notary, who has drawn up the Marriage Contract, is paid with one thousand livres. [?] and it is a [?] [?], as the Commandant of the Castle at Nantes has been archived; but one does not know what [?]. The Chancellor has gone to cancel the law days at his country estate the [?], it is known that the machines of the Messieurs de [?] will be withdrawn for the establishment of [?] Court, so that the conduct of the Directors and subordinates, both of the Bank and the Company, might be examined. One [?] and the Registry must have [?] and especially give; some think that such may well still encounter difficulty: Meanwhile one has [?] a certain private [person] which have been guided by the System, hope through this [?] to be [?]. The council meetings will be until the 20th of this month, in case something [?], nor will that of the third Constitution be separated before that time either. On Wednesday next the Sorbonne meeting of Monsieur de Jancourt is being held; and one expects that disputes will certainly occur among the Doctors; which are there well for the Constitution; as well as about the members, which dwell in this meeting [?], and the later [?]. the said Monsieur d'Argenson has given to his young son; for [?], has proceeded in the Paris [?], but it is not said that it prevents, but that Paris has given there more memorable [?]. The sale of the [?] inversion, on this Ministry, has been forbidden [?]... [?], which one after much good is passed through, itself through its excellencies has [?]. It is his predecessor, [which] began to great wonder. One has the Constitution, according to which Jan Law in the pensions would [?] out of materials to fulfill. One wants that in the whole the [?] [?] will be [?] ... [?] that the whole hundred [?] children the trade to the Financier, which that the whole hundred [?] children the trade to the Financier, which [?] provided that, which the Archdukes wish to have; it [?] dear as [...]

Great Britain

LONDON, 3 June. On the previous Sunday the Prince and Princess of Wales went, with the King, who was accompanied by many great ones, to the chapel of St. James; the Viscount Falmouth carried the sword of state before his Majesty. On the coming Sunday his Majesty is to go into mourning over the deceased Queen of Denmark, and since his Majesty comes to his 62nd year on that same day, all the great [ceremonies] etc. are therefore postponed, [because] this birthday falls on a Sunday, [and is] until the following day put off. It is assured that Parliament will separate shortly, but that the same will be prorogued for several days. It is assured that an address will be presented through Parliament to his Majesty, to provide assistance; [and] it is supposed that in all possible ways [they] will strive to remedy the failing affairs, and [to keep] the public funds in profit and action. Yesterday Monsieur Hungerford presented a Bill to the Lower House, to authorize the Earl of Arran, brother of the Duke of Ormond, to purchase the deceased Duke's goods; on the third [?] Mr. Clayton made a report of the further procedure; thereupon a Committee of the whole House was appointed, [?] above on the last [?], for which [?] the day to receive the same was set on Monday, on which day all the members of [?] the 15th were appointed, on which day all the members of the House [?] [?] will be found, which by the said Committee are [?]. Acts &c. for themselves, and one [?] helps [?].

GREAT BRITAIN, 6 January. The Prince of Wales has [?] the King, [his] nephew [?] 1200 men will, in the season have palliated, has made [?] with Dirck common through the direction of Parliament strong, Master Builder of houses [?] great, and foretold of the Hall. One of the Protestant, 7 [?] haste on Tordormem, and [?] Anna, at cost of the House, since the King's in the Houses underwent, is House on the Duke's which also must possible. The [?] of the Liste, Brokers; and the Commission united with the Credits after that sold Bridge, and Turner is surprised, 22 [?]. said [?] these days (which Lesming well 13 Mill the world difference of [?] own loaded have, [?] again healing done [?] in their unfounded, and all London, where on the Lander, Guide could have, sometimes still could convenient assert; [?] gentlemen Process-Procedure: would it them

ENGLAND

THE NETHERLANDS

DORDRECHT, 7 June. On the 1st of this month have arrived at this City the Ships, the Skipper Sophia William Miller of Newcastle; on the 4th Cornelis Springer, Corn. Bosman, Abraham van Cappel, and likewise Hayden van den Sanden, all of Dunkirk; Ulke Ulsing of Newport, the Providence James Bois of Newcastle, the Free[?] and the Klaas Legate of Bordeaux, and today at Schiedam have [?] of Newcastle, the William Steven Dixburg of Scarborough; in contrast have departed from here, Michiel Lugten toward Dunkirk, the Joseph Wilson toward Newcastle, the Happy Return Anthony Smith toward Norway, and the James and Elisabeth, Joseph Davidson toward Sunderland.

AMSTERDAM, 1 June. The Portuguese Ship N.S. della Guide, from Lisbon [?] mustered, has near Mozambique remained; being from [?] 180 [?] toward Rio Man boarded. At Lisbon has arrived Manuel Taixera Viveytox of Madeira, and Jan Hekker of this City, the Ship of Jan Appelman being there still [?]. At [?] the [?] has through [?] been lost Jan Overjtai, from Genoa toward Lisbon mustered. At La Rochelle has arrived Elias Wyting, Hendr. Milder, Jacob Jacobs Harpe; at Bordeaux Pieter Huyk, Pieter Schuirlianie, Pieter Brandaniis, Corn. Schouten, Michiel Colombeau, and Hike Fokkes, all of here; in Island Brother [?] and 1 half [?] of Doreninjgen at St. Maries-Heydt. Tymen Marck the Young, and others, of here and the Maas; at London Pieter Blom of Hamburg; at Rotterdam Sjoerd van Chareatie; yesterday in Texel Piett. Re-Verle Klopper [?] the Mediterranean, and Jan Hekker of Lisbon; and for the Wai Hend. Cok, H. Bonk, and Adt. Th. Smith of Suriname.

HANOVER, 1 January. According to letters from Saxony, the King of Poland on the 27th preceding in Dresden again returned. It is assured that, notwithstanding the [?] in Sweden [itself] in good posture place, the Peace between the 2 Powers already very advanced should be, and that the provisional [?] the Newstadt, the Fleet itself shall should be able to deliver.

REGENSBURG, 2 June. The Answer of the Roman Catholic [party] to the Protestant Corps, contains in substance the following. That they to the Treaty of the Imperial Deputies, wherein the Religion in the Empire determined is, not subjected are, or can go; but that the 4th Article, confirmed by the Peace of Baden, without any change to make, in its full vigor must remain: and concerning the [?] of the Imperial Deputies to discussion of the Religious Grievances in general, will they their [?] and what they from the Imperial resolution still remain [?].

HANOVER, 1 June. According to letters from Saxony, the King of Poland on the 27th preceding in Dresden again returned. It is assured that, notwithstanding the [?] in Sweden themselves in good posture place, the Peace between the 2 Powers already very advanced should be, and that the provisional [?] at Newstadt, the Fleet itself shall should be able to deliver.

COLOGNE, 6 June. The pious [persons] of our Elector and the Cathedral Chapter have departed toward Arensberg, to [attend] the Diet [?] the [?] dwell.

THE HAGUE, 8 June. The Lord Roufe, Burgomaster [?] the College announces that he on 14 July 1721 shall complete the Obligations in his possession; [numbers] 619 through No. 906 inclusive, requesting that the Holders of the Obligations come with their Goods-papers with the unpaid Interest thereof, and the same on 13 and 14 July 1721 shall be paid, but the aforementioned Obligations concerning the four previous Interest [payments] of the Obligations, marked No. 206, 285, 195, 197, 603 [?] 200 [?] the Holders of the same Obligations as soon as possible come receive the same, as no further Interest runs or shall be paid. [?] The Directors of the Commerce and Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn, notify in the said Company, whereupon five per cent interest was paid, that the same shall vacate [?] since [?] lent two per cent interest, and the [?] manner as then done have begun of [?] Month, and longer not, these [?] this Evening 1654 hours, being Monday, 9 June 1721, shall the Brokers A Pieter and de Bolck, at Amsterdam sell in the new [?] Heybergh, a lot of Takewood on the Cinzel over the Mint. Filip Piek Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Thursday 12 June 1721 Bread, on the Coll [?] Burgwal, a lot of Inland Cloths, Kol-Arm-Ren, Silk, half Silk and Woolen Fabrics, Chints, Nettle-cloths, Cotton Jackets, white and Breads &c. are the [?] these days and on tomorrow in the said dwelling Leyberg of Togeren and Pieter van den Boech Brokers, shall at Amsterdam sell [?] hours, on the Cinzel in the Burg, a lot of [?], a lot of extra fine Cat-Ten-[?] Daniel and B apb of [?] Brokers, shall at Amsterdam sell on Thursday on Dijkstraat, in the Old Burg, a lot of extra fine [?]-Tobacco, lying on the [?] Minerel [?] Cousin Tuning. Elias de Mattos Broker, shall at Amsterdam sell on Thursday 19 June [?] at 2 hours, in the Colveniere [?] in the Doelen-street, a lot of made [?] at 2 hours in the said Doelen to see. Jacob van der Houve, Martinus Juweel, and Corn. Verlinde Brokers, shall at Rotterdam at 6 hours, in the Wine-street, in the Heyberg the Black Letter, a Shipload of [?]-Tobacco, of the Weight 1919 and 1720, and that by Cavelings as [?] named Houses of the Lords Picklart and Follet. Jacob van der Houve, Marinus Juweel and Kornelis Verlinde Brokers, shall on [?] Rotterdam, in the Heyberg the Black Letter, sell a Shiploading fine [?] [?] extra [?] party London Fine-Sweetened-Tobacco, and that by Cavelings, [?] of the Lords Jakob Seneef and Son. The Amsterdam ARGUS, three Parts Complete, is everywhere at the Book-sellers for sale, for two Guilders five Stuivers, bound. NB. [?] never to suffer the Doelen Market of Amsterdam, especially the Wednesday, [?] shall [?]

Course of Exchange

A New Matter, in one of the foremost Distinguished of the British Navigation has been; that he [?] 500 for himself, as Fortis in the Ships money has; that being a particular equipment, joined by the Admiral on Poultsh in the Ships, which in Company were; for the year still above 30,000 pounds sterling light amount; that he at Paupa lain has, and [?] of the [?] Geography has become, and back come glad; not proposed has against the belief at excessive [?] in Actions, wherefore he hopes to shall work [?] in the [?] to the Should of the Unfortunate in the [?] to forgot-den. Since last [?] to the Toll announced 1406 ounces Gold to France, and 30085 Silver for the Company to the East Indies. [?] [?] Ships sail [?] lie, where in the same loaded is. The Captain Hales, commanding the Packet-boat the King George of Gobert of Tynemouth, that he himself [?] voyage of Lisbon, his Ship [?] contrary Wind is directed toward the Island St. Michael, where he found a drifting Island Pumice, about [?] Topp-te while [?] [?] middle and burning, on both sides with a sort of Crevices set being. Friday has the Lord of Beugen [?] Minister of the British States General of the United Netherlands, a Memoir delivered to a Secretary of State; concerning the chief-style of the 3 Scottish Regiments; also has aforementioned extra. Envoyé, out name of his high Principals, the Compliment of Felicitation over the newborn Prince, by the [?] and Principale of Wales laid down, being the same with particular signs of affection, as well toward their High Mightinesses, as the Person of seventh Minister, by both Royal Highnesses answered. The Actions of the Bank are 130. E.I.C. 135. [?]. 132 and 1 half. Bank 1750. Lottery and Bank Annuity 96 and 1 half. E.I. Exchange Rate [?] 6 and 3 quarter. ditto London 6, and [?]. B. 16 and 1 half. The Exchange Rates of the Wisselbank: today on Madrid 88. Cadiz 113 and 1 half quarter. Seville 112 and 1 quarter. Bilbao 88 and 1 half. Lisbon 43 and 3 quarter. Venice 81 and 3 [?] 2 quarter. Livorno 93. Genoa and 3 quarter. Geneva 94. Paris open 42 [?] 41 and 7 [?]. London 13 Sch. 10 8 11. Current Fractions 23 [?]. Danzig 189 [?] gr. and 1 quarter. [?] 169. Elburg 9. Arnheim 12 and 3 quarter.

The 9th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 70.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 11th of June, 1721
Great Britain

LONDON, 6 June. Last Tuesday, the Prince and Princess of Wales went to their country house at Richmond, where it is rumored they took their midday meal, and will shortly retire there to spend the summer season. Knight John Lambert has had the principal furnishings of his estate brought to the country house, as mentioned in our previous issue. On Wednesday, the Lords heard the case between the Duke of Marlborough and Mr. Strode, Master Builder of Blenheim House, and their Lordships decided this great matter with 43 votes against 25 in favor of the latter, whereupon the Duke, beyond the costs and expenses already mentioned, shall be obliged to pay 9,134 pounds sterling. His argument was founded on the fact that this same house was built at the cost of the Commons during the reign of Queen Anne; and that the Lower House had granted a certain sum to complete it according to the aforementioned expenses, which had previously been expended by order of the Duke, and it was understood that he must also pay these back. On this day, the Lord Mayor of this city delivered to the Lower House a list with the names of the contractors; and the Commissioners of the Excise also presented an account of the revenue from customs duties of the [?] past years, ending at Christmas 1720, with an annual breakdown; and also another account of the private revenue from impositions on certain goods arriving from the Indies, likewise for the same three years; and it was ordered that the said accounts be examined and referred to the consideration of the great Committee of the whole House, taking into account the state of public credit. When the House proceeded to a bill to bind the estate and personal goods of Knight George Caswell, the gentlemen Sir [?], and Tunner to guarantee the sum of 250,000 pounds sterling to the South Sea Company, which had been postponed for a fortnight, the House went into Committee to oversee the matter of public credit; and when the names were read of those who had borrowed money from the South Sea Company on their actions and subscriptions (which licensing amounted to some 1,000 [?]), a great debate arose over the repayment of these monies; some wished that full repayment should be made by those who found themselves in a position to do so, and others that the licensees should be completely discharged, whereupon Mr. Lade, a present Director of the Company, showed that this was difficult, and pointed out that Knights Samuel Oughly and Isaac Schard, Mr. Rush, the city printer, Colonel Charles Gamley, Owen, Stroud, and Shaw, together with several other gentlemen, of whom some had borrowed 30 and others 4,000 pounds, had done so; and considering whether full repayment could be obtained from all of these, he could not name anyone who could pay between 600 and 10,000 pounds; however, he was released, and finally the proceedings were adjourned; but when it was brought into question whether the said actions and subscriptions held by the Company and by those who had lent upon them should be discharged, that was not further conceded in this matter, and Knight Oughly's proposition was rejected with 210 votes against 41, and the previous note was understood to remain as before. Besides 180 members of the House of Commons, 12 Dukes and various other Lords, among whom those who have pledged their actions and subscriptions to the Company, it appeared from the evidence that the confederation upon the report of the secret Committee of 25 April, concerning Mackay, Owen, Stroud, and Shaw, would be postponed for a fortnight, after which the House went into great Committee to consider the bill for relieving the losers in the South Sea Company, and to ensure that Aislabie does not leave the kingdom; in which bill great progress was made, and it was agreed on the 25th that certain directors should give up their goods; and thereupon it was accorded with 113 votes against 104 to Knight Lambert Blackwell 10,000 pounds, to Mr. John Bellows 5,000 pounds, to Mr. Charles Joice 5,000 pounds, to Mr. Will. [?] John Bellows 5,000 pounds, and to Knight John Blunt, whom one held to 1,500 [?] from the ruined projects, was granted only 1,000 pounds, and one of the members said that if anything more remained to be paid, he would vote to give him a shilling. Afterwards, the Lords postponed the consideration of public credit, which was scheduled for this day, until Thursday three days hence, and adjourned until that day. The Gentlemen Commissioners and [?], and the handling of East Indian affairs to proceed; and it was ordered that a certain Luydon, the clerk in the office of the South Sea Company, shall be placed in prison forthwith, because Mr. Watts, another clerk, who has been examined this day by the secret Committee, had the audacity to reveal what should have remained hidden. Thereupon the consideration of the Bank of public credit was resumed; and concerning the lending of 11 millions on the actions and subscriptions, there was again a great debate; in which Burry with good success again [?] the gentlemen Rob. Pim[?] has become chief Register of all those employed in commerce in Great Britain. Alexander Pendaves Esq. has been elected as a member of the Lower House for Launceston in Cornwall, in place of the deceased Baronet Edw. [?]

Several members of the Lower House, because of their sorry condition and that of their families, had to sell their house according to the requirement of their actions, and all would have wished; their excessive want goes so far that they make themselves out for a poor person, and scrape and bow before others; and they conduct themselves so miserably, as if they were very ready, so that one could not [?] if they were inclined, any Highland[?] would undertake; the whole world thinks it wonderful and astonishing that they dare appear in public with any composure at all, given the contempt which they must continually endure; yes, if the law had not prescribed a penalty for murder, it would have been impossible that they should escape from the hands of those people, whose terrible conduct has been their ruin. According to letters from Edinburgh, the general assembly of the Scottish Church which was to be held in the month of the last past has been prorogued to 12 May in the coming year, on account of an indisposition with which the Earl of Rothes, the King's Commissioner, was struck. Yesterday evening the Guard appeared in new clothing to do honor to the King's birthday. Yesterday evening, Lords Formore and Polwarth arrived here, the first from his government of [?], and the other from Scotland.

Wednesday's Exchange Report

Germany & Neighboring Realms

HANOVER, 6 June. There is a general rumor here that peace between the Czar and the King of Sweden is being greatly advanced, and that the Hessian Castle troops, which have already begun their march toward Swedish Pomerania, have been ordered to halt for the time being, and some claim that they will withdraw to their own land. The Brunswick troops, which were also preparing to march thither from this Electorate, remain still in their quarters. The day before yesterday, the High Countess of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, daughter of the Landgrave of Hesse-Cassel, passed through this city toward Bützow in Mecklenburg. The false coiners who were imprisoned here will receive their sentence in the coming week; their head will be cut off and their bodies afterwards burned.

France

PARIS, 6 June. The King, who has celebrated Whitsunday with great devotion, made a journey on the 2nd of this month to the castle of Meudon. His Majesty has established a Board of Health in this city; it has already met several times for this purpose, and at its head as President is the Sieur Amelot, Councillor of State, who previously served as Ambassador to Spain against this Crown. The Sieur d'Argenson, the youngest son, took the oath last Sunday into the hands of the King as Chancellor of [?], though for that reason it was not completed, because his older brother and sister came in opposition and claimed 100,000 livres. Through the death of the Abbé de Mornay, who was nominated to the Archbishopric of Besançon, the Abbey of Orcas has come open, which is worth 30,000 livres per year. Orders have been sent to the Intendants to cease with the establishment of real Tailles [a tax], though those already instituted shall be collected. The Marquis de Bonnal d'Autin, Lieutenant General of the King, departed from here on the 3rd of this month toward the district of Montauban to command the Corps of Troops which is gathering there and will amount to approximately 12 or 15,000 men, under the command of the Duke of Berwick. The Turkish Ambassador very frequently takes the carriage to go to the Observatory; last Tuesday his Turkish Highness remained there from five o'clock in the evening until ten o'clock to view the planets. Yesterday he visited the castle of Bercy, but finding himself somewhat indisposed, returned immediately to his lodgings. Yesterday the aforementioned Ambassador played at the Marquis de Baron's, where His Excellency was splendidly entertained. The Duke du Maine will come to live in the Arsenal. It is said that the Cardinal de Mailly has requested lettres de cachet against various clergy at Reims, who are placed on the first list of the province, but that the Court has refused them to him. Although letters from Rome report that the new Pope [Editor's note: this refers to Pope Innocent XIII, elected in 1721] was not inclined to come into the accommodation of the bishops of France, or at least to hold the Bull of his predecessor, and that His Holiness the General of the Jesuits visited him and said that he recommended himself to his consideration as well as those of his Society, but that he also requested him not to meddle with ecclesiastical affairs, saying that the Pope of the Appellants is not so strict; nevertheless it appears to have been hastened, inasmuch as to 5 Doctors of the Sorbonne, who are known as defenders of the Four Articles, each one a lettre de cachet has been sent to expel them.

AMSTERDAM, 23 June.

It has been a great loss to the city that we had to do without [a publication] on Monday, and this is hardly surprising, given that Mr. de Stotijn [?] and firmly on his Duties as Teacher, how [?] Walfenaar, Co Sanen, requires an Explanation of the Dispositions, which one greatly wishes to read from the Holy Scripture in the municipal Language. Mr. [?] has done such that the House, the Apostle is du 80 k.n, and it is solely for the sake of the Lord [?] that he will abstain from the presence of the Community for twenty days onward. The Abbey of Antin finds itself recently removed from a [?]. Gillet tomorrow the Abbey at Lyons has been vacated through its death, the Benefices have fallen open, among others the Priory of St. Mary des Champs, which [amounts to] 40,000 Livres. It is reported that the same will be given to the Abbot St. Albin, who is Coadjutor thereof. It is said that the Count de Morville will shortly go to court. The Students of the Jolyten performed a Comedy this day for His Majesty; it is said that Monsieur Antin has left the Opera, but will go under the King's Auspices. [?] 23 and 26 June. Madame de Orleans, who was seen here day before yesterday, has come to port, but she will depart tomorrow to go to the Court. The day after tomorrow the Tuscan Ambassador will go to Meudon to hold [meetings], and will go to Versailles the following day to pay his respects. For some days past, the former Duke of Württemberg has been frequently seen walking in the Tuileries here. The Duke de la Tiernouille, together with some Students of the University of Clermont, [?] the King and the Lodgekeepers of the Lord d'Argenson have had the quarrel of [?] put down, and give as the reason of the Excommunication that was involved therein. Yet some hope may be given for other arrangements of the Prince, such as the former one, which has been canceled. Since the Knight des Gravelles has taken [?], one begins to notice a small improvement, whereby he is recovering. According to the latest letters from Provence, everything at Marseilles is improving day by day: it is reported that the Deaths were about 3,000 Persons: the Capuchin Gabriel, of whom it is written that he is a man of understanding and piety, is said to have performed Wonders there: and since the Count continues his previous duties in Provence, one is not without hope that the Sickness will disappear through the Help [of Heaven]. The Plague had in the Port Valley, from a letter from Marseilles, made 200 great [losses], so that scarcely any Person has remained alive. For some days past, a Tract in 12 [pages] has been seen here, from the Brief M[?], Doctor of Medicine at Geneva, treating of the general corruption which reigns among Christians, and especially in Switzerland, [caused] by Medicines, Debauchery, Profanity, and the unbridled Pride with which these evils are accompanied; the Author stands [firmly] that we ought to seek the Help of the Almighty against them: and one finds that this Tract is of such great use especially in the present time, that it is universally appreciated.

ARRAS, 7 June. For some days past here, both among the Roman Catholics as well as among other righteous People, great rejoicing has been experienced, because one saw the matter extended, allowing the Holy Scripture to be read. Some days ago, 4 Countrymen, charged with Heresy, were condemned here to Banishment and Confiscation of their Goods; when the Sentence, which was given by the Council of Artois, was carried out, both the principal [offenders] were posted; [?] was executed and at Mazideu with [?], on [?] Path, because he could go no further;

AMSTERDAM, and Sta. Cruz by patience [?], since [?] two Stuyreai of the Inhabitants in the age Leo yen and other was from the Kraa Houw Sulanna great, so few [?], means [?] Community Schd rie ingrandend bays of Vilh Hope no [?] ship Go tender being to [?] have set [sail]. The [?], toi di insede [?] landsyding To Bilbao [?] London, Homae Agg &c. h[?] worked even so a [?] of here, and [?] friend 20 Schezders, 200 from the Thomas hei Tirefel, RozVistion [?] yet the Ship St. Clara Cap Gevelier has [?] come, and the General CA that is, m. Haitano with Delft; for the Capen den [?] these: Jaan [?] on its [?] Briefs Sapandene De Coun

[Marinus Groesinck] hereby notifies, as General Receiver of the Excise, that he will proceed on the 12 July 1721 with the redemption of the Bonds held by him of 6 years [maturity]. He therefore requests that the Holders of these Bonds bring their Principal Sums with the unpaid Interest thereof, and [?] on the mentioned 12 and 14 July 1721, shall be redeemed, whereupon the aforesaid Bonds and the mortised Interest on the Bonds, numbered No. 206, 383, 590, 797, [?] and the Holders of the same Bonds as soon as possible, indeed the sooner the better, shall bring the same, as no further Interest will run thereafter.

Since a great quantity of Ordinances, issued by Her High Mightinesses for the benefit of His Princely Highness the Duke of Wittemberg and his [?] for the issuance, or [?] any thereof to bind, t[?] to be given to the [?] of Ha[?], Minister of high common Lord Duke, and [?] be, of themselves concerning this matter to address to the high common Lord Duke H[?], [?] be, of themselves concerning this matter to address to high common Lord Duke, [?] to do, to the end that through the diligence of the [?] itself, t[?] no [?] may suffer.

Since on the 17 September 1720 within the City Leyden came to die Wynsuere Bigisus, son of the late Gerrit van Hatdelt and Claarje van Gissell, in her [?], it is said that the [?], 100 of the Father's and Mother's side intestate, [?] is his, [?] therefore [?] of the Prisoner [?] in [?], and [?] to address to Johannes Ovendiia and Joh [?] Naelschuapp, who [?] there, the aforesaid [?] were capitalized, of common [?] and God [?] her [?], there [?] Scrivener Canon [?] shall dispose, as a [?] per Cent, [?] be in its third [?] in all [?] 100 Coallicjse of common Company shall be [?] there, on the 28 [?] [?] s on such [?] the Adm of the Gemeenten fell due to the [?] of the Company.

Philip Siceu Broker will sell at Amsterdam on Tuesday the 17 July [?] in all that such a [?] good [?]; as well as old Cracked Porcelains and much Good [?] Glass; and [?], some fine Paintings &c., all [?] the Lodging of Dusft, Jan van Baaden, and Jan Wittig Brokers, shall [?] on [?] days at 2 o'clock, in the New [?] Lodging, on the Haarlemmerdijk, to [?] [?] [?] [?] in [?].

[Some] Copies [?] by J. vander Klos are still to be obtained of some few Exemplars: Observations on certain Passages of the Holy Scripture, in particular the Epistles of Paul [?] the Gospel according to Monday, in Quarto.

PETERSBOURG, 19 May. We trust, further still from the Sea [?] Westlof [?] itself [?] [?] [?], regarding [?] morning [?] works [?] great [?] [?] in itself [?], is where Newness [?]. becomes nothing other, than that

ROME, 24 May. Such [?] to the [?] so His Holiness with [?], and on this joyous [?] to the Crown, [?], was [?] began yesterday early morning, in this Processional [?] which the [?] not yet [?] Cardinal Imperiali, Cardinal [?] by [?], the [?] [?], and not yet [?] [?], Faolucci, and Barbarim [?] of the Crown [?] are mentioned [?] Cardinals [?] well. The [?] Adbaptaai le Adger, or Theon, with which [?] [?], and the 25, as we have said, [?] as the Els [?], and the Duke of Gran have the Crown[?] [?] ceremony was [?] Don Car [?] third [?] of the [?] which [?] [?] of the [?] [?] and his Wife [?] [?], Koen [?], even [?] exit. Ambassador of the King [?], but nothing has [?] him on [?] 3. the [?] as [?], and his place was in the Chapel:

Course of Exchange

The Exchange rates are: Amsterdam 34.5.7; sight exchange 34.4. Rotterdam 34.7.9. Antwerp 33.4. Hamburg 34.4.5. Paris 24 and a half. Bordeaux 24 and a half. Madrid 47. Cadiz 4 and a quarter to a half. Bilbao 46 and a half. Livorno 52. Genoa 54 and three-quarters. Venice 47. Lisbon 5.4 and three-eighths to a half. Porto 5.4 and a half to three-quarters. Dublin 10. The shares of the Bank are at 110; of the East India Company 144; of the South Sea [Company] 129 and a half; the Million Bank 120; Lottery and Bank Annuities 96 and a half; ditto at [?] C. 51; Africa Company 53; the Exchange Insurance 6 and a half; ditto London 5 and a quarter; and J.B. 16 and a quarter.

The 11th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 13th of June, 1721
Russia

PETERSBURG, 12 May. The Fleet has indeed departed from here toward Kronstadt, but it cannot proceed from there [on to the next destination] on account of the ice. The [?], and the [?] are obstructed by ice. Seven of the [?] Ministers, via the Dutch Resident, have [?], including the Grand Chancellor Golovkin, who has not been able to proceed further from his [?]. The matter stands to proceed. Mr. Okkerman has also [indicated] nothing other than that he will be engaged in the [trade] matter.

Italy

ROME, 24 May. On Sunday morning, the Pope was conveyed in a papal chair to the Gallery of the Height, where His Holiness, seated upon the Throne with the Bishops, received the Triumphal or threefold Crown, which was placed upon his head; this ceremony began in the morning at eight o'clock and ended with [?] hours: in this Function all the Cardinals participated, even those who have not yet [?], with the exception of [?] Cardinals Pamphilj, Schönborn, and Altieri, who were prevented by gout, [and] the second was obliged to keep to his bed [?] is the cause of the Process which has been formed and yet remains undecided; but [?] Cardinals Pignatelli, Pinelli, and Cabannis saw this great and solemn Function of the Coronation, though they were accommodated [otherwise], and when the common Cardinals departed, the solemnity was concluded. Don Carlo Albani performed in this Function as Ring [?] of the Throne, with which dignity the Pope was honored, and the [?] of the Cardinals [?] from the deceased Pope Clement XI, who in general sits in the College. The Counsel Colloiuon, and the Coronation [?], which also Prince of the Throne ceremony was granted to Don Carlo Albani; to this end he should bear before the seat of the Chapel, which [?] to the worthy [?] Princess, [?] and his [?], which were also present, was [?] on the second day of the month. The great [?], [?], Ambassador of the [Church?], had arranged something similar for himself, but he received word by order of the Pope that he is to [go?] to Monte Cassino, and to remain there during this Summer, in order to return here again at the end of November; and the [?] will be handled by H. Count [Bridge?] of the [?]. Word has come from Ferrara that Colonel Medici, Governor of the papal Arms in that City, has died; to which [office] [?] is he who has long been in the aforementioned Benedictine Order, now offers himself on this occasion, and the Cardinal of Rohan does all that is possible to preserve it for his [colleagues?].

His H. Baccari, new Vice-Regent, has arrived here and has taken full charge of that Office. The Cardinal Orsini is [continues...]

GENOA, 24 May. Three English warships have arrived in this harbor, and the Commander of this Squadron has sent his Lieutenant, accompanied by the Secretary of the Great Britain Resident in this City, to the Secretary of State, against whom the Mayor and Secretary stood [?], [having received] order that the [?] [heirs?] of the Family Giustiniani should pay at a certain determined time all that this Family owes in England; whereupon our Secretary of State replied that this was a matter for negotiation with London; but the British Secretary replied that there was a means to obtain payment: from which incident the Secretary of State has given notice to the Government, and all the Colleges stand ready to assemble to deliberate thereon.

BOLOGNA, 24 May. There is a rumor here that the Pope will create [new] Cardinals in the strong [?] Consistory, namely His H. Albani and His H. Count [?] Bishop of Terracina.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 13 May. It is said that the King, although he has not yet been well recovered, being in reasonably good health, has permitted His Majesty's Ministers and their servants freedom to wait upon him in his Bedchamber. On the preceding Friday, 9 May, the Count [?] (being very satisfied with his Commission) was asked for couples [?] and has sent a Courier to his Court. Word has come from Nymeguen that our Plenipotentiaries have hastily worked out [?] against those of his Czarish Majesty's Plenipotentiaries Bruce. The same [news?] is sought every hour on our roadstead; a ship of the aforementioned Fleet, [?] in May, in which 3 ships of the line were located, has been wrecked near Karskrona.

Germany & the Bordering Realms

REGENSBURG, 3 June. Although the Imperial Diet has not been convoked concerning the [?], the Protestant Ministers have held a private meeting therein, where their written Remonstrance against the [?] was read. The [?] [?] however [?] [continues with significant transcription difficulties]

REGENSBURG, 5 June. Although the Imperial Diet has not been convoked concerning the [?], the Protestant Ministers have held a private assembly here, where their written Representations, for the last time, were read and it was resolved [to send them] with the Appendices to [?] by the Agent of Saxony to be sent to the Emperor; but the [?] wish that the Roman Ministers, having knowledge hereof, [?] [continues...]

France

PARIS, 6 June. On the preceding Sunday the Duke Regent was at St. Cloud, and the following day at Chelles. The Duchess-Dowager of Orleans has not been with her Son on Wednesday as she is accustomed, but will do so the next morning. Miss de Catinat, Daughter of Mr. de Catinat, Councillor in Parliament, the Marquis [?] of that name, has [entered?] the cloister of the [Sisters], in the City of [?], where she has taken her vows as a nun. On the aforesaid Monday to the Turkish Ambassador [?] to dine at Meudon, and having spent the night there, [he] betook himself the following day to Versailles, where the fine reception of him was awaited, and where all preparations are being made to receive this Minister well. Word had been given that the King would also go there at that time, but this has been postponed. The Globe, which His Majesty [?] the day before yesterday through the Vicar of St. [?], was found [?] and fine, so that [they have] ordered a new one to be made, which will be larger, because this one is too small in the King's Cabinet. [?] the Count d'Aiguillon, regarding the Office of Chancellor Seal-Bearer of the Royal and Military Order, at St. Louis, [which] His Majesty has given him, the [?] of [comfort?]...

LEFT COLUMN

[The text in the left column is severely corrupted and illegible in the transcription provided, with numerous transcription errors, broken words, and unclear passages. A reliable translation cannot be produced from this garbled source material.]

---

THE NETHERLANDS

AMSTERDAM, 11 June. On 24 April at St. Eustatius the fine ship Albertus Hatings arrived; at Martinique the St. Jan Baptista, Captain Aarmours of Rochelle; at Bayonne the King David, Willem de Keunieg, and the Hooker Willemina, Will. Hendt, from Zeeland; at St. Sebastian Captain Darow, [and] from this City to Bayonne [arrived vessels]; at St. Marien the brothers Gerrit Stuyk [?]

Since a great quantity of cannons have been cast by order of His Serene Highness the Duke of Württemberg and his associates, and must be returned to him, or should anyone have any of them in their possession, they are requested to report them to the Lord of Pfau, Minister of the aforementioned Lord Duke, under penalty of law, and to address themselves to the aforementioned Gentlemen Councilors in order to provide the necessary information, so that through the delay in returning the cast cannons themselves no damage may result.

Whereas Andreas Zeetgess, Cooper in the Brewery of the Two Climbing Lions at R[otterdam], has broken out to steal a coffee-colored coat and cambric shirt of [?], and two pairs of [?] of his wife, together with her jewels, gold, silver, bonds and [?]; having contacted C. J., named Reelsiya, and gone away with other things in her bed [?], from which she could tell that he had come to her in some manner [?]; he being in drink, located under the Paderborn [?], still has a woman [?] whom whoever can deliver him into the hands of Justice [should do so], and address themselves to the aforementioned Brewery.

Thus on 17 September 1720 within the City of Leiden there came to die the fifteenth [?] heir of the late Gerrit van Hadsfelt and Klaaije van Geffel, in their will made [?], so that it is believed 200 from the Father's and Mother's sides [?], until the proper time, their further [?] on [?] October 1721, with suitable [?] have to be provided, and address themselves to Johannes O'cdum and Jaa l'A, executors by name, who [?], the aforementioned time being set [?] of the aforementioned will (under the keeping of sufficient security) shall dispose, [?] as behalf of Johannes Stasis, Jan Lemmers and Hendrik Linduet brokers, shall at Amsterdam at 6 o'clock, on the [Eilogel?] in the Burg, one lot 200 [?] as white Muscovite [?] and other [?].

Jacob vander Hoove, Martinus Juswell, and Corn. Verlinde brokers, shall at [?] at 6 o'clock, in the Wine Street, at the inn the Black Lion, sell a ship's cargo of heavy tobacco, from the ship the Gerrit [?] 1719 and 1720, and that by portions [?] from the houses of the Gentlemen Fickart and Folter.

Jacob vander Hoove, Martinus Juswell and Kornelis Verlinde brokers, shall at Rotterdam, at the inn the Black Lion, sell a ship's cargo [?] an extra fine lot of London sweet tobacco, and that by portions, [?] of the Gentlemen Jakob Senler and Son.

Gommaris van Duyft, Jan van Raalen, and Jan Wirtig brokers, shall at Amsterdam at 7 o'clock, at the New Lord's Lodging, on the Haarlem dike, [?] one [?] one lot [?].

Mic Jan vander Streng and Carck [?] brokers, shall on Monday the 23 June [?] at [?] sell an excellent cabinet of French sculptures, [?], and vases, as well as white marble statues and vases, very skillfully executed, all of which are from renowned masters; furthermore some fine [?] of statuary marble [?]; all of which may be seen several days before the sale, and [further information will be provided].

Cornelis de Roos and Jan vander Streng brokers, offer for sale or [?] located at his [?] on the High Road, being thereon a fine country house [?], furnished with fine [?], [?], orchards, gardens, [?], large about 4 morgens, and the land also 4 morgens.

Jan vander Streng and Dirk Nagel brokers, offer for [?] furnished with beautiful orchards and the finest fruit trees, furthermore [?], fine [?], mostly furnished with new [?] [?] as well as stabling for [?] morgens, located on the Ban of Velzen [?], called [?].

[and further property listings...]

RIGHT COLUMN

ENGLAND

LONDON, 12 May. The Gentlemen have so far maintained a meeting of no importance. Against the opposition at Cartagena one has sufficiently many 300 men; even with other [?] and robbers with 20 [?]. It is a wonder that [?] and [?] with the [?] regarding some concern [?] particulars in provinces see, some great ships [?].

CADIX, 20 May. These new captured [?] and ridiculous. [?] continue, yet we hope again free [?].

MADRID, 27 May. The [?], but one suspects [?] in this city, [?] is of his in opposition through [?]. At [?] at Aranjuez the Marquis [?] is [?], is the Count Gonzalis Zegri de Salazzy.

LIVORNO, 23 May. [?] the [?] against [?], and for these reasons [?] of riding [?]. [?] Barques, which [?] a year [?], shall be admitted by French bark [?] of the [?], also of this reason, that [?] [?] still 11 others in sight [?] on board had. From [?], we have [?] Armours in 2 days [?].

FRANCE

PARIS, 9 June. When [?] were [?], they received [?] in the Society, in order there [?] of the Church [?] Rector, and the Syndics [?] the government of the [?] Church [matters], [?] previously [?] [?] of the sealed [?] of the Archbishop [?] not the [?] Doctoral shall [?] not the [?], Captains all [?] Doctors, and the last Parish of St. Marguerite; after [?] of the Sorbonne [?] decided, and the [?] [?] not yet [?] the [?] through the press to a further [?]; [?] has also [?]; his concealment, and he asks naked [?] the Jesuits Congregation appointed to [?] [?], because all [?] of the Evangelical their appearance, [?] of which [?] we yesterday [?] examine the bound [?] given in [?] of the Bishop of [?]. Notices & Advertisements

On Monday the 17 June 1721, there shall be sold at Dordrecht by the auctioneer Adria Loopen a fine household inventory of everything that belongs to a household, fine [?], gold, silver, and many jewels, all with extra large stones, [?] with [?], and extra large stones, a great quantity of fine paintings by Old Masters, B. Gaal, [?], and other renowned painters; the jewels and paintings may be viewed on the 11th of the same month, in the morning, before the auction.

On Monday the 23 June 1721 and following days, there shall be sold at Dordrecht in the [?] fine and unconditioned libraries, consisting of [?] very complete, and Miscellanea [?] left by the late Honorable Gentlemen Johannes Groen and Pet den Oudcnbolch; of which the catalogs may be obtained at Amsterdam with [book dealers] Baertclen van Keilei, Delft Beman, Rotterdam J. D. Beman and the Widow N. Bos, Leyden vanWyk, Utrecht Wyzens, [?] Parton, Breda Evermans, Bergen op Zoom [?], [and other cities].

On Wednesday the 2 July 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning precisely, there shall be sold at Leyden [?] the Widow Lucas de Léncer, [?] and to the highest bidder [?] in [?] [?] of various [?] [?] together with many weapons [?]; the catalogs of which may be obtained at Amsterdam [?], Rotterdam Beman, Dordrecht van Braam, and Leyden Vermey.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 13th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 16th of June, 1721
Barbary

ALGIERS, 18 May. The twelve corsair ships that have been at sea from here have thus far brought in no other prizes than a Genoese bark with wool from Alicante bound for Genoa, and a Spanish vessel sailing from Malaga to Cadiz, both robbed by corsairs. In the second action, they also captured at Cartagena the corsair ship the Gilded Sun, with 30 guns and more than 300 men; indeed, a rumor circulates here that near Sardinia another bark and sloop have been found, and the crew made into slaves. An English bark has arrived here from the North. Work is underway on a ketch at the Mouille, as it appears, to address certain concerns held here. Since the growing plague in Provence is a matter of concern, no French barks are being admitted to this bay. Shortly, 2 family barks with some Persian slaves of departed nations will depart under sail for Livorno.

Spain

CADIZ, 20 May. Since the owners receive daily heavy shipments of gold and silver on deposit, money circulates here readily. It is uncertain whether the galleons, in which loading continues, will be fully loaded and able to return before the end of this month.

MADRID, 27 May. The Royal Family is still at Aranjuez, though it is expected that the entire Court will return to this city by the 6th or 10th of July. The Prince of Asturias has been indisposed for twenty-nine days. The Great Britain Ambassador Stanhope, upon receiving an expedition from his Court, has concluded his business; the Marquis de Lejda still remains here. The Marquis de Majorca, Councilor of State and Chamberlain, has died during a hunting expedition, as has Don Gonzales Zegri de Salazar, Commander of the Coast of Granada.

Italy

LIVORNO, 21 May. Of the aforementioned French bark which, because it was reported at Marseille to be infected, one still awaits news, and for this reason departed from here; since the order has come that no ships from barks which departed from Marseille in January are to be admitted, the last French bark from Smyrna, which had been granted permission and sailed to Mandello, has also been forced to leave this anchorage. From Turkey, news has arrived that 7 Turkish ships of Gedde arrived at Snes, and that 11 more under the same flag were said to be en route, which carried a large cargo of coffee on board. From Algiers, 2 French barks have arrived here, namely Captain Stefano Audibert in 6 days, and Guillet [?] Atincbis in 8 days; in the return voyage, the Thomas, Will. Muut sailed toward Cadiz and Lisbon, and the Marthe, Rich. Lumly toward London.

Monday'S Courant

FRANCE

PARIS, 9 June. When the Doctors of the Sorbonne were asked not to remain in their assembly regarding ecclesiastical letters without first sending the same to the Chancellor and having communicated it through the Synodics; and that they must make known to Parliament those matters concerning the Rights of the Crown and the Freedoms of the Gallican Church; the [?] of said letters affected Mr. Quinault, Librarian of the [Four Nations], whereby he is forbidden in future [to attend discussions]; several Doctors and others appearing against the Lords des Moulins, Curé of St. Jacques du Haut Pas, Boucher, and Eu, all three Doctors, and the last against Mr. Pinochoteavx, Vicar of the Parish of St. Marguerite; whom he, with this prohibition, no longer counts among the assembly of the Sorbonne; it came to be believed there that the Assembly in its entirety was dissolved. The common people say the Pope is austere, but the impartial well know how to draw a godly [?] between him and the Pope; however, it appears that he, according to his birth, is of a noble spirit; his inclinations agree much with the distribution, and he does not allow himself to be blown about by the Italians in such political matters; he has appointed a particular Congregation to examine this, and the work of the Congregation is much noted; it is said that this [?] is somewhat against their undertakings.

The 300 thieves which are in the prisons are to be conveyed to the Islands, and the Archers will have orders to add to them such criminals as they find in the prisons on their route. News has been received that a Spanish warship of 60 guns has caught two rovers near Corunna, and that same ship has retaken two Dutch vessels. According to the latest letters from Marseille, all was well in that city, and there had been many sick there in recent days. At Aix the sickness began to diminish, but at Toulon it still looked gloomy.

Great Britain

LONDON, 10 June. Saturday, Mr. Lechmere complained in the House concerning a certain Weekly Journal that was printed, pointing out that the author, under the guise of defending the nation in the matter of the heretical branches of the Royal Family, had committed one of the most abominable acts against the count and the royal family, which ought to be pursued; upon this complaint, the entire contents of this paper were read, and such dangerous insinuations to sedition were found therein that, as well those of the party of the Tories as the Whigs thundered that the said printer Mitt be brought to the prison of Newgate; whereupon the House with shouts of approbation ordered an Address to the King to be presented, to beseech His Majesty that his Commons were ready to defend his peace and government with great and blood, and to assure the King all diligence in prosecuting sedition that he might in future be fully secured. Subsequently, a Committee was appointed to investigate the Authors and Printers of all such false, malicious, scandalous, and traitorous libels; this affair has also caused that the reading of the books of the second Directors of the South Sea Company, which was planned for that day, was postponed to Thursday; whereupon the House adjourned until Wednesday. That same day the Session ended in the Old Bailey, with eight malefactors receiving sentence of death, one to have his hand burned, 27 to the Colonies, one to be whipped, and one to be set in the Pillory. Sunday, being the King's Birthday, the King, with the Prince and the Princess of Wales, betook himself to the Chapel of St. James in the Park of Bolton through the Sword of State, and the Bishop of Gloucester, Lord Almoner, preached before His Majesty; in that sermon the King received compliments of congratulation from Her Royal Highness, as well as from all the Nobles of the Court and the Foreign Ministers; but the great festivities were curtailed by the most pressing ministers, as when the Court was magnificent and very lively, and in the evening everything was concluded with a Ball.

---

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 14 June. On this date, the following have arrived here: the sloop Chaudos, master Tob. Jerman, from London on the 11th; the Diligente, master Robbin van Wenningpoole, consigned to Jan van Rotendaal; and Gerr. Otbes at Droitwich; the T. and Mary, John Scarpt; the Wirwig, Corn. Butler; the Elizabeth and Mary, Matthew Speck; and the Forvidence, David Caftap, all from Sunderland. In the opposite direction, the following have departed from this city on the 10th: the Matthew [?] to England, master Corn. Ordreod; on the 11th, the O'Goodwill, John Booth, consigned to the Hedwig, Ralph Man, both bound for Newcastle; and likewise the Magnier, Edward Robinson, and the John and Farrachio [?].

AMSTERDAM, 14 June. At Bysande have arrived: Kapt. Louis de la Fourete, Pierre Breyn, Pieter Bakker, [?] Hardin, at Larne, the Joseph Furnele, all from here; the Kadir John Houtloe, and at Bordeaux Jacob Kunn, both from Hamburg; at Ulburg, Picter de Wilde and Jooch Dirkie Kaft; at Danzig, Maarten de Joon; at Bordeaux, the Vergulde Valk, Willem Cord, from St. Maarten; at London, the Providendale, Alx. Coot from Aberdeen; at Cadiz, the [?], Jac. Janfles Boor from Rotterdam. From Danzig (where Jac. Janfles Tiches, Corn. Dirkke, Corn. Albert Dukee, and others were together remaining) has departed Jan Meercfke Bakker for this city; from London, the Roos, Robb. Burwood, bound for Grenoble; and the ship [?] has been dispatched to Hamburg; and recently a ship arrived here with [?]. Skipper Doeme Kiemee, from Limerick to Aardenburg; in Tiel has recently arrived Jan Gilliaie Klee from Duisburg. According to letters from Tynemouth, there has been recently captured the Dutch East India Company ship Melerom, whose master was Captain [?] Graef (a vessel of the Lord of Sommelsdijk), with [?] of the gelees fells, and the great Mars included therein. According to letters from Carton and dispatches from the Chamber, the ship named S. Maarten, from Amsterdam, recently came on the River; it is said that there the College of Fidelity [?] there; the medium ship and cargo on a certain place must hold Quarantine [?]; however, the Merchants and other good traders have not allowed this, as they knew it from their Embassy beforehand.

---

On 17 September 1720, near the city of Leiden, has come to be born, [?] of the Uiteriaets were Geent of Haledir and Klaaiste of Tergel, in [?] made, that the [?] year, 200 of the Father's rude Mother's side [?]; her for the first October 1721, with all due propriety and her to [?] effects to Johannes Ordann and Jan [?] aforementioned inheritance, which [?], the beforehand, time dispatched were, of the said Valk of Rooda; [further administrative and property transfer details follow, partially obscured]

---

Stockholm, 4 June

Yesterday various sessions in Conference were held between the Russian entourage and the gentlemen of the Swedish Crown, and it is that General Hamilton has no feelings toward this, 100 [?] mentioned Conference; came to make, notwithstanding the great opposition and dispute, which for some time among those War Fleet has been sitting.

---

Germany

HANOVER, 13 June. Also the Count Gallitzski comes to make, and spoils his situation with [?] the great Montenier of our youngest letters from Danzig of that date, that in a Council of War the Fleet has been sitting, yesterday three more made [?] have been launched to water, as well as there two against one this as four also for the Great-Duke, and this likeness for long for two years, where altogether all principal Lords and Officers of the Great-British Envoys [?] have been, having, so that there great trouble was required to become. It is said moreover that there behind these works by end of June so many matters must be concluded as behind these works before the said War-fleet of 13 June seemed to him also for his better use and Career at sea, underlying were some Letters of Freedom on this whole Month.

COLOGNE, 15 June. The Elector, who yesterday the great Frontier examined, say the eye for his Pleasure-house; [further details partially obscured].

FRANKFURT, 11 June. By the Elector Tier is of Territory up to toward the Emder-Bad escaped, where the Elector of the Falks this latter toward the water also conveyed forth.

RYDEN, 13 June. The [?], who yesterday the great [?] of the [?] of the Sacraments Day, [?] has attended, that a ceremony [?] toward [?], utmost Brothers and one there one-gasped to see, [?]. One waits that the Italian Cavalries Baat [?]. Zeal-[?] to [?].

[Further mixed entries regarding various appointments and estate matters, with dates in January-July 1721]

---

England

ENGLAND, FRANCE &c.

GREAT-BRITAIN

LONDON, 11 June.

On the King's Holiness day, namely the Direct day, where the church has bounded [?] that underneath cedies [?] the seeking, on set her all the [?] of the Knights. There and presents [?]

IN THE COPPER, on the Presedestrat

16 June 1721. With Privilege.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday, 18 January 1721, in the Chamber, between the Schepens and The Hague, [?] the Animals previously: all [?] to see, and further by Henrick Alles [?], will sell in Amsterdam on Thursday, 19 January [?] and on Wednesday, 18 January and following days, will meet at Dordrecht in the [...] old library, Lord Johannes Groes, and [?] per [?]; catalogues to be obtained [?] at Amsterdam [?]. At Rotterdam [?]. Various locations and booksellers listed, with announcements of auctions and sales of books, provisions, and merchandise throughout the Netherlands.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 16th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 18th of June, 1721
Stockholm

Stockholm, 7 June. Admirals Spar and Norris have arrived in this city and have held several conferences with His Excellency. News has been received from Gefde that 3 to 4 thousand galleys had arrived in that vicinity, which General Hamilton, with a corps of approximately 8,000 men, showed readiness to oppose, in order to prevent the landings, since the Russians, [?] the aforementioned General, after some movement, with his [?] and then some miles further retreated; It is reported that in a council of war it would be proper to send the combined fleets to the coast of Åland.

Germany & the German Realms

Hanover, 13 June. According to letters from Brunswick, Count Gallofski, Ambassador and Plenipotentiary of the Count of Great Britain, had gone to Schweinfurth; the aforementioned Minister also gave notification of his arrival to the Swedes; and likewise to the Plenipotentiary Count Welling, upon which this last-mentioned Minister then engaged in compliments. According to the latest letters from Riga, the Czar of Great Britain was still found there. The letters inform me that Mr. Oesterman at [?] Flemming was still found at Warsaw; general news had been received there, indicating that the Prussians around [?] of Cochim had caused marching; that dominion was in motion toward the Carpathian [?]; and that the Imperial [?] with the Czar were sending a corps of army and Kalmyks toward Ukraine. It is reported that the King of Poland, for these reasons, has been able to return to his kingdom sooner than had been thought. According to the latest letters from Vienna, the Imperial General [?] of Vimund has recovered his health and stands ready to go to his government [?] matters, there to undertake, for which necessity [?] Studies were in hand; it was likewise reported there that the Prussians [?]; a certain one of their Ambassadors in France, Minister Count Kinsky, it is reported that his return [?] from [?] the Imperial [?], so that the air there was harmful to his health. This morning one heard here [?] and to his neighbors a foreseen discussion, in order there aforementioned three [?] to undergo, and 3 have received expectation of [?], which toward the aforementioned is said, in order there to go in chains; it is also said there would [?], in order there to go in chains; it will be there loaned [?] over the aforementioned guard at the head, which is first afterwards [?] to be [?].

Great Britain

London, 11 June. Wednesday, the Controller of the King's Household presented to the Lower House that their address of Saturday regarding Printer Milt had been presented to the Queen. His Majesty was moved by the Commons to say that he recognized theirs as an obedient and faithful address, and that the Queen would pursue the reparation of all the scandalous and forthright libels, and [...]. Thereafter presented the Mr. David Crawford, Marshal showed that the aforementioned Milt is under a condemnation, led by common tailors, and he is also charged with an act of 300 pounds sterling over debt; that he broke the [?], ordered the House to transport the printer Milt to the prison of Newgate, was ready to obey the orders, but that he, because the aforementioned Milt was in prison under sentence and over debt; the House humbly refused, to help him for the future, and invited him as aforesaid shall be done; upon which the House, after some debates, ordered that the aforementioned printer Milt remain imprisoned in the prison of King's Bank, and one withdrew and discovery of the authors, printers, and distributors of all the opposition libels, of the postponed [...] among the condemned prisoners, for the House, or by few committees have been assembled, and thereof shortly to the House report is made the further consideration on that day proposed was, shall be deferred until today. Yesterday a request was presented to the House by the City of Bristol, which the regents over the tax on soap, and the candles, the House ordered to the keeper of the prison of Gatehouse, to lead the imprisoned [?] Clayton from time to time before the assembly committee; as the aforementioned committee shall direct; upon which the House in grand committee returned over the bill to relieve the sufferers in [...]; and the aforesaid over assigned the appointed directors of the [...] C. from their appointed approval shall assign, was allotted to Knight Robert Chapplin, Knight [?] Chapman, Knight Will. Hinson, the Gentlemen Chelser, Holley, Child, and de la Pagne, each 10,000 pounds sterling; to the Gentlemen Eyles and Gore, each 20,000 pounds; to Mr. Harvey [?] and to Mr. Jr. Edmonton 18,000 pounds, and Mr. Hector direct [?] pounds sterling; whereupon the further receipt thereof until today was deferred. Today the Commons have passed the following allowances to the appointed directors of the [...] C.; and Knight Theod. [?] is allotted 10,000 pounds, with 134 against 114 votes; to Knight Jacob [...] 10,000 pounds (from Knight John Lambert 10,000 pounds; Knight Harcourt [?] 9,000 pounds); to Mr. Ingram 12,000 pounds, and to Manley 1,800 pounds on that day

---

(which on page 10 shillings 3 pence according to his entire inventory is.) 574 [?] 10,000 pounds, [?] 1,000 pounds, and to Mr. Reynolds 1,000 pounds; and the committee stands tomorrow the remaining allowances, regarding [?] Mr. Millard, Sawbridge, Turner, Grundy, and Sarmain, likewise the [?] by the [?], the House hereby concludes the further consideration of public credit [?], which postponed to Wednesday. Today the committee; appointed to in the gross the author to investigate of the traitorous brief, which [?] 1,000 men, week in the Journal of Mift printed is, as likewise the authors, because the printers and distributors of opposition libels. In the vestry of the [?] his speaker together sat, and examined Mr. Crawford; the affair, namely of the prison of the King's Bank, and also the book-keepers with regard to the aforementioned prison, regarding the aforesaid acts [?], of 100 pounds, at the charge of Mift; as likewise a copybook-seller, and. which in the act is involved; since this examination, found men summary, which say that this act alone tends [?] to keep Mift from the prison of Newgate; and men [?] of the foremost advocates, which are of the opinion that the brief in the aforementioned journal is high treason. Regarding this when one the discovery of four the matters is to hear. Wednesday their Royal Highnesses, the Prince and Princess of Wales were at the Opera at the Haymarket. Forbidden has been [?] on the Tennis France. The Convocation of the clergy, which was to assemble Wednesday, is deferred again until the 27th of October next. It is reported that Mr. Thomas Vernon, who was put out of the house to draw the write [?] King in the interest of Aislaby; is elected to Member in the Lower House for the borough of Whitchurch, in the county of Southampton, for which he was then also Member. Yesterday the gathering of all the children, which are brought up in the school of charity, both in and about the city, gathered in St. Sepulcher's church: their number consisted of approximately 2,500; there was a sermon delivered, and an appropriate hymn of praise sung. Yesterday 3,780 ounces of sugar were entered at the toll, to be transported to Holland. The shares of the Bank are 132, E.I.C. 133; S.S. 128, the Million Bank 132, Lottery annuities 96 and 3 halves a fourth, Bank annuities 96 and 3 fourths, ditto 4 per cent by aff. C. 3 and 3 halves, the Exchange afflicted [?] 8 and 3 eighths, ditto London 8 and 3 fourths; and J.B. 16 and 3 fourths.

France

Paris, 13 June. On the 8th of this month the King heard Mass, which was sung in music, and went that afternoon, having assisted at vespers, to the Elysian Fields, to show himself. On the 10th, Feast of the Holy Sacrament, the procession of St. Germain l'Auxerre came to the chapel of the Tuileries.

Paris, 14 June. That evening of this month the King heard Mass, which was sung in music, and went thereafter that afternoon having assisted at vespers to the Elysian Fields, to divert himself. On the 10th His Majesty saw the tragedy performed. On the [?]th, Thursday of the Holy Sacrament, came the procession of St. Germain l'Auxerre to the chapel of the Tuileries, where the King went to meet it as far as the door of the court, where the Queen [?] were changed; after His Majesty had given the blessing, he accompanied the Holy Sacrament into the chapel, where a motet was sung in music: the King attended it back to the front door of the court, when the Sacrament was brought back, and received another blessing; subsequently His Majesty went again into the chapel, and heard a thousand [?] music sung. The evening in the Salut in the Capuchin church. The same day the King, accompanied by the Duke of Bourbon and Marshal Duke of Villeroy, went for a walk. Further, on Sunday the lord Sourdeval, Gentleman of the Archbishop of Cambrai, fetched the Turkish Ambassador from his lodging to conduct him to Versailles; the entire ambassador departed in the middle of [?] Wednesday, being provided with all the officers of his house, and was escorted by the guard, which kept watch with him; he arrived at Meudon around ten o'clock, and after he had conducted the necessary business, that same night he went to sleep at Versailles; upon entering, His Excellency betook himself to the chamber where the baths are, where he said his prayers; subsequently he passed through the royal apartments and went walking in the park, as likewise in the gardens, where the waters played before His Excellency, and returned again to the castle, where an evening meal was prepared for him. The next day he went again to the apartment of the great Admiral Count of Toulouse, which was furnished by the King for him: the next day he went again to walk in the park and saw the fountains play, as likewise the ornament that was to be seen there and at Trianon: Thursday he went to Marly, and after he had conducted all business there, His Excellency came that night well to Versailles to sleep, from where he Wednesday evening returned to his lodging in this city, in the hope satisfied of what he had seen. There was an innumerable multitude of people at Versailles during the 3 days which the aforementioned ambassador stayed there; but the officers of the detachment, which were also present there, had pity [?] the while, in order to let no one in the castle, except persons of distinction and others whom they could recognize. His Majesty granted to the Count of Choiseul Beaupré, Lieutenant General of the King, the survivorship as Lieutenant General of Champagne for his second son, the Marquis de Choiseul, Captain in the regiment of horse of Orleans. The King has recalled the Abbot de Gamache, auditor of the Rota at Rome, and invested him with the archbishopric of Besançon; it is expected that Abbot Tulpin will take the place as auditor of the Rota at Rome to fulfill it. On the 11th of this month, being St. Barnabas, the knight d'Orleans, grand priest of France, for the first time which [?] in a night gallery, in the great gallery of that church, gave to the Gentlemen of the great council, which there yearly on that day [?] honor, that with a servant to [?]

Examples of problematic passages: - "kêN; ik de Haarltyd wierd aak eik Hert van de Graafisap" (opening section) - "zekerbeid genomen; zoo dat deze zaak niet der de Gemet van de Oridonnansie" - Multiple instances where words appear randomly concatenated or fragmented

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 18th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
