Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 20th of June, 1721

NAPLES, 27 May. The Viceroy is occupied daily with the utmost vigilance in public matters; and the Collateral Council meets every day to find means of raising the funds which the Emperor has ordered, in case the Turks should come to attack that Kingdom. On Saturday the Viceroy visited the Church of Donna Romita, where he witnessed the Miracle of the Fluidity of the Blood of St. John Baptist, which is commonly venerated in this Church. On Thursday the Marchioness of Gravina, Orfina Raspoli [?], the present [?] of Italy, departed for Rome, where she now resides. Yesterday the only Daughter of the Duke Vincenti was married, with which many Ceremonies were performed by Paulina Nantius. A few days ago some Ships were sighted on these Coasts, and one of the Captains reported that upon this news a few Captains ventured into the Sea to pursue them; but without success, as they were lost sight of [?].

ROME, 31 May. On Saturday the Pope gave Audience to his Ministers, in which His Eminence the Cardinals of [?] discussed the dispute and consulted about the Archbishop of Naples. Prince Pamphilij had also gone to the Quirinal to have Audience with the Pope; but he was given to understand that he should first reconcile himself with his Brother the Prince of St. Martin, which was done immediately thereafter. That same evening the Marchioness of Gravina from Naples arrived, and was received joyfully by her Spouse and her Father, Prince Ruspoli, with whom she has taken up residence. On Sunday in the Church of St. Maria Magdalena de Pazzi, His Holiness visited the Church and Convent of the Religious Nuns, an occasion in which the little Nieces of His Holiness were present, with the Pope blessing them with his Seals, where the Nobility and Prelates attended in great number, and among them were found the Lord Count Bishop of Terranova, [?] of His Holiness the Judge of the Court, the Lord [?] Treasurer, and the Constable Colonna, besides the Cardinals Cornadell and Chigi: a part of the Pope's Carriage was there: after His Holiness had spent some time in this Convent and given his Blessing to the Religious, he visited the Church of St. [?], where one finds the Account of Paola, His Holiness having on 16 May in the Year 1651 come to attend in this Church, not only in a Money-chest, but also to the Burial [?], as will be approved by the aforementioned College. The Pope has declared the Abbé Bianchiní, presumably the Secretary of the Congregation over the Calendar, as his Sunday Preacher, for which reason Cardinal Scotti gave him ecclesiastical Vestments. This morning the Monks of the Jesuit Order held their General Chapter in the Church of Minerva, and therein elevated to General Father Pio Apollio Pippen. Cardinal Patriciu is appointed Legate of Ferrara, and Cardinal Bentivogiio of Ravenna. Cardinal Alberoni has dismissed part of his Court Officials and Servants, because His Eminence intends [?], and has assured this Cardinal that he will receive full Justice, as well regarding matters already done as those still pending.

LIVORNO, 31 May. It is reported that the Count of Chávignon, who resides here, has gone to the Court of Parma.

GENOA, 31 May. Last Sunday the English Minister residing here gave a splendid Feast to the Ministers, on account of the Birth of the Son of the Prince of Wales; and the Commander of the English Squadron, which was mentioned in our previous [issue], together with many other Officers of Rank, were also present at this Feast. Fifteen Ships were equipped here, among them the Antonio Gisgow from Cadiz, Willumkox from Alicante, and Hendrick Welt from Ancona.

ROME, 4 June. Since Cardinal Orighi has taken leave of all his Relations, who had remained here, it is believed that Cardinal Ruffi, who is appointed to this Legation, will arrive here shortly.

VENICE, 7 June. News has been received by various Letters and Dispatches that a Turkish Tatar from [?], who came from Turkey, had encountered difficulty there, to the point that the Imperial Subjects clashed with the Ottomans, in which both Christians and Turks were killed; but those of Triello, being the first, defeated the Tatar, and the Turks withdrew; notice of which event has been given to the Imperial Court. By a Ship come from Smyrna, certain News has been received that the Ship St. Joseph, which was said to have been taken by the Barbary [pirates], has arrived there safely. The Prince of Modena is still here, and has [?] Express [messengers] regarding various matters: our Nobility has expressed its Satisfaction with a magnificent Serenade on the Grand Canal.

MARSEILLE, 11 May. At present 200 people have recovered here in good health, so that no more Sickness is heard spoken of; and one has received with great satisfaction the news that in Aix and other neighboring Places, likewise within a few days no kind of Evil has been reported. Permission has been granted by the great [authorities], to send our Ships and Vessels under Quarantine; the Ships, which are destined for the West Indies, shall now soon be set afloat, and remain there with their entire Cargo 20 days, to see whether any Sickness might arise among the People, either through the Goods or otherwise, which not being the case, every Health Certificate shall be obtained; the Churches, Bourse, and Markets remain, with precaution, however still closed. The advices from Toulon are slight, for although the Inhabitants of that City have been severely visited by the Plague, still 70 or 80 [cases] remain, by which the Misery and Wretchedness there is uncommonly great.

BAYONNE, 4 June. The Magistrate of this City became aware that the Ship of Captain [?] of Lixzoro had departed under guard, and suspected that some Contamination must exist both among the People and in the Goods; thus they had the Citizens assemble to take the voices of the Community, whether they would permit the Ship to enter the Port or not; the majority were of the opinion that, since there was no suitable place here in the vicinity to hold Quarantine, they should not admit that Ship, to avoid all inconveniences; whereupon it was resolved that the Captain, Officers, and Pilots should not enter the Bar with a Bark but should cruise along the Coast, and if possible, intercept the said Ship and warn it not to cross our Bar, since it would not be admitted here, but that if the Captain, to continue his Voyage, should need some Provisions or Refreshments, these should be sent to him with a Bark, which at a distance from his Ship shall come to anchor, after which the People [?] shall [?] with the aforementioned Officers and Pilots a smaller boat to fetch the Provisions with a Sloop; provided that the Bark, being guided by the Workers, shall be left in the Roadstead; and from which decision it can well be inferred that here all possible Precautions shall be employed against the import of Goods that are subject to any Contamination. From Amsterdam, and lastly from St. Sebastian, there has arrived here Captain [?] Dugas [?], and from Havre de Grace Captain Jan l'Espagnoux; and conversely some Ships are bound for Amsterdam and elsewhere.

PARIS, 13 June. In the Procession of St. Eustache, which took place yesterday, the Abbots Regent with two Guards, and the various Sergeants of his House, conducted themselves with uncommon distinction; in this Procession one saw for the first time the Officers of the Military Order of St. Louis, who were all in ceremonial dress; the Lord de la Janche and St. P. were dressed in black Damask, trimmed with burning [?] Lace, under which was a red Scarf, and over all a black Cloak, lined with red; the Sleeves were bordered with Gold embroidery, and the Cross hung from a red ribbon tied at the Hip [text becomes illegible]

Regensburg, 13 June

It is certain that a representation from the Papal College to the Emperor was sent to Vienna last Friday evening; it is no less forcefully drawn up than the previous remonstrances, and contains more than seventeen letters from a great number of prelates against the rescript from the Palatinate, which is in no way less than His Royal Majesty's order has authorized; but rather to establish everything in these lands as in former times under the Hadenburg Vicariate: against this, the Palatine Minister wishes to publish a writing to demonstrate that the Emperor, as mediator therein, has not only obeyed the requested restitution, but has even done more, and...

Cologne, 17 June

The Papal Nuncio, the Count of Artoceldt, has received orders from Rome to dispatch the English Nuncio, Mr. Grahams, from Poland, with Mr. Santini being appointed in place of Mr. Archinto as his successor.

The Netherlands

### AMSTERDAM, 15 June

The following ships have been attested as arrived: the St. Jan Bautista, Captain Etienne Collan, at Juda near Guinea; the ship Spes, Captain Simon [?], at Juda near Guinea; the ship la Jolie, Captain Allung [?], at Ceudy; the Eenhoorn, Laurens Hendricks, returning to Java; the ship Goudeaux from here; the St. Maarten, Evert Bulen of Rengvn; the young Gouda, Daniels, Skipper Welling, and Mr. Boode of Dordrecht, Matthias Burman of Hamburg; Willem Willemetis, the young Daniels, Evert Fieteric, all from here and the Marias, Pieterszn of Nordey; Jan van Cleetes of the St. Heller, Captain Klok, the Juift Elizabeth, Jan Abrams, and 2 others from here; and the Maas of Stockholm, Pieterkie, from Hull [?], of others; at Bicemew, Wilh. Rith of Hull and Carsten Eliers of London; at Hamburg, Willem [?] Ofters.

Cadiz 115 and one-half. Seville 114 and one-half. Bilbao 89 and one-quarter to 90. Limassol [?]. Venice 81 and one-half. Livorno 92. Genoa 93 and one-quarter. Hamburg 12 and one-quarter. Danzig 1 89. [?] and one-half. [?] 16 [?]. Berlin 3 and 2 eighteenths, 3 [?]. Hamburg 11 and same. St. Antwerp and one-quarter a one-quarter a 1 per cent. Ghent 2 and one-quarter. Brussels 32. [?] and one-quarter. Rotterdam 1 half. The Agio of the Bank 1 and direct agitation 1 half. The E.I.C. is 78 [?]. W.I.C. 105.

Subscription [?], cash and English Bank 129, E.I.C. 115. [?] and Deposit.

### THE HAGUE, 19 June

The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland have convened again today after the religious observance. The Gentlemen Commissioners of the Admiralty have arrived here, as have some chief commissioners of the West India Company. The Governor Domanede of Benftlien-termfort has arrived here with his three sons. It is reported that an express from the British court has departed for Madrid.

### THE HAGUE, 22 May

According to letters from Riga, His Caesarean Majesty's [fleet] numbered 145 to Revel by the twelfth of the intervening March. From Niemelandt it is reported that the Swedes [continue...]

Cadiz, 27 May

The letter is still uncertain; but it seems, and indeed the same [?]. In these [waters] the Hamburg and the St. Francis Holland, which are to be obtained.

---

P. Optenberg & Others, Rope Merchants, Will Sell In Amsterdam the White Swan, A Copper Vessel, 1000 Deals [Wood], & 1000 Fire Deals; 1000 Laths & 1000 Support Beams; 250 Hides; 50 Smooth Hamburg Borders By [Various Measures], Mast-Wood Pieces of [?] To 30 Feet, Mast Beams, Keel-Beams, Etc

Gommarius van Duyft, Jan van Baalen, and Jan Wittigh, brokers, will sell in Amsterdam at the Second Lord's Lodging on the Haarlemmerdyke, a good quantity and pretty household items in lots.

Hendrik Keribyl, broker, will sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, the 15th of June, a party of foreign linens and silk stuffs, namely 500 ells of serdarines, 300 [?], 140 fl. doublets, 300 fl. Syrian [?], 100 fl. carvedaries, 640 fl. white Maimo [?], 300 fl. diverse rozemals, 150 fl. doublets, 130 fl. bed-damasks, 300 fl. roll crenaix and rowans. Daniel Aritsanus Beukelaer, broker, will sell in Amsterdam at 2 hours' notice in the Old Lord's Lodging, certain obligations on diverse companies, the sum for which will be sold by the notary who belongs to the said brokerage.

Cornelis van Neck, broker, will sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, the 22nd of June, at the Calimini marble flowers, consisting of graduated white flature and [?] drape, as well [?], for the details and requisition machines and conditions; a party [?] and the half-dram, 175 black [?] [?], of 12 drams, and a good party of marbleware.

Dirk Handte, broker, will sell in Amsterdam from the [?] the 1st, in the evening at 2 hours, in the New Lord's Lodging on the Haarlemmerdyke, [sale continues...]

Jan Wolders, broker, will sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, the 2nd of July, a great party of English hats, silk and [?] colors, silk gloves, linen and stones, glass coral, clasps and other [goods], buckles, spurs, whips, thick crockery and a party of English tinware, namely pots, copper vessels, vinegar cans, and the rest of assorted service; all these will be sold 3 days hence at said broker's on the [?] near the [?].

Antony van Len will, on the 2nd of July 1721 and the following days, sell at Haarlem his second-hand books, comprising 100 old and new theological works, histories, Keys of Aesculapius by Antonius Swal, in his lifetime preacher at the Bakenstein Church, his books, all by [various authors], etc., obtainable in Amsterdam from A. van [?] Mage, I. Kitte, Publisher E. Wagnus, Haarlem M. van Lée. N.B. there and [information] from J. T. Kop, notary, doctor and professor at the University of Leiden, in 8vo.

Printed in Leiden by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedelstraat.

10 June 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

### The Exchange Rate COURT is today at Madrid 80 and one-half.

Notices & Advertisements

On Sunday, the Emperor observed Whitsunday with the customary solemnities at Luxembourg and appeared in public. On Monday, His Imperial Majesty attended divine service at Modlingen with the Capuchins, after which he returned to Luxembourg and took his seat in the great council. That same evening, dispatches arrived here from the third of the previous month, which were not entirely satisfactory. The threatening movements of the Turks around Chotin and Kamianets are causing ever-increasing concern, which is why several powers are also keeping their troops ready on the Polish borders, in case the occasion should require them to come to their aid; for the same reason, the Emperor has equipped all the grenadiers in Hungary with the necessary provisions and is completing several other regiments in full strength.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 20th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 75.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 23rd of June, 1721
The West Indies

CURAÇAO, 1 May. Recently arrived here were the ships of Dirk Houthuyflen, Klaas Regtop, and on 2 April the Fortuyn, Fredrik Laland, all three from Amsterdam, as well as a large number of barks from Tucacoa, Coro, and St. Domingo, among them the young Balthasar, Frans Farey, and Pieter Buridge (the latter having been seized by a Spanish privateer and ransomed), Jan Stevens via Porto Rico, the Second Concordia from Rio de la Hacha, Jan Klasfie, Jan Legrave, Jan Vos, Harmen Tyfsle, Jan Mulder, James Carwalle, Juliaan Exter, Jan Bont, and others, all from the Cataquel and Bulks of St. Thomas, and another from New York, together with ten to thirteen barks from the Cuff, which were mostly driven off by Spanish cruisers. Guard was so strictly maintained on the Cuff that no goods could be unloaded. Several second ships lay there, as well as that of Willem Houthuyflen, which relieved the brigantine of Jan Bontcke, which had been seized by a Spanish privateer from Cumana (where it had its first cargo), has since arrived here. The Spanish cruisers have captured several other barks, and the island ship the Sara, Captain Thom. Urinning, ran aground on the island of Roca, and all salvaged goods have been sent to St. Thomas. The ship of Dirk Koeler from Amsterdam is not being lifted in purchase, so it is feared it may have suffered some damage. From below, two ships had come to the upper Cuff. Pieter Toethouwe is preparing shortly to depart from Anit.

CURAÇAO, 1 May. There is no news from the coast of Porto Bello; but according to reports from a bark from St. Domingo that has arrived here, a good number of traders have been discovered in the region, without it being known where they are headed. The Spanish ships that lay at Porto Cavalho, etc., have sailed from there to Porto Covo and New Spain; as well as on 2 June the brigantine the Fortuyn, Fredrik Laland, from here to the lower Cuff, from which word had come to the Cataquel that in good condition lay the ships of Christoffel Heynt and Pieter Bernard, and that no robbers had been reported there. On the upper Cuff lay, apart from the Captains Jan Akervelt and Bendt, still the Second Ships of Baltian Wiggers, Jan Corn., Cornelis Barbers, and three others, as well as Captain Ermantois, etc., of which some, together with that of Will. Houthuylen, are expected here. Before this city have arrived two barks from Jamaica, as well as the Anna Maria and Adam Legraaves from the Cuff.

Spain

CÁDIZ, 27 May. The precise day of the departure of the galleons remains uncertain, but it is said this shall occur within 8 days, and that warships of 50 and 70 guns shall sail with them. Arrived in this bay are the Thomas Headon from Hamburg and the St. Franciscus Xavier from Tortola. Finally, the Holland Convoy, consisting of the warships under Captains Arnout van Buuren, the Baron van Hardenbrock, and the Baron van Whitenbold, on 1 June departed from here with the following merchant ships for Amsterdam and Dordrecht, namely Jacob Buys, the Agata Galy, Jacob Rave, the Debora Galy, Skipper Klavedin Genneman, the 2 Company of Dordrecht, Gérard Schaep, the St. Pieter Galy, Corn. Matelenie, the Prosperity, Gerrit Hadegraft, all to Dordrecht, and Corn. Vilser to Oudegraft.

France

PARIS, 16 June. Last Wednesday the King granted the survivance of the Lieutenant General's rank of the Government of Champagne to the Marquis de Choiseul, Captain in the Regiment of Picardy of Orleans, and became the son of the Count de Choiseul Beaupré, Lieutenant General over the King's Troops. On that day also the Marquis d'Armanouille, by order of the Duke Regent, expelled the guards from the supervisors of the Grocery Guild and informed them that they would do well to cease their prosecutions against the grocer Grivet. In these circumstances the Guild however hesitated. That same day Mr. Grivet, Doctor in Medicine, was ordered by the Church authorities to return to the Carmelite Cloister and submit himself to penance for his apostasy: his marriage to a young woman who had requested its dissolution was annulled, and the children declared [?]. On Thursday, the Feast of the Holy Sacrament, there was again an attempt to capture the notorious thief Cartouche; and it is certain that he never felt more secure against being taken into custody in the Gotheline, where very many people go to see the costly tapestries which on that day were hung there: but through the tumult caused thereby, the devil was not caught, and he was brought to a place very far off, whereupon many archers found themselves in the vicinity, so he understood to exchange his clothes with those of a companion; by which stratagem he once again escaped the young servants; which would not have happened well if the spy who pursued him, and indeed his young wife, who had married through coercion [?], wherein Cartouche's child was wearing the clothing, had not recognized it; but an hour later it was discovered that the arrested person was the son of a buckster; who, having employed all necessary means to prevent any consequences coming to him, had sent his child away to Louisiana, from whence he sent means by which to regain his previous profit from Breda: he confessed immediately that his clothes had been exchanged with those of Cartouche, though it is believed they could not hold him in custody on that account, yet finally [text becomes corrupted in original] [?]

Great Britain

LONDON, 17 June. Saturday the Lower House, in full Committee, voted from the accounts presented of the late Directors, allocated to Mr. Sawbridge £5,000, to Mr. Tillard £1,300, to Mr. Suiman £5,000, and to Mr. Turner £800, that is to say, within £96 of the entire sum which he had borrowed; thereafter the Committee, appointed to investigate the authors and distributors of seditious and treasonous writings, made report to the House regarding the matter of the printer Mr. [?]. To the relief of the House, the Speaker related that he had that morning received a letter which had come to his house, stating that said Mr. would be released that evening to flee to France, and indeed by the connivance of the Warden of the Prison of the King's Bench, wherefore the wife of Mr. [?] converted everything that was possible to money; moreover the Committee said it should be warned to pay close attention to the Prison of Mr. [?], as his wife had sold everything, and the likelihood was that he would in one way or another escape; whereupon the House brought the said Marshal before the Bar and examined him as to how Mr. was held in custody, to which the Marshal replied that he was kept in a house near the Prison; thereupon the House ordered the aforesaid Marshal to bring Mr. immediately before the Bar, who, having arrived there, was unwilling to answer any of the questions put to him; and being brought outside, the House spoke to the Bar, and ordered that he be led to the Prison of Newgate and there be very closely confined, without being given pen, ink, or paper, and that he should be spoken to by no one, except with knowledge of the House. Subsequently the House commanded the Keeper to bring the aforementioned Mr. before the Committee as often as the same shall deem necessary, and the Committee was given power to examine such persons as it should deem necessary. Thereafter Mr. Clayton (who, in place of the sick Mr. Broderick, presided in the late Committee) made a further report to the Lower House, wherein various Members of both Houses of Parliament, and indeed both Whigs and Tories, were charged with having accepted bribes; so that there was much to relate, which gifts and receivings had occurred because they would have been [?] for the Bill of Subordinates, though the report being read, it was proposed by Mr. Cowper to consider it tomorrow; whereupon two former Members demonstrated the difficulties and consequences [?] which would arise from such proceedings, and though they were undertaken with suitable and careful caution, yet one preserves the reputation of such great men thereupon, and sees that the charges are weak and founded on mere rumor; but Mr. Joseph Jekyll and others expressed themselves warmly in agreement with the sentiment of Mr. Cowper; and after some debate, it was resolved to take this great matter into consideration on Thursday, instead of tomorrow; following which resolution Mr. Pormacher, one of the Members of the House, rose and complained in the strongest terms that he found himself among the number of these 132 accused, saying that his case in particular should be taken into consideration by the House, since he was ready to make his defense and without doubt convincingly to demonstrate that one had wrongly damaged his reputation; and it was hoped that the charge, that he had accepted £1,000 in bribes, would be examined on Thursday the sixth. The said Rampoli [Editor's note: likely "Report"] furthermore mentioned that £3,000 in bribes, being a portion of £37,000, had been taken in the names of Campier and other brokers, who when examined on this declared they knew nothing of it, and that one had made use of their names without their persons having any knowledge of the disposition of the bribes. Further, that £33,000 was taken in the name of Coltheend in Ireland, and that this name was verified and indeed, according to the report, there stand £134,000 in the proper names of Members of both Houses of Parliament. Yesterday the lady wife of the Lord Mayor of this City was delivered of a son and a daughter in childbed, which were immediately baptized by the Presbyterian Divinal [?], and the son was named Gabriel, and the daughter Delicia.

Today a hearing was held before the Commission for the second time, and he was again made to swear an oath that he would answer honestly to whatever questions might be put to him regarding the author. Concerning the nature of the matter, he subsequently declared that a clergyman unknown to him had written the letter and had asked him to deliver its contents, whereupon he was taken into custody again and placed back in prison. Nothing else of importance occurred in Parliament today. It appears that Mr. Vethon at the Borough of Whitefriars may have lived in the Luperus, given that it was found that Mr. Frederick Tilney had been elected at the said Borough. Several days ago the Ministers of that Court drew up a report from Madrid with news that the differences between Spain and this Court have been completely settled: that the King of Spain has renounced his claim to Gibraltar, and that both parties have agreed upon a convention regarding commerce with that kingdom, which is necessary for the trade of the [Dutch East India] Company in America. The Princess Caroline finds herself unfortunately afflicted with the same illness as that which some weeks ago inconvenienced the Princess Caroline, from which the King's cousin died, and the Princess Anna was also somewhat hindered by a cold stomach. Bank shares are at 128; East India Company 134; [West India Company] 131; Million Bank 111; Lottery Annuities 90 and 3 pence; Annuities 10 and a half; ditto 4 per cent 90; [?] 30; Marine Insurance 6; ditto London 7 and a half; and [?] 16 and a quarter.

---

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 11 June. Arrived at this port: on the 16th, the Godolphin, Theodore Reetz from Albion; on the 17th, Hans Federkmos from [?]; on the 18th, Jan Leghem, Abraham van Kappel, and Jacob Janssen Spaan from Dunkirk; today the Exchange, Christoffel Fox from Sunderland; the Prime-role, Curbert Crafton from Whitby; Cornelis Bosman from Dunkirk and Adrian de Lange from Newport. In departure: on the 14th, the Chaudois Sloop, Tobias Jewells toward London; the Content, Will. Estby toward England; laden the Thomas and Catherine, William Lewis toward Newcastle; today the Thomas and Mary, John Scarpe, and the Warwick, Gut Butler toward Sunderland; the William, Steven Dixbourg toward Scotland. At Childerhouse has arrived from here the Wapen van Amsterdam, captain Anne Koning.

THE HAGUE, 22 June. The Gentlemen States of Holland and West Friesland have continued their meetings this week.

All those who yesterday claimed to have some right to the estate of [...] Dogeert of fifty such as then belonged to the Honorable Count of Hornes, are requested to apply to Mr. N. Nicolaus Milder, agent, residing on the Toorenstraat in The Hague, of the Guild of [Affilante] &c. of the city of Rotterdam. The Commission and conditions on which the publication is closed have been [?] the general signature from the competent authorities, and therefore on the [...] of July coming, until the 13th following, for which time the Directors of the Leyden Newspaper [?] must apply.

To the extent of one per cent, the second furnishment will be completed of the said Company there, drawn up on the 12th hereof and fifth of the Act of the Deductions shall fall to the benefit of the Company. This Monday the 3rd June, being Monday the 23rd June 1721, [...]

---

Stockholm, 7 June

Since the arrival of Admirals [?] and Nooth in this city, their orders have been willingly received. The assembled war fleet has approached as far as Elfsborg, and on their orders the Russian troops, with their galleys, have made another invasion in the Kingdom, destroying some villages and houses, and burning 3 or 4 buildings. The said Admirals, after holding some conferences with the Swedes, have departed diligently back to the fleet to counter any further enemy activity. The Swedes have burned some galleys and vessels lying on the stocks, and had audience with His Majesty, who enjoys good health. Prince Eugene of Hesse-Kassel intends to sail in a warship to Königsberg to meet the King of Prussia there. Regarding the negotiations at Niuweld, continuous conferences are being held at Court daily on this matter. The French Minister, Mr. Campédon, still awaits his dispatch from Paris.

---

Vienna, 11 June

News has arrived in a dispatch from the Imperial bath, reporting that the Empress will not return here before the 8th or 10th of the coming month of June, as Her Imperial Majesty finds herself in very good health from the use of those baths; and it is believed the Emperor also will not leave Luxembourg to enjoy the benefit of the mineral waters.

On the 13th, the Count of Virmond, along with many other generals, had a private audience with Prince Eugene; and the said Count of Virmond departed that afternoon from his governorship of Zevenbergen, heading toward [?], still uncertain. The Count of Harrach has married the widow of the Count of Gallus.

---

Basel, 16 June

It is reported here that the Emperor [has secured] Comacchio on this Papal throne [and] he has offered not only a great sum of money for this, but also [... lacuna ...] and the Turks, to prevent them from [...]

---

Regensburg, 16 June

The Protestant Corps is very strong against a member of the Diet, [and] the Imperial [?] has been [?]; because he continuously communicated the matters under discussion to the Ministers of [?] Court; from which it is concluded that [... lacuna ...]. Regensburg is also known to [... lacuna ...] and it is certain that the said Ministers [... lacuna ...] the aforementioned [?] seek to corrupt in order to [... lacuna ...].

---

Hanover, 17 June

It is confirmed that the Hessian troops are withdrawing to their lands. The troops of the houses of Brunswick have so far maintained their positions, but regarding their march all remains quiet. News has come that the Russians have made another landing in Old Sweden, and that the British and Swedish fleets have sailed toward the island of Åland.

---

Schwetzingen, 17 June

The journey of our Elector to the Ems-bath is postponed to a later date.

---

Cologne, 20 June

The journey of the Elector of Cologne to Liège remains uncertain. The Bishop of Münster and Paderborn held in recent days a solemn musical service in the Cathedral of Münster, and had the Te Deum Laudamus sung for the election of the Reich-Bishop Pius, and that afternoon entertained the University with a great feast.

The Widow Countess Anna of Nassau, who recently lost her son in this city with great sorrow [... lacuna ...] against the [?] [... lacuna ...] the books for transportation will be [... lacuna ...] of the said [...] Directors distribution will yet declare.

Extraordinary notices of Commerce, at Amsterdam and [?] Company at Edam.

---

[The remainder of the document, containing dispatches from Leipzig, Hamburg, and London, is too corrupted in transcription to render accurately.]

Printed at Leyden by HELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Driestraat. The 23rd June 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**AMSTERDAM, 11 June.** The ship of Hans Honimoor, from Bergen-op-Zoom, was chased for some time off the Portuguese coast by a Sallee pirate with 10 or 12 guns, full of yolk, but fortunately escaped at night due to the onset of strong winds. The following day, ships from Pieter Hendricks, Pieter Timmerman, and Willem Roelsfke departed from Saint Lucia toward this city and Hamburg; and from Saint Wintset Trou, heading this way. At Bordeaux has arrived Pieter Klein, and at Bergen Baltuan Gelding, both from here. The exchange rate today stands at: Madrid 80 and three quarters; Cadiz 115 and a half; Seville 114 and a half; Bilbao 80 and a quarter; Lisbon 44; Venice 81 and a quarter to three eighths; Leghorn 91 and a quarter to three eighths; Genoa 93 and a half; Geneva 93 and a half; Paris [?] and a quarter to [?] and a half; London 34 shillings 8 pence and a half; Danzig 280 Polish groats; Breslau 4 eighths; Hamburg 21 eighths; Antwerp [?] and a half per cent; Brussels 2 and a quarter; Zealand 1; Rotterdam a quarter. Bank agio [?] and [?] eighths. Regarding East India Company shares 78 and [?]; West India Company 105; [?] 100. English securities show no fixed price.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 23rd of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 76.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 25th of June, 1721
Sweden

SDERHAM, 9 June. The Russians, seeing that they could not carry out their attack on Gefleien, since an army was positioned in that vicinity, having taken their galleys northward from that city, and having come ashore there, they burned the village of Hambringe with its ironworks, as well as the Oxmar ironworks, Simon ironworks, Lobna ironworks, and several other places, having destroyed everything far and wide by fire, up to and including the fine free village of Hoedigswald; which has caused such terror among the inhabitants of the countryside that they, abandoning their houses and farmsteads, have fled into the forests, not daring to come forth until now; also the Russians had taken many vessels and all the mills which they had not yet destroyed; so that the damage is greater than was initially reported.

GOTHENBURG, 12 June. One learns here immediately that the Russians have landed troops on the English island Breno, near Winghioc, 3 miles from this city; and because the exact circumstances are not yet known, it is feared that they will have deposited the kettles that stand on that island in the ashes. Likewise, Russian frigates have taken two Swedish galleys from this city, and another vessel besides.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

REGENSBURG, 16 June. The Elector Palatine's minister has, according to what has been made public through print, declared that persons who have acted on the orders of His Electoral Majesty, and who have contributed so little to the Protestant Corps, although it was necessary to provide relief: the Palatinate authority wishes to make complete execution dependent on the restoration of the freedom of confession, in this time encompassed among the redressing of grievances, without saying anything regarding the infinite nature of the following matters; that His Electoral Majesty, insofar as it concerned him, had given the necessary orders, and which did not please him also would be executed, holding moreover over it the still unresolved matter of the Revenues, the transplanting of Reformed Church members, and the history of those who have not refrained from the Protestants complaining of being mistreated, even at the time when one was occupied with redressing their grievances; but since the Protestant Corps, assured of their conduct having already been reported to the Elector, that said Palatinate minister sought to conceal, the same has informed His Electoral Majesty insofar as all the improprieties of the matter, the aforementioned difficulties so clearly shown, that one doubts whether the Protestants shall obtain satisfaction thereover, and the Elector be obliged to make an unsatisfactory restitution besides. Since the Roman Catholic ministers have complained as if the King of Prussia, as Elector, would not have submitted to such measures, and consequently full reprisals would not have been lifted, the Protestant Corps in its conference has resolved to write thereupon with the first post to His Prussian Majesty, to be informed of the truth; with the request that if there should still be some Prussian matters to be commanded thereafter, to cause them likewise to be restored, and to bring no harm to the Protestant Religion in the Palatinate.

LYON, 13 June. One is assured that news has arrived here that the preliminary peace points between the Count and his King of Sweden have been settled.

HAMBURG, 20 June. An express messenger passing through this city has, as one learns, brought news that the preliminary peace points between the State of Groningen and the arrival of Mr. Osterman at Neustadt have been approved by the Russian and Swedish Plenipotentiaries, of which one awaits the confirmation with longing.

England

LONDON, 26 June. The King came to the House of Lords and gave his Royal assent to the Bill, forbidding the making of ships and buttonholes; those to determine the wages for the tailors' guild, which on account of the raw silk and [?] to make a river navigable, besides another 2 private matters, which on account of the silk [?] one man very much has made no claim upon.

That day the House of Commons met in grand committee on the state of public credit; and after a long debate regarding the 1 million on actions and shipping, it was resolved by 122 against 314 votes that all those who sit on the fund South Sea actions at the rate of four hundred or less, as well as at the rate of 300 pounds sterling per hundred, etc., not by the last Saint John distribution loaned, and have bound their actions or subscription to that for this purpose, shall be exempt from that, and the South Sea Company shall hold said actions and appendages in particular; and that the said Company the actions at the rate of 300 shall sustain, for the payment of all moneys which they have paid out on any rate as the aforementioned matters, on actions: furthermore it is provided that the House upon its report this day shall decide. Thereafter Lord Hinchingbrooke, on behalf of the Commission to investigate the authors of seditious writings, made known to the House that Dr. Capton and Mr. Nath. Watkinson having been summoned before the Commission; whereupon the question was raised why investigation was made regarding them in relation to the Sergeant of the Guard to [?], Gilbert examined also the Lords on the state of public credit, whereupon little progress was made. Those of Lord La Evidijs, in grand committee meeting, made arrangements for the hearing of the former Committee against Mr. Forcelier, whom one, without that his case was decided upon, declared innocent; thereafter the House examined the case of the widow Will. Carew, over which a long and difficult debate arose.

Until tomorrow adjourned; as well as the Bill for the relief of the Lords Committee taken: and the House has approved that 3 million pounds public [debt] must be paid; but being put to the question whether one should also remit the remaining 2 million, this passed in the negative with 166 votes against 155: subsequently all the other resolutions were approved, excepting that regarding the drawings, which, without voting, was again referred to the Committee. Then it was resolved to request the King by address to publish a Proclamation, with offer of a premium for those caught, and the aforementioned Gaylard, or Wilkinson, printers of [?], shall come to be apprehended, because they, or by order of the Committee being delayed, have not appeared before the same. Among the members of the House of Parliament who in the last report were Essex, Rothes, and Huddington; the Lords Lansdowne, Gower, Cherwynd, and Blundel, together with the Marquis of Winchester, General Pepper, Baronet Tom. Wynne, Knight Robert Chaplin, John Roberts, Sam. Tufnall, John Burridge, Col. Schorr, Will. Cherrybury, Stapleton, Bamfield, John Bamfield, John Bland, Tom. Sawbridge, Hen. Drax, Col. Graham, Will. Gordon, James Trasas, Col. Munroe, Will. Forrester, Patrick Haldane, Joseph Bankeer, Col. Normandy, Wilfred Lawlog, Will. Younge, and Knight Will. Carew: and in consequence of said report, 12,800 pounds were, which the aforementioned gentlemen were also charged with, sold for 30,400 pounds 10 shillings; and 31,900 pounds on borrowed moneys for 68,230 pounds. Yesterday Sir John Blunt was in the Portugal Yard Parliament House, and gave to each member a printed account of his case, to which a submissive petition for the relief of his overburdened family was attached, without showing any other motives; whereat one has wondered. The Committee on seditious writings has, since my last, again examined Mist under oath, as well as the printers and publishers of the London Journal and other papers; and one is in expectation that on the report of this Committee, all the weekly journals must be properly inspected. The secret Committee, which likewise continued to sit daily, has again questioned Clayton, who remains in Gatehouse. One of the King's messengers has found means to obtain the original manuscript of Mist's journal of the 11th of this month, wherefore one trusts that to prevent unlawful traffic we shall soon track down the author; indeed one finds some people who assure that he the writer has received from him some treasonable letters. The shares of the Bank are 325 and a half, East India Company 134, South Sea 114, 133 and 110; the Million Bank 115, Lottery and Bank Annuities 96 and a half, ditto 4 per cent 29 and a half; the Exchange assurance; and 7 August, ditto London 1 and three-quarters, and York Buildings 15 and a quarter.

France

PARIS, 10 June. The King has, during the week following Corpus Christi day, given his blessing in several churches. Monday His Majesty was in the church of the Convent of Calvarien, [?] and departed thence with pomp; Tuesday the young monarch was in that of the Visitation, in the street of Saint Anthony, and went thence to the Castle of Bercy; Thursday the King went, accompanied by the Duke of Bourbon, the Marshal of Villeroy, and the old Bishop of Fiesole, to the Palace of Luxembourg, where His Majesty saw the procession of Saint Sulpice pass; heard on Thursday morning the Te Deum in the churches, and that evening at the home of the Duchess of Ventadour, who is called Madame Maman of His Majesty. The Pope wrote a missive to the aforesaid Duchess of Ventadour to compliment her on the care she has taken of the King during the years of His Majesty's childhood; and His Holiness has sent her a rosary of agate with gold mountings, along with other trinkets as a gift. Although work is being done in the old Louvre to place the King's library there, one learns that some difficulty will be made at the Bank building, because the Abbé Bignon, librarian of His Majesty, will lodge there at Saint Jean. The sphere, which was presented to the King by the vicar of Saint Siré, is found more and more beautiful, and His Majesty has given him a pension of 2000 livres. The Cardinal de Noailles has had a reading conference these days at Madame's, at Saint Cloud, but one does not know what it was about. The Duke of Chartres was yesterday formally received in his new dignity by the groups, which had assembled in various places. The Duke of Bouillon is lying at death's door. There is talk as if well some change might occur at court, that the controller general would go to Fiennes, and that the Duke d'Amelonville and the Duke of Noailles would be placed anew at the head of affairs; and if such follows, in view of the said circumstances, the servants of finance and the military may well find themselves the least satisfied thereover. The Council of Health, having met at the house of the Chancellor, has decided to renew the previous prohibitions, and [?] partridges, pigeons, rabbits, or hens may not be eaten. One does not yet know whether the Abbey of Cercampe will be given to the Cardinal de Rohan or someone else; but it is assured that it is already encumbered with a pension of 3000 livres. The Bishop of Avignon, intendant of Tours, has laid before the King's hands the certificate of fidelity concerning the office of Chancellor Keeper of the Seal of the Military Order of Saint Louis, vacant through the voluntary resignation of his elder brother, the Count d'Argellon, lord of Maubouige. Mademoiselle de Choiseul, daughter of the [text cuts off]

Left Column

[The opening section of this column is heavily corrupted in the original transcription and cannot be reliably translated. The text appears to reference various French officials and nobility but lacks coherent meaning in the source material.]

Amsterdam

[Several paragraphs follow with administrative notices regarding governance, appointments, and official matters, but the transcription is too corrupted to render accurately into coherent English.]

A private individual who holds a position has requested the necessary authorization from the States of Holland to prepare a comprehensive list of all nobility in that Province; and to provide such documentation with appropriate care, he has asked the aforementioned States to furnish him with the family trees of more than 100 families in that domain. The Congress in the Golden Lozenge is now proceeding, and it appears the dispute concerning Gibraltar between the Kings of Spain and Great Britain is quite serious and official. For this evening there was an Opera Ball to entertain the Ottoman Ambassador; several days ago his Excellency was received by various gentlemen; and the Ambassador will shortly proceed to the Castle of Bléry to observe certain chemical experiments which are being conducted there; he recently recovered from a serious illness that had befallen him; and this coming Sunday he will attend the Opera to see a play performed in Turkish costume, which will be in the Turkish style that has lately been favored; some say that this Ambassador will not depart until the coming year. The Marquis Damilli has since then lodged at the Hetberg inn, in the Bianchetti. The two Golden Lozenges which had been set on Canochet have been confiscated...

The Netherlands

### BRUSSELS, 23 June.

The War Council has ordered the libellous pamphlet of the Baron d'Audignies against Lt. Col. du Soirzy to be burned in the prescribed place. On Saturday, the gentleman Vegelin, Lt. Col. in the Regiment of the Prince of Orange, laid down his arms before the Muses; [?] Commandant Major of the Castle of Doornick; that day the Countess of Windiegratz arrived at the Arches, where the Prince of [?] erected a manifesto on the 11th day. From Bruges it was reported that the Regiment of the Count of la Noy, which was to go into garrison at Ostend, had first arrived there; and that the Regiment of Bouthemellie came into garrison at Nieupoort for the Monday, and suffered considerable loss there through the second contingent; because

All those who may have claims upon the estate of the late Douart, Count of the Hongefoorden, Lord Count of Hornes, must present themselves to M. Nicholaus Melder, Agent, residing in the Toornstraat, in [?]. The broker Cornelis van Nek of Amsterdam will sell Italian marble blocks, consisting of various white alabaster slabs and other items, all of the finest and most skilful artistry and conditions; a quantity of fine linen damask, and good quality marble fixtures, being sold this week. The broker Cornelis de Roos and David [?] will offer various obligations on different comptoirs, as well as loose [?] and bonds of the City of Nijmegen; accounts of the City according to various interests are still outstanding, which should be settled: holders of these should be willing to withdraw the capitals thereof against one hundred, this to be done before the first of September of this year with the aforementioned broker, represented in the Redumbe. Further, Jan Hoos, Jan Riedemaker, and Corn. Hoos, brokers to be at Amsterdam, in the Dykstraat in the Old Burgh, will sell a quantity of fine colored silk goods and old wool fabrics.

Jan Woelfers, Broker, will sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday, 2 July, in the suburb of [?], silk and satin goods, silk handkerchiefs from France, coral, scissors and other metals, glass, spoons, brooms and the like fabrics; furthermore a quantity of English textiles, such as scarce and fine items for various series; all by the aforementioned broker, on the Outsergracht near the Reguliersgracht.

Right Column

### AMSTERDAM, 23 June.

The barks which, following on recent letters from Batavia, were sighted at the height of St. Domingo, were, according to reports, French vessels sailing toward the Mississippi. According to letters from the S. Ioanna, named [vessel] of Gibraltar, which were on the English East corners, [the situation with] the Minister of Great Britain and that of the King of Morocco and [?] had been reported, according to our intelligence, there was to be a fleet week between those two Nations to settle the matter: at Gibraltar there still lay the privateering yachts, the Forrest Willi Hunter with the min Caly, Capt. Stuart, and King George Cale. Lying at moorage, together with the Seaship the Unicorn, and others.

At Nerva have arrived Rynders Tjecis and Jelte Molezaat; at Dantzig the batcat Klaas de Jong; C. F. kan Normen; and at Danzig various other vessels of this city.

The SHARES of the E.I.C. are 780. W.I.C. 104 4 and 1 half. Subler 995 to 1000. The English estates have no fixed [?].

### THE HAGUE, 23 June.

A circular letter from the gentlemen of the States of Holland and West-Friesland has been sent to the respective cities of this Province, being among other things of the following content:

"Whereas we have come into certain knowledge that through the long-lasting winter, the well-usage of the land has been much constrained and in many respects severely damaged, the royal hunting ground has proceeded poorly and it is reported that the cultivation thereof has been set back some time, and the royal hunting jurisdiction of the same remains binding, which in previous years around the [?] of May came to the hunt, in this present season now after all the ordinary and aforementioned reasons could well be that also for the aforementioned reasons the flat or low [?] must be wholly stripped of hunting, livestock and other game; this is necessary, should the meadows on [St. Jacob] become degraded, as before, the hunt would come to be diminished, all the remaining game, as also the old [?] which would then for these reasons stand forth much insufficient and weak, and would once be exhausted, to great damage of all which can be done through dunes, wilderness and dune-keepers, but also to the diminution of the enjoyment of certain meadows which further and near the Lord and the Pool coopers were separated [?] 200 [?], that we after information concerning the condition and remedies of the Ackennaem over our domains have approved that in the hunt, 200 in dunes and wilderness, as in other dunes and wildernesses, by us from private parties to be hired or otherwise assigned, on [St. Joseph] and St. [?] day following, shall come for decision on the twenty-fourth of August following, at the request of private parties, and [St. Jacob's] day in the aforementioned season after the customary dunes and the [?] thereof..."

England

[Opening paragraph heavily corrupted in original transcription.]

At [?] the 27th [month]: Faulkshire chapel [?] the considerable ones in the General [?] of the Brethren of that Order. [?] Further text is too corrupted to render reliably.

The [?] has, [?] day, second day of the [?] Church, [?] his Guard, of [?]. In particular procedure is [?] [?] the side, as following such [?], which [?] the Lord still [?] had; but [?] did not comply; and [?] of Don [?] of the Council are [?]; according to [?] turned with [?] the old [?] gave, and new in place [?] Brief until [?] it has [?] death of Mr. Medici of the [?] Ambassador declared Imperial Ambassador not of Africa suitable until the coming [?] Vice-realm on the Galleys of the [?]...

---

Course of Exchange

The course of EXCHANGE is today at Madrid 30, Cadiz 115, [?] 114, Bilbao 91, Lisbon 92 and 1 half. [?] 11 and [?] 12. Livorno 92, Genoa 91 and 1 half. Paris sight 81 and 1 [?]. London 13 sch. at 1 and 1 half gr. Danzig 25 gr. Breslau 13 and 1 half sch. Hamburg 12 and 1 sch. at half [?] sch. Antwerp 1 and 2 fourths per cent. Ghent 1 and 1 fourth. Bruges 2 and 1 fourth. Zeeland 1 and fourth. Rotterdam 1 and 1 fourth. The stock of the Bank 1 and 2.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 25th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 77.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 27th of June, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, 1 June. On Sunday the Viceroy paid a public visit to Cardinal Pignatelli, our Archbishop, who had arrived in this city from Rome on Thursday, and in return the Cardinal paid a visit to the Viceroy on the following day. The Pope and St. Severo, Cardinal of Spain and Knight of the Golden Fleece, has travelled in good time from Vienna so that his sister, the Princess Joanna, as First Lady received him from the reigning Empress.

ROME, 7 June. On Sunday morning, the first day of Whitsun, the Paschal Chapel was opened, and 23 Cardinals attended. In the afternoon, Father Fra Augustin Pippia (who has recently been appointed General of the Fathers of the Order of Preachers) proceeded with 212 Religious of the Order in procession to the Vatican, where they kissed the Pope's foot. The post of Secretary of the Index, which was previously held in common, has been given to Father Michael Perez. On Monday, at the Pope's Feast, the Pope, accompanied by all the Cardinals and very many other Prelates, the nobility, and the customary choirs of Rome, attended Mass, and particularly in the Chapel della Casancia, after which he proceeded on foot in procession to the Church of the Holy Ghost in Saria, led by those from the office, conducted by the entire ecclesiastical and secular clergy, and accompanied by all the Cardinals, including even the new ones who have not yet received the hat, which His Holiness had permitted to them; but the Spanish and Portuguese, who also govern the Arms and even the old Roman Cardinals, to whom His Holiness had not permitted such, were indicated in this procession by a banner in the Palace of Don Antonio Colonna. When His Holiness had entered the aforementioned Church of the Holy Ghost, he wrote a Jubilee Feast to all the Cardinals, including the new ones who had not yet received the hat, to which His Holiness had permitted such, but also to the new Emperor; know only the original following with the accompaniment. The Pope has dismissed all the old servants of the first Antechamber and appointed new ones in their place, though some who were retained by a particular Brief to that office continue. His Holiness has given the Government of the Arms of Ferrara, vacant by the death of the Marquis, to the Count of Godi. The Count, who has been declared by the Emperor as his perpetual Ambassador to offer the Tiara, if no Imperial Ambassador should be found in Rome, and that the Cardinal of Albani shall continue his Ambassadorship, and therefore in the coming month of November, when he shall proceed to the Viceroyalty of Naples, the Prince Borghese shall act in his stead. Some days ago there arrived at Civitavecchia some galleys of the King of Sardinia, on which the Count, Governor and Military of Genoa, handed over various amounts of gold money to the Commander of those galleys, which money was brought here by common decree, which the Kingdom of Sicily had handed over to the Emperor. Last Saturday the entire House of Conti betook itself to the Venetian Ambassador to thank the Republic of Venice that they have enrolled said House in the Golden Book of Venetian nobility. The Marquis Urbano Spada, who after several years served in the Militia of the Holy See, has died; now being set aside and after years without any employment, he has now received a company again from the Pope. His Holiness has assigned to Signor Carpoli, Secretary of Memorials, the task of examining all matters. Signor Palmoxiet, Secretary of Letters of Loreto, has been declared Nuncio in Switzerland, and His Excellency Luccheidni has obtained that Secretariat. Recently the Pretender, the Princesses Ursini and Piombino, together with several other persons of distinction, were entertained at a meal. The next morning the Cardinal Caraccioli took his leave audience with the Pope and departed that same day for his bishopric. The Spanish Cardinals Belluga and Borgia, as well as the Portuguese Aguia and Pereira, have each had a special audience with the Pope. It is assured that Cardinal Alberoni has departed from this city, and have conducted themselves very quietly, and one cannot know where His Eminence is residing, though it is believed he will soon return here. From the Pope's proceedings to be executed, which will likely take place shortly, one can discern that his affairs are well arranged, so that he will soon be on his way and yet come into consideration. Cardinal Schönborn, who shortly after the Pope's Coronation went to Frascati, entertained there on Thursday the other German Cardinals, Count Kindt, and many other persons at a meal. It is assured that the Pope will hold a Consistory on the first day of the Jubilee Feast to give the hat to the Cardinals who have not yet received it; and that His Holiness will then make a promotion of 2 Cardinals, which will be for the Church and Portugal, brother of the Holy Church and Signor Conti Bishop of Terracina, brother of His Holiness. This morning His Holiness, to move the People through his predecessor to Devotion and welfare, with His Eminence Our Culture, many Prelates, and a very great multitude of people, went in procession to the Church of Santa Maria Maggiore to obtain indulgence, and His Holiness celebrated Mass there in particular at the altar.

GENOA, 7 June. According to advices from Provence, at Toulon from the 10th to the 21st of the past month, 780 people were admitted to the hospitals. A ship from Anzio, loaded with wines, which wished to enter here, took course to depart from this coast on heavy paper; the sailing was therefore entrusted to the Commander of the English fortress, and in return it was answered that the Senate of the King of Great Britain shall cause the residence to be removed, which the noble Justiniani never paid, whereupon the Commander prepared to return to England, and it is hoped that he will exercise reprisals against our ships.

---

Venice

VENICE, 14 June. The Electoral Prince of Modena departed from here, having taken his way through Treviso to proceed thence to Italy, though it is not known whether he will remain there for some time. Thursday, Feast of the Holy Sacrament, a procession was held here as is customary, as also the following day, on the Feast of St. John, Protector of this city. His Grace the Archbishop Mocenichi has been accredited with the ordinary Ambassadorship to the Court of France.

---

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 11 June. Prince George of Hesse-Kassel, the King's brother, has postponed his journey to Frisia until the 11th of this month. The King has recovered to such good health that he will ride in public tomorrow. The Count Horn, the Great Britain Minister, and the Chancery Councillor Cochrane, have returned here with the combined War Fleet. Yesterday the British Minister departed again; all this week the Fleet of Lisnaap will sail toward the island of Åland to prevent further hostile designs. An express has returned from Petersburg yesterday with the Second Minute and news; although it is reported that the last-mentioned dispatch has brought tidings, yet nothing is spoken of except by those who arrive; and Monsieur Oxenstierna was also there; and recently the Russians and the Ministers of the Great Crown held various conferences.

---

Switzerland

GENEVA, 14 June. One sees here with great wonder that the neighboring states exclude us from community with their subjects; though one enjoys here a perfect freedom of religion, and that all possible precautions are taken to prevent communists against fugitive situations; one allows here no Mennonites, cattle, nor any goods to enter from Provence, Languedoc, or that part of the Dauphiné which borders on Provence. At Charleraine a lazaret has been opened for merchandise and other goods which come here from the outlying territory of this city, and at the River, begun by one of the Councils of the 25, or the Council of 200, to examine everything that enters with the utmost precision, to such a degree that even the first Sunday must submit to the most careful examination if he wished to walk but half an hour outside the city; and if this were not the case, his riding clothes would have to be examined likewise; if he wished to return into the city. Notwithstanding [text becomes unintelligible]

---

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

REGENSBURG, 19 June. Although the Imperial Diet continues on the customary days, little is changed in the matters at hand, because the Protestant Corps wishes to delay everything concerning this until they shall receive the Emperor's answer to their petitions, after which matters may well proceed, and by which time the Hanoverian ministers have not yet returned. Meanwhile the Catholic ministers are busily engaged in pressing the Abbot of Fulda into the regency of the Chamber of Wetzlar, in place of the Prince of Württemberg, which is not because the British hold him, but because the aforementioned Abbot and such a position has in the Imperial Diet, and the Protestants believe that this Prelate in the mentioned legal chamber would be harmful to them, and therefore they apply everything that is possible so that the Emperor may turn his eye to this matrimonial question, as was the case last, wherefore it is trusted that this matter could still have some movement. According to information from Paris, the Lutheran Church State has applied all diligence to also obtain redress of their grievances, for which the Lutherans have not yet given orders, and it appeared probable that they will not be given. The letter which the Protestant Corps wrote to the magistracy of Hamburg to prosecute a certain Estandy, against an infamous published libel against the customs [?], in the letter which the Protestant Corps [?] to the regents, has been delivered to the correspondent of that city and already sent thither. The past week some young English Jacobites have arrived here again to enter the convent of St. Mary, where there are already several, to be received into study. The presumed Baron Eychles, after the pronouncement of his sentence, departed from the city hall, so that the way to the Jacob Door (where he was released [?]) was so traveled that his blood ran down his back for the second day; yet it was merciful that the judge who convicted him showed him mercy, for which reason it is not believed that he will recover from it.

COLOGNE, 24 June. On Sunday the Electoral Prince's Chamberlain Bekker, who is staying here, suddenly passed away. The Papal Nuncio à Gradu of [?], who has prepared himself to take the journey to Rosifen, has already begun preparations to [text becomes unintelligible]

France

### TOULON, 16 MAY

From the second of this month until now, there has been a notable disturbance in this city and in Mediterranean affairs. The Cavalry is not well-positioned to remain in this place, which lacks all necessities in the countryside. There has been a theft of bread, which is why they prefer to borrow. Given the shortage of bread, since all the bakers have been emptied, a remedy has been found by breaking open the flour that is still in abundance here. Since the sickness has somewhat diminished among men, there is greater comfort. The Commissariat for bread is managed by the Seine, and they also bake it and transport it here by boat. A religious procession was conducted with the participation of many ecclesiastics, including the Jesuit fathers, the Jacobin monks, the Bishop, and the Mayor. The people, bound by their houses' condition, have viewed this procession from a distance. The procession has not been able to fulfill its purpose according to plan, and it is noted that from the beginning of the sickness until the end of this month, no deaths have occurred in this city.

### PARIS, 22 JUNE

The King has lately granted the Abbey of St. Averd to the Abbot of Rouen, of the Order of St. Benedict, [Editor's note: This refers to ecclesiastical appointments]. The Abbey has 2000 livres yearly income [for the Abbey with 4000 livres in total]. His Majesty has also granted a pension of 4000 livres to the Marquise of Lambert, known in the Recherches des Titres through the records from the beginning of the Leyden priestly light assembly. By this party, which has been resolved, the Jeune-Gyrd [?]; concerning the change of conditions and hereditary rights. The Duke of Nailles leads at St. Germain and Laye a magnificent laboratory for the teaching of Chemistry; it is believed that he will undertake experiments, and there one will attempt remedies for certain diseases, and there will have operations performed, which already beg curiosity. The Abbot Criquet of the March is to go out after some days have passed, which will be the first tradition, but what of that work in France during these days will be; also he recently departed, as withdrawn and expected to order, the life of St. Suber; Abbot of St. Denys, a former Minister of State and Regent of this Kingdom, under the young Louis VII.; this treatise is by this author of the Herren of Aberglaur. The Sieur de Fismni, VIT his certificate. At Bouder having departed to write the Mercere Galand, he made in January Christ a covenant with the British poet Funelier, and the letter of the Duynkenen Leeri to the boroughs Goodame in the Custulde, such that these Gentlemen there gave Jaelle Cokvell and they go to work. The Count de Rouih, who so every year in the East, in the Drodusta these Maucnge no longer willing to order for him themselves. In Telle neeren, and him this, as already done, abandoned, is in Vile, od von de Sva denne uttercke condition gathered by a Boat, which Danzig, Royal together with the Count given to restore. What this Boer the Court of the V 900 well succeeded, the Count already in the Plain of Grenelle have 11 livres Bilbao to go walking. The Flight of Rochefoucault is to day for a nation and fourth against the Parliament, in the chamber of St. Louis to gather, and honor is fourth. [?] the judicial at the done entitles. the advocates of the Cha Breilau; and [?]; and the Great Council; but this gathering is by the Order... and no more paid. de. Rotterdam [?]

---

The Netherlands

### DORDRECHT, 25 JUNE

The day before yesterday, there sailed from this city toward Suriname, led by Francois and Elisabeth, Benjamin Brown and Pieter van Galen, Corn. Lautenste of the Company of Dordrecht, the gentlemen at St. Pieter Gale, Corn. Lautenste of the Company of Dordrecht, merchants, Gerrit Staetsgaart, all three from Gerrit Schaap of Dordrecht; Willem Dartsdon, and the Skipper de Kadyx of the Elisabeth; and James, Jofeph van Schandandt; also present being the ship of Matthias Pieters from Sanderland: John Woolford, David Callop, and recently the engineering; James Boyes, all toward England.

### AMSTERDAM, 25 JUNE

At Bilbao have arrived Juriaen van het, from Ooltende Jan Moriarty; who had endured rough storms; and there were yet other ships for the Wake [?] Bouwen the Young lay and toward here [?] and the ship St. Perouwen the Young lay and toward here [?] and the ship St. Perouwen and others were lost. At Batavia lay dro Mordants etc., and sailed from there hither; there are no ships. Captain Romein de Fort has arrived for this city, but Captain Remein de Port [?]; and ships the Braamonte, Captain Mondoit, lay at Rochelle toward Guadaloupe; ship the Neptunus, Captain Piett; Matten Klyn, it is said. At Bordeaux has arrived the Hoffnuit, Captain Endras Blom, and English Jan Ghistiaanse Staate, at Liymouth [?]; the Dynkelaken Lendeet Loos, all of not. The ships the Constantia and the Golden Apple toward the Levantine go; Jantis Kokvelt and Pieter Jans, and St. Mauren to that the ships the Constantia and the Golden Apple toward the Levantine are towed. [?] are defended ships as also in the State fleet, vie, for the city, approximately 21 Merchant ships from Stockholm, Riga, the Commission of the EXCHANGE in Madera, and more others: Danzig, Königsberg, Stettin, Udert, and more others.

The Commission of the EXCHANGE in Madera, and more others. Danzig, Königsberg, Stettin, Udert, and more others. The Commission of the EXCHANGE is hereby at Madrid 90. Cadiz 113. Seville 111. Bilbao 95 and a quarter. Lisbon 44 and a quarter. Livorno 95. Genoa and a half. Paris consideration and a quarter. London 34 Sch. 4 and a half gt. Danzig 230 Florins gt. Breslau 3; and a half agile St. Hamburg 4 and a half agile St. Antwerp 5 pet Ghent 5 and a quarter. Runstel 3 and a quarter. Zealand 1 and a quarter. de. Rotterdam a half agile. The Agio of the Bank 3 and a half agile a quarter. de. The Action of the O.I.C. are 779. W. K. 350 gabotie 300 covered. The English Effects have no full price.

### THE HAGUE, 26 JUNE

The Gentlemen States of Holland and West-Friesland have again gathered today. It is thought that their Ed. Gr. Mog. shall convene the ordinary Assembly in the year this coming Saturday. The Gentlemen States of the Southern and Northern Quarter have been in conference together. The Herr [?] has departed toward the Province of Groningen. The Great-British Minister the Sieur d'Ayroles and the Elector-Cologne Minister the Baron Godeneau have been with the Gentlemen of the Government.

[...] hereby also an alike, which few chapters to the fall of der-related are all those same capitals, which after the first of September ago the drawing of the Obligations to pay, unless the Great ones wish to let them stay; all kinds of set circumstances, which those capitals are annually to be made known to the States' Treasurer, and all others, [?] have not yet been arranged.

the 8 July 1721. Afternoon at 5 o'clock, in the Shop of the Sale around 3000 or 3500 sacks of Cadiz salt, which has been recovered from the sea, from the province, and will be sold at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 18 July 1721, on the Singel, a considerable party of Linen and other goods-tage, therefore in Slooteren, geldergen in Holland and the Chapter Utrecht. ### CADIZ, 5 JUNE

### PARIS, 25 JUNE

According to the Palace of the Turk new given having the his darkness Hotel stood to pass dark to be seen old, and how is cell in her foresight go geo-most become, and they go then to Peanteurter in the white ge-sy their folk aoe d Eveueit from in sieaen service Troeseelle of St. is one thereinlike and well would is book passed, and it [?] h-in nie can uphold stren toward the remaining Alstou on the place of the Lieutenants still beyond the Postkisel, and to themselves in these French poor some wise, of which one Sruardes is A Keyen on the co of Forus, and also some oos tuls, depicting more nes Amboldaden the Gronquillars of Forus, and also some so tiuls, depicting more nes Amboldaden the Gronatillars and Kliguiens Master zu aisanks on the Drabcute yn ded zuigulich appointed. ANDRE DE KLOPPER, on the Breederlraat The 17 June 1721. With Privilege.

The 27th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
