Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 30th of June, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 3 June. Because the loading of the galleons is proceeding so slowly, it has therefore been postponed by at least 15 days, and thus until well into the end of this month; moreover, the new Spanish specie is being distributed much more slowly than before. The following vessels have arrived in this Bay: the Africa, with Captain Charles Brilock from Gibraltar; the Chinese, Captain Arthur del Girno; the Spanish Vivifier, Captain Piercer Molenaar from Amsterdam; the Maria, Captain George Morris from London; the Thomas, Captain Robert Mill from the Canaries; and on the 2nd, the Hamburg ship Anna Maria Galy, Captain Fredrik Meyer from Málaga, armed with 12 guns and 16 men. This ship, the day before, near Cape Trafalgar on this side of the Strait, came into combat with an Algerian pirate ship with 28 to 30 guns and 240 to 250 men. The engagement lasted from approximately ten o'clock in the afternoon until late evening. The pirate ship lost 50 to 60 men, but the swivel guns inflicted such excellent damage that all those who boarded the ship with their lives, though the survivors nevertheless departed with the ship, which was heavily damaged and riddled with shot. It is noted to the credit of those on board that no one was killed or wounded in this engagement. According to the captain's report, the Turkish rover would have been unscathed by the four guns that he had mounted in the stern of the ship, which were loaded with double shot and cannister. A Dutch boy, who was being carried away in slavery on the Turkish pirate ship, ran away with several men to the Spanish ship during the turmoil. From Tétouan, by report dated 22 May, word has come that the Great-British Minister, who had arrived three days earlier, is soon to depart for Morocco to conclude peace with the Moroccans. From Tétouan, two Turkish merchant ships set out to sea once more.

MADRID, 10 June. The Bibliothecary Minister, who has spent several days at the court at Aranjuez and conducted there confidential meetings with the ministers of this court, has returned to this city yesterday. The present ministry has sent back the express [messenger] that he received some days ago from his court, yesterday evening, as one is assured, with the project of a treaty between this kingdom and Great Britain regarding Gibraltar. A minister on behalf of the Republic of Genoa has come to this court. The King has appointed Don Manuel de Samaniego y Saca as Ambassador to Saint Petersburg, and Colonel Isquierdo Zeton as the King's Steward at Alicante.

France

PARIS, 23 June. Wednesday the Curé of St. Sulpice proceeded to the Palais Court, where the Council of Cardinals is being held; the event fortunately had a good [...]

Paris, 25 June. Wednesday the Curé of St. Sulpice went to the Palace of the Tuileries, and having notified His Majesty that the Holy Sacrament was to pass by the great Hotel the following day, he requested that the Ambassador give such orders to his Domestics that the Procession in passing receive proper respect and no mockery; and His Majesty assured the Curé that he had already set all necessary precautions in place, and that he himself with all his people would be at the windows of his Palace. The Procession of St. Sulpice, which took place on Thursday, was one of the finest and most well-ordered that one has ever seen before, with its various displays brightening the Street of Turenne, then proceeding toward the magnificent Altar that was erected in front of the Choir of the Church, in the place of Julsbourg. The King was on the balcony above the Portal and witnessed the Procession coming before him in this fine order, which was not disturbed by the great multitude of people, inasmuch as several Detachments of the Guards were stationed at two points on the passages. When the Procession had entered the Church, the King went to the other end and the Balcony, turned over the Altar, and the Court, which was very brilliant, took their places on the two Wings of the Balcony. The Choir had the most splendid decoration of that time, as one had selected the lovely Hymns of the Ambachtskingen of the Crown; and among these lovely Confetti-like [displays], one saw those with the previous procession in the Church of Alexander, the Family, the Religion of Porus I, and also some of Confitantie, the Paris Leon, and of Attila, displaying more of the fruits of War. The Ambassador of the Oratorians, together with some family, witnessed this religious march from the windows of his Palace, and the attentiveness of the Slaves of His Excellency was so great that they remained kneeling through the Diadochtal prayers, and all proceeded with great foresight. The Cardinal of Polignac and several Bishops performed the Vermitried. One has furthermore proceeded in the Procession; nothing was forgotten that this popular Märtschalcarge might louder and rejoice in Byzanien; there were verses and the sweet symphony of Keith Hobing, and 24 Incense-Bearers, which at once rose into the air with wonderful effect. The Turkish Ambassador made every effort to show himself in great visibility over all that this Procession had displayed. In the evening the King heard singing at the Coutubrugres, [...] and there was such Mystery in the Sanc zym [?], and other religious people were also admitted; the King found much charm in the intercourse of a Religious [...] Marichtchte the Casenrit in their Soul Legacy. The Bequeli, which the Boat of the Foutre has presented at the Edge to pursue Guillaate to pursue the Proceedings of Parliament; the Proceedings of Parliament were approved, thereupon [...] of and the Proceedings of Parliament were approved, thereupon [...] of that Dalog previous Vigag confronted against 4 [...]

Great Britain

LONDON, 24 June. According to the latest Report of the Secret Committee, it appears that in the name of Weymouth 43,000 pounds have been found, to wit: 20,000 pounds in the name of Fudget, and 1,000 pounds in the name of Stroud, all being Brokers. That Report further states that Mrs. Summon was examined, and the Account of the sold Shares having been shown to him, he said that although he was making up that Account under the direction of Knight to present it to the House of Commons, Knight had read him those Sums in a large Book; that he truly believed the Sum and Names in the Book had been correctly set down, and that those Persons had indeed retained those Shares for themselves. On Saturday, those of the Lower House gave the second Reading of the Bill to continue the Import on Malt, Cider, &c. for the service of the current Year, referring it to a Committee of the whole House, and ordered the same to take due care in preparing a Clause for the relief of Merchants. Leffingham, 14,000 pounds in the name of Fudget, and 18,000 pounds in the name of Stroud; all being Brokers. That Report further states that Mrs. Summon being examined, and the Account of the sold Shares shown to him, said that as he was drawing up that Account under the direction of Knight to present it to the House of Commons, Knight had read him those Sums in a large Book; that he truly believed the Sum and Names in the Book had been properly set down, and that those Persons had indeed retained those Shares for themselves. On Saturday, those of the Lower House gave the second Reading of the Bill to continue the Import on Malt, Cider, &c. for the service of the current Year, referring it to a Committee of the whole House, and ordered the same to take due care in preparing a Clause for the relief of Merchants who bring in the Olds in small Parcels. It is reported that some Gentlemen would speak for the House, which the consideration of the Losette Committee postponed for several days; in which same Committee, sitting in grand Committee over the Bill for the relief of those who lost in the South Sea Company, that part concerning the allocations was examined; by virtue of which was assigned to the former Chancellor Grigsby from his presented State [?]; and after debate, it was voted by 113 votes that he should receive what he possessed on 18 October 1718, being the time when he became Chancellor of the Exchequer; some wished to grant him only what he possessed in the Year 1714, when he was made Treasurer of the Fleet; and postponed the consideration of the accusation against the Gentleman William Garew until next Saturday. The House, having sat in grand Committee, again held a heated Debate over the eleven Millions which the South Sea Company had lent out on its Shares and Subscriptions; and upon a proposal of Knight John Eyles, sub-Governor, it was voted by 114 votes that those who have their Shares or Subscriptions pledged should be free of their Engagements, provided they pay ten per cent to the said South Sea Company, and the Report on this was deferred to today.

The Lords then heard the Advocates for the two Alliance Companies and the East India Company, regarding the Clause in the Bill concerning the East India Company, which it is not to be allowed all Contracts after the 24th, &c. [date], to give Money on Bottomry on Ships sailing from the East to the East Indies; whereupon the Advocate for the Alliance Companies objected that for the said Company the Folio [?] should be believed; one should not take it from her without some compensation; and that although these Contracts were already concluded, it would not yet break the East-enders from receiving Money today; against which the Advocate for the East India Company remarked that the nature of Bottomry is that Merchants, who have money to fit out ships, take it out on Bottomry; that those of Ostend rely wholly on the Credit of Money from England; that if these Hindrances were to come to pass, their Trade would be completely and entirely ruined; that such unworthy Trade being subject to prohibition to the detriment of England, he nevertheless insisted that the same be preserved and not to the detriment of England, upon which the Lords permitted the said Bill to proceed in Committee without any amendment. Today the House passed the Report of the Resolutions, which yesterday in grand Committee had approved itself by 150 against 127 votes; and subsequently decided the advancement of the Bill to continue the Malt, Cider, &c. on Thursday to proceed; and tomorrow that for the relief of those who lost in the South Sea Company. The three House-men this reported that the House had postponed; with which the Gentlemen wish to receive the remaining Report of the said Committee; which was approved on the following Friday, the said Committee examined [?] in M[?] Cobb Childe.

England

London, Sea Trade: Reports continue steady; Mr. Wilkinson has 356 subscribers. The matter was heard in the London Toll House Hall; also heard before the Committee for investigating the Authors, Printers, and Publishers of seditious papers. It was requested that the sitting in July be adjourned, and Kensington was preferred for receiving the King there; it is thought he will remain there until the sitting of Parliament is concluded. It is believed that His Majesty will also go to Hampton Court to spend the summer. On Friday, the legitimate son of Lord Carteret was born, and on the 23rd of July he was christened. On Saturday, the Directors of the South Sea Company made it known that, given the regularity of the shareholders of the [?], the books should be closed, and the time for reopening shall be specified. Yesterday a proclamation was published, wherein 280 pounds reward is offered to the Doctor Gaillard, associate of Bishop Mill, and Nathaniel Wilkinson for apprehending [suspects]. It has also been made known that the King has pardoned [?] and Johann Perron, who are also said to have been guilty of the murder of three domestics of Lord Polwarth; [details follow] with a reward of 100 pounds for whoever delivers one of the murderers to justice. Some days ago, Mr. Belly, one of the Overseers of the Toll, seized a shipment containing 1,000 pounds of tea, which is believed to have come from Osfield.

P.S. A committee of three Gentlemen at this Court has also brought the Project of Convention between Colonel Stanhope and the Minister of Commerce of Spain, settled concerning Gibraltar and the Committee of the English in Africa, on which this Court is very content.

Stock Exchange: The shares of the Bank are at 127; East India Company 113; South Sea 312; the Exchequer Bank 113; Lottery and Bank Annuities 96 and a half; Mittos per Cent 91; Africa Company 28; Bonds and Lord Alban 6; J.B. 16 and five-eighths.

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 23 June. Yesterday the following ships arrived at this city: the Scherpsheer, the Liefde, Jan Hend., the Hoop, Gezegend Liefde, Ulke Sikkes, and both the Eendracht, Hymen Verhoeven, and the Donkelaer; and some departed: the St. Augustinus, Augustijn Neyt, to Ostend; the St. Michael, Adriaan de Lange, to Newport; the Diligence, [?] Obideen Waleren, to Liverpool; the Exchange, Christopher Vor, to Sunderland; and departed yesterday: the Holy Trinity, Jan Gielinger, the St. Jacob, Jacobus Leakount, the St. Pierre, François Valcro, Vlart, and the St. Jan, the Robertin, François Heye, all to Ostend.

AMSTERDAM, 24 June. From Danzig, after their voyage there, sailed Dirk Haak, Anna Houck-Villers, Ann Monffoort, Corn. Com. Tom, and others. At Martinique the ship la Constantia, Captain Payor, from Bordeaux, has arrived, which departed from the ship le Pria, Captain [?], also from Bordeaux, proceeding thither. From Blaye departed the Eenhorn, Roelof Klye, the Sint Ann, Corn. Pietcels, and J. de Kundtren bound there, and the Maan to St. Malo; and 4 Cruisers, Jochem Lenduroff, to the East Indies, and the Lanie, Jan Will. Greet, to Harfleur. According to the latest letters from London, approximately 3 small boats have been sent around to New York. In Zeeland, the ship of Captain Piet. Groen-Rmuffen has arrived from Guinea, also bringing 100 marks of goods. Yesterday, from this merchandise ship (which was called a Lorenaiser) it was learned that from here it was dispatched on the Guinea Coast and Captain Daniel Sanders, who also departed from there with a ship, has already left that place for Angola. At St. Maarten came Jan Meter from Bergen, Halo Tom. Jacobus the Boatswain the Bndi, Willemina, Jan Adriaanso, and at Wolgall Corn. Hend., all bound here. Yesterday departed from the Vlie to the sea. From Duyne departed the Eendoorn, the Vlieg, and several others. [Details of other departures follow.]

CURRENCY EXCHANGE TODAY: - Madrid 99, Radix 114 and three-fourths; Seville 91 and one-fourth; Bilbao 95 and one-fourth; Lisbon 11 and 10, 3 August; Venice 81 and one-fourth 332; Livorno 93; Genoa 95 and one-fourth; Geneva 94; Paris 96 and one-fourth; London 14 Schillings; Frankfurt 50 and one Ayd.; Danzig 130 and one-half; Breslau 95 and one-fourth; Hamburg 32 to one-fourth; St. Antwerp à per Cent 1 Gerst and one-fourth; Brussels à and one-half; Zeeland 1 and one-fourth; Rotterdam one-fourth. - Bank Agio: one and one-fourth, 3 August. - Acma of the O.L. [?]: 99 to 100 and 1; 104.

Municipal Announcement of Nijmegen: The Mayors, Aldermen, Senators and Council of the City of Nijmegen hereby announce that no city loan having been delivered for fifty years, it being resolved that all of these [matters] of this year shall provide for 2 Kapitulein at four percent interest henceforth, the same before the first of September of this year to the City Treasurer must be paid, held firm in the Reduction at four percent per year.

AUCTION ANNOUNCEMENTS:

Hendrik Altes, broker, shall sell in Amsterdam on Tuesday the first of July a lot of 8 calves, Maagent, Serfym Merschaelen 5c. and an Kerefschap 5c.

Dirck in the Apotheek or Ascaldo brokers shall sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, 1 July 1721, Dykstraat in the Old Burgh, a party of extra fine new Vatoas-Tobacco.

Moleken-bieder shall sell in Amsterdam on Wednesday, 1 July 1721, a large party of fine English hosiery, silk and satin stockings, silk gloves, curtains and silences, glass, coral, scissors and other cutlery, clasps, cords, sweepers, silk and other goods; further a party of English tinware, and chinaware, cinnamon, azine vinegar canisters, and other items belonging to a service; everything shall be [viewable] 2 days before said broker, on the Place, carefully by the Regulations, on Wednesday, 1 July 1721, in the morning at precise time.

At Leiden, at Gerrrryn Alkzboam, Widow Lucas de Lenter, shall sell and to the highest bidder sell Houses at about 100 Orangeflowers of different sorts, likewise various [items]; details in the Catalogue thereof to be seen, which can be obtained at Amsterdam Boitet, Rotterdam Eelman, Dord van Braam, and Leiden Vermey.

Various, Bruel, Jan Rickevaleer, and Coorn. Hoos brokers shall sell in Amsterdam, in the Dykstraat in the Old Burgh, a party of extra fine new Vatoas-Tobacco; Korsia and Red Molken-bieder.

Nikolaas Evd, broker, shall sell in Amsterdam on Thursday, 3 July 1721, Burgh, a ship's cargo of extra fine Virginia Bay-Tobacco; leaves laying before and the Leentuia, under the house of Jacob Lasmond.

P. Rainer, D. Rakels, Abraham van Dordella, and Hendrik Linquet, brokers, shall sell in [Amsterdam] on Wednesday the 9 July in the evening at [time], in the Burgh on the Graepel, an extra party belonging to the Outward-Ship the Fortuin, Captain Jan Pincisele, consisting of Brittania, Cammedoek, white linens and fishes, finished and striped bedclothes, [?], Parufflory, Verrens, Mora, Moertwijge, and all other items, [which] by note days before, and on the Market-day alone can be viewed.

---

Italy

MILAN, 10 June. According to letters from that place, with the Archduke, and Count Salvatio would continue as secretary of the Church; that a certain Ecclesiastic would remain in Bailingcap; the Dean in Plijns would proceed against particular [matters] as before; and the Emperor Comacchio to retire, and [would] conduct [?] to the Pope and Emperor; [some] time with passages and Emperor [?] [some] time in all [approximately, so Emperor some time shall...] [text becomes unclear]

The high Government against malversation and is punished; upon which Gravene in [...][...] appeared [?] would give; and moreover, that Ferrara well again at the House [?] Government Albiceti in further be examined concerning malversation.

Germany & Adjacent Lands

VENA, 18 June. According to some letters, a Turkish Priest, in the governance of another Ministry, had opportunity at the Keyzersberg... to attend the measures for liberating the lands of Germany; [the] weapons were laid down, so that the Emperor would be sent; but their Majesties would not be punished further.

SCHAFFHAUSEN, 11 June. The letters from Provence bring news that the city of Toulouse, as it was already, in the living east; in the Hospital there breaks out in the whole other goods; which moved [them] in all the houses of that city [?] to be done. At Atlas the inhabitants were advised, since they in the city were renewed by the Graft, and there outside by [text unclear].

REGENSBURG, 21 June. The announcement which the Palatine Minister made at the Protocol, concerning the restoration of the Protestant Religion in the domains of that [territory] from 1746 of the Batavian [?], gives less and less satisfaction to the Protestants; and it is now more than clear to see that the Elector's promises but partially executed, and various points still unfinished.

MAINZ, 21 June. By the present good season at hand, the wine harvest in this land in short [time] has been exceptionally abundant.

FRANKFURT, 21 June. Word is that the Margrave of Onolsbach will go to Holland, and from there to England. Kingly Lords in the inspectorate of Hessen-Darmstadt to Embs departed, of which the Count of Hanau in short will be expected [to follow].

COLOGNE, 21 June. The Pontifical envoys of the Count of Archido, who once made a journey to Münster, have here yesterday returned; the reason for this journey is kept secret, and it is believed that he has brought news of the Bishopric of Liège. The Princess via la Toutch [?] arrived here from above at Wass and brought [?] to her side for council. So quick the rumor goes [around].

---

With Privilege.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 30th of june, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 79.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 2nd of July, 1721
Italy

MILAN, 10 June. According to letters from Modena, reports suggest that the Dowager Duchess of Modena, with the Duke her Principal [Editor's note: likely referring to the reigning Duke], would remain in exile; the Duke would have yearly access to the ordinary Council. It has been arranged, as if the Emperor would restore Comacchio to the Papal Chair, under the condition that a sum of money would be given; and then another sum would be due if the Emperor were to go to war with the Turks at an earlier time; concerning this matter in the Congress it was settled that Ferrara might well be restored again to the House of Este. The High Government receives daily in these matters examinations regarding Malfeasance, by which they are hindered in the restoration of the Land's Finances: it is reported that some in matters of debt have been named; and others who held office would be dismissed; but their sentences must first be resolved by decree.

Germany & Neighboring Realms

VIENNA, 18 June. According to certain advices from Constantinople, an Express from Egypt, in the [?] High presence of the Great Turk, and certain other Ottoman Ministers, made an Address, to exhort them to the defense of [?], to through [?] the fulfillment of the Treaties, [...] [text continues with diplomatic and military news from various European locations and the Ottoman Empire]

REGENSBURG, 21 June.

Furthermore we are informed from the Bavarian Ministry that the Count of Königsfeld, on both sides placed himself in this City, and conferred shortly thereafter with the Count of the Caesarean of the Roman Imperial proper Ministers; who very important proposals were made with respect to [?] extensive negotiations for the restoration of the Religious disputes; among other things that the Lord Merville Cæsant that Head-article of the disputes over the tidings received, through Representation against the K.V.D. of the Palatinate, this to the Agent Van Sacom executed, through their at his K.M. own hand signed, thereby favorably [?] had, the contents of such discharge further used in the reading, and upon finding that in equity of the matter was [?], so that of the Religious belonging Corporate wanted in [?] not, so that of the Religious belonging in the Palatinate; a well-founded allonage might obtain: the Lord

---

No. 79

Wednesday's Courant

van Reek departed thence to the alike of the Waters toward Swabneck, so that with the last Flight no tidings of him came. Defeat on this shore, and therefore had to flee, is not through [?] Spain passed, being provided with a Letter from the commandant Troops of that Crown to employ.

DANZIG, 24 June. The King and the Prince, who had returned here. From Warsaw comes news that the differences have been settled; and that Prince Sangusco will continue in the bewijd [?] until the general Polish Diet, which will decide it.

LEIPZIG, 2 July. The Count Flemming, who once toward Dinsburc had come thereat: men [?], who find themselves in the Königergen. The Baron von Regenski, esta. Envoyé of France, who had come from Warsaw to Dresden, has returned thence to Paris.

France

MARSEILLE, 12 June. Health increases here from day to day so strongly, that already public Functions without interruption will shortly be resumed and restored. During the Sickness here of the Rowers on the Galleys, as much among the Troops, and Galley-Boys, who cared for and buried the Sick, more than 3,000 have died. According to the tidings the Evil has abated so forcefully, that one deems it sufficiently overcome.

PARIS, 27 June. It is said that the Duke du Maine will be restored again in all his Honors and Dignities; that he will carry out the Prize as Substitute of the upbringing of the King; and that he will be restored in quality as Prince of the Blood: it is assured us that he has again begun to execute his Function as Grand Master of the Artillery, and Colonel General of the 100 Swiss: it is added thereto, that the Prince of Dombes and his Count d'Eu his Sons, will tomorrow marry the Mesdemoiselles de Montpensier and Valois, Daughters of the Duke of Orléans. There is also spoken of the King marrying out with the old Daughters the Prince of Wales, Son of the King of Great Britain not: but all these Marriages are still so uncertain, that one-third should not affix itself, in order therewith more rapidness of speech to be able to speak. On the day of St. Jean, the Turkish Ambassador kept on the Eastern manner, the Lord Coulier des Fortes, and other Persons of distinction, to the number of 15, at a splendid Meal: 2 days thereafter the Lord de Coulier des Fortes, said Ambassador and others invited themselves, according to their Custom, in that time of the drinking of Chocolate, Coffee, and the smoking of Tobacco; eating not foot to be done; subsequently they go sleep. After midnight risen, to make a roasted meal, and go then again do and eat; it is assured us that his Excellency at the ending of this Valley toward Constantinople is standing to return; he has seen the Galleries and the Coffee of Miette, and viewed all the Chambers. The Mr. Baitley, who since 18 Years has been Curate at Versailles, is sent as Superior of the Seminary to Arras, and the Lord Buitly Coule of the Invalids, has in his place become Curate at Versailles again, without one knowing the reason for this change. The Duke of Orléans, who had amused himself some days at St. Cloud, came yesterday evening here back again. The 21st of this month passed through this City the Secretary of the Great Britain Ministry the Colonel Stanhope to Madrid, going toward London: it is related that he went to the British Court bearing the heard Treaty between these 2 Crowns. It is said that the Duke of Villeroy, Son of the British Duke of Villeroy, by Survivance his Father's place as Governor of the King, by his Majesty, will perform. Since one is not permitted any Seizures, which one has ordered against Debtors, on Sunday, or other Holy days; in the Houses on Street, against their execution to bring, so it is practiced that since some time, in favor of some debt claimers Letters de Cachet have been granted, to those which to them are owed where they might find them, to execution to lay. By the 24th, that the Son of the Duke of Grammont will obtain the Survivance as Governor of the Ellas, which the Marshal de Huxelies still holds. The rumor will that the Abr Thelu Secretary of State will become, in place of the Count de Marçous, but that certain Lord, is this Master alterhood Secretary of State has become. The Process between the Count d'Eyreux and the Duke of Albert is settled; the last stands off from the Usurpation, which he made on the Land-pond of Tancarville. The Duke Regent does on the City Hall place by contract the 1000 Livr. which the Duke of Albert had configured for that Usurpation. The Intendant of Clermont has received orders from the Court to betake himself toward the high-Auvergne, to make there the necessary Prosecutions, and to provide everything in state; one has also sent an Express, to bring orders in Provence and other besieged [?] and makes that a great Quantity at Beaucaire shall have arrived, which is sent from Auvergne, to those of Provence there with to aid. Nica Mica [...] an Article of the King's Council of State of the 17th of this month, concerning the justified Adrian, and Tithes of Kent-Adrian of which there [...] Certificate made are hands of Comp. of the Indies, which by the British held are in hands of

France

PARIS, 17 June. According to the latest letters from Rome, Cardinal de Rohan was on the verge of bringing great glory to the French there at Naples. His agreeable manner, outstanding deportment, and generous expenditures made him beloved by both great and small. The Count of Chalaix, brother of the Count of Bourbon Buffet, went out last Tuesday evening at 7 o'clock from the house of the Carthusians, where he had been residing on the Rue Saint-André, intending to take a walk, but nothing has been heard from him since. It is most perplexing what might have become of him. These gentlemen of the Buffet household are all descended from a Count of Bourbon, who was fathered by the Bishop of Liège. On the same night, at 4 in the morning, Cartouche with great violence broke into the residence of the Marquise of Pont de Verz, living on the Rue l'Aliètte in the Faubourg Saint-Germain, where he stole many articles of clothing and 2 weeks' worth of ligatures. [?] Madame Damit is presently much burdened with three lawsuits, which consume the greatest part of her income, and which have forced her to take a modest dwelling in the middle of the city in order to be at hand for the solicitation of her affairs, though she comes and goes from her former lodging. Bank notes stand at 92, and shares at 49 livres 10 sols. A certain Advocate from the former time has protested that the Legitimate Value of the Bank notes was in fact nothing, and therefore they could not validate them in this sense of borrowing for genuine value. Madame de Saint-Condé has been taken to the city, and the death of Madame de Bohain, sister of the Prince of Rohan, is reported. The illness of the Duke of Bouillon is no better, and for several days now nothing but death has been anticipated; his children are held in recollection at his bedside. There is talk of the creation of a new office of General Treasurer of France in favor of the Count de Hérens, but this as yet lacks foundation.

The Abbey of Chalis, which the King has given to the Count of Clermont, is situated at the end of the park at Chilly, and is consequently very well suited for hunting. This benefice has been burdened with the following pensions: namely, to Monsieur d'Ancuil Leru, of the Count of Clermont 1400 livres; to Monsieur Guichon Piecepourrar of the Count of Clermont 1300 livres; to Monsieur Fontia the Son 1300 livres; to Monsieur de Farges and Clermont 1500 livres; to Monsieur Bontac Preceptor of the Count of Clermont 1400 livres; to Monsieur Bontac Almoner of the Duke of Bourbon 1500 livres; and 600 livres to Monsieur Claudius of the Chaplain of the Duke of Bourbon; and 130 in the weekly distribution of Saint-Sulpice, where he is preparing to take up the clerical habit. There is talk of a marriage between the Marquis and Nesle Mailly, the daughter of King Manuel, [?] who will bear his title once His Majesty is crowned. A certain Pietidaen, who was going to take the waters, has also been much involved in the verification of the paper, for which reason the matter of the Commissions has increased: [?] it is said that the grace period will not be extended; nevertheless one finds many holders who do not delay in bringing their papers; since they have already brought their effects for verification, they will finally be dealt with equally. It is said that the former Directors of the East India Company are in tight correspondence.

PARIS, 17 June. The day before yesterday, the King went out walking toward the Library. The Dowager Duchess of Orleans, who is accustomed to coming to this city every Wednesday, did not appear on that occasion.

England

LONDON, 27 June. A certain vessel with dispatches from Sicily concerning the matter of the third Naval Wardenship will continue for as long as may be necessary, saying that in this circumspection the proceedings of the Baraque [?] held in a ship under the two Procurators heard this proceeding from Palermo. The Congregation has established [?] issued for a payment of the second installment, in full according to his faculties relieved, issued in the ordinary chapel, being the ordinary [?] and precedent held. No Account of the Capitulation of the Countess, regarding which the Cardinals de Rohan and others are to thank one another with a compliment, 18 to give thanks for the honors.

France (Continued)

PARIS, 27 June. The Secretary of the British Ambassador, Colonel Stanhope, who had been specified here from Madrid toward London, has also brought the stipulated Convention between the Kings of Spain and Great Britain, whereby among other things it is provided that the Contractor [?] the King of Great Britain shall restore to the Spaniards the Gibraltar and the Castles [?] by the ships which have been seized in the strait near Algeciras, and by the precedence of Great Britain shall depend, etc. All the general grievances which have arisen from a private negotiation, which have proceeded from the Briton, to the detriment of the Emperor and the [?], are entirely vain, and through malicious invention.

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 15 June. On Thursday, Milord Forbes (whom the Emperor sent to the King of Great Britain to govern specially those affairs which His Imperial Majesty, toward the Admiralty, and there traveling toward) now departed: this Lord conducted himself as his subject Leunis [?] complimented him. That day, Monsieur Fellen, Resident of the State of the United Netherlands at this court, gave a dinner, with two foreign Ladies, the table which Ladies the Vries [?] had provided from the Marquise de Pitt.

The Count of Windgraes is still restraining himself; and as there from these Commissions [?] by the Field Marshal Count of Vles are put together, one finds such Commission [?] by the Field Marshal Count of Vles with daily held [?]. Notwithstanding the grievances against the set terms of Thursday, something else was not pursued, which a burgher in this city had noticed, has committed, where buildings found we see money expended; but the testimony against him is not yet complete. On Saturday the Thursday was something else, and on 21 June the Bishops will hear, the wound of the nose and both ears being [?], and the other hand through the spirochete. [?]

GRAVAMINA [Grievances] of some Gentlemen of the Government regarding the Princes William and William and Family: Sunday of the late Prince William and [?], it being by the authorization of his now Lord Father, shall all that is needed be created, held and also brought, and that for respectability there opened: the last penny Pitis, who as General in this Chronicle of the all, where the passage [?]; Volunteers has these there Bishops in London, where the Cardi[nal].

PARIS, 27 June. The day before yesterday the King went out walking toward the River. The Dowager Duchess of Orleans, who is accustomed to coming to this city on Wednesday, has not yet appeared there, but the Duke Regent has visited her at Saint-Cloud, as has [?] he at Chelles been, and intends to betake himself hither tomorrow. The Duke of Richelieu continues his intention with Mademoiselle de Chatenton; these days he has been walking with her in the Tuileries, and has had magnificent clothing made for himself. One sees that the Prince of Conti considers himself before the Duke du Maine, and that he has dined with the Duchess his wife. The Marquis de la Fare has been separated from his wife in bed and board; and they shall both remain dwelling in their Palace; the Marquis, who will still maintain her, has made her for that a Petition of 1700 livres. The Abbé Petit Pied, Author of the recently printed [work] against the Accommodation, has been forced to go into hiding, together with Monsieur Feydoul, who compiled the Beautiful Advertisement; that is at the head of the said Treatise: it is said that they are sought for by Lettres de Cachet; and one begins more and more to see that this matter, seemingly, cannot be concluded. It is said that Monsieur Mabou, Bishop of Aleth, was a few days ago trodden upon in his stable by a Mule-Ass, which a Mare wanted to cover, which he wanted with force to prevent. It is confirmed that at Brest three ships are being armed to transport the Turkish Ambassador to [?] London, which will be commanded by the Marquis de Cagni, and that they will depart on 15 July toward the port of Ceste. In the blockade concerning the whole, held between the 21st and 25th, stood [?], the Ambassador of the Porte. Regarding the verification of the Paper Effects, one wonders not less at the exth. [?] in the work; as the excellent order, and one cannot refrain from saying that it is an arrangement worthy of having come forth from the great talents of Messieurs Paris. The shares are today at 56, and the Bank notes of 1000 at 91 to 92 livres. For several days now Parliament has been taking information about the Duel of the heirs of Breteuville and Gravelles, which last one now assures oneself of.

LONDON, 27 June. On Wednesday there arose in the House a very severe debate over the clauses in the Bill for the [?] of the established [?], which clause the said Directors had altogether. In the House sitting, or in the government office from [?], and the matter is necessary thereon; but some wished that one should subject the most criminals of the least: but the clause was defeated 151 to 96 votes. This ordinance of the community regarding the clause for the continuance of the Impost on the Malt; these first both of Capt. Perry, to such recommended for a [?]. H. Ost heard the clause as security of the common Bill. He. Oel is of the North [?], according to the Parliamentary Act, accordingly the last upon the Malt established from 23 June 1719 to [?] 1721, on the Register of the Land Tax for this year. IV. To authorize His Majesty that he dispose of the surplus therefrom to persons of needy situation to relieve: for the House, in great committee yesterday, which till [?], some said [?] has the House in last report heard from the [?], with whom Monsieur Hampdon was Treasurer of the Knight Harcourt Matters. Receiver General of London and [?], together with several other Receivers, are accused; of this report was set for next Wednesday, then they also read upon this to the King, requesting concerning a [?] agreement with Sweden, and 4200 p[ounds] the owners of 2 out of Turkey and the distant places coming from the E.I.C. 157, Z.Z. 133, Million Bank, etc. [?]

---

By order of the Noble and Venerable Gentlemen Burgomasters of the City, there shall be held on the eleventh of July 1721, and the 14th following thereto. On Wednesday evening at 6 o'clock, July 2, 1721, the [?] will be auctioned, on the Dike Street in the Old Burgh, a parcel of good quality [?] between the Corties and Red Mill Fences.

And on Friday, the second of July 1721, in the afternoon at [?] o'clock, in the courthouse, there shall be publicly auctioned a well-equipped Fluyt ship, named the Coat of Arms of [?], hollow and the Hold: for the [?], the Deck at Board 6 feet 5 inches, feet, with its 8 [?], standing and running rigging, as at the ship [?] desiring, [?] shall apply themselves to Willem Pasman, Notary in Dordrecht aforesaid, and D. Bontekoning, Broker.

At Amsterdam, at the White Swan, there shall be sold a good quantity of Northern wood wares, consisting of all kinds of beams, Mast-beams, Ridge- and Post-beams, Hay- and Gutter-Malts, and 150 [?].

STOCKHOLM, 18 June.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 2nd of july, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 80.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 4th of July, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, 10 June. The day before yesterday a courier arrived from Vienna with letters for this government and for that of Sicily; the latter were forwarded with a dispatch. It is understood that the present viceroy in the viceroyalty of Continental [territory] will soon be clarified, as one will learn who is designated to succeed the Prince of Borghese in this dignity. Tuesday evening the procession of the Papal [feast] of Calvagiere took place here at Barcelona. At the end of this procession was, according to custom, a triumphal wagon with musicians playing all manner of instruments, followed by a great crowd of people, which pleased greatly, and the viceroy observed this procession from the windows of the Royal Palace. That same day the fishermen caught a monstrous fish, which a few days earlier a fisherman said was caught, some six ells long, scarcely less than four broad, and weighing 1,600 rotoli or Neapolitan pounds: it was considered a rarity on the order of the viceroy and was burned the following day; on which day several other fishermen, who came home that evening, also brought two sea creatures, which have been kept in [?]: one of these two, caught near [?], is called a Ciprin, and the other, found in the basin of this harbor, is said to be a sea calf.

ROME, 12 June. On Sunday it was announced in the papal church that the Pope had ordered the chapel in the Quirinal, in which the Duke Colonus [?] attended, the cardinals having not yet received [notice]. Monday morning the cardinals Davis, Cutani, and Barbarigo had audience with His Holiness, and said they had received letters [?] from their bishoprics to depart. Tuesday morning the Pope held a public consistory, in which His Holiness gave hats to the cardinals of K. Spigna, Archpa, [?] S. Cicesky, [?], Belugis, Paciila, Borgia, Cinf'ugos, and Schoonboru: all the cardinals attending this ceremony were lodged in the cloister of [del Popolo], and were treated by the Cardinal of Rohan, as the officiant of this [ceremony], with all manner of courtesy; but after they had rested on [?], the papal procession departed, before which the Roman nobility and the gentlemen of the cardinals, all mounted, arrived at the Quirinal, where they received the hats from the Pope with the customary ceremonies; at the end of which the Cardinal de Rohan, both for himself and in the name of the other gentlemen, made a compliment to the Pope, to thank His Holiness for the honor shown to them; whereupon the Holy Father answered in Italian terms: after the end of the consistory the great cardinal, all the German cardinals excepted for the officiant of Rohan, were sumptuously entertained at dinner.

England

From LONDON, 12 June. The King has received express news that the combined fleet of some eighty [ships] has advanced as far as [?]. The great British [fleet] which is there has returned, and after holding a conference with the ministers of Sweden and the commissioned [representatives] of the Crown, advice was received to send express with all diligence to Sweden. It is assured that the British Admiral Norris also served. An English ship with 50 great cannons and a Swedish frigate have had the misfortune of striking against a rock, whereby the aforementioned ship was so badly damaged that Admiral Norris ordered it to be brought [?] to be repaired. The reports received daily, that the Russians burn and ruin all wherever they come, cannot be denied: however, some still wish to assure [us] that the negotiations over peace at [Nystad] are very far advanced; and that daily a courier is expected that our plenipotentiary ministers, with those of Great Russia, will have regulated the preliminary points, although others think that the propositions from the Moscow side are not acceptable to this court; this uncertainty produces more caution: all the more so because everything is handled at this court with the utmost secrecy; yet it is communicated that matters of very great importance must be on the table, because the Senate is assembled extraordinary daily. The King has completely recovered to his previous health; His Majesty was, along with his brother the Prince of [?], at the grand ball last Saturday which was given in the Orangery; but the Queen was not present due to a slight indisposition. It is said that the King must make a journey to Gravesend in the coming week. The King's brother, Prince George of [?], accepted the day before yesterday, the 16th, his journey to Königsberg with a Swedish frigate, where he will land with His Prussian Majesty [to be received]: the mentioned prince has, during his stay here, conducted himself in every sort of manner well: His Highness was, among other gentlemen of distinction, escorted by Mr. [?] and a gentleman of the chamber of the King and the [?]. The late minister the Count Frytzag, who was still very actively involved in maintaining the Brunswick Congress, as a plenipotentiary minister of the Czar was already prepared there, awaits his expedition from Vienna daily to return.

France

MARSEILLE, Sunday 19 June. On Corpus Christi day all the gates of this city were kept closed to prevent all country people from being able to participate fully in the great procession which was then held; and although no publication has yet been made to open the churches, the stock exchange, or the public markets, the streets through which the procession passed were nonetheless full of people. Notwithstanding that one now enjoys complete health here, and continues all necessary precautions to remain free of plague; during which, both within and outside this city, 30,000 people have died. No ships are yet lying [here] for the West and [?], but last week three barques sailed to the Levant.

PARIS, 27 June. These days the beekeeper guild has presented to the King a gilded metal beehive with crystal inscriptions, through which one can perfectly see the bees' operations), the same was brought that day to the castle of [la Muette], where various other small wooden buildings and paintings, which these religious [brothers] had added there. The dispute between the Duke of [?] and the Count d'Evreux, concerning the landed property Tancavilla, has been completely settled by the arrival of the Duke Regent, whereupon the Count d'Evreux has made his concession; the Sieur Salnot, introducer of ambassadors, has these days received [?] livres for the arrangements for the audience of the Turkish ambassador, which will be conducted with no less ceremony than his first audience. According to the latest letters from [?], among the cardinals [were] the Rohan, the [?], and the...

Germany & Adjacent Kingdoms

DURLACH, 24 June. Tuesday the Margrave held the hunt feast and many distinguished persons, nobles, and great lords sat at table in a large room, set up for this in the garden of the castle; this room was arranged inside round about with green trees, and from above hung various crystal crowns, and here and there very costly mirrors; during the meal there was fine music of kettledrums, trumpets, and hunting horns, which was interrupted by an excellent concert of all the musical instruments and voices; when the meal had ended, all the gentlemen retired to the castle, and about half an hour later, a wind arose so strong that the aforementioned room collapsed, whereby the costly decorations were shattered and a chambermaid was crushed; the damage is estimated at more than 1,000 florins.

REGENSBURG, 24 June. After the Roman council has long asserted that they have weightier grievances to lay against the Protestants, the Papal minister has finally made those [grievances] public, concerning the county of [?] with [?]; these are lengthy and many so petty that the King of Prussia will have little trouble having them refuted): the aim of the Catholics being to counter [them] by further reduction of the Protestant grievances: they claim that in short a great volume, with well-founded complaints of the Roman [citizens] of the [?] territory, will be presented to the Holy Roman Emperor [?] and His Imperial Majesty will be asked reciprocally that satisfaction be given both [to them] and to the Protestants; as the Elector Palatine has also requested privately of the Emperor, concerning the lands occupied by his predecessors to the King of Prussia; under the condition that His Majesty aforementioned would have broken the treaties through the common [?]. The minister of the Elector Palatine everywhere claims that his master has completely rectified all the complaints of the Protestants in the Palatinate, although the Lutherans continue to show [?], and that only some points remain which, due to their delicacy, must yet be examined jointly.

Rhine-town offers some hopeful prospects, whereby the Laak will shortly be expected to return to this place; Cardinal de Rohan was also somewhat troubled, because he did not fully credit the Pope's word regarding the people's design of an affront; which temperament concerns the matter of the Constitution; and it is confirmed that His Eminence returns hither. From Alicante it is written that the Spanish fleet and its companions were in the harbor at Almería, ready; but that the provisions, which were sent from Denia and elsewhere, had not yet arrived there; at Barcelona and other ports preparations were being made for the sea armada; and orders were sent to all the bakers in the army and thereabouts to prepare themselves; all the commanding officers of the artillery and the cannoneers [were informed], and it is written that the troops are well recruited. Letters from Lisbon report that a pirate from the coast of Barbary near the Island of St. Michael pursued a Portuguese bark, which bark had run aground on the shore of a drifting island of cinnamon, which had washed up some months prior; but since this island, of which the pirate had never heard, appeared to him most fortunate, he abandoned the bark and let it go with the utmost goodwill. The Abbot of Bois Franc has transferred the Priory of St. Nicholas d'Acy, yielding 200 livres, to the Abbot Riguen, Auditor of State and librarian of Rome; where all trophies in the Order of St. Benedict and of the Congregation are most fitly displayed, within the gates of the city of Senlis, wherein is situated a notable house. The Viscount of Tavaunes, first chamberlain of the Hérault, married Mademoiselle du Bieuil, daughter of the Lord du Bieuil, general farmer [of taxes], who, beyond the 30,000 livres which he gives to Monsieur Duedes for the wedding, has further obliged himself by marriage contract to lodge and maintain the young couple with their retinue for 4 years, provided they pay 4,000 livres yearly. The Lord Colbert of Théon, ensign in the first company of the Fire Guards, married Mademoiselle Gouffier, daughter of the Marquis of that name; in consideration of this marriage she gives a pension of 8,000 livres to Lord Colbert, and to the Marquise Gouffier the Order of the Red Ribbon, with an annual stipend of 4,000 livres. A certain Bulletin, a speculator from Languedoc, engaged in wagers against all comers that from 20 June to 25 July inclusive it will rain every day, and the foundation of his wagers has no small amount, ordinary wagers running among the people that it will rain for 40 consecutive days, as at the festival of St. Gervais, which is set for 19 June in the new gold lottery against jewels, and one may choose those one wishes to place in security; whosoever wagers must stake, according to what he has wagered and placed in security, to win or lose 5,000 livres. The notorious Cartouche, captain of thieves, was himself present at the ball held last Sunday night in the opera house, and he was attended by a light escort of his gang, and moreover there was no one from the record keepers to capture him; some ladies of pleasure, who reside in the Regiment of Guards, have been ordered for the past two days to avoid men as much as possible, under promise that they will be generously rewarded if he comes to be captured through their assistance. Wednesday evening a young thief was arrested, who was initially thought to be Cartouche, but upon examination it was found to be merely one of his confederates.

---

GOUDA, 1 July. The Directors and Head Participants of the Company of Commerce and Alliance within Gouda, until now most fully involved, have appointed classes of [persons] [?], on which their lives are noted; in the negotiation of life insurance, under the guarantee of the City of Gouda, begun on 1 April of this year 1721, the calculation of life expectancy with the diversity of ages of persons in each class, maintaining such persons at the proper weight; and it is inquired whether the determination of persons of unequal and diverse ages, particularly at Linen, such at the request of different persons to be sought. [This] negotiation under the aforesaid guarantee continues until 1 September next, at the latest; and then to those who shall wish to do so, the opportunity is afforded to select within that period fifty or more persons to be placed in all or in one class of the aforesaid negotiation by a noted and approved transaction, which such arrangement and addition of persons in the selfsame period having been received, the Directors and Head Participants in the aforesaid company, as under guarantee as before, shall administer and accept.

GOUDA, 1 July. The Directors and Head Participants of the Company of Commerce and Alliance within Gouda make known to Your High Serene Highnesses the Princes of Hesse-Philippsthal, Father, to all and each one who has anything relating to the account of the aforesaid Princesses, to address themselves to the High Princes [as specified]—

The brokers of the Best [Goods] of the City of Dordrecht shall on Tuesday, 8 July 1721 at the auction, on the high New Street there, and the goods thereby forthcoming, with ships wherein no oil goods have been, of which the search is released; other instruction is to be obtained from the aforesaid brokers.

Jacob vorder Beeck, Marinus Juweel, and Cora Verlinde, brokers, shall on evening at 6 o'clock, in the Wine Street, in the inn of the Black Lion, sell a ship, household goods, boards, gaming tables, and [?] effects of 1715 and 1720, and this at the offices of the Lords Prickfan and Folter.

F. Raker, D. Raker, Abraham van Dordtlaet, and Hendrik Linquet, brokers, shall (Wednesday evening, 9 July) at 8 o'clock in the market sell goods from the ship the Foorfuya, Captain Jac. Pieterfs, [containing] diapers, linen, white heathen and flannels, flowered and striped bedding, pantiles, canvas, voiles, muffs, &c. lying on three pack-saddlers, all as before, and on the sale day they may be seen.

Cor. de Loon and Jan vander Struuig, brokers at Amsterdam, present for sale a stately house with large rooms, stabling for 10 horses, coach house and Haarlem at the Klever Lane; said homestead being provided with most excellent [?], grass and water commons, ponds around the place, orchards and [?] plantations, and at the end of the third place, a wood with hovel, the [?] very fine pasture lying adjacent thereto, with a new farmhouse, stabling [?] dwelling at the lane; further instruction is to be obtained at the homestead, or [?]

Frans Kox and Jan vander Struug, brokers at Amsterdam, present for sale a stately farmhouse, dwelling house, stabling for 10 horses, coach house and garden house, mercury, and fencing with fine fruit trees, large [?] adjoining fine pasture; all situated outside Haarlem, at the Brouwers Street, near [?]

Printed in Leyden by FELIX DE K[?] over the Town Hall. 4 July

AMSTERDAM, 2 July. It is said that the bark of Captain Pieter Bermie has been taken in Spanish West Indies. The Hocker commanded by Captain Pieter Gibonstaaten has been detained by Justice. A young lad recently returned [reported] that a hocker from Iceland came, where about some ships were seen which he thought to be from the East Indies; but such is uncertain. The ship of Adrian Bowen lay at Diego lap at Bonti Galetto. From Bayonne have departed the Juffrows Adriana, Marijn Gerriffe; from Bordeaux the St. Joseph, Captain Étienne Ducart to St. Jago and St. Martha, the Vergarde Luck, Jan Juniene to Honfleur; from Dieppe all ships to the Well; from Dort Christoffel Gudding; and from Rotterdam Christoffel Pietets, and yet another merchantman by his council of the Godessen, coming from the Levant, would be detained. At Honfleur has arrived the galliot St. Jan; Frans Will, from Zealand; at Bordeaux the Greyhound, Willem de Boer, and two others from here and the Maas; and at Duykeiken Loon from here as also in Dieppe, before the city, ships from the East, &c.

---

Upon the notification made regarding the death of the Landgrave of Hesse-Philippsthal, to the Lords States General, their High Mightinesses, from the midst of their assembly, commissioned the Baron of Wymerberg, Lord of Glinckhoff, on behalf of the Province of Gelderland, and the Lord of Hoorn on behalf of the Province of Zealand, to the assembly of the aforesaid Lords States to present the compliments of condolence on the loss of the Princess [Gertrude], which ceremony was yesterday carried out in a solemn manner by the aforesaid Lords, their High Mightinesses, proceeding from the name of those States. Yesterday and today the Chancery Minister, the Baron Godenau, was with some Lords of the government. There have been for some days various dispatches both from Sweden [?] 1806, and sent from England; whereby the council of state shall have matters of the utmost importance upon the table; and according to the latest letters from Stockholm, one expects daily an express from the Swedish capital with news that the preliminaries will have been signed. The Swedish ministry has [been] first in the letters of negotiation. The Lords of Sonsbeeck and Pickaart, who on behalf of the Council on the street have been in commission to Spanish Flanders to the execution of the obligations, and visited the magazines and fortifications there, shall [have been] reviewed last evening, and have in the said council various [matters]. Yesterday the foot guards at the Cook House, in the presence of Generals Hompès, La Loe, and other persons, 300 of the high government, as others, made a display of arms. The Prince of Brandenburg, Alpshagen, will shortly be seen in Germany, to visit the remarkable in England. It is thought that the Duke of Parma has much contributed to the obtaining of the provisional accord between the crowns of Spain and Great Britain, by informing the Spanish court, and the Queen that the present Pope did not wish to form some pretense regarding commission states in Italy, which according to a preceding tract should pass to the sons of the King of Spain. But that does not come to the evening, in our preceding in the article of 27 June from Paris mentioned, still among others notice, that should the Parliament of Great Britain come to approve Gibraltar to the crown of Spain remaining, British Majesty would not lift themselves in reciprocal [terms], provided that Spain then give the crown an equivalent for it.

The Hesse-Philippsthal, sons and heirs of the late Lord Landgrave, all of the aforesaid High Serene Highness would be able to present to the High Prussian in the lodging of [?] in The Hague.

Tuesday, 8 July 1721, at 3 o'clock in the afternoon, in the Auction House of the governors, will be sold approximately 3,000 or 3,500 sacks of Cadiz salt, true quality sent from sea, and of which the salt can be tested, and this by lots of 200 sacks to be obtained.

Wednesday, will be sold at Rotterdam on Monday 14 July 1721, at the Golden Lion, a ship's cargo of extra curious and fine type from old and new in 1720, and this by lots as they shall be named, lying in the [?]

Linquet brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday 16 July 1721, the Burg on the Singel, a considerable party of linens and other merchandise [?] layette, goods in large party fine [?], guarantees, satinettes, [?] and striped bed-cloths, silk and brocades, painted and cut drinking glasses, in falconry, as under other items, by notice shall be designated; where the goods two times [?].

[At Amsterdam,] present for sale a most magnificent homestead, with the same fine [?] for guardians, cooks and gardeners' dwelling and closet, situated outside, provided with most fine [?] and stocks of larch trees, all under care, orchards with fine choice of fruit trees, vegetable garden with extra much [?] hovel, great large [?] ranging in great [?] in moor through the house, stabling for 24 cows, wagon house, barn and shed, [with] a dam on the [?], being the aforementioned brokers.

[At Amsterdam,] present [for sale] in some [?] a most remarkable homestead, with the same fine [?] and gardeners' house &c., the same homestead being provided with fine brambles, great circle and half morg, as well as yet approximately 5½ morgens in the Blue Street, at Oetsveen &c.

FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedelstraat [verso] 4 July 1721: With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

The Course of the EXCHANGE is today: Madrid and Cadiz 115. Seville 114. Bilbao 90½. Lisbon 94¼. Valencia 81¼. Livorno 92. Genoa 91½. Paris 41¼. London 14 shillings 6 pence. Danzig 120 Polish groats. Reval 5; and 2 [?] Gothenburg 6. Hamburg 12½. St. Petersburg 6 groats 0. Ghent 2¼. Brussels 1¼. Zealand 1¼. Rotterdam 2 [?]. The action of the Bank 5¼ to 1/8. The actions of the East India Company are 777 à 740 [?]. Goods 590. The English effects have no fixed price.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 4th of july, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 81.
