Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of July, 1721

WEST INDIES.

JAMAICA, 29 April. News has been received here that on the 13th of this month, on the lower Coast, some 6 to 7,700 Dutch and Zealandic ships were lying in good condition, namely the Pieter van Maele, Pieter Ber[?], Jan de Ruiter, Christoffel Tym; Captain Zacharias; and two others; also arrived on the said Coast was Captain Jacob Everise; who had previously separated from Captain Jan Vaik; and Captain Pieter Brouard departed for Rotterdam; along with another ship bound for another place; on the mentioned Coast several Spanish captains were occasionally sighted, and near Panama a pirate reportedly sailed ashore and caused some [?] damage there; but on this Coast one is now rid of them. Within 14 days, a Convoy with several merchant vessels is to depart from here for London and Bristol.

PORTUGAL.

LISBON, 3 June. The Dutch warships are making ready to put to sea with the merchant ships lying here and at St. Ubes within 6 to 8 days, and the Maas is ready. On this Coast no Turkish pirates have been heard of, but near the Bay of Port à Port 2 vessels of 16 and 11 guns were sighted, which were sent back without prizes. News has been received via the Liguards, with letters from 10 February from Bahia, that the fleet comprised about 50 ships, with some 12 to 1,300 kills of sugar, and nearly 10,000 rolls of tobacco, along with a great quantity of gold, which on the 13th of that month, under the escort of 2 warships, set sail from there homeward; but it is thought that the same will arrive in late February.

SPAIN.

CADIZ, 11 July. This week several consignments of new specie have arrived here from the Mints, which the merchant traders have been shown in the usual manner [?]. The Galleons now have, as one is assured, their cargo on board, and must, upon receipt of dispatches from the Court, depart within 3 to 4 days. The Admiral of this Fleet, which is 12 ships strong, is named Don Marido de Cuebas Ypardo; who will sail with the same to the 4 Bruefs [?], from where he will leave the Fleet behind him; daily one awaits from the Casques the gilt ship N. S. del Rozario, Captain Juan da Gire, which in the beginning of April was still lying in the Guayra with 200 sails. In this Bay several ships have arrived, among them the English warship of Captain Onofre O: from Lisbon, and others departed. Which letters of the 3rd came back by way of Tetuán, with word that the English and Maracaibo Ministers, with much persuasion towards [?] Capuchins and Franciscans [?]...

MADRID, 17 June. Last Tuesday the King, the Queen, the Prince of Asturias, and the Infantes came to the city from Aranjuez; yesterday Their Majesties attended the Church of Our Lady of Atocha; and on Thursday the King, together with the Prince, assisted at the great Procession, which the Queen and the Infantes, being on a balcony, saw pass by the Court; but during this Procession so heavy a thunderstorm came down that one was forced to postpone it for the day and continue it the following day, when His Majesty attended it in person, and after which Their Majesties retired to the Church and [Infanta], where by the Marquis Perdamad and Virgile gold [?] was presented, which had been taken by a Maltese warship from a Turkish pirate off this Coast; over which gift Their Majesties greatly rejoiced; and the Dispatch [?] Their Majesties which had been taken by a Maltese warship from a Turkish pirate off this Coast; over which gift Their Majesties greatly rejoiced; and the Dispatch shows that the same shall be reduced from 120,000 to 70,000 men. The Marquis Amat has been promoted to Lieutenant General for his distinguished services. It is assured that Mr. Schaub has arrived with Dispatches concerning matters relating to Gibraltar, etc., and that His Majesty, together with Lord Stanhope, has held conferences with the principal Ministers of this Court. It is assured that a Conference has been sent to Cardinal in Britain and Vankurk; as also that the differences over some points with the last-mentioned Court have been settled, which is said to have been sent. Yesterday the Court returned again from Madrid to the Escorial.

FRANCE.

PARIS, 30 June. One sees a Royal Ordinance of the 21st of this month, for the various Military [...] the Ordinance, their freedom to go everywhere under the same [...] is in the Royal Palace also not [...] Momme Mand, concerning the [...] Majesty Albertus the Redewaarje. This Assessment, which several are of the Royal Dame Nobility that His Majesty in his Malbitain [?] against the Duke of Chartres [played?] made the King entertain on Majesty the balcony of the Princesse of Vauxdour, whom the King greeted. Yesterday His Majesty entertained himself, several days among other things sent; were the Washing in the Tuileries gitett very pleasantly, there were many Gentlemen and Ladies of the Court very pleasantly, there were many Gentlemen and Ladies of the Court very pleasantly, there are many Gentlemen and Ladies of the Court [...] those [...] situated on water the Wardships in the Tuileries gitett many Gentlemen and Dame of the Court very pleasantly, there [...] and where the Meidetollons of the police, Do., and the Mantle [...] acted workmanlike and accommodating justifications, which at Court [...]

Monday's Courant.

are to be found. The Church festival days were very religiously observed at the Tables in the Royal Palace; as the day for St. Peter was set, nothing other than Soup with Wine was presented thereat, among such boiled Eggs, Fruit-Pastries, Preserves and Fruit, without any Fish, Eggs, or Baked goods being brought, save only at the King's Table, who always from both, and only with Fish with Flesh as with Fish was served. The for those of St. Peter, the Papal Nuncio, concerning the Feast of the said Saint, made magnificent Illuminations for the Palace of Avignon, where he shall lodge in the Maud d'Ocher, when he shall first move in, in what he has rented. It is said that the Abbot of Gamache will attend to the affairs of this Court pro interim at Rome, if the request of the Diocese of Citeaux to return here be accorded by the Court. All that is arranged for the departure of the Ottoman Ambassador under Sikenskens honored, although his h. Bailli will not undertake the journey until the end of August or the beginning of the coming month of September. Parliament continues to discuss 1 merchant and to take matters concerning the duelist of the river Bretereil and the Gravelles, and has published Monitors for this purpose. Daily many Servants continue to be caught, but one holds all captive, without Process upon them: Cartouche, the Captain of that Band, whom one, with diligence seeks to catch, would slowly divert order to dissipate [?]. Friday were both 10 40 Archers in the Forest of St. Martin on the heels, but the Attack has already failed again: it is assured that he is armed with Philobten, and once £ (are to be done, for which the Maréchaussée somewhat wary of him: he has been a Spy for the Lord d'Argenion, whereby he knows all the Archers and Maréchaussée quite well: it is a resolved and unrelenting Guilder without worth; and therefore it is feared that he will himself Poniard himself, if he can no longer escape the tight grasp, for which one endeavors to seize him by betrayal. It is trusted that the number of Cominzes, which the Journals keep of the Vicinage, will be considerably increased, because those of the great Boughs cannot furnish them sufficiently. One no longer speaks of a Vicinage prolongation extension, yet it is trusted that an elusive great change will be allowed for a short time. The Actions are at 60, and the Bank Traders at 1000 on 102 Liv. Although it rains every day heavily, Mr. Bulio has concluded his (aforementioned) Wagers, and many believe that he would now like to rid himself of the matter. The Duke of Bouillon grows better daily. The Duke de la Valière lies ill at Petitbourg by the Duke of Antin. It appears that at Lyon, and in the Lyon Territory, one is much afflicted by the Plague; on the 21st of this month one publicly shot in the open a certain Durand, who was an Officer of the Lazaret, because he had had some Bales of Silk conveyed from there to the City, without having them properly purified first, for which he lost 43 Liv.; his Father a [?] K. Man [?], and having only the Son, offered in vain a great sum of Money for his life; and the Magistrate of the Gendarmerie judged this matter of such great consequence, to set an Example; the Lord Robert, who was Owner of the said Silk, against the deluded Bales came with Couplets of Radi grain, which one suspected to be suspected, were burned outside the Gates there and one means thereby to force the People to be cautious.

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 1 July. After those of the Lower House on Saturday had made the Reading of the Message sent to them the previous day by the King, the Lord Robert, who is first Commissioner of the Treasury, made a lengthy speech concerning the Treaty with Sweden, and showed the great advantages one has to expect from it; both in respect of the Britannic Religion, as those which the Nation in particular will thereby obtain: whereupon Lord Molesworth answered, and brought forward reasons to show that the said Treaty was wholly unnecessary, and after he had further brought forward some of his Sentiments in this connection, Mr. Horatio Walpole also made a speech on the necessity of granting a Subsidy of 72,000 pounds to be paid to the Crown of Sweden, according to the said Treaty; wherein he was supported by the learned Lord Harrington, for other Members, which Proposition was opposed by Mr. Shepin, Member of the secret Committee, who after having brought forward some Reflections, was supported in his Sentiments by Knight Joseph Ickyl Master of the Rolls, and fellow-Member of the secret Committee, Mr. Smith, and other Members; and the House resolved that the said Proposition should be taken into consideration on Monday in grand Committee. Mr. Tieky, Secretary of State for the East, presented to the House a Bill to appoint Commissioners for the execution [?]; and against the affairs of the Receiver-General's Debts; of which the first Reading was made; and the consideration of the Report of the secret Committee of the 11th being made, a week was postponed. Yesterday the House gave the second Reading of the Bill to appoint Commissioners, and committed the same in Committee for next Friday; and as the postponement of the Members of the House was also 3 days fulfilled, it was resolved to resume the consideration of public Credit tomorrow; after which the House went into grand Committee, the King's Message on Friday deliberated upon, and came after some brief Debate to conclusion, that a Subsidy should be granted to enable His Majesty to fulfill the same Engagements of the Crown of Sweden; which Resolution passed not without 135 Voices against; also stipulating that a Subsidy be reserved for the payment of the Arrears of 5 Ships [?], which Guarantees hold, if one should need to call upon the said Ships [?]...

DORDRECHT, July 5. On the eighth of this month, three Dutch merchant vessels arrived in the city: the Douwe Bouwes from Christiania, the Young Tobias, the Samuel Christiaans from Aizeloe, and the Paul Volkerts from the Hoop, along with Hans Pe[...]

Yesterday in Goederden, goods totaling 300 [units] were burned through [?] of munitions and ordnance; which measure was expressly [?]; 114 Marks.

However, it was understood that regarding the Subsidy for the Crown, although it was thought that concerning the Subsidy for the Crown Sweden would have some difficulties to contend with; and the willing [?] like the municipality both Resolutions, yet it was favorably received by the House, which in the disposal of this sum had much of its well-being and [?] freedoms to bring, in great [?] was led over the Bill to the benefit of the afflicted Persons in the D.Z. Company; where it made little progress, and the renewal of the least agreement was postponed to tomorrow. The Mint Bill was postponed until Saturday. Yesterday the Spanish Ambassador, the Marquis de Posto, delivered a dispatch with confirmation of the concluded Convention between [?] and the Minister and the Crown and this Realm; it is understood that the aforementioned [?], by occasion of this matter, shall [?], and that the Plenipotentiaries for this Crown shall depart shortly for America; if the Ratification of the above-mentioned Convention is given, then in return an Express shall be sent back to Colonel [?], and against those of Spain executed. Yesterday in the Printer Mill and Wilkins [?] sent again by the Committee, for examination of Adhesions &c. of [?] Writings. The Gentlemen Gorden, Gordon, Toffier, and Molloy, which Bill the House itself sat in the custody of the Sergeant of Arms, [?] that not otherwise, and it is held, their [?] [?], also no one of them could [?] Baron Charles Everslield is for Horsham, in the County of Surrey, elected Member of Parliament, in place of the Burgess Rubat Injam, which [?] Lord Irwin. It is understood that the Earl of Carlisle is to be elevated to one of the Dukes of Great Britain. It is said nonetheless that the Duke of Montague; before the [?] in other Lands and go traveling, concerning the sale of East Company. in the Gezardy negotiation is with a King of the Crown of Pembrook. According to the Letters of the Governor of Barbados, the Corsairs there cause many irregularities on the [?]; even they had some [?] for the number in this [?], a French ship of 30 [guns] seized, on board of which the Governor of Martinico [?], and the [?] hanged, as well as the Captain and many other Persons who were on that Ship: they have also taken a Dutch Ship of 150 tons, the Loyal Galley, and a Sloop of Barbados and also [?] that the number of such Corsair ships would increase considerably, so that in this time the Mr. Richard Billet of Wooleygreen, who is [?] years old, and 5000 pounds is valued at, has been robbed with his Maidservant. The entire Sum of the named Goods by the former D.Z. West[?] [?] and 1742415 pounds Shillings 7 and a half Pence, of which [?] is 1131 pounds [?] Shillings 2 and a half Pence, such for the D.Z. Company, over [?] is 164303 pounds Shillings 5 Pence. The Stocks of the Bank are 124, [?] 148, D.Z. 120 and 1 [?], the Millions Bank 194, Lottery and Bank Ann. 96 and a half, ditto [?].

DORDREGT, [?] July. We see these [?] before this City had arrived [?] 105th before departed the Priesthood Fleet, Culbert Kaffon, and the French [?], Benjamin Browne notes English, Adrian Malt to Ostende, the Hoop, Hans Pe[...]

THE HAGUE, July 8. The Gentlemen States of Holland and Westvrieland shall commence their ordinary Meeting next Wednesday, eight days hence, for which the Points of description are to be forwarded speedily to the respective Cities of this Province. Yesterday morning the Swedish Minister, Mr. Prys, was with some Gentlemen Counselors of the Government.

PETERSBURG, June 11. Word has been received of the Czar's journey to Revel, His Czaric Majesty having received a Courier from His Imperial Majesty, with favorable news from Newland, [?] has been requested not all bodies [?] thereunto; since has been requested not all bodies [?] the Czaric High Rampart Majesty, at the request of the Swedish Plenipotentiaries from Warsaw [?], whom one expects shall be present today; also the great Admiral has ordered [?] under command [?] in the line or [?] against Sweden [?] must remain. The [?] Meushof [?] ostentation [?] in [?] must remain. The morning, and has today its [?] Hall nickers and Nobility given. Concerning [?] the Prince Harry of Friesland, and the goodly Princeling of the Czar is still as 150 [?], and so it is the body that [?] yet some all regulated. This Court [?] in the country 100 men will know here remain.

GENOA, June 15. The Great Britain Minister residing here celebrated the birthday of the King of Great Britain with little pomp, and all the English Gentlemen, who were here, [?] the security of Great Britain [?] the occasion entertained; the British Warships lying in this Harbor have made several Discharges from their Cannons on that occasion. One reads in the Papers that the Count Salicien might be sent to Rome or Paris in the second Commission.

MILAN, June 18. Upon received report, as if the destined Sickness in certain [?] places would be discovered, the Magistrate of Health has ordered that all Persons who have [?] in the common Quarantine should have to [?], under that one [?] truth of such [?] time must be scheduled.

VIENNA, June 21. Sunday in the Chapel of Lazenburg returned, and Monday in the Favorita the Mansch Werder [?] was all the Horses here Gala, concerning the Name Day of the King of Portugal; and that same Majesty both the Empress Wednes and the Empress Wedas and the Empress Wedues and the Empress Wedoes with the other in the Chapel and in the [?] Chamber shall appear Yesterday officially from Toulon, with word that the Count Erdoddy, according to the Imperial permit, from Warsaw [?] to the East, for which the fulfillment of its grace [?] the [?] [?] [?] dernarijken. Today one has begun [?] to [?] the specific number of families, [?] Estate, household [?], and [?] exactly upon heavy [?] exacto raised must be. The Sr Mun [?].

VIENNA, June 27. Sunday the Imperial Widow returned, and Monday in the Favorita the Ministerial [?] began to drink. Yesterday was at all the Courts there Gala, concerning the Name Day of the King of Portugal, and that evening the Imperial Widow, and the Archduchesses with the other in the Favorita [?]. One awaits word of the Empress [?] with the [?] shall become [?]. Yesterday arrived an Officer from Poolco, with word that Count Erdody, according to the Imperial permission from Wardesgue to the East traveled, and with the fulfillment of his grace there to busy use. Today one has begun to make [?] the specific number of the Families, Households, children and [?] which are in the [?] found, on writing, which specific [?] upon heavy penalty is raised must be. The [?] Rhumelier Count Bielke, who has already taken dissident at the Court, and with the Emperor's Portrait, with Diamonds [?], closed is [?] displaced over Sweden and his [?]. The Bishop of Batavia is awaited here in a few days, to lay the [?] Hand to [?] the establishment of the Archbishopric [?].

STRASBOURG, July 28. A Dispatch from the Emperor, Minister Baron Bentionticher, has traveled from Paris to Vienna, to bring word there of various [?] between Britannia and Spain, concerning Gibraltar &c. has departed. Here the [?] met the Druyt [?]; the Roads have become unsafe [?] [?] [?] one is here raised [?] [?] and Goods from outside coming in.

FRANKFURT, July 2. The Margrave of Brandenburg [?] [?]. Thursday with his Consort from the Embleibad come, [?] the Count and [?] of Hanau to the Castle Ph[ilipps-hqel] gone, where they are yet found.

COLOGNE, July 4. Yesterday the Margrave Ludwig of Baden of Bon arrived here, to begin his journey to the Mediterranean Sea. Wednesday in [?] days the Well[?] Church-Envoys there are awaited, to open the Circle-assembly. One still has yet a word from [?] the [?] Structures of Brussels the path shall come, to discharge the Count of Aichinshan.

The Company at the Chamber on the Maze, makes to each [?] known, that their E.E. on that Thursday thereof, a party of their rare Surinam Sails, [?] by other, address themselves to the Well-Indian House aforesaid.

of the Lots of 's Lands negotiation the Lottery of the to the Classe. following, and that not the remaining of the Classe [?] of the [?] Fallen than to

Seekers-dealer of the City of Leiden is made known, that the Quay-Market is said and Lode following Grain-Market.

[Advertisement section - Brokers:]

The Sworn Brokers, shall on Wednesday the 16 July 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, in Loscoopen and large [?] loading [?] [?] from large [?] Work [?] to be [?]; along with an extra [?] party [?] Londish [?], [?] shall lie in the Boombjes in the Warehouses of the Gentlemen Jakob Sensori and Son. [?], [?] Amsterdam, shall in Amsterdam sell on Wednesday the 11 July 1721, one, in the Burg on the Cingel, a considerable party [?] and other Merchandise-goods, [?] and in said party [?] [?], Plantaine, [?], Bleumde and [?] [?], Silk- and [?], [?] and various [?], three Warehouses, as further by Notice shall be announced; also the Growers two [?] large [?].

[Advertisement for property sale:]

[?]sterdam, [?] to sell a very [?] Hofstead, with its [?] Nice [?] and Garden-man House &C.; said Hofstead aforementioned with Beautiful Tree-gardens, Fruit- and wood Gardens, on Wednesday still from [?] to 1 half Morgen, likewise on the Diermeverstaart, by Overseeus &C.

[Final advertisement for book:]

[?] shall [?] of the needed Boettien [?] of the Werberge, on new [?], and improved with the good Writings of the [?], through [?] this M-

FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Briedeltstraat [?]. July 7, 1721. With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 82.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 9th of July, 1721
English Translation

---

Italy

VENICE, 24 June. Word has arrived from Rome that the Duke of St. Blafius and Don Joseph Donetto, both from Palermo, had fought a duel there near Campo Vatiotto. However, the manner in which this occurred was prevented in time by certain persons who had come there on behalf of Falconieri, Governor of that City. [They were called] to his house; as they did not wish to proceed, and [it was stated] that they were subjects of the Emperor, no regard should be taken without orders concerning this matter (other than those from the Emperor's Minister respectively). Whereupon the two gentlemen betook themselves to the Palace of His Imperial Majesty's Minister, Cardinal Albani, who ordered them to remain in their house in this matter until they should be reconciled. However, when they left the Palace of the aforesaid Imperial Minister, they betook themselves to the apartment of the Secretary of that Embassy, through whose intervention they were reconciled that very evening, of which notice was given to the above-mentioned [parties]. It is written from Mantua that a certain Hessian soldier in the Emperor's service, quartered there in Gazzana, having obtained leave, had sought a match with a daughter of the Count of Gazzola, [and] by some means had found a way to come into the house of the said Count at night and to carry away the daughter with her consent; and having taken her to his quarters, he immediately married her in the presence of the chaplain of the regiment under which he served. However, after this action caused great displeasure and violent complaints against him, and he had gone forth from the house greatly beset with difficulties, among the Mantuan nobility, the Prince of Hesse-Darmstadt, Governor of the said city, wishing to give all possible satisfaction to the offended nobility, had the newly-married soldier placed in the Tower and confined, and this lady was placed in the Ursuline Convent. In the said city, the Doge and Government held a feast on the day of St. Vitus and [?] in the church with great solemnity to commemorate the discovered conspiracy in the year 1711 [Editor's note: referring to the Conspiracy of the Ten]; and the Eucharist was celebrated with a solemn procession in the said church, whereupon the Doge, the nobility, the Government, and distinguished foreigners, who were from the said city, attended. The Papal Nuncio, having received the Bull of Milan regarding the jubilee feast of Rome, has given it to the Nuncio, who will attend to the further arrangement of it to hand it over to the Patriarch for publication; this jubilee feast will last fourteen days, during which time solemn processions will be performed; and [those] who will be appointed thereto. The said Nuncio, whose public entry should have been made on the said day in July next, [?] appeared in great numbers, whereupon [?]

---

Norway

CHRISTIANSAND, 24 June. On the 21st of this month, near this city, a strong flood of water in the river swept away wood stored in beams; all the beams and other timber lying in the river were driven out to sea, which has caused great damage.

---

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 25 June. Regarding the negotiations at Aix-la-Chapelle [Editor's note: Peace of 1721], nothing further has been received; however, reports are general that a certain [?] from the coast may have taken place. The Minister here of the King of Poland, who on account of that kingdom will go to the Brunswick Congress, have requested safe passage from the court for that time. Nothing further has been received from the combined Gothic fleet.

---

Wednesday's Courant

---

Germany & Neighbouring Kingdoms

REGENSBURG, 30 June. Inasmuch as the Emperor in his latest decree has made known to the entire assembly that the Principal Commissioner for the Protestant corps, it is also his intention that the Protestants shall proceed, they are applying themselves vigorously to have the grievances that they possess printed and published publicly. And as they have come forward with those of the County of Mark the previous week, which are found to be a heap of grievances and external abuses that have no substance; meanwhile, as a grievance, [they report] that a Lutheran patriot, with an Orange [?], and was treated with scurrilous language; that in a certain village a Protestant minister had always [sought to sway] 200 toward the Catholic religion; and similar improper accusations more; whereby they are making great complaints against the Protestants to the assembly, and [hoping] His Imperial Majesty might be moved thereby to prevent further redress of the Protestant grievances, for which the King of Prussia has undertaken [?], as his master [?], sent to his court, it is said that the Catholics will also issue the grievances of the Land of Cleves this week; which would already have happened, if He were not so occupied with other work; since that tract will be twice as large as the aforesaid one of the County of Mark. The Protestant ministers are still concerned for the Reformed and Lutheran [churches]; whereof they have spoken there with the Imperial Principal Commissioner, who has undertaken to dispose the Palatine Minister to write in their favor to his court. The assembly of the ministry requests of the Lutherans that the grievances of their fellow believers are now also redressed in the Palatinate, but until now nothing has been received from here and further. The Bamberg minister has also departed from here.

---

HAMBURG, 1 July. These days several ministers of the Duke of Holstein-Gottorp have departed from here to Eutin, where the Holy Father's Envoy from France has also betaken himself, to attend the celebration of the birthday of [the] Canon and Bishop, which is being celebrated there today. This matter, however, since it was [?], was postponed. One sees here the cargo of goods from the ship of Captain Niels Hofman, which on the 30th of this month is to be sold at Copenhagen, consisting of 2 [?] last of Tranquebar goods, 300 pounds of saltpeter, [...]

---

France

PARIS, 4 July. On Sunday, the Major of the Bodyguard received orders to go fetch the Wether Bullion and lead it before the King, whereupon that officer having taken it from the enclosure of the stables, brought it before His Majesty, who made many objections to it but dealt graciously; and the King also showed that he was well pleased to be able to put his wagers upon it, whereupon he again proceeded homeward over the same subject; however, it remained then in question whether he would not lose his wagers. The investigation of this is the reason that those inclined toward him say that it rained, whereas the supporters of his regency claim that it was not heavy rain, but rather a heavy fog that should be called such; meanwhile this investigation has caused him to flee in a great hurry, so that one saw whole rows of people assembled to observe him in this drift; also it was hoped that [?] would appear here; meanwhile he has been called by the jesters already with a beer as a reformer of the admiralty regiment. It is said that the Duke [?] said that he, coming to win as a sorcerer, should be [?], or that one should lock him up in the madhouse, where Sunday's fire occurred, but was yet extinguished. It is said that the Royal Library will be transferred to the Hotel Mazarin in order to be nearer at hand. On Monday evening at 7 o'clock, the Mr. John received a registered letter, by which he was removed from his position as syndic of the Sorbonne, his successor in office [being appointed] not to be found in the assembly of synods. The following day, as it was the day of the first of the month, the members of the Sorbonne assembled according to custom, and there 180 doctors [were present], to whom an order of the King was delivered to appoint Mr. Komigney, nephew of the Dean Observta, who is a great Molinist, in place of Mr. John as syndic, and it was resolved by 108 votes to send a deputation to the King to have Mr. John restored to that dignity...

France

PARIS, July 4th.

In the preceding month of October, matters came to an end, and for certain reasons in France, several matters at Court required handling. Among these, certain individuals who would not declare themselves were nearly brought before the Court for punishment; and this order was maintained quite strictly. In the King's consideration, various further circumstances were noted, as certain licenses failed to be obtained. On the same day, 200 Swiss Guards and Pages of the King's Household were chosen for the new appointments, and the Public Halls had given these the preceding Sums in the beginning of April.

The Duchess of Varignon, having completely recovered from her indisposition, intends soon to travel to the Estate of Jousatte to visit Mademoiselle, the great-granddaughter, who is of [?], who is there. It is said that the Duke of Chartres is in negotiations regarding his House in Pellarue des Forts, his country-seat in Meuil-Montagne; it is thought that the Duke agreed to surrender the Park of the same, a mile distant, to the Order of the Knights of the Duke of Orleans, which was considered no slight matter.

The King has agreed to the Brigadier Chiefs of the Gendarmerie, Bieverlet and all Militia of the Camp of his Army. It is said that nothing has yet been heard of the Marquis de Chalus; and it is not expected for some days. It is said that the Count de Gamache is under pressure; and for four days nothing more has been heard. The Sieur de Mille, First-Aide in the King's Troops and Officer in his Majesty, is said to have entered the service of Spain without the cause of this change being known. It is said that young Colbert caused this change, with three fair Ladies of the Court, and that on the night when the Ball was held, he was provided for this purpose with a Hunt and other suitable Places. There is still talk of the deeds of the Duke du Moane in all his Prerogatives and Freedoms; the Prince is currently in the Court with the Duchess, his lady, from which some pleasant news emerges. They are busy restoring the Arsenal, and at this time there will be a great Audience, because his new Palace opposite the Tuileries cannot be brought into condition, nor [?] great constant Money. The Counts have had these expenses, if they are to be obtained, as it is; but the mistrust here goes so far that no [?] history in [?] can be heard against his [?]. On the last of the preceding Month, the Sieur Reynolds, Lieutenant-General of the Swiss Guards, to visit the Duke du Moane, obtained no Audience with that Prince, because he showed little diligence during the disgrace regarding the House du Moine. Nevertheless, that Duke is heading toward St. Cloud to remain there some days. It is said that the new Boursier, formerly Treasurer of the India Company, finds himself much elevated, and an open-handed post of 76 Thousand to make good, which causes some to say that the Millions in the declining Line of Paper are being carefully guarded, although the same for a Year was so forceful in Rotterdam that the Street-brokers were running with various Millions daily.

The Directory has indeed been convicted, and it is not known yet what can be said concerning what was brought in on the last afternoon by the Notaries, whose Letters concern the 17th separately. The Bank of Amsterdam stands at 33 13 136, and the Bank-notes in Leiden are 50 Sols. There is talk of establishing a new Company, and of new Stock.

---

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, July 7th.

Today the Count of Windiguste was received with great joy by the Prince of Aasfeld at his Castle Boisfort, with a splendid meal, for which occasion a considerable hunt was arranged. The Countess d'Aspremont, Daughter of the Marquis de Thy, died at Maastricht; few having left that region since, after which illness proceeded like smallpox. Today the Prince of Baalen was expected here under the title of Count of Sponsheim; his private seat here has been established as Governor's residence, arranged according to the arrangements for his Excellency's convenience.

AMSTERDAM, July 7th.

On May 17th the Rivière lay frozen at [?], and it was expected that it would be passable by mid-July 1720. The Birds of Petersburg occupied themselves pleasantly with [?] and Cosel. The King of Sweden would see the Brilliant of Arms; and on the 29th [?] his Majesty, who with his entire Court still remains at [?], not until the end of July toward Petersburg. The Letters from Stockholm stated not only that there was a Splendid Display of Arms, but also that all Ceremonies would be signed to the [?]; however, this news has more air and servants. At Marseilles one watched with longing and glad heart the requested permission for the Birds and Armada to reopen again, to which the Ships, having been held 20 [?] on the Stream, would be dispatched. From Genoa have departed Maximus Dringet and others with ships toward London and Lisbon. At [?] lay [?] Thomas Agga and Fred. Riddes, as well as the Castle-Crown Jacob Hacke, all toward this City; the Bezemen Robert Abbon and Jan Beuzeman toward London; and at Dantzag Harm. Janke and others returned. At Dantzag were salutied Adr. Harraard, Corn. Meesls, and another from Boulogne.

---

Exchange Rates [Dutch Currency Tables]

The Rates of Exchange are today:

Madrid: 90 and 1/4 Seville: 114 and 1/2 Bilboa: 19 and 3/4 Lisbon: 41 and 1/2 Venice: 82 Livorno: 91 and 3/4 Genoa: 91 Yarva: [?] London: 14 Sch. 4 gr. Dantzag: 219 Foslie gr. Biscau: 5 and 3/4 Hamburg: 10 and 7 [?] Antwerp: 1 and 1/4 per Cent. Ghent: 2 Brussels: 2 Zeeland: 1 and 1/8 Rotterdam: 2

The Agio of the Bank: 1 and 1/8

Bank Stock Rates: East India Company (W.I.): 109; Subordinate: 930 English Bank: 115 East India Company (O.L.G.): 141 to 142 Zeeland: 116

---

The Hague, July 8Th

This morning the Swedish Minister, Herr Prys, was in Conference with the Gentlemen Deputies of the States-General.

[Various notices of public announcements and estate auctions follow]

---

Rome, June 21St

[News of Cardinals and Church matters]

---

Auction Notices

ROTTERDAM

The Burgomasters and Regents of the City of Rotterdam notify all that the City Lease will end on July 31st, 1721, and their Excellencies are of the intention on Monday, the 23rd of this month of July, to auction it for several Years. The same [?], beginning August 1st, 1721, with the arrangements necessary thereto; for information, apply to the Secretary of that City.

Messrs. P. Raket, D. Raket, Abraham van Dorelard, and Hend. Linsquet, Merchants, will on the evening of [?] at the Angel in the Burg, sell a considerable party of Linens and other Goods, consisting of circa 15,000 Coarse Linens, 1600 to 1100 broad and fine Muslins, 2000 Sanginettes, 3600 common Bedspreads, 600 broad and fine Breeches-cloth, Brittanies, Seals-cloth, Royals, Sun-silks, and [?], and on the Free-sale day can be viewed; the place where by [?] Cornelis Boezfelius, residing at Amsterdam beyond the Gate-port, at the Slip-Miller, [?].

AMSTERDAM

The Makelairs will sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday, July 16th, 1721, a party of Linens and other merchandise, salvaged from the stranded Ship Linacen, [consisting of] 1600 tons [?], 1200 tons and more, [?] Bedsprings, 500 [?] Bales, 700 Round [goods], 400 graduated Cannons, 1200 Dartes turpens, and other Goods. For further details, apply to the Notary [?], or to the Makelairs themselves.

Printed at Leiden by FR. LIX DE KLOPPER, over the Town Hall. July 11th, 1721. With Privilege.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday, all the Princes of the Royal Houses of the Blood assembled, excepting the Duke of Chartres and the Count of Toulouse, in Parliament, where all the Chambers gathered to deliberate on the matter of the Duke. The O.H. through Madame by the Protector-Magistrates presented the Request with like matters; the Curé requested that certain of the 22 Forcign Houses might be spared to them and the Rhine; the Liquor sellers and Distillers of the City also presented one, to approve the withdrawal of his Alicante Wines. It was ordered that this should be confirmed with them and communicated. The Cardinal would decide in these matters; and various procedures in these matters have not awaited that such Handling-Affairs would be signed by 200 [?]; even four Steeples of these Croyeurs in the Confrontation against him [were present], saying that he had made no better Period with them regarding the transport of certain goods. Thereupon Parliament resolved [?], and it was determined that all Individuals would be compelled to declare to what Guild of Godefield they wished to belong, since the first July 1720, until the day of the Ordinance; but because this order was made public through a Declaration of the Chamber, it is hoped that Parliament will approve its legitimization.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 9th of july, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 83.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 11th of July, 1721
Italy

NAPLES, 17 June. Since the week following Corpus Christi has not yet ended, the customary Procession is being organized anew; and the solemn singing of the Church takes place under our Cardinal Archbishop; the Viceroy with all the Ministers, and the officials of this City have arranged it appropriately. From Reggio comes news that the 2 requested galleys from Malta have arrived there, which are to join a Squadron in order to drive the Barbary Corsairs from their coast.

ROME, 21 June. Sunday morning, 2 Processions of the Venerable took place here, one in the Church of St. Maria dell'Anima of the German nation and the other in the Church of St. Louis of the French nation; in the first-mentioned procession, 36 Cardinals attended, who were invited there by the Cardinal of Althan; in the other came the Cardinals of Rohan, Biffi, Gualtieri, Borgo, Ottoboni, Aguillera, Belluga, all of whom were invited by the Cardinal of Rohan; the 2 last being indisposed, remained in the Church. That evening, Count Richelieu, the Princess of Avellino, and the Princess Cesara Cesarini, as well as many other distinguished Persons, were entertained magnificently at dinner, after which a magnificent Ball was provided that night. That same evening came the Courier from Count Kaunitz [Editor's note: possibly referring to diplomatic correspondence from Vienna] from Vienna with the Emperor's answer, and orders that this Count return to the Imperial court. This morning the Pope held a secret Consistory, after which he will go to all the Chapels, and then retire to his Court via Milan, to accompany his Sister the Countess Colloredo. Wednesday morning the Pope held a secret Consistory; in the presence of the nation, wherein the Cardinal Ruffo, Legate of Bologna, was present; and after various Bishoprics were presented, the Pope opened [proceedings] and through His Holiness the Bishop [and] several Cardinals, [decisions were made]: at the end of this Consistory, Innocent XIII elevated to the Cardinalate his Brother, whom he loved, according to the ordinary Ceremony at Rome, whereupon this new Cardinal was complimented by the Sacred College, and at the same time a general changing of the order of precedence at the Castle of St. Angelo took place. This promotion was celebrated for 2 days with uncommon rejoicing. That same afternoon a Congregation was held at the Quirinal by the Cardinals Ruffo, Patritti, Orsini, and Althan, attended by the gentlemen Celigola and Fiocaldi, Commissioners of the Apostolic Chamber, together with the respective Ministers: in this Congregation, discussion was held concerning the affairs of the Geliasians, and the trade in foreign cloth. Wednesday morning, it was rumored without much foundation that the matter of Cardinal Albani would be resolved soon, and that His Holiness might soon give him the Cardinal's Hat; but the Eminences are mistaken, [as he] has renounced the Bishopric of Malta, retaining however an annual income of 10,000 Scudi. That early morning Cardinal Ottoboni, Vice-Chancellor, had a Procession with the Venerable held in his Church, in which the Holy Laurentius and Damianus, which was conducted very magnificently, was accompanied by 33 Cardinals, 80 Prelates, and many Nobles: the Cardinals Aquaviva and Korban, the Commander with his Wife, the Ambassador of Venice, various Princes, and several gentlemen, witnessed this Procession pass through the Vestibule and the Balcony of the Church. Thursday morning a public Consistory was held, in which Cardinal Conti was admitted to the offices of the Pope; His Eminence received the other Cardinals; as is customary in such matters, the Court took leave; which having been done, the Pope gave him the Cardinal's Rod with the customary Ceremony on Thursday afternoon; His Eminence then visited St. Peter's Church, after which He received the visit of the Sacred College with Cardinal Tandin, Deacon of the same. Yesterday morning His Holiness with the Cardinals conferred the vacant Ecclesiastical Benefice of 14,000 Scudi annual income, vacant through the death of the Abbot of Lochringen [Editor's note: likely Lochreugen]; subsequently His Holiness declared the same Abbot Don Stephano Conti, Son of the Duke of Poli, to be Apostolic Protonotary, with provision over all the Apostolic Procuracies; [and] Commissioners; Sir Libstemon, his [relative] from Zutten, has been declared Commander of the Holy Ghost, in place of Mr. Doris, who is currently His Holiness' Curfew-keeper [?]: Mr. Nori Komery has been appointed Secretary of the Congregation of Military Orders: the Gentleman Convensatti, Bishop of Terracina, who had been ordered to come to this Court, on account of many complaints about his administration, has submitted many written documents; he has been admonished here; this Bishop has sought audience with many Cardinals, but was admitted by none of the same, not even by the Papal Usher; Cardinal Conti, whose Bishopric became vacant, has not even his Prolocutor supported; one is currently occupied in making his Prolocutor return, and his previous services [are being reviewed]. His Holiness has ordered that in the Church of Trastevere, [details concerning funds and compensation for expenses incurred follow, though the text becomes unclear here].

LEGHORN, 2 July 1721. Here it has been reported that the 2 Maltese Warships on the East [coast] - Here are mentioned two Warships, which the said Algerian Corsairs from Carthage in Malta, above Sicily under Carthage, [captured] 3 well-manned Galleys; and from this mentioned Algerian Pirate ship: having seen to take People aboard, the said Corsairs from Tunis, which was very heavily fought by the Spanish [?], have been taken and brought to Malta; the Government has given notice of this to its Minister at the Courts in Italy.

GENOA, 21 June. The English Warships, of which was spoken in the previous [dispatch], have departed from here, without obtaining satisfaction from the Republic on their claim; and some news came that the said Ships have meanwhile appeared near Reggio; the Genoese Ship of Captain Decotto, which had taken refuge in the Gulf of Spezia [?], [has been reported].

MILAN, 22 June. Monday morning the Governor received an Expedited message that the Pope has elevated the Gentleman Conti, His Holiness' Brother, to Cardinal. From Vienna has come an order concerning [matters regarding] those who here, whether ecclesiastical or other goods in the aforesaid State, possess, and live outside, [are to be] taxed higher, than those in the State proper by others.

[He] will continue his journey to Genoa tomorrow morning.

VENICE, 21 June. Sunday morning the Prince of Modena departed from here to Padua, after all his Equipage had been sent ahead. That morning one received by various Expeditions the sad news that the Adige had broken through at a place, and thereby considerable damage had occurred. The election of the Nautius is fixed for the 14th of the coming Month.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 24 June. The King, having recovered to his previous health, has departed, together with the Queen, to Kullaberg, where His Majesty yesterday took up the pleasures of the Hunt; it is believed that he will spend the greater part of the Summer outside in the Country. Two Dispatches from the Second Plenipotentiaries of the Crown have come to this Court, which Despatches, having been examined in Council, were sent back with all diligence. The combined War Fleet has advanced to approximately Cappelchail. The Imperial Minister, Count Frizke, still presses strongly at this Court that the Ministers request this Crown to send [representatives] first to Brunswick. The Crown Resident Buack has been dismissed from his Audience under certain conditions. It is much disputed whether shortly a Settlement between this Crown and Muscovy will be concluded.

Switzerland

GENEVA, 1 July. Since our Magistrate of Health has had many Emissaries in Provence, and a great number of private Persons receive news daily concerning the situation there, one flatters oneself here that few Places are better informed of our condition and the rage of the Sickness there; it is certain that the City of Marseilles is already completely freed; that it is ending at Aix; that the Sickness at Arles also nearly ceases: but at Toulon it continues in full force, and mortality in that City is as high as it has been; two-thirds of the People have already died there, and the remainder is in a pitiable state; but so long as the Sickness continued to rage only in Provence, one was more at ease here than now, on account of Languedoc and Dauphiné, because of their Barriers on the Rivers Rhône and Isère, which they have carefully guarded with Troops; but notwithstanding all precautions, it can no longer be concealed that the Contagion has penetrated into the Cévennes, and will particularly [affect] the Town Canourgue, which the Duke of Rocquelajure [has taken care to shut in], and will so well blockade it that nothing can come out; and yet he is reported to have burned the Village Curgan, [which lay] in the proximity of the affected region and its inhabitants, after some of them had already fled, and the remainder, sufficiently well-disposed, went into a Camp, where Huts were set up for them, and [they were] supplied with the necessary Clothing and Victuals; [it is] feared that [they will] not lack anything from Dolebreuced: [it is] said that there is no Sickness from that Land, which is confirmed by those from Lions, which are but 2 miles from there. Meanwhile this Government has taken good care to break off all trade with the said City of Lions; whereupon the latter have risen high in protest; but it is hoped that they will finally see that this does not press upon them so heavily as an Enemy; and that the unfortunate ones here find themselves sufficiently provided with their Paper, by which private Persons in the third City for certain Sums are provided for.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

REGENSBURG, 5 July. The Elector Saxon Minister has communicated to the Elector and the Protestant Ambassadors a writing from the Palatinate, containing again some Excesses, which occurred through a Procession in a Protestant Village there, [which causes the opinion] that the Vicar in this Place was badly affected; but the correct circumstances are not yet known: however, the Elector Palatine's Minister has well assured the Protestants that the complaints of the Community and Lutheran Subjects there through the good foresight of His Majesty are daily reconciled, [and that] a letter from his Court to some Protestant Ministers, which shows little regard for [it], has been confirmed by the Reformed Church Council there, being that in church, which at Swalbach has arrived, and which places itself in the company of the Hanoverian and Hessian Ministers, is daily in Conference, regarding his first ardor in the Palatinate; also, the matter is to be occupied [in] preparing the said Deduction, regarding the pretended Redress of Grievances published through the Palatine Court here, to refute, and concerning the abusiveness of the mentioned Articles [to be] kept before the eyes of the Protestant Corps. No news has yet arrived from Vienna, what the Catholic one courses, [and] which has taken upon the Protestant Rights; though those there with the Protestant Ministers [have] concerns on such matters.

MARSEILLE, 24 June. According to letters from Toulon of the 21st, the condition of the inhabitants of that city was not in good health; for besides the precautions taken, daily still [?] 66 to 70 people from the small number daily [...]; the administration has been forced to send more galley rowers, surgeons, etc. to that place to help those in distress. In this city they have again locked up some trusted inhabitants [...], and given the large number of people who attended to help [...] the sick; so that they hold hope of receiving health certificates, and more payment for the wages on ships bound for America. It is not yet known on what day the German churches and markets of this city will be opened.

PARIS, 15th. [Editor's note: The text here becomes heavily corrupted.] Of the envoys from Rome, Monday with his suite having arrived, it was thought there that the Pope would establish himself here in his family, with the hat likewise, which his [?] to his brother and also a good [?] to his other brother the Duke of Poll, a widow in the Celle; this would be disposed, it was thought, in favor of Alexander Albani, nephew of his Holiness the Predecessor. The appointment of the Cardinal of Koban is confirmed. The King of Spain has appointed the General Marquis [?] Linyt, General of his Fleet in consideration of his good and faithful service. The Priory of Poix in Picardy, belonging and having come by inheritance to that of la Trimouille, now comes to pass to the House of Richelieu, by decree of the [?] in [...] the Chamber on the first of this [...] and the Duke de la Trimouille first gentleman of the King's Chamber, and the widow of the Duke of Richelieu Peer of France: this decree, making the Secretary of Requests of the month of July 1720 void, exempts the Duke de la Trimouille from his inheritance of Naulinge on the Trimouille [...], and declared bare of offices of 23,000 livres with the conjugation which the Duke made in the month of May of the previous year. The trial of the Duke against the Abbot of Appoigny of the Order of St. [?], who has brought an actual office particularly to possession of the priory of Pompaone, near Lagas, which the said abbot claimed for longevity and advantage, but through the Jesuits for the Jesuits before the Council of the King is used, to the annulment of the [?] and 49 and is found Adam Zanillidis the pronouncement of the great Council [...] 249 Poolie 1 Breit [...] has not become. By the Chapter of Paris a trial is [?] and against the 2 Curates of St. Jean le Rond, Patrons of the Cloister [?] [...] and a half [...] it is [...] that on the day of St. Jean the [...] of the [...] Paris soon his small pastorate, a canon, as Primary Curate [...] would like to go through, what of the Curates having [...] much earlier in the church began and would settle him in the work of the Deaconship, that they [...] not otherwise than with the utmost [?]

---

LISBON, 12 June. [Text heavily corrupted] ...the Dutch merchants, and the band of merchants, which together [...] testifies the expedition Coimbra, Capt. Darby from Museum drawn, also one has [...] ...Coimbra of Bellanon, Laurens Andicelis Boge from Tars, and [...] for [...] [...], has arrived within this day.

---

CADIZ, 13 June. From Cartagena, and [...] of this [?] used [...] it is the case that [...] to [...] so that there is [...] in the South through the great quantity [...] common [...] market [...] [...]; the [...] with the [...] arrived [...] [...] has had [...] in the [...] of May in the hat [...] although [...] ship; arrived than Amsterdam, and the Holland [?]. The ship the Sonika and the Drome v. Capt. This [...].

MADRID, 14 June. [...] came; [...] but the Great Britain the Marquis Grimaldo, Councilor in State and [...] form Order of Philip [...] government of Guipuzco.

Madrid: 90 1/4 Cadiz: 1 1/2 to 1 1/2 Seville: 114 1/2 Bilbao: 89 1/2 Lisbon: 94 1/2 Venice: 84 Livorno: 91 1/4 Genoa: 91 Paris: 49 7/8 to 3/4 London: 14 1/4 1 gr. Danzig: 9 1/8 Poolie gr. Breslau: 1 1/4 gr. Hamburg: 11 7/8 1/2 ch. Antwerp: 1 1/4 pct Cent Brussels: [?] Zealand: 1 1/4 Antwerp: 1 [?] Bank Agio: 1 1/2 East India Company Agio: 760 [?] West India Company: 1 1/2 ag. Subscription: 970 to 975 English Bank: 130 East India Company: 140 1/2 South India: 130 1/2

---

THE HAGUE, 10 July. The posters of the convening, on which the States of Holland and West Friesland will assemble next Wednesday, have been sent to the respective cities of this province.

In all and further, that the right of redemption of the bookhouses within the state of receipt [...] on the day of this common land farming, upon the charges, the same right of redemption of the bookhouses have hastened, or are inclined to speak orally about it, or the conditions [...]

The lottery from the 9th to the [?] class [...] with the drawing of [?] class [...] on 4 August [...]

Thursday, at the house of the late Most Serene Lady Countess [...], will be publicly sold some very fine paintings by masters. [...] can be seen: the catalog is to be obtained at the [...] house in The Hague.

Negotiation and allowance within Gouda, until now not having been able to [...] in the [?] of life and [...], under guarantee of the city of Gouda, the irregular [...] in widely differing age [...] [...] [...] said [...], having been informed that for those eternal reasons [...], will for the sake of various persons the aforesaid negotiation [...] at the latest and no longer, and leave to those which [?] or more persons to improve [?] all on [...] [...], which and what kind by an arrangement and by a joining of persons in the aforesaid negotiation and under guarantee as force [...] [...] [...] and allowance within Gouda; will on Tuesday the 30th of this month a commission for convening [...] can participate in [...] orders paid with two parts in actions in the aforesaid comparison, the same each share, and one part paid in money; all further following the negotiated [?] in the [?] and bookhouses [...] will [...] 1913, will be sold at Rotterdam on Monday the 14 July 1721, [?] a [...] a ship's cargo extra curious purks purk selected old and new [...] by Catelines 200 as the community gallons [...] 3, and lie in the [...]

[?] and will be sold at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 16 July 1721, linen and other merchandise, will on the [?] ship the linen, 1600 new and white dishtowels, 1300 rough and white linens [...], 600 printed ballons, 700 [...], 400 printed [...], 2000 white chickens, and many other goods likewise [...] days before the [...] by [...] is indicated, which at the brokers is to be obtained [...]

[...] sold at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 16 July 1721, on the New [...] of [...] [...] of 16, 17 and 18 palm; 1800 long [...] [...] at 60 feet, and 7 to 10 palm; 300 hay and ditch [...] &c.; lying in [...]

[...] will on Wednesday the 16 July 1721, in the evening [...] a great ship's cargo purks purk all selected new Virginia [...] and a curious party [...] London [...] on the [?] ship [...]

in the [...] in the warehouses of the gentlemen Jakob Sende and Zoeitz, and will on the [...] [...] sale [...] [...] [...] to be obtained [...], and [...] for a civil [...].

DE KLOPPER, on the Breedstraat.

11 July 1721: With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES FOR TODAY**

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 11th of july, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 84.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 14th of July, 1721
Portugal

LISBON, 11 June. The ships that have been stationed off the coast, which were to be in the harbor from Bahia, are the Dutch ships with their convoy from São Ubaldo, with Captain Hardenbroeck and the merchants under his command, who are being set to wait and to prevent [them] from sailing to Teffal. From Saint-Malo, the expedition has been authorized for this bay under Captain Juan Vignon; and at Vieira the ship Coumba, Captain Daaij from here. The ship that departed from here last year for Macao arrived there safely in September; and there is also a report that the missing East India ship N.S. da Graça has also arrived. There have also arrived here Jean Nie. de Cournes from Bayonne, And. Kelli from Cork, Jan O[?] from Genoa, Laurens Andresde Boye from Amsterdam, Anth. Carvalho d'Affiliera from Para, and among others departed Will. Pupijte toward Hamburg. The fleet to Pernambuco, and the other ships to the Kijl, are to depart from here within 14 days.

England

MADRID, 24 June. On Monday last, the court moved to the Escorial, which the French ministers followed, but the [ambassador of] Great Britain remained in this city. The King has appointed the Marquis Grimaldo, in addition to his secretaryship of state, also to councillor of state. It is assured that the plan for the reformation of the troops, which his Majesty has consented to, has been entrusted to the execution of Guipúzcoa under Don González Chacón.

France

MARSEILLE, 27 June. The plague has ceased in this city for some time, so that no deaths have occurred in 13 days; but misfortune has willed that a servant maid, without the knowledge of her master (who had expressly forbidden it to her), went outside this city to a small farm, where she contracted the plague; and after she returned here, she was immediately brought to the hospital, where she died within 24 hours. This fatal misfortune has caused the quarantine to be begun anew, which will continue for 40 days, provided that no death from plague occurs in that time; otherwise the time must be begun anew. The inhabitants are currently praying earnestly that the sickness may be extinguished, without the least return, which they are tending with great diligence.

PARIS, 7 July. It is believed that Monsieur de Comenius, newly appointed Syndic of the Sorbonne, is a strong proponent of the Constitution. The commotion concerning the appointment of the said syndic has been related with some detail. This faculty, under the leadership of the said syndic, in the first meeting of the assembly next August, hopes to be attacked; so it is preparing itself against attacks to hold firm, and rather to sacrifice itself than to yield. On Friday three carriages full of people were brought to the Bastille; but it has not been possible to learn who they are, or for what reason they were imprisoned, though it is suspected that some doctors of the Sorbonne will be among them, who have acted too vigorously against the Constitution and the procedures of the Parliament. The Bishop of Soissons has been appointed in previous days to occupy the vacant place of Monsieur d'Argenson in the French Academy. According to news from Toulouse, the religious students of the university of the city held the opening of the Congregation of Saint Thomas in the church of the Preachers; the Lord Regent, dean of the cathedral church of Saint Stephen, celebrated the high mass, which was sung in music. In the afternoon the Vespers of the feast day was celebrated, and the doctor [?] followed in the singing of the Vespers; the prayer of the Congregation was sung in the chapel of Saint Thomas, and then an official [?]. The Count of Chelus, who had been missing for some time, is reported to be in the Bastille, without having been heard from. Today a Monday [there] passed through the gates of Paris of Sicily [?] to be in Parliament, where all the chambers assembled [?]. He shall then be obliged to appear in court without sword, cane, or dagger, [as ?] the whole [?] was raised through a bequest of relatives.

Great Britain

LONDON, 8 June. On Saturday the Queen appeared in the House of Peers with the customary ceremonies; [those of] the Lower House upon the message of Knight Will. Sanderson, Usher of the Second [?], being assembled, His Majesty gave his Royal assent to an Act to continue the impost on malt, mum, cider and perry drink, and to raise money by means of lottery for the service of the year 1721, &c.; and to an Act to grant his Majesty's subjects the trade in the East Indies, under the commissions of foreign powers, so that through favorable trade in those lands it may be better sustained, and to better regulation of the ports of Dover, Deal and the Isle of Thanet; and some other public and private bills. The King, having taken his council, has appointed Lord Carleton to be president of his secret council, in place of Lord Townshend, who is secretary of state, which office his lordship immediately took possession of. That day the King with the Prince and Princess of Wales was in the Royal Chapel, where His Majesty heard the preaching of the Duke of Barnet; Lord Carpenter bore the sword of state. Apart from the [report] concerning the annual report of the South Sea Company, it is reported that the gentleman Locket in his examination said that it appeared to him that the South Sea Company had loaned 218,1244 pounds on 578,888 pounds. [There was such instruction, which no longer had any further reference to the Company;] the Knight John Bland added to this, that he believed the man of these circumstances might discover something among the 574,160 pounds in fictitious names, which were laid down to be sold, and he still believed them to be transferred to the account of belief. The gentleman Surman says he believes that well, what persons which [had] fictitious names, [had] borrowed money on the same on the company's account, and for this reason no report could take place; and he was even of the opinion that the 578,888 pounds, on which money was taken, without [the other] having been forwarded, is a corruption of 574,400 pounds in fictitious names. The unfortunate owners of the bankrupt shares have approached Mr. Walpole in body, to make that gentleman a clear representation of the damage which has been suffered in their case; but one cannot yet ascertain what answer they received; meanwhile it is certain that they have decided to likewise present a petition to the King in body (which shall be drawn up in the French language). On Saturday those of the Lower House elected the member for Whitchurch, and the choice was made in favor of Mr. Conduir, in place of Mr. Telney, who had already taken his seat in the House. Yesterday the Earl of Lecester, Lord Viscount Cheyne and Miss Elizabeth Mather presented a petition to the Lower House, requesting that the said lady be allowed to keep the interest of the money which she brought as dowry to Knight Hurcoin Mather, and that her sister's money should not be placed at the disposal of the commissioners, who were appointed for that purpose to the execution of the bill, for the benefit of the unfortunate losers in the South Sea Company, but that to the Earl of Lecester, and the Viscount Cheyne, as guardians of the said Elizabeth [?], there be allowed to receive the distribution of 3,100 pounds capital in the company, because the said lady had desired this sum before the date of marriage; which petition [one believes] whether it will be read; wherein the House in grand committee [turned to] the report concerning the bill for the relief of the unfortunate losers in the South Sea Company, and the clause concerning Mr. Aislaby, [they have decided], to wit, whether one should confirm his received orders since the year 1719, or those since the year 1720, and the aforementioned year it was to be continued since 1720; when the gentlemen Walpole, and others in opposition, particularly saw to it that such [a matter] was [brought] in the House of Lords, and likewise it was foreseen that...
