No. 1721.No. 97.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 13th of August, 1721

SWITZERLAND

BERN, 2 August. Several French deserters, having approached our borders, attempted to seize the opportunity; and since the said 100 are closely guarded, and it was reported from Savoy that this canton had ordered [?] such persons, as well as all those concerned, some [?] were sent on 27 [?] to turn them back. Some delegations from France coming via Liège, where they underwent quarantine, are not permitted here in the usual manner; those who claimed to have undergone quarantine must still submit to it, so that their clothes will be burned and themselves perfumed with certain essences; it is hoped that all these measures will prevent the communication of contagion. Recently a small doctor, whom the King of Sardinia sent to Germany to inform himself how matters there were managed during that time, and by what means the plague was combated, has returned; meanwhile it is said that he discovered many useful matters for the public. The assembly at Frauenfeld is concluded, but that at Baden continues its sittings.

GERMANY AND NEIGHBORING KINGDOMS

REGENSBURG, 4 August. Yesterday a great solemnity was celebrated by the Nuncio of Saxe-[?], who will depart this day or tomorrow to the monastery of [?], to remain there 12 days, and this week kept a court day with his entire household concerning the fire in [?]. The Palatine Minister expressed [?] for the situation; the good society is guided by the arrival of the [?], and tomorrow, and the following day one expects also the ministers of Prussia and of the Bishop of Bamberg, so that the same [?] will return to resume the [?]. Since the announcement which the Lord of Keck made of his journey to the Palia, it has been understood from him differently; some do not fail to surmise that something disagreeable must befall him, yet others judge that he is [?] to do [?] for the court, which on account of his [?] came too soon, since the nearest important ministers are still employed and engaged, and through their arrival the Palatine Minister is also [?], and makes it known especially that one does his main reason in [?] case, to the end of the person who has no fitting character and is very disagreeable to him; yet regarding the execution of the ordained [?], expositions of his minister in [?] of Ammenrode would have [?], he reports at present [?]; should one be informed that if necessity required such obligations, the Armand [?] of the land would be mentioned for this.

FRANCE

ART. 5, 24 July. The plague continued to cause great ravage here; for in Tuscany at a burgomaster's residence it came through, and all the servants who were in service by people of rank, [?] of 30, which were employed to accompany the body of Cardinal Imperatori, and the Prior Jesuit [?], who was near the Archbishop; this Father was confined to Civita Vecchia and [?], who also followed from the court there below; thus possibly the Pope through all his company was greeted, and in this in turn those of these, which may have happened, are that the inner [?] flattered themselves that the inner nation would, which may have happened, which yet may be considered Domestic of all Markets will follow.

---

Wednesday's Courant.

In these recent days we have had 800 dead in two days; but that number at some borders has diminished so much that there are now but 100 persons dying daily; what is worst is that all remains still in great confusion, and they, having the bodies, for lack of burials, must leave them lying some days in the streets.

PARIS, 9 August. The King finds himself at present in perfect health. The Duke of Villeroy had sung a prayer in the Cathedral Church the preceding Sunday for the dead. On the 4th of this month the King wrote the following letter to the Cardinal de Noailles, Archbishop of Paris.

MY COUSIN,

I come to receive a new token of Divine protection in my recent and dangerous illness, from which His providence has preserved me; in this occasion I have felt the sense of His power and His goodness; both one and the other oblige me to express my submission and gratitude. How great may be the thanksgivings which I must render in these duties, and the repeated demonstrations of affection which I have received from my subjects; assure me that they will support me with zeal: I write you this letter by the advice of my uncle the Duke of Orleans [Editor's note: the Regent], to tell you that you shall have the Te Deum sung in the Archbishop's Church of my good city of Paris, on the day and hour which the Grand Master, or the Master of Ceremonies shall tell you on my behalf. I command him to invite my courts, and those who are accustomed to assist therein. May you remain in God; I pray that He keep you in His holy care. Written at Paris the fourth August 1721, signed LOUIS.

and [?] Parisians.

On the sixth day the Parliament had the Te Deum sung in the great hall of the Palace for the restoration of the King; which the court of the nobility and others did likewise, which occasioned an illumination and rejoicing throughout the entire city; which continued for three or four following days. The citizens of the city brought diverse hogsheads of wine for the public good. On the 3rd of this month the Parliament complimented His Majesty on his fortunate recovery; the first President spoke, and after that gentleman was [?], he found the carriage of the Duke of Villeroy, in departing from his carriage, by which he was brought to the hall of the guards and [?] was carried back in his carriage. That day the ambassadors and foreign ministers and majesty likewise complimented on his recovery. During the illness the King showed courage and reason far beyond the reach of his years, having not the least fear, nor any impatience by [?]. On the 5th his Majesty dressed again according to custom. On the 7th day [Te Deum] was sung in the Church of Adorable [?], in thanksgiving for the King's recovery, with all the usual ceremonies; wherein the Cardinal de Noailles, Archbishop of Paris, performed pontifical functions; the Duke Regent, the Duke of Bourbon, the Count of Charolois, the Prince of Conti, and the Count of Toulouse attended; as did the Counselor of France, attended with many councilors of state and [?]; yes ladies, the most spiritual clergy, the Parliament, and various courts, the Court of Aids, and the government of this city, who were invited by the Mr. de Granges ceremony master for this thanksgiving, attended the same; the crowd of courtiers was very great. They continued to have it sung every day; on the 7th the gentlemen of le Louxe sang in the Church of St. Thomas, wherein the Marshal of Villeroy and other nobles attended during the Te Deum; and this thanksgiving was there attended by the firing of 12 cannon for the gate of this church. The King showed himself publicly on the balcony to the joy of the people; and all the foreign ministers likewise showed joy over His Majesty's recovery. The gentleman Belletust, A. of the nobility who spoke at the foot of the 3rd King's illness, has been gifted with a pension of 6,000 livres. At night between the 3rd and 4th the Duke of Chartres [?] and spoke a [?], but after he on the 4th in the morning [?] got up, this Prince felt better, and has since recovered completely. The Duke of Albert and Duke of Bouillon, as great chamberlains of France, take an apartment in the Palace of the Tuileries; to this end one of the [?] galleries of the Marshal of Villeroy will be [?], where one will now make that of the Duke of Bouillon, and one will take from the gallery of the palace, to place the 40 [?]. On account of the newly elected Councils, one has also appointed new [?] in the Council of government, the decision in these matters being postponed until one has collected and assembled all the declarations of the provinces, which is being worked on without ceasing. The request that was presented in the Parliament by more than 100 doctors [?] has been answered; one has resolved not to defend any more theses in the Sorbonne before the King himself has approved and the rectified President is authorized to present the request to the King. The preceding Sunday, the 3rd of this month, the Turkish ambassador departed thence, and took several days on the Seine, in a boat of the Count of Toulouse, after that prince had entertained His Excellency at evening meal; the following day the Count gave him the pleasure of the hunt, and on Tuesday this ambassador departed thence, and the following night lodged at Fontainebleau, from where he will continue his journey to betake himself to the harbor of [?] to embark.

France

The clergy in this city are much troubled by the matter into which Cardinal de Noailles and his subordinate clergy have been drawn. Nevertheless, His Majesty will be in Medoc in a few days: the joy in this city over the recovery of the King's health is extraordinary; considerable gifts have been made to the needy, and a certain notable figure has erected a renewed [monument?] that has attracted attention. There has been talk that the King will soon hold a Lit de Justice to settle certain matters concerning the Parlement of Maine at the outset [?]; however, it appears for reasons that such matters may still be delayed somewhat. Some letters suggest that negotiations may yet take some time between the Young Prince of Bavaria and the Archduchess Josephine before the matter is concluded, although others have no doubt about the progress of this marriage. It is now said on the contrary that the marriage between the Prince of Piedmont and the Daughter of the King of Portugal is agreed upon, and has been concluded, and that an order was sent to Turin for a magnificent equipage for the aforementioned Prince, and his future Bride, should she travel to Italy, to be collected; although it is said that this journey thither may well not take place until autumn. An envoy of the British Minister Stanhope has been sent from here to London. It is said that the former Duke of Ormond has betaken himself from Madrid towards [?]. It has been discovered that a person who was dressed as a Hussar and was apprehended by the guard of the King between the 3rd and 31st of July last near St. Roch is a clergyman in that parish; the officer who held him has been dismissed from his post, although he has since been reinstated: this educated priest spent the night in the wagon and was brought before the commissioners the next day in the same wagon; after a brief examination, they had him taken back in the carriage: it has not yet been possible to ascertain the reasons why this clergyman dressed himself as a Hussar. The so-called house of the late Ambassador has, during his stay in this city, been thoroughly cleaned and repaired: it is assured that the costs will amount to several thousand livres to restore everything to order: and it is said that one of his servants, who fell in love with a young lady in this city, took his own life in a melancholy manner. A shipment from London containing certain drugs was brought [here], and having to observe quarantine at the frontiers, upon arriving in this city found a gracious reception from the Commander, and the same, speaking with no certainty, [suggests?] that presents were given to His Turkish Excellency by those who were attending to him. Albert Köhl [?] has made [statements?]; it is reported that the aforementioned Ambassador costs this crown over 2 million livres, the livres being valued at [?]; this will be besides what the Princes and other great lords of this realm had to pay for the same. The woman [mistress?] who is with the same, and now seeks to purchase a great house, was on Easter day, the day before his departure, driven away in an open carriage. At Lyons it is said that [the] magnificent Beltrain is to receive the Turkish Ambassador there. That the reconciliation between the Duke of Parma and the Princess Friedels is as complete as was reported, though he himself still speaks of it with some doubt.

The Netherlands

LONDON, 8th August. On Wednesday the members of Parliament made their way in procession to the House of Commons to discuss the Revolution and public credit, concerning which the Government has decided in my previous Courant to hold Parliament. It is said that very many gentlemen appeared sorrowful there. The Viscount of Linton has been made Lord of the Treasury of Wales. The King did not appear in the House of Lords as in the previous year, this being a measure concerning the Act of Pardon, which was read from the Lords [?], approved, and handed to the Commons, who have read and approved it. The Smugglers of Paris have the bill for the license; through the return of the Smugglers of Paris, several more days have passed and all from the closing of the Port of Parliament, which the King will make. The shares of the Bank are 131, O.G. 143, Z.Z. 140, Mill. Bank 150, the Exchange [?] ditto London.

BRUSSELS, 11th August. The dispute between the members of Flanders and the Administrators of the revenue of this province has not yet been terminated; and although the Council of State has been assembled, the Council of State has made provision in the matter through two strict commissioners of the French; regarding the condemnation of a debt of 30,000 guilders which the aforementioned members claim; and the aforementioned administrators, not showing that they have reciprocally transferred the administration in collection, to the disadvantage of the province, have been unable to obtain such good justice as they had proposed for themselves; the matter has been suspended, as it is said that the members will take up a sum of 30,000 guilders on the administration, in the paying of the mortgaged [property?], on that which makes the security and credit of the province, and thereby, as far as possible, to pay, and the administration [?] those who are concerned know that the messenger within the city [?] on Monday from the 13th of July on a Saturday or Sunday coming the [auction?] on Monday eight days, as they are held, the Admin. There is missing an obligation for the community land company Leyden, dated the 17th [?] entered as No. 11, fol. 46, signed and registered on 1st June 1791, for the sum of 600 guilders; whoever brings the same to the author of this Courant [?] on Monday the 13th of August 1721, there shall be in The Hague at the Kolveniere. [?] at the Gravesande; likewise two other plots of land, [?] on the [?] carried in the gazette to the [?], the Amsterdam by Mr. Maarten Schilperooorde de Wael, and in Rotterdam at [?]

On the 20th of August 1721, in the evening at 6 hours, at the [?] in the Burghery; a parcel of [?] leather for merchant goods, a parcel of Muscovite yarn, ditto linens, and other fine [?] [?]; 1200 Muscovite rough hare-skins, 3000 on [?]

COIN. de Ruos, auctioneer in Amsterdam, hereby offers for sale a fine dwelling, situated in the city of Beverwyck on the broad street, called the [?] house; [?] the house previously [?]; and the [?] next to the [?] is to be seen by the aforementioned [?], dwelling on [?] house [?], dwelling on the [?] the [?], in the [?] of the city of Leydensdorp, and is intended to erect a coal school there where a [?] 3 [?], as also in the Sheds, [?], and [?], like [?] which [?] will be carried through by him, to which he [?], and a beautiful garden for [?].

Italy

NAPLES, 22nd July. On the previous Thursday for the first time a public visit was made to the city, which [?] the great Palace of the Lord [?] over the happy arrival of her Excellency [?] distinctions of the past [?] the good [?] with her [?] Maria of Piz da Gratie the [?] her lady of Carmine, has attended the [?] is the church of the Name.

West Indies

JAMAICA, 14th May. Our barks do not lie readily thereabout at the seaside [?]. Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 15th of August, 1721

WEST INDIES.

JAMAICA, 16 May. Our barques still lie at anchor on the coast, as we are awaiting about two more ships for this purpose. News is received here from England that the trading posts at Portobello, Vera Cruz, etc., will be opened as before the war. Within 14 days, ships laden with merchandise will depart from here, bound for London, Bristol, etc. No news is heard here now of any corsairs or Spanish fleet. From Bristol have arrived the Salaams, Robert Chappel, and the Royal Flute, George Winton.

ITALY.

NAPLES, 22 July. Last Thursday the Viceroy's wife made her first public outing on the occasion of the Feast of this City, during which she went from the Palace under a ceremonial canopy with her Family, and on this occasion displayed many noble courtesies. Saturday morning the Viceroy's wife with her two daughters attended Mass in the Church of St. Maria del Carmine. On Sunday, the Viceroy himself held Mass in the Church of the Holy Name.

ROME, 26 July. Saturday morning the Pope purchased the Library of the late Cardinal Michelangelo for 14,000 scudi, [Editor's note: the cardinal had bequeathed it] to the Holy See through his nephew Don Gontarello. That evening Count Kinsky, the Portuguese Ambassador, made his first public entry, which he had not done upon his initial arrival, setting out in a coach without a train, and passed through the grand procession to do so. Sunday morning the Pope went to the Church of the Sisters of St. Ursula, where His Holiness performed the ceremony of giving the religious habit to the Sisters Professes, daughters of the Prince [?], to whom His Holiness, when he was Cardinal, had given the veil in the previous year; this solemn profession was attended by Cardinals, many priests, and noble personages. After the ceremony His Holiness went into the cloister, where he sat in the choir and administered communion to all the Sisters and priests with the required reverence. That same day the Portuguese Ambassador visited the Court at Tivoli; the Pope granted the Ambassador a grand reception and to make a splendid equipage for his public entry, for which purpose on that same day the Pope dined with Cardinal Bichi, Count Kinsky, and the Cardinals of Althan, Schönborn, Schurenbach, Chittegros, Botta, del Giudice, Gualterio, and Orthebase, dressed in great magnificence. Wednesday the said Count dined with Cardinal Bichi, and then in the afternoon began his journey to Germany; intending to remain in Milan during the hot days to enjoy the air, and subsequently to proceed to Vienna. That afternoon the Father [?] with his entourage departed for Germany, and after taking leave of the Pope at an audience, went to the Cardinal's residence at Albano, where Cardinal Aquaviva [Editor's note: prominent Vatican official], in accordance with the esteem in which the late Pope Clement XI held him, gave him a splendid reception; the following morning they departed with great pomp. The daughter of Don Carlo Albani was christened in the Palazzo Farnese with great ceremony, the Portuguese Ambassador performing the function of godfather in the name of the King, in the presence of Cardinals Farnese, Acagna, Barberino, Scotti, Olivietti, and the two Cardinals Albani. After the baptism the said Ambassador hung about the child's neck a portrait of the King of Portugal set with diamonds, valued at 6,600 scudi. Notwithstanding the opposition that Cardinal Aquaviva had made, His Holiness gave out of devotion several hundred scudi and built a chapel in the city of Civitella in the Papal States, which the Pope, before he was Cardinal, received with all Christian civility and hospitality when His Holiness passed through Spain on his way to his Nunciature in Portugal. The tears of Cardinal de Noailles, which as mentioned in the previous Courant were reported by Cardinal de Rohan to His Holiness, have been referred to the Congregation of the Holy Office, and according to the usual procedure of that Congregation, His Holiness will determine whether to respond to him or not. That Cardinal Albani remains incognito in these territories is confirmed by the fact that Cardinal de Rohan has paid him a nighttime incognito visit.

BOLOGNA, 19 July. Last Saturday a great flood occurred here, as a violent torrent broke loose at the beginning near Castle Giulio, extending to the fortress of St. Raphael outside the gates of St. Etienne, overflowing four times, and spreading the plains toward the side of Ciozzo. The damage caused by this flood is so great that it cannot yet be assessed.

GENOA, 21 July. With various ships that have entered this harbor, it has been reported that some Algerian galleys and a caïque arrived, having pursued and captured an enemy vessel.

MILAN, 30 July. The two famous bankers, the Sine and other merchants dealing in exchange, have gone bankrupt.

VENICE, 4 August. The reliquary of the Blessed Mary, having been displayed in the Church of St. Marcus for several days and being venerated by the people on this occasion, the Relic was carried in procession, the Doge and the magistrates accompanying it, and the weather having been favorable, many prayers were offered; still, no sign of summer weather has yet been felt, wherefore it is feared that if this continues, the harvest will suffer. The Cardinal Bishop of Padua, Conti, has come to this city from Rome; now he has received compliments on his return from all the nobles. Besides the ship from Marseille mentioned in my last letter, another has arrived for this harbor, its cargo valued at about 30,000 ducats, consisting of sugars, elephant tusks, spices, coffee, and cochineal; the magistrate of health has likewise sent it to [the lazaretto at] Lido, to undergo along with the other a rigorous quarantine, the said ships being continuously guarded and watched by two galleys.

SWEDEN.

STOCKHOLM, 30 July. Another courier has arrived here from our plenipotentiaries at Nystad; according to their report, the courier last sent there had not yet arrived at Nystad, and it is expected that the conditions (by which the signing of the preliminary articles had not yet taken place) will be clarified. The dispatches brought by this latest courier were delivered and concerned various other matters. Since it has been reported that by order of His Czarist Majesty several galleys from Finland were to proceed to Petersburg, there was some suspicion that the Tsar might be contemplating some undertaking; however, it is otherwise stated that the fleet came merely to put such vessels in readiness at Helsingfors. His Majesty's nameday was celebrated with great magnificence at Karlskrona, where Admiral Norris and the volunteers of the fleet were present; His Majesty being complimented by the senators and ministers on this occasion. The Tsar's ministers are expected here this week. Count Horn, who is nearly of the same age, has not yet left [?].

GERMANY AND NEIGHBORING REALMS.

VIENNA, 2 August. This morning Their Imperial Majesties attended Mass in the Cathedral. The Dowager Empress Amalia Wilhelmina, who is at her country house at Schönbrunn, where she will remain until the 22nd of this month, has begun to use the taking of the waters [at the spa]. Count Erdődy, the Emperor's Ambassador at the court of Poland, is expected here daily from [?] to report to His Imperial Majesty on certain matters concerning the kingdom of Poland. The Religious Commissioners at Pest have received an Imperial Decree, in accordance with which they may absent themselves during August to attend to their private affairs. From Sicily it is reported that a natural son of a certain great personage was executed there, after his hands and feet had been cut off, which sentence was carried out upon five coins. It is remarkable that his brother, a legitimate son who is a clergyman, was sentenced to death and led to the gallows. It is said that the Scottish Lord Forbes, who has entered the Emperor's service with the knowledge of the King of Great Britain, has submitted a project to His Imperial Majesty concerning how one could augment the Emperor's naval forces in the Adriatic Sea to 25 or 30 warships, apart from the galleys which could be added as needed, to be ready at sea for all contingencies; the same Lord has moreover submitted an estimate of the costs that would be required for such a construction of ships. It is spoken of as if the court would be inclined to establish a commerce company in one of the ports of the Mediterranean Sea to begin this trade with Italians and other merchants. It is even said that one would wish to use the harbor of Civitavecchia for this purpose. It is reported that the Emperor is not well pleased with certain writings from rulers in the Empire containing threats; moreover, there are complaints concerning the Greeks, who suffer persecution as Protestants in those lands. An expedition with important dispatches from the King of Poland from Saxony has arrived at this court; it is said to concern negotiations regarding the peace in the North. One cannot well understand why some neighboring powers are gathering their troops on the frontiers. The revival of the Patriarchate has begun to show itself anew in some places in Italy; and this was discussed in the farewell audience that His Holiness gave to the Imperial Minister Count Kinsky. This court desires that the Congress regarding the peace in the North, to be held at Brussels, should make progress; but the ministers of some powers do not seem to make great haste to go there.

REGENSBURG, 7 August. Up to now the matter remains as it was, regarding the objections that the Elector-Prince Minister raised against the Lord of Reck, because he had returned to the Palatinate in the company of the Empress; indeed, from the said Lord of Reck, no news has been received by the Protestant ministers here, though something unpleasant may have befallen him, which was also suspected might occur, owing to the delicacy of a demonstration that could have produced bad consequences. The affairs of the Empire cannot yet be settled, since the minister of the Bishop of Bamberg has departed alone, and the Cardinal of Saxony has gone to the Imperial court to remain there for some days, undertaking to present the matters that his predecessor was recently instructed to bring before the Imperial Diet, which concern religion chiefly, though the Chamber of [?] is also somewhat affected; until these are brought to effect, [?]. The Palatine minister is frequently in conversation with His Eminence and does all he can to have the Count of Reck leave the Palatinate [text becomes fragmentary]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 15th of august, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 18th of August, 1721

PORTUGAL

LISBON, 15 July. After conferences have been held here regarding the forthcoming marriage between the Princess Infanta and the Princess [?], of which mention was made previously, the Marquis Carafelli has likewise departed from Portugal and England, being well satisfied with the negotiations regarding the marriage, which has been approved.

CADIZ

CADIZ, 23 July. Finally word has arrived via Tripolis in Barbary concerning what was sent along with the Galleons, etc., which has now been brought above the Canaries, so that people are hopeful of greater abundance and [?] in this land at the end of this month here, and people are pleased to report affairs again at Seville. Meanwhile here in this week several announcements have been made. Wherefore the Patriotic [?] currency pieces, 200 in pieces of Eight Reales, and other currency, [?] from the Mints of Madrid, Cuenca, Seville, and Segovia have arrived, which have also been distributed to the [?], which has caused growth in commerce—

MADRID, 29 July. Yesterday afternoon the great British Minister Stanhope sent the Courier to his Court with the concluded Treaty so that everything is in order.

FRANCE

PARIS, 7 [August]. On the day when [?] was sung in the Amsterdam Episcopal Church, there was a Consistory [?] the Bishop of Leon, [?] of Paris, and the other Bishops and Paris, and while the [?] have [?], tomorrow the Chancellor had him confess to take his place at the head of the [?] Bishops. The King finds himself very satisfied, as well in the stomach, so that a certain healthy red and pale color over [?], and one must say that the King—

PARIS, 11 August. On the day when His Majesty [?] in the landing of the episcopal church was received, there was a Confession [?] between the saints and Paris [?] of the saints in the sitting have [?], whereby the [?] has him through confessions to take his place at the head of the [?] Bishops. The King has not sufficiently recovered, his Majesty being but a slight [?] and pale color over bleached, and one must say that the King's faithful years have been constrained, likewise there has been an absolution, a [?], a Laurentia, and two [?]. The [?] community legends shown gratis; but through the great abundance of [?] Lorges, the Companies were unable; and here they committed a great sin where their souls were constrained to cut short of the pale: the following day the Opera also performed for that.

The same day much illumination, and in a [?] of that previous day fell illuminations, and in a [?] of the piece a royal Fireworks was discharged, which the long gentlemen; were well recovered in the [?] table [?] the discharge of the subsequent Fireworks, the Friends [?] have made.

GREAT BRITAIN

LONDON, 12 August. Friday the Speaker of the House of Lords reported that the House, the preceding Wednesday, had waited upon the King with his Address, whereupon His Majesty was pleased to give the following gracious Answer.

Regarding the death of Cardinal de Noailles, which seemed to occur in order that he was thereby prevented from this, having himself [?] why [?] at him was not first made known in the Parliament, answered that one in this matter occasion never could be ready with his Serene [?]. The King daily attended Mass in his Chapel. Except to take over the great [?] Arrangement of the Finances, which of a Baptist [?] in the [?] by an Arrest there to learn. There is a Report from the Marquis de Musftnrier from the Spanish Court here arrived. Yesterday another Express from the Great Britain, with the authenticate of the Crown of Spain on the privileged Corsication, with Great Britain [?]. One sees an Arrest from the King for Patents of State of 1 August, to diminish the Royal [?]. The letters from Madrid speak strongly of [?], Daughter of the Regent [?]. The Count de Tabai from the House of Beveroo, on his Balcony [?], is through a Faint [?] Stratagem, and from above on the ground fallen dead. It is the Cardinal de Robas, and not the Cardinal de Bou, who is endowed with the Abbey of Orleans. From Orleans it is written that the used sleds in the Wood of that same name is questioned [?], and that such was [?] through means of [?] of a man from the wages, which now also his [?] became, and him under that appearance betrayed has, [?] is he well beaten and turned from there to this City sent, where one him to all [?] brought to meet not.

"I have such complete confidence in the diligence and promptness of this Parliament to me, and the same piety before the Fulfilloquien service, that I very readily shall allow in them no [?] the all You well haste a [?] to do what [?] to the briefing of the public Credit, to the same of the Kingly.

Yesterday the King and the King in the House of Peace, and in his Royal Garment with the customary Solemnities upon the Throne seated, was the Lord William Sanderton, Bringer of the Black Rod and [?] Message of the King toward the House of Commons sent, to have them beseech themselves in the House of Peace to withdraw. Arriving there, His Majesty, that Royal [?] Hemming from to the Adcom shown p. St. the same.

One Monday is the Queen in the House of Peace, and in his Royal Garment with the customary Solemnities upon the Throne seated. Here was the Lord William Sanderson, Bringer of the Black Rod, with two Headship of the King toward the House of Commons taken, to have them here in the House of Peace care, where they come having, his Majesty, thereof Royal [?] Hemming gave ter com Adcom 50000 p. St. to place on the Incomes of the City [?]: to an Adcom Money is placed on the claimed [?] Goods of the [?] under the circumstance Government, Directors, Officers, &c. of the Z. Z. Company, and still to 4 public, and 6 private [?], were under the public those of the King's general Hantos; after which the Lord Chancellor, at the King's command said,

Miland and Extraordina.

His Majesty's, Royal, will and pleasure is, that this Parliament be prorogued to Monday the eleventh of this month August, to then be heard: being this Parliament thus to respective Monday the eleventh August prorogued.

Last night the Queen in the House of Peace, whereupon the Lord William Sanderson, Bringer of the Black Rod, with a Message of the King to those of the Lower House went [?] where they come having, the Speaker to the House said, that the King the same hereafter [?] in the House of Peace, upon which this one come was, where upon the Chancellor in the King's name the following Address made to both Houses of Parliament,

Miland and Extraordina.

The reason why I call you together again so quickly is to give you opportunity to [?] the matters of Consideration over the public Credit.

Extraordina of the Lower House.

The progress which you have made in this matter during the last sitting has laid such ground for this necessary work, that the world has a complete beginning of what, in every wise in this present Conjunction to hope for is.

Miland and Extraordina.

I must commend you all possible progress, and I hope moreover that in this time of the year your Deliberations well closed shall be, is how great ablaut; necessarily is on this ordinaire occasion.

This of the Lower House is thereupon back seized being, opened the sitting with the first Reading of the Bill for better arrangement of the public Credit, in the preventing of the bad [?] in the Act in debt, wherein the King's Address, on the proposition of Mr. [?], in consideration taken is in great Committee, which Committee good [?] all the foregoing [?] well; as the public Credit, and the same that Rapport today done good would be, and like the House common Rapport today has approved, and prepared thereupon Bill in [?] and so must spacious Bill soon presented, and for the [?] time taken be. The great Book of the People's session in of thought is still for the Procedures of the Parliament in this quote making of a good Outcome will work. Therein the Furnishing of the Lower House comes now the Crown foreseen [?]. You artists a Cedit of Richness, [?]

The Netherlands

KAMERICK, 12 August. Madame de Morsville has assisted our Magistrate of the Counts in facilitating the occasion to witness the Procession of this City, which has been agreed upon; this Procession shall be held on the 15th of this month. It is planned, with triumph wagons included; in the surrounding bishoprics great [?] have been made for this Procession in their dwellings, wherein the Church shall be made magnificent. On Sunday, in celebration of our King's appointment, Te Deum was sung, accompanied by triple volleys of all the Artillery of this City and the rattle. Monday, the Lord de Moutville gave a gallant entertainment at the Town Hall, where all the notable gentlemen present were invited. The Marquis vetti Landi has been indisposed by a cold in his Arm, for which reason he was obliged to keep to his bed for some days, though he now finds himself better. It is hoped that he will soon be restored to full health.

16th, 13 August. There have arrived before this City the vessels [W. L.], Bleding, William Hodglon, de Wilt, and Rebecca, Jan Aithan, Arrow Smith, the John and François, Ralph Aire, and the Contest, Joseph Hegdon, all from Scotland; David de Bont, Heymen Verstleeg, Henry van Hoof, Fredrik Igel, Abraham van Capel, Lolke Iges, and Jan Hendrick, all from Dirksland: [vessels] which have departed were [bound to] Cortina, William Lewis to Niemelsutel, the Company of Dordrecht, Gerrit Schaap, and Dordrecht's Prosperity, Gerrit Statisaart, both from Haaswick; and Jan Hendrick to Dympkerken.

AMSTERDAM, 14 August. By the Ship of Jacobus Wijgedu, Weybright Bak, which has arrived, letters have been received dated June from Curaçao, which report that there arrived the Ships the Judith, Captain Theod. Rentrant of this City, and that of [?] Bertrand, which had departed from there earlier; being at the Kuli (from which road some barks had entered into Curaçao) all still [?]. One offers for sale from hand a newly built Model Kitchen, of which six of the same Rooms are decorated or painted; furthermore a Garden with two Summer Houses and various Garden lands; similarly, one notes the Land, being at [?] and time the Sand: someone the whole. House and Condition being viewed, these Katwijk form., and the [?] at Monsieur Hendrik Koot, are offered at the Time Market [?].

Switzerland

SCHAFFHAUSEN, 7 August. A few days ago a severe Sickness broke out here; for near Geneva on French soil and the Regent published an Ordinance of the King of Expedition forbidding all People to [?] the King of Sardinia, Switzerland, the Natural [?] and the Foreign [?]: with justification that all Traders, with certain Air Posts, such as Wines, Grains, Cattle, and Linen goods: with the notice that all Traders who were not founded on those said shared [?] from Poland had arrived: the Field Authorities near there were favorable, and one was already [?] with the Winter Grains in the sowing. The differences, which [?] passed affecting an English Bombardier [?], and a Trading affinity of Frederick the Foreign, became [?] by the English Admiral Norris last attempted [?], were still not said, until from Spain came [?] that there was equal [?]. The King of Great Britain, and mentioned Admiral Norris. In the [?] have come Whaling Vessels from Greenland, and one year from Davis Strait, both laden with [?] in Texel, Klaas Wool from Bergen, Gerit Lids from Bordeaux, Jean Mathieu from Bayonne, and in the Vlie one or two of the Ships [?] Pietets.

The Hague, 7 August

The Lords States of Holland and West Friesland have remained and conducted a third Day over days of [?]. Meeting of the lords Commissioned Council from Reusdorp arranged. The Bank Welderen has departed to Gelderland. The Swedish Minister and Lord Fryg has been visited by some Lords of the Government.

Vienna, 8 August

Since the arrival of a [?] from Count Colonna of Milan, and another from Paris, in [?] Rome reduced from and Regiments [?]. So [?] South and the King's Courtesy arranged, so shall the Prince [?] in the Castellot Count of [?] have been styled. It appears that the Merchants [?] pleased, and [?] Britain [?].

Cologne, 13 August

Ratified just now is the new Family [?]. Nuncio Saunti with the Bishop of Antwerp here arrived, and [?], further with a brock and [?] images [?], and [?] are, reddish a Stable for 3 [?] Yards, Coach and Teamster's House, and decorated with Espaliers and Fruit-bearing Trees, all installed according to a [?] and Letter to the Duke of Keslyn at this House: and [?] is seen, can direct himself to the mentioned [?], dwelt: and on the Road in the [?] [?].

Germany & Its Surroundings

DRESDEN, 12 August. Next Thursday there is the joy of that Crown-Prince [?] of [?] where the Queen, our [?], has her Residence [?] doors by the Praetor, which one by the Margrave, and in the presence of the Market...

---

AMSTERDAM, 18 August. By the Ship from Danzig coming from Archangel, which yesterday passed the Sea Toll, letters have been received from June from Curaçao, which report that the Ships arrived there [?] Angel, [?], Theod. Deittrant of this City, and that of Peter Bernard, who departed from there; being at the Kuli (where some Barks had entered into Curaçao) all still [?]. One offers for sale from hand a newly built Model Kitchen, of which six of the same Rooms are decorated or painted; furthermore and also a Garden with two Summer Houses and diverse Garden lands; likewise one notes much Land, being [?] and time the Sand: someone the whole. House and Condition being viewed can be seen at the Katwijk purchase, and at Leiden by Monsieur Hendrik Koot, are offered at the Fleet [?].

Johannes Staarts, Cornelis van den Heuvel, Jan Lemmens, and Hendrik Lisquet, Brokers, on 12 August 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, in the Exchange, a parcel of Moscow Goods, likewise Linens, and other Good Cloth Bales Hidden, [?] 1600 Moscow gray Hare Skins, 1000 on Chamois prepared [?] John Hosa, Jan Kigeunmaker, Daniel d'Astevedo, and Nicholas Eys Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam on August 1721; in the evening at 6 o'clock, in the Dykstraat, in the Old Burgh, a Ship load new [?] 1722; lying on the Pijl-Hergen, and the New Weyts House Forthing, on and the Play and House, Jan Riedenaker, and Coene Heos Brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam at 6 o'clock, in the Dykstraat, in the Old Burgh, a parcel caza [?] new Varassa Table the King's and Red Mill Hedges, near My Gias runs [?].

Jacob wander House, Marinus Juweel, and Cornelis Verlinde Brokers, shall in Mass at Rotterdam in the Weighhouse, in the Inn the Black Lion, sell a Ship load [?] Leaves [?] Tobacco, of the Harvest 1720, and that by Cavelings as well as the named of the Heathen Allen and Roycroft.

Jacob van der House, Marinus Juweel, and Cornelis Verlinde Brokers, shall on Thursday at Rotterdam in the Weighhouse, in the Inn the Black Lion, sell a Ship load Bales and [?] Tobacco, and that by Cavelings as the named shall lie in the Cart [?].

N. Ficact, Art Dealer at Amsterdam over the old Luysterach Church, makes all [?] and [?], [?] is, frame [?] [?] and [?] the Church, by which the first Confectioners of that Time, (by Pliny, and other mentioned Work, [?] is forth in [?] given; and with special [?] out and mind out the fore [?], by the Lord Ph. de Stooch, Councilor of his Royal Majesty of Poland &c.; can be viewed by the Lovers, at Amsterdam by B. Ficact aforesaid, at Paris by G. Tel, and at Rotterdam [?], at Hamburg by Liebezeit and Felgieset; Berlin [?] in Council and Castellan, Leipzig T. Filitch, Augsburg J. Wolf, Nuremberg Endres, The Hague by [?] van Raam, Leiden Liitman, Brussels Tympelmans; on such Terms now this invented are, and stands to end last January

---

DE KLOPPER, on Breedel Street 18 August 1721. With Privilege.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday, 19 August 1721, in the afternoon at three o'clock, there shall be sold at Utrecht at the house of Toorn the Ambushed Estate; [?] of Danhuis, with all its [?], Household goods; as well as a certain large Parcel of Scottish Pans, and Bonte Huy on the Amer.

On Wednesday, 20 August 1721, in the afternoon at three o'clock, there shall be sold at Utrecht at the house of Toorn the Ambushed Estate, with all its Contents, Property and [?] house Zuylenburgh, located in Over-Langbroeck, with its [?] House the fashionable Billiards seen, and by the Lord Jacob van de Doontaag, Procurator and Notary at Amsterdam shall be managed.

On Saturday, 23 August, in the [?] of [?] on the Burgh of the City of Leiden in the [?], a remarkable [?], held in a modern House on the Rhine, Orangeworks, Play and Toymaker's House, Stable for two Horses, [?] beyond the new Works Gate, under the Ambacht of Soeterwoude, item also a New House Shutter there, long about one hundred forty fathoms, occupied with [?] of Soeterwoude, in the Coalbury Field. And finally also a House Garden Land, [?] the Floor in the Ward, in the Ambacht the trading [?] and Fore-yard on [?] measured Place read out shall be.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 18th of august, 1721. — With Privilege.
