Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 20th of August, 1721
Translation From 18Th-Century Dutch (1721)

---

SCHAFFHAUSEN, 7 August. For several days now, French letters arriving here have been read with some concern, and indeed at Gex near Geneva on French soil, a new Royal Ordinance has been issued, containing that the King forbids the repetition of all commerce from the King of Sardinia, Switzerland, the Republic of Geneva, and the States of the German Reich, with prohibition on previous provisions of any kind, such as wines, grains, cattle, and salt, not greatly affecting certain individuals in this matter, as has become known: the King has ordered that no provisions whatsoever shall enter from the aforementioned lands; they shall not be permitted to be imported. From Geneva, word has come here that they, against this Ordinance, have been compelled there regarding the inspection toward [?], have neither complaints yet, nor meanwhile Switzerland and the bordering Kingdoms.

---

Germany & Bordering Kingdoms

DRESDEN, 11 August. For some time now, a new Ordinance has been in place; moreover, the marriage of the Crown Prince of Denmark and the Princess of Brandenburg-Kulmbach has been concluded at St. Petri, where the Queen, our Electress, has her residence. The ceremony was performed by Doctor Ripping, court preacher, in the presence of the Empress of Kulmbach, and Field Marshal Count von Flemming, and the Baron of Leipzig, President of the Electoral Court [?], who had gathered there on behalf of the King.

---

Great Britain

LONDON, 17 August. On Wednesday around six o'clock, the King, with the three young Princesses, went to Kensington, where they intend to remain during the summer season; and the mines glitter in the house of Wilds, and third [?] are also closed at Richmond. Monday, the printer Mill and Mr. Wilkinson were removed from the prison of Newgate, where the entire city was brought to the King's Bank, and the prison in Southwark was made. Wednesday evening, Jacob Walter, the notorious smuggler in Southwark, who has never broken in his youth in the City, and on order of a Secretary of State was locked in the prison of [?]. The same by order have brought 44,392 ounces of silver at the toll to be transported toward Holland. A great curse of builders of the loose trade, which more commonly [?], and on the slain were on open, they were in the grave of Flemming [...] a request signed to present it to the House of Commons, and having been present the necessity of the hand the House of Commons [...] that binder [?], that there must be advocates [?] to [...] the same footing held as all the other blessed people in the Z. Z. Comp., or there they might be [?] in such a manner as the House shall order their obligation to hold and [...] or by themselves, or their advocate might be heard [...] to prove their public credit. Glittering had the Commons ladies contributed toward the restoration of public credit, as well as 300 of the aforementioned request of the Annuitants, which is not to be wondered that it has been retained 27 divided legacies to Waller to that of the Bill on this day was placed in a Committee. The same thereof granting very great favorable Annuitants for the Parliament House, the geese then each of the people, which went in there, a letter over, that these words in the hands of [?] the house is to the administration of the great [?] the [...] holds, but they vane 200 in their [?] the right to be [...] those people, which the House for public vak Foreigners [...] to Constables sent, to which ordered is these Annuitants of to do sign, which painter could not do [...] without the leading of the Doucharte. Since the whole Act [...] Pardon, have the Bubbles worth begun to [?], so they in the Gallery of the Best particularly wet to or z pe Boit [?], that by if the Parliament this trade not House, the same necessarily shall increase, that the Charity for the commerce greater shall become, than the there not needed to be forced. Both have the Commons in large Committee the Bill toward the restoration of [...]

Broad Street [...] lies.

Wednesday's Courant.

public Credit passed through, and therein about the glance of the Mortgages this change made, namely in order that all the common private Gold lent have on Actions or Subscriptions, after that they sufficient security for the same given shall have. D in the Z. Z. Comp. but that you not therein second such security to give, the whole shall have to pay; and that all Brokers, or those who themselves as Brokers have let be used, absolutely in all pay shall all that they for their own account lent have, which last Period with 63 against 46 Votes was taken over in the City of Payment are fixed on Christmas approaching, and mid-Summer in the coming Year 1722. The Actions of the Bank are 177 and the quarter, O. I. C. 147, Z. Z. 146, the Mill. Bank 113, Lottery Annuities 100 and the quarter, Bank Ann. 99 and the quarter, ditto 2 per Cent 94, Aff. C. 30, the Exchange Aff. 10, ditto L. 5 and the quarter, J. B. 33 and the half.

---

France

PARIS, 13 August. These days when the Te Deum was sung in the Church of Notre Dame concerning the restoration of the King, the Duke of Orleans had ordered the Ceremony Master to lay a carpet in the choir of that church, but since there was dispute about removing the carpet, which he refused to do; but after his Eminence had deliberated with those of the Chapter thereupon, he had the said carpet removed by the Sweepers; since no one but the King or the Queen ought to enter to pray in that church. For some days now, the Lord Vendôme, formerly the great Prior of France, with very great request of his beloved has sought to be relieved, wherein the [?] has finally agreed; the same from that purpose go to St. Louis, in his Hotel to give a great Feast to the Duke of Orleans, and several Lords and Ladies of the Court. Last Monday afternoon a troop of 26 Biscayans came before Toulon there; they were all alike, and dressed in fine white shirts, with fitting girdles on the arms, knees, and around the waist; with plumes and cockades on the hats; but what was most remarkable was that one of them rode on a horse woven of straw, richly adorned, and another offered the same horse from a straw basket, constantly dancing hay to it, while all the others danced around the same; which so pleased the King that His Majesty had them given 10 pistoles; and tomorrow this troop goes to St. Cloud to dance for Madame and the Duke of Orleans. Wednesday His Majesty used some medicines, which agreed with him, so that Thursday for the first time he went walking in the Tuileries woods. The Lord Maffei, extraordinary Nuncio, [?] remained residing [?] sought after lodgement, and thereafter his public interest to make. The King's health is still not as it should be; the nights following this the Duke of Orleans in the Palace of His Majesty and gave the next morning audience to the Foreign Ministers. The Cardinal du Bois has given audience to the said Ministers, in his lodging again audience given as before. [?] this month the Bishop of Soissons, as a Member of the French Academy, is to be received. A project has been drawn up regarding the Artillery and the liquidation of the second effects, but it is not believed that the trade will grasp it; but it is expected that those, which no convincing proof in their declarations have given, and in no certain determined time have come. If they other effects have come, those same shall be declared void. Previously, the week of the wedding, he at one of his blood relations, in the Faubourg Marceau, where he with him had taken 2 of his children aged 8 and 6 years; after the meal he went to the middle in the King's garden, where the children had been picked up from the ground and [?], after which the same so dull and bruises brought themselves home, that one dared only bring them home, that they thus once 24 hours as [?] have lain, with the eyes wide open, without any movement, as the beat of the heart; they also let them see [?], who having consulted thereupon ordered a blister, whereby the 2 old have lost their 2 eyes, as still no power enough having, [?]. [Editor's note: The text here becomes increasingly corrupted and difficult to parse.] were the rites of the Duke and Duchess of Longueville attended, of which previously spoken is, shall this year not take place. The said [?] the Congress at Cambrai is said to open, on chance has been said separated; but one shall well do this warning by all the previous on the Ley to set. The Imp[erial]. Minister Baron von [?] not an expedition of his Court [?]: the rumor, as if Jacobite Minister at the Imp[erial]. Court would have requested [?] very well where Vienna to might do, falls entirely. The French Jacobites (as one calls them here) give themselves quite much movement to spread under the people rumors, as if between the two Kings of Spain, Great Britain, and this Crown an alliance has been made, which the Ministers of the said Courts fully are in the Ley lain; some think that which well might become eternity (to all disputes over the Ceremonial to untie) now that the Congress there shall be concluded. There was in this part of the city a very splendid coach [?], which shall be kingly, although still not the Empress knows for whom the same has been made [?] that which by the Prince of Piedmont would be [?]. It is said that the [?], which by His Majesty to the Turkish [?], have [?], and the same son given are, but about the [?] line [?] has at him the portrait of the King, the great [...]

[From August 11.] Some months ago, the Director General of Fortifications in the Kingdom of Milan wrote a letter to an engineer officer here who was without employment, informing him that he would be pleased if he would come with several other engineers from the Eastern Netherlands Fortifications to Milan, in order to make a plan of the entire Milanese territory. This officer recruited about twelve engineers who were without any employment, and after agreeing upon terms to be settled by the fifteenth of the month, they received their travel money from Milan and departed from these lands under the supervision of the aforementioned officer. However, they were soon discovered and arrested before they had gone far. The officer has been taken into custody, and the guide has been placed under arrest, until proper inquiries can be made into this matter; as it is claimed that it is not permitted to leave the country without express permission from the States, the Marquis de Prie the General, and that this should have been arranged through the Governess.

Last Friday evening there arrived in this city from Holland Mr. Baffacourt, who, as is reported, comes with a commission to settle the differences between the West India Company and [?]. It was announced that the Market in the city of Leiden shall be held on Monday of the week following the thirteenth of July (on a Saturday or Sunday), and the Market shall continue Monday through Thursday. And the members of the Company of Commerce shall meet in the Company chamber in September next, on Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday mornings at nine o'clock, to make an accounting of their invested capital and the profits of the company.

There is sought an Obligation of the Community Treasury of Leiden, dated 1706, registered No. 11, fol. 46, issued the eleventh of 1706, No. for 600 Guilders; whoever finds it should bring it to the office of this newspaper. On Monday and Wednesday afternoons, the merchants Pieter Cortet, [?], and [?], brokers at the Diemerberg, shall sell a valuable dyed cloth [?], a house, a fruitful garden, all adjoining one another, called Jaapshaner, [?] where [?] can be purchased outright from hand, apply to the aforementioned merchants.

Jan Hoos, Jan Bleemseeker, and Corn. Hoog, brokers, shall sell at Amsterdam at six o'clock in the evening, in Dyk-straat, in the Oude Burg, a parcel of extra choice new [goods] in the Konjtes and Rode Moolen-steegen, near My Glass at 121.

Jakob vander Houve, Marinus Jauectl, and Cornelis Veilinde, brokers, shall sell in Rotterdam, in the inn called the Zwarte Leuw, a large merchant ship's hook-tack, of the year 1720, which has never been [used?]; sound and in good condition, and that it Raveligen [?] shall be stored in the warehouse. On Monday, the 28th [and] Saturday, August 1721, at nine in the morning and two in the afternoon, shall be sold at Bree [?] some fine textiles and several curious furnishings and household ornaments; which will be made available as soon as the catalog can be seen; the house is to be obtained from Filip Pick, broker, who on Wednesday, the 3rd of September 1721, at nine in the morning, in Asen Burgeval, [shall sell] a small house for a baker, with [?], in the Hoop [shall sell] a parcel of cloth, red, blue, heavy, and other colored silks and common fabrics, silk, fine fabrics, cottons, Nile linens, &c., a parcel of paintings which can be seen at the aforementioned.

---

PETERBURG, July 25. At [?] in Finland, His Czarist Majesty held [?], and the aforementioned [?] is here not only in person concerning the water matters, about which [?] [rest illegible in original]

---

ROME, August 2. In this week there was a solemn ceremony concerning [?] and the old [?] were [?], and His Holiness held council with Brother [?]; and Michael [?] the feast of St. Ignatius, founder, holy [?], to the church, which church the [?] of the southern Capuchins, which was held [?] and the feast was devoutly observed; and by Cardinals Barbarini, Fiore, Conti, and Albani. Sunday evening the [?] company of confessors and solemn ones participated in [?], for which [?], as to a minister. [?] with a ruby seal from the Emperor to His Holiness. [rest illegible]

...at the castle, to Katelskroon Hans Boerman of Copenhagen; at Limmerik the St. Peter, Brother Boyle of Bergen; on the London river the Heeren, Captain Steven Hull of Hamburg, Robbe Finck and John Honisden of Rotterdam, along with several others from Norway; and at Falmouth the Pred.; Captain Chiobb, from Blockhalen heading to the west, &c. In total they received about 20 ships.

The rates of EXCHANGE today in Madrid & Valencia; also at Amsterdam at eight to eight and one-half days have been. Bilbao 44 days at 3 pence. Venice 32 and 3 pence. Leghorn 92 and 3 pence. Genoa 92. Lepe [?] 30 and 3 pence, 4 and 1 half: London [?] sch. and 3 half St. Leipzig current 33 and 3 pence St. Frankfurt current 38 and 3 pence the [?]. Danzig [?] groat. Breda current 38 and 4 pence St. Hamburg and St. money St. Antwerp and 4 pence a p. st. C. Ghent [?] and 3 pence. Brussels 2. Zealand. &c. Rotterdam 2 money. The action of the Bank is 1 and 1 half. The actions of the E.I.C. are 75 p. V.C. 152 and 1 half. Subie. 271. The Bank 113, E.I.C. 172. Z. 149, current.

THE HAGUE, August 10.

As the couriers have returned, and one moreover the Britannias on the 14th. The Count Taroueca, Ambassador and Plenipotentiary of the King of Portugal, has been here some time on business of the garrison.

Within the city of Leiden, yearly shall be held on the 13th of July, until Saturday or Sunday coming, the market shall be Monday following through Thursday, at the Great Church, the pulpit on Bredelstraat is requested to register her.

[Agreement with Gouda regarding] that on the thirtieth of September in the Silk [chamber?] of the aforementioned Company, the [?] and before the aforementioned Company to Leiden, in the name of Taaneke le Poole, dated the 19th February [?] July 1721, No. 1750. Register v. Pol. 214; containing a capital in this Treasury shall [?] five silver Ducatons to a [?], and [?] Janitors and Com: of Ncc. at Amsterdam, the house of Peter Hal-rtaac-dye-house, with its equipment and [?], manure bins, and [?] of Jaaphaes, located at the high dike, before Zeeburgh, beyond the [?], and on the making of a [?]. [rest illegible]

---

PORT TO PORT, August 3. The [?] used by the shipowners [?]. [?] of Baia de Todos los Santos; the third ship of the Fleet departed on the 10th of March and [arrived?], so that three ships could see [?], but the Fleet [?] sent two ships to Tela. These two ships to the north, and during their voyage they will remain behind; but they heard that other ships were before, which besides the 1800 kegs and parcel of tobacco and other goods at Port [?] have remained. According to other advice: from Hamburg to Lisuon, there has been another [ship?], and would with ship, where the captain [has been taken?]. Here ship [?] would help here in the [?] and hopes it will be the first with an Englishman, and not through a [?].

Course of Exchange

[Editor's note: This text is heavily corrupted in the original transcription, with many illegible or uncertain passages, especially in the latter sections on Italian and Portuguese news. The financial section contains exchange rate information for various European trading centers.]

Notices & Advertisements

On Monday, the 27th of August 1721, at six o'clock in the evening, shall be sold at Amsterdam a fine country house, stables, coach house, &c., with fine timber, lying between Maralen and Zuylon, called Engela; at three o'clock in the afternoon, in the Dornis Tower, a fine farmers' dwelling with surrounding land and meadow, and two small orchards, located near the aforementioned property; a meadow and an orchard, lying near the Zuyle; at Rode Woolen, likewise to be used as before: for further particulars, apply at Amsterdam to the office of Eeterhard Viiaar, Notary and trustee.

At Amsterdam at the warehouse of Waterbergen, book dealers on the Water, [there are] some new works in the warehouse, and their warehouse [goods], through the advertisements of the Paters Abram van St. Clara, second part, with figures, in [?].

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 20th of august, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 101.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 22nd of August, 1721
England

PETERSBURG, 25 July. Upon the arrival of a courier from England, Count Molt, who has recently returned to Petersburg, held a council meeting with his ministry. A courier has been sent to Moscow regarding this matter. Here there is not complete satisfaction, both regarding Swedish proceedings and concerning the armistice, for which reason it is requested that His Czaric Majesty, under express command, apply himself to unanimity. However, it is expected that peace is on the point of being signed.

Italy

ROME, 2 August. This week the Pope has visited many churches, namely on Sunday that of St. Paul [?], whose feast was celebrated. His Holiness had in that church the two cardinals Conti and Bonetti, and Alexander Albani. On Wednesday, the day before the Feast of St. Ignatius, founder of the same [order], [the Pope visited] the church of that saint, at the door of which church the Holy Father was received by his cardinals and the General of that order. On Friday morning, [he visited] that of the Sisters of the Poor Clares, which is near the Quirinal, whereby His Holiness stayed in the same [church] and was received at the door by Cardinal Bailarini, protector of the church, and two cardinals of Albani. On Sunday past, Cardinal de Lugo hosted the cardinals at a magnificent meal in the patriarchal hall, among which the principal [guests] were Princess Sforza and Knspoli [?], as well as various other ladies, the two Granatelle Rocolpgetti with his wife, Cardinal Conti with the nephews of His Holiness, [and] very many other prelates, and of these the distinguished guests were cardinals Aquaviva, Ottoboni, Guelcietti, Althan, and Biffi. [?] On this day many cardinals were drawn [for some purpose] so that afterward they could go to table. The Pope has also used certain medicines by way of precaution, for which reason His Holiness has held no audiences. [?] The ministers [of the] Protodiacconi went with Cardinal Althan with a large retinue of Prussian nobility, enjoying for the first time the authority of the Grand Infant; and also all this pontifical [ceremony], at the first audience, in which by his credentials from the Emperor he presented himself to His Holiness, to assume the character of ambassador. And in the same audience he paid a common visit first to Cardinal Conti, and subsequently to Cardinal Spada, secretary of state. On Thursday the Pope attended the Congregation in the Holy Office, after which he gave a private audience to Cardinal del Giudice. Yesterday, the first day of the Novena, the Pope received the gifts from all the doctors and naturalists, which the ministers of state are accustomed to present to His Holiness on that day. Among these gifts Frederick [?] also brought that from the Hawking, the most remarkable because there were six imported pheasants with it. The office of Grand Penitentiary, vacant by the promotion of Monsignor Pieta to the cardinalate, has been given by the Pope to [name not provided].

Portugal

PORT, 2 August. In the preceding [days] there came in 2 ships, N.S. da Parelida and Santa Rosa, from the Bahia de Todos os Santos [Editor's note: Bay of All Saints, in Brazil]. These two ships had returned from [?] on the 3rd and subsequently came toward Pernambuco to await the ships there, so that the same [ships] in August were separated from the fleet by the former admiral due to their anchors having given way against the strong current. These ships suffered many men [casualties], were repaired, and had very harsh contrary winds, so that from Ribeiro they took the violations from Bahia and Pernambuco well for 14 days more, which was very much against them. It is expected that 3 other ships will still arrive, which will also be richly laden with 2000 tons of sugar and tapuia fruits, tobacco and other goods on board. At Aveiro a boat came in with 13 men from the ship Antoinette, captain [name not provided] of Haarlem, in such distress as further follows, to the effect that it was taken for an Algerian corsair, and so her people abandoned her, with the ship being towed by the captain mate, or the hope having been abandoned. The ship of the merchant of Rotterdam, called [?], came into Vila do Conde in order that from there it might be loaded with water; during August [?] the ship was said to be English and would be ready, for which reason orders were given to proceed with another ship.

Friday's Courant.

Jan Stieffens is preparing to sail toward the Baltic Sea, and others toward London.

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 6 August. The King, having taken pleasure in hunting, has again returned here. Yesterday there arrived here an express [courier] from His Majesty's plenipotentiaries in Sweden. Regarding peace, we have no further news than that it is understood that now only the final orders of the Czar are awaited, to issue the preliminary points.

Germany & the Adjacent Kingdoms

VIENNA, 9 August. On Wednesday the Neapolitan Henry Orda [?] received the bed as actual secret councillor of the Emperor, and took possession of that high office. On Thursday the Emperor, together with the youngest archduchess and the young Portuguese prince, amused himself with the hare hunt, and on the same day [?] three more were killed. On the third day there arrived a courier from Rome with letters of recommendation from Count Kinsky from that city to here, and regarding the reason for the audience which Cardinal Count Althan had with the Pope, to present to His Eminence as the Emperor's ambassador the brief credentials; but although the said cardinal has assumed this character well, he is ordered by the Emperor to remain [?], and will still be assigned according to his bishopric of Breslau, together with the supreme management of Silesia. The Cardinal of Schönborn, prince-bishop of Speyer, will succeed him in the ambassadorial post.

REGENSBURG, 14 August. Last Monday there was held for the second time, since the return of the Elector-Palatinate's Director, an Imperial Diet, in which the Hanoverian minister also participated. The chief matter under discussion was that concerning the Chamber of Wetzlar. It is said that the Cardinal of Saxen-Zeyta will have received a commissorial decree from the Emperor regarding religious matters, which he will shortly communicate to the Protestant corpus. There has come forth a tract, whose author maintains that it would be easy, all prejudice being removed, to unite the Lutheran with the Reformed religion, to their mutual advantage and to prevent that through their good correspondence and harmony harmony be gained [?]. It is said that the writer will be of the Lutheran religion. The ministers of Hessen-Kassel, Württemberg, and Brandenburg-Onolsbach are daily expected here, as which the Protestant corpus will be more complete. The resident of the Spanish general [?] of the United Netherlands at the Imperial Diet has kept His Eminence Cardinal Saxen-Zeyta and all the present counts and Protestant ministers at his table.

France

PARIS, 15 August. After the King took a purgative yesterday morning and found himself better, yesterday he was in his majolica [apartment], yet maintains still a pale color. One hears nothing but of the 20th psalm and fine musical concerts. On the 11th, 12th, and 13th the French Academy, that of sciences and inscriptions [of the Academy], held theirs in the church of the Fathers of the Oratory. The celebration of the Duchess of Orleans at Bagnolet was much praised; there 12 or 300 books were served at tables, which were placed in the open air, and in the apartments were still various [arrangements] for people of distinction; the fireworks were extraordinarily well ordered and finely executed. Certain houses of the professors' house have had fine illuminations on the beautiful facade of their church, which were ordered by the architect Bemain [?]; the fireworks that were set off there were beautiful, and the whole was preceded by a Te Deum and 200 instruments and voices. That same day the white hoods of the Faubourg St. Germain offered the King a heifer and a lamb, crowned with flowers and adorned with ribbons, such as would be offered to deities; they had on their head a pretty young man, who was very finely dressed and harangued with much dignity. The confraternity of Jerusalem, which exists since July 14 and consists of men of every rank, came that day also to greet the King, but could not do so due to circumstances, because His Majesty held the midday meal. Hereupon the shoemakers go, which also are Savoyards, marching with a banner upon which are painted the arms of France and Savoy. Tomorrow the King will go to the church of Notre Dame to thank the Lord there in procession for his recovery. Yesterday morning the regent duke was at St. Cloud; work was being done on the extension of the ground floor and with vigor on the great apartments, which he has ordered for himself and his apartments. The Duke of Bourbon departed yesterday toward Chantilly, where he will remain until the 18th with such court and music; there appears to be hunting, to which many newcomers are making their way. On Tuesday the Knight of St. Hubert celebrates his annual feast, to which many newcomers are making their way. The visa for provisions has still been given over to the controllers general for a few more days. Publication [of the financial accounts] is proceeding well enough; the trade letters [?] for 70 to 74 louis. The silk still [?] [text uncertain—?] would belong to the court [?] [text uncertain—?] one finds methods, letters on the state, and on the whole large parties [?]. Also the lord of Montauban has received orders to search the gallery of [?] in the great [chamber], of which one has not yet been able to find the author. In Languedoc it is also [?], by which various documents, the extract of visa of this city has already a few days ago been delivered to the controllers general. On Thursday at Flouation there was much salt [?].

France

From the Abbot Négua and other matters, we report that against His Majesty, [the news came] of the death of Cardinal de Noailles, by [order of] the deceased [official], his effects being brought to St. Lazare d'Eveux by the Theatines. Last Sunday the case of the Billouage of Dijon was heard before the Abbot and the Monks of the Cistercian Order of the House of Cîteaux, in the Council of Regency; the final decision was that it was not permissible at Mâcon, where their Order had the right to act in cases of deceased persons, but they were sworn off and also condemned; the Chancellor and the Attorney General having examined all previous opinions, they have come to the following conclusions, which are being reviewed by the Council of Regency for approval [details follow]:

I. That all Children's endowments made by the King shall not be lost. II. That payments from a private to a private shall be reduced by one-fifth. III. That all those holding such Papers [shall lose] one-fifth of rents from confiscated properties. IV. That Money brought to the Mint and Bank shall [have] four-fifths [returned]; those who cannot come for their Papers shall lose everything. The King shall maintain himself as well on Millions in Rents upon the City, above and beyond the heavy burdens of the State. The confiscated properties of the Missionaries shall not revert to the profit of the King, but upon valuation shall go to those who stand in the four first Classes. The Academy of Sciences, following this same system of government, has been appointed by the King on Wednesday as Honorary Members of this Illustrious Academy: the Lord André Hercules de Frichis, his Bishop, Preceptor, and Monsieur Henry du Troubet, Lord of Valincourt, Secretary General of the Marine, both from the Academy Family to the five Classes; the Lord de Refe, Son of the President Bernard de Refe, and Nephew of the Bishop of Angoulême, to the Class of Chemistry; the Lord Damy d'Isnard, as Professor of the King over Plants and Growth in the King's Garden, in the Libraries; and the Lord de Beaufort in the class of Geometry, and the Abbot or Monk in that of Mechanics. The Lord Donenis, Director of the Accounting [office] of the Quarter of January, has received an expedition from the Ministry of State. The Lord Harcourt, Count of Thory, who [recently] passed away, has instituted [the heir] as universal Legatee, whereby after his death in Languedoc and other properties, to which he as Enfant held the Monthly Children from the House of Harcourt. From Hebron word comes that the King incognito attended the last Conference of the Royal Academy, wherein the Count de Frichis gave a notable Discourse on the reputation and great qualities of Innocent XIII. His Portuguese Majesty having granted a Patent for the establishment of a new Academy in the City of Braga, was then presented at the first Conference.

The Count Retmaud in Alexandria, who has upset the World, or rather the esteemed Doctor Clement Rodriguez, Commissioner of the Inquisition, disputed before the Victor, and Doctor Soares de Gama, great Regent of the Kingdom, defending Philosophy; they bring forth such fierce arguments and proofs that the matter remained undecided. To the Orators of the next [conference] to be held at the Conference, this mathematical Question was raised: whether [they should] present before the King with the laws of the City of Cartagena to hold or restore [them], and to compose a Heroic poem, in praise of the Cardinal Coris for the Pavilions brought forth. A Thief of the Band, who involved himself only with considerable Thefts, having made near Tours a suitable Theft near a Pawnbroker, they received 200 grands [coins] in dispute one upon another, whereby the wound in the Belly was made; the Son of this Monsieur de Vienne received 900 grands offered on the Wirette Castle Lands.

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 20th August. For this City and thereabouts: the ships Brierly Love (T. Ferrable), de Wilham, Steven Diehuying, [multiple ship names follow] arrived or departed with various captains and merchants trading to and from England and other ports. [The text lists numerous vessel names and merchant names in a shipping report format.]

AMSTERDAM, 20th August. According to Letters of the 12th from Lisbon, the following ships had arrived there: the Jan Hilbert from here (Captain Riqueau from Havre de Grace), and others. The Angra and Tercera departed from there; and the Lisk. Knrman (Captain Joseph Clark) [set out]. The Fleet to Pernambuco and the ships bound for the Coast and Angola had not yet departed from Lisbon, and hope [remains] that the Bailiff [may be seen] on the Bogies of the Bay. This morning the Lords Administrators of the East India Company received by Express notice that yesterday evening 2 Ships, and principally the Cameralyk from Boxhuizen, passed by the Chamber Enkhuizen; Rynsorf before the Chamber Hoorn; from the East Indies and in the East returned: then 2 other Ships, namely Batris, [also listed], Pirtzenburg, Elizabeth, [and many others from Batavia, Ceylon, and other ports arrive listed by name and origin].

These are all the Ships which sent out around the Good Hope were scattered about the 7th of April at the Land by the Warships and Privateers, and the 21st April jointly departed from the Cape of Good Hope, at which point five Ships remained behind, namely the Huys te Vlotien, and Meerluyiken, for the Chamber Bathovcien; [others listed]. The purchase of the six for this, and the now counted 1 Ship, besides the still lying at the Cape, amounts in Tons of Goods and [figures]. It is not yet known whether any Ships from the Fatherland have come to the Cape. Besides the already mentioned Ships, two and others' Boats, etc. still to follow. So that the Return this year will consist of 14 or 15 Ships.

Spain

CADIX, 29th July. Since the recent coining at the Mint of Madrid, Segovia [has come], which has also already [affected] a large mintage in the short term more [will come]; for the Mint of Segovia minted 5000 [coins] in the 3 Mints and [?], one had a rumor as if ours are the bonds, but that did not prove true [?] such disappeared; yet the same is [?]. On the 21st came into this Bay the Hendrik from Lisbon (Jacob Boijkoff), [and others]; on the 24th the Eduard Louis from Bristol, [and others]; on the 22nd the Jame Muifer from Bilbao, Miles Aulfuch, and others. On the contrary, on the 23rd departed the Helmo Schouman and John Divel, [others] toward Amsterdam [and] Hamburg; on the 2nd and other [ships] [listed]; on the 2nd the Warships de Doriellen and the Duraly (Captain George F...) and the Concordia (Joseph [...] Porter) toward the Canaries: so that there [were] 6 Frigates, among them [from] Amsterdam, Holland, and 1 Danish Ship [of the] Lord van Sommiluyk, which [has] not been seen outside [?] yet, also our [ships] from Alicante have departed, [going] rather [to] a small Algatten.

MADRID, 4th August. The Commander having ordered [matters] in the Woods of [...], went with a Troop of 4 [units] and besides [with] the Cartel of 100 [units] with Provisions, unguarded having [occurred], shall return back. The forward Vice-King of Sicily [sent] by Express notice that there on the 29th before the ship of the Tollman, which named ship among others also [carried] 12,000 [units] of [goods], with saved Gold. One knows at Charles [?] to [go] out [?].

On behalf of the Director [?], notice is given that the replacement of the Governors [in] the month of September, according to the known penalties, must be completed, and [they must] begin, whereby everyone shall enjoy the loose wisdom of the distribution of the 10 [Guilders], as to the second Clause in [?]. On Monday the 23rd August 1721, in the evening at 6 o'clock, in Amsterdam with his Lordship Housing, Stables, Stable-house, etc., further Dwellings, with [the] Warehouses of Amsterdam, in the Cathedral Tower, a beautiful House-Dwelling, with fields, Hay-fields and small Orchards, besides the aforementioned Property Hay-field and small Orchard, located near the South Grain-Mill; [and] other [properties] [listed]. Morning Hay-field, located on the [?] and Brokers [names listed] will on Monday the 25th August 1721, in the evening at 5 o'clock, [sell] [property] on Shareholder curiosity recent pick [?] by Cavels so [that those named] shall lie in the Boathouses; in the Warehouse in Amsterdam to be sold on Monday the 23rd August 1721, at [the aforementioned] time; 1100 [units], 1000 Colliers, 4000 [other items].

[Advertisement section continues with lists of auctions and merchandise sales in various Dutch cities with specific dates and items for sale.]

Paris

PARIS, 19th August. Saturday the Princes presented to God in His Majesty's Church, His Majesty had [?] in these times, the Lord of Chartres, the Lord of Coveert, the Ambassadors of Villeroy. He has said to his Prayer and Panel [?] [details become unclear in transcription]. [Additional notices of administrative and legal matters follow.]

---

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES:** The Course of the EXCHANGE is today on Madrid 30 and 1 quarter. [Multiple exchange rates for various cities listed:] Radis 115; Seville 113 and 1 quarter; Bilbao [listed]; Lisbon 40 and 1 quarter; Venice 52 and 1 quarter; Livorno 91 and 3 quarters; Genoa 93; [continuing with rates for Rostock, Leipzig, Frankfurt, Danzig, Stralsund, Hamburg, Antwerp, Ghent, Brussels, Rotterdam]. The Agio of the Bank 5 and 1 half. The Stock prices of [Company] C.C. price 308; W.L.C. 193; Z.Z. 148. [Editor's note: This transcription presents significant challenges, particularly in the France and Paris sections where the original transcription appears corrupted or unclear. Many proper names and specific details have become illegible or were recorded ambiguously in the source. The exchange rates and shipping lists appear most reliably transcribed.]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 22nd of august, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 102.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 25th of August, 1721
Spain

CADIX, 29 July. Recently several ships of the new type have arrived here again, including vessels from the Mints of Madrid, Segovia, Cuenca, as well as Sicily, which have already been distributed to their owners; this has brought great benefit from the capture, and more is expected shortly; for it is reckoned that daily in the Bay of Segovia 1500 pieces [?] are taken, and that in the 2 other Mints much more is taken. There had been a report as if our Flotilla had come into the Harbor, but of the same twenty ships were captured there; thus it is distributed; however the same is seen there daily.

On the 21st there came into this Bay Hendrik Keck from the Canaries, the Renier van Lubeck, Jacob Roijcoft, and Guillomo Schoram from Amsterdam, 24th Edward Louis from Bristol, Piercer Wyngaert from Amsterdam, and Jone Dirctel van Cock; from the Great Road the Commandeur, Diego Muffert from Bilbao, Milles Aultech, and Diego Geraldino from the Canaries: in contrast on the 23rd departed Jacob Roycroff, Guillermo Scheuram and Johan Direcl, all two toward the Harbor of Gravelingen, Villet, Piercer Molenaar toward Amsterdam, and the Catharina, Jochem van Xuelen toward Hamburg; 24th the Victoria, Capt. Gomes toward Ostend; on the 23rd the Origiospeligcen, the Doree, Guillermo Oche and Gabrielen, and the Dundy; Capt. George Parvis, toward Lisbon; both English; and the Concordia, Jofeph Dircpel toward Genua and Amsterdam, and toward the Canaries: so that there still lie in this Bay 26 English [ships], 5 French, among them Capt. Nicolaas Forcal sailing toward Amsterdam; and Holland, with 3 Direct Ships. From England here the Lord van Sommeldyck, since he is still outside the Strait, is observed here every day. [?] which does not proceed. From Alicante ships have departed, since it is reckoned that there is but 1 small Algerian in the Sea.

CADIX, 4 August. The King having ordered a Hunt on this day in the Woods of Ballayp, their Majesties with the Duke of Albuquerque, properly arranged to hunt toward the Castle of 200 [?] mentioned Bullayn, with the intention of holding the Hunt, having the first time at the Escorial in very vigorous [health]. Of this Councillor of Spain who was previously Viceroy of Sicily, he is at the Age of 55 Years deceased. From Cadix news has been received that he departed from there on the 22nd of the previous Month; a Ship of Don Jofeph d'Aguire departed, which on the 23rd February in the following Year [?] was stationed. The mentioned ship brought among other things 2000 pounds Cacao, 1200 Hides of Eight, 100 Mark uncoined Silver, and 34 Coils of Gold. It is said that at Cadix several more ships are to be made, of which one toward the mentioned Province of Caracas, another toward Campeche.

France

PARIS, 18 August. Saturday morning the King was in the Church of Notre Dame to give thanks to Heaven for his miraculous recovery & the joy he had with him in the same Church the Duke of Chartres, the Duke of Bourbon, the Prince of Conty, the Marshal of Villeroy, and the Captain of the Guards. The King appointed four hundred new Orders of Knights. All at the head of the chapel they, received, and by hand given. His Majesty's [?] the first pipe led: in our Service his Majesty was not in the Choir waited for the Consultation had a concluded most absolute service and the choir, the Chapter of Notre Dame, which each priest in the right to be to bring it over him Dirck. The Choir the Music and the Musicians of the Church, Overall there the omitting toward the Church going and returning placed of replacing Mass of Notre Dame; which the young Monarch, who is presently in very good health, made great joy. That day was the feast in Politaretten well set, and the Walks, where the Duke Regent was also stationed, not very numerous. The preparations for the [?] on the day of St. Louis [were] set to be made, since the 200 [?], with still more zeal than before [were] practiced. The Ambassador of the Waters will his Excellency has caused to speak. The same day were with following dismissed to Ships for Moors, and 2 days later the Bishop Royal and Waleran, went here Sautereau, brother of the Lord Sautereau, who in the quality [?] gave the Lord Maisobde Son, Ruling race to the General [?] and Hanange to the [?]. The Duke of Noailles has and majestic [?] given in his Convalescence at St. Germain and [?], which was well with Illuminations, besides a Firework, Music Concert and [?]. [Editor's note: This section is heavily corrupted in the original transcription and meaning is uncertain.]

Germany & the Adjacent Kingdoms

HAMBURG, 19 August. With the Letters from [?] of the previous regular [mail], we have received, that the King of England, it is said that the King of Prussia on the 17th with the whole Retinue had arrived in the City of Stettin; the day after that there in [?] against the Regiment the Foot of the Count of Lottum the Review paraded, and over the good state of the same so satisfied was, that his aforementioned Majesty the Colonel mentioned Count of Lottum, to General Major, and the Lieut. Colonel van Embeck to Colonel promoted had. The Governor of Stettin, the Lord Nikolai was from his Government with the King come, and with his Majesty, and this so great promotion of the King, besides mentioned Governor likewise at the meal had entertained, toward Passewalk returned, from where he toward his Government returned was. The joy, which the Burghers and Inhabitants of the City Demmin heard from that aforementioned his Majesty, besides the General Bork, the [?] President of Mallow, and so many other Gentlemen of Rank in their City came in very great excitement: the Burghers held out the Bridge before the Gate decorated not with Flowers, and continued up to the Foltekhuis [?], which with all manner of Colors and Flowers was adorned, and stood outside the Gate in the Wagons to his Majesty received, who arriving, then there 1 Servo Battalion well the Gate elevating his Majesty out of the Carriage City: the King is over this favorable meeting over the making up of the [?] in that City directed the Materials to [?], and promised the Quartering to lighten: [?] again slight [?] of the King. Two houses Wilhelm, which by great a small, old and young persons [?], spoke the daily [?] of the Justices dismissed under the [?] of his Majesty, to [?] dwell. Regarding the tidings from Stockholm and Petersburg there is nothing fit to mention, since the events there have been heard very recently.

The Netherlands

KAMERICK, 19 August. Recently [?] the Marquis [?] Landi a Magnificent Banquet given at the Town Hall, [?] the Banquet and then there was one of the crucifix [?] Concert, after his Excellency, has no Flambeaux [?] the whole Town Hall [?], because he the [?] of all [?] alone wanted, which the first [?], which first [?] in justice in that Count of St. Kilveti, and of the King in Spain, where for the House of his Excellency, after all now Lights [?] is, which feast [?] was [?]

Leyden

Thursday evening the Count of Frovana, Minister of the King of Sardinia, gave a magnificent dinner and ball at which Her Majesty Froretta will be received, for which reason something more elaborate will be arranged than at the previous occasion. Great rejoicing has been reported at Valencia in the garrison over the King's narrative: the fortress Guaratizade, which was in the Arsenal, fired salvos, after which each Company received a barrel of beer from the garrison stores; the Infante being absent from the House, a distinguished gentleman was asked to represent him there with his wife. Everyone is eager to hear that the House of the Count at Taonca will be completed.

DORDRECHT, August 23rd. The following ships have arrived at this city: the Sultan Soleib, Matthew Pierlon, the Chandois Sloop, Thoobis, and the new ship England, and Hubert van den Sande from Dunkirk; the ships called the Palke have departed, [namely] the Wapen van IJssel, Michiel Jurisfaafle, and further Petronger, Uyken Hiddes, toward France, the Broederyke Licht, in this [case] Th. Ferrabic, and the Edward and Thomly, John Man, toward England.

AMSTERDAM, August 23rd. According to letters from Curaçao dated June 28th, the ship of Zacharias Zachariasden was seen there as a baron; the ships the Golden Apple and the Concordia had sailed from Zeeland to the West Indies and were equipped, and Captain Jan Deurteleck expected to depart from Curaçao in late July, though the other ships were to follow later. At Genoa the Maria de Gouda, Captain Joseph Eolupau of Paimboeuf, [arrived], intending to sail from the River toward Königsberg; at La Rochelle Flanders reportedly, Kuyper; at Bordeaux the Miss Anna, Leendert Winter; and at Nantes the Catarina, Gerrit Blom, all from this city. At Nantes lay another [ship]; there the ship Steven Ypes also [lay], and the ship of Nicolaas Brandi Blifera, intending to sail from the River toward Königsberg, came in, but it was hoped to salvage the cargo. Corn. Wilkes has come from Lübeck to Riga; and Engel Janile Cosksveld, traveling from Danzig to Bordeaux, has arrived. In the Maas [came in] citizen Col. Picter Winter with 2 vlieën and 90 hundredweight of bacon from Greenland; and at Texel Jan Pieterille Krimp from Riga; also all the returning East India ships for this Chamber and the Northern Quarter were anchored before the Old Shield. In the Vlie have entered Jacob Hoek the Keur with 5 vlieën 170 hundredweight, Ary Adam, de Ooms, 8 vlieën 180 hundredweight, and Teenis Roep, 5 vlieën 240 hundredweight, all from Greenland; and for the city Jan Hope Hollan from Riga, Jurgen Goyken and Hilbrand Teker from Danzig. The following have departed from Texel to sea: Hend. Bionk toward Suriname, Pieter Roepte toward Demerara, Matthyas Eduard toward St. Croix, &c.

THE HAGUE, August 24th. The Lords States of Holland and West-Friesland will convene next Wednesday. According to the latest letters from Paris, an Express from the British Court has passed through there to Madrid; it was assured that upon the return of the same ambassador, a time for opening the Cathedral Congress would be established. The Prussian Minister, Mr. Myndertshaagen, in the name of the Deputies of the States General, has been in conference. The Lord of Elfen has departed for Gelderland; conversely, the Baron of Yffelmoydc has returned from Overijssel.

DRESDEN, August 15th. An expedition has arrived from Warsaw to the King, with news that the Primate of the Kingdom of Poland died at the Stone, on his estate at Skiernieurcz; it is said that the Bishop of Eimland would come into consideration for this high office; and that the King will shortly return to the same realm. It is reported that the Bishop of Plock, coming to Warsaw, was there struck by a heroic misfortune and was in danger of his life. The Bishop of Culm was seen approaching here.

REGENSBURG, August 15th. This morning the Imperial Diet convened, and both the Roman Catholic and Protestant estates sat in conference in their respective collegiate chambers; the former to take up the matter of the Chamber of Wetzlar according to the Kryzelt Commission Decree and to conclude it; and the others to make their reply to the proposals of the Roman Catholics, which were laid before the protocol on May 30th of this year. In essence, according to the Roman method, it was thought that all grievances on either side could be settled to satisfaction; regarding some matters, one must deliberate further, as it is assured the Protestants will do, in order with the first to deliver a further representation concerning the religious affairs in the Palatinate to the Kertzell, principal commissary, with whom the matter of the Kurfürst has since been long at audience; for some conditions from the citizens also [show] that there is yet no apparent [resolution].

Concerning the Director Capias, it is advised that the carrying out of the coming month of September, upon the known formalities, must be completed and can commence. Note: each shall be suspected that the citizens enjoy freedom from the fourmissement of 10 guilders, [only] when transferred to the 10th class. Notification is made that the market within S. de Leermarkt will be held on Mondays, though on July 13th on a Sunday and the Leermarkt on Monday eight days thereafter, and [there] will be offered for sale a new modern timbered house with [multiple] kitchens, of which six of its rooms are furnished or painted; together with a garden containing two summer houses and diverse small structures, previously provided with all manner of places in the surrounding land, [containing] roughly one morgen of land with convenient situation; anyone wishing to view the house and conditions should inquire at Monsieur Hendrik Koor, residing at

Jacob Magnus, broker, who will sell at Amsterdam [and at] Dordrecht on the 28th at 2 o'clock in the afternoon, a good lot of new colored goods, fine broadsides and various other manufactures; as well as silk goods, bonratts, striped and solid calimancoes, Eltamines, Serge du Bois, Serge Jacobs from Dort, Martaus jewels, and Cornelis Verlinde, broker, will sell at Rotterdam in the Wy. Bristan, in the inn the Black Lion, [various] goods and [sweet] Tobacco, and this by Cavelings as all the aforementioned colors.

Berlin, August 19Th

[A ceremony has been held and the child Fredrik M. has been chosen as [something]; the court officials and second family of the King's goodness, that they]

Great Britain

LONDON, August 22nd. On the occasion of the restoration of public credit through the passing of the Bill, a keen meeting of the Annuitants arose, in which [a] spokesman made an address stating that [matters] wherein good faith greatly guided, they said whether the excess was well; but said that the dealings of the [East India] Company were right, that he sought at Duntsdeln to obtain counsel and was elected a respectable man as Director to proceed fluently to London so that all was better regarded. Mending the welfare of [his] mentions would at least hear what they concerning this by printed course in the good week. Subsequently his Lordship spoke of those which for the past and the latter comprehended [?] they had never had the good ceremonies and [?]. Subsequently his Lordship spoke of those which for the past and the latter comprehended [?] they had never had the good ceremonies and [?]. Subsequently his Lordship spoke of those which for the past and the latter comprehended [?] they had never had the good ceremonies and [?].

Holds to a mysterious order in my name and words, which I have spoken to say.

Upon which the Lord Chancellor [stated] that by the King's address at the

Thus have the Commons only commanded the bill for the public credit in proper form with a clause to the benefit of the brokers. The annuities have risen in agio and Amsterdam, since they greatly [?]. By the [members] of the Lower House none of the imagined advantages [seemed] still common, wherefore a very great number signed a Request and the same shall be noted in the [proceedings], as [has] been done in the preceding days at the Commons. Yesterday those of the Lower House much debated and put forward to the Lord [their] opinion on the bill for public credit, upon which the House adjourned until Thursday following a certain [matter] and his will [regarding] the Camp Day. That same day a certain Mr. Cole, a wild [?] broker, [was found] with his wife at the foot of his bed, lost [himself?]; Credit will [be] attributed to the worse handling of affairs of the [East India] Company. Today the Lords read the bill for public credit a second time, discussed it in committee, heard the report favorably, and ordered that the third reading be done tomorrow in [?]; many annuitants today heard the report submitted, and that the request of the Lord [?] was presented, and [the?] not read, whereby all their anxious concern for the Parliament's good will [remains] uncertain. The Hardilutor Kendal runs the Second Church in Trinity Lane, which they have all selected, to repair and beautify on their account. This week Sir Wibiardi Wright, who has long been a Lord strenuously occupied in Warwickshire, died. The Brigadier General Stanwix [is bound?] for Newport in the island way to Lud in [?] parliament to be chosen. It is assured that the brother of the Lord Fards has arrived in this city. The advice from the Bank is: O.I.C. 146, Z.Z. 143, Best Annuitants 10, same Londensis y.

also much goods have been shipped to be conveyed to Holland.

England

also much goods have been shipped to be conveyed to Holland.

I have given my Chancellor order in my name and words, which I have [begun] to forget.

Upon which the Lord Chancellor [stated] that by the King at his wit being from the Throne as follows.

Advice and Hardilude.

I am pleased that the affairs have been brought to such a point, that they [?], genre [?] upon the Report the those of Z. Plan was for you. to come together. Warning from the most able minds was [?] may it in a good hand credit will begin [?]; which will welcome [?] shall be made.

I have great compassion for the

The Netherlands

I thank you heartily for the [?] and very for the lending service of you in that concerning [?] from the civil [?]; I foresee truly that the law of your [?] will likely keep course of your sufficient pains to

Advice and Hardilude.

I serve my for this occasion we all our traders of Commerce have on the [?] of all the [?], which I most necessarily you further well do.

I commit then you and I God's blessing and state to preserve.

Resolved are all careful that the great relief which my parents have been granted to preserve and appeared.

---

Printed at Leyden by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedel Market. August 25th, 1721. With Privilege.

Notices & Advertisements

At Amsterdam by the Jan Sloons of Waesbergen, booksellers at the Water IJk and pleasantly residing in 100 Netts, and their Narrenpoesten, everywhere through pleasing diversions, by Father Abram of St. Clara, second edition with figures. The broker Dirk Biethouwer advertises herewith to all that he is prepared to rent some stables on the great Market at Rotterdam, [suitable] for persons of high and lower rank, as well as to all other faithful travellers, arranged on scattered alleys as before; whoever may wish to give him occasion, as he has done hitherto and ought to be done,

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 25th of august, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 103.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 27th of August, 1721

---

Year 1721

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

BERLIN, 19 August. Today the King honored Baron von Vriethuysen by [?] his son, and keeping the child Fredrik Wilhelm [?] with Madam von Forcade, wife of the General of that name: the mood in this region has been most cheerful; and the entire family finds itself so exceedingly delighted by the King's benevolence that they can scarcely praise it enough.

Great Britain

LONDON, 22 August. On Wednesday the Lords read the Bill for the Restoration of Public Credit a second time, and it passed without any alteration being made to it: the third reading. [Text uncertain] the Request of the Annuitants, regarding which Debate Lord Compton spoke at great length; wherein he cited many instances which he had well read, or rather said that he had read in good faith, [?] the heart of the remainder of Parliament under Oliver Cromwell; but by the same token the actions of the former Directors of the [South Sea] Company, the first laws, that he mistrusted, and concerning the Directors to become Scheimen, and thereupon added that as soon as such a Man became Director: Scheimen began to become: subsequently his Lordship complained that the House had not given sufficient confidence to the better of this whole matter, and said that he knew no one for such a long time among Your Lordships, to maintain this matter rightly, with rejecting such a bill as this, whereby the welfare of 5,000 people would be curtailed, or that one should at least hear what they had in their breasts [i.e., their grievances], that consequently and he during his 2 weeks' confinement in the Tower, in the circumstances had seen the profit of the Noble Lords their forefathers, their rights, and those the People had forgotten. Subsequently his Lordship said that he did not doubt he would live to see those who have stood for the Bill; and why more by their same confrontation against the list of those who have come to Adrian. Yesterday the King came with the customary ceremonies into the House of Peers, where the Commons were summoned and having come, His Majesty gave his royal address to the Advancement of the Complete; Then [?] to an Act for the Naturalization of James Addius:

King George I's Speech to Parliament

I have given my Chancellor orders to deliver to You, in my name and authority, a needful discourse to be said to You on this occasion.

Whereupon the Lord Chancellor made known to both Houses that he had received from the King's hand the King's Address to both Houses of Parliament from the Throne, which he read, being as follows.

My Lords and Gentlemen,

I am pleased that the affairs of this and the preceding Session have been brought to a reasonable point, so that I have the opportunity to give You some time for rest, after the great labor which You have endured in the three preceding [sessions].

The common misery, arising from the ill management of the [South Sea] Plan, was upon Your arrival so greatly increased, that the provision of the most capable means for the government was [necessary]: but it is so great satisfaction to me to see that public credit is restored to a better state; which gives me great hope that it will be completely restored, when all the measures which You have taken to that end are brought into effective execution.

I have great compassion for the suffering of the Innocent, and very severe displeasure against the Guilty, and have already given my approvals to seek an accommodation by You, which You have presented to me for the Institution of the Authors of our last affairs, and for the improvement of the [?] Justice against all which You have thought necessary. I was at that time willing and prepared, by my council General Fardou, to promote arrangements and the transfer of the orders of those, which many in such general confusion unwisely brought about, in [?] the overseas.

I thank You heartily for the Supplies which You have granted for the current expenses of this Year, and particularly for Your relieving me of the Debts and the Arrears fallen to the Civil List; to enjoy in good [credit] in the foreseen goods, which probably [?] shall soon be settled. These provisions of Yours, welcomed for the maintenance of the dignity and honor of the Crown in the Kingdom and beyond, are new evidence of affection for my Person and Government.

My Lords and Gentlemen,

I make use of this opportunity to make known to You that We have now restored the Treaties with Spain to the same footing as they were before the late War, which is an indispensable advantage and absolutely necessary for the Trade and Manufactures of the Kingdom.

I commend to You all earnestly in Your Good-natured Handlings Godliness and conscientiousness, and to preserve the Peace and Rest of the Kingdom.

You are all aware that the uncommon [troubles] caused by the great disturbances and intrigues have stirred up: But settled and quieted by wise opposition to Libels; but I am free [to say] that by the previous service in [?]

---

Wednesday's Courant

of the different places, or all the Enemies of my Government, who have shown themselves to be in a position to draw advantage from our impatience, and to inflame the sufferings of my People more by stirring up discontent and aversion, shall find themselves disappointed in their evil intentions and designs.

What the Lord Chancellor said at Court on His Majesty's command:

My Lords and Gentlemen,

It is His Majesty's royal will and pleasure that this Parliament be prorogued until Thursday the 30th of October next, to meet here on that day; and this Parliament is thus prorogued until Thursday the 30th of October next.

It is now seen that in the Bill for the Restoration of Public Credit, the Brokers who have held their Actions and Subscriptions shall not be relieved at once with 10 per cent, but that the Company has been authorized to retain their [funds] according to the Laws as is fitting. On Tuesday the Lords Commissioners of the Treasury met again until Tuesday the 2nd of September at Delfthuysen, which is why Mr. Walpole, with some Commissioners, is hastening tomorrow to his country house in the County of Norfolk. The Polish Minister, Baron Steiches, is departing hence to his Court. The two aforesaid Turkish-crowned Ships, which were mentioned before, have not yet been burned, but the order to do so has already been prepared; but the Owners thereof insist strongly that the 6000 pounds sterling, which has been granted by Parliament to make them whole, shall be given to them before fire is set, and otherwise 113 Middlesex prisoners, who are held in Newgate, have been brought to Ship, to be transported to Virginia. On Tuesday there were delivered at the Mint 137 Ounces of Gold, and 6,750 Ounces of Silver, and yesterday again 400 Ounces of Gold, and 1,764 Ounces of Silver, to all be conveyed to Holland. An Express has been sent to the North Sea. The Actions of the Bank are at 133½, East India Company 147½, South Sea 146-147, Bank of Millions 113, Lottery Annuities 101, Bank Annuities 100, ditto 4 per cent 94, Africa Company 30½, the Amsterdam Exchange 10-10½, ditto London 8½-[?], Joint Bank 34-33½.

France

PARIS, 22 August. The Parliament, the Chamber of Accounts, the Courts of Aides and Mint, as well as the Magistracy of this City, have congratulated the King's body on the happy recovery; being presented to His Majesty by the Count of Moreüsan, Secretary of State, being introduced by the Heer de Granges as Master of Ceremonies; the word was given by the Head of the same Body. The day before, the Bishop of Orleans [?] in the hands of the King [?] a great audience of the Canons of Orleans. The red [?] the great [?] first President spoke. The Heer Gilbert Béchou of the Head of the University having been, have likewise congratulated His Majesty. On the 20th the King was entertained by the Count of Clermont and the Marshal Duke of Villeroy, his Governor, at the Castle. Several other [occasions]; in the afternoon His Majesty took the diversion of Hunting and shot various Birds. His Majesty [was] entertained in the evening by the walk at Rouille, where the French and Swedish Guards were arranged in Battle formation, and to demonstrate their joy at His Majesty's recovery, three Salvos were fired by the Musketeers in his honor. The Heer Montmorency, Son of the Duke of Luxembourg, Governor of Normandy, is to marry Mademoiselle van Angieerg, Daughter of the Duke of Bouillon, Grand Chamberlain of France. The Prince of Conti is to be received shortly as Head of the Superior Members in the French Academy. The Baron von Beutenrieder, as Minister of the Emperor, has again inquired of the Ministers here of the Kings of Great Britain, Spain, and this Court, whether among the aforesaid three Powers, to the prejudice of his Emperor and Catholic Majesty his Master, a Triple Alliance was recently concluded and is being maintained, and whether a certain Power would also wish to come forward to enter into it; to which all the mentioned Ministers have again declared that they have no knowledge whatsoever of this, and that such rumors are absolutely false and spread by malicious intent, yet with certainty one can be sure that such rumors shall remain utterly false. According to some advices from Spain, it is said as if the Queen Isabelle of Castile [Editor's note: likely referring to a Spanish queen consort], from Bayonne where she has been residing, might well return to Madrid, and that one might well grant to her Majesty the 40,000 Spanish Ducats, which the deceased King her Husband has left to her by Testament. According to letters from Rome, it is reported that the three Cardinals Albani, upon the intercession of the Pope, have made complete renunciation of all their Paternal Goods, which they possessed to the advantage of their brother Don Gusto Albani, who in the hands of the eldest Cardinal, as Cardinal Camerlengo regarding his Principality of Soriano, had made the customary renunciation: the same letters report that although Cardinal Alberoni continued in secret to seek to receive [something?], and at the same time [?], his Holiness [the Pope] had him informed that he is to abstain, if he does not wish to be forced to do so by the castle of St. Angelo: meanwhile in the Palace where the mentioned Cardinal intended to take up his residence, many Bales with Goods had arrived: his Palace was [?] in Rome by those known to smell out his service [?] spirit, and [text cuts off]

Left Column

Recently there have been remarkable occurrences. It is noted as quite remarkable that at the Cathedral of Santa Maria Della Scala in Rome, in the Church of St. [?], the Cardinal Beluga recently [?], where he had absolutely refused [something] to some Ladies who were very devout and pious. [Someone] notes that a visit is being made to pack up the paintings of the deceased Queen of Sweden for the departure of the Duke of Orleans, who purchased them some time ago from the court of the King of Sardinia; which purchase by the said Queen has now been completed. According to the latest letters from Genoa, one has received news that in that harbor, by misfortune, a merchant ship caught fire, and they salvaged almost all the cargo from it, and made a hole in the ship, whereby the same [ship], already sinking, was preserved. The hull remained; and the greater part of the cargo was saved within two days thereafter the aforesaid ship was again raised from the bottom. The severe storms with thunder, lightning, and hail had multiplied around three cities and lands; the same [ships] were all damaged. The King of Sardinia had [?] in Piedmont some income from spices, and namely woolen fabrics from the State of Milan, in such manner brought into the War [?] closed in [?]. Father Quinquet, celebrated preacher of the Order of the Theatines, has received from Rome his Bull as Bishop of Tripolis; he has been appointed by Cardinal du Bois as his Suffragant in the Archbishopric of Cambrai; for which he shall receive annually 4 or 300 livres until he be given a fatter bishopdom. The preparations which are made in this city against the 25th of this month, being the day of St. Louis, are indescribable; every family, be it great or small, strives therein to excel.

There has arrived here since the departure from Madrid of the King's Minister to the court, and another has been dispatched thither, [news] about which one is assured regarding the forthcoming evacuation of the strongholds Fontarabia and San Sebastian; concerning which some difficulties still arise; this court wishing, one is assured, previously to have regulated some affairs, in order to accelerate the opening of the Royal Congress. The expedition which the Lord Campredon has sent from Sweden to this court, regarding the preliminaries concerning the peace between the Czar of Great Russia and the aforesaid Crown, has been sent back there again within two days. The Turkish Ambassador which has been at this court and [?] his journey towards Cette in Languedoc, somewhat slowly, because he had to observe the holy days according to Turkish law: one has received news that according to orders from this court on the Greek islands considerable marks of honor have been shown to him. The aforesaid Ambassador has sent a handwritten letter to the President of the Oriental Languages [Society?] in this city, thanking him for the good services during his presence there.

One has sent the aforesaid Ambassador several fine bouquets of flowers and other plants; it is said that one of his servants, who received them, has ensconced himself in a cloister near this city, to have himself instructed in the Roman religion. One brings from all parts news that the inhabitants of this kingdom were much grieved at the first report of the King's indisposition; but afterwards experienced great joy over his recovery. One praises greatly the splendid festival, etc., which the Lord de Merville gave in Cambrai on this occasion. Some wish to make it known here that the celebrated Lord John Law [Editor's note: the Scottish financier], in the manner of traveling over Germany toward London, departed, and that he had himself transported to Ostend thither, about which in this city various discussion occurred; because merchants and other correspondence from Venice received [news] that the aforesaid [person] was found there not long ago. One assures that the matter concerning the liquidation of the [?] was finally resolved in the council of the regency on such terms as had been mentioned in a former [report]. The aforementioned Monday the commissioners, which [?], have been thanked for their trouble, and one [?] hear that also [?] the old Louvre [?] are used. One sees two ordinances from the King's council of state; one of the 2nd of August ordered that the receivers of the [?] shall be held to carry within a month their receipts from Florence, so that arrangement could be made concerning the payment of arrears, of which the debt is [?] per penny; the other of the [?] of this month contains that the commissioners who must give certificates of the accounts [?], sent by messengers and other carriers, shall be held to have said certificates verified by the officers which are designated by the registrars and placards of the aforesaid good merchants, aldermen and [?]. One sees in the gazette of this city an extensive account of the exceeding great joy which has been shown by the regency, the magistracy, and inhabitants of the city of Lyon over the recovery of the King.

From Abroad

CAMBRAI, 23 August. On the 23rd of this month the Count of Frovano gave a ball and festival, which was exceedingly splendid, at the city hall; there were more than 100 persons masked, and over 500 without masks; the Count of Frovano was masked, as was also the Marquis Severini, minister of the Duke of Tuscany; and also all the civilities were without masks; at the city hall 44 torches were placed, and about 600 burning lamps, from which one had made a crown around the portrait of the King. Below there was breakfast in the morning; and as the 25th of this month is the day of St. Louis, all the ambassadors which are present have found it good to go that day to [?] to perform more ceremonial honors, which day also King Louis [?] will be celebrated, in his 13th [?] being born on 23 August 1707. One brings daily a great quantity of [?] in this to the magazine that stands on the Esplanade. The house of the Count Torqua is furnished with all [?], and sufficiently readied.

AMSTERDAM, 23 August. The dispatches from Stockholm report that the Russians with a great number of galleys, supported by some ships of the line, had come to England and the Baltic; but one does not know that they had landed; the English Admiral Norris was also with his fleet taken to that region. According to the latest news from the East Indies, [report] that on [the?] April the Cape of Good Hope was departed from, some ships there [?] to the fatherland [...]

WEST

SURINAM, 15 January

The ship de Graviane Spa, being like the other 200 here at Paramaribo, [?] driven, had split; there is [?] the ships as well as of Simon van Ooms, and the aforesaid ship [?] July from here with good [?]

FROM INLAND

PETERSBURG, 4 August the Duke of Holstein, [?] the Lord Chancellor Ruß [?] has arrived; his Czarish Majesty [?] the ministers shall [?] at [?] the reception [?] the great ones and those dispatched there and the requested cannon that [?]

Italy

NAPLES, 3 August. One from Monticene, Sub-prior [...] Valentine discharged [?] over the same [?] lost [?] the feast of the Holy Chapel was held. The [?] of civil and of capital [?]; that is also ordered [?] [?] to send, to the guard [...] order [?] [?] concerning the war boats which one took on the aforesaid beaches [?]

ROME, 9 August. [The feasts of] St. Petronella, and N. Ignatius [?], holy [?] were [?] a [?]. [?] [?] St. Peter [?] from his [?] was in this church through 28 [...] the day [?] the Passion [...] in the Roman Swiss canons, [to do] [...] to go, as here [...] that corpus to send. Cloister of the Swiss of [?] St. [...] and [?], an altar [?], [?] with [...] and Colonna, much [...] and nobility, which were there [?] by 23 [...] here the same [...] and there long [...] the entire nobility of the [...] to gather themselves. Through [...] the father [...] the permission given the cloister to come. [?] same Roman [...] with [...] the [...] province, the magistracy, [...] had, in response to [?], and [?], [...] [...] was done [...] timely that the aforesaid [...] [...] had become [...] were. The [...] Tuesday some architects [...] sent thither, to [...] to give at first notice.

LIVORNO, 8 August

[...] of this month, [...] where an Alicante [...] was offered, and [...] the day after

GENOA, 9 August. [...]; from half of the agenda [?] against: [...] day, which [...] of [...] the [...]; [...]

General CARGO or Lading of 22 East Indian Return Ships; in letters, cheese [?], rice cakes, sandarac stone, 2 Amboina [casks?] for the chamber Zeeland; Barzadia, Fratenburg, Elisabeth, Manila, de Clat Cerdao, Commerce from Batavia, [?] Dickamage, Elanpoin, and the [?] Dunk of Ceylon; for the chamber Amsterdam; N.[...] from Batavia, for the chamber Delft; ter Hail and Streza from Batavia, for the chamber Amsterdam; Voordeel from Batavia; for the chamber Hoorn; The [?] of Enkhuisen from Batavia, for the chamber Enkhuisen; being Batavian ships departed on the 1st December 1720; and this from 13 November 1720 named:

24,293 p. pure ginger. 2 bales p. THE HAGUE, 26 August. The Lords States of Holland and Friesland [meet?] tomorrow. [?] were received. Yesterday morning was the H[is] M[ajesty], [?] of the[?] of [?], [?] of Leyden, [?] of the Baron of [?] on the [?] meeting of the aforesaid States and [?] of the province of Overijssel. There has arrived an expedition from Vienna to the household [...]. In [?] of the morning [...] go toward night to [?] concerning the arrangement, until the time of the lease [?], and [?] on the city hall. 27 Aug[ust]

Course of Exchange
