Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 29th of October, 1721
West Indies

CURAÇAO, 5 September. Since the departure of the last ships, various vessels from the surrounding islands have arrived here; as well as [news from] Willem Stoel and Jan Buysman, along with 2 Zeeland vessels, which are anchored off Tabago. Our Bark the Diamant has come from near Cuba to Cariquete Cay; and it is said that a small bark of ours was taken by a Captain there, though we could well make our position felt with our ships. Regarding the profits of the ships, there is much discussion of ships which have not produced any return through trade; moreover, one received word by letter from Captain Jan Valk, commanding the Sint Gely, that on the sixth of June he lay in good condition with the ships of the Captain Jacob Juyn and F. Verhuizen near R.O.L.A., and that Captain Valk had lost his sloop but recovered it again. The ships of Piet Lootshouen and Bontekoe are on their way. One hears nothing here or in the vicinity of any damaged ships.

Portugal

LISBON, 11 October. Since the first [of the month], various merchant ships have arrived; as well as Gillis Pekehatring, Inse Fierdinck, Jan Kolter, Jacob Knaap, Willem Beverdige, Jacob Heyman, Hiram Joubert, etc., [with cargo] valued at 300 [units], paid as in Amsterdam for goods from the city of Archangel, without having encountered any pirates. On the [?] departed from here Jacob Buysman bound for Amsterdam, and some ships for England in the Strait; and also on the [?] of this month Pieter Appelman for Amsterdam. Despite the unpleasant weather, one already hears of several vessels on the quay and also not all the Algerian or Salée pirates here in the vicinity have seized ships. The salt at St. Uber is firmly settled for the owners at [?] per kettle, 300 [units] delivered on board, as in the previous year 1700, [price] in Reals shall be fixed.

Germany & the Adjacent Kingdoms

VIENNA, 15 October. It is reported that His Imperial Majesty intends to have the Palace of La Favorita rebuilt within a short time, so that Their Residency may be established in that city. There is talk of a new Opera House, which is to be built in proper style. His Majesty has bestowed upon the Count of Althan [Editor's note: likely Ernst Rüdiger, influential court official], his great Chamberlain, on the occasion of his name-day a considerable gift of 15,000 Reichsthaler. It is reported that this Count shall be declared Grand Master of the Emperor's Household, in place of the deceased Prince of Liechtenstein; and the realm has already taken this into account regarding its interests. The Count of Brunn in Moravia has received the Regiment of Bagni, and that of Galeyd has been given to the Marquis of Villa Nova, Colonel of the Regiment of Cordua, which has been discharged. There is talk that the Dragoon Regiment of Beteitst may well expect considerable promotion from the Principality and the authorities to grow, which increase noticeably daily around this city.

HANOVER, 14 October. [Towards] the end of the previous month, Mr. Samuel Bruinman has been [?] here. It is also reported that there [?]. By advice it is not yet probable that in Vienna [?]. Some advice is not yet probable that in Vienna shall be followed.

BRUSSELS, 14 October. It is reported here that on 10 October, Mr. Samuel Bruininning [?] gave there the appointed Officers, that the King of Prussia, one does not yet know it is probable that in Vienna shall be followed.

France

PARIS, 23 October. The Archduke of Naernemis has departed from here with [?] his departure by the Lords of [?]. The Bishop of Languedoc has [?] his reputation from Languedoc from Languedoc from [?], and his reputation has from the sickness [?]. The Lord [?] is [?] there much needed as [?] froyaatte has yet [?] week down [?] Bruissengte Rutte, fair son of the Castle of [?] counting. There that Gade [?] is no [?] in the New [?] the Arlende [?] would [?] the Harcene Rome and Loonie [?] were to his Council established, with his Council established, Ride of this, [?] and of Lagen) and there already established, with his Council established, [?] and of Lagen) and there already established, [?]

PARIS, 24 October. Last Tuesday evening, Carouche and another criminal who was with him attempted to escape from the Prison by means of a hole which they had bored in the wall, and came out through the cellar of a house near the Prison, from which cellar they reached the house of a milk-seller, who had a small child, which was so frightened that it awoke the milk-seller, who made such a noise that the Guard, who was on watch in the house, came to his aid; the Watch in the house door sent [?], help came; the Watch in the house was stationed there, [word] Christopher reillion with [?] and Boys under the bench lay, there they took him from there and placed him again in the Prison; that they kept him in a guarded [?], and after they had bored the foot-irons also still others to the hands and so much around the body had been done, that he with much trouble should resist, was given him a Syrup Martas to lie upon, and one placed a Guard of 2 Archers in Prison beside him, who [?] is excluded from the same. Since that time Mekaer in the Prison [?], one has still several of his associates in fetters placed. Among the Convicted of the foot bound there above are more than 30 Soldiers from the Regiment of the Family Guard deserted, from [?] one a faithful months are. Those days there were also some English seized, who wished to [?] ships to go to England to travel over; conducted, because they were accused of having supplied false Money.

Yesterday were 70 Commissioners of the Capitol dismissed, although one has kept 12. The Office of the Colonials, which must be under [?] may be diminished in short. In the arrangement of the measures, the same shall be diminished in short.

I don't need the text to be perfect—corruptions and gaps are part of working with historical documents. I just need you to provide the specific Dutch transcription you want translated.

The 29th of october, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 131.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 31st of October, 1721
Portugal

LISBON, 16 September. Departed: Captain Pierre Bernard of Nantes, Jean Iandat, master of Bayonne, Benoit Bortolo of Genoa, John Evan of Cadiz with grain, Corn. Vilders of Archangel, Will. Fidler, captain, Bletin Smit and Manuel Henriques bound for Tercera; John Harris for Faro; Joseph Ricford for Challoge; Dimoa for Sylviania; Joseph Pestum for the Virginias; Francois Epiranlier for Cadiz; and Eduard Sipley for London. The outgoing fleet for Fez-Lambourg and that bound for Angola still lie on the River.

LISBON, 8 October. Since the 9th of last month, departed various vessels: Paul Francois Podella and Lazarus Osman of Cadiz; Pedro Bittomo of Gueira with grain; Joseph tablo of Barcelona; Salvador Roquin of Genoa; Domingo Fernandes of Villa Nova with grain; Francois Alegre for Venice; Jean Bautista Jourdain of Genoa and departed Cadiz with grain; Louis Eleven for Viana with brandy; Agc. Burmando for Amsterdam; the English-Cologne ship, Captain George Pioteraw for the Strait; Robert Fagan for Shirley; Thomas Thomson for the East Indies; Jonathan Capel for Genoa; the English passport [for] Bullen for Plymouth; the East India ship, Captain George Porris for London; George Whitta for Cork; Benedetto Marville for Cadiz; John Theod. for Cape Verde, etc. Two hundred vessels lie on this coast, comprising English, Colonial, Hamburg, a Genoese, 13 French, 11 Dutch, and 1 Spanish ships. Note is taken of new orders including a prohibition on the export of grain. Only 6,000 moios of salt have been produced this year. A merchant and fishing vessel laden with salt, forced by storm to come before St. Ubes, has been seized by the populace; it is said there were certain Moors and a Cossack aboard; however, a Dutch ship [intervened] and the Dutch Minister secured its release. On the coast of Algarve, a small vessel was robbed by a pirate ship; such occurrences have not been seen for a long time. The storm currently rages on this coast.

---

Friday's Courant

[Text continues with religious imagery of the Archangel Michael and references to schismatics departing the land to enjoy the pleasant season.]

GENOA, 11 October. As the pestilential sickness grows severe in Languedoc and certain other provinces, and this sickness has been halted in Provence, though difficulties are occasionally experienced at the gates, our Board of Health has increased the number of sentries posted near the Lazaretto, who as commissioners shall oversee the green areas for three days to ensure nothing passes through. The same magistrate has also requested the seals that shall be placed on all ships departing for Spain, so that these may be admitted in Spanish harbors without burdensome further quarantine. Mr. Cesar de Francarelli has been elected to the Great Council by the privilege of Siemmaca as our new member.

MILAN, 11 October. It is confirmed that the Italian Council in Vienna has rendered judgment regarding the pretensions of the King of Sardinia as Duke of Savoy concerning the Langhe.

VENICE, 18 October. Wednesday: The Feast of St. Theresa—the Doge with the Council held chapel in the Church of the Nuns of the Name. The Czar's envoy, Count Sara, [announced] regarding the execution of a peace [treaty] between His Majesty and the Crown of Sweden, containing all conditions. Word comes that the Galley of Dalmatia has arrived in the Province of Istria and that Commissioner Mocsaigo is stationed there.

---

England

PETERSBURG, 6 October. Yesterday an express arrived from Novgorod bringing the ratification of peace between His Majesty the King and the Empire, with a letter which, though sealed with great joy, brought certain sorrows: a general Thanksgiving is to be held on 2 November.

E.N.

NAPLES, 7 October. Last Wednesday, being the birthday of the reigning Empress, High Mass was celebrated in the ordinary Palace chapel, presided over by the Chief Priest. Subsequently the Queen received compliments on this account; and in the evening an opera was performed at the Palace, one that had been shown in Paris, but in all exceptional matters was altered as to title and rank. Saturday the Papal Nuncio performed the customary functions from the Ecclesiastical Grade of the Noble Order of the Franciscans, who had come here to take their leave of the new Royal Monastery. Sunday was celebrated in the Church of St. Dominicus at [the feast of] the B.V. [Blessed Virgin]. Sunday was observed at the [church of] B.V. of the Rosaries, with the feast at the Church of Minerva of the Order of St. Dominicus for veneration, [and] religious ceremonies were carried in procession with great crowds of people. That day the majesty of Allilain, Cenfiaqos, Rohan and six other persons of the highest rank were brought forward; subsequently various activities of this new invention were provided. Monday the [?] used the place of the four [?]. The ordinary celebration occurred in [?] St. of Malta on the celebration at the Collegiates at Duidenlijem, where Clementina maintained her custom for the feast. Tuesday the celebration was performed in the Quinalt by Cardinals Spinola and Cruduall, Conti and Imperiali; as secondly, regarding the majesty of [?] in the chapel of the [?], as this was performed through the priests, [and] procurations of the Commander of the [?] were carried in the Better Commander of the [?] under great crowds of people.

ROTTERDAM, 11 October, Sunday. Feast of the B.V. of the Rosaries—those positioned on the French frontiers cannot come here, as no one is permitted to pass.

---

Spain

BILBAO, 10 October. The ships for Amsterdam, England, etc., having put to sea, few vessels now lie here, as the tide has arrived. Those located on the French frontiers cannot come here, as no one is permitted to pass. After our correspondent held [?] by order of the Court, a Council [meeting] regarding the ships [?] respecting the customs houses here established, which the Council has forwarded by express to Madrid, so it is hoped that the customs will be restored as before.

SAN SEBASTIÁN, 14 October. New and more pressing orders have been received from Madrid requiring that water or air through which French goods have been imported, though they themselves are spoiled, and which by merchants or residents are kept, must be entered in a manifest and the keys preserved by the General, which is most injurious to commerce. Some militia have been sent to the quay of Iron; and all [?] in the Palace there to [?]. Regarding the Admiralty, one awaits daily updates; the return [?] of the Court.

---

Sweden

STOCKHOLM, 15 October. Word has come that the Czar has had the peace with this Crown published; the ratification has not yet arrived here. As the bad weather has confined Their Majesties to this city. Brigadier Cmmftrom has arrived from Holland and has had the audience of His Majesty. Great joy is being observed here over the hoped-for peace, so that the ratification is expected soon.

---

Germany & Adjoining Realms

VIENNA, 18 October. The recently arrived Papal Nuncio, Mr. Grimaldi, made great preparations for his public entry and has sent some of his attendants to Leipzig to purchase six of the finest three-year-old horses, without regard to His Majesty's order in these matters. His Imperial Majesty having settled the matter of Traubon on this following occasion likewise as the Dutch Nuncio [had done]; the same being arranged in the Court [?] and Chancellor [?]; the aforementioned matter being arranged in the splendid audience [?]; minute details and prominent gentlemen paid him their compliments, which had formerly been done by the French envoy, who has since departed with dignified generals' retinue to Mildern to arrange the celebration with the Imperial Vice-Chancellor. Equally, and today [?] in the funeral services [?], and today in the court [?] the matter of Liechtenstein, in the Imperial court of the Augustinians.

REGENSBURG, 23 October. As the Imperial Diet was not complete for this Monday, no discussion of business took place. The question of the maintenance of Philipsburg and Kiehl, the oldest matter among [?], has not been resolved because it cannot be done there, so that this matter must soon come to a conclusion, and must be committed to the committees in the colleges. It is, however, determined that the Elector [?] shall be bound to [?], and it will be seen to. Hereupon the Imperial [?] corps remains here, as the matter is yet pending and to proceed and has been prepared, [and] on Monday next the military [?] shall depart.

[Sent from Vienna]

MARSEILLE, 11 October. The great Council of this city, having observed the good health that prevails in the air here, has made this known through proclamations. There is some satisfaction in maintaining a strict quarantine in the harbor, which has also been newly published; the previous quarantine is set to end on the 21st of this month. It is desired that as of the first of October ships may return and sugar may also be prepared to sail to Genoa and Livorno, provided they carry proper health certificates, even though they come from places that are not yet fully cleared.

PARIS, 12 October. It was yesterday morning between 6 and 7 o'clock that the Duke of St. Simon departed for Madrid; he had been in conference beforehand with Cardinal de Bois, where it is believed the Duke of Orleans was present incognito; he has told his friends that he hopes to return shortly. Gilbert is a counselor at the Spanish court and confirms that said court is resolved to have the Duchess depart. It is said that the approaching Queen of France is to take her leave from Madrid around the 20th of November and proceed hither. According to the letters that the aforementioned express courier brought, the Duke of St. Simon engaged a convoy to meet him on the road; a courtier then departed who spoke with him on the journey. It is assured that the journey of the approaching Queen of France hither may be postponed even until December, and that she may remain so long on the Spanish borders until the marriage of the Prince of Asturias is arranged, which is said to be confirmed by December; orders have already been given to the court servants to hold themselves in readiness. Madame de Ventadour will accompany the journey to the borders with the servants of our future Queen, though they will make the voyage with smaller retinues; there will be two more guards of the corps who will depart within 5 or 6 days. The Duke of Orleans is expected shortly from the Spanish court to this city, in quality as ambassador to this court. The Abbot de Viloa has died these days; by his death the Abbey of St. Seine in Burgundy has been vacated, and the Order of St. Benedict, under the bishopric of Langres, which yields 200 livres in annual rents, besides the enjoyment of the same, has been filled. The First President of the Parliament of this city is ill in the country, as is also the wife of the Marquis d'Ambrese. The Count of Laval marries the widow of the Marquis de Baye. There is new talk of undertaking the canal between the channel of the River d'Eure, to lead it to the Seine. Mademoiselle de Rohan, Abbess of Jouar, a small priory of Mademoiselle de Ventadour, who was very seriously ill, is reported to be better. One continues with the sale of the furniture and household goods of the Grand Duchess of Tuscany, though one finds in this magnificent items. Moreover, on Monday last the Comédie-Française presented the promised play of Cartouche; [Editor's note: Cartouche was a famous French bandit of the early 18th century] they were to have played it on that day, but it was judged that the piece required alteration; they have asked the public's pardon for the same. The Italian comedians presented the same play in their manner the same day, and it pleased the public so well that the next day no place could be obtained even for double money; yesterday they presented it at the Royal Palace, where nearly all the court and nobility gathered. The archbishopric of Rouen has not yet been filled; the Bishop of Nantes has many claims to it, but it is said that the pontifical brief for the general liquidation is imminent; there is talk of good appointments, and should there be few without houses, it would amount to an excessive sum. There is mention of a composition for 100 commissions to expedite the tax collection from Normandy; this office will be placed in the [?], where the revenue officer has made a decision that is before the gallery. News regarding the pestilential sickness is somewhat better. At Bordeaux they have encountered the plague (in January), though very carefully no access is allowed to the trade in goods; at Orange there are still various cases, though with good hope; at Avignon and other places in that county, as well as at Sables, Coutelas, Monteux, and Sorgues, the sickness is also being treated; indeed it is said that the ports receive neither men nor beasts readily. The latest letters from Marseille published by the ordinary courier report that in those quarters there were no more sick, all the contingencies having passed, and that around 200 had recovered. At Salon there were about 1,200 cases and others had since become ill. With regard to the matters concerning the loans, it is announced that the extension of the loans for the ninth year of this year must be completed, and that the drawing of the tenth class shall proceed. For each day it is appointed: that the loans through the tenth class have been freed from the renewal of their lots, and the same sum must be executed as was tallied on the 1st of July of this year 1721; so long as the other tickets are not yet drawn out shall the continuance proceed on advancing conditions, whereby they shall be obtained in the names in the cities of this province and by the customary annual drawings.

---

LEIDEN.

[Various health reports on plague conditions in southern French territories]

AMSTERDAM, 29 October. Word comes here from Brest of Tarses, which reports that it is said the merchant ships from Marseille, and the traders from Brittany and Normandy will shortly be fully cleared. The shipwrecks from the fleet of Calonne are to be forgotten; it is said that there the drought and continued dryness have caused a slight spirit of [?]. From Port-au-Port there has come a ship of [?]. The Company's ship the Lima, arrived from Brazil, in the month mentioned is there [?], into the [?] here in the [?] and other goods were brought.

At Port-au-Port arrived Gerrit Blom of Gutenberg, James Brockman of Riga, and a ship from Stockholm; at Bonedale [?], Peter Brandaris, Simon Ham, Egge Rykers, [?] Jobs, Harmer Eliseum, and five others, 200 from here, the Maas, six others; to Cordoba Michel Jubos of Brussels, and [?], [?] and other [?]; at Gigon [?] Bank from here; and ships from Monmouth; in England the British warship of Capt. George Hoo and [?] to London, Burgomaster Ackbert Bussum from here; to Stockholm And. Dut, Roos, from Bergen Jan Cainer Hille, and in Danzig Tel. Brack Hou, all from here, shipmaster Jan Brander, from Lubion to Stockholm, have been captured in the course.

The ship the Egmond on Zee wrecked, is owned by Cola Vander Hutten, a [?] damaged; the good wheels the goods robbed. At Bordeaux lying Door [?], [?], and more at Bordeaux in the book-shop of Johannes van Brasse appear a large quantity of [?], alongside the [?], Burgomaster Samuel Castle, in his house [?] by them and under the Lindens, Kitterdamm etc., to do, [?] by the [?]. Lysmans, Gouda Stael, Garcum van Wylt and said.

Lots of the third class drawn on Monday on the 1st of November, lots that have been marked in the third class, and only the aforementioned lost lots are verified, but in all classes all the other unopened lots are verified.

Commerce at Alicante brings news advertised, that they the English goods, which on Monday the 2nd November and following days are believed, they the aforementioned [?] on court gladly, now [?] the living [?], to the [?] goods are given [?]

Amsterdam sold on Monday the 1st November 1721, in the White Swan 1200 green Drenthe parts, 1800 fire parts, 1100 Bildersma, 2000 ... from [?] balsam, 1700 Nismonals in parts, 2100 Zealand parts. Middeldam sold on Wednesday the 3rd November 1721, in the White Swan [?] to the wife, Samtalia Cusmanen.

Hendrik Liquet Maksman, who at Amsterdam sold on Monday the 5th [?], a quantity extra pure juice of about 115 cartons of [?]. [?] customary [?].

---

GIBRALTAR, 1 October. The sailing of the usual ordinary [?] of which captain is from the red many brickships about in [?] and other goods, the said [?] of the days, there lying in our gulf, by the [?] lieutenants and [?], the not allowed in remedy, best those days, one in earth and godliness, good existing here there six exchanges of the fleet of [?] on Spain and Genoa; the captain [?] Camelus Stirula goes toward his England [?]; the encounter [?] [?], to Bremen [?] on Gibraltar, and there the bear Dutch ship Lisbon; in the same sold the beasts; the [?] [?] [?] Joseph Long toward Rix [?], the Simbi Reidam; l'Esperanza Francisco del [?] [?] toward Stockholm, and [?], and Samuel Anderson toward Gibraltar; at England, at France, at [?] the ships; and also the entire slaves, without that anyone better. Indeed [?] or [?], that one from a [?] has to say.

MADRID, 24 October. On that day the Herr Basdi [?] of Almansa married Mademoiselle de [?] that day here also on the solemnity and now with the [?].

The [?] of the aforementioned France and [?], through [?] of the [?], the Count of [?] [?] to [?]-Salamander; the now [?] are out hung and there [?] was also to Salamander [?]. [?] are to [?] still not [?] on [?] to [?] to [?] in the [?], that one from a [?] has more to say.

Printed at Leiden by F. H. LAX DE KLOP above the Weighhouse. 31 October 1721. With Privilege.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 31st of october, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 3rd of November, 1721
Monday'S Gazette

Year 1721.

### BARBARY

TETUAN, 21 September. One of our privateers has brought several good Moroccans here this month to [?], the new lighthouse vessel with Captain [?], in which the ship caught fire; being [?] with Moors and [?] Christians, among them 3 Spanish [?], sailing from Amsterdam to Cadiz.

GIBRALTAR, 3 October. The Dutch warship of Captain Oosterwyck that entered here is busy preparing to sail out again; and that of Captain Roo is still before the Wall, to cruise off the Spanish Coast, as Vice-Admiral Sommeldyck does off [?]; it is said that the aforementioned Vice-Admiral will depart for Cadiz with his Squadron within 8 to 10 days.

CADIZ, 7 October. The Gold, Silver and other goods that arrived with the Fleet, which was reported this week to have been delivered to the English merchant; and within 2 to 3 days will be handed over to the shareholders: the [?] that has been gathered here is said to be for His Majesty, a Don Gregoriaan von Huiztten named. Not enough that within 8 to 10 days the Squadron of Vice-Admiral Sommeldyck, whom they wish to have chosen, will see to it that the healthy merchandise does not return to Amsterdam. It is reported that near Tetuan a pirate has been captured. Arrived in this Bay are: the St. Rothe, Captain Carlos Snilly, from Alicante; the Anna Maria of Duykevam from Genua; the Margareta Laurents Tallohon from Martinico; the Rencuatro Henri Angier; the Africa, Captain Girald Britaine; and the English vessel Captain Umpitery Orme from Gibraltar; and the British merchant ship Loo, Captain George Trotteau from Lithon; in return departed: the John, Benjamin Leys for Gibraltar; the Alma, Gerrit Hauvert for Alicanten; the British [?] Krook, Joseph Long for St. Lucas; the Samborn, Francis Ward for Antwerp; the Concordia, Vander Hinde for Copenhagen; the Marquis, John Mevily for Malaga; and Captain Anderton for Gotland; so that by 24 December eighteen ships had passed, and still three English, one French, one Dutch; 2 Hamburg and 2 East Indian ships; as well as 2 English warships with their crews, would depart toward England in a moment. Since then a new report has been sent here again, which it is said a Rotterdam man has announced, that one had the happy news of the marriage of the Prince of Asturias with Mademoiselle de Montpensier, whereupon the same marriage was made known on that very day according to circumstances; and thereupon many signs of joy were shown in the following days, so that it is said that the circumstances will continue. The Marquilla of the aforementioned [?] is [?]; the [?] of [?] is named to [?] of the Count of Haarlem, the Count of Almancha, the Baron of Gandia, the Marquis de [?] of Gandia, those of the Balbalros and the Marquis del Suro as Chamberlains; that [?] also as Stablemaster appointed. For the Princess have been named the Marquis of Valtrio as [?] and Chamberlain; the Count of Caltelot as chief Chamberlain, which [?] was with [?] Stablemaster; the Count of Angitiola and Dorigo as chief Stablemaster; which [?] was with [?] Stablemaster; [?] for the Duchess of Montpensier and [?] of the opposite Chamber; the Duchess of Lilia, the Duchess of Montpensier and of the opposite Chamber; the Duchess of Gandia, the Duchess of [?], Donna Maria de los Nieves Auguisada, the Marquise of Almansa, and the Grande Dania d'Ulla as Ladies of the Bedchamber. The office of Father-Page of [?] of the Prince of Asturias is said to have been assigned. The Marquis of Santa Cruz will be the future King's Secretary, [?] as chief Marshal; [?] will be future Secretary of War, and [?]. The Marquis of Montpensier [?] that by the Marquis of [?] named Secretary of War, and [?]. Following [?] in the order [?], that [?] the third [?] Brienne of London [?] in the Secretary of War of the King of France [?] to Secretary of War of the King of France [?]. The Marquis of Cordiale [?] will in the [?] Duchess of the Navy, which [?] in the [?]-Secretary; the [?] Banquet will be [?], and the [?] Brienne will in the London [?]. The [?] Banquet will be remade according to the saying that in the [?]-Secretary will in the [?] Duchess of the Navy, which [?] in the [?] of the [?] in the history of the [?], so that the [?] Brienne [?] his [?] [?]. In the head [?] in the lodge [?] the Great [?] [?] [?] [?] so [?] in high will the [?] of the Infantry by [?] [?] order, so that the Gold-[?], where the castle Gold [?], where they have in the [?] already in the castle Gold [?] [?], and the [?] Duchess will on the last [?] [?] do a [?] [?] [?] sent, which with an Express letter [?] sent.

BILBAO, 12 October. On the [?] it is said [?] until late [?] had published the news of the marriage of the Prince of Asturias with Mademoiselle de Montpensier, which was made known [?].

---

PLAGUE was reported: from 14 September to 9 October, approximately 200 people died in Mende; the Prior of the Abbey of Chambons was among some other clergy who died there. The disease spread considerably in the Church of Plesance; thus the Bishop of Viviers, and that of Orange went through this city without [?] their bishoprics; all the bishops of Languedoc have received orders to betake themselves to their bishoprics.

PARIS, 27 October. The departure of the Princess of Montpensier is expected to take place on 20 or 21 November; she will be a month on the way to Bayonne, around which time it is expected that the Infanta of Spain will also be able to be on the Frontiers, who naturally [?] of Montpensier [?] the Spanish Count, for which the same [?] has engaged a Master who comes to give her lessons every day. Madame de Cheverly, Governess of the daughters of the Duke of Orleans, will accompany not only to the Frontiers, but even to Madrid. The clothes and other precious items that are sent a day and a night, will be by extraordinary transport, indeed Royal ones. The Marquis of Maulvrier will assume the character of Ambassador and Plenipotentiary at the Spanish Court, as soon as the Duke of St. Simon arrives there to sign the Marriage Contract. It is believed that Madame de Vantadour will depart on the 13th or 20th of the forthcoming month with a numerous suite, which will be sent ahead for the service of the future Queen of France, to the Frontiers. The Man of the [?] which the young King has chosen, will be some Hofmeister of the Infanta. The 50 Guards du Corps, which under the command of Brigadier Brilladene, and two [?] near the Frontiers must [?], have orders to hold themselves ready. The Duke of Grammont departs to his government of Bayonne, to keep an eye on the Frontiers of the Crown in this occasion. The Duke of Osuna is expected in a few days in the city in the capacity of Ambassador of the Spanish Crown; he brings about a million Livres in cash to cover the expenses of this Embassy; it is said that he will have fully 70 to 80 [?] gentlemen, pages, valets, etc., in his service; the first state coach that is being made here, and on which both day and night have been worked these 14 days, will be exceptionally costly, as well as its [?], and it is said that they will be doubled for each [?], so that on the departure of this state, as is chiefly the matter, one can with splendid decoration provide security to the household at Court; in this city work almost 100 people to accomplish everything as much as possible for his arrival; probably they will already have on one day a Diamond crown with a [?] on, which is worth over 150 thousand [?]; it is assured that at some audiences [?] silver coins will be scattered among the people; in short, everything will be at the most splendid and glorious at that time at Court and in this city. The Duke of St. Simon has indeed left this city, but he will remain in the government of Burgos as long as he receives news that the Duke of Osuna has arrived on the Frontiers of this Kingdom, after which he will make his way to Madrid. The King is, through favorable condition, His Majesty has recovered considerably since his last illness, and yesterday was taken to La Muette. Mr. Palsaprat, whom his misfortune makes known among the fair French, died last week of an apoplexy. The handsome Count Gordon wins at Court with gambling; last Friday he lost greatly in Europe with Mr. Fiesteville, who for the second time [?], and the third in the second game the second [?], and the third time lost all three. The office [?] it is said [?] that [?] now from the Minister and Secretary of State for the Dispatches [?] the States [?] the Count of Erreux intends [?]. It is said that the Count of Erreux intends [?] military [?] as Colonel General [?] to do so; Marquis de Coigoy in the same sentiment [?] in the Dragoons. The Duke Regent is presently [?] the Commissioners of State of [?] in the Control of the Louvre to examine the State of [?] in the Control, named [?] to see which [?] come to Chablis, and which not which [?] will be; there would be 70 Commissioners in the Louvre, and 30 in the Bank; already would 6 Commissioners of Visa in the Control be, in a strange Chamber erected. The Duke of [?] as Marshal of France, [?], and Duke of Rabbier, to examine and inspect all the differences which might come before the fall of this State [?] in the Bank, to bring to the Rocks of the [?] State the Commissioners of [?]. The general Pages have [?] to pay 150 thousand Livres, [?] [?] [?] the Mr. Adine, one of the other [?] is overloaded, and the Mister Durant is ([?] it is reported) [?], is overloaded, and the Mister Durant is ([?] it is reported) [?] form of 150 thousand Taxation and [?] on their Taxation [?] presently [?] Taxation of [?] will be of the Harbors and [?] all kinds with the [?] will be of the Harbors and [?] all kinds [?] the Commissioners of Embassies [?] of the [?] and or Commissioners of other Letters of Confidence which [?] [?] other letters of Confidence which [?] [?] or Commissioners of other Letters of Confidence which [?] or ordinances of other Letters of Confidence which [?] [?] of all ordinances of other Letters of Confidence which [?] [?]

LYONS, 17 October. With [?] of [?], since 14 [?], [?] of the most important illness [?] to decrease. In [?] Burrette [?] in the same, and [?] had [?] still [?] notable persons, occasioned [?] during their lives, that the illness for the [?], that the illness for the head [?] in 1694 none would have perished, to [?] the city [?] that in Sunday [?] to provide. [?] has [?] occasioned that in the [?] Duchess [?] [?] occasioned that in the [?] Duchess [?] in London [?] went, and the [?] which in this [?] were [?], and notable ones on the last [?] [?].

From the Printing House [?]

---

Great Britain

LONDON, 18 October

This evening the King returned to his Palace of St. James from Kensington, after their Royal Highnesses the Prince and Princess of Wales had made their departure yesterday from Kew House in Litterholt. On Saturday, Mr. John Ward, along with several other merchants trading with Portugal and corresponding with Mr. Wilfeld, and also the gentleman at the Counting House, who recently arrived with wine, waited upon the King at Kensington. The representations concerning the state of affairs being heard, His Majesty requested the necessary measures to be taken in all cases, and the matter was immediately referred to the Ministers. It is reported that an expedition has been sent to Portugal, and it is hoped that certain ministers and the Consul will have such influence with the Government that the matter shall be moderated, as our trade with that realm has suffered some damage on account of [?].

From the Commons of Dublin, it is heard that on the 16th of this month, in order to prevent frauds against the Crown, the matter was taken under consideration, of which the Report shall be presented in the following January. The Earl of Rotterdam, the Lord Viscount Bledington, and the Lord Althem were ordered by the Lords to examine the Officers of the Black Rents, to ensure they maintained their privileges properly. The Bill for the establishment of a Bank in Ireland has been referred by the Commons to the Committee. It is reported that the settlement of the Draper Mill with alterations shall be permitted by virtue of the last Act of Pardon. The Commander of the Guard at Ford has marched to the sea coast to assist the Toll Officers in case of need, and to compel incoming ships to observe strict quarantine.

At Bletchford, 200 acres of ground have been set aside, which will be made into a fortification, where some 20,000 people shall have their tents. The Order is being built by order of the King, and Dr. Mead has taken the necessary steps concerning the Model thereof. Given these prudent precautions, while the most serious sickness is raging in a neighboring realm, it is deemed necessary that in this City the health should not be complete, and also that one should be well prepared against this plague as a preventive measure. And concerning the danger of the Pest-Sickness, it is found that those who have contracted it are now fewer advised than our Doctors have mastered, only Qualimiles knows, as they make their proper journey to the Province which lies beside Italy, where it is hoped the plague shall be curbed by the great precautions which the Italians have taken. To leave no means unused for the prevention of any unforeseen misfortune, the Commander of the Council shall sit every Sunday and Wednesday to consider the most suitable means by which the Pest-Sickness might be removed from this Kingdom. The Committees of the City and Tolls have received the answer from all the Harbors, upon the letter which they dispatched to them at the inquiry of Lord Townend, concerning the Dutch warship that favorable Pelicans should have had; but such tidings were forgiven in all the Harbors.

The Members of the Houses of Parliament have begun to arrive at the City to be present at the opening of the monthly session, which has been fixed for the coming Thursday. The preceding Sunday shall be the trial of the servant of the noble Lord Bellingham at the courthouse of Newgate, because he had caused the windows of the house of the inhabitant of London to be broken. The Common had broken into this lady's chamber at night with a dirk in one hand and a firewood in the other. To Plymouth a frigate has been sent by the British toward Lisbon to intercept [the enemy], and thus it has come to pass that this ship shall not fall into the hands of the Government of Spain. It is reported that the silk merchants and traders about this city have been in a dispute in the case.

The Director Copnis makes known that the drawing of the Lots of the ninth shall be completed on this day with full execution, and that the drawing of the next shall commence. Note: Each one to whom it matters shall be assured that the Lots which have been drawn (though they may be lent from multiplication of their Lots, when they shall be drawn out) shall all be well, and in proper place, must be accounted as [?]

---

Auctions & Sales Notices

Tomorrow, Tuesday, 4 November 1721, the brokers Jan de R[?] and his associates shall sell at Amsterdam in the Old Gentlemen's Lodge a party of cloths and lakeens, 200 in weight, blue, red, and other colors; silks, half-silks and wool sundries; also a party of East Indian merchandise, such as extra curious Chicanes, 200 codfish, tea-goods, and other useful linens; a party of genuine linens.

On Monday, 3, 4, and 5 November next, at Leyden at the house of [?] the named Library of Dr. L[?]; the [?] November from J. da Vivie and the remaining Faculties shall continue according to the order of sale. Among these: Cora. from the Lieuvel, Jan Lemmers, and Hendrik Linquet, Brokers. On 30 November, V.P. and further [?]; on the Singel in the Doelstraat, 30 packs of Moscorite Linen, together with about 20,000 pairs of extra large Moscorite.

On 19 November, continuing on following days, through F[?] Library, books of all kinds to be sold in various languages, such as under Philosophy, Jurisprudence & Greek by Gropin and Grosmne, complete in 24 French Parts of Pribbend, including one of the Catalogs (together with 2 others, which the week before were to come on Grobsein, University, Ware, Leyden, Lassemans, Dordt., and Harlingen, Delft, 4. Ermac, Haarlem by L.t, Gorki by the Kiel, Groningen by Vries, etc.

Jacob Magnus, Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday, 19 November at 3 o'clock in the afternoon a good party of newly fashioned coloring and a variety of pipes, including foot-wear and several other manufactures, such as flowered stockings and groceries and more; all viewable the day before.

In the Doelstraat, a party of red Dasmusten and fashioned jewelry; [?] tomorrow in the Zuden, in the former jewelry; being Lejlke Boef, Corn. Aldeis, and Tjirk Corneliste Schanp, Brokers, presenting by agreement the young Eumile, in the year 1704 newly displayed; all under by the deeds [?] and all good visits in the [?], his name.

At Amsterdam on the Dam by the bookseller J. Oslietsyk is now available and following the Mareldoot of the Macluberde, the J. Luy, and in which 1 a Sapphile Maatice in Folio, on large Printed Paper, for 1 [?], and delivered from the Dutchmen: for the Lovers some are on [?]

---

Leyden

---

East India & Overseas

GOA, 22 December 1720

Yesterday it was learned here that the Vizir Allacan, General, [?] of great ships more [?] of that people were placed, who was the first Minister [?]. The [?] as ordinary, had [?] to be measured for the King by the Embassy [?], [?] the [?] State of the news [?] as ordinary, had [?] to be measured for the King by the Embassy [?], [?]

BRAZIL

BAHIA DE TODOS OS SANTOS

The Vizier has received my letter from [?] as well as great [?] of the land, various clearings to work upon [?] to be heard, cut off [?] the fortifications of S. Beato.

---

Italy & Central Europe

FLORENCE, 14 October

The Court shall hear the great Mass on the 27th of this month, upon which great ceremonies shall take place in this city. In the Church of St. Lawrence these shall be said. These Abbots, it is said, were among the numerous monks here about, have been spurred on. We further read that the Pope of Modena was pleased with the Duke as Father had given.

VIENNA, 2 October

This day the anniversary of the birthday of the Dowager Empress Maria Amalia was celebrated with great joy. For the preceding days the Princely Chancellor had departed from here by order of his Court to elsewhere.

FRANKFURT, 29 October

[Entry appears corrupt or incomplete]

COLOGNE, 31 October

The levy of Cologne toward Liège may well remain behind, and the brigades are disbanded.

On the terms of the tenth Clielle for twenty on 17 November, the Light Clielle shall absolutely on Monday the first December proceed with the following Lots, after expiration of the red Clielle (when in the Clielle other occasions of Lots [?], a party [?] Leyden lakeens, of 8 and 9 quarter perveisle Leyden pile, half silk and wool stuffs; about 200 Camerali-halte, as extra curious Chicanes, Chithe Coats, Graperers &c.; a party Poceily-Leyden in hope of h. Ara. Importantly, it would be wished all Faculty people to see this day.

Hendrik Linquet, Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Monday the [?] party extra purks purk of about 130 Cans Moscovie Talk, [?] following days, through P. Halma, at the City Doeles, shall be sold a [?] of Indigo, without which the Consul [comp.] in 24 French Banders, and many [?] large Furniture and [?] [?]

JOHAN KNOCK, in his lifetime, Member of the [?] State [?] all which would [?], at Amsterdam [?] according to [?] of silk to [?] at [?] and by the afore-mentioned [?]

Wednesday 19 November 1721, in the Old Gentlemen's Lodge, in the morning [?] coukers, blue, purple, [?], as other known, silk and [?] stuffs; curious spice-dealers and chil-ties, Delftware, Calamander, kirammen, Stepien, Saistte Kouthan, Wool [?]

Thursday 20 November 1721, in the morning at [?] o'clock, in the [?] halte [?] by the sale, on Wednesday 19 Nov[?]

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 5th of November, 1721
East Indies

On 12 December 1710, from the Empire of the Great Mogul, the following reliable news has been received here: namely, that the Vizier Abdulkao, together with his brother Alao Alihan, General of that realm, where they had risen to such high station through their [?] and great influence that they had become the most powerful in the kingdom; for after the Vizier Abdulkao had caused the death of the previous Mogul Phemuzar by putting out his eyes, and subsequently causing him to die, he placed upon the throne in his place [?] of Kenjir [?], who had been the third [?]; but since the same died shortly thereafter by [?], the aforementioned brothers immediately had another brought to the throne, namely Pius Sayan Baduu, to the residence of the capital of the realm called Agra. Not content with this, they sought help from Lodging in Nicomer, a Prince from the Kingdom Birud of [?], (who) according to some reports is said to have [?], which was brought here by a local [?]; and various [?] having taken place, the Vizier Abdulkao, through the power of money, had the gates of the city of Agra opened and seized the new King and brought him [?]; but shortly thereafter through his treachery, when [?] as King dismissed, and himself crowned by his enemies, under the pretence that the third King Sayan Baduu was brought to his death, so [?] has also been reported. It appears that the Vizier Abdulkao and his brother have meanwhile made much [?] change, and [?] in [?] and had killed the third King, and [?] after the spilling of 300 much blood, [?] in full [?]. It is reported that [?] Island Salloe, since the island Sumbra had also been [?] by Portugal; the same [?] of [?], and certain Metal-Mines &c. [?] aforementioned.

Brazil

BAHIA DE TODOS OS SANTOS, 4 July. After the departure of the Fleet, on the fifth of April here [?] such severe weather as has ever been seen, wherefore in this city many houses and on the land various [?] with [?] civility have [?], which by order of the Magistrate, to prevent greater inconvenience, were entirely closed; also it should not be forgotten that the churches of St. Bengt were damaged, and thereby 12 [?] houses were [?]: one of our Bulwarks in the night was greatly [?], wherefore the Viceroy had the metal artillery [?] take that. Outside the city in the [?] much greater, for the river S. Amaro in Cachoerva through the manifold [?] and [?] through the St. Amaro more than two houses washed away, and in the [?] also [?] closed; whereby in the [?] much [?] was suffered; then a great [?]-hand of the Citizens was [?]. To those [?] high [?] [?] [?] to [?] and [?] were flooded. West Indies

CURAÇAO, 4 September. On 22 July there arrived here a NASSAU from the Stephali[ans], very great Concordia of Roeland, in Holland, a Bark of [?] and [?]. Bernard named [?] brought from [?] and further brought from [?], and further more [?] situated a ship from Porto and St. Dominigie, and [?] and situated a ship from [?] as further, and thence [?] are [?] further [?] [?] of the Barcelona by Bark from the Vliet toward [?] [?] n [?] much [?] to the [?] further brought from [?] and [?] 14 back laid toward [?] [?] A time before [?] [?] of the [?] and [?] [?] in [?] Old [?] Lodging, morning [?] all Ships, mostly; [?], [?] [?] all Ships, common Ned. England

Paris, Jan Mulder &c. from various places, and the young [?], [?] in Amsterdam; from [?] and until the 33rd of Aug. Lisbon, Picter Berga, Jan van Hoorn, Wil. Exton, Jaq Muller, Wil. de Vos, [?] Andrews Kook from various places, a French Bark from Guadeloupe, one from Martinique, one from St. Thomas &c.: from the 23rd until the 23rd a Bark from St. Domingo, Capt. Willem Seel of Amsterdam, and [?], Will. Kranevelt &c. from various places; on the 23rd past here the Ship of Capt. Petri [?]; from the 23rd to the 31st arrived the Barks the great Concordia from the Islands, Elias Conry &c., as well as Wil. de Vos with news that the Bark the Diamond, Capt. Balth. Jantse is at Tunis. Past here have also been mentioned the Zeeland Ships of the Captains Jao de Moor and Francois Thijlen coming from Zeeland. Here lies presently the Bark the great Concordia, Capt. Jan Gentilfij and at the Cataques-nic Coast in good state [along with] those of the Captains Balth. Exten, Jac. de Weyder, and Capt. Jao de Joog.

Portugal

LISBON, 30 September. The King has signed an Ordinance in his own hand, causing it to be announced to all people of whatever estate or quality they may be; ordered is [that they bring forward all the great [?] they have under their care, whether Statues, Medals, ancient Paintings, rare Books, Memoirs, Manuscripts and Inscriptions, whether in the Phoenician, Greek, Carthaginian, as Gothic, Arabic, or Moorish languages, whether from the times of the Phoenicians, Greeks, Carthaginians, Romans, Goths and Moors, that have been drawn out of this Kingdom; even from the time of the Occupation of Don Sebastian, who in the year 1578 in Africa, in the Battle of Alcácer was shot dead; and against the [?] in order to fulfil his Majesty's [?] and against civil persons to be punished according to the [?] article of the Ordinance established on the Mint. On the Coast of the Mines some of these [?] have been [?]; but concerning the great Mines it goes [well] [?]. On the [?] of the previous month there came in here a Spanish Orthonship of 18 [?] with news that there were a great [?] seven French ships of 80 to 90 [?], which along this Coast [?] the beds had done; but through a gust of [?], which one [?] meant to be, which had been committed on various [?] in the side-sea; being upon a flat [?] alone the Priests spared. Here was [?]-[?]ship has here [?] slipped in, since it was to be seen from before [?], [?] was: on the same bound the Marquis of Château [?]; on the same bound of the [?] to Madrid [?]. With the Count de Gadelligo &c., both toward [?] to Madrid [?]. With [?] Advice from Bahia one has news that on the 17th of their August there [?] by Capt. Joseph de Simmen-

France

PARIS, 27 October. It has cost the Government some 60 thousand livres of French money before Cartouche was captured, since various parties of people in this city have engaged [in the search], and as [?] lands have borne the cost of capturing him; and after the name of Cartouche all Farmers and [?] in a Land-Map to all Farmers and [?] in a Land-Map it is published that the report [?] the Farmers [?] and [?], which thus entirely according to [?] is not, but of another [?], who on the flat land between &c., which [?] by our [?] had. England

[The first section is heavily corrupted in the original transcription and cannot be reliably translated. The text appears to discuss parliamentary proceedings, but the Dutch transcription is too garbled to produce accurate English.]

France

PARIS, 11 October. Yesterday evening, around 5 o'clock, the Duke of Orleans arrived in this city from Spain. In recent days the Cardinals have not dined, but at midday His Eminence dined with the Duchess of Vendôme. After dinner, His Eminence, together with this Princess, arranged the ceremony to receive the Infanta of Spain. Monsieur Damehe has been promoted from her services to this function by the Queen, at a salary of 1,500 Livres, just as the ladies-in-waiting shall likewise have, excepting the first, who shall receive 6,000 Livres for this purpose. It is said that the young Infanta of Spain will come to lodge at Saint-Germain-en-Laye, on which occasion solemn suppers have been ordered, each lodging to be sought in the proper manner. Meanwhile, the Duke of Lorraine paid a visit to the Duchess of Vendôme, and her relations informed the Duke Regent that they could arrange apartments in the Old Louvre, where the Infanta of Spain will lodge. At the beginning of the coming month, one intends to begin with the dismissal of all corruption that exists in the civil service, and subsequently to honor the parliaments, where the young Queen shall be received. Also from Vitry and Mademoiselle de la Laude, who have been named as under-governors of the Infanta, and [?] the King's foot [?] as her first lady-in-waiting. There is mention of a general Paymaster, whom they intend to assign to the mediation, and each Paymaster shall have to advance 40,000 Livres. These days the Lord Baron of Orléans had a grand feast prepared at his estate, Luxembourg Renty, to entertain the Cardinal du Bois; but His Eminence was prevented by certain serious matters that arose from attending. Wherefore the Abbé du Bois, his brother, betook himself there instead, with the Abbé Fallienti, the King's confessor. It is reported that the Cardinal of Rohan has requested to be invited to Rome because the cool French air is detrimental to his health; and it is believed that the Duke Regent has also granted His Eminence this permission. The Abbey of Saint-Seine, of the Order of Saint-Benedict, which yields 6,000 Livres in annual revenue, has been given to the Abbé de Corlieron, Canon of Notre-Dame, son of the late President of the Sovereign Council of Alsace. Monsieur Law has increased the number of his servants with a candidate and some additional lackeys. The Countess of Polignac, who has obtained a priory from her husband, lodges with the Count de Stainville, her brother. The Officers of the Châtelet, having received their induction from the Prince of Carrouche [Editor's note: Cartouche, the notorious highwayman and criminal], he is said to be transferred to the Conciergerie under guards; and since this murderer has become notably active these days, his trial is expected soon. It is said that the jubilee will not be proclaimed here; whether this will come to pass remains to be seen.

Lower Provence

Monsieur Carllas writes from Aix on the 12th of this month. The city of Aix has modified its quarantine regulations; the countryside, excepting 2 or 3 villages, has everything in good order as regards health.

Monsieur L. confirms the complete restoration to health of the city of Aix, and that the hospitals have been emptied; there are still some remnants of the sickness at Beaulieu and Salon.

The announcement from Langeron of the 13th of October states that since the 19th of August there have been seven reported cases, and he sends a printed document that was published by the health board.

Upper Provence

PETERSBURG, 13 October.

To promote their religion through the Swedes is quite another matter in this land, but not in it.

On the 11th of October, the Russian Fleet under the command of General Admiral Apraxin gained a great victory at sea against the Swedes, which will have consequences for a complete change in these lands. The cannonade lasted from morning until evening, and was so heavy that all the ships on both sides were greatly damaged. The Swedes have suffered very great losses, and will, it is said, retreat to the shore to escape the Russian assault.

From the beginning of this month, a man has died, whom some believed to have been an Emperor; later, however, it proved to be quite otherwise, the man who died being merely a person of the sick; and all the dropsy cases are in good health.

The Lord van Bruyn, from the 19th of October, has learned from the Gentlemen de Bonas, Ligniac, and de Baune, that all is in good health in the districts of Rouergue and Auvergne; he hopes that the wine and harvests will long remain free of sickness.

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 3 November. The Count of Callenberg has brought news of the birth of the Princess of Saxony. [?] The week before, the burials at Ghent and Stuppes were changed; [?] none in the other places. The Flemish middle course is to be observed.

THE HAGUE, 4 November. The Prince Kantemir, Ambassador of the Czar of Great Russia, last evening at the end of dinner, under the weather of the day, has given trumpet fanfares, which was not successful, and continued into the morning. The Great-British Minister Cadogan was present at this gathering. On Tuesday, Monsieur Grys of Amsterdam was visited. In like manner the package, which the Count of Great-Duitsland has brought to the Gentlemen States-General, has well departed from the court at Paris.

LONDON, 31 October. The session of Parliament having opened yesterday, the King appeared in the House of Peers, where the [?] were heard and various [?] were discussed. The Chancellor, in the King's name, among other things stated that since the last session the chief officers of the Land's Government have made very good use [of their time], and [regarding] the King of Sweden; and this much of his reported fruit, whether what the King has made in the dignity in the negotiation [?] the Government, [?] on that footing the Spaniards and Austrians are in that great affront to His Majesty, and it is hoped that the whole world [?] give Manufactures in the decline of its time in dishonor of Trade, [?], which thus [?]; whereby one [?]. And thus hold the Germany with struggles of all governments and themselves the whole world not yet proved, of which one [?] uses; [?] whereby one [?] nourishing in dishonor of Trade, always particularly their [?]; that the whole world [?] give Manufactures in the decline of its time in honor of Trade [?]...

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 5th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.

No. 1721.No. 134.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 7th of November, 1721
Left Column

GENERAL NEWS

PETERSBURG, 13 October. The Reformed congregation at Riga has been granted by His Czarist Majesty a public church in which to practice their religion according to the rights previously accorded to them by the Swedes. All Swedish prisoners have been placed on good footing and clothed anew, and will shortly be transported back to that realm.

ITALY

ROME, 18 October. On Monday morning, Cardinals Paulucci, Orsini, and Conti paid their respects to the Pope. On Tuesday His Holiness granted farewell audience to Cardinals Bichi and Schadoni, who departed the following day on their journey to Germany. Before their departure, His Eminence presented to the Prince of Ruspoli his table silver, valued at 2,000 scudi, and to Prince Ruspoli table linen along with 200 fine damask napkins, which are 200 pieces of very fine work, so artfully wrought as to appear to be of silver. On Wednesday morning, Monsignor Cibo held a general audience with the Apollonians; for the second time placing all his secular affairs in the Pope's hands to undertake a wholly spiritual life; but His Holiness granted him this dismissal with reluctance. That same day, the feast of St. Teresa, the Pope visited various churches dedicated to her. On Thursday, the Cardinal of Althan departed for France on some business, though he promised to return here to celebrate the feast of St. Joseph, the Emperor's nameday. That same day, the Prince of Ruspoli sent as a gift to Don Carlos Conti a walking stick mounted with tortoiseshell, with a small damask knob on the head, and a saddle of crimson velvet embroidered with gold and Spanish point lace, to be used during the festivities, which solemnity will take place at the Church of St. John Lateran. It is said that His Holiness has remitted the ecclesiastical tithes to the court of Turin, so that His Sardinian Majesty might not burden his worthy subjects too heavily, and might be able to maintain his troops positioned on the borders of France to protect Italy from all interference. Cardinal Alberoni, who followed the court to Luisburg, has been detained not far outside that city; indeed, it is said that all the cardinals would be confined to the countryside, his processional privileges would be revoked, and His Eminence would be placed in captivity; some even believe that on the day of the papal ceremonies he will be taken from the Church of St. John Lateran.

[Continuation of Italian news - partially illegible]

FLORENCE, 13 October. Our Grand Duke, who has recovered so completely from his recent indisposition that he finds himself much improved from before, has shown great favor to his first physician and is content with his attendants.

GENOA, 18 October. For several days past, much snow has fallen upon the mountains in this region, such that the air is now as if the depths of winter had arrived.

SWEDEN

STOCKHOLM, 22 October. There is wonder at court and in this city that the Cardinal upon the Cologne Swedish [matter] could not be properly approached; it is understood that the expedition will be unsuccessful. According to the absolute confirmation of His Eminence, Riga has been secured.

GERMANY AND NEIGHBORING REALMS

REGENSBURG, 30 October. The Romans are making great commotion over the edict recently issued by the Emperor regarding several matters concerning the Protestants; upon which the Evangelical Corps will make a reply, so that there is much expectation here for further clarifications on this matter. It is said that the treaty will be held. The Carmelites of the choir [?] still hold in this matter of the church some precedence, [?] on a document that is over 400 years old. [Editor's note: This section becomes increasingly difficult to parse from the original transcription.] The Emperor Charles IV was once concerned that this distinguished political matter be maintained. The magistrates occupy themselves much with this, so that one sees in this realm the principal public matter relating to the treaty of Vienna. The Duke of Friedland has acted in coronation, the rest of his governance [?]...

[Remaining German section becomes heavily corrupted in transcription]

THE NETHERLANDS

TILBURG, 11 October. The Prince of Orange has through the efforts and charity of the faithful secured the care of many sick among the ill, and concerning their recovery [?]...

Right Column

Friday's Gazette

[Section regarding plague deaths and conditions in various locations]

...since 30 September... ...at the hospitals; the... ...of the troops are in good health... ...has been reported...

...on the 3rd of October through... ...a letter that all is in good health... ...to Aurenge; it is hoped that... ...the days pass.

THE LANDS [NETHERLANDS]

...the Count of Callenberg has issued the... ...of the troops in the Sint Ricen, [?]... ...at Ghent and Bruges has been... ...appears to be...

...how many, as the Prince and... ...Cassi of Great Rustaud, having agreed... ...under the sound of instruments... ...most notably, and has extended to... ...The Grand [?] Ministry Cadogarl... ...The doctors Michlet... ...at Amsterdam are heard. In the region the... ...of the Zydé and Madrid visitations.

...The sitting of Parliament glittered... ...greatly in the Priory and Hall, where... ...both Houses; was in His Majesty... ...of Justice the Third is held... ...of the treaty recent since [?]... ...so that many be His Majesty's... ...which has been held... ...through the Negotiations on those lands...

[Diplomatic correspondence section]

PARIS, 31 October. Since today an order from the King to all the sea-ports of this kingdom has been sent, to clarify the Article of the first of October last past, it has been handed to the Abbot Hope, Ambassador of the Lords States General of the United Netherlands. So follows a copy of the King's letter to the Controller General de la Hoopaye, as follows:

"Lord Ambassador of the Lords States General of the United Netherlands, having continued [the matter], the sought article of the Articles was in the Article of the first of October last past regarding the precautions that should be taken concerning foreign ships, however they should to comply in the Havens of this kingdom, which are not fully subject, except the ships from the Havens of the United Provinces; you have in hopes after examination of this matter, rightly desired, relation from these matters, upon the representation of the common Minister, and the Article has been examined by me upon Your Excellency's account, that the aforesaid article with certain public acts to be derogated, so it is not that Articles 4 and 6 of the Article of the Council of State of the first of October last past shall have had equal application to the ships coming from the Havens of the Republic of the United Provinces into those of this kingdom; the King not having wished by the aforesaid Articles to derogate from Article 18 of the Treaty of Commerce concluded at Utrecht on 11 April 1713 between His Majesty and the States General of the United Provinces, which exclusively establish consuls reciprocally.

"By Royal command I have been ordered also to ensure you, that the formalities guided by the preceding Regulations, which are spoken of in the old Article of the Council of State of the first of October last past, be the same as those contained in the Article of the Council of 30 previous 14 September last past, concerning the commerce of the Levantine merchants.

"Here declare you, on behalf of the validity of the Royal decree, shall you henceforth serve as regulation for Your Excellency's conduct with regard to the ships coming from the Havens of the United Provinces, and you shall conform yourself herewith, without however forgetting that the aforesaid article of the first of October shall be particularly observed. I am &c. &c."

PARIS, 31 October. The Duke of Orleans, who was somewhat indisposed on Tuesday, [?] such matters followed last Sunday to the old Louvre to occupy the apartment prepared there; it is said there is readiness made. The Prince of Conti has given to this Monarch the services of several nobles of Clermont [?] has there [?] lands. Rumor runs that the Count of Evreux, who is [?], might well [?] in the domain of the cavalry. From Saint [?] it is learned that the Chevalier Landais has climbed there because the King has resolved through expeditions to [?]; one continues to send five scudi in specie to Avignon.

[...] the city in the presence of the painters there [...], Merzowe, Law has built the Palace of Luxembourg in the Street of Michon [...], where the Chevalier of [?] will soon dwell; whereby the British in Holland will gain much advantage; the Palace of Treville is decked by the Duke of Orleans. Many are [?] here and Treville is adorned by the Duke of Orleans. Many are of opinion that one builds here so many palaces to the service of..., because his Eminence may travel to Caurobie.

The Comelien knows not how hastily the second troops pass through there, which will thereby be; in the Italian manner, the Cot of Cartouche [?] can [...]; in the Italian manner the Cot of Cartouche [?] was observed by the militia. The Lt. Criausetti [...] of the trial of Cartouche was [...]; the lawful transport there, whereby in the morning he will be brought before the Council [?] having been trapped and thus bound as captives there where [...] the Procureur General will examine his trial [...] at Saint-Austis, and thus his conclusion examined from the [?] the third day by the learned judges in his compliment [...] to do most...

Such murders: Callen would the warrant of from these continue to be pursued in Rouen; the through-sleeping murderer the [?] [...] touche begged to conduct himself, and the thieves who many Monsters sought, having been thus bound and sought the thieves which many Monsters [...] ley and would many people have had [?] [...] in the seminary of the Buyck shall [...] be made, considering he had, because upon the Schemes to meet the reasons still have [...] that by him to Sorla bleeds, [...] there rested there, through these him tomorrow, on good day has been, which late heretofore had Trieff there wished to Heda; on good day has been.

Left Column

[The opening section of this column is heavily corrupted in transcription and cannot be reliably translated. The text appears to discuss arrests and legal matters in France, mentioning a woman arrested in connection with Cartouche and a theft involving 2000 Louis d'or from President Frouleau's residence, but the original transcription is too uncertain to render accurately.]

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 13 November. According to a letter from Lisbon dated the 10th of this month, all ships had departed from there for Pernambuco and Angola; the fleet was also completed on 30 September. The following ships have since arrived at Batavia: Hudsonderck and the barbier-sloep for Zeeland; Buchenroode; Wyneudzal, Oldemanden, Castricum &c. from Amsterdam; Nieuzland from Delft, and Valkenburg from Rotterdam. The purchase of East Indian goods amounts to 4 tons of gold and 2000 guilders. At Bilboa (where Thomas Adi T'Thomela of London is located) lay ready for sail the English ship Coesmarts Blok, Tamea Klaver &c. From Bayonne the St. Jan Teunis Klok, the Coat of Arms of Zierikzee, Villem de Konin, and two others had departed. From Friesland likewise homeward [the text becomes corrupted here].

The Director Copreits makes known that the completion of the distribution of the lottery tickets of the third year must be accomplished; and that the drawing of the large lot shall soon begin. [Several lines follow regarding lottery matters and various business notices regarding the East India Company, book sales, and merchandise auctions, but the transcription quality deteriorates significantly.]

[Multiple auction notices and sales advertisements follow, with many becoming increasingly difficult to decipher due to transcription quality.]

Jacobus vander Kloos, bookseller of Gouda, will on Monday the 17th [November] sell his library, containing various subjects and languages. [Catalogues available at various locations.]

Jacob Nieouw, broker, will sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the [?] afternoon at [time], a great quantity of new-dyed coffee, and green type, double fool; and various other manufactures, as well as a great quantity of fine wool blankets, burnished sayens, bays, crepes, [and] other goods: all to be viewed the day before.

Jan vander Streng, broker, will on Monday the 22nd of November 1721 sell a very well-situated country house, named the Weigele Haasitlen, just outside the great gate on the Basin, with marked rooms, including sliding frames, laminate kitchen and tea house, with rain and pump water pumps; and fine grounds &c.; the property is provided with various fine fruit trees, gardens such as orange trees, with four [beds], named Onorcteet, with fruit bodies, and taxus [and] other [plantings]; [measuring] 2221 [units], suitable for a stable and coach house.

[Multiple further property sale advertisements follow.]

On the evening of 1st December 1721 at 5 o'clock, in The Hague on the New [street], a house and grounds [will be sold], together with a coach house and stables [for the] honorable gentleman [who is] Ambassador of His Czarist Majesty of Great Russia [Editor's note: This appears to refer to a property belonging to or associated with the Russian Ambassador].

[Multiple further advertisements for mill sales, book sales, and property auctions follow, with increasing transcription degradation.]

England

From the 19th and 22nd of the previous weeks we have received [word] that the King [has received] a fleet of 200 ships, 200 from Danzig, Riga, Narva, Wyburg, Stettin &c. [that have] entered.

GRAAFWIJKSCHE, 7th of November, Here departed the Minister Cadogan [for] Great Britain, [to travel] coming Monday or Tuesday to London, being dispatched thither for English [affairs].

France

LYONS, 12th October. They write here that the contagious sickness has penetrated into this city; whereupon all wise precautionary measures are taken in order to root it out.

LYONS, 29th October. [The text becomes too corrupted to translate reliably, discussing the spread of disease and various military and administrative measures.]

PARIS, 1st November. [The remainder is too corrupted in transcription to render accurately, appearing to discuss events at court and administrative matters.]

Course of Exchange

The course of the EXCHANGE RATE is today [Monday] as follows: Exeter 119, Seville 118, Bilbao 95 and 1 quarter à 1 half, Lisbon 44 and 1 quarter, Venice 94, Livorno 93 and 2 quarters, Genoa 94 and 1 half, Paris [rates], London 34 shillings 12, Hamburg Current 132 and 1 half, Danzig 207 Poles gt, Breslau 35 and 4 quarters St. Hamburg 32 and 1 half St. Antwerp and 3 quarters per C. Ghent and 1 half, Brussels 2, Zeeland 3 quarters, Rotterdam and [further details]. The shares of the Bank [rate and] [?] half, The shares of the D.I.C. 755 à 60, V.I.C. 96 resident, Sublet. 94 à 150.

Notices & Advertisements

On Tuesday the 11th of November 1721 and the following days, a collection of fine and well-conditioned books [will be sold]. The catalogues may be obtained in Amsterdam from B. Hartlem of Reifel, and in other cities named.

On Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday and Friday, the 11th, 12th, 13th and [14th] of November, at Haarlem in the hall of the Prinsenhof, under the direction of Abraham van der Foel, [a sale will take place] of a great quantity of fine and half silk fabrics, ratinnes, ardozzines, dyed Moorish goods, and English woolens and damasks; as well as a great quantity of ebony goods, worsted, linens, linen, calicoes, serges, catlaras, [and] fine floor coverings, and furthermore everything pertaining to a complete cloth and fabric trade.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 7th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.
