No. 1721.No. 135.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 10th of November, 1721
Spain

CADIZ, 4 October. Despite the fleet having arrived several days ago with goods from the silver [shipments], these have not yet been delivered; however, it is expected that delivery will commence within a few days. It is certain that some prominent merchants have remained in Algiers, while eminent persons from the same have come ashore. Since the beginning of this month, the following have arrived in this Bay: the Th. Robberts Williams, the S. Ant. Fra Benita Muiando, and the S. Samitha, Capt. Jan Pauli, all from the Canaries and the Bay of Bedford. Also arriving here are the Dutch Vice Admiral, the Heer van Sommerdyk with East Indiamen from Amsterdam, and a French Frigate from [?], which was seized by the authorities on account that the same's sails were too old, which the Admiral has restored, and upon inspection by Turks and the French Captain [?]. In the meantime, the following ships departed: the Somme, Capt. Fran[?], and the Leonora, William Daay, towards Lisbon; the Good Success, Jam. Rooss, towards Dublin; and the Maurits, Schanhan, towards Bilbao; N.S. Jan and Engeltje, Capt. Sapirote, towards the Canaries, so that there remain lying at anchor: 11 English, 16 French, 13 Dutch, and 4 ships bound for the East Indies. The Vice-Admiral Sommeldyk stands ready at the beginning of November with his East Indiamen, the merchant fleet being prepared to sail towards Amsterdam; though some ships [?] have had their cargo broken open.

France

LYONS, 29 August. Evil tidings have been received here that the pestilential disease has penetrated into the Dauphiné, and that this contagion has revealed itself along the way near Mauritillons and Pierrelatte. A troop has been drawn across the River Isère: this puts us on guard in every way. Some enemies of this nation, now inhabitants in the area of Ardèche, have broken ranks, for the pestilential disease was brought through [?] to Ferù, which would have been confirmed there at night, yet soon again an evil outbreak [?]. Word has been received from Languedoc that the pestilential disease makes no progress on that side of Rouergue. Avignon, the unfortunate Bordeaux, Grenoble shall be guarded in the vicinity of Guienne. The Petit does much damage at Avignon; yet others say that there it would go better towards Orange.

PARIS, 3 December. Yesterday, Friday and Saturday night, the infamous Camouche was brought from the great Châtelet into the Conciergerie, under proper escort and other officials, for the prompt consummation of [justice], for this is the [?] that brings the Province into great longing and concern. The burial of a good [woman], the [widow] Lyzaun of St. Meme, who was 88 years old, has taken place. The Abbé de [?] is going to live in the Street of Little Ambitions, and the Hotel Boulanger, where the Cardinal de Folignac has lodged. In the house of the King of Portugal it was agreed to establish a fund from all ecclesiastical goods in Portugal and Brazil, and to make thereof a fund for the maintenance of the new Patriarch; and for Portugal the yearly use [?] of the Pope's Brothers named for the promotion of the Crown. The Gentlemen de la Mandry, Controller General, and le Blanc, Secretary of State for War, are both of ready disposition. The Count de Marck has had a second attack of infirmity.

VAR, 3 November. The King finds himself in perfect health, as does the Duke Regent, whose slight indisposition of the 28th of the previous month has been without consequence. His Majesty has given 20,000 Livres to the Duke of Guiche, to demonstrate the dignities of his government of Lower Navarre in proper fashion upon the passage of the Infanta, for which he will proceed there without delay. The Princess de Soubise, who accompanies the Duchess de Vanvandour as her Governess, has received a Brief to command the affairs of the King's House in case [the Duchess] through illness, death, or otherwise [become unable]. Thus it shall be: the French and Spanish Ladies who accompany her shall take their leave from their Princess and each shall return to her Court with the replaced Princesses; and the Spanish Lady to whom the Infanta is entrusted shall remain with her only to teach her to learn the customs of the Spanish Court, so that the young Queen shall become accustomed to the French Ladies. It is thought that the King will go to meet the Infanta at Fontainebleau. Next Saturday the Marquis de la Farre will undertake the journey to Spain. The Ladies of Wickquefort and Nonant shall be under-Governesses of the Infanta, and the noble ladies of the two Dukes of Brittany shall be under the charge of her Chamber-Women. According to letters from Spain, the Catholic King will have, within some months, a regiment of Swiss Guards, as our King has; the said Catholic Majesty keeps many troops on the frontiers to prevent the French from entering Spain, on account of politics. The Cardinal du Bois is going to live at the Bank, where they have ceased to transport the books of the King's Library, as long as the commissioners have examined the accounts; and [?] the Company of the Indies is being moved from that house to the Bank, to the Hotel Bouvier. It is reported that the Count of Evreux is not included in the foregoing for the detail of the cavalry affairs. The Abbot d'Auvergne will not go to Flanders; he has come there solely to serve the marriage articles of his niece; it is said that the same Abbot, who is Archbishop of Vienne, and came from Cluny to this city last Wednesday, has refused the Archbishopric of Rheims; he is prepared, it is assured, this is the [matter] that remains in the account not yet settled for the payment of his bulls. The first President is still greatly incommodated in his limbs.

Next Monday afternoon, before the King in the hall of small entertainments, a representation of a ballet was given. The most guilty [person] gave the final [scene] on the side of Switzerland and Italy, through the rivers carrying heavy [?], which the glass for several could bear, indeed well diminished, but not so much that one [cannot speak of] the plague. Word has been received in [?] for the encounter of confidence in the counters of the Bank to [discuss] new prices of confidence in the counters of the Bank; the Parisians of the liquidation of the [?] [?] thousand Livres, and there began; it is thought they [will] the 18th or next of this month make a beginning there[of]; yet it is thought that the Parisians of [?] ... on account of [?], we [?] on account of lit[?], we [?] on account of the extras of the books with column [?], to commission [?], which has agreed, because they have illuminated the work very poorly.

Germany & Bordering Kingdoms

HAMBURG, 4 November. Today a proclamation has been affixed here that, although the magistrate of this city has applied all considerations, although the magistrate of this city has applied all [precautions], that although the pestilential disease, which hitherto had had opportunity from France, must not be brought here, and those coming from there must also not bring it here and affix [announcements]; and through various ordinances the council makes all that [necessary] precaution, not without heavy costs, [to prevent] all that which can tend toward the warding off of this plague: yet while all green goods, the pestilential disease on [?] advices of all plagues [?], that the disease in France is somewhat increasing, that all hope, even neighboring [?], [?] upon that foregoing precaution [?] [?] The begone is received daily with care, so had the fore-magistrates yet determined, to the warning of [?] [?] were, 1. That of all green goods, the pestilential disease on-[?], silk, linen, wool, and what is made therefrom, out of most fabrics and edges of silver or gold; and other thereof made galanteries, flax, [?] etc., whether with or paper, ribbons, felt, hides and leather until further order; but the ships which have such merchandise from France, or from [?]

Left Column

[Mediterranean news regarding quarantine procedures for ships arriving from infected ports, with provisions for inspection and isolation measures at designated quarantine sites...]

### THE NETHERLANDS

DORDRECHT, 6 November. The following vessels have arrived at this port from May: the Chameleon, Captain Tobias Jeans of London; the Anna and Jan Sara, Captain George Joppeling; the Brotherly Love, Captain Thomas Ferraby; and the Hope of Spain, Captain [?] Saatsdal, all from Sunderland; the Catrina, Captain Dirk van Bergen, from Rotterdam. Ships departing were the James and Elizabeth, Captain Joseph Davidson; the Virgin, Captain Edward Pelier; the John and Franconia, Captain Alec; and the Chandon, Captain Thomas Jeans, all bound for England; the Jan Lucien bound for Dunkirk; and the Adrian [?] bound for Newport.

AMSTERDAM, 6 November. Captain Christoffel Thym, who sailed from the lower coast on 2 August, reports that news arrived there of Spanish galleons having safely arrived at Cartagena. Captain Thym did not put in at Curaçao. The ship Celali has been lost; Jan Hendrikke, which was bound from here to Bordeaux, sailed in rough weather under Captain Egbert Kramer. From Nantes, the ship [?] Joanna Laars Willemette was sailed for Danzig, and the ship Rompel l'Amoutine, Captain Rofean, for Martinique; also Michael Wolter departed from the Vlie on the third of this month for St. Ubes. Arrived at Lisbon: the Joel Cymene, Genitor Idis, and Jacob Rapon, all from here; the Holland [?], Captain Jan Matyas from Alicante, and at Bordeaux (from which the ship l'Aurora, Captain Bouillon, has departed for St. Domingo), the Laas Balfers and Marie Sequem from Danzig, and [?] Teunls from here. The ships l'Eadion de la Lisbonnaise, Captain Simon l'Auren; the Leopold de Bordeaux, Captain [?] Heneioa; the Grand Auguste, Captain Pierre Mogon; and the Grand Marin, Captain Pierre Joseph le Gros, all four from St. Domingo; at Amsterdam: Mathias Brujnbaak and Owen Glad from here; at London: Jan Belman from Hamburg and an English warship from Lisbon. From Dunes and Dynes and various cities, the following have recently arrived: Huybert van Duyn and van Guines, Syptet Nagel-val from Danzig, Pieter Wybrands from Narva, Pierct Tiebes and Grijbriet Boskhoet from Dioulhem, and Pierct Claudie Schaap from Groningen; also already in the city: Picter Sinevis from Riga, Roelof Tylien and Oske Symons from Königsberg, Geseit Harmede and Jelle Roheche from Bremen.

The Directors of the Commerce and Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn hereby notify that on the tenth of this month, from 9 to 11 in the morning and 2 in the afternoon, instructions regarding the fourth percent dividend will be given, and this will continue on the eleventh, thirteenth, and fourteenth; as it has been concluded to dissolve the said Company, each person is advised that on the twenty-first of the month no [...]

Jacobus [?] Klees, bookseller in Gouda, will on Monday the seventeenth of November sell a fine library of books in various faculties and sciences; of which the catalogue may be obtained from Leyden [Vermey and Hunszoens] van der Aa, The Hague [Bouquet and Swa Oudsman], Harlem [?] van de Water, and in the main streets from [?] Elias de Mattous, broker. At Amsterdam on Thursday the [?] in the Doelstraat will be sold a party of young damasks and finished Ijssel linens; viewable mornings from [?] to three in the aforementioned Doel.

[Additional sales notices for furnishings, books, and property continue...]

### RIGHT COLUMN

THE HAGUE, 12 October. A letter from Russia [?] Gelders Majiers from the Great Library, Bishop Cadogan with the Lords Deputies of the States General in conference, of which his Excellency has borne the name of the great supreme [authority] of Britain, [...]has been delivered to these lands, [and arrangements have been made] with favorable wind toward London, to which a yacht [...] transport has arrived in the Maas. Moreover, the State Britannia has arrived with [...] the yacht Paulus Poltebaenus, the [?] Ambassador of the Kings of Spain to his Britannic Majesty, delivering a letter from his Master the King, though no [?] British mail [was sent]. Meanwhile, news of the marriages of the King of France with the Infanta of Spain, and that of the [?] with Mademoiselle de [?], daughter of [?], so that the King of Spain through his Britannic Majesty has been felicitated. Friday the new [Lord], Commander of [...] and Deputed [?] the Lord [?] that [?], the Deputies of the States General of the United Netherlands gave report of the state of affairs there in the land; they were met and treated with courtesy by the British, [and] proposals were produced at [?] in Zeeland; likewise the Lords Mayurel and Scoupt regarding the Chamber at Amsterdam, the Lord Montbey regarding the Chamber at Rotterdam, and Lord Advocate Alvin. The General Staff [...] [?] have made it their business to oversee all, [and it is] being arranged that a yacht next Tuesday go to the court of the King of Prussia. The sessions of [?] on the Boats were from Holland and West Friesland next Wednesday over eight days, when they will gather. This week to [?] to investigate in these provinces and to take measures, [the] Extraordinary Envoy of the King of Great Britain, as [?] of Brunswick-Lüneburg to this State, with his Majesty has been sent.

FLORENCE, 21 October. A Minister of King Augustus has arrived here from Dresden, and the King's early return is hoped for within a few days, to felicitate his Papal Majesty on [?] his devotion to the Papal majesty. The Cardinal Borgia is awaited here [regarding] [...] but the Grand Duke has [?] the visit.

GENOA, 22 October. Private letters from Rome report that the Principal [?] there with no great gold [?], wherefore many thought that city would soon be abandoned: those same burghers wish to assure [us] that the King of Sweden [?] 94½ is in the Treaty between the Crowns of Great Britain, France, and Spain.

MOSCOW [?], 29 October. Sunday the Czar's chamberlain Cantitky, who resided here, [delivered] a petition regarding the concluded Peace between the Czar and the King of Sweden. Sunday also departed the Prince Eugenius of Savoy. Monday news was received of the birth of [?] the Prince of Saxony. The Directors of the Commerce and Navigation Company of the City of Hoorn hereby notify the shareholders that on next Wednesday from 2 to 4 o'clock, [there will be] a settlement regarding the [?] on the eleventh, twelfth, thirteenth, fourteenth, seventeenth, eighteenth, twentieth, and twenty-first of this month [continuing], since the mentioned Company has been dissolved, and such [will proceed] according to the above-named [regulations] and will be conducted in the manner [...] at the end of the month.

On the eleventh, twelfth, thirteenth, and fourteenth of November, the Executors and Heirs of [?] [the] Peel, as Director of the High [?] of D. de Witte Stoefga will sell a large party of Holland and English cloths, such as [fine] damasks [?] silk fabrics, silk damasks, double and single [?], Fortelopes, taffetas, silk damettes, ditto embroidered. And also a large party of [various] woolen goods such as calamanders, [?], serge du bois, calamayan, baize, serge de Dame, crowns, carpets, and [?] silk, white and Lüttich [?] on ruothen. [For] all these goods to be seen: [inquire of] the aforementioned Director Abraham van der Peel, at the Market in the Old Town Hall, on Wednesday the eleventh of November 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning, and during the day at the Bruikke side, so that by subdivisions all the aforementioned [items] will very [?] be delivered as promptly [as possible].

Open on Thursday the twentieth of November 1721, at 9 o'clock in the morning, in the Collegiate Church [?] a general auction; sale day for the sale [?], on Wednesday the nineteenth [of] November [...] Pikelaans, presenting [and offering to sell] a very well-prepared small craft, [called] [...] [with] measuring, so [measured] that [?] [items] of its haveniall [?] are [?].

[Additional property and auction notices continue...]

Course of Exchange

**EXCHANGE RATES for this day:** Madrid 91½; Cadiz 119; Seville 111; Bilbao 91¼; Lisbon 92; Leghorn 82⅞; Livorno 93½; Genoa 94¼; Geneva 94; Paris (upright) 42¼; London 14 Sch. 11s.; Bordeaux 41¼; Frankfurt (current) 123¼ Reichsdam; Danzig 208 pounds; Excilau 15¼; Hamburg 91½; Antwerp 1 guilder per C.; Ghent 2; Brussels 2¼; Gelderland ¼; Rotterdam 1 guilder. **Agio of the Dutch East India Bank:** 1⅑%. Yesterday the shares of the Dutch East India Company were at 755.

Notices & Advertisements

On Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday, being the eleventh, twelfth, thirteenth, and fourteenth [of January?], under the direction of Abraham van der Toel, as Director [...], at Haarlem in the Hall of the Prince's Court, will be sold a large party of fine linens and drying goods; as well as a large party of whole and half silk fabrics, silk brocades, damasks, watered moiré, borders, brocatelles, fine silk draperies, Haarlem and English woolen damasks; as well as a large party of minor goods, such as half pieces of ribbons, chintz, serges, cartassian, baize, green deckers, and various linen suitable for a complete cloth and fabric shop, for further details inquire of the aforementioned Director Jeremiah van Good, broker. At Amsterdam on Wednesday the thirteenth of November will be sold a party of 1 [?] Assaline silk, and moreover Ravelin's notice will be shown.

On Monday the seventeenth of November and following days, [will be sold] a very fine library, of which the catalogues may be obtained at Amsterdam by [?] Oosterwyck and Klumpeir, [?] Jarge Houquet and Swart, Delft Boelet, Rotterdam Konen and the Widow Bos, [and] Dordrecht by the aforementioned booksellers.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 10th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.
• • •
No. 1721.No. 136.
— ☥ —

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 12th of November, 1721

LEYDSE WEEKELYKS COURANT.

SWEDEN.

STOCKHOLM, 19 October. The Ratification of the Peace concluded between this Crown and Muscovy has arrived here.

GREAT BRITAIN.

LONDON, 4 November. Word comes of a marriage being negotiated between Her Highness the Princess of Orange, daughter of the Prince of Wales, and the young Prince of Nassau, concerning the most illustrious Families in glorious memory of William. Wednesday evening the Princess and Princesses attended the Comedy at Drury Lane, together with a very great assembly of nobility, as well as Her Majesty and her Son. That evening the King held a meal at Lady How's in Summerplace House. The [Prince?] lodges in Conduit Street, near Hanover Square, until his house shall be repaired; it is said he receives daily a great many Visits from Lords of the Garter. From Dublin it is reported that the Parliament of Ireland had resolved to request the King by address, that the War now against the Soldier who is in service in that Kingdom with a [?] might be reduced, excepting those who belong particularly to the City of Dublin. The Corporation of the [?] in the County of Dermagh [?]. It is understood that the Baronet Robert Lowther, formerly Governor of Barbados, having obtained grace by the last Act of Pardon, shall be tried before the Bar of the King's Bench. Wednesday the Proclamation for a general Thanksgiving, prayer, and a day of fasting was published, and the Archbishops and Bishops are busy drawing up an instruction for Prayers, which shall be used in all the Churches and Chapels. That same day the Commons approved the Report of the great Committee for a Subsidy for the service of the sitting Parliament, and resolved to request the King by Address that the approved estimates of the expenses at Sea and Land for the Guards and Guardians in the service of the Year 1712 might continue to them: after which a proposal was made that the estimate of expenses for the making of Barracks at the House might be considered; whereupon considerable opposition was made in the Debate by Messrs. Hungerford, Ward, and others, who were of the opinion that one would be attacking the King's Authority; finally [several lines heavily corrupted and unclear]. The Lords passed some Resolutions, among which an ordinance [?] that the counting of Members should be done over 14 days, and they adjourned until Tuesday. [Text becomes increasingly corrupt and difficult to parse regarding budgets and parliamentary proceedings for the years 1711-1712, with various figures in pounds shillings mentioned, particularly sums of 379 M. and references to 4 p. per Man per Month...]

FRANCE.

PARIS, 7 November. On the day of St. Hubert the Duke of Bourbon gave a magnificent Meal, at which more than 50 persons were present; the Princess of Hanover and [?] were also invited. [The text becomes increasingly difficult to parse, with heavy corruption and repetition. Multiple passages appear to describe court events, visits to the Conciergerie, and various administrative matters, but the transcription is too unclear for reliable translation.]

[Accounts from Provence regarding plague/sickness (la Ziekte):]

The reports describe the spread of plague in various towns of Provence in October 1721, with detailed death counts and containment measures in towns including Alais, Mende, and Avignon. The Duke wrote of the disease spreading to the suburbs, though the city of Montpellier remained in good health. Various officials reported on the disease's progression and its effects in the region, with mentions of deaths in scattered locations, and indications that the plague remained severe in some areas while subsiding in others. [The text in this section is heavily corrupted and repetitive, making precise translation difficult.]

[The text ends with incomplete references to Bicedelstadt Privileges.]

France

[The opening section on France is severely corrupted in the original transcription and cannot be reliably translated. The text appears to discuss trade matters and the end of the year, but the legibility issues prevent accurate rendering.]

Mr. de Bermage, dated 27 October, sends an extract from a letter from Algiers from the Bishop of Alais, dated 23rd, containing news that the sickness continues between the Languedoc regions. Those who had the illness were driven out of the city, where the evil also seemed to be intensifying. It is not yet certain whether Monteil has been reached. However, Mende is well, and then it is clearly recorded. At Marvejols there have been only 9 to 14 sick persons per day over recent days.

Mr. de Pedenie, dated 25 October, sends a letter from Avignon dated 3rd, containing news that since then some 600 deaths have occurred; the mortality is principally among the common people, in the hospitals, and among the beggars. Bedarides has had 360 deaths; there are no more than 4 sick. At Orange 25 deaths; at Avignon 60, and the sickness has not reached. At Mente 70 deaths; at Château-Neuf by the Pope it is being somewhat contained; the rest of the Comtat is still healthy, except for one place in the territory of Camont. The Intendant Vedille has reported that for some time the deaths at Avignon amount to no more than 7 to 9 per day: the Pope has been told this. The sickness is near Carpentras in the countryside. The evil is being contained in other places of the Comtat; but they fear it for the new moon.

Mr. Jacques reports (himself?) dated 23 October, that the situation at Montpellier is very bad and that the Consulate [?] has also been infected.

Mr. de Forste [?] dated 27 October reports that Provence is generally afflicted; from the first to the eighth of October 14 deaths were recorded in the suburbs of Alais and in the city; in the hospitals 5 died, some of them from the city. From September to 23 October, the total of 399 people have died. The sickness is generally milder at the beginning and end of the months. At Charolles there are no sick; there were formerly some 400 inhabitants, 50 of whom died, the rest well, which are very strictly observed. Notable days are between Courrières and Garmouilles near Pertius.

[The text becomes severely corrupted here, with fragments discussing shipwrecks, Algerian rovers, and Dutch ships near the River Lisbon and other maritime matters, but cannot be reliably translated due to transcription errors.]

The Netherlands

BRUSSELS, 19 November. The day before Friday his Royal Highness, accompanied by his Corps, attended the meeting of the Council of State to deliberate jointly on certain preliminaries concerning the arrangement by the States of Flanders. The States of Flanders met on the 6th in Ghent, where the Count of Lalaing and the senior Councillors presented a proposition in the Emperor's name for a subsidy to maintain the Troops in that State. These States met the following day to decide on the matter. Three days ago an officer of the Court passed through here towards Paris, to proceed from there to Madrid. The Count of Mecklenburg, Governor of Aachen, is in this city.

AMSTERDAM, 19 November. There are 10 galleons and four warships which arrived from Cartagena in early August. Several warships remain there to sail on. Letters from the West Indies from Lima reported that a second serious illness had occurred in Seville, which had spread among the merchants on the coast.

[The text repeats with variations, becoming corrupted]

Also sold for some time in Amsterdam, from the warehouse of No. 9, a person named Ruisselaar, on the corner of the Gace, several hundred Moselle mats, and [?] has committed a theft in Martyn's cooperage; the fugitive [has?] [...] middle and brown hair, being a tickler and gray worker, residing on the Roelgraste. He was handed over to the authorities. His [?] mentioned goods were properly brought by Jacob Timmerman on the Keizersgracht over the corner, consisting of all sorts of faience and pottery; of which the Catalog will be seen by Mr. Lee, Leyden Vermey and Janszoons van der Aa, The Hague, Bouguet and Swart, Ueckerman, Ueelers and de Hove, and in further places with the aforementioned Jacob van der Hoeveliere, Marimin Hussel, and Coun. Verlinde Michaelis, will be sold at Rotterdam in the Windstraat, at the Herberg de Zwarte Leeuw [The Black Lion Inn], and [?] Bay, Tybugh, from the year 1710, and by Cavalcati as those [...]

Jacob Magnus, Broker, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 19th November of this year a good quantity of new fashionable fabrics, French, Holland, Flemish, and great types, double Pool and various other manufactures, such as [...] fabrics, and various everyday embroideries, to be seen the day before.

Aerlaud van Leuve, Silk broker, shall on Friday the 21st November 1711 sell a parcel of Pinweet and silk fabrics, half-silk fabrics, Faenza linens, and other [...]

Jan van der Sueug, Broker, shall on Tuesday the 25th November 1711 sell a very well-situated and pleasant country house, named Welgelegen, situated in Haarlem, and outside the great wood gate on the highway, having the view of newly mowed meadows, fitted with sliding doors, wainscoting, blue stone fireplaces, and rain and pump waters, and other waters behind, named Opgelegen, with fruit trees, tulips, pyramids & Press, in the yet aforementioned house at Starl and a kitchen; anyone interested in more information should apply to the aforementioned broker, residing on the Herenlac.

Lucas de Schepper and Cornelis de Roos, Brokers, shall on Monday the 2nd [?] at Amsterdam at the Oude Drie Logement, sell a very well-furnished and situated large family house, both above and below the ground with beautiful cellars for sale, among which are the aforementioned cellars of the same kind; gardens with their aforementioned props and planks, without trees; various orchards, which lie outside the house and some [?] of trees of [?] here very rare fruit and almond trees; and most of the bank of 12 [...], 200 men as well as women in the church, and also [...] over the door of the Way, in the [...] under supervision, whether [...] or apply to these brokers, or on the aforementioned, [...], under the condition of [...]

'S-GRAVENHAGE, 11 November. The Points of description, upon which the States of Holland and West-Friesland will be voting over the next eight days near The Hague.

England

[The England section is severely corrupted and illegible in the original transcription and cannot be reliably translated.]

Leyden

LEYDEN, 11 November. Yesterday were elected as Burgomasters of this City the Honored Gentlemen Mr. Karel Crusius, President, Gentlemen Mr. Nicolaas van Burgherij, the Gentleman Mr. Hendrik van Willigen, the Gentleman Mr. Petrus Cauzon.

On the Prinsengracht on the east side, between the Leliegracht and Nicolaas Marck, some sack-mats are missing, and on 24 October there [...] place on the Roelsgracht; a reward of 30 guilders being promised to whoever returns the same [...] under [?]; if anyone [wishes to acquire] this Monday the 17th November and following days, to sell a very fine library, the catalog can be obtained from the shops in Amsterdam, by Oosterwyk and Klumpert, Haarlem, Delft and other principal book-sellers.

Genua

GENUA, 23 October. So [...] English ships, [...] Barcelona, [...] from the court closed and [...] days on these ships, [...] without [...], [?] that the General Damage [...] would be able to [...] many large [...] and other goods [...].

Marseille

MARSEILLE, 23 October.

[Further details not provided in corrupted source]

Bayonne

[Bayonne news section too corrupted to translate]

Course of Exchange

The Course of EXCHANGE today stands at: Madrid 55 and 5/4. Cadiz and 1/4. Señor 10 and 1/4. Bilbao 1/4. Lisbon 44 and 1/4. Venice 34 and 1/2. Livorno 93 and 1/2. Genoa 96 and 1/2. Leghorn current 91 and 1/2. Lesotho current 129 and 1/4 at 9 Reichstaler. Danzig 205 [?] pct. Rostock and 1/4 of 7th. Hamburg 92 and 1/2 at 7 shillings. St. Antwerp 1 and 1/4 pct 5 [?]. Bruges. 2 Zeeland 1/4. Rotterdam 1 [?] and [?]. The Bank Agio 1 and 1/2. The Agio of the D.I.C. 7500 as are W.I.C. 50 and Subject. 940, both traded. The Eng. D.I.C. 133, Bank Index 520. 2. 91.

Notices & Advertisements

On Wednesday the 19th November 1721, at the Oude Heere Logement [Old Gentlemen's Inn], in the morning at 9 o'clock, [...] shall be sold houses, house goods and black linens; calico; purple, blue [...] and other wares; to be viewed the day before.

On Sunday the 23rd November 1721, at Amsterdam in the Neu in the Brakke Grond, shall be sold linens and other wares; to be viewed the day before, and Wednesday the [...] at Amsterdam at the Oude Heere Logement, [...] linens and other wares; to be viewed the day before.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 12th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 14th of November, 1721
Italy

ROME, October 25th. Last Wednesday an Express arrived here from Tivoli with news that the Governor of that city had been murdered, upon which a detachment of mounted archers was immediately dispatched to apprehend the perpetrators if possible. On Sunday, Cardinal Pauli departed from Rome to Albano, where he held a venerable gathering of cardinals and prelates in his cardinal residence of St. Paul; after which his Eminence, eleven cardinals, the Ambassador of Venice, together with various other gentlemen numbering 47 in all, were lavishly entertained at a banquet; but the joy of that gathering was disturbed by two gentlemen who, having exchanged heated words, nearly came to blows, whereby the scene became quite violent. On Monday, Abbé Bernini, Agent of the Grand Duke of Tuscany, presented to Prince Don Marcus Antonio Corti, in the name of that Duke, his Blessing and the Key of the Order of St. Stephen, richly set with Diamonds, valued at 3,000 Scudi; besides a Commander's stipend of 300 Piasters annual income. On Tuesday Cardinal Borgia of Nichi departed for Spain. On Wednesday morning a ship from Livorno was brought to port here; the case concerned the murder of his step-mother or mother-in-law, with whose death he had falsely accused another man, claiming that this man had robbed him of 13 Scudi; thus seeking to clear himself from guilt, saying that his father had left her all his goods; but this innocent man being unwilling to confess to such a grave crime of which he had no part, was by order of the court tortured before execution; [he] declared that the accused man was guilty and had committed the deed alone, whereupon the falsely accused was set at liberty. On the day when the aforementioned Bourgia came of age, reaching his 21st year, the Ambassador of His above-named Majesty exchanged compliments; in the evening His Excellency gave a fine concert of music in his palace, and a splendid supper at which 10 cardinals, various princes and princesses, &c., were present. On Thursday afternoon His Holiness visited the Church of the Nones, [?] Babylonish [?]. One sees reason itself a Manifesto in favor of Cardinal Alberoni. His Holiness has granted to the Knight of St. Justi at Alberoni one [?] Province and a second [?]. The Abbey of St. Leocardus in the Province of Apulia, which had been vacant since the death of the Cardinal of Touron, has by order of the Holy Father been conferred on Cardinal Don Alexandre d'Albani.

GENOA, October 25th. Soon after, four galleys of Malta arrived in this harbor to take on provisions aboard.

GENOA, October 23rd. Soon there will arrive in the harbor English merchant ships, whose captains and owners are not yet known; but it is being continued to be reported that these vessels, arriving in this quarter, will be admitted to the harbor, though the quarantine on ships will be shortened, without the general quarantine in that harbor requiring much time; one hopes that this news, which causes great joy to our merchants, will be confirmed.

France

MARSEILLE, October 26th. The situation continues thus in this region, though not from serious sickness most prevailing in this place; one sees not much mortality any longer nor much variety of plants [?]; there is no reason that from various plants [?], nor much mortality any longer; there is no reason [text becomes repetitive and unclear in original]. We also have no navigation, notwithstanding the great precautions which are here continually employed upon incoming ships, and to prevent infection, even though the goods have been found [?], and yet no navigation...

---

No. 154

Friday's Courant

...considerable robberies have been committed, and besides Magdalaine de Catouch shall be tried, and various others will be hanged. The French comedians have offered Catouch and Emeet fifty Livres, but one has not approved giving him this money, because for his sustenance he uses nothing but a good glass of wine; he receives at midday with his meal a bottle of wine and in the evening half a Fougliarde or hen: he maintains himself tolerably and in good spirits. The Curé of St. Bartholomew has visited him against the [?] and all proper observers of a [?], and sometimes sought him out to speak of spiritual matters; to which he replied with all propriety that such things did not concern him further; or whether he wished to have any religious book; to which he replied:

"My Lord, I can neither read nor write (although one does not dispute this), as I have always said at my examination; therefore not to vary thereupon: he uses great precautions that he has water mixed in his wine when he wishes to drink, which I do not know how to prevent; he governs his spirit with no difficulty, and what is very singular is that he reasons even about his proper conduct, and the least wickedness is beyond the company with which he has been associated: he says that he is not the vilest murderer of one of his comrades (which one has found that he has committed; for when he killed Pepin the Exempt, he says that he did this in order to defend his life. The Procurer is presently busy making his conclusion of guilt against him. It remains uncertain whether the Chamber of the Tournelle shall hear that famous process next Thursday and will judge. One is still seeking other miscreants, which fugitives are and which by the Châtelet are discovered: for this Isaëtte one is indebted, as everything has been obtained; it is also for this reason that the King has granted him a letter [of pardon]; but he shall remain in eternal imprisonment, which he himself has requested; for he says if he came to enjoy his release, he would be instantly murdered by the remaining members of that band; he receives no more than 10 Stuivers daily to live in the prison, and offers himself for better thoughts. A certain lady of distinction, having the curiosity to wish to see him, went with an Exempt of the Constabulary to the chamber where he lay upon straw; seeing him in this state, she was much moved, and stepping back a little, touched the fetters of his feet; and after he had seen the lady take the crucifix, he asked laughing whether she still held something against him. To content the public (who have long complained that the best performers of the Opera do not appear except when it pleases them), His Excellency Francine, Director of that Academy, with the approval of the King [?], shall at once issue orders concerning this; to wit, that the performers shall not be paid except in proportion as they perform, as was also the case in the time of the famous dancer Ballon; but in case of any indisposition of the performers, they shall draw half pay. The Lord Grajlot, Councillor-Pensioner in the Judiciary of La Rochelle, and Canon of the Cathedral Church of that city, has arrived these days: through his diligence the Priories of St. Nicholas de Laîne (which the King has conferred on Abbot Gautier) and that of St. Valentin have come to his knowledge; for His Majesty has not yet disposed of them. The Abbot [?], Bishop, of which His Majesty in the same [?] has not yet disposed. The Abbot Bisqou, Almoner of the King, is dangerously ill, as is also the Lord de Gourgucs, under-Deacon of the [?] of the Regulars, who formerly said mass in Caen. The Marquis de Coquet [?]. On All Saints' Day, Father d'Ardennes delivered [?] His Majesty, where [presently] the President has greatly succeeded; His Majesty, where [presently] the President [?]; [?] in compliments to the Queen upon his marriage [?]. Madame de Visontiefte of Folignan is in confinement, and the Lady of the Marquise de Bethiany has [?] of a daughter; ladies of [?] presently [dwell]. The Princess of Arenberg [?] [?] where she presently dwells. The Princess of Arenberg has retired these some time to the Convent of Bon Secours [?], and she has settled in the Convent of Bon Secours in [?], and at Montpellier in that city [?]. The Marquis de la Ferre, Colonel of the Regiment of Normandy, has himself sold to the Lord [?], his successor in the Regiment of Normandy, his commission to the Count of Liguy his heir. One is printing a Letter from the Pope, [?] of Panticin, Marquis, [?], and concerning bishops, and of that of Tournai, to beseech His Holiness to [?] of Tournai, to beseech His Holiness to give the [?] to the Church, after the precedent of His Holiness Comrade and [?], Paul Innocent the Third. The said President is upon the eye [?]; shall now first publicly appear; also the Archbishop of Orleans shall now first publicly appear; with such titles as with the honor and dignity of his station, which shall be placed forward, after which he shall soon hold his first public audience over the double marriage: he is having new livery clothes made. Mademoiselle de Montpollier is busy with (taking various things) the clothes which are being made for his Royal [Majesty]. Follows the letter of the King of France to the [Lord] in Spain, in answer to that of His Catholic Majesty, written concerning this marriage.

From the Letter of Monday's News:

I cannot sufficiently express to Your Majesty with what joy I received and accepted the proposal which was presented to me on your...

Left Column

[This section of the left column contains a lengthy letter or official statement that is heavily corrupted in transcription and largely illegible. The surviving fragments appear to reference royal lineage, the King's great-grandfather, Spain's Infanta, and are signed "Louis," but the original meaning cannot be reliably reconstructed.]

On Germany and the Neighboring Realms

VIENNA, 12 October. Highwaymen continue to make the roads near this city quite unsafe. The collection of tolls in the vicinity is proceeding as lightly as it has not been accomplished in many years.

There is hope here for peace, though the Congress at Brunswick has thus far reached no conclusion; some believe it would be best if it were abandoned altogether. There is talk that the Emperor might undertake a journey to France and then to Hungary and Pressburg to settle matters of religion through his high authority between the Roman and Reformed [Christians] in those places; others wish that concerning the succession of the Imperial hereditary lands at Pressburg, certain other matters might also be brought to the table. The reduction of the 12 Imperial regiments in the provinces of the hereditary lands of the House of Austria continues, and to relieve the inhabitants of Hungary and Zevenberchen, some troops from there will be sent through to Italy.

COLOGNE, 6 November. Again no Imperial Diet was held on Monday last; however, it is assured that tomorrow the matters of delegation will be addressed in the third committee, and that the secretaries will communicate regarding this to the others, and subsequently reach a decision. In the meantime, it persists that the Provincials are obliged to busy themselves with their [?] over the removal of both old and new grievances; which would be the surest means to turn everything upside down; so that from this no complete corps of Protestants will be served, but rather it depends upon the complete removal of the Baden grievances; concerning which lately no further news has come from the Palatinate. The fore-mentioned [body] has indeed received some new grievances; and as one perceives, also the reflections upon the last counter-reply of the Palatine government, which in short will be powerfully supported by the aforesaid corps.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 12 November. According to report of Captain Dirk Gaarden, certain letters from Tripoli brought this month of November from this city have arrived, [noting] that a piece of jewelry at a certain trade house fetched, from his ring, a diamond stone of approximately ten [?], being a brilliant cut, the aforementioned lord promised a reward of 30 guilders to whoever returns this diamond.

[Multiple notices of auctions and sales follow, listing various commodities, real estate, and merchandise to be sold by various brokers and merchants in Amsterdam on specified dates in November 1721. These include spices, timber, textiles, manufactured goods, and a country estate near Haarlem with accompanying meadowlands and outbuildings.]

[Several book announcements follow, advertising newly published or available works on theology, religious commentary, and other subjects, available through various booksellers in Amsterdam, Leiden, The Hague, and other cities.]

LEIDEN, 14 November.

ARCHANGEL, [date missing]. [Text illegible/corrupted]

CADIZ, 21 October. [Regarding money brought by priests, distributed to owners; text largely corrupted]

Extra Letter from Amsterdam. [Contains reports of plague or disease outbreak in the city, with details about deaths and the involvement of doctors and surgeons; however, the text is heavily corrupted and specific details cannot be reliably extracted.]

PARIS, 16 December. [Text fragmentary]

---

Right Column

Market Report and Shipping News

[Various ships mentioned as having arrived at or departed from ports, including vessels from Guinea Coast, Jamaica, and European ports. Captain names include Jan Andr., Pieter Valk, Dirk de Walt, Stoffel Boevel, and others.]

Bank Agio: 3 and 3/4

East India Company Letters: 14 1/2 East India Company (other): 302

THE HAGUE, 13 November. It is reported that the King of Prussia, Elector of Saxony, has given notice to the representatives of the States General of the birth of a son to the Electoral Princess of Saxony; for which she is to be congratulated by official letter. Similarly, General Count [?] is in conference with the appointed representatives of the States General; the aforementioned General departed this morning for the court of the King of Prussia in Berlin, and has taken with him the Secretary, the Honorable Charles Rumphius. The Prince Kurakin, Ambassador of the Czar of Great Russia, has made a journey to Amsterdam. The Honorable Kettelär has arrived in the Province of Zeeland, and appeared before the Assembly of their High Mightinesses.

[Multiple auction notices follow for sales on November 17, 19, 21, and 24, 1721, listing various commodities including Chinese and Drapers' linens, wood, Turkish fabrics, tobacco, and a country estate with orchards, buildings for horses, hopbines, and fruit trees. A large library sale is also announced.]

GREAT BRITAIN

LONDON, 11 November. [Text heavily corrupted; appears to reference the States General, American matters, and some official business, but reliable details cannot be extracted.]

[Additional fragmentary reports and notices of sales and publications follow.]

---

FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedesttraat

14 November 1721. With Privilege.

Course of Exchange

Leydse Courant

Monday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 17th of November, 1721
Muscovy

ARCHANGEL, 8 October. On the 4th of this month, the warehouse and ship of Pieter de Ridder were launched into the water, and they have prospered well. Since it is already beginning to freeze, the ships will depart within 3 or 4 days.

India

CADIZ, 22 October. On the 11th of this month, they began distributing the money that the Prince of St. Bueno brought from Cartagena to the owners; 200, and this with what will come from Vera Cruz, with the ordinary interest rate of 5 percent will also be distributed; which has caused no great discontent among the interested parties.

France

Extra Letter from Saint Ambroix, 22 October. According to news from London, it is reported that in the region governed by them, the entire situation has changed, particularly in the small villages or places: there have died at Mondi over 400 persons; they have great need of physicians and surgeons there; to help the sick in Arles, the Reverend still [loses] 5 or 10 people daily: at Junaville about 40 persons have died; this is a small place or town; but what is remarkable is that in the vicinity of [?] the unmeasurable sickness [and] the deadly plague have spread uncontrollably throughout the city; some people have died in the streets; and there are still 60 dead and sick at St. Genets. The latest letters received from the County of Avignon report that already 700 have perished; and from the 10th to the 100th, there 10 or 12 persons die daily; the shortage requires many medicines. There have already been quite a few throughout the whole region who have died, 200 for themselves as for their livestock, which in some small streams has caused much loss of sheep and other moveable goods; the persons who died or were sick had been collecting, instead of being buried (according to strict order), to be burned; but one is still in agreement, though it has been altered by the strictness and authority. All the news from Provence confirms that it is improving there considerably.

PARIS, 10 November. On the 5th of this month the Duke of Orleans was arrested and brought to the prisons of this city. On the 8th it was learned that the chamber of the Lieutenant of Fauchet [?] has taken a carriage with the young Princess.

Great Britain

LONDON, 11 November. Friday the ship Tompkins was condemned for piracy at Suffolk. On Monday [further proceedings]. One feared that [the crew], which for some time had been detained in the Indian Sea, [would be executed]. One feared that the bill concerning the infection had been overlooked: which for some time had been detained in the Indian Sea.

England

LONDON, 11 November. This time Lady [...] and her Sapphire Ivisham [...] departed and [...]

---

Monday's Courant.

Pieter Graafschepe, Abram van Cappel, and Gilles Badens of Guiskirchen, Corn. Sprangers of Nieuwpoort; and departed on Friday the ships Willi. Davidson, the Anna and Sara, George Jopling, and the Brotherly Love, Thomas Ferrubie, all for England.

AMSTERDAM, 13 November. On the 26th [October] ships departed from Lisbon and Pieter Valk, Rynder Schout, and also, it is said, Klaes Duyt; it is not rightly known what ships were there on the 31st, except for that of Anthony Orcffal from here. At Fort [?] arrived Jan Pietersse Rave, and the Vrouw Aart Janssen of this city; at Valence Gerrit Houweert, last from Cadiz; at San Sebastian Pierre le Cost, last from Dunkirk; at Nantes (from whence Christiana Brandt departed for Hamburg) Michael Oehte of Danzig and Sirkzee Nicolas van den Hoven, in 13 days from Lisbon; and it is said that at Hamburg, when the letters departed, word was that Paulus Knaile and Robb. Wildson from Lisbon had arrived on the Elbe. At Tegel there entered Egbert Kremer from Malaga, Jao Calmoft from Lisbon, and Manuel de la Sade from Bilbao.

The Netherlands

DORDRECHT, 14 November. This time departed [...] and Benjamin John Graves [...] and Sapphire [...] and Ivisham [...]

THE HAGUE, 16 November. A further Proclamation has been issued by the Lords States General and sent to the respective Provinces, in which, among other things, it is stated:

We from Our knowledge perceive that the pestilential and terrible sickness which in the preceding year first revealed itself at Marseille, and subsequently passed to Toulon and various other places in Provence, continues from time to time to extend further in the neighborhood thereof and to progress, and considering the just reasons which We had for believing that this plague could be conveyed to these lands by persons or goods, directly or indirectly, sometimes from places where the terrible sickness has revealed itself, the longer it continues the more it seems to diminish, and We are therefore obliged to employ still further and greater precautions, as have been heretofore set forth in three previous Proclamations, in order to prevent so great a calamity, God willing.

Prohibition of Ships coming from Languedoc or Provence.

That no ship or ships, coming from Marseille, Toulon, or from any places in Languedoc or Provence, and generally from any province of the kingdom of France in the Mediterranean Sea and neighboring lands near the coasts of the same province, [or] from the kingdom of Spain at all, or the bare lands there, shall be permitted to come into these lands, on pain that such ships with their cargo shall be burned, or otherwise destroyed, and the crew punished with death. Likewise with death shall be punished the owners and shippers, and all those who have given any order for the entry of such ships, or who have had any knowledge thereof, unless, waving [?] such load, their goods are restored to them.

Ordering and prohibiting the same to all pilots immediately, to permit any ships coming from the mentioned quarters to enter within the pilots' jurisdiction or before the moorings, to bring those [ships] there, on pain of confiscation of goods. Charging and ordering the pilots hereby likewise to warn such ships that they may not come into these lands, conducting themselves strictly [according to these orders].

Ordering and prohibiting by Us to anyone, whoever, in any other places, coasts or harbors of the kingdom, or of the territory of France, or of Jersey, Germany, and other islands lying near the aforementioned French coasts, whether with ships coming from there, or also over other lands, by water or by sea, to introduce any goods, wares or merchandise, or any goods in the territory of [?], or ships any goods, or from Jersey and Germany, of whatever nature these may be, on pain of loss of all goods, on pain of loss or confiscation against those places followed, as on pain of loss or ships, vessels and carriages, wherewith the goods all captains and skippers, likewise also most strict action against all captains and skippers of Jersey and Germany, come or there territory of France, or from Jersey and Germany, come or there have brought in, likewise also against all those, or that those have brought in, likewise also against all those, or that those have given any order or mandate for this purpose.

The aforesaid goods shall no longer be permitted to be withdrawn, such as straw, pepper or the like to be embraced or goods in sacks, straw, pepper or the like to be landed in coasts, vats, towers, forts, flashes, of wood, stone or glass made, on pain of banishment or loss of the eighty goods, confiscation of the ships, wherewithal they shall be used, and arbitrary correction of the place overseers and [?].

And in order that what is prescribed above may be strictly executed, all the captains of all ships coming from the ports and all ships of all ships coming from the [?], coasts or harbors, with power to take over all ships. Shall by Us none, and by sea from other ships may be taken, except those pass shall be from the places lying in the goods territory, which toward Amsterdam the northern quarter in Goerce or the Maas, in Teel or the Vlier, Vlieland, in Goerce or the Maes, in Teel or the Vier, Vlieland of Veere or tie, Vlieland or place in Land defined, in Teel or the Vier, Vlieland of Veere or tie, and to Zealand defined, in Goerce or Mass, telt Vlieland, accordingly [permitted].

The Netherlands

[Left Column]

Ships Coming from Italy, Spain on the Mediterranean Sea, and Further from the Levant

The captains of all ships coming from Italy, from the harbors and places of Spain that lie on the Mediterranean Sea, and generally from all lands and islands situated on the Mediterranean Sea (excepting the Levant, of which shall be spoken hereafter) shall not be permitted to take on any goods at sea from other ships, etc.

Ships and Goods Coming from the Levant

Under the name of Levant are understood and meant the Sultan and Egypt, Syria, Colomaaden, Natolia, Greece, Morea, the Island of Cyprus, and all the islands of the Archipelago, up to and including Santes.

The Island that, with regard to the Island of Cyprus, is here particularly disposed of.

The captains of all ships coming from the Levant shall not be permitted to take on any goods at sea from other ships, etc.

Ships and Goods from Cyprus

No ships coming from the Island of Cyprus, or having been there, shall be allowed to come into these lands, under penalty of having their cargo burned.

And also no goods coming from there, or found there, shall be permitted to be brought into these lands, also under no [?] of the strict prohibition and medical provisions united. And those who shall have brought such ships or goods into these lands, or shall have given order or command therefor, or shall have rendered assistance thereto, shall be punished with death.

Declared Goods

All goods that are entered on the manifests of the captains or boatmen as unknown shall be reckoned among the suspicious, and concerning this matter must be complied with all that which on account of the important suspicious goods has been ordered, and under the same penalty.

Ships Coming from Other Quarters

That moreover all captains, from whatever quarters, except those which are mentioned above, who shall also come, must make declaration at the first watch or customs post, and shall deliver the exact and specific list of all their loaded goods, with the expression of numbers and marks, without that on any pretext whatsoever, of however natural a kind it might be, their inspection, reservation of right, being avoided, which is considered [?].

The Lord Coper makes known that the exchange of lots of the ninth lottery drawing has been completed, and the drawing of the second class will soon begin. Note: Let each who has received notice be advised that the lots that are found to be of the ninth class up to and including [?] shall be drawn within a short time or noted, and just as well, and under the same penalty, must be exchanged as all other undrawn lots.

Jacob Magnus, auctioneer, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 19th of November 1721, at the Old site lodge, in the morning at the [time specified], Paris, blue and heavy lacquered gums, pipettes, blue-colored, Paris, blue and green piqués, double pool, and various other manufactures, such as flowered silks and fine stuffs, curious needle-cloths and chintzes, bays, catalans, calamancoes, laminates, sergies, fine-woven cloths, woolen goods, etc.

Daniel and Raphael d'Azeudo, auctioneers, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 19th of November 1721, in the evening at [?] o'clock, at the [?] Voelau, the house, with goods of the last ship from Cundao lying at the [?] Honswaart, old house of the gentlemen Elias and Manuel de Craito [?], from the [?], [?], jewels and Co. [?] auctioneers, shall on Wednesday the 19th of November, in the evening at [?] o'clock, at Rotterdam, in the [?] party pop out [?] with permission [?] Jorin, from the Graadt [?] in the Boompjes, in the warehouses of the gentlemen John and Reel Guacouae.

Jan du Rieu, auctioneer, shall sell at Amsterdam on Wednesday the 12th of December [?] in the [?] southern [?] ground, a lot of extra pure cashmere wool and other hatmaker stuffs, [?].

Roeland van Lewe, auctioneer, shall sell at Amsterdam on Friday the 21st of November, in the morning at [?] and in the afternoon at [?] o'clock, in the [?] de Bruijck ground, and a lot of manufactures, consisting of various colored porcelains, [?] and Moorea, such as footstuffs, half silk and woolen stuffs, in Haarlem in the [?] colonels, also heavy [?] and silk stuffs, 200 damasks, silk and woolen stuffs, linen goods, etc., 700 English [?] etc.

Johannes Staats, Jan Lemnets, and Hendrik Linquet, auctioneers, shall at Amsterdam sell fast, in the Nes in the Brakke ground, a lot [?] of fine garceus, a lot of muscoville mauves, bear-hides, and other felts, etc.

The Directors of the Ruemoiloen near Leiden, as by the [?], [?] of the measuring [?] on Wednesday the 17th of December 1721, in the evening at 5 o'clock, the extra [building] well-conditioned rye-mill, situated on the City's field [?] in [?], and on which place all [?] with [?] well [?] little boats, also a great woodhouse with various chambers and [?] for this purpose, a large house; and for everything that belongs to a complete rye-mill the condition and provisions [?] to be seen, [remainder as transcribed].

[Right Column]

CONSTANTINOPLE and more from there the Black Sea has been sent over: there has now come to Crete and defended would salt-yet the Plague in the plague-houses [?] great number die. In general [?] from these places or not back to Crete, one is still fearful [?] Germany and surrounding areas.

VIENNA of the 1st of November. The Count Kisski and the Crown-Baltoe of Mecklenburg have been written to [?] the chief priest of Bavaria in title dealings with the Turk-Prince, the [?]. The hope which one still cherishes leads to hope for speedy peace, Majesty [?]. The States of Hungary are closed, but one sees it under the deliberation of [?], Count of Glou and Count of the [?] raised: [?] there the greatest ceremonies of the same brother [?] and on Monday still there in this at the Court Chapel [?] buried, the same [?] in the God-house and surrounding the [?] by step great Majesty of Majesty's and of the Queen in three in the Court Chapel.

List of Goods, 200 from France, as from the Levant, which are held to be against punishment [?] [in] [?].

Earth, earthenware of kind [?] Joerin, Alvna, Armandilies Zander Dep., Anchovies, Acyn, brandy and [?] maritime [?], Coelo, Cspeira, Cardosias, Celers, Gulgoneten, Gelantis in Manche, Cranshilde, Dragos [?] medicine of all sorts, [?] [?] other [?] mentioned. Gaunesta, Gallifiem, Gumbler, Glass, Gazelle, Saint Orains or Hope [?] Comis are [?] Grue Grim em Orden, Jomtim and [?], Granier of all sorts, Harpuys, Harly, Houseing, Heif, as Weldekkaete Heist, Workbeast, Understood, and Saint all sorts of wood, Indigo, Kapt, Marcerilans, Hamierlach, and all [?] of Siren, Oil in barrels, Pattens and Elfscin, Olyseren, Orlands or [?], Pantel, Figs, Freyuans, Ratabarbe, Ryls, Ryseram in [?], Salin, Cammelas, Copijart, Cos [?], Cognijus, Ceuchjaul, and all other sorts of sail, Darram, Saltpeter, Senckhoudren, Spamangre, Suehsnd in deep of Sel-ladt, Darlet, Syryea, Spyssiets, Shrimp, Tin, Taleynus, Tupyy, Vooch, Driven and [?] other sort[?], [?] as [?] and [?], Walch in [?].

Lots of the ninth class sort exactly on the 27th of this of the night old [?] on Monday the [?] December there: attached that the lots which so [?], in the third class have drawn, and therefore not may their lots, after expiration of the [?] class claims in the class not would [?] as all other undrawn lots.

Printed in Leiden by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedstraat The 22nd of November 1721. With Privilege.

Notices & Advertisements

On Friday the 7th of this month of November, a gentleman departed from this city toward the Magtije Schouw and returned from there, and at the house Boothoomen now again in the Northerly [quarter] found [a diamond] from Uydig and Brsilannd, having a slight yellow tinge, and having found a diamond of the ninth size, for which the aforementioned gentleman offers a reward of 50 Guilders to whoever brings the same diamond to the office of the author of this Courant.

On Wednesday the 13th of December 1721, there shall be sold and auctioned publicly at the Lord's Lodge at the Burg within Leiden, for ready money and good conditions paid in cash, arranged as [?] near the city of Leiden, at Leyerdorpe [?], under the jurisdiction, [with] two hung chambers, a house manager's dwelling, stabling for 4 horses, and newly [?] there shall be found [?], and a moss-house [?], large 31 and one-half roods, in the churchyard, next to 1 rood in the chalk-holder under the jurisdiction of Leyerdam, both lying [with] view of the aforesaid homestead not to be encumbered.

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 17th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Wednesday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 19th of November, 1721
Turkey

CONSTANTINOPLE, 6 October. Captain Baffa, who arrived here some time ago with various galleys from the Black Sea, has died after waiting several days: the Grand Turk has given his position to one of his sons-in-law, who is a man of very great military skill and learning. It is reported here that the plague in the city and in Galata has not yet completely ceased, as this sickness still rages strongly. It appears that the Christians, who have not withdrawn from the plague as they desired, have not yet removed themselves from there. The envoy Enroyde of the Khan, who arrived here some days ago, has received his credentials from the Grand Vizier: it is trusted that he will soon have his audience with the Sultan. The new fortification that has been erected at Tophane is so large that one can scarcely see through its eternal casemates.

Germany & Neighboring Kingdoms

VIENNA, 5 November. The Court has received news from Count Kinski that the Czar has inclined in favor of the Duke of Mecklenburg. The Empress of Hungary has made a mild decree there: people continue to say that the Elector-Prince of Bavaria will come here shortly, when his court may well consider its circumstances. The rumors which were had that the Empress would be with child now seem quite uncertain; it is assured that His Imperial Majesty will soon take the waters at Karlsbad again. The States of Hungary have begun to assemble at Pressburg, but it is trusted that matters there will only be touched upon, and a deeper deliberation will be held in the approaching month of January. His Imperial Majesty has appointed to his Privy Council and open officials [?]. The Count of [?] is elevated to Privy Councillor and Count of the Reich, to which latter dignity the Prince-Bishops of Augsburg and [?] are also appointed as Court Marshals, as well as their brother Francis Ludwig, Free Lords of Vollheim. On Sunday and Monday the Court of Their Reigning Imperial Majesties and of the Empress attended the services during the recently passed Feast days in the Court Chapel and also in the Court Church; likewise the aforementioned Their Imperial Majesties attended services in the Court Church which was held in the Church of St. Michael; and notwithstanding the strict fast, a great [?] was kept for the Imperial Name-day. Today Their Imperial Majesties attended a service in St. Stephen's Church in the customary annual manner. It is reported that the Emperor has for some days been [?] [?] it is believed Ferdinand Reiger, Count of [?], is also elevated to his Privy Council and Count of the Reich, to which latter dignity the Knighthood of Augsburg Court Marshals are also appointed, along with their brother Francis Ludwig, Free Lords of Vollheim. Sunday and Monday the Court of Their Reigning Majesties and that of the [?] Friday, the recently passed Feast days in the Court Chapel and also in the Court Church were attended; on which occasion Their Imperial Majesties have performed services in the faith, which in the Church of St. Michael was conducted; and notwithstanding the strict fast kept for the Emperor's Name-day with some few [?], Their Imperial Majesties have likewise attended in St. Stephen's Church in the common annual Service. It is assured that the Emperor several days ago has [?] orders against the French and [?] to recruit; to which end as Court Chamber already with the [?] is in examination over the delivery of the necessary provisions. The Count of the [?] has great solicitude, because regarding the supposed dispute between His Majesty and the Crown of Sweden, it being that a prescribed Petition has been filed, upon which various Ministers of this Court have been [?] for some time.

REGENSBURG, 10 November. Previously the peace came the Russian and Prussian commissioners on the Town Hall together again and departed there with [?] of some unresolved matters, as the well-known moderation of some States, between the respective, by the Prussian Corps in Bohemia has also [?] nothing, because one still waits on another Imperial Decree. An Imperial Rescript has been given to the Prussians, wherein the same [?] over to the necessary execution of the Imperial Orders, and is beforehand from these Prussians [?] the Revolution, which Revolution [?] it is believed that shortly through the present Imperial [?] to the aforesaid Corps [?] would be, whereby ten or three days with [?] [?]; but one is [?] from Herr von Reeck to trust [?] such [?] [?], for his great good [?] is [?]; as him at Condition, whereupon the Lutheran subjects (upon whom one will that [?] shall remain [?] at the Lutheran on [?] in service of the Regency, to as the [?] [?] and middle (upon whom one will that [?] shall [?] at the Lutheran subjects (upon whom one will that [?] shall [?]; which through at the great [?], which Resolution from their Kingship and Ministers, establishing because regarding the supposed dispute their Kingship and Ministers, establishing because regarding the income is, and which through the written Subjects on the prescribed Corps [?] was. Whereout [?] [?], when the Papist Regency soon Week [?] Letter from the Duke of Blankenburg; likewise Week. On the negotiation

REGENSBURG, 12 November. Previously of [?], the Queen of [?] the Prince of [?] on the Town Hall again to sit or [?]: [?] the common Imperial Decree against the Revolution of the Lutheran and [?]; and there out remained [?], whereby the present Ministers of the supposed [?], who at the shortly through the present Ministers have all sufficiently, in the matter of affairs [?] of the life Matters, that the [?] Corps one of the [?], for have been much pressed.

France

AVIGNON, 26 October. The sickness increases here; and daily about 40 people die in this city.

ALAIS, 27 October. The cities of Montpellier, Nimes, Uzès and others, together with the villages lying around them, are all likewise afflicted with this sickness, because the infamous plague has established itself at the end of the Vieux Port, where the evil is very rife; and about 40 to 50 people have died; however, the Quarantine has been begun there: in the city 2 more people have died: a woman living near the great church has fallen and been found to have the plague-sickness; one has been careful to maintain a man who had a plague-bill, which one has [?]; his wife died yesterday morning; and heavy [?] [?] are still 4 people [?]. A league and a half [?] has been drawn to half a quarter from this city, whose gates are closed, so that no one can get in or out; the necessary merchants have closed their shops. V.S. Thus it is reported according to the Duke of Roquelaure that we are obedient, and it is forbidden for all life-[?] the league to 30 [?] approaching; so that one hopes the gates will be opened again; but it is in great alarm.

PARIS, 14 November. The third [?] all the Commissioners of the Visa, which separately in the Apartment, were dismissed for the Infante, where with the Bank removed, to there the Receipts of the necessary ended, to cause: the workers appointed by this Duke of Antis to the preparing of this Apartment, their this week to begin there. The [?] elected a Bachelor, whom two years ago following an examination had refused to accept, requested that the Faculty would give him other Examiners, which without prejudice being over his Abilities may judge: the Doctors who one [?] named, were of the advice that one his Matters would [?]; but the Rector Tournelli replied that the Faculty had their Statutes, which could not be transgressed: where upon a Curé said that the Statutes of the Faculty would be [?] said that the Statutes of the Faculty could be much changed by the Curé; the handling of his Discourse was heated, but one rang the bell, and the Assembly ended without taking any decision on this. The opera of la Farce is not [?] Monday departed for Madrid; he has taken a small Suite along which House [?] [?] [?] was after a [?], the evening-Meal shall go Round, and this the next the Paris [?], mad [?] the Mademoiselle Montalembert and shall [?] Royal go, where Mademoiselle Montalembert and shall [?] Royal go, where Mademoiselle de Montalembe[?] and shall [?], upon which the King the Opera of [?] not [?] was; lasted: the the Yta this [?] that this the next the [?], shall no one be allowed to enter all those [?] become.

On the Broad Street With Privilege.

France

All judicial proceedings and Sundays, as well as at the Tuileries in the old Louvre. The Count of Verdun will take command in the [Bolschen?]. The Czar of Great Russia has had a magnificent wire-drawing factory made at Pirenburg, which the people honor through the offices of the Lord Pajor d'Ozembray. The Lords de Firmarzour and Jalchaymont, Musketeers who had endured some difficulties in the long Tuileries alone, and in order to [...] through [or from?] the Marshal of Villeroy, one was brought to Port l'Evecqué and the other to the [Commtiergejele?] weapons, after some days when we left them; they reconciled the other during that time, and when they agreed to go fight in Flanders, which they accomplished near [Pzonne?], but the sword of the Lord de Firmarzour being held, his party told him to go fetch another sword: they then sat down at Fandin, and then joined the route near Meenen, where the Lord de la Rochaymor was kept; the Marchechaussée of Ryfel, having received that of kennels, let the house be prepared to which he was bound, but nevertheless he found means to retire outside the [Kenwyc?]: the Lord de Firmancour thereupon returned directly to the city, and presented himself 2 or 3 times in public, but when it was learned that Parliament wished to pursue him, he retired for 2 days. It is said that during this the pestilence began around some places. They are making a street from the Portal of the old Louvre, beside the Palace of Longeville, towards the Place of the Carrousel Tuileries. The Duke of Orfuna is presently the Palace where the Ambassadors are lodged, and will be inscribed on the King's rolls, as the King of Spain will also do with the Duke of Simon. It is trusted that the Spanish Ambassador will shortly have his public audience with His Majesty, if [he does?] return to Madrid. The Mother of Cardinal de Fagnae has passed away.

The Netherlands

AMSTERDAM, 19 November. At Peinau, there had already been great progress with much snow. Among the ships that will sail from Danzig back hither are found Cornelis Jellier, Pieter Dirk de Willie, Murren de Jong, Tenuis Janssen, etc. In the Zund there was unfavorable weather; near Schagen the English ship The Young Woman and Mary Thomas Mills, from Yarmouth to Danzig, was wrecked.

Pieter van Schagge, Procurator for the Respected Management of the Affairs of Ship Sales at the house of the mentioned J. Wynamns in Spuytstreet, and [Hutpen?] and H. Scheublzea among the booksellers, will be available to purchase [information].

Since a certain gentleman on Friday the 7th of this month of November, having arrived an hour or two after passing the front Doelen, offers to seek a small diamond of about ten [currency?] in weight, the mentioned gentleman promises a reward of 50 Guilders to whoever brings it to the office of the Courant.

Lucas de Scheppei and Corn. de Roos, brokers, will on Monday the 24th of this [month] sell a very well-located estate, named Harten-Lust, located in Landduven, comprising in total with the plantation about 18, and without 12 morgen; furnished with a large four-sided manor house, two-man dwelling, stable for 6 horses, coach house, hay barn, barn, dove-water [structure], shed and boat house, a royal orangery with its contents of frames and planks, without trees, various fruits, pears and apricots, [?], kitchen garden, without sons, 30 men as well as women in the church; and still 6 guilders yearly ground rent from the estate; anyone wishing to view it more closely, should contact either the brokers or at the aforementioned estate, where the grounds can be seen.

7 October. J. and D. Bontekoning, brokers, will in Amsterdam on the Nieuwedyk [sell?] 8000 green [wood?] pieces, 11080 burger woods and Noerite spars, 9000 [junkers?], 1000 twelve-ell [measures?], 2000 etc., 60 [?], 1000 copper-work, 1100 [?], 1000 [?], and [various other items?], in quality as certificates, December 1721, at 5 o'clock in the evening, in The Hague at the House-[?], and indeed through [?] house and [?], alongside a coach house, for the north side in The Hague, and coming out from the house, [belonging to the?] Ambassador of His Czaristic Majesty of Great Russia: named in the new [Mohlistaat?] in The Hague, and at the broker Lucas de Scheppei.

The Widow of Corn. van Sonneveid, in his lifetime Secretary of [Seignamt?], [wishes?] to sell or rent a well-conditioned house, furnished with various orderly rooms and garden behind; located on the village of [Noorrlading?], barn, horse stalling, wagon house, and a fine garden behind; with a [?] yard and garden opposite, as at the village and of the Company, which [is situated?] at the aforementioned part; [those wishing to purchase or rent may apply to] Waddinxen, or with Jasper van [Executum?], [?] in the governance of [Sevaard?], a house and [?], a garden behind with double land and the [?] house [?]: all this near the Golden Wagon, where the [?] [is located?]; anyone wishing [information?] should apply to [Fietet van Sittuciue?].

In Amsterdam at Andries van Damme, bookseller on the [Nokkin?] south of the Beurs, is printed and is being published a Treatise [?] of Athens, arranged in many learned [works?], in French maintained by [?] [?]. In the German [language?], and now translated into Dutch by I. A. R. G. Also at the same [place?] by [Grootebroeck?], [?], also translated from the French. Item [?] for Daniel Willink, [?]: to which is added a [?], where from [?] [?] and the same D. Willink [can be obtained?] in Leiden at Hendrik van Damme, and

In Amsterdam at And. van Damme, bookseller on the Rokkin south of the Beurs, is printed and is being published [a work on] Geography: Collection of [?], History, Geography, [?], Chronology, and [?] [?], which can be obtained at H. van Damme in Leiden; further [works?] sold at [?] Alchemy at [?], as newly ordered by various booksellers in the St. [?] Choorfstry is [printed?] and will be [?] [?] Aeternitans Logude [?] o Principiis [?] [?] Daniel [?] Booksellers [?] [?], which [?] as [?], in a

Printed in Leiden by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedestrat opposite the Town Hall. 19 November 1721. By Privilege.

---

Continuation - Right Column

THE HAGUE, 11 November. The Lords States of Holland and West-Friesland will meet tomorrow. The Lord Councillor van Linden, Governor of the Prince of Nassau, Stadtholder of Friesland and Groningen, has arrived there, and the meeting of the Lords States General will convene.

[Additional property sales and notices follow in corrupted text]

Printed by FELIX DE KLOPPER, on the Breedestrat opposite the Town Hall. 19 November 1721. By Privilege.

Course of Exchange

[The right column text is severely corrupted in transcription and cannot be reliably rendered into coherent English. The text appears to contain shipping information, exchange rates, and news from various European cities, but the original transcription is too fragmentary to produce an accurate translation.] **EXCHANGE RATES:** Madrid 94. [?] 119 c. Seville [?]. Bilbao 91 [?]. Lisbon 41 [?]. [Various other rates for?] Livorno, Genoa, Paris, London, Leipzig, Danzig, Breslau, Hamburg, Antwerp, Brussels, Zeeland, Rotterdam, etc. [Editor's note: This newspaper, from the era of the Dutch Golden Age's later period, contains typical elements: French court news, shipping information vital to Dutch maritime commerce, real estate sales, book announcements, and exchange rate tables essential for international trade.]

Printed at Leyden by Felix de Klopper, on the Breedestraat, opposite the City Hall.
The 19th of november, 1721. — With Privilege.

Leydse Courant

Friday Edition
Printed at Leyden, the 21st of November, 1721
